```
Re: printer not printing.

I think I have this nailed down, but I do not like the answer
much.

It appears that V4 cannot write generic ASCII to a printer.
A print file *must* be associated with a font, and that font
*must* be available for the printer that you are using. That
also means that you *must* have the full blown, up-to-date
windows driver for the printer ... there is no "generic ASCII"
anymore.  When I try to do things with everything set up that
way, *and* the assignment is to "printer" *and* the printer that I
want is the windows default printer, then the program works. Use
any "fontless" printer, and you get an error reading the font
file.

I have listed the test program below, BTW.  There are a whole
slew of problems with this.  First of all, the default is about point
6 text, so you also have to set the font to get a legible print
out.  Secondly, the driver inserts a form-feed on close, like
it or lump it.  When you are printing single page invoices, this
creates a problem in that not all printers can be set up for the
correct page size. One is forced to manually adjust the paper
after each page in this case.

Lastly, I cannot get it to work with *anything* in the assignment
statement except "PRINTER" .  In the case of a second printer
there seems to be no way to print.  The documented PRINTER-2
and "LPT2:" all give a runtime error of "unsupported", as does the
assignment to a network printer.  The only way to change printers
seems to be to exit, and change the windows default.

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020 PROGRAM-ID. "TESTP".
000030 AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
000040 SECURITY.
000050          Copyright (C), 1998, Donald L. Tees.

*********     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER-1. error
*********     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "lpt1:". error
*********     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "LPT1:" error

000240     WRITE PRINT-LINE.
000250     CLOSE PRINT-FILE.
000260     STOP RUN.
```

```
I had a similar problem with RM/COBOL on Win95. Trying to send the control
characters to a laser printer to set it to 16.67 cpi. It worked when the
SELECT was set to LPT1:. Otherwise (using PRINTER, PRN:, etc.) the Windows
spooler just did'nt let the non-printing characters through. I don't think
it was a COBOL problem. Also, you have to send a FF afterwards to make it
print.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<744ntr$aiq$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73urce$c33$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QT9tB4tod2Gd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7416i8$3f2$1@news.igs.net> <3664B544.E39A2DC8@mindspring.com> <743ip2$j3t$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GkOzgkfSYRzG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <743pnp$n2c$1@news.igs.net> <3665AE3D.EABF8C22@infonet.com.py>`

```

Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <3665AE3D.EABF8C22@infonet.com.py>...
>
>I had a similar problem with RM/COBOL on Win95. Trying to send the control
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>print.


That one is actually solvable.  You go into the printers section
and find the spool settings under printer properties.  Then click
the "print directly to the printer".  Then you go to your printer
and set the timeout.  BTW, there is an interesting trick you can
do with Lasers --- you can home the cursor and then send
ASCII text as an overlay, *then* send the FF.  It is a quick and
dirty way to print onto a form; you create the form using a word
processor, and write it to a file. Then the file is copied to the
laser up to but not incuding the FF.  Then you home the laser,
set for a standard font, and send an ASCII overlay.  Then the
FF.
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>One "not so clean" idea is to write the print to a line sequential
>file (Be careful - with Fujitsu WRITE AFTER 2 to a line sequential
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Actually, that is exactly what I have done. though I did not use
the CBL_COPY_FILE routine.  I simply executed a DOS copy
in a closed window.  If you look real hard, you can see it appear
on the task bar for half a second or so ... dirty, but they are
running.  One more thing to fix.

I'll tell you a funny story about such patches.  In the late seventies
or early eighties, I had a microprocessor based concrete plant.
This was pre-PC; I think it was an F8 processor.  Anyway, the
plant went down and I traced the problem to the power supply.
A new one (+-12,+5) was going to take a week to obtain, so I
patched it.  I took two loader batteries (about double the size of
a car battery), and connected them back to back to give me
plus/minus 12.  I then grabbed a handful of diodes, and soldered
then end to end (each one will drop the voltage about 1.1 Volts)
and onto the plus end to give me plus five.  I hooked it up,
turned it one, and told them to call when the power supply came
in.

Three years later, I got the next service call.  Turned out the
batteries were dead.  By that time the power supply had come,
gone, and was no longer being made.  We changed the
batteries, and it ran like that until 1992, with BI-yearly
maintenance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Woes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7479il$orc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73urce$c33$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QT9tB4tod2Gd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7416i8$3f2$1@news.igs.net> <3664B544.E39A2DC8@mindspring.com> <743ip2$j3t$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GkOzgkfSYRzG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <743pnp$n2c$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-MSb2s1Rbf3du@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <746u4g$id6$3@news.igs.net> <36670FBD.732C62C@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36670FBD.732C62C@home.com>...

>I knew a guy who was happy when a company came out with a third party
>floppy drive for Atari 800's.  This had a Z-80 chip and 64K, and I have
>even run a CP/M program on the drive using my Atari for a terminal.  But
>that's not why he liked it.  He liked it because it was 12 volts and he
>lived in a Tee Pee!

One get get *very* inventive in a spot.  I used to work with a
techie who was in his fifties back when I was in my twenties.
He was about 4 foot 10, and had red hair, and so was called
"Big Red".  Big Red was an old relay man, and became a fan
of LSI circuits the day they were invented.

One day we were 9000 feet underground, working on a hand
built computer that weighed the boxcars travelling between
two gold mines (they blended ore for uniformity, and it froze
into the cars on the surface[this is NORTH]).  I was having
sporadic glitches that I was sure were hardware, and sure
enough Big Red traced it to a burnt out resistor on a specific
board.

We were halfway between the mines, at a location that took,
by the time you reserved elevator time, four hours to get to
the surface.  However, they have some big stores down there
(keep in mind they repair trains, if not computers) and there
was an electrical shop only 1/2 hour away.  Big Red went to
the electrical department, and picked up a large spool of
standard, red, number eight wire.  He phoned the surface,
and asked the engineering department to look up the resistance
per foot.  He then measured off about 1700 feet of wire,
rolled it up neatly, and wired the two ends into the board.
Everything worked just fine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Woes

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bsR92.36$ot.77976@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<73urce$c33$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QT9tB4tod2Gd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7416i8$3f2$1@news.igs.net> <3664B544.E39A2DC8@mindspring.com> <743ip2$j3t$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GkOzgkfSYRzG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <743pnp$n2c$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-MSb2s1Rbf3du@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <746u4g$id6$3@news.igs.net> <36670FBD.732C62C@home.com> <7479il$orc$1@news.igs.net>`

```
The last few posts in this thread illustrate something I have been saying
about Y2K.  The programming and technical community is a creative, clever
bunch of folks.  When the ox gets in the ditch on 1/1/2000 there will be
millions of those creative, clever people thinking up every kind of kludge
imaginable to 'get by' until things stabilize.  I believe this to be the
primary mitigating factor that the knowledgeable, responsible doomsayers
are either missing or underestimating.
```

```
>The last few posts in this thread illustrate something I have been saying

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

I could not agree with you more.  But then there
have been people running around screaming "the sky is falling"
since Greek times, and I do not expect it to change.  It is just
a way for some to get the attention they crave.  One of the first
things I learnt in business is not to get too excited over my own
advertising department, and to *never* believe anybody but the
guy actually doing the work.
```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36670a1f.29530570@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<73urce$c33$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QT9tB4tod2Gd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7416i8$3f2$1@news.igs.net> <3664B544.E39A2DC8@mindspring.com> <743ip2$j3t$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GkOzgkfSYRzG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <743pnp$n2c$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>
>Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Donald:

FormPrint can allow you to position borderless data fields on a blank
"form definition."  This will allow you to print to a pre-printed
form.  The other thing you can do is scan in the form image and place
data fields over the giant bitmap image of the form.

Before you get too far down the road, the latest version of FormPrint
(not yet on our ftp site), allows you to set a virtual margin as well.

This is pretty important for pre-printed forms because different
printers have different margins, so if you want the data to appear
exactly in the same place every time, you'll need to set a virtual
margin from your program.

Send an e-mail outside the group and I'l see if we can't help you
solve the Fujitsu printing problem.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

---

<a id="t-82554313ed"></a>
## Thread: Old COBOL code — 15 msgs

### Old COBOL code

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3663f7b4$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
Hello:

I'm a consultant looking at old COBOL code, supposedly running in
production on VSE today, and found some unusual syntax in ASSIGN names. 
My guess is that this is D-level COBOL that hasn't been compiled in 25
years.  Can anyone confirm this? Thanks.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. 'XX052010'.
         DATE-WRITTEN. TRANSLATED FROM GE-400 SERIES MAL ON 08/25/69.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
         SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-360 G40.
         OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-360 G40.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT NAME-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS012' DIRECT-ACCESS 2314
                   ACCESS IS RANDOM
                   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                   SYMBOLIC KEY IS NF-SYM
                   RECORD KEY IS NF-REC-KEY.
           SELECT CARD-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS009' UNIT-RECORD 2540R.
           SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS004' UNIT-RECORD 1403.
           SELECT HISTORY-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS010' UTILITY 2400.
```

#### ↳ Re: Old COBOL code

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981201175403.11960.00000856@ng155.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3663f7b4$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 1998 14:05:04 GMT

Posted some curous ASSIGN clauses ...

<<
...
 SELECT NAME-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS012' DIRECT-ACCESS 2314
                   ACCESS IS RANDOM
                   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                   SYMBOLIC KEY IS NF-SYM
                   RECORD KEY IS NF-REC-KEY.
           SELECT CARD-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS009' UNIT-RECORD 2540R.
           SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS004' UNIT-RECORD 1403.
           SELECT HISTORY-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS010' UTILITY 2400.

>>

And asked about the antiquity of the forms ...
<<
My guess is that this is D-level COBOL that hasn't been compiled in 25
years.  Can anyone confirm this? Thanks.
>>

I believe that you have no indication that it has been especially long since a
successful compile.  Very recent compilers had backwards compatibility in order
to tolerate this kind of code. This was done to allow users to avoid code
changes.

In later compilers "Any assignment-name after the first is treated as a
comment", according to a VS COBOL II Language Reference. Although I can not say
that this applies to VSE (my experience is MVS/XA and MVS/.ESA).

You state that the code is "supposedly running" in production.  Don't assume
that the program is obsolete. Also don't be surprised if it compiles yet.  The
2314 device identifier certainly suggest a very early origin for this code. 
But I don't think you have displayed anything at all in the posted code that
supports an assumption of obsolescence of the code.  It may well be a cuttent
program.

Best Wishes,




Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Old COBOL code

- **From:** Ralph Jones <leclay@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36647C56.EBEBDF8B@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3663f7b4$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
I think you are right.

The state of S.D. had a 360-40 with a 2314's attached running DOS ver 14?.
The 2314 had 8 40mb removable disks attached with 2 spares.  They leased
for $5 grand a month.  They were replaced by 2319's sometime in 1970?.  The
2319's were the same device leasing for $3.5K per month.  D COBOL was
replaced by USAC - ANSI COBOL around this time. There were some significant
differences between the compilers.
Ralph Jones

Gilbert Saint-flour wrote:

> Hello:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>            SELECT HISTORY-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SYS010' UTILITY 2400.
```

#### ↳ Re: Old COBOL code

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%iU82.550$Lx2.1031@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<3663f7b4$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
I dug into some reference material from that era. The ASSIGN clause
appears to be ANSI 68. My IBM Full ANS COBOL manual says that
the ASSIGN clause was changed in version 3. VS COBOL was two
releases later. My best guess is version 1 which dates from about
March 1969. Version 2 was 1970; hence too late for this program.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

Gilbert Saint-flour wrote in message <3663f7b4$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>...
>Hello:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
SERIES MAL"?

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Old COBOL code

- **From:** Walkdavw@NaSPA.Net
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<91260346466820@NaSPA.Net>`
- **References:** `<3663f7b4$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
Yep, that is D-COBOL alright! I remember having to convert hundreds of
D-COBOL programs to FCOBOL, when one of the new reserved words was SKIP
1 SKIP2, etc. However, the programmer who wrote all the old code was
named SKIP. Guess what almost all his paragraph and data names were?????



David Wakser, TrackAmerica, Leonia, NJ
```

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

#### ↳ Re: help required

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36634032.4089660@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<365b1d8d.3699473@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Tue, 24 Nov 1998 20:59:51 GMT, gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com wrote:

>I got the following errors in cobol. can anyone explain them to me, in
>human terms?
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>procedure nam required, verb expected SCREEN found

Your compiler probably considers SCREEN to be a reserved word.  Try
changing that paragraph name to something else.
```

##### ↳ ↳ (relatively) new reserved words

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666F57C.D28E98BE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<365b1d8d.3699473@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <36634032.4089660@news1.frb.gov>`

```
> Your compiler probably considers SCREEN to be a reserved word.  Try
> changing that paragraph name to something else.

I long ago got in the habit of using the prefix MY- in front of real
words such as SCREEN.  This habit has followed me from language to
language and has saved me from problems such as this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help required

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ebnh$7vh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<365b1d8d.3699473@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <36634032.4089660@news1.frb.gov>`

```
Fujitsu is very fussy about columns. The line right after 260
has a TAB rather than 8 spaces in from of it, from where
you cut and pasted from another program.  Go into the text
editor, and type 8 SPACES before it and your problem
will go away.

And yes, I agree that an editor that cannot figure out TABS
is as Mickey mouse as hell.

WDS wrote in message <36634032.4089660@news1.frb.gov>...
>On Tue, 24 Nov 1998 20:59:51 GMT, gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com wrote:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>-------------------Decoy@Spammer.Trasher----------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is one period per paragraph a good idea?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36665ac0.85096840@netnews>`
- **References:** `<365EAE47.64A4@swbell.net> <19981127221812.23446.00001715@ng153.aol.com> <3660cff4.32520762@netnews> <JPb82.8802$w_6.5401276@news2.mia>`

```
'Twas Sun, 29 Nov 1998 13:17:29 GMT, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Randall Bart wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>As I recall, it was Medium Systems COBOL 74.

Most of our applications (all our big programs) were in COBOLV (68).  I
wrote a user-coded DMPALL in COBOL 74, and it had a big ELSE IF cascade.
It probably wasn't bigger than 15 levels.  Before COBOL-85, I was a heavy
user of ELSE IF and I've never hit an ELSE IF nesting limit in any
compiler, though I did have one where I could tell each ELSE IF increased
the nesting, and nesting was limitted.

How deep were your ELSE IFs?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is one period per paragraph a good idea?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wFA92.1001$Jl.230064@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<365EAE47.64A4@swbell.net> <19981127221812.23446.00001715@ng153.aol.com> <3660cff4.32520762@netnews> <JPb82.8802$w_6.5401276@news2.mia> <36665ac0.85096840@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
>
>How deep were your ELSE IFs?

It's been a long time, but there were quite a few, perhaps fifty or
more.  It happened at least twice, as I recall.  This was during the
time I was trying to abandon GO TO completely.  Once I started using
PERFORM/EXIT and GO TO EXIT there was no problem. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is one period per paragraph a good idea?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3664065B.71139EB9@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<365B309F.1F2@swbell.net> <19981124234854.07622.00000250@ng-cr1.aol.com> <365CC024.9BABAE35@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
8<

> ... I am also maniacal in getting the indentation right,
> and changing it as I modify a program, so the visual impression *is* the
> logic. I'd be interested to hear if any others share this view.
>
> Bill Lynch

Which reminded me of the executable code in the )PROC division of ISPF Panel
Definitions.
These actually have NO period, NO scope terminators or whatever. When you see ...

)PROC
 If condition1
    statement1
    If condition2
       statement2
    Else
       statement3
    statement4
 Else
    statement5
 statement6

... it does what it shows: statement4 is within the true-branche of the outer if and
likewise statement6 whint the else of it, purely by indentation!
Nice, because no extra type work for any kind of delimiter (dot nor end-x)
required.

The Frog
```

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36640AFA.AB4C006D@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<19981124231101.07622.00000238@ng-cr1.aol.com> <73g565$8ad@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <365c2333.0@news3.uswest.net> <6sW62.5417$w_6.3243227@news2.mia> <365C3D11.62943D24@home.com> <N%X62.5436$w_6.3293801@news2.mia> <73hli7$sms$1@news.igs.net> <365c7f59.0@news1.ibm.net> <73idtu$crh$1@news.igs.net> <9B2619169B4A3C12.FD7265450D18EA10.CFD9F7670F8B6A55@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3660ec8b.39824359@netnews> <3661613F.E71E9553@ix.netcom.com> <36617174.0@news1.ibm.net> <73shnd$l1t@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <3661d80b.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif did it correct. He wrote:

> >>The stepping was boustrophedonically to minimize mechanical forces.

As usual in COBOL syntax, non-underlined words are not mandatory.

> >However: "The stepping was [PERFORMed] boustrophedonically ...."

Hey, I remember my school teacher telling that the"ly" was required in case of a
verb:
The stepping was xxx. It stepped xxxLY. In natural speech, naturally spoken.

Is my grammar still correct[ly used]?

Jumpingly Froggedly yours.
Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: boustrophedon - Was Is one period per paragraph a good idea?

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<743jmi$jf9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981124231101.07622.00000238@ng-cr1.aol.com> <73g565$8ad@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <365c2333.0@news3.uswest.net> <6sW62.5417$w_6.3243227@news2.mia> <365C3D11.62943D24@home.com> <N%X62.5436$w_6.3293801@news2.mia> <73hli7$sms$1@news.igs.net> <365c7f59.0@news1.ibm.net> <73idtu$crh$1@news.igs.net> <9B2619169B4A3C12.FD7265450D18EA10.CFD9F7670F8B6A55@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3660ec8b.39824359@netnews> <3661613F.E71E9553@ix.netcom.com> <36617174.0@news1.ibm.net> <73shnd$l1t@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <3661d80b.0@news1.ibm.net> <36640AFA.AB4C006D@IMN.nl>`

```
COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote in message <36640AFA.AB4C006D@IMN.nl>...

>Hey, I remember my school teacher telling that the"ly" was required in case
of a
>verb:
>The stepping was xxx. It stepped xxxLY. In natural speech, naturally
spoken.
>Is my grammar still correct[ly used]?


Yes.  The "ly" changes it from an adjective to an adverb.  My
old man was a stickler for grammar; I can remember being told
a thousand times at the dinner table: "you did not do
"REAL GOOD", you did "REALLY WELL".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New "REFERENCE FORMAT" (was: Is one period per paragraph a good idea?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OOj230aH#GA.117@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<365d4953.1299010@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <19981124231101.07622.00000238@ng-cr1.aol.com> <73g565$8ad@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <73hqdl$2rm@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <365CC635.3512438E@att.net> <73v88v$5ua@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36638786.CAB3C18A@att.net> <740j4n$5jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote in message
<740j4n$5jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>In article <36638786.CAB3C18A@att.net>,
>  wblynch@worldnet.att.net wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The parsing process in any competent COBOL compiler could easily identify
the end of a paragraph or end of a section without explicit periods (full
stops) or END-xxxx features, at least in the standard format using A and B
margins.

A paragraph end is indicated by presence of the next division or section or
paragraph name, or the physical end of the source code.

A section end is indicated by presence of the next division or section name,
or the physical end of the source code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New "REFERENCE FORMAT" (was: Is one period per paragraph a good idea?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36664ddc.81805569@netnews>`
- **References:** `<365d4953.1299010@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <19981124231101.07622.00000238@ng-cr1.aol.com> <73g565$8ad@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <73hqdl$2rm@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <365CC635.3512438E@att.net> <73v88v$5ua@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36638786.CAB3C18A@att.net> <740j4n$5jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OOj230aH#GA.117@upnetnews03>`

```
'Twas Tue, 1 Dec 1998 20:55:05 -0500, when "Dennis J. Minette"
<dennis_minette@email.msn.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The parsing process in any competent COBOL compiler could easily identify
>the end of a paragraph or end of a section without explicit periods (full
>stops) or END-xxxx features, at least in the standard format using A and B
>margins.

Forget A and B margins.  Too many compilers have already done away with
them; too many J4 decisions have been made which assume margins are gone.
You will not revive margin restrictions; they are dead, dead, dead.
```

```
some compilers are want you you enter the full size of the variable:
0000000034
```

```
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. MARC.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 NOM PIC X(10).
       01 MONT PIC 9(10).
       01 remise PIC 9(10).
       01 cliex PIC x(4).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Calcul-rem.
           PERFORM obtention
           PERFORM UNTIL cliex EQUAL 'zzz'

           PERFORM remi
           PERFORM sortie
           PERFORM obtention
           STOP RUN
           .
       obtention.
           DISPLAY 'donner le nom ou fff pour stopper'
           ACCEPT NOM
           IF NOM EQUAL 'fff'
              MOVE 'zzz' TO cliex
           ELSE
                DISPLAY 'donner le montant d achat'
                ACCEPT MONT
                MOVE 'vrai' TO cliex
           .

       remi.
           IF MONT GREATER THAN 5000
               MULTIPLY MONT BY .25 GIVING remise
           ELSE
               IF MONT GREATER THAN 3000
                  MULTIPLY MONT BY .15 GIVING remise
               ELSE
                     MOVE 0 TO remise
           .

       sortie.
           DISPLAY 'le nom'
           DISPLAY NOM
           DISPLAY 'mont achat'
           DISPLAY mont
           DISPLAY 'remise'
           DISPLAY remise
           .
```

```
Hi

I don't run COBOL on the PC much, but when I
did I had similar problems with ACCEPT statements.

Looked around for sample programs that came with
the compiler and just cut-and-pasted.
Love "cheating" that way !


Have you tried a REDEFINing to ensure you
are ACCEPTing AlphaNumeric characters ?

01 mont-achat-alpha.
      05  mont-achat   PIC 9(10).

or

01  remise-apha     PIC X(10).
01  remise  redefines remise-apha     PIC 9(10).


ACCEPT  mont-achat-alpha.

ACCEPT  remise-alpha.


regards,
     Bob Sharp

=========================================================
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<743rc0$mdo@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3662F6E2.6B402CB9@yahoo.com> <3663434b.0@news2.uswest.net> <cKH82.515$Lx2.962@news1.atlantic.net> <3664cb2b.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote in message <3664cb2b.0@news2.uswest.net>...
>Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>>ACCEPT NOM is a valid form of accept. On a PC it will wait until
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just to add to this, ACCEPT without a FROM is supported by ALL ANSI
conforming compilers.  Where it gets its input is defined by the implementor
(for example, it can be the console or a system input device or something
else).  On IBM mainframes, it defaults to the SYSIN DDname.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<743jpc$qlr$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com> <3662dbd2.5877763@news> <O0lnFNcH#GA.179@upnetnews05> <36650652.1282040@news.shoresoft.com>`

```
In article <36650652.1282040@news.shoresoft.com>,
Eugenio Belli <swp@hi-net.it> wrote:
>On Wed, 2 Dec 1998 02:11:56 -0500, "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank you  for the attention. 

Mr Belli, *many* problems which seem *very* difficult are the result of
'one trivial oversight' (after all... imagine how happy some people would
be if my parents had used a condom on one particular night!)... this
reminds me of... A Story!

A decade and a half ago my eldest brother was in the computer lab at a
university where he was studying for his Master's Degree in Business
Administration; the lab had just received a shipment of IBM personal
computers and these were still rather... new in 1983.  Anyway, he heard a
student call out, rather distressed, 'Does anyone here know *any*
FORTRAN?'; my brother walked over, slowly, and said 'Well, I only have
about twenty, maybe twenty-five years at it... what do you need?'

The poor student pointed at his screen.  'I cannot get this subroutine
invoked... and I've been working with this CALL statement for 2 1/2 hours
now and I cannot get it to work!'

My brother glanced at the screen and said 'This might be because *here*
you spell the name with one 'b' and *there* you are spelling it with two?'

Mr Belli, this technique is also called, at times, 'four-eyed
debugging'... it is *very* useful and happens here all the time.  Your
apologies are accepted, your gratitutde is appreciated... but now you have
taken on the burden of giving the same favor which you have received here
to someone else.

Carry this responsibility well, Mr Belli.

DD
```

<a id="t-e7ce9aa220"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL NT gnt/int — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-11](1998-11.md#t-e7ce9aa220)._

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL NT gnt/int

- **From:** "Stephen Thomas" <thomass@logica.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be1d4f$d2836180$0b47ea9e@GDLPPC04.logica.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<73uc1n$971$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You don't say the nature of the problem you are running into.  Any specific
error messages?

But one thought that occurs to me ...

Were they compiled on NT with the 32bit Workbench you are now using, or
were they compiled with the 16bit compiler?   INTs produced by the 16bit
compiler would continue to run under the 32bit runtime but the GNTs would
not.  This is because GNTs are native code, geared to the platform - or at
least the chipset - on which they were compiled.  INTs are, up to a point,
platform independent.  So an INT compiled under DOS would run under, say,
Solaris.  Assuming that the program does not request any DOS specific
services, of course :)
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL NT gnt/int

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<743n47$935$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<73uc1n$971$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <73uc1n$971$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
 <formula_ffo@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
>I have a problem running .gnt files on NT 4.0, but I don't experience the same
>problem when I use intermediate code file (.INT)files.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I use Micro Focus Workbench v4.0.07

If it runs as an int but breaks as a gnt then you almost
always have an NGC (Native Code Generator) bug.  Often only
one processor type's NGC has the problem.  Micro Focus
insists you isolate the problem and build a small demo
program before they accept the bug report.  I have spent
days doing this kind of thing, inserting DISPLAYs until I
find the point where the gnt goes south.  Occasionally
inserting the DISPLAY makes the problem go away, or subtly
modifying the statement that is failing.  I think code
optimizations and odd instruction pipelining / register
changing interactions are responsible.  It must be tough
being an NGC maintainer.

Get the latest version of the NGC before you start
this process.  Maybe the bug is fixed already.  If not,
Micro Focus will be more interested in assisting you
with your problem.

You probably can't wait for them to get around to fixing
your bug, so work with the failing statement until the
problem stops occuring.  Breaking up COMPUTE statements is
often efficacious, as are insertions of unnecessary MOVE
statements or changing USAGE clauses of involved
variables.  Find something that works and then put a
comment in the code explaining why it's there.

Good luck.
```

## Thread: Saving Fujitsu Cobol files. — 1 this month / 2 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-11](1998-11.md#t-e523a9b28b)._

#### ↳ Re: Saving Fujitsu Cobol files.

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36640ECD.44B2C0B9@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<365b3ce3.285336424@news.trxinc.com>`

```
Brad wrote:

> Does anyone know offhand how to save a COBOL file in the Fujitsu text
> editor so that when I load it up to the mainframe it won't come out
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> P.S. I'd read the docs but FINALS are upon me <S>

Save it as ASCII source.txt file and then upload it to that main frame.
Take care of the usual stuff of deleting CR/LF delimiters etc. and
perhaps you should explicitly specify the file to be converted to EBCDIC
i.s.o. taking the bitpatterns unmodified. Most tools are able to do this.
I think you have the CR/LF=variable-to-fixed-format conversion wrong, and
/ or no ASCII->EBCDIC conversion?

The Frog
```

<a id="t-8c91916e61"></a>
## Thread: Netexpress 2.0 embedded SQL.. — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Netexpress 2.0 embedded SQL..

- **From:** tommy nilsen <tommynilsen@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3663AC07.E7B7E92F@yahoo.com>`

```

When I read a record from Oracle 8.0 (using embedded SQL) I get the
following message:
"Indicator variable required but not supplied"
Sqlstate = 22002

Does anyone know what this means, and how to avoid it ??

Cheers,
tommynilsen@yahoo.ocm
```

#### ↳ Re: Netexpress 2.0 embedded SQL..

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36645A93.45532F3C@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<3663AC07.E7B7E92F@yahoo.com>`

```
tommy nilsen wrote (I snipped some):

> "Indicator variable required but not supplied"
> Does anyone know what this means, and how to avoid it ??

Likely you SELECT or FETCH a column INTO :hostvar ?!
Likely this column is not NOT NULL, so may at run-time be not filled
(nil value).
This type of columns shoud be INTO :hostvar:flagvar.
Note the *two* colons, *no* comma in between.
In the DATA DIVISION define:
    nn  HOSTVAR  PIC whatever.
...
    nn  FLAGVAR  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
        88  NOT-FILLED  VALUE -1.
These definitions may be in different group items or even be separate 01
levels.
In the PROCEDURE DIVISION, after the SELECT or FETCH, assumed that
SQLCODE = ZERO, you can test wehter the column was nil or not. IF NOT
NOT-FILLED does not read well, is it not?! I suggest you name the 88
level something like XXX-IS-EMPTY to avoid linguistical NOT NOTs. It is
good style to hqave a separate XXX-IND var for each host variable XXX.

As I recall, SQL defines -1 as "not filled" ("empry") and any other
value as "filled" ("not empty").

Good luck. I hope!, you tell? this helped.

The Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36654AFA.68EEB320@home.com>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <3662B383.3179F426@home.com> <#vzrbmcH#GA.169@upnetnews05>`

```
> >I also don't like having one person maintaining a called program while
> >another person maintains the calling program.  Or worse replace those
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> illness I also readily admit)?  <g>

Partly.  My main problem is that I have seen again and again problems
when one person is maintaining one program while another one is
maintaining a dependent one.  Especially in large organizations where
they don't even know the applications well outside of their
department.   Someone implements a change and tests what he knows, and
something else breaks.

Where this is really supposed to be good is with system wide changes
such as Y2K.  But in a large organization, this can't all be thoroughly
tested at once.  One department comes in and does a rigorous test on the
test system, it gets implemented and tested again, then another
department does the same thing.   If the modules are isolated, this is
possible.  If some calls are integrated across the enterprise, this
can't be done.

Isolation has its advantages.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** iscdavid@aol.com (ISCDavid)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>`

```
One of the delights of COBOL if properly written, is that it is wonderfully
maintainable. I'm not yet convinced that the supposed "efficiencies" of
re-usable code at run-time are worth losing the ability to absolutely know what
the code is doing!

I'm not talking about heavy reuse of source code. That has been done probably
since the second COBOL program was written...its just easier now sitting at a
PC instead of lugging bundles of cards around. I too hate to retype...and hated
card punches even more!

But I had a system where I originally used a ton of COPYs and CALLs. It worked
OK, but... the application was one where if (should I say WHEN) the system went
south, it needed to get fixed in minutes, not hours or days. So except for
system CALLs or a few functions, I ended up copying all the source directly
into each program. Sure the sources got a whole lot bigger, but thats a small
cost for having everything right there when researching a problem.

David Olson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36658962.5F0335C5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com>`

```
I don't often quote a whole message, but I agree with you 100%.  I have
written C++, written and maintained Java, and written and maintained
Paradox for Windows.  I like having my code in one window for
maintenance purposes.

Also - I like being able to isolate a test to a single program, well
understood by users.  I can't do that if my change impacts a dozen
programs across the enterprise.

ISCDavid wrote:
> 
> One of the delights of COBOL if properly written, is that it is wonderfully
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> David Olson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com>`

```
But if you choose your components from a code reposititory, then the
components are throughly tested and perform as specified. Little
testing is required, and debugging is a LOT easier because 
*almost* always, the bugaboo is in your code, and even when it is not,
you are probably using the repository code in an unspecified manner.
<grin>

I used to think differently, but experience has taught me I was 
incorrect. Throughly tested and maintained modular code is not 
only reusable, but dependable. 

The problem with COBOL, and the major problem as I see it, is that
you do have write access to the source. I would wage war on 
someone doing full copybook copies into individual source modules.  
But then, as a lot of folks her eknow, I don't come from a COBOL
background, and I have seen and used OO technology to great
advantage.

-Paul



Howard Brazee (NOSPAMbrazee@home.com) wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote in message <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com>...

>The problem with COBOL, and the major problem as I see it, is that
>you do have write access to the source. I would wage war on
>someone doing full copybook copies into individual source modules.


Now that is crazy.  If you have something like a record layout that
is used in hundreds of programs, then *not* putting it into a copy
book is close to criminal.  Another place is for the simple
standardization of datanames. By copying a standard constants
section you can create a system wide "reserved word list" that
increases readability and standardization. Things like ON-STATE,
OFF-STATE, yes-flag(always"Y", no-flag(always"N"), etc.  You
say you are not from the Cobol world; it is obvious.  Would you
program in "C", and wage war on ".H" files?

When you say things like that, I start to question whether you have
done any large projects at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666A3DD.4D5AE243@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> paulr wrote in message <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> done any large projects at all.


One advantage of compile-time includes such as copy members is that we
can change them and then test each subsystem separately instead of
having to test the whole enterprise at once.

Of course we have to be organized and know which programs contain the
copies and/or calls.  And make sure everybody is compiling with the
correct compiler switch.  And implementation requires compiling each
program so it has to be slow.  (If you skip testing, you would resent
that slowness)
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald, the quote was in reply to somone copying the copybook into
individual source files - with an EDITOR not the COPY command.


And if you have hundreds of applications depending upon a record layout,
a copybook is *not* the right place for those record layouts, an 
*object* is. If nothing else, it protects you from changes in the 
record layout and such - in that when you change it, the ripple
changes are very limited. Most of the hundreds of applications 
won't have to be changed at all.


Donald Tees (donald@willmack.com) wrote:
: paulr wrote in message <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com>...

: >The problem with COBOL, and the major problem as I see it, is that
: >you do have write access to the source. I would wage war on
: >someone doing full copybook copies into individual source modules.


: Now that is crazy.  If you have something like a record layout that
: is used in hundreds of programs, then *not* putting it into a copy
: book is close to criminal.  Another place is for the simple
: standardization of datanames. By copying a standard constants
: section you can create a system wide "reserved word list" that
: increases readability and standardization. Things like ON-STATE,
: OFF-STATE, yes-flag(always"Y", no-flag(always"N"), etc.  You
: say you are not from the Cobol world; it is obvious.  Would you
: program in "C", and wage war on ".H" files?

You are damn right I would- record layouts don't belong in .h 
files anymore than they do in copybook files. 

: When you say things like that, I start to question whether you have
: done any large projects at all.


You obviously have *not* done any large projects, or you would realize
what an utter nightmare doing things that way in COBOL can be. Mind 
keeping your insults to yourself?

In non-object COBOL, the situation you describe above would be 
better handled with a set of callable routines that return 
specific data values. As in more formal object technology, this 
greatly insulates the rest of the system from changes. In C,
you would have functins returning specific values.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Donald, the quote was in reply to somone copying the copybook into
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't have to be changed at all.

Every decision has its costs.  In this case, the cost of making it an
object is that each application gets changed immediately upon changing
the object.  So each application has to be tested and implemented
simultaneously.  Often this is desired, other times it isn't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee (NOSPAMbrazee@home.com) wrote:
: paulr wrote:
: > 
: > Donald, the quote was in reply to somone copying the copybook into
: > individual source files - with an EDITOR not the COPY command.
: > 
: > And if you have hundreds of applications depending upon a record layout,
: > a copybook is *not* the right place for those record layouts, an
: > *object* is. If nothing else, it protects you from changes in the
: > record layout and such - in that when you change it, the ripple
: > changes are very limited. Most of the hundreds of applications
: > won't have to be changed at all.

: Every decision has its costs.  In this case, the cost of making it an
: object is that each application gets changed immediately upon changing
: the object.  So each application has to be tested and implemented
: simultaneously.  Often this is desired, other times it isn't.


Not always true. Objects, if designed properly and kept under control
in a repository can usually be used dependably with module tests. This
is true even in defense environments, and is one of the *big* benefits
of OO development. 

It really feels like it goes against the grain, but it works. I would 
still be nervous without some integration testing, but for a well known
application, this can also be pretty well automated.


-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
> : Every decision has its costs.  In this case, the cost of making it an
> : object is that each application gets changed immediately upon changing
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> application, this can also be pretty well automated.

That's what they claim.  But enterprise-sized applications (for instance
Windows NT) need to be enterprise-wide tested with each day's changes. 
Usually, in OO environments, widely used objects get changed very
rarely.  And large enterprises also have full test environments for when
they are changed.  But I don't know of any significant environment where
full testing is not a requirement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com>`

```
Well, believe it or not, Windows NT is TIGHTLY coupled, despite the use
of DLL's and all that jazz. Even there, how much testing happens when
a DLL is updated? They certainly do not test every application that uses that
DLL. And in general, nothing breaks, even given Microsoft as the code manager.

I agree that I integration testing is pretty important, but there are 
numerous enviornments out there where it is not actually required. 

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
If a DLL (or any other component) has a well-defined
interface it can be tested in isolation and no testing of
all the applications using it is needed.

If on the other hand, the DLL is ill-behaved (many Micro$oft DLLs
seem to be) then there will be applications that (knowingly or
unknowingly) will break when the DLL is updated, because
they either rely on the bugs or are zapped by the DLL.

Integration testing should not be needed if unit testing has
been done and the interface is clean, but since these two
conditions are often not met, integration testing (or production
deployment!) becomes where the bugs surface...


paulr wrote in message <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Well, believe it or not, Windows NT is TIGHTLY coupled, despite the use
>of DLL's and all that jazz. Even there, how much testing happens when
>a DLL is updated? They certainly do not test every application that uses
that
>DLL. And in general, nothing breaks, even given Microsoft as the code
manager.
>
>I agree that I integration testing is pretty important, but there are
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

changing
>: > : the object.  So each application has to be tested and implemented
>: > : simultaneously.  Often this is desired, other times it isn't.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>If a DLL (or any other component) has a well-defined
>interface it can be tested in isolation and no testing of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
   <much snippage>

Just a "plug" for others to tell J4 (send a note to das@microfocus.com) that
I am right and they are wrong ....

One of the things that I really REALLY dislike about the draft of the next
Standard is that the new user-defined functions can have "side-effects".  In
other words, you can create a function that does not simply return a value
but that can change the parameters passed to it; change the values of
external data; generally "silently" update anything that it can get
addressability to.

See the above paragraph for WHY I think this is such a bad idea (but have
yet to convince J4 or WG4).
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74m3ai$1gi$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>...
>
[...]
>
>Just a "plug" for others to tell J4 (send a note to das@microfocus.com)
that
>I am right and they are wrong ....
>
>One of the things that I really REALLY dislike about the draft of the next
>Standard is that the new user-defined functions can have "side-effects".
In
>other words, you can create a function that does not simply return a value
>but that can change the parameters passed to it; change the values of
>external data; generally "silently" update anything that it can get
>addressability to.
>
There is a great need for functions to return two or more values.
Generally, this includes the normal, expected modification of a
data item and the return of a flag to indicate success or failure.
Such operations are rather common in C. IMNSHO, it is also
one of the reasons why COBOL is so much easier to use than
C. Nonetheless, if COBOL is to have functions; then having
such "side-effects" is reasonable.

OTOH, the function could return one expected value and RAISE
an exception for the failure; but I have yet to find an exception
mechanism that I like.

Furthermore, I do see potential problems with the use of functions
(with "side-effects") and the EVALUATE statement. I mentioned
this previously.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
Randall Bart wrote in message <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews>...
>'Twas Tue, 8 Dec 1998 17:21:01 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
>
>>One of the things that I really REALLY dislike about the draft of the next
>>Standard is that the new user-defined functions can have "side-effects".
In
>>other words, you can create a function that does not simply return a value
>>but that can change the parameters passed to it; change the values of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>--


Just to be clear (and to respond to this and another note in the thread)

A) Yes, I am most certainly a fuddy-duddy

B) I have tried to get someone (anyone?) to help me understand what the
difference (in purpose) is between a user-defined function and a called
sub-program in the draft.  It seems to me that "in theory" a function
returns a value and a sub-program acts upon the parameters passed to it.
However, in the COBOL draft, a sub-program can return a value and a function
can update any of the parameters passed to it.  It seems to me that this is
WHY the current draft is "crazy".  The only real difference between the two
(as currently defined) is that a function can be accessed "in-line" while a
sub-program is accessed out-of-line via a CALL statement.  If this really is
INTENDED to be the difference, then we shouldn't have introduced functions -
but just "inline subprogram invocation" - (just like inline object
invocation).

I am in no way against subroutines that can update every parameter (passed
by reference) or anything else they want. This is a feature well embedded in
COBOL.  I just thought that user-defined functions were supposed to be
something "new" - and (as I understand their theory) are supposed to be
limited to something that cleanly "returns a value".

Oh well, this is definitely a fight that I am not going to ever win at J4
(or WG4) - but I still would like someone who is on the other side to
explain to be what they think the difference between a subprogram and a
function is!
```

```
>WHY the current draft is "crazy".  The only real difference between the two
>(as currently defined) is that a function can be accessed "in-line" while a
>sub-program is accessed out-of-line via a CALL statement.  If this really
is
>INTENDED to be the difference, then we shouldn't have introduced
functions -
>but just "inline subprogram invocation" - (just like inline object
>invocation).
>
Of course, another name for "inline subprogram invocation" is function. ;-)

There are many similarities among CALL, CALL RETURNING, and
user-defined FUNCTIONs; but, they do have different purposes.

Use a user-defined FUNCTION, if you want to use but do not want to
retain the result (or the result in its returned form).

Use CALL RETURNING, if you are calling a program (possibly in another
language) that does not or cannot use an implementor-defined method
for returning a value; such as, the RETURN-CODE special register.

Otherwise, use CALL.

>I am in no way against subroutines that can update every parameter (passed
>by reference) or anything else they want. This is a feature well embedded
in
>COBOL.  I just thought that user-defined functions were supposed to be
>something "new" - and (as I understand their theory) are supposed to be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
A FUNCTION returns a value to the point of invocation; a subprogram
does not.

As I see it, this is the most significant difference.

The languages PL/I, FORTRAN 77, Pascal, and Visual Basic all make
this same distinction; the programming languages C and C++ do not.
In the latter languages, all procedures are functions that may or may not
(void functions) return a value.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74s4fo$qq1$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <74rkkb$gnp$1@server.cntfl.com> <36716B12.F276F51A@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```

Warren Porter wrote in message <36716B12.F276F51A@ASPMnetdoor.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>If it can be used elsewhere, how?  What would be the syntax?  Would a
different
>form be necessary so it wouldn't look like an element in an array?
>
Note: The following is my interpretation from CD 1.2.
The code has not been verified.

In the draft standard, a function has a type and can be used in any
place the type can be used. A numeric function can be used where
a numeric value can be used; an integer function can be used where
and integer can be used; and so on.

The function types are: Alphanumeric, Boolean, Integer, National, and
Numeric.

An example of an alphanumeric function is:

function-id. composite-person-name.
data division.
working-storage section.
01 ptr pic s9(4) value 1.
linkage section.
01 last-name pic x(30).
01 first-name pic x(30).
01 middle-initial pic x(2).
01 composite-name pic x(64).
procedure division using last-name, first-name, middle-initial
              returning composite-name.
    move 1 to ptr
    move spaces to composite-name
    string first-name delimited by space
        space delimited by size
        into composite-name with pointer ptr
    end-string
    if middle-initial not = spaces *> prevent additional spaces in output
        string middle-initial delimited by space
            space delimited by size
          into composite-name with pointer ptr
      end-string
    end-if
    string last-name delimited by size *> last name may contain
                                                            *> an embedded
space
        into composite-name with pointer ptr
    end-string
    .    *> the exit must be in a sentence by itself - CD 1.2
    exit function.
end function composite-person-name.

One would define a function-prototype for the function and
reference that prototype in the REPOSITORY paragraph.
The file (? - method is implementor-defined) referenced would
contain the text:
-----------
function-id. composite-person-name is prototype.
data division.
linkage section.
01 last-name pic x(30).
01 first-name pic x(30).
01 middle-initial pic x(2).
01 composite-name pic x(64).
procedure division using last-name, first-name, middle-initial
              returning composite-name.
end function composite-person-name.
-----------
Using the function:

    move
      composite-person-name ( em-name-last, em-name-first, em-name-mi )
        to pr-em-full-name

The fact that the function is defined as a function-prototype and
referenced in the repository paragraph is sufficient to prevent it
from being recognized in other contexts, such as an array.

Furthermore, referencing the function-prototype in the repository
paragraph allows the translator to verify that the parameters to the
function are of the correct type.

If the function-prototype is not referenced in the repository paragraph,
the function-name must be preceeded by the word FUNCTION.
Hence, MOVE FUNCTION COMPOSITE-PERSON-NAME ( ... .

I hope this helps; but I also hope I interpreted the draft standard
correctly.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74s3f3$mok@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <74rkkb$gnp$1@server.cntfl.com> <36716B12.F276F51A@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```

Warren Porter wrote in message <36716B12.F276F51A@ASPMnetdoor.com>...
   <snip>
>
>Does this mean a function could be used in a COMPUTE statement or ONLY in a
>COMPUTE?  I.e. COMPUTE X-OFFSET = X +  FUNCCOS(ANGLE) * RADIUS.
>If it can be used elsewhere, how?  What would be the syntax?  Would a
different
>form be necessary so it wouldn't look like an element in an array?
>
  <snip>

I am not quite clear about your question but hopefully the following will
answer it anyway.

A) In the amended '85 Standard, an INTRINSIC numeric function may ONLY be
used where an arithmetic expression can be used. (Therefore, it can be used
in a COMPUTE statement but not in an ADD or MOVE statement.)

B) In the draft of the next Standard, a numeric function (intrinsic OR
user-defined) can be used wherever a numeric sending item can be used.
(Therefore, it can still be used in COMPUTE but can also be used in ADD and
MOVE.)

Does this answer your question?
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3672D3BC.D6A12934@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
--------------snippage--------------

> something "new" - and (as I understand their theory) are supposed to be
> limited to something that cleanly "returns a value".
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> function is!

Once upon a time, the idea of function was proposed as I recall it
as an extension to those Functions added in '89.  Some of the extensions
are specific to COBOL perhaps, but I know I felt early on that
there would be "functions" that would surface that might be universal
within certain types of applications. It was thought that the user
could DEFINE a function statement that did what ever the function 
required in the manner the language defined the statements in the
language. The procedure for controlling the DEFINE, and extending
the user's compiler to recognize it was not yet understood.

While it's not clear to me that I have clearly stated the direction of
DEFINE and that it is the same idea as FUNCTION now, it does seem to fit
the DLL, OO kind of idea in some manner.

As a means of illustration, my early work background was in COST
PLANNING in a Tubing Specialty Plant. We used formulas constantly to
determine the weight per foot of pipe, and other business functions
not defined in the language except via the arithmetic or compute
functions, and these needed to be accurate and consistent.

I believe for example, that FORTRAN added DB to the language because of
the huge investment in airframe design in their FORTRAN Libraries.

Perhaps, those working on an extended FUNCTION do not have this same
thing in mind, but it would have value to be able to do what I have
defined. Some people might want to call this ability as a macro
language. Certainly, screen handling, and other newer considerations
might be simplified by the creation of something that eased the writing
in that area. Why is it necessary to resort to the use of Visual Basic
in some quarters to create a GUI for business programs.
There is a need to keep up with the times.

Warren Simmons
warrens@iname.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> B) I have tried to get someone (anyone?) to help me understand what the
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> function is!

When I learned Eiffel at Uni recently (piece of paper for post Y2K
blues) I learned an interesting rule that might apply here:

Procedures update, functions enquire.

This is also called programming by side effect.   For example when
reading an integer from a console the routine that reads the integer
could also set an EOF flag, how is the following code intepretted?

	MOVE function get-integer( file ) to feild.

Under eof circumstances the answer is ?????

Now if we restrict to update only by procedures

	call  read-ingeter-data.
	if eof
		exception handling
	else
		move function last_read_integer(file)  to feild
	end-if
	
After carefully thinking about this I feel that this is a good idea,
though it might be more difficult to acheive than you might think.

Ken
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message <3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
[...]
>> I am in no way against subroutines that can update every parameter
(passed
>> by reference) or anything else they want. This is a feature well embedded
in
>> COBOL.  I just thought that user-defined functions were supposed to be
>> something "new" - and (as I understand their theory) are supposed to be
>> limited to something that cleanly "returns a value".
[...]
>
>When I learned Eiffel at Uni recently (piece of paper for post Y2K
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This sample appears to be at odds with your earlier comments
concerning the purpose of procedures and functions. That is,
it appears that the CALL (a procedure) is enquiring about the
end of file status.

I think you are correct in your logic. A "read" is an enquiry!
It enquires about the status of the operation and it also has
the side effect of making the data available to the program.

Though, IMO, it would make more sense as:

    if get-integer ( field ) not = 0 *> get-integer returns status
        *> error getting integer - do not use field
    else
        *> use field
    end-if

or by using the exception mechanism:

declaratives.
get-integer section.
    use after exception ec-user-get-integer-error.
    set get-integer-error to true
    resume next statement.
end declaratives.
...
    move get-integer () to field *> get-integer returns the value
    if get-integer-error
        *> error getting integer - do not use field
    else
        *> use field
    end-if

Neither of these solutions requires a second program; nor do
they require EXTERNAL data to pass the integer to the second
program.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3674E4B8.7D186ECC@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au> <750p4p$jhj$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote in message <3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au>...
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
> e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

Rick,

The second option 'move' then 'if' is basically an error waiting to
happen.  I write in C and the above checks are commonly required for
allocating memory (zero is allocation failed).  It is common for these
checks to be omitted even by experienced programmers.  Both the first
and second method are not significantly better than coding a call.

Focusing on files is possibly obfuscating the issue so I will try again.

If you review the internal functions (by fortune or design) show the
principle:

If 'function currect-date' updated the system clock by two minutes every
time it was called it would not be useful.

If the date conversion functions altered the values passed to them then
the usefulness would be limited at least.

If you call a defined function 'yield' in a farming notion this could be
done in the following way:

	yield:
		get values
		calculate values
		return values

Then if you wish to alter the use slightly to use a range for the yield
suddenly the function has to be changed to 

	perform get_values
	
	yield:
		calculate values
		return values

It is generally better to start with the second version and if everyone
accepts that the functions CANNOT update then code debugging is
restricted a bit when checking where values have changed.

Possibly a better way of saying it is that if you move A to B, the
programmer does not expect A to change, so do not do it.  It would be
good if the compiler could enforce this even if it as optional.

I hope that this clears it up a little.  I will try and find some
references to this concept that explains it clearly.

Ken
```
