```
> > declaratives.
> > get-integer section.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>checks to be omitted even by experienced programmers.

This is rather interesting because the second option is more
closely related to the C++ try/catch mechanism. The first is
more closely related to C!

>  Both the first
>and second method are not significantly better than coding a call.

Eliminating a program and an EXTERNAL data item could be
considered significant.

Correct me if I am wrong; but did your example "get-integer" come
from Bertrand Meyer's OOSC2, page 422-423? If so, reconsider the
second option. I think you will find it to be very close.

[...]
>If 'function currect-date' updated the system clock by two minutes every
>time it was called it would not be useful.

I would call it broken.

>If the date conversion functions altered the values passed to them then
>the usefulness would be limited at least.

If it has unintended side-effects, it is broken.

[...]
>Possibly a better way of saying it is that if you move A to B, the
>programmer does not expect A to change, so do not do it.  It would be
>good if the compiler could enforce this even if it as optional.
>
If I might modify your statement to read "move F(A) to B"; since we
are discussing functions.

The programmer MAY not expect A to change in function F.
Ultimately, it is the responsibility of the function writer to ensure
that F(A) will have no "unwanted" side-effects with respect to A.
It is the responsibility of the function user to ensure that function F
is used as the designer intended. If the modification of A in F(A)
is an intended consequence of using the function, the user (of the
function) should know that!

>I hope that this clears it up a little.  I will try and find some
>references to this concept that explains it clearly.

-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<758imt$clk@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au> <750p4p$jhj$1@server.cntfl.com> <3674E4B8.7D186ECC@zip.com.au> <758eq5$erp$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <758eq5$erp$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>
>  <much snippage>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>I hope that this clears it up a little.  I will try and find some


I know that the Standard cannot prevent EVERY "programmer error" but WHY
should it be "the responsibility of the function writer to ensure that F(A)
will have no 'unwanted' side-effects with respect to A" ???

There are several VERY simple rules that could be added to the Standard that
would eliminate many problems - without limiting much functionality:

A) In a function proto-type, all USING (as opposed to RETURNING) parameters
must be by CONTENT or VALUE - but not by REFERENCE

B) The EXTERNAL data attribute would be illegal in a source unit defined
with a FUNCTION-ID paragraph or which was called or invoked by such a unit

C) (and I am less certain about the "benefits vs costs" of this), the File
Section (and all other related items) would not be allowed in a source unit
defined with a FUNCTION-ID.

  ***

Yes, as stated in another part of this thread, "tools are dangerous" - but
why make them more so - unless a REAL need is met by the added
functionality.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<758ts5$mfp$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au> <750p4p$jhj$1@server.cntfl.com> <3674E4B8.7D186ECC@zip.com.au> <758eq5$erp$1@server.cntfl.com> <758imt$clk@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <758imt$clk@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>Rick Smith wrote in message <758eq5$erp$1@server.cntfl.com>...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>function) should know that!
[...]
>
>I know that the Standard cannot prevent EVERY "programmer error" but WHY
>should it be "the responsibility of the function writer to ensure that F(A)
>will have no 'unwanted' side-effects with respect to A" ???

For the same reason that it is the resposibility of the CALL writer to
ensure that CALL F USING A RETURNING B (where A is BY
REFERENCE) will have no 'unwanted' side-effects with respect to A!
This also applies to CALL F USING A B in COBOL-85 and earlier
where B is a value to be returned and BY CONTENT is not available.

>There are several VERY simple rules that could be added to the Standard
that
>would eliminate many problems - without limiting much functionality:

What problems? I have no problem with functions! I have no problem
with functions that modify arguments to those functions! It is normal
and useful with other languages. (I do not accept that all concepts
from other languages should be included in COBOL. I simply have
no problem with including functions.)

>A) In a function proto-type, all USING (as opposed to RETURNING) parameters
>must be by CONTENT or VALUE - but not by REFERENCE

Except that this option eliminates the possibility of using some functions
that are written in other languages. (I do find it curious that BY CONTENT
is not available, except by implication.)

>B) The EXTERNAL data attribute would be illegal in a source unit defined
>with a FUNCTION-ID paragraph or which was called or invoked by such a unit

Except that this option eliminates the possibility of setting a global
status indicator or using a global parameter (unless passed to the
function).

>C) (and I am less certain about the "benefits vs costs" of this), the File
>Section (and all other related items) would not be allowed in a source unit
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>functionality.

Though we, previously, discussed differences between CALL and
FUNCTION, we never discussed the advantages of FUNCTIONs.

1. It takes fewer statements to use a FUNCTION.
2. FUNCTIONs require no intermediate variables.
3. The return value of a FUNCTION may be used as an argument
    to another FUNCTION.

Additionally, IMO:

4. Both the word and the concept of FUNCTION is more familiar to
    those with an inclination toward mathematics.
5. The use of FUNCTION may be more comfortable to those with
    a lot of experience in other languages.
6. It is conceptually easier to use FUNCTION when interfacing to
    non-COBOL programs.
7. It is conceptually easier to translate some non-COBOL programs
    to COBOL by using functions.

Overall, IMO:

There is not a major difference between CALL and FUNCTION; but
COBOL is enhanced, not diminished, by the presence of FUNCTION.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
Snip....
> 
> >A) In a function proto-type, all USING (as opposed to RETURNING) parameters
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Compatibility with other languages is important.  This could be an
exception
though as it is not coded with the cobol compiler the rules would not be 
enforced.

> >B) The EXTERNAL data attribute would be illegal in a source unit defined
> >with a FUNCTION-ID paragraph or which was called or invoked by such a unit
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Internal calculation variables are naturally allowed to be modified but 
anything that changes state must not be part of a function.  Inserting a 
function call above the one you are performing now should NOT alter the 
result of the call.

There are times when retained flags internal to the function are useful,
 i.e. the zip code in Aus is 4 digits and postcodes are randomly grouped 
into mail centres, the first call could load a table with the mail
centre 
codes from a file but subsequent call just use table lookup.  In this 
case the data is 'part of' the function not setting flags outside the 
scope of the function itself.  (Another zip code function would have to 
create it's own table if it wanted one.)

> >
> >Yes, as stated in another part of this thread, "tools are dangerous" - but
> >why make them more so - unless a REAL need is met by the added
> >functionality.
> 
Programming style is very important, just think about someone producing
a 
program full of periods in a 'period free' shop.  This is moving a 
fundamental rule of good programming into the compiler IF there is
consensus.


Snip discussion on functions in general which I agree with.
```

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <367AC3C6.B53891E2@zip.com.au>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
>> >A) In a function proto-type, all USING (as opposed to RETURNING)
parameters
>> >must be by CONTENT or VALUE - but not by REFERENCE
>>
>> Except that this option eliminates the possibility of using some
functions
>> that are written in other languages. [...]
>>
>Compatibility with other languages is important.  This could be an
exception
>though as it is not coded with the cobol compiler the rules would not be
enforced.

BY CONTENT is enforced by the COBOL system. It is enforced
by copying the value (contents) of the data item into a temporary
storage area. That temporary storage area is then passed
BY REFERENCE.

The COBOL system has no knowledge of which language is used
to implement the function.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <74kcc6$5eh@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <3670a1d6.171497929@netnews> <74qusp$1lq@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3673464E.CF2F41A1@zip.com.au> <750p4p$jhj$1@server.cntfl.com> <3674E4B8.7D186ECC@zip.com.au> <758eq5$erp$1@server.cntfl.com> <758imt$clk@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <758ts5$mfp$1@server.cntfl.com> <367AC3C6.B53891E2@zip.com.au> <75luam$32$1@server.cntfl.com> <367F972D.24A471B2@zip.com.au>`

```
Both CALL and FUNCTION prototypes are added in the draft of the new
Standard.  It does allow you to tell whether the parameters to be passed are
by REFERENCE or BY VALUE (and possibly by CONTENT - I haven't looked
recently).  It also allows for items to be defined/required to be "integers"
or "numeric".

I think this answers your question.  (This is a feature that some - but not
all - vendors have already implemented - as an extension)
```

```
Leif Svalgaard (leif@ibm.net) wrote:
: If a DLL (or any other component) has a well-defined
: interface it can be tested in isolation and no testing of
: all the applications using it is needed.

: If on the other hand, the DLL is ill-behaved (many Micro$oft DLLs
: seem to be) then there will be applications that (knowingly or
: unknowingly) will break when the DLL is updated, because
: they either rely on the bugs or are zapped by the DLL.

: Integration testing should not be needed if unit testing has
: been done and the interface is clean, but since these two
: conditions are often not met, integration testing (or production
: deployment!) becomes where the bugs surface...


: paulr wrote in message <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com>...
: >Well, believe it or not, Windows NT is TIGHTLY coupled, despite the use
: >of DLL's and all that jazz. Even there, how much testing happens when
: >a DLL is updated? They certainly do not test every application that uses
: that
: >DLL. And in general, nothing breaks, even given Microsoft as the code
: manager.
: >
: >I agree that I integration testing is pretty important, but there are
: >numerous enviornments out there where it is not actually required.
: >
: >-Paul
: >
: >
: >Howard Brazee (NOSPAMbrazee@home.com) wrote:
: >: > : Every decision has its costs.  In this case, the cost of making it an
: >: > : object is that each application gets changed immediately upon
: changing
: >: > : the object.  So each application has to be tested and implemented
: >: > : simultaneously.  Often this is desired, other times it isn't.
: >: >
: >: > Not always true. Objects, if designed properly and kept under control
: >: > in a repository can usually be used dependably with module tests. This
: >: > is true even in defense environments, and is one of the *big* benefits
: >: > of OO development.
: >: >
: >: > It really feels like it goes against the grain, but it works. I would
: >: > still be nervous without some integration testing, but for a well known
: >: > application, this can also be pretty well automated.
: >
: >: That's what they claim.  But enterprise-sized applications (for instance
: >: Windows NT) need to be enterprise-wide tested with each day's changes.
: >: Usually, in OO environments, widely used objects get changed very
: >: rarely.  And large enterprises also have full test environments for when
: >: they are changed.  But I don't know of any significant environment where
: >: full testing is not a requirement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366E8CA8.F2CBB10B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
> Integration testing should not be needed if unit testing has
> been done and the interface is clean, but since these two
> conditions are often not met, integration testing (or production
> deployment!) becomes where the bugs surface...

So if you were managing an enterprise which had mission critical code,
you have a choice  (assume you are also doing everything you can to make
sure that those conditions  are being met):

1.  Assume no bugs will surface.  (OO proponents seem to prefer this)

2.  Assume that when bugs surface, they won't be critical (Microsoft
appears to do this)

3.  Keep REALLY critical code separate, losing the advantages of
integration and reusability.  (Old timers tend to do this - width broad
defintions of "REALLY critical")

4.  Test everything which could possibly be impacted by a change in a
module/object.

5.  Test the "REALLY critical" applications which could be possibly be
impacted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74mdts$cgu@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <19981202112218.03726.00001177@ng148.aol.com> <36658962.5F0335C5@home.com> <744nc5$ttq@lotho.delphi.com> <7457hn$ksj$1@news.igs.net> <747djs$9d6@lotho.delphi.com> <3667F04E.2447D0BB@home.com> <74hp97$efl@lotho.delphi.com> <366D3582.82DFBE48@home.com> <74k6jh$gr1@lotho.delphi.com> <366da47a.0@news1.ibm.net> <366E8CA8.F2CBB10B@home.com>`

```
No - not at all.

First off, have really good methodology implemented.
Have Configuration Control
Have module / unit and integration testing

It is hard to imagine that unit / module testing 
can be just as reliable as full scale integration 
testing, but it certainly can. You won't be able to 
do it in traditional COBOL shops though, at least,
I do not think so.

-Paul
```

## Thread: Use of ELSE-IF. — 26 msgs

### Use of ELSE-IF.

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10cf3e9f1f07a56b9896c3@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Hi Gang,

I have been reading some of the postings related to the topic ELSE-IF 
and I have a couple of thoughts on the subject. 

First, I have to admit that I could not see the purpose for this. It 
seemed to me that the existing IF/THEN and the EVALUATE covers almost 
every possible condition that could come up. And the ELS-IF was just a 
bit redundant, IMO.

Then I thought what the heck, why not add features and if I need them 
later at least they would be there. I may not use it right away but at 
this point Cobol could use more options and this may not be a bad idea.

After some thought I started to wonder what code using the ELSE-IF would
look like, I have not used it so I was curious. So I though let me code
up a few lines and see what it looks like and compare it to the current
methods available and here is the result.

Example 1:
Problem:

To make this simple lets say you have four variables A, B, C, and D. 
And only the following conditions could be possible:

If A = B then perform TEST-1
If A = C then perform TEST-2
if A = D then perform TEST-3

The following is code is not using a scope terminator.

Using the ELSE-IF: 		Using the IF:
                          
IF A = B 			      IF A = B
THEN				      THEN
    PERFORM TEST-1                PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE-IF A = C                 ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-2                IF A = B
ELSE                              THEN
    PERFORM TEST-3.                   PERFORM TEST-2
                                  ELSE
                                      PERFORM TEST-3.

Comment: It looks ok, I guess. I think it is a lot cleaner
than the existing structure and it makes quick work of a 
simple example. In this case the standard IF is a bit large 
and you really don't need to use an EVALUATE, so this works
out real well. 

Now here is my question, how do you apply scope terminator(s)
to the ELSE-IF in the above example assuming that the scope
terminator would be END-ELSE-IF.

Any comments, thoughts???
```

```
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of ELSE-IF.

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981202142013.29940.00000459@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10cf3e9f1f07a56b9896c3@news.earthlink.net>`

```

In article <MPG.10cf3e9f1f07a56b9896c3@news.earthlink.net>,
JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas) writes:

>Now here is my question, how do you apply scope terminator(s)
>to the ELSE-IF in the above example assuming that the scope
>terminator would be END-ELSE-IF.

I use ELSE-IF quite a bit in Easytrieve-Plus. There is no need for yet another
scope terminator because the ELSE-IF keeps the same conditional depth as the
IF/ELSE/END-IF,  e.g.,

IF A = B
   ...
ELSE-IF C = D
   ...
ELSE-IF E = F
   ...
ELSE
   ...
END-IF

The ELSE statements would be executed if neither the IF nor any of the ELSE-IFs
were true.

COBOL'85 EVALUATE statement serves a similar function, only the syntax would
be:

EVALUATE TRUE
   WHEN A = B
      ...
   WHEN C = D
      ...
   WHEN E = F
      ...
   WHEN OTHER
      ...
END-EVALUATE

I have a question, though. Would the introduction of an ELSE-IF in COBOL buy us
anything that the EVALUATE doesn't?

Mark A. Young
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of ELSE-IF.

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XXh92.646$Lx2.1626@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10cf3e9f1f07a56b9896c3@news.earthlink.net> <19981202142013.29940.00000459@ngol08.aol.com>`

```

Mark0Young wrote in message
<19981202142013.29940.00000459@ngol08.aol.com>...
>
>I have a question, though. Would the introduction of an ELSE-IF in COBOL
buy us
>anything that the EVALUATE doesn't?
>
EVALUATE does a fair job of defining decision tables.

IF/ELSE does a fair job of defining binary decision trees.

IF/ELSE-IF/ELSE would be better than IF/ELSE in defining
non-binary decision trees since the non-binary portions remain
at the same nesting level. For example,

IF TAX-STATUS = TS-MARRIED
    IF FILING-STATUS = FS-SEPARATE
        PERFORM ...
    ELSE-IF FILING-STATUS = FS-JOINT
        PERFORM ...
    ELSE
        PERFORM FILING-STATUS-ERROR
    END-IF
ELSE-IF TAX-STATUS = TS-SINGLE
    PERFORM ...
ELSE-IF TAX-STATUS = TS-HEAD-OF-HOUSEHOLD
    PERFORM ...
ELSE
    PERFORM TAX-STATUS-ERROR
END-IF

The four possibilities for TAX-STATUS occur at the same level
and the three possibilities for FILING-STATUS occur at the same level.

IF TAX-STATUS = TS-MARRIED
    IF FILING-STATUS = FS-SEPARATE
        PERFORM ...
    ELSE
        IF FILING-STATUS = FS-JOINT
            PERFORM ...
        ELSE
            PERFORM FILING-STATUS-ERROR
        END-IF
    END-IF
ELSE
    IF TAX-STATUS = TS-SINGLE
        PERFORM ...
    ELSE
        IF TAX-STATUS = TS-HEAD-OF-HOUSEHOLD
            PERFORM ...
        ELSE
            PERFORM TAX-STATUS-ERROR
        END-IF
    END-IF
END-IF

The full evaluation of TAX-STATUS and FILING-STATUS occur
at different levels.

EVALUATE TAX-STATUS ALSO FILING-STATUS
    WHEN TS-MARRIED ALSO FS-SEPARATE
        PERFORM ...
    WHEN TS-MARRIED ALSO FS-JOINT
        PERFORM ...
    WHEN TS-SINGLE ALSO ANY
        PERFORM ...
    WHEN TS-HEAD-OF-HOUSEHOLD ALSO ANY
        PERFORM ....
    WHEN TS-MARRIED ALSO ANY
        PERFORM FILING-STATUS-ERROR
    WHEN OTHER
        PERFORM TAX-STATUS-ERROR
END-EVALUATE

In the EVALUATE, it is not entirely clear that evaluation of
FILING-STATUS is entirely dependent upon
TAX-STATUS = TS-MARRIED. One may interpret that any
FILING-STATUS is Ok for single or head of household rather than the
intended "FILING-STATUS is not to be evaluated."

The effects of ignoring or evaluating data become more important
with the introduction of FUNCTIONS. Evaluation using a FUNCTION
with invalid data could cause a program failure or the unnecessary
execution of an error routine, if the evaluation causes an exception.
Hence, with FUNCTIONS, "any value is Ok" is not the same as
"do not evaluate."
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of ELSE-IF.

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981204205810.08058.00000136@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<XXh92.646$Lx2.1626@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
There are a number of good reasons for ELIF (or ELSE-IF or ELSIF).

Indenting IF/ELSE clauses can get excessive in situation that an ELIF construct
would apply to. And the indentation is not only needless it can be confusing
when you have code that has matured over the years and has a number of
conditionals nested within one another.

With current IF/ELSE coding techniques, it is comparatively hard to insert an
IF in the middle.  In many shops that will require indenting all subsequent
'unchanged' code, placing those functions in needless jeopardy. ELIF construct
coding is sequential just like a sequence of imperative statements - insert or
remove without major side effects on the surrounding code (indentation).

When applied to an ELIF situation the IF/ELSE coding construct frequently
necessitates a long, and basically pointless sequence of END-IFs.  

I call this the END-IF dragon, because it looks like a saw-toothed tail, and is
a monster that eats vertical realestate, a monster that causes indentation
maintence costs, and removes meaningful information from the screen.

IF/ELSE are great, and EVALUATE is great.  The ELIF proposal relates to a
certain subset of what can be handled by EVALUATE, and handled better by ELIF.

EVALUATE has the slight disadvantage of indentation for the WHEN clauses, that
puts the body of each when stanza atleast one indentation further than it would
be within an ELIF construct expression.

Many EVALUATES are not really case structures at all (that is a compliment to
the folks who wrote the standards, they stretched its syntax elegantly).  But
when we code 
  EVALUATE TRUE
we have wasted one increment of the vertical dimension.  On a twenty-four line
screen (and there are plenty) with headers and pull down menu bars, there are
perhaps 20 or 21 lines of code (less if you are in a split screen or windowed
mode).  One-twentieth of the vertical realestate _IS_ worth fighting for,
because something meaningful could be on that screen and it is bumped off by
the EVALUATE TRUE, or as in a _small_ set of cases, 
   EVALUATE TRUE
       ALSO TRUE
       ALSO TRUE
      .....
for complex conditionals, wherein sometimes the WHEN clauses do similar skip
line patterning.

The basic point of an ELIF construct is that it relates to a situation in which
we will make a sequence of tests, and the tests are mutually exclusive.

Sometimes we have only a handful of simple test to do before an homogenous case
structure: for example in file I/O; did we OPEN successfully, did we get an
initial position from a START successfully, then AT END / error status / normal
read.

If we attempt to put the special process into the EVALUATE, we break it from
the simple case syntax; and must resort to placing all of the details in WHEN
clauses anyway. If we code the intial portion as IF/ELSE we end-up with an
EVALUATE sometimes severely indented within the last ELSE (making it very
difficult to indent any conditionals within the WHEN stanzas, ((especially for
adherents of the PERFORM THRU philosophy)) ). ;-).

EVALUATE is extemely good when the conditional involves a repeating subject
that we can represent efficiently in the EVALUATE phrase, and avoid repeating
in the WHEN clauses. But whenever we are motivated to go ahead and put the
entire conditional expression in the WHEN clauses, we are right then close to
an even trade off with the proposed ELIF construct.

The thing aout many mutually exclusive test sequences is that they are not the
same complexity at each point. 

And the presence of ALSO does not help because it requires extra code in the
WHEN clauses that don't need it and the ALSO ANY phrase.

(Don't take me wrong I am defintiely not against EVALUATE.  I even favor it
strongly to implement sparsely populated table logic, and find the ALSO ANY
mechanism self documenting and powerful. But it is rare that you can find an
opportunity to apply it to purely tabular data.)

The EVALUATE becomes misleading (in _some_ case) when ALSO or ALSO ANY phrases
are used to implement mutually exclusive test against conditionals that really
do not have similar topology.  Besides AND is just as good.

Overall ELSE-IF sequences are similar in nature to any CASE coding construct,
including COBOLs successful and powerful EVALUATE. Like EVALUTE ELSE-IF
sequences could have a 'default' case, coded as ELSE in ELIF constructions, and
OTHER in EVALUATE.  And like other case constructs it requires a scope
terminator, and END-IF could be a perfectly adequate scope terminator for ELIF
sequences.

One weakness of EVALUATE, and this is small but every bit helps when you are
talking about the definition of the language itself, is that it seems to say
this is a CASE OF construct involving a mere variation of a simple field test.

In a select subset of applications ELIF would clarify test sequences as
essentially mutual exclusion expressions and not CASE OF constructs. 

In maintenance that would _occassionaly_ pay off as a worker may realize they
need to examine the expression more carefully than they might if they saw an
EVALUATE expression. In other words, people sometimes get the wrong idea from
the leading portions of a EVALUATE sequence of WHENs. Although it is just as
erroneous to cut short a review of an IF/ELSE casecade, I am saying that the
pedagogical notion that EVALUATE is a CASE structure leads programmers latter
to pay less attention to the fact that it might not be a CASE situation at all.
I think ELIF can help clarify those cases.

Availability of an ELIF (or ELSE-IF or ELSIF) construct in COBOL would preserve
considerable vertical realestate.  It would preserve some horizontal
realestate. With it we could eliminate the irksome END-IF dragon, sometimes.
EVALUATE syntax is somewhat misleading in cases where the test are divers; ELIF
can be more emphatic of the eclectic nature of some test collections.

Best Wishses,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74c8ga$949$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<XXh92.646$Lx2.1626@news1.atlantic.net> <19981204205810.08058.00000136@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981204205810.08058.00000136@ng-cr1.aol.com>...
>There are a number of good reasons for ELIF (or ELSE-IF or ELSIF).
>
IF A = B
    PERFORM A-EQUAL-B-RTN
OR-IF A = C
    PERFORM A-EQUAL-C-RTN
OR-IF A = D
    PERFORM A-EQUAL-D-RTN
ELSE
    PERFORM A-ERROR-RTN
END-IF

A missing hyphen between OR and IF should be "catchable" by a syntax
checker and the use of OR denotes exclusivity.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208070323.19089.00001289@ng-fq1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74c8ga$949$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
The OR-IF suggestion has been commented on by no more than three persons, if
the dejanews search engine has the complete picture.

In reverse chronological order:

1 - Re:ï¿½Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)ï¿½
Author:ï¿½Rick Smith
ricksmith@aiservices.com
Date:1998/12/05

2 - Re: ELIF Proposalï¿½more options ï¿½
Randall Bart
Barticus@att.spam.net
Date:1998/12/04

"How about OR-IF..."


3 - RE: ELIF Proposalï¿½more options ï¿½
Michael C. Kasten
mck9@swbell.net
Date:1998/11/30

"Other possibilities, with or without hyphens:

	OR-IF
	OTHERWISE-IF
	ON-THE-OTHER-HAND-IF
"

If this information is complete Michael C. Kasten originated it: 
Date:1998/11/30. Looking beyond dejanews, does anyone know of the exaact
spelling OR-IF in any other language or tool?

Also, I want to offer apologies to anyone who has discussed this with just the
variation "OR IF", (not hyphen) as that is real difficult to isolate with a
search engine. 

I harewith post a long comment on this. You could decide if there is 
anything of interest to you by considering this organization:

1) OR-IF is a Nice proposal 
   
2) AND-IF cousin by implication

3) UNIX C Shell analogue and nomenclature
   "Conditional Execution on Success - && (And)
   "Conditional Execution on Failure - || (Or}"

4) difference is the COBOL Stork

5) more on mathematical people and linguistic people

6) precedence and operator interaction

--------------

1) OR-IF is a Nice proposal, I think it has a lot strength.  I notice that
Michael C. Kasten is frequently a creator of ideas around here. I look forward
to comments from all as to other earlier proposals relating to OR-IF or 'OR
IF'. And I am grateful to Michael for introducing it in this thead.

The OR-IF construct would _be_ the ELSE-IF or ELIF or ELSIF under recent
discussion. (We would not code ELSE OR-IF).

The idea is that OR-IF would replace ELSE IF in compound IF statements that
are case structures, or mutually exclusive test sequences. For example

IF    file-not-OPEN-successfully
   PERFORM e100-diagnose-miss-file
OR-IF file-stat-from-START-bad
   PERFORM e200-first-rec-not-found
OR-IF file-stat-oaky
   PERFORM p1000-process-it
OR-IF file-stat-not-found
   PERFORM s1000-summation-logic
ELSE
   PERFORM z999-this-aint-kansas
END-IF.


2) AND-IF cousin by implication
   
We might also consider an AND-IF cousin.  The same author, Michael C. Kasten  ,

deserves credit for that too, since mere symmetry implies it.


3) UNIX C Shell analogue and nomenclature

The suggestion of an OR-IF encoding for the ELSE IF construct immediately
brings to mind the conditional operators of UNIX command shell syntax.
Permit me to briefly quote an excellent book on this subject:

"UNIX Unleashed", SAMS publishing
1994 First Edition
ISBN 0-672-30402-3

The book has numerous authors, the part I quote is from 
"Chapter 13, C Shell", written by Rick Rummel and John Valley. 

In a section titled "Executing Commands Conditionally", subtitled
"Conditional Execution on Failure - || (Or)" the authors state

"The or operator is used to join two commands:
  command1 || command2
It causes the shell to execute command2 only if command1 failed ..."

Their first example is

  grep '#include' *.c || echo No files | pr

and they offer further comments ...

"..either grep succeeds, or else the words  No files   are piped to
the pr command ..."

"..use the || operator to provide an alternative action. .."

Another example is given that we might call a make or break encoding,
(meaning it makes or breaks a script, it is an all or nothing requirement)

   mkdir $tmpfile || exit

This or operator (||) works like a case construct, somewhat analogous 
to COBOL EVALUATE and the ELSE IF cascades discussed under the topic the 
ELIF proposal.  (Although there is no OTHER or final ELSE syntax for this
particular construct in UNIX C Shell, UNIX it is rich in other related
constucts).


But note also
In a section titled "Executing Commands Conditionally", subtitled
"Conditional Execution on Success - && (And)" the authors state


"The double ampersand operator (read and) is used to join two commands:
  command1 && command2.
It causes the shell to execute command2 only if command1 is sucessful ..."

The first example 

 grep '#include' *.c | pr && echo OK

My description: you get the Okay if everything works out; and no news is
bad news.

Another example
  cp file1.c file1.bak && rm file.c
with the comment from the authors
"...The rm command will delete file1.c only if it is first successfuly 
copied to file1.bak."

I hope I have done justice to the authors. With so many contributors to 
this fine book it is hard to get complete attribution into a usenet posting.

The UNIX C Shell operators described are an excellent model for discussion
of OR-IF and AND-IF, and the SAMS Publishing book, "UNIX Unleasehed", is 
a very useful book.


4) difference is the COBOL Stork

In these C Shell expressions the command or program execution is the essence of
the
'conditional'. It is not an IF conditional that will permit or disallow 
some other command per se. It _is_ the command, and the operator (&& or ||)
just makes the next command in the cascade dependent upon the outcome of 
preceeding commands.

A COBOL OR-IF would have, not only a conditional, but a bundle of subjuncted
commands, much like a stork. (this would be the 'THEN' clause of the initial 
IF and each succeeding OR-IF, even though we nolonger code THEN).
A case statement expressed as an OR-IF cascade would 
then be a flock of storks. When a stork arrives it may or may not deliver
its bundle. The OR-IF and AND-IF connectives would determine the cascade of 
tests (detemine the test sequence continuation). But that would be distinct
from the delivery!  

Any true test delivers (the baby is dropped), the conjuctions determine how 
the cascade will progress. Deliveries or not. I think it is really useful 
to be able to think of conditionals on two different levels like that.  It 
is actually done in COBOL already, all the time by means of ELSE IF cascades
and EVALUATE statements. But the linguistic particles ELSE IF and WHEN do not
bring out the subjunctivity of the sequence. And there is nothing like 
AND-IF in COBOL just yet.

AND-IFs would have zero, one or multiple deliveries. OR-IFs would have zero 
or one delivery. 

OR-IF would be the classic CASE or ELIF sequence.  Its ELSE clause (call it 
the default: or the OTHER clause), is fairly easy to conjure up.

The ELSE clause of an AND-IF sequence is something more engaging isn't it.


5) more on mathematical people and linguistic people

Use of a construct like the And / Or operators in UNIX C Shell might appeal
more to those I would describe as mathematical individuals. There is a 
chance that some other folks would shun  AND-IF / OR-IF constructs should 
they be added to COBOL,  because, as I would describe it, they are 
perhaps more linguistic. 

I believe that the COBOL world is somewhat segregated, having relatively
few mathematical people. I think that the COBOL world would be better off 
if we had more mathematical people.

And with reference to the OR-IF construct. I think that the different 
expression modality that it could bring forth, would make all users of the
language occassionaly think more mathematically and less linguistically,
which could be beneficial.


6) precedence and operator interaction

In C Shell || has lower precedence than both && and |.
Lower precedence means happens later.  So in a long expression,
two commands on either side of a pipe (|) glue together right quickly,
and things on either side of And (&&) glue together before Or (||).

This comment would get very long if I try to take on examples of combining
OR-IFs and AND-IFs. 
But the issue of permitting combinations and the precedence deserves
discussion. If you consider it, please keep in mind that simple AND and OR 
operators are much hotter and resolve inside individual conditional 
expressions (not stretching across IFs and not across the OR-IFs or AND-IFs
under consideration).

An important alternative is to just disallow combinations of OR-IF and 
AND-IF within a single conditional statement.  Combining this stuff in OS 
commands is remote to say the least, and it is less than clear what a 
coder's intent might be in a procedural language context.

It is certainly sure that the ELSE clause gets weird if we were to
allow combinations of OR-IF and AND-IF.

*) Concluding remarks, 

But abstraction or practicallity, OR-IF is just as compact as ELSIF and just
as distinct as ELIF,  and it does not have the potential for coding fault
that ELSE-IF has ("ELSE IF" visually close to "ELSE-IF)'

OR_IF has the elegant aspect that it could be used by linguistic 
coders as a brute force case constuct just like current ELSE IF 
expression, and not bring the END-IF dragon to life, and not 
engage excessive indentation. The surface of such source code would
be flat, mirroring the topology of the semantics.

An AND-IF constuct would really be a very different kind of thing than was 
originally under discussion for an ELIF construct. Because the stork would
keep dropping _additional_ bundles upon successful links in an AND-IF 
cascade, such a construct is really a progressive conditional, where as 
OR-IF is a mutually exclusive conditional; with reference to the the
delivery of imperative functionality bundles.  

Although both have sequence as the the essence of the subjunctive 
semantics. And that is germane to the original discussion, wherein 
I maintain that case expressing ELSE IF sequences should not 
be indented at all, since there is no more hierarchical depth to 
them than to a simple sequence of imperative statements.
The indentation silliness and the pesky END-IF dragon are much more 
assailable when considered in the light of the flat topology 
conditionals of the UNIX C Shell.

I think the Storks of COBOL OR-IFs and AND-IFs could bring forth some 
badly needed renewal.


Best Wishes,
Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366E09A0.2FAC@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<74c8ga$949$1@server.cntfl.com> <19981208070323.19089.00001289@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
> The OR-IF suggestion has been commented on by no more than three persons, if
> the dejanews search engine has the complete picture.
> 
> In reverse chronological order:

[snip -- citations]

> If this information is complete Michael C. Kasten originated it:
> Date:1998/11/30. Looking beyond dejanews, does anyone know of the exaact
> spelling OR-IF in any other language or tool?

I believe you started out proposing "ELIF", citing some language as
precedent.  PL/SQL uses "ELSIF".  I am not aware of any languages using 
"OR-IF" or "ELSE-IF".

> 1) OR-IF is a Nice proposal, I think it has a lot strength.  I notice that
> Michael C. Kasten is frequently a creator of ideas around here.

[snip; mentions AND-IF]

> The same author, Michael C. Kasten, 
> deserves credit for that too, since mere symmetry implies it.

Thank you for the kind words.  I'm willing to take credit for OR-IF, at
least until someone advances a prior claim, but I'm not so sure about 
AND-IF.

I don't even know what it's supposed to mean.  I suppose (and Thane 
has posted a similar interpretation) that:

 IF A = B
    PERFORM FOOBAR
 AND-IF C = D
    PERFORM QUUX
 END-IF

...would be equivalent to:

 IF A = B
    PERFORM FOOBAR
    IF C = D
       PERFORM QUUX
    END-IF
 END-IF

The advantages apparently are that we can eliminate an END-IF and
plausibly reduce the degree of indentation.

These advantages would be more significant if we often had reason to 
code long chains of AND-IFs, but I don't often code anything that 
could use such a treatment.  Usually, if I have several levels of 
nested IFs (other than a case structure), they are ugly and irregular, 
wriggling snakelike down the screen.

The disadvantage (speaking only for myself) is that the AND-IF is 
vastly more obscure.  Maybe I could get used to it, but I rather think 
I would have to stare at it each time, mentally translating it to the 
older syntax, before I could proceed further.

Worse: with the kind of indentation shown, it looks confusingly
similar to a case structure, whether with EVALUATE, ELSE IF, or OR-IF
(by whatever spelling).  If I don't look closely I could easily confuse
one with the other.

> 3) UNIX C Shell analogue and nomenclature

[snip -- discussion of || and && operators (i.e. OR and AND) in
Unix shell programming]

The suggestion, apparently, is to adopt something like the Unix
syntax whereby:

    do_this || do_that

...exploits short-circuit evaluation to mean:

    do_this; if this doesn't work, do_that

Likewise, the following:

    do_this && do_that

...means:

    do this; if this works, do_that

I think I got that right.  In the course of about ten minutes, I 
reversed myself twice.  Partly I was confused by the fact that in the
Unix shell, zero represents true, and non-zero represents false -- 
exactly the opposite of what you might expect.

In Unix shell programming, "if" statements are clumsy and awkward (at 
least for the Bourne and Korn shells -- I'm not familiar with the C 
shell).  Perhaps that's why this construct caught on: It's only slightly
worse than the alternative.

The same trick is available in C, which uses the same operators for the
same purposes (except that zero is false and non-zero is true) and
offers short-circuit evaluation.  However, people seldom encrypt their 
"if" statements this way, even though many of the same people also 
write shell scripts.

In both contexts, this syntax relies on the fact that each operand has
two roles.  First: it is a command, invoking a procedure which may have 
side effects.  Second: it is a expression, which may be evaluated to 
yield a value.  If neither of the operands has side effects, evaluating
either or both of them would be pointless.  If they didn't both have a 
value which could be interpreted as true or false, then there would be 
nothing for the operator to operate on.

This duality is not very common in COBOL.  There's no obvious way to 
regard a PERFORM or a CALL as true or false, but perhaps you could use 
a FUNCTION.  If a built-in function didn't do what you needed, and if 
your dialect allowed it, you could write your own.  

Another possibility would be to CALL a subprogram which returns a value.
Instead of coding an explicit GIVING or RETURNING clause, you could
implicitly test the returned value, interpreted as a boolean.

Still another possibility would be to test the values left by the two
procedures in the RETURN-CODE special register.

So far I haven't come up with any improvement over an ordinary IF.
Perhaps you could explain more precisely what you have in mind.

[snip]

> 6) precedence and operator interaction
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and things on either side of And (&&) glue together before Or (||).

Do you really mean "happens later"?  I don't know about the C shell,
but in other contexts I am familiar with, "precedence" governs the 
grouping of operands (as might otherwise be expressed by parentheses)
but not the order of evaluation.

[snip]

> It is certainly sure that the ELSE clause gets weird if we were to
> allow combinations of OR-IF and AND-IF.

Things are weird enough when you allow combinations of OR and AND.  If
it were up to me, the compiler wouldn't let you combine them without
disambiguating parentheses.  I know the compiler knows which one has
precedence, but I don't, unless I look it up.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981209012839.05933.00001771@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<366E09A0.2FAC@swbell.net>`

```

In article <366E09A0.2FAC@swbell.net>, "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
writes:

> am not aware of any languages using "OR-IF" or "ELSE-IF".

Easytrieve-Plus uses the ELSE-IF and a number of our batch applications use it.

The ELSE-IF can be used anyplace where ELSE IF can be used, except that it does
_not_ increase the IF nesting, so only one END-IF terminates the
IF/ELSE-IFs/ELSE statement(s).

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981209042207.12061.00004302@ng133.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<366E09A0.2FAC@swbell.net>`

```
"Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
Date: Tue, 08 Dec 1998 23:24:48 -0600

Was generous enough to comment further on discussions of OR-IF and AND-IF.
After repeating a bit of history of posts, he stated ...
<<
Thank you for the kind words.  I'm willing to take credit for OR-IF, at
least until someone advances a prior claim, but I'm not so sure about 
AND-IF.
>>

Permit me to say that I surely did not mean to state the matter wrongly.  I
just feel that he deserves credit, since actually, it is easy to think of
AND-IF once someone has suggested OR-IF.
I was to go on a bit in my post about both constructs, and wanted to make
certain that I state clearly that my original post, suggesting ELIF, did not at
all possess the intriguing qualities that OR-IF (and the similar AND-IF)
possesses as a language building block.

Somethings happen spontaneously.  I think something remarkable has happened
here. Truely, as I work through the language possibilities of an OR-IF, I see
new things. And I really wanted to state clearly that it is the contribution of
Michael C. Kasten that sourced it
(and not my meager bland original ELIF).


Upon review, I think my writing technique may have implied agreement from Mr
Kasten about the AND-IF elaboration, which was not fair, and it was not
intended that way.

Commenting about pros/cons on AND-IF
Michael C. Kasten stated
<<
These advantages would be more significant if we often had reason to 
code long chains of AND-IFs, but I don't often code anything that 
could use such a treatment.  
>>

As I have stated elsewhere, I agree with that. The AND-IF would have much less
frequent application than the OR-IF. But the more I have worked it out, the
more I see the ELSE clause of an AND-IF as being useful. You cannot code that
in classical syntax without a switch, and extra code.

Contrasting AND-IFs to traditional nested IFs Mr. Kasten expressed a view that
I believe we could all agree with, " AND-IF is vastly more obscure".

But OCCURS DEPENDING ON is obscure, and two ODOs in one layout is obscure. And
the revision in the standard, 74 to 85, concerning ODO is obscure enough to be
causing real world expenses as Y2K concerns many shops to catch up.

I am not sure that obscurity alone decides the matter of the appropriateness of
the idea as a language element.  OR-IF is the real prize, AND-IF is like the
less attractive puppy in the litter. The ELSE clause of an AND-IF expression
definitely offers coding efficiency opportunities (it is a sleeper, but it is
ferocious when you wake it up).

In traditional nested IF statements, there is no comprehensive ELSE statement. 
Each ELSE reduces back only to the nearest IF. So an and-if situation is one in
which you try to get through the whole sequence of tests, adding more
functional bundles upon each passed hurdle.  
 
The final IF is the place to detect completion of the whole sequence, but to
catch a short circuit you need (in calssical syntax) to plug into all the else
clauses, which is redundant.  In an AND-IF encoding, the ELSE clause would
catch any short circuit (it would supply nand-else logic hooks).

There was also discussion of the C Shell syntax. I am not suggesting that we do
the functional language thing here at all. In COBOL the functions would be in
the 'then' clauses and ELSE clauses.  The conditionals would not be anything
besides tests, they would not have side effects.  

The point of my mentioning the UNIX shell syntax was to indicate that useful
development and fields experience already has occured in an area somewhat
related to this discussion.

Also I wanted to record that some of the terminology and nomenclature;
particularly
  Conditional Execution on Success - && (And)
  Conditional Execution on Failure - || (Or)
are not my original. And to lead interested parties to text that discourse
about this.

But in no way am I suggesting that we make conditionals 'functional', just to
get the OR-IF and its less lovable bretheren AND-IF out for a walk. My proposal
is that OR-IF and AND-IF would read very much like current IF/(then)/ELSE
syntax, just much tighter, conserving realestate in the human-machine
interface.










Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
> it were up to me, the compiler wouldn't let you combine them without

Frankly, this whole discussion has seemed rather ethereal to me.  I
think I understand what everyone has been talking about, but it doesn't
seem that any of these proposals fill a great void in the existing
language.  Like the AND/OR combinations mentioned above, the subtleties
of an otherwise "elegant" statement can haunt you.  The last thing I
need when debugging a problem is that queasy feeling of "I *think* it
should execute THIS way, but ..."  

If faced with a problem that required extensive nested IF statements,
I'd probably break it apart into smaller bits anyway.  Why fold all the
complexity into one statement that may look prettier but may cause just
as much confusion as the older style?
```

```
standard is that people tend to forget the basics in
lieu of it.  Stuff is coded so that "it looks pretty" instead
of "it is obvious what it does".  No fixed set of rules
can ever replace reading the code back, and revising
it until it reads well.  Conventions can make life easier,
but they should never replace read/revise.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366e968c.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74c8ga$949$1@server.cntfl.com> <19981208070323.19089.00001289@ng-fq1.aol.com> <366E09A0.2FAC@swbell.net> <8E52610713DECD70.AF5578C68ABC31F6.03E96637CA10DA85@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
David W. Furin wrote in message
>Michael C. Kasten wrote:
>> Things are weird enough when you allow combinations of OR and AND.  If
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Having seen the many proposals (and participated in the debate myself)
I have come to agree with 'the simplicity' group. The old way is perfectly
workable:

IF cond1
      do1
ELSE
IF cond2
      do2
ELSE
IF cond3
      do3
ELSE
      do4
.

If any of the 'do's have further conditions, make them performs.
The net result is easy to understand, easy to maintain, standard
compliant code.

'Problems' with this simple construction are:
1) some (older) compilers have limitations on the number of
dependant IF clauses. You can overcome that mechanically
by breaking the chain at the offending point and performing
a paragraph where the remainder (possibly repeating this,
if needed).
2) screen real estate is eaten up by placing the ELSE
on a line by themselves, but the alternative ELSE IF indents
the conditions unnecessarily.
```

```
On Tue, 8 Dec 1998 12:03:23, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

> I think the Storks of COBOL OR-IFs and AND-IFs could bring forth some 
> badly needed renewal.
> 

Tons of good stuff snipped....

AND-IF is already there - 

IF First-Thing
    Do something
     IF Second-Thing

is the same as

IF First-Thing
    Do Something
    AND-IF Second-Thing
    do something....

However, given the OR-IF (ELIF), I think AND-IF is necessary to 
differentiate - although IF would still be accepted I think.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208213036.07840.00003827@ng20.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Gdvnim94uEom@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
Date: 8 Dec 98 17:13:32 GMT


Posted on the subject of the AND-IFs and OR-IFs, ....
<<
AND-IF is already there - 

IF First-Thing
    Do something
     IF Second-Thing

is the same as

IF First-Thing
    Do Something
    AND-IF Second-Thing
    do something....

However, given the OR-IF (ELIF), I think AND-IF is necessary to 
differentiate - although IF would still be accepted I think.  

Neat stuff.  
>>

You are right and you are wrong. Current day nested IF statements are
progressive conditionals, wherein the flock of storks deliver more and more
individual functional bundles upon successful completion of a sequence of
tests.  

However, with existing available code constructs, the nested IFs are followed
by a sequence of distinct ELSE clauses.  An AND-IF would be slightly different
in that regard.  

Just as an OR-IF sequence of conditionals could be terminated by at most one
ELSE clause, the AND-IF sequence of conditionals could be terminated by a most
one ELSE clause (as an aside, and only academically speaking, these would be
nor-else, and nand-else clauses: lower case non-construct teaching names).
(('nor-else' has a ring to it, doesn't it?))

The ELSE clause of a normal nested IF reduces back to the most recent IF, and
relates to none higher.

In an AND-IF coding construct, the ELSE clause is a global exit upon failure.
The nand-else gets executed if any one test fails. This is part of the feature
that the conjunction (the AND-, and dash; the OR-, or dash) is occuring at a
structural level above the low level AND/OR of simplistic conditionals. The
subjuncts are conjoined, in a manner comparable to but distinct from the
conjunction of portions of the specific conditionals.

For example,

IF this-is-a-building
   PERFORM a100-needs-foundation
AND-IF this-is-a-residence
   PERFORM a200-needs-bedrooms
AND-IF city-inspector-approved
   PERFORM a300-schedule-full-crews
ELSE
   PERFORM z999-warn-incomplete-build
END-IF. 

As we build records, our construction process may only get partially completed.
 At which point we could prevent it from being written or place it on a
suspense file.

The ELSE of an AND-IF is a 'process on _any_ exception' where we want to make
as much _progress_ as possible (for optional partial or full commit), but need
to know in the end whether we failed to complete the whole process.

((For completeness, let me state that the ELSE of a classical nested IF, only
posts exceptions to the final condition)).

Let me also point out that in the relatively rare case where nested IFs with
progressive conditional intent do not have discrete ELSE functions, then (and
it is a small subset) there is a needless END-IF dragon, cousin to the END-IF 
dragon that case structure IF/ELSE encodings produce.  

AND-IFs would reduce the waste of vertical realestate in these few cases, and
OR-IF would reduce much more waste. (Because of frequency of occurence).

Every vertical line counts, and the AND-IF construct, will also reduce needless
indentation; in comparison to classical nested IF encodings.
This is especially important for practitioners of PERFORM THRU styles, as that
excess usually requires yet another vertical line for the THRU clause in heavy
indentation situations (and then, of course, folks get into pretty symmetry
excursions and do double lines on the earlier less indented conditionals, just
so all the THRUs will 
'look right).  (Tee-hee!).  So there is much realestate to be gained.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 8)_

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981209004612.17038.00002287@ng135.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<366dfc89.610833215@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Perhaps our nor-else and nand-else teaching models would be something like
this.

1) OR-IF and the nor-else:

In an OR-IF sequence the ELSE clause gets executed only if _all_ tests fail.
The OR-IF construct is intended for mutually exclusive test sequences.  Any
successful test (mutually) excludes the ELSE as well.

Classical syntax -
  IF condition-a
    PERFORM a100-aaa
  ELSE
    IF condition-b
      PERFORM b100-bbb
    ELSE
      IF condition-c
         PERFORM c100-ccc
      ELSE
         PERFORM n999-nor-else-routine
      END-IF
    END-IF
  END-IF.

Revisionist syntax -
  IF condition-a
    PERFORM a100-aaa
  OR-IF condition-b
      PERFORM b100-bbb
  OR-IF condition-c
      PERFORM c100-ccc
  ELSE
      PERFORM n999-nor-else-routine
  END-IF.

OR-IF is like the EVALUATE statement in that it implements a case structure.
OR-IF may be more appropriate in situations where the complexity of the tests
at each stage in the sequence varies, EVALUATE may be more appropriate where
the testing is relatively more homogeneous. EVALUATE is definitely more
appropriate in its ideal application where a subject can be announced in the
EVALUATE header, and then the WHEN clauses are much less verbose than IF
clauses of any kind.

Use of OR-IF offers a much more efficient use of the human-machine interface
than do conventional nested IF statements, entailing less indentation, no line
break between IF and ELSE, and no END-IF dragon.


2) AND-IF and the nand-else:

In an AND-IF sequence the ELSE clause gets executed if _any_ test fails. The
AND-IF construct is intended for progressive test sequences. The coder indends
to achieve an accumulation affect from tests that succeed, and desires
notification if the sequence did not complete. 

The final AND-IF is the location to detect all test successful, the ELSE is the
place to detect any departure at all from the sequence of tests.
The ELSE indicates that you did not progress all 
the way to completion.

Classical syntax -
 IF this-is-a-building
   PERFORM a100-needs-foundation
   IF this-is-a-residence
     PERFORM a200-needs-bedrooms 
     IF city-inspector-approved
       PERFORM a300-schedule-full-crews
     ELSE
*   **not comparable to nand-else    
*   **throw a switch
     END-IF
   ELSE
***not comparable to nand-else    
***throw a switch
   END-IF
 ELSE
*   not comparable to nand-else   
*   **throw a switch
 END-IF. 

 IF switch-thrown
   PERFORM z999-warn-incomplete-build
 END-IF. 


Revisionist syntax -

IF this-is-a-building
   PERFORM a100-needs-foundation
AND-IF this-is-a-residence
   PERFORM a200-needs-bedrooms
AND-IF city-inspector-approved
   PERFORM a300-schedule-full-crews
ELSE
   PERFORM z999-warn-incomplete-build
END-IF. 

The AND-IF construct is not comparable to the EVALUATE.  The AND-IF conserves
human-machine interface resources, just like OR-IF.

3) application:

OR-IF and AND-IF apply to sequences of tests wherein there is no individual
ELSE clause for each segment in the sequence (Just like EVALUATE).  If you need
an else that is not generic (that is it is not a nor-else in the OR-IF
sequence, and it is not a nand-else in the AND-IF sequence), then the topology
of such commplex requirement does not match the flat surface capability of the
more simplistic OR-IF and AND-IF constructs.

OR-IF and AND-IF are intended for simple situations and provide a means of
making the code look as simple as the requirments: avoiding misleading
hierarchy implying indentation.


4) combinations:

OR-IFs and AND-IFs would be nestable in any manner with other conditional
expressions.


5) subjunction and conjunction:

An IF clause, an AND-IF, an OR-IF and a WHEN clause are subjunctive expressions
indicating the conditions underwhich code is to be executed.  The portions of
the conditional expressions can be conjoined with the logical connectors AND
and OR.

Subjunctive expressions can themselves be joined by means of AND-IF and OR-IF
expressions, as well as EVALUATE/WHEN and classical nesting of IF/(THEN)/ELSE
expressions.


Best Wishes,




Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74l6d6$fa8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366dfc89.610833215@news1.ibm.net> <19981209004612.17038.00002287@ng135.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981209004612.17038.00002287@ng135.aol.com>...

For the life of me I cannot see how this clarifies anything at all.
You change ELSE to OR, and put a dash in. The construct
already exists, and has since Cobol was invented.  Why
give a second wording for the exact same function?

>Revisionist syntax -
>  IF condition-a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF.


is the same as

  IF condition-a
    PERFORM a100-aaa
  else IF condition-b
      PERFORM b100-bbb
  else IF condition-c
      PERFORM c100-ccc
  ELSE
      PERFORM n999-nor-else-routine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981209032252.12061.00004293@ng133.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74l6d6$fa8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Dec 1998 01:59:42 -0500 
Is asking why...

<<
For the life of me I cannot see how this clarifies anything at all.
You change ELSE to OR, and put a dash in. The construct
already exists, and has since Cobol was invented.  Why
give a second wording for the exact same function?
>>

(he quotes an earlier post)
<<
>Revisionist syntax -
>  IF condition-a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF.


is the same as

  IF condition-a
    PERFORM a100-aaa
  else IF condition-b
      PERFORM b100-bbb
  else IF condition-c
      PERFORM c100-ccc
  ELSE
      PERFORM n999-nor-else-routine.
>>

Well by the definition offered, I would try to make it the same functionally,
but the syntax is way different.  In ordinary open code where an IF statement
like this occurs, many people would code the END-IF dragon. Quite a few people
would recursively indent, wasting space and creating a visual image that
falsely suggests structure.

When a test similar to this is nested within yet other IF statements, the
END-IF dragon is not optional in classical syntax.  (And in that situation, any
indentation is exasperated).

None of us knows the whole world of COBOL, and I would make the following
statement with much careful qualification; but the truth is many programs I
have seen do not place the ELSE and IF on the same line as you have displayed,
although your style is excellent.

OR-IF (and ELSIF and ELSE-IF) prevent that line break.

The needless consumption of vertical realestate is the exact point of departure
for this thread, with an emphasis on slaying the END-IF dragon, and the ELSE/IF
line break.

The AND-IF construct is a companion idea to OR-IF.  OR-IF would likely have
much more application. I think that the ELSE of OR-IF is more readily
understood.

So anyway, the semantics are comparable, and are intended to provide an
alternative.  The syntax has very different surface characteristics. 
And actually the major struggle here is for more efficient use of the media
surface in the human-machine interface. This is the major struggle because with
relative ease we could win back a lot of wasted interface realestate. But a
minor struggle, that I pursue in parallel, is for a greater cultural balance in
business data processing. The latter minor only in the sense that it will
require much more herculean effort to actually impact it, and one should
atleast be level headed when assessing the prospects and allocating ones
efforts.

Should a construct such as the ELIF be permitted in the language, I think that
many traditionalists would be less disquieted by something literal like
ELSE-IF, and I believe that the exact reason is that we have a serious degree
of isolation in the COBOL world; heavily favoring people who are more
linguistic than mathematical, as I call it.

Don't take offense. Honestly, I think I am more linguistic than mathematical. 
So truely I am trying to be as direct as possible. The business data processing
environment suffers from a lack of mathematical people. We are experiencing a
major brain drain. We are witnessing a serious decline of commitment from
educational institutions for the basic tools of our trade.

OR-IF is less linguistic in that it does not allow us to explain it as a
contraction of the verbose ELSE IF  expression.  OR-IF and AND-IF as
suggestions, compel us to disguss the nature of the construct. We are
conjoining subjuncts: we are connecting conditionals with logical connectors,
not just incidental sequence.  OR-IFs and AND-IFs explicate the relation of the
IFs.  That techical expressivity would allow some practitioners to think in
very unique ways with this language. And inclusion of the constructs is
justified on interface efficiency grounds.

As I have opined elsewhere, quite a number of conditional requirements will not
fit into OR-IFs any more than into EVALUATEs.  The traditional IF/ELSE would
definitely be retained, and is not diminished at all.

But classical IF/ELSE expressions are very clumsy for conditional sequences
that are simply mutual exclusion or progressive tests.

So if you can't see the minor theme, or even hate the idea of getting some
fresh blood in here, then don't worry. The spelling OR-IF just means ELSE-IF
(and meets a few concerns about easy errors with ELSE IF versus ELSE-IF).  The
essential gain is the improvement of management of the veritcal attribute of
the graphic interface.  

PERFORM THE-LINE-COUNT-DECREASES.

PERFORM MORE-MEANINGFUL-STUFF-ON-SCREEN.

PERFORM CODE-VISUALLY-MORE-CLEAR.

PERFORM FASTER-PERCEPTION.

PERFORM MAKE-THE-HUMAN-EFFICIENT.

The thing I like about the OR-IF spelling proposal is that it is elegant. In
pursuit of easing indentation and END-IF concerns, a spelling problem was
encountered (in ELSE-IF). But the suggested spelling brings forth a brand new
set of possibilities.  The particle OR-IF is un-linguistic, it is mathematical
because it is explicative of the algorithm. It would actually indicate the
relationship of the subjunctive expression in a sequence.

I can imagine that it would take time for folks to warm up to this, and
strongly welcome the candor of an expression such as Donald Tees',
"For the life of me I cannot see how this clarifies anything at all." But the
tag 'OR-IF' identified in postings by Michael C. Kasten and Randall Bart, is a
real bullseye. I believe it opens a major doorway.

Best Wishes, and keep the candor meter pegged!

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74lol8$o6m$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<74l6d6$fa8$1@news.igs.net> <19981209032252.12061.00004293@ng133.aol.com>`

```
Perhaps the reason that I cannot see the use of it
is that I would never blindly indent the way that you
suggest.  Indentation, to me, means successive
ifs (the and-if, if you will) rather than the else-if.

It strikes me as rather silly to first indent, then because
you do not like the indent, to introduce a new construct to
overcome the indent that you do not like. Instead. why
not just keep it simple, and do not indent where you do
not like indentation.

The same is true of the end-if.  If a stylistic construct
makes the meaning more clear, then it should be used.
However, when someone says that that are locked into
a stylistic pattern that they find makes things unclear,
and so need an even more complex vocabulary to
overcome the unclearness that they have forced themselves
into by their particular style, perhaps the style should be
examined.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981209234300.20543.00003961@ng23.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74lol8$o6m$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Frequently styles are not of ones choosing but are discovered to be in place
when one arrives on the scene and enforced.  Indentation, senseless as it can
be in case constructs _is_ enforced in some shops.  OR-IF or ELIF would
definitely discourage the evolution of this.

In classical syntax for OR-IF and AND-IF encoding situations, the entire
expression is often times nested within other conditionals, necessitating the
END-IF dragon.  Truthfully I think it is the bizarre appearance of an erect
END-IF dragon that leads to indentation.

Indentation standards often come from standards committees and project managers
who are not technical at all. OR-IF and END-IF would constrain them, as non
recursive indentation components from the start. 

The problem indentations are there.  They do not come from me. The problem is
real. Many junior programmers think that this is the right way to go because
they see so much of it.

Also, the semantics of the nand-else of the proposed AND-IF coding construct is
not currently available, no matter how you indent.

And with classical syntax the END-IF sequence is not a stylistic issue in many
cases, it is a requisite, which would disappear with the proposed syntax.

Thanks for your direct comments.

Best Wishes,


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is OR-IF a better candidtate? (was: Re: Use of ELSE-IF.)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74m19l$na$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Gdvnim94uEom@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <19981208213036.07840.00003827@ng20.aol.com> <366dfc89.610833215@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <366dfc89.610833215@news1.ibm.net>...
>On 9 Dec 1998 02:30:36 GMT, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Robert Rayhawk gave an example with a switch and nesting.
Without the addition of a switch or nesting, the code becomes:

incomplete-build-check.
    IF NOT this-is-a-building
        go to ibc-warn
    end-if
    PERFORM a100-needs-foundation
    IF NOT this-is-a-residence
        go to ibc-warn
    end-if
    PERFORM a200-needs-bedrooms
    IF NOT city-inspector-approved
        go to ibc-warn
    end-if
    PERFORM a300-schedule-full-crews
    go to ibc-exit
    .
ibc-warn.
    PERFORM z999-warn-incomplete-build
    .
ibc-exit.

In other words, the AND-IF construction is one more means for
eliminating GO TO without adding switches or nesting. It also
has the advantage of using less vertical screen space.

An alternative with a switch and no nesting is:

a500-incomplete-build-check.
    set no-incomplete-warning to true
    perform a501-ibc-check-parts thru a501-exit
    if give-incomplete-warning
        perform z999-warn-incomplete-build thru z999-exit
    end-if.
a500-exit
    exit.

a501-ibc-check-parts.
    if not this-is-a-building
        set give-incomplete-warning to true
        go to a501-exit
    end-if
    perform a100-needs-foundation thru a100-exit
    if not this-is-a-residence
        set give-incomplete-warning to true
        go to a501-exit
    end-if
    perform a200-needs-bedrooms thru a200-exit
    if not city-inspector-approved
        set give-incomplete-warning to true
        go to a501-exit
    end-if
    perform a300-schedule-full-crews thru a300-exit.
a501-exit.
    exit.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
When I code up an IF and it starts to get long I shift to an EVALUATE to
```
