<a id="t-2fa8326c0c"></a>
## Thread: OS390 Called Programs Errors — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS390 Called Programs Errors

- **From:** DOUG-PARTCH@WORLDNET.ATT.NET
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<744edl$f71$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Here's the problem:

Program "A" calls Programs "B" and "C".  No errors yet. The errors were
getting occur when Program "B" also calls "C". Program "A,B" use the same
data difintion that "C" uses in the Linkage section. "B" sends data to "C", 
"C" then runs a DB2 Select Query to retrive a Data row that is in the linkage
area (We know that this is working).  When "C" finsihes executing and returns
control back to "B" the Data area that was populated in "C" has been
initizaled in "B".  Where did the data go in "B"?

Any ideas?

    "A"
     |
  -------
  |     |
  v     v
 "C"<--"B"

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: OS390 Called Programs Errors

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<744h7p$b1d@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<744edl$f71$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
First, for those who didn't see it, the subject line of this post relates to
OS/390 - therefore, things stated below are written in MVS-ish.  There is no
claim that they will work the same in other environments.

(see actual response at bottom)

DOUG-PARTCH@WORLDNET.ATT.NET wrote in message
<744edl$f71$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Here's the problem:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>"C" then runs a DB2 Select Query to retrive a Data row that is in the
linkage
>area (We know that this is working).  When "C" finsihes executing and
returns
>control back to "B" the Data area that was populated in "C" has been
>initizaled in "B".  Where did the data go in "B"?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

I am not positive what your problem is, but have a couple of ideas.

1) Are the data-areas that you expect to remain "filled-in" defined in
Working-Storage in A *only* and in the Linkage-Section of B and C? (One of
your comments makes me think that it is in Working-Storage of B - which
would mean that anytime you go back from C to A - you will lose it)

2) You do not seem to be using the EXTERNAL attribute on any of your data
(available with VS COBOL II or newer compilers).  I think that this may
actually be the best way to do what you want to do.  In this case, you
define the same data structure in all 3 programs in the Working-Storage
section - but with the External attribute on the 01-level. With this feature
you do NOT pass any data in the CALL statement (or put anything in the
called program's linkage section).  In my opinion, this would make your
application "cleaner".

3) Under MVS (or OS/390), there are some serious issues related to multiple
ways of calling the same program and getting it in last used state.  In
order to INSURE that this happens correctly, you will want to make certain
that you use
  - the RES compiler option (if you are on VS COBOL II or OLDER)
 - the CALL identifier option (rather than CALL literal)
 - Do NOT use the "IS INITIAL" statement on the Program-ID
 - Do NOT try to link-edit all your programs into a single load module

Normally, you could use the DYNAM compiler option to make this work, but you
have mentioned using DB2 - and DB2 will "die a horrible death" if you
compile your COBOL programs with DYNAM (but it will work fine if you use
CALL identifier for your application subroutines).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS390 Called Programs Errors

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366674D0.AAFB3DA3@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<744edl$f71$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <744h7p$b1d@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> 2) You do not seem to be using the EXTERNAL attribute on any of your data
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Please don't recommend this except in dire circumstances.  Passing
through linkage only passes 4 bytes per item passed,  If you make the
pass implicit then the maintainer will not be sure what routines
actually update it.  If have debugged to many C programs using lots of
global to know that this is a very bad idea in the long run.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS390 Called Programs Errors

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981204192528.08058.00000064@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<366674D0.AAFB3DA3@zip.com.au>`

```
Two requests.

To original poster

DOUG-PARTCH@WORLDNET.ATT.NET

please post some code so that we can see the actual problem (or facsimile there
of).  You probably just have simple linkage problem. But with a three level
structure you will need to show us a bit to enable a serious review.


And a second request,
to "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
who on  Wed, 2 Dec 1998 15:06:34 -0800

posted, in part (heavily snipped) ...

<<
Normally, you could use the DYNAM compiler option to make this work, but you
have mentioned using DB2 - and DB2 will "die a horrible death" if you
compile your COBOL programs with DYNAM (but it will work fine if you use
CALL identifier for your application subroutines).
>>

Please tell us what you mean by a horrible death.
And I apologize if this is asking for repeat info for this NG, but I am
interested in compiler parm combinations. Your use of quotes around the phrase
implies this has been previously discussed.

Can you say if the problems with DYNAM occur in certain combinations vis-a-vis
RES/NORES and RENT/NORENT, and not other combinations?  

The following is a leading question, so I don't mean to trap you.  If problems
with DB2 or any other environmental package cause you to avoid DYNAM then are
you not then compelled to place whole classes of routines (like Y2K date
solutions) into CALL data-name syntax? Does this not represent a change for
some systems, a coding change, that should not be necessary; except for parm
combination conundrums?

In your experience are you faced with different parm combination issues under
OS/390 then you were before?

My regrets to the original poster if this causes too much distraction.  If
you're still losing the DB2 data, please post more detail. And can you state,
exactly how you know things are going well in module C prior to return to B?

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

<a id="t-5142175a41"></a>
## Thread: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF.... — 13 msgs

### Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10cf46b32dc1428a9896c5@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Hi Gang,

I have been reading some of the postings related to the topic ELSE-IF 
and I have a couple of thoughts on the subject. 

First, I have to admit that I could not see the purpose for this. It 
seemed to me that the existing IF/THEN and the EVALUATE covers almost 
every possible condition that could come up. And the ELS-IF was just a 
bit redundant, IMO.

Then I thought what the heck, why not add features and if I need them 
later at least they would be there. I may not use it right away but at 
this point Cobol could use more options and this may not be a bad idea.

After some thought I started to wonder what code using the ELSE-IF would
look like, I have not used it so I was curious. So I though let me code
up a few lines and see what it looks like and compare it to the current
methods available and here is the reslut.

Example 1:
Problem:

To make this simple lets say you have four variables A, B, C, and D. 
And only the following conditions could be possible:

If A = B then perform TEST-1
If A = C then perform TEST-2
if A = D then perform TEST-3

The following is code is not using a scope terminator.

Using the ELSE-IF: 		                         
IF A = B 			
THEN
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE-IF A = C
    PERFORM TEST-2
ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-3.

Using the IF/THEN:
IF A = B
THEN
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE
    IF A = C
    THEN
        PERFORM TEST-2
    ELSE
        PERFORM TEST-3.
			
Comment: 
It looks ok, I guess. I think it is a lot cleaner
than the existing structure and it makes quick work of a 
simple example. In this case the standard IF is a bit large 
and you really don't need to use an EVALUATE, so this works
out real well. 

Now here is my question, how do you apply scope terminator(s)
to the ELSE-IF in the above example assuming that the scope
terminator would be END-ELSE-IF.

Any comments, thoughts???

Jeff
JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net

Web Site:
http://home.earthlink.net/~jeffreyfarkas/
```

```
Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...

I cannot see how

>IF A = B
>THEN
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    PERFORM TEST-3.


is any clearer or much better than

IF A = B
THEN
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE
    IF A = C
        PERFORM TEST-2
    ELSE
        PERFORM TEST-3.

If you really want to clarify matters, then the following
does a better job without anything new.

IF A = B
THEN
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE
    perform test4.

test4.
    IF A = C
        PERFORM TEST-2
    ELSE
        PERFORM TEST-3.




Or any clearer, for that matter. I would write it
as:
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981202154429.03678.00000611@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7447rg$qg$1@news.igs.net>`

```

In article <7447rg$qg$1@news.igs.net>, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
writes:

>IF A = B
>THEN
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The problem with this construct, particularly for a long list of conditions, is
that it moves most of the compares down to additional paragraphs, giving the
false impression that the additional conditions are conceptionally subordinate
to the first condition.

If there are multiple conditions that are conceptionally at the same level, it
is better to have them together. This is easily achieved with the EVALUATE
statement or, in other languages, with the ELSE-IF.

The problems with building an ELSE-IF ladder using IF ... / ELSE IF ... is that
the indendation gets quite deep with just a few conditions and there is too big
of a risk that at least one of the IFs wouldn't be properly terminated. (It's
easier to see the end of a bunch of conditions with an END-EVALUATE or, in
other languages, an END-IF, than it is to count END-IF statements to see if the
first IF was terminated, that is, if one doesn't use a period to end all the
IFs.

Mark A. Young
```

the

the
>IFs.




If the ELSE IF ladder is just an emulation of a CASE statement,
then it is much better NOT to indent for each IF (also no reason for
the THEN):

IF A = B
     something-1
ELSE
IF A = C
     something-2
ELSE
IF A = D
     something-3
ELSE
IF A = E
    something-4
.

If the somethings have other IF statements, you can use END-IF
within these or PERFORM a paragraph instead.

- **Message-ID:** `<744ob7$auk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Mark0Young wrote in message
<19981202154429.03678.00000611@ngol07.aol.com>...

>The problems with building an ELSE-IF ladder using IF ... / ELSE IF ... is
that
>the indendation gets quite deep with just a few conditions and there is too
big
>of a risk that at least one of the IFs wouldn't be properly terminated.
(It's


The conditions are not nested, why would you indent them?  I only
use indentation when the second if is part of the perform.  The else
undents back to the original column, so all the IF's and the ELSE's
in the construct lie in a straight line (which also reflects the construct).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981204211659.08058.00000145@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<744ob7$auk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
If I amy add to this, also keep in mind that the ELIF coding construct (or
ELSE-IF or ELSIF) can not be split onto two lines. The two words ELSE IF can be
placed on two lines. Thus ELIF reduces the number of _possible_ indentations.
And the cacophony about it is, per force,  less.

When folks do simple non recursive indentation of ELSE/IF codes they can end up
with a large number of END-IFs that look like the Empire State Building
constructed with END-IFs for each floor.

When folks do recursive indentation they end up with Puffs cousin, END-IF The
Pointless Dragon, that I have commented on elsewhere.

Dragon or skyscraper is hard to modify in real code, especially when embedded
within other conditionals. ELIF is linear in appearance as well as function.

With the absence of the END-IF dragon I would predict that the use of ELIF
would lead people to stop recursive indentation of ELSE/IF constructions,
because it would dangle distractingly.

Best Wishes,


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10cf46b32dc1428a9896c5@news.earthlink.net>`

```

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
>Hi Gang,
[...]
>
>If A = B then perform TEST-1
>If A = C then perform TEST-2
>if A = D then perform TEST-3
>
[...]
>
>Comment:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
END-ELSE-IF makes things rather awkward.

Two level:
IF A = B
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-2
END-IF

Three level:
IF A = B
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE-IF A = B
    PERFORM TEST-2
ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-3
END-ELSE-IF
*> would END-IF still be required?

Four level
IF A = B
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE-IF A = C
    PERFORM TEST-2
ELSE-IF A = D
    PERFORM TEST-3
ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-4
END-ELSE-IF *> close ELSE-IF for A = D
END-ELSE-IF *> close ELSE-IF for A = C and IF A = B
*> what about here?

Better to have infinite level:
IF A = B
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE-IF A = C
    PERFORM TEST-2
ELSE-IF A = D
    PERFORM TEST-3
* more ELSE-IF
ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-n
END-IF

An IF increases the IF-nesting level by 1.
An ELSE-IF closes and maintains the same IF-nesting level as
the previous IF or ELSE-IF.
An ELSE closes and maintains the same IF-nesting level as
the previous IF or ELSE-IF.
An END-IF closes the previous IF, ELSE-IF, or ELSE and reduces
the IF-nesting level by 1.
An end of sentence (period/full stop) resets the nesting level to 0.

These rules still allow ELSE IF to work correctly.
IF A = B *> nesting level 1
    PERFORM TEST-1
ELSE IF A = C *> nesting level 2
    PERFORM TEST-2
ELSE IF A = D *> nesting level 3
    PERFORM TEST-3
ELSE
    PERFORM TEST-4
END-IF *> closes IF A = D nesting level 2
END-IF *> closes IF A = C nesting level 1
END-IF *> closes IF A = B nesting level 0

An end of sentence after PERFORM TEST-4 would also reset
the nesting level to 0 eliminating the requirement for the three
END-IF scope terminators.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10cfddc61577e6819896c8@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10cf46b32dc1428a9896c5@news.earthlink.net> <lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
In article <lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net>, 
ricksmith@aiservices.com says...
> 
> Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-ELSE-IF makes things rather awkward.

Yup, you bet it's awkward but it may just be needed. 

> 
> Two level:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> *> would END-IF still be required?
If you look at the diagram I have drawn it shows the scope of the first 
IF and the second ELSE-IF.
> 
> Four level
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<SNIP>

Let's stop at this point and see what we have. If can be assumed that the 
ELSE-IF is going to help bridge the gap that exists between the IF and an 
EVALUATE why not treat the END-ELSE-IF sort of like a END-EVLAUATE? I 
know it sounds real crazy but it is just an idea since I don't know how 
to properly show how to terminate the scope of the ELSE-IF.  

How about this example:
IF A = B --------------+
THEN                   | 
  ELSE-IF A = C  ----+ |
      PERFORM TEST-2 | |
  ELSE-IF A = D      | |
      PERFORM TEST-3 | |
  ELSE               | |
      PERFORM TEST-4 | |
  END-ELSE-IF -------+ |
ELSE                   |
  PERFORM TEST-1       |
END-IF ----------------+

In this example the ELSE-IF works just like an EVALUATE and why not 
terminate the scope just like an EVALUATE?? I know it is just like an
EVALUATE but in the above situation you don't have to mix an IF with an 
EVALUATE it starts off as an IF and stays that way. 

I guess that I view an EVALUATE and an IF as two entirely separate 
structures and mixing the two is messy. By having the ELSE-IF you bridge 
the gap between the two and it does not look as messy.

Hey, I know this is going to get torn to shreds because it does not make 
any sense but what the heck?? 

Any thoughts?????
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981202150005.03678.00000599@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net>`

```

In article <lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net>, "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> writes:

>END-ELSE-IF
>*> would END-IF still be required?
>

The ELSE-IF is just a continuation of the IF statement, and thus would be
terminated by the END-IF.

Otherwise, why would you need an END-ELSE-IF? You don't have an END-ELSE now
because it serves no purpose that the END-IF doesn't already serve.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Full repost of: Use of ELSE-IF....

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10cfcd6aca3da12e9896c7@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net> <19981202150005.03678.00000599@ngol07.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981202150005.03678.00000599@ngol07.aol.com>, 
mark0young@aol.com says...
> 
> In article <lEg92.644$Lx2.1572@news1.atlantic.net>, "Rick Smith"
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> because it serves no purpose that the END-IF doesn't already serve.

I guess you don't need a separate scope terminator for an ELSE-IF clause 
but I just assume that it might help clarify the end of a "regular" IF 
and the new ELSE-IF. And given the number of different scope terminators 
I would not be surprised if it would be required. 

> 
> Mark A. Young
>

hair out over tables. I got two of them to work, But this one just won't. I
am going to leave the code below and maybe you can help me.. I am not
looking for someone to do it for me.. Just let me know where I need to look
to fix it..

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
-----------------
01 SALARY-TABLE.
          05 TAX-VALUE.
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00100000010".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00300000015".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "01000000035".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "02000000050".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "05000000065".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "10000000085".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "10000000110".

          05 TAX-TABLE REDEFINES TAX-VALUE
               OCCURS 7 TIMES
               INDEXED BY TAX-INDEX.
                10 TAX-AMT    PIC 9(8).
                10 TAX-CODE   PIC 9(3).


230-TAXES.
           SET TAX-INDEX TO 1
               SEARCH TAX-TABLE
                  AT END
                     MOVE ZERO TO D-TAX
                  WHEN SALARY >= TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX)
                     MOVE SALARY TO D-SAL
                     COMPUTE TOT-TAX = TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX) * TAX-CODE
                      (TAX-INDEX)
                     SUBTRACT SALARY FROM TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX) GIVING
                       D-NET
                     MOVE TAX-CODE (TAX-INDEX) TO D-TAX
               END-SEARCH.

unemployed... lazing about the house wearing stained underwear and
drinking beer, arranging tawdry assignations with a neighbor who's spouse
is off earning an Honest Day's Bread and possibly taking part in an
'under the table' work-scheme which denies the State the taxes It
deserves... scandalous, I tell you, and a blight on society!

DD

#### ↳ Re: Need help with this table..

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<744or1$b9p$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<c7i92.2415$U02.5300216@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
You appear to be searching backwards.  Your table is sorted
from lowest to highest, but you are searching based on a greater
than condition.  Since the first element is the smallest, the result
will always either be the first element, or out of the table.  I would
re-order the table from highest to lowest, or change the condition
                  WHEN SALARY >= TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX)
to
                  WHEN SALARY is less than TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX)

David Watson wrote in message ...
>Hi, I hope I am not breaking any newsgroup rules. But I am almost pulling
my
>hair out over tables. I got two of them to work, But this one just won't. I
>am going to leave the code below and maybe you can help me.. I am not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
>-----------------
>01 SALARY-TABLE.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-7dc265da98"></a>
## Thread: Tso commands from cobol — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** spalmeri@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Does anyone know how to run TSO commands (like ALLOCATE and DELETE of files)
from within a Cobol II batch program?  If you could provide specific jcl
and/or Cobol II statments, I would be grateful.

The reason I need to do this is that I need to dynamically create anywhere
from 1 to 5400 unique sequential files from within my cobol program.

I tried looping on the cobol statement:    call 'dynalloc' using jcl-card

where jcl-card is an 80 character jcl statement, but 80 characters is not
enough room to specify the jcl syntax (i have to provide dsn=, disp=, space=,
dcb=, etc. and it takes more than 80 characters to do it.)

So I need to run tso statements to allocate new files.

Thanks,

Sam Palmeri
spalmeri@hotmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<743l20$b6l$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
 <spalmeri@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
>Does anyone know how to run TSO commands (like ALLOCATE and DELETE of files)
>from within a Cobol II batch program?  If you could provide specific jcl
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>from 1 to 5400 unique sequential files from within my cobol program.

I'm sure there is a Very Good Reason for this... be that as it may..

>
>I tried looping on the cobol statement:    call 'dynalloc' using jcl-card
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>So I need to run tso statements to allocate new files.

How about a CALL to a subroutine which writes an IEFBR14 jobstream to
something like

//OUTFIL    DD  SYSOUT=(,,INTRDR)

... although I am not sure what diddling you'll have to do with CANCELs
after the CALL... and maybe a FREE=CLOSE to insure the jobstream gets
SUB'd.


DD
```

rules in almost 3 decades, but is there any good reason (that you know of,
that is) that _all_ IBM JCL is still restricted to 80 characters in length,
as if all SYSIN media was still _only_ 80-character Hollerith cards?  Or are
there actually some accepted exceptions to IBM's 80-column JCL 'standard'?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<744cjl$j56$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <743l20$b6l$1@callisto.clark.net> <e7TQIljH#GA.142@upnetnews05>`

```
In article <e7TQIljH#GA.142@upnetnews05>,
Dennis J. Minette <d.minette@computer.org> wrote:
>Just idle curiosity here, Doc, I have not been troubled by arcane IBM JCL
>rules in almost 3 decades,

Neither have I!... I just learned 'em, adhered to 'em and I ain't been
troubled by 'em since.

>but is there any good reason (that you know of,
>that is) that _all_ IBM JCL is still restricted to 80 characters in length,
>as if all SYSIN media was still _only_ 80-character Hollerith cards?

Hmmmm... what constitutes a 'good reason', *especially* when it comes to
JCL?  Phrases come to mind alike 'Backwards compatibility', Because Big
Blue Says So', 'Because it won't work any other way'.

It seems that some Magick Numbers will be with us a rather long time...
80-col cards, 133-char prtlin and 1920-char screens (with, of course, only
1916-char user-addressable).

> Or are
>there actually some accepted exceptions to IBM's 80-column JCL 'standard'?

None that I know of, nope!

DD

docdwarf@clark.net wrote:

> ...and 1920-char screens (with, of course, only 1916-char user-addressable).

Huh! Are your 3270-style buffers different from the ones I've known and

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<746avm$3is$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <e7TQIljH#GA.142@upnetnews05> <744cjl$j56$1@callisto.clark.net> <36661E4B.D2EED422@att.net>`

In article <36661E4B.D2EED422@att.net>,
William Lynch  <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>still user-addressable.

Granted, they are; I was mixing modes of address here (no pun intended)
and I got sloppy.

One of the first things I do when dealing with users is make sure they
realise that things are seldom what they seem... Deep Forces are at work
and a character or position might not be readily available.  I should have
clarified this by stating '133-char prtlines (with 132-char
user-addressable)'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 133 column reports

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666C5F8.C86AE7A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <e7TQIljH#GA.142@upnetnews05> <744cjl$j56$1@callisto.clark.net> <36661E4B.D2EED422@att.net> <746avm$3is$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```
> One of the first things I do when dealing with users is make sure they
> realise that things are seldom what they seem... Deep Forces are at work
> and a character or position might not be readily available.  I should have
> clarified this by stating '133-char prtlines (with 132-char
> user-addressable)'.

I have converted between systems where the COBOL report shows 133
columns and where the COBOL report shows 132 columns.  One would think
there would be a standard as to whether or not to include the carriage
control in the size of the record seen from within COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 133 column reports

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36676886.154010359@netnews>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <e7TQIljH#GA.142@upnetnews05> <744cjl$j56$1@callisto.clark.net> <36661E4B.D2EED422@att.net> <746avm$3is$1@clarknet.clark.net> <3666C5F8.C86AE7A@home.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 03 Dec 1998 10:10:16 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>I have converted between systems where the COBOL report shows 133
>columns and where the COBOL report shows 132 columns.  One would think
>there would be a standard as to whether or not to include the carriage
>control in the size of the record seen from within COBOL.

There is a standard:  Don't count the carriage control position.  Ancient
IBM compilers made the control character part of the print record, but
ANSI said that was wrong, so IBM started following the standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 133 column reports

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Randall Bart wrote in message <36676886.154010359@netnews>...

><NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

More accurately, IBM provided both a Standard and an upward-compatibility
option.  Please check out the ADV/NOADV compiler options (available with
most IBM compilers - I don't know about COBOL/400)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366e4ef7.27883344@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <743l20$b6l$1@callisto.clark.net> <e7TQIljH#GA.142@upnetnews05>`

```
"Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote:

>Just idle curiosity here, Doc, I have not been troubled by arcane IBM JCL
>rules in almost 3 decades, but is there any good reason (that you know of,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, let's see, 72 is continuation in some cirumstances and 73-80 are
reserved for sequence numbers. It would be kind of hard use those
columns and maintain compatibility. .  

Also, IBM used to have a 96-column reader/punch to work with AS400
stuff. I think that could attach to the internal reader but was
limited to 80 columns. It's been a long time since I've seen one of
those, though.
```

#### ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3665632e$1$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In <743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, on 02 Dec 1998 at 14:30,
spalmeri@my-dejanews.com said:

>Does anyone know how to run TSO commands (like ALLOCATE and DELETE of
>files) from within a Cobol II batch program?  If you could provide
>specific jcl and/or Cobol II statments, I would be grateful.

Here is an example.  Good luck.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>



 //IBMUSERJ JOB (ACCT#),COB2TSO,
 // NOTIFY=&SYSUID,
 // CLASS=A,MSGCLASS=X,COND=(0,NE)
 //*--------------------------------------------------------------------*
 //*                                                                    *
 //*   This sample program illustrates how to invoke TSO                *
 //*   commands from a COBOL program.  The TSO services used            *
 //*   are documented in the TSO/E Programming Services manual.         *
 //*                                                                    *
 //*--------------------------------------------------------------------*
 //COB2 EXEC PGM=IGYCRCTL
  CBL NOLIB,APOST,NODECK,OBJECT,NOSEQ,BUF(32760),DYNAM
  CBL NOMAP,NOLIST,NOOFFSET,NOXREF
 
        Identification Division.
          Program-ID. CB2TSOEV.
          Author. Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>.
 
        Data Division.
         Working-Storage Section.
          01 Filler.
            05 ws-dummy        Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-return-code  Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-reason-code  Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-info-code    Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-cppl-address Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-flags        Pic X(4) Value X'00010001'.
            05 ws-buffer       Pic X(256).
            05 ws-length       Pic s9(8) Comp Value 256.
 
        Procedure Division.
 
            CALL 'IKJTSOEV' Using ws-dummy
                                  ws-return-code
                                  ws-reason-code
                                  ws-info-code
                                  ws-cppl-address.
            IF ws-return-code > zero
              DISPLAY 'IKJTSOEV Failed, Return-code=' ws-return-code
                                      ' Reason-code=' ws-reason-code
                                      'Info-code='    ws-info-code
              MOVE ws-return-code to Return-code
              STOP RUN.
 
            MOVE 'ALLOCATE DD(SYSPUNCH) SYSOUT HOLD' to ws-buffer.
 
            CALL 'IKJEFTSR' Using ws-flags
                                  ws-buffer
                                  ws-length
                                  ws-return-code
                                  ws-reason-code
                                  ws-dummy.
            IF ws-return-code > zero
              DISPLAY 'IKJEFTSR Failed, Return-code=' ws-return-code
                                      ' Reason-code=' ws-reason-code
              MOVE ws-return-code to Return-code
              STOP RUN.
 
            DISPLAY 'ALLOCATE Worked ! ' Upon Syspunch.
 
            STOP RUN.
 /*
 //SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
 //SYSUT1 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT2 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT3 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT4 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT5 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT6 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT7 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSLIN DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1),DISP=(,PASS),BLKSIZE=3200
 //*
 //GO    EXEC PGM=LOADER,PARM=NOPRINT
 //SYSLIN DD DISP=(OLD,PASS),DSN=*.COB2.SYSLIN
 //SYSOUT DD SYSOUT=*
 //SYSLIB DD DISP=SHR,DSN=CEE.SCEELKED
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** "Steve Tunks" <steve_tunks@g140.aone.net.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<745ojl$kit$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

Not having my code handy, I might make the odd mistake, but basically:



01 cmd-select value 'select' pic x(8).
01 clist-stuff.
    05 cmd-clist     value 'clist-name' pic x(8).
    05 filler              value '( your parms here {including normal
bracket})' pic x(nn).

(assuming total length of clist-stuff is 80 bytes...)
01 clist-length valur 80 pic s9(6) comp.

call 'isplink' using cmd-select clist-stuff clist-length
if return-code etc...

If you can wait a coupla days, I'll take a copy of my real code, and send it
to you.

#### ↳ Re: Tso commands from cobol

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666728C.681E3AE8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<743ius$lsj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Most of the dynamic allocation exits are written in house, so your
80character restrictions may not apply elsewhere.

My suggestion is to allocate a model dataset and then use
dcb=model.file.   This is great because if you get the space settings
wrong then you do not have to worry about it.

My second suggestion is to use SMS:  create a DATACLAS with the exact
settings that you want including DCB, and space.  then the only thing
you will need is DATACLAS=SAMPLE.   This again has the advantage that if
you get it wrong just change the definitions.  Document carefully though
trying to figure out how to give it more space at 3am may be a real
teaser in a couple of years.

Hope this helps
Ken

spalmeri@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> Does anyone know how to run TSO commands (like ALLOCATE and DELETE of files)
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-d7b30d5ea0"></a>
## Thread: How do I build an EXE of a C program calling NetExpress COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I build an EXE of a C program calling NetExpress COBOL

- **From:** pitchl@tdbank.ca (Lew Pitcher)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36659f61.3892095@news21.bellglobal.com>`

```
NetExpress 2 comes with (sparse) online documentation which mentions it's ability
to be used in a mixed-language environment. The examples extracted from the
documentation (like pulling teeth, without Novocain) all relate to COBOL calling
C, but the notes refer to a C-calling-COBOL scenario as well.

The *closest* I can get to C-calling-COBOL is to build a .OBJ of my C code, and
an .OBJ of my COBOL code, then use CBLLINK to link them statically together.
CBLLINK doesn't like my C code having a main() function, so I had to rename it
and use the -M option to get it to accept. I built an EXE, but it fails on
trying to initialize \.....\CBLRTSM.DLL.

What I want is to be able to build a C-only EXE file that calls a COBOL DLL.
I'm using Microsoft Visual C++ v5 to build the C program, but LINK complains
about missing externals (_cobolprogramname). There's *no* information in regards
to using DEF files to resolve the name, nor is there any information in VC++
as to how to import the COBOL DLL/entrypoint (yes, I've looked at _declspec(dllimport),
but all it does is change the missing external name to _imp_cobolprogramname).

Can a C program (EXE or DLL) call a NetExpress COBOL program (without resorting to
LoadLibrary()/GetProcAddress() calls)? What do I have to do to make it so (
_declspec or #pragma in the C code, changes to the cobol.DEF file, parameters to
LINK or CBLLINK)?

Does anyone have experience here?



Lew Pitcher
System Consultant, Delivery Systems Architecture
Toronto Dominion Bank

(pitchl@tdbank.ca)


(Opinions expressed are my own, not my employer's.)
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I build an EXE of a C program calling NetExpress COBOL

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7460ac$ajn@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36659f61.3892095@news21.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hi Lew,

I think you are almost there with the _declspec(dllimport).

You will need to link with a LIB file which is created when the COBOL DLL is
built.

If you use use CBLLINK -D -K COBSUB.CBL this will create COBSUB.DLL and also
a COBSUB.LIB that you need to use when linking the C module.

Using the following commands on MS VC4 work fine on my system.

    CBLLINK -D -K COBSUB.CBL
    CL CCALLER.C COBSUB.LIB

Hope this helps.
David.

## Thread: Download Hypercobol — 2 msgs

### Download Hypercobol

- **From:** Thierry bricman <tbricman@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3665B0E0.4B4C0F24@bigfoot.com>`

```
Hi,

Have you ever had to work on old COBOL programs that were both not documented
nor explicit ? And with no debugger available because hardware and system are
too old ?
Well here is a small shareware I wrote that can save you a lot of time in
understanding how the hell do those wild and unstructured programs work !
You need two things first:
1 - A way to import COBOL source codes as ascii text files on a PC running
Windows (ftp or whatever).
2 - Winword (I tested Word 97 but Word 95 should work fine. I need feedback on
this matter)

What Hypcobsh.exe does:

It reads your COBOL ascii source code and makes a .RTF file of it.
After loading the RTF file with Winword, you will see that colored hypertext
links have been added in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
You will be able to use the Winword web tool bar to navigate into your source
code as you would do in a HTML text.
Try it. It's easy and self explicit.

you can download it here : http://multimania.com/nirrey/shareware/HYPCOBOL.ZIP
```

#### ↳ Re: Download Hypercobol

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3665b0c7.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3665B0E0.4B4C0F24@bigfoot.com>`

```
Thierry bricman wrote in message <3665B0E0.4B4C0F24@bigfoot.com>...
>What Hypcobsh.exe does:
>
>It reads your COBOL ascii source code and makes a .RTF file of it.
>After loading the RTF file with Winword, you will see that colored
hypertext
>links have been added in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>You will be able to use the Winword web tool bar to navigate into your
source
>code as you would do in a HTML text.
>Try it. It's easy and self explicit.
>
>you can download it here :
http://multimania.com/nirrey/shareware/HYPCOBOL.ZIP

Thierry,

I downloaded your program (BTW, you need a http://www.muliti... in the URL).
Seems to work fine. Two things to make it useful:
1) handle TABS better
2) being able to run it like this:  hypcobsh [file] | @batch
It is important to be able to run it in the background, without
an annoying screen and accompanying beeps.
```

## Thread: entry level positions for Y2K??? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### entry level positions for Y2K???

- **From:** levin@pathcom.com (Carolyn Levin)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<levin-0112981959520001@ts43l31.pathcom.com>`

```
I'm an engineer who lives in Toronto and I want to make a career change to
computer programming. I've been informed that a good way to do it, is to
learn cobol and land an entry level position at a Bank (who ususally
trains).

But do these positions actually exist? Recruiters all say that you
actually need at least 2 years of experience to find work.

Carolyn
```

#### ↳ Re: entry level positions for Y2K???

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981201215247.26237.00000019@ng101.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<levin-0112981959520001@ts43l31.pathcom.com>`

```
Under the impact of Y2K the market is good in all industries.  The more
training you bring to the table the better your entry level remuneration.  

It definitely is _not_ true that you would need two years to get a foot in the
door.  Make direct contacts. Many Financial institutions have online
application processes,... use search engines.

Why limit yourself to banks. Get a Fortune 500 list and apply everywhere.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

Bill Lynch
```

## Thread: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix — 2 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-11](1998-11.md#t-1e5336402a)._

#### ↳ Re: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<743ou7$f0p$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
 <andyho99@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
>
> Currently, someone is modifying our Cobol program for Y2K
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thing?

You can call C functions from COBOL.  Study the "COBOL
Interfacing Environment" and "Mixed-Language Programming"
chapters of the "COBOL User Guide" manual for details.

It looks from the Web site as though newer versions will
support extended year info, although the details are not
obvious.  I would guess there's a directive controlling the
format of what ACCEPT ... FROM DATE generates.
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix

- **From:** yzhen@hpis.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<742pm6$15f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi Andy,

Of course, you can make a system call to OS to get current date and time. You
have to contact the Cobol supply if you are not sure how to do it.

I think the key of Y2K is not how to get a date, but is how to store a date
and how to operate on it.

I am using a VOS Cobol, it is running on the VOS which is an OS like UNIX. In
my Y2K solution, I use a comp-5(integer) variable to hold the julian day
number of a date. I create a subroutin to transform any input forms to integer
and transform integer to any output forms.

Hope it helps.


Regrads,
Rex Young

<a id="t-7d20b16151"></a>
## Thread: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback). — 33 msgs

### Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** danticd@one.net
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```
Hi Folks!

I am new to the world of COBOL and am struggling to understand all the
idiosyncracies and nuances of the language (actually, I haven't even
gotten that far yet).  I am attempting to write my 4th COBOL program.  I
am using Micro Focus COBOL for Windows 3.1 (came with the text book).
Anyway this is my problem.  I am trying to get a handle on some of the
fundamentals and how to code a bit more efficiently.  In an attempt to
do just that, I came across this handy dandy little COBOL Verb,
INITIALIZE.  When I mentioned it to my instructor, his discussion on the
verb left me somewhat fuzzy.  He had indicated that this verb "may use
up more resources than the normal initial MOVE statements that are used
to place actual initial values of either zero or spaces into the
recieving field variables."  (What a mouthful)   He added several
warnings about how careful one must be when using this VERB.  I have
been reading this group for about two weeks now and I am very excited
about the minds I have been allowed to glimpse.   I would love to throw
this up in the air for a little friendly babble.  Thanks for any
responses and to all that take the time to respond.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e96bRpxH#GA.303@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```

danticd@one.net wrote in message <3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>...
>Hi Folks!
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>warnings about how careful one must be when using this VERB.

The overhead involved in using INITIALIZE, in comparison to hand-coding
moves to all the various fields that must have initial values set, is
_negligible_ when viewed in the light of all the bloated apps now filling up
our hardware capacity.  The instructor must have learned his concept of
efficiency in the early days of very expensive core memory and rotating
magnetic storage media (that's what they called memory and disk in olden
days.)  IMHO, the INITIALIZE verb saves a lot of busywork otherwise expended
in coding MOVEs to load initial values.

> I have
>been reading this group for about two weeks now and I am very excited
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667d5df.181929121@netnews>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <e96bRpxH#GA.303@upnetnews05>`

```
'Twas Thu, 3 Dec 1998 19:07:27 -0500, when "Dennis J. Minette"
<dennis_minette@email.msn.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The overhead involved in using INITIALIZE, in comparison to hand-coding
>moves to all the various fields that must have initial values set, is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>in coding MOVEs to load initial values.

As a general rule, writing something declaratively (ie, in the DATA
DIVISION) is less errorprone than writing it procedurally (in the
PROCEDURE DIVISION).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667F22B.6B832855@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <e96bRpxH#GA.303@upnetnews05> <3667d5df.181929121@netnews>`

```
> IMHO, the INITIALIZE verb saves a lot of busywork otherwise expended
> >in coding MOVEs to load initial values.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> PROCEDURE DIVISION).

Another general rule - if your compiler can set the values at compile
time, it runs more efficiently than if your program sets the value at
run time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<el$HYu$H#GA.259@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <e96bRpxH#GA.303@upnetnews05> <3667d5df.181929121@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <3667d5df.181929121@netnews>...
>'Twas Thu, 3 Dec 1998 19:07:27 -0500, when "Dennis J. Minette"
><dennis_minette@email.msn.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>_negligible_ when viewed in the light of all the bloated apps now filling
up
>>our hardware capacity.  The instructor must have learned his concept of
>>efficiency in the early days of very expensive core memory and rotating
>>magnetic storage media (that's what they called memory and disk in olden
>>days.)  IMHO, the INITIALIZE verb saves a lot of busywork otherwise
expended
>>in coding MOVEs to load initial values.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Is this an oblique reference to using VALUE clauses in the DATA DIVISION?
They don't help much for data elements that might require more than one
INITIALIZE during the course of a COBOL program execution.  And INITIALIZE
has no respect for the VALUES you may have specified that are not the
standard initial values.  But you knew that already, didn't you?  :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366f366a.7316833@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <e96bRpxH#GA.303@upnetnews05> <3667d5df.181929121@netnews>`

```
On 4 Dec 1998 13:02:19 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net wrote:

>
>As a general rule, writing something declaratively (ie, in the DATA
>DIVISION) is less errorprone than writing it procedurally (in the
>PROCEDURE DIVISION).
>

So true.  In addition, maintenance chores are easier.  For example, if
a value is referenced repeatedly, it is better coded in the DATA
DIVISION rather than in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.  Should the value
change in the future, you will have to make the change in only one
place, rather than every time it is referenced.
```

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36671415.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```
danticd@one.net wrote in message <3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>...
>Hi Folks!
>I am new to the world of COBOL and am struggling to understand all the
>idiosyncracies and nuances of the language (actually, I haven't even
>gotten that far yet.  I am trying to get a handle on some of the
>fundamentals and how to code a bit more efficiently.

at your level efficiency is not your main concern. Later on it
may or may not (the most common) be important.

>  In an attempt to
>do just that, I came across this handy dandy little COBOL Verb,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>recieving field variables."  (What a mouthful)   He added several

INITIALZIZE is fine; there are some (pathological) caes where
you have to be a bit careful, but again at your level, there is
nothing wrong with INITIALIZE as such; one might argue that
maybe you don't *need* to initialize (or move to) all the time,
but that is different matter.

>warnings about how careful one must be when using this VERB.  I have
>been reading this group for about two weeks now and I am very excited
>about the minds I have been allowed to glimpse.   I would love to throw
>this up in the air for a little friendly babble.  Thanks for any
>responses and to all that take the time to respond.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667CC5D.2AD5D9CD@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <36671415.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
(snip)

> INITIALZIZE is fine; there are some (pathological) caes where
> you have to be a bit careful, but again at your level, there is
> nothing wrong with INITIALIZE as such; one might argue that
> maybe you don't *need* to initialize (or move to) all the time,
> but that is different matter.

Very good point from Leif; let me just toss in an example, don't
INITIALIZE areas which are used for input files. It's also not
necessary, as a rule, to INITIALIZE (or to specify VALUEs) on save areas
into which you always move something before you try to use it for
anything else.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10d0da2bb6875b7d9896c9@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```
In article <3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>, danticd@one.net says...
> Hi Folks!
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

First I would change your signature, another estimated member of this 
group already makes use of it. <g>

> 

Ok let's get down to business. Assuming your instructor is correct about 
the the extra resources that "may" be used with the INITIALIZE verb I 
think any possible increase is far less than the cost in human resources. 

While I know machine resources need to be considered in this situation I 
think it is not worth the time to figure out the additional machine 
overhead vs the human cost.

For example, if you change a record layout and add a new field using his 
method you would have to make sure to include an additional MOVE to make 
sure it is initialized properly. By using the INITIALIZE correctly that 
would almost never be an issue. I say almost never because I feel that 
any thing can happen and I don't like using the term "never".

In the long run using the INITIALIZE verb will make your life a lot 
easier and your code will be a lot easier to maintain for yourself and 
the pour slob who gets to maintain it in the future.

Just my 2 cents.<g>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>

quote to see the new input.  White space would help to separate the
quoted message from the reply.  Good COBOL practice too!

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
I not only agree with Jeff, but would add a point: you generally
only initialise data once.  Even if the initialise verb *does* take
a bit longer (and I doubt it does, frankly), unless you are in the
main loop, the extra time is almost irrelevant. A couple of
milliseconds here and there only count if you are repeating
something a few million times.

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** vrex@my-dejanews.com

```
In article <MPG.10d0da2bb6875b7d9896c9@news.earthlink.net>,
  JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas) wrote:
> In article <3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>, danticd@one.net says...
> > Hi Folks!
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In My opion the Initialize verb IS more effecient
If for instance you have a group level name that includes both numeric
and Alpha fields the initialize will not only MOVE ZEROS to the
numeric fields but will also MOVE SPACES to the alphas.

example:
01 Ca-Calculations
    05 Ca-Balance                        pic s9(06)v99.
    05 Ca-min-payment                    pic s9(06)v99.
    05 Ca-Status                         pic  x(08).
What makes more sense to you (style wise)
Initialize Ca-Calculations
         or
Move zeros to Ca-Balance, Ca-min-payment
Move spaces to Ca-status
both instances initialize the fields to zeros or spaces but when you use the
Initialize verb you only need to enter the Group level name instead of
remembering a bunch of the elementary items.
BESIDES

Programmers HATE to type
:)
Dean Bathke

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

It is not that we HATE to type. It is that typing is an errorprone activity.
I would gladly type my programs three times over, if you would
guarantee that there would be no bugs in the program.

Programmers HATE to think. Thinking is like a cavalry attack:
you can't do it too often and it requires fresh horses.

Progress is measured by all the things we don't have to think
about.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-12-25T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<36844349.F7B35E0E@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <MPG.10d0da2bb6875b7d9896c9@news.earthlink.net> <75hhv3$v7j$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
vrex@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> In article <MPG.10d0da2bb6875b7d9896c9@news.earthlink.net>,
>   JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas) wrote:
> > In article <3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>, danticd@one.net says...
> > > Hi Folks!
...
> > > Anyway this is my problem.  I am trying to get a handle on some of the
> > > fundamentals and how to code a bit more efficiently.  In an attempt to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > warnings about how careful one must be when using this VERB.  I have
...
> > > DD
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> numeric fields but will also MOVE SPACES to the alphas.
...

The places where initialize can "cost" are (1) large complex group items
where you really only need to initialize some fields, but choose to
initialize all fields because the INITIALIZE statement is "so easy"; (2)
multi-dimensional Group items with occurs-depending-on items with many
subordinate occurs-depending-on elements.  The INITIALIZE statement may
generate the equivalent of a PERFORM loop for every OCCURS DEPENDING
element, so complex multi-level structures can generate a lot of control
code.  If the INITIALIZE is only done once in a batch program, this
probably is not worth worrying about.  If done repeatedly during the
execution of the same batch program, or if it is in an on-line program
executed 1000's of times daily, then there may be better alternatives. 
One common trick is to create a single group item structured like the
outer-most group OCCURS item, initialize that single group item and use
it in a single loop with a group-item move to initialize the larger
structure (a really clever compiler could be smart enough to do this
optimization for you, but unless you know this to be the case for your
compiler, assume a separate move for each elementary field item and a
loop control structure for every OCCURS clause).  Another technique is
to avoid having to initialize all elements of a large array by keeping a
"max index" pointer and designing the program logic so that you only
reference items into which actual data has been stored. If a complex
structure only needs some items initialized, you may also be able to
improve on efficiency by separating the group structure into two
separate structures, separating elements requiring initialization from
those that don't.

If you have a group item containing no OCCURS clauses in itself or any
subordinate fields, and if all fields in the group item really require
initialization, and if the default initial values do the job, then
clearly the simplest and most efficient way is the INITIALIZE
statement.  Other cases may require some thought and analysis if
efficiency is a prime concern.
```

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667CAE4.E08BE55C@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```
danticd@one.net wrote:
> 
> Hi Folks!
> 
> I am new to the world of COBOL and am struggling to understand all the
> idiosyncracies and nuances of the language 

Just for openers, forget doing what you wrote above. Just browse through
the NG and look at the looong threads we generate on GOTO, EVALUATE vs.
IF..ELSE..IF..ELSE..., etc. People who've been coding COBOL
professionally 20 years and more don't understand "all the
idiosyncrasies and nuances of the language". Concentrate on learning how
to make things work.

Your professor's reply concerning the INITIALIZE verb is a standard one;
however, I haven't observed any problems in practice and we use it a
lot. Make sure you understand the limitations of INITIALIZE; for
openers, it doesn't do FILLER.

Bill Lynch
```

When coding up an record layout that I am going to use as output I have 
found that adding a numeric suffix to FILLER to be helpful. 
Example:
FILLER-1
FILLER-2

By using this it is unlikely that it will cause any confusion and it is 
initialized correctly so it will not contain low-values.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
>
>When coding up an record layout that I am going to use as output I have
>found that adding a numeric suffix to FILLER to be helpful.


>Example:
>FILLER-1
>FILLER-2

You really do not need to make them all different. I have
done a gobal search and replace of "FILLER" by "FILLERX"
to solve both that problem and one other ... some screen
sections display filler, and some do not.  As I was used to
the former, I have systems where filler was used for
literals in the screen section.

The only problem that I can see with it is if you also use
move corresponding on the same records.
```

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-mcnlwuSFtKGf@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```
On Thu, 3 Dec 1998 21:14:04, danticd@one.net wrote:

> Hi Folks!
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> recieving field variables."  

Lookout - I'm going to plug my book.

Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours by yours truly is now available.
 If it's not in your local bookstore yet - it will be soon.

I have a discussion of Initialize in the book, when to use it and what
to watch out for.

In readers digest version:  Some compilers DO suffer from poor 
performance when  using Initialize, but most modern compilers have 
overcome this issue. 

One MISUNDERSTANDING about initialize that is common is the idea that 
when you have a VALUE clause specified for a data item, Initialize 
does NOT replace the field with the VALUE specified.

However, that said - you can TARGET INITIALZE to specific data types, 
and you can even use it to replace field contents with other than 
zeros or spaces.
```

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<747cvv$9d6@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```

Iinitialize is wonderfule, as is a local-storage section. :) 
However, non IBM compilers often implement them in wierd
and disgusting ways. For example, AcuCOBOL 3.x implements it,
but it doesn't work. 

Check the compiler you are using and if it it is implemented,
test it. If it works, use it. (Caveat: make damn sure your 
compiler is supported!) 

I disagree with a lot of folks around here in that I will
always trade off efficiency for maintainability; you can always
add hardware. :) 

-Paul

P.S. - I just found out today that a large chunk of my email
has been lost for the past couple months; if anyone here is waiting
on a replyh from me, please resend. (*sigh*) I guess I learned a 
lesson - never depend upon a former employer to forward your mail
to you!

-Paul

#### ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36691FCB.D40F081B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net>`

```
danticd@one.net wrote:
> 
> Hi Folks!
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

To: Tuther DD

The initialize verb (spelt wrong because of the yanks) is a very handy
tool if it used where it really needs to be used.  All to often I see
some coders put an initialise (sorry) at the beginning of a paragraph
that fills every field with no if's, but's or maybe's.  Know your code
and don't waste any steps.

Consider the order of your fields.  There is an instruction to load a
single character across a large section of storage by one command, so
put you X fields together (with no fillers) if possible.

If you can guarantee that the numeric fields are filled and the other
fields needs space use 'move space' and then do your numeric moves, but
this is possibly a bad idea from a maintenance point of view.

The use of initialise with fillers is great for date formatting

	dd pic x(02)
	filler pic x(01) value '/'.
	....

Once again if you are storing the year month and day you just don't need
the overhead of an extra useless step.

Initialising group fields can be very confusing to a beginner, we have
all been caught by thinking that we have tracked the modifications of a
field to find that it has a group initialise, sometimes it is clearer to
explicitly clear fields, eg:

	01 ws-counters
	   05 record count
	   05 detail count

when moving between accounts you need to clear these counters, sometimes
(not always) it is better to be explicit and clear using two move zero
statements.  Consider the next person along and not the shortest path to
a coded result.

My 2 cents with 47.5% tax
Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appreciate Some "InPut"..(or feedback).

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366A581E.D65395@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3666FF1C.12F10109@one.net> <36691FCB.D40F081B@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

(snip)
> 
> Initialising group fields can be very confusing to a beginner, we have
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> a coded result.

Maybe we've wandered into another discussion of STYLE. I routinely put
all my packed-decimal (IBM mainframe alert) fields under "1  
PACKED-FIELDS      PACKED-DECIMAL." along with an "INITIALIZE    
PACKED-FIELDS" in my initialization section. Same for switches (although
they aren't packed-decimal). I believe it's pretty straightforward. Of
course, sometimes a switch or counter is reused in one execution of the
program, so I INITIALIZE it again before the loop where I use it.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus And dates

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36671b18.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<73u598$ig7$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
Frik Lombard wrote in message <73u598$ig7$1@hermes.is.co.za>...
>When I use the accept w-date from date the system returns a date in the
>format yymmdd. What can I do for the system to return a 8 digit date for me
>in the format ccyymmdd.


This question comes up about once a week. answers range from:
1: you call "VOODOO" using strange    to
2: you use (what you don't have) the ACCEPT ccyymmdd from DATE YYYYMMDD

It is actually quite simple:

01  DATE-NOW       PIC 9(8).
01  DATE-CCYYMMDD  REDEFINES DATE-NOW.
      02  DATE-CC           PIC 99.
      02  DATE-YYMMDD  PIC 9(6).
      02  DATE-YY-MM-DD  REDEFINES DATE-YYMMDD.
            03  DATE-YY        PIC 99.
            03  DATE-MM      PIC 99.
            03  DATE-DD       PIC 99.

....

     ACCEPT DATE-YYMMDD FROM DATE
     IF YY < 98
          MOVE 20 TO DATE-CC
     ELSE
          MOVE 19 TO DATE-CC
     .

This should work for the next 100 years or so
```

Frik Lombard wrote:

> When i use tha accept w-date from date the system returns a date in the
> format yymmdd. What can i do for the system to return a 8 digit date fro me
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

```

<a id="t-20dece04fd"></a>
## Thread: Check Digit — 2 this month / 12 total

_Thread started [1998-11](1998-11.md#t-20dece04fd)._

#### ↳ Re: Check Digit

- **From:** jraben19@mail.idt.net (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666b6e8.2520343@news.idt.net>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com>`

```
suevarma@aol.com (Sue Varma) wrote:

>CAn anyone out there help me. I am working with check digits at the moment and
>need to know how to work out the modulus of a number. What is the modulus? and
>how is it calculated. Many thanks in advance to anyone who can help.

with some minor variations 'mod' is the remainder of an integer
divide.

15 mod 10 = 5,   25 mod 10=5

however checkdigits are often far more complex being weighted by
occurance of digits in different positions of a source number.

say  # 12345   may be  1*ckd1 + 2*chk2 + 3*chk3 + 4*chk4 + 5*chk5=sum
     sum mod 10 = ?    there is the checkdigit for 12345.
where ckd-n  was a weighted occurance. 
in some formulas, if the wieghts were even take the carry and add it
back to the first digit..
7*2=14 don't use the 4 but use 5  (1+4) for summing.....

for a project i was on ckd1=7 ckd2=3 ckd3=1 ... ckd4=7 again etc.....
our checkdigit was a little more complex but....
  simple variations are based on how often the first digit of a (say)
5-digit part number went from 0 to 9.  

the idea is to catch transposition errors during writing/typing.

enough????


jr
and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Check Digit

- **From:** "Martin G. Diehl" <mdiehl@nac.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3672A172.BFBAC9E3@nac.net>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com> <3666b6e8.2520343@news.idt.net>`

```
Jeff Raben wrote:
> 
> suevarma@aol.com (Sue Varma) wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> and stir with a Runcible spoon...

Another example of Check Digits is the identification number that 
identifies a security (bond, or stock) -- the CUSIP number.  The 
check digit is based on "Modulus 10 Double Add Double" and uses the 
complement of the last digit of the sum as the check digit vlaue.  
See http://www.cusip.com/ for a full explanation with examples.  
```

## Thread: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler) — 38 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3666C505.66E4724E@home.com>...
>> (yow... how's *that* for a rather 'unaesthetic' mixture of New and Olde
>> Styles?)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>at now).

You don't care about run-time efficiency??  Have you never been associated
with a large-scale batch processing system trying to reduce over-long run
times?  Some businesses have to be acutely concerned with completing their
batch processing applications during the night sometime before their doors
open for business on the next day.  Run-time efficiency is paramount in that
scenario.

Dennis J. Minette
CCCO
Minette Data Systems, Inc.
d.minette@computer.org
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05>`

```


"Dennis J. Minette" wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3666C505.66E4724E@home.com>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> scenario.

I don't think run-time efficiency should *ever* be paramount in a
business application.  Maintainability and reliability is always king. 
If the runs are too long, after doing the usual tweaking where it can
make a difference, getting faster hardware is the best viable solution. 
Otherwise you'll be spending many hours of heartache keeping
temperamental programs running smoothly.

That's my 2 cents worth :\
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#yEnu$yH#GA.211@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com>`

```

Scott Ramey wrote in message <366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com>...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> (yow... how's *that* for a rather 'unaesthetic' mixture of New and
Olde
>> >> Styles?)
>> >>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> You don't care about run-time efficiency??  Have you never been
associated
>> with a large-scale batch processing system trying to reduce over-long run
>> times?  Some businesses have to be acutely concerned with completing
their
>> batch processing applications during the night sometime before their
doors
>> open for business on the next day.  Run-time efficiency is paramount in
that
>> scenario.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>make a difference, getting faster hardware is the best viable solution.


I prefer to do my "usual tweaking" in advance,  with an eye toward
minimizing runtime, _before_ the code goes into production, so that the
maiden voyage of the production system does not immediately cause a need for
faster hardware.
And where did you get the notion that run-time efficiency may not be
consistent with reliability.  I am definitely not advancing the proposition
that programs should be made less reliable in order to make them run faster.

My approach to achieving run-time efficiency basically involves reducing, to
the extent possible, the number of machine cycles required to process the
given job requirements, and designing all input-output procedures to be as
efficient as is reliably possible within the constraints of the target
environment.


>Otherwise you'll be spending many hours of heartache keeping
>temperamental programs running smoothly.


And where is it written that efficient programs are tempermental.  You seem
to be advancing the argument that efficient programs are not easily
maintained, but if programs are easily maintained, you might need more
capable hardware to make up for their inefficiency.

>That's my 2 cents worth :\
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667F567.AF3893D4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com> <#yEnu$yH#GA.211@upnetnews05>`

```
> And where is it written that efficient programs are tempermental.  You seem
> to be advancing the argument that efficient programs are not easily
> maintained, but if programs are easily maintained, you might need more
> capable hardware to make up for their inefficiency.

We're really concerned here where run-time efficiency conflicts with
maintenance efficiency.  
I don't recommend putting all my code in-line for run-time efficiency,
but I do recommend initializing variables before performing a paragraph
a thousand times instead of within the paragraph (for equal results).

The former conflicts with maintenance, the latter does not.
```

I am too dense too extract any meaning from that exercise.  Where, exactly,
does the former conflict with maintenance?  I could, however, understand a
conclusion of "the latter conflicts with efficiency, the former does not."

I am beginning to see that your concept of run-time efficiency has something
to do with "in-line" code, whatever you might mean by that.  What does
in-line code mean to you? Absence of PERFORMed routines?  No CALL
statements? Lots of GOTOs sprinkled amongst the IF statements?

I once worked in an environment, where we were told to make our on-line
COBOL programs as much as possible as one large paragraph.  Couldn't be
done, but the "as much as possible" did encourage some maintenance
difficulties.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<366815BC.2E614C7C@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com> <#yEnu$yH#GA.211@upnetnews05>`

"Dennis J. Minette" wrote:
> 
> Scott Ramey wrote in message <366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com>...
[snip]
> >
> >I don't think run-time efficiency should *ever* be paramount in a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> that programs should be made less reliable in order to make them run faster.
[snip]
> >Otherwise you'll be spending many hours of heartache keeping
> >temperamental programs running smoothly.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> capable hardware to make up for their inefficiency.

It isn't written that efficient programs are temperamental.  It isn't
written that we generally can't have our cake and eat it too. 
Programming, like life, involves compromises, and if you make one thing
number one priority then something else will slip down a notch or two. 
A general rule is that performance and reliability don't go hand in
hand.  If they did go hand in hand then we might see a lot of taxi cabs
winning road races ( and I don't mean destruction derby ), or retired
Formula 1 cars used to zip pizzas around down (whatta bummer too, I'd
order Domino's every day just to see it arrive) :-[
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>number one priority then something else will slip down a notch or two.


That is the _purpose_ of priorities. After all, everything cannot be of
equal importance.  All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal
than others.  Right or wrong, I will place a higher value on efficiency of
operation than on appearance or 'religious' consistency (which are sort of
the hallmarks of maintainability) but not to the point of detriment to
maintainability.

>A general rule is that performance and reliability don't go hand in
>hand.  If they did go hand in hand then we might see a lot of taxi cabs
>winning road races ( and I don't mean destruction derby ), or retired
>Formula 1 cars used to zip pizzas around down (whatta bummer too, I'd
>order Domino's every day just to see it arrive) :-[

Yeah, pizza by Lamborghini would be worth not waiting for :-)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ga0j$dvg$1@news.igs.net>`
