```

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...

>> A general rule is that performance and reliability don't go hand in
>> hand.  If they did go hand in hand then we might see a lot of taxi cabs
>> winning road races ( and I don't mean destruction derby ), or retired
>> Formula 1 cars used to zip pizzas around down (whatta bummer too, I'd
>> order Domino's every day just to see it arrive) :-[


Actually, I would disagree with the above.  I think that they do
go hand in hand.  I have seldom seen an illegible and poorly
organized program that ran like spit, and I have seldom seen
a well organized and written program that ran really poorly.

Sure, it can be done.  But as a rule well written code runs
better than poorly written code. People that try to excuse
really bad work in the name of efficiency are generally
just poor workers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366C08E7.370101CD@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <366730A5.AA112788@bdssoftware.com> <#yEnu$yH#GA.211@upnetnews05> <366815BC.2E614C7C@bdssoftware.com> <MPG.10d4ece5ea77e82e9896d3@news.earthlink.net> <74ga0j$dvg$1@news.igs.net>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> just poor workers.

Donald, let me try to explain myself a little better.  There is never an
excuse for sloppy code.  Poorly written code is not be easily
maintainable code and that's always my fist priority.  When
maintainability comes first then the code will have clarity, probably
doing some things in a less efficient manner in order to make it easier
to follow, and so on and so forth.  On the other hand, if efficiency is
paramount then some of the times the purpose of the code may not be so
obvious because speed is more important than simplicity.  Hopefully the
author will have provided copious notes.  It's the ole tradeoff in life,
if you want a fast woman you probably have a poor wife, even though life
will be more exciting at times.  For sure, you (and maybe a few others)
will get a good ****.
```

```
Lurking and learning!  IIRC, the discussion centered on knowing assembler so
that one could appreciate the machine-level effects of actions taken in a
HLL such as COBOL.  Since I don't really "do" much assembler, I didn't jump
into the fray.

My earliest grounding in programming involved setting switches in an RCA
trainer (i.e., machine language programming.) I then went into electronics
technician work in the USN for a few years that didn't involve digital
computers.  When I got back into the programming game, while getting a BSEE,
standard HLLs such as FORTRAN and COBOL had formally arrived on the scene,
so I more or less skipped over any formal training in Assembler languages.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** mrbiueskyz@aol.comatose (MrBIueSkyz)
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981211163312.04729.00000816@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<#yEnu$yH#GA.211@upnetnews05>`

```
>I prefer to do my "usual tweaking" in advance,  with an eye toward
>minimizing runtime, _before_ the code goes into production, so that the
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hear, hear! Efficiency and stability are not mutually
exclusive. I pride myself on writing programs that run
as quickly as possible & that are stable and reliable, such
as being able to handle empty input files (abending when
appropriate), checking for table overflows (abending etc.), avoiding division
by zero & making numeric checks 
a part of the read routine. A lot of efficiency can be obtained with simple
common sense. Example: some years
back, the insurance co. I work for modified its IMS drafts
data base by adding two segments to it. The monthly
download pgm was modified to retrieve these new segments. The new segments were
only required to be
downloaded for the December run, but the programmer
retrieved them first, and THEN looked at the date parm
to see whether they should be written to the output file.
With a data base numbering in the millions of segments,
this was not a trivial concern. I modified the pgm to look
at the date parm first, and skip the retrieval calls for months other than
December. Result: an annual savings
of $25,000 in execution costs. This one simple change
has literally saved the company a quarter of a million
dollars in the ten years that it's been in place.
To sum it up, design the pgm to do only what it has to
do, when it has to do it.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667176B.66814C11@home.com>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05>`

```
> >> A proof of the undeniable efficiency of this method is left to someone
> >> else.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> scenario.

I was exaggerating, I have done all of the above.  But standards should
be designed around the most expensive efficiencies, which are almost
always maintenance and the costs of not having a correctly working
product.  But not always.

Senior programmers need to know when to break the rules.
```

```
first... my vote goes to 'Thou Shalt Not Pay A Good Programmer More Than A
Bad Manager'.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05>`

```
"Dennis J. Minette" wrote:
> 
(snip)
> You don't care about run-time efficiency??  Have you never been associated
> with a large-scale batch processing system trying to reduce over-long run
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> scenario.

What you say is true, Dennis, but I've never seen an example of anyone's
shaving an hour or two off their batch run by coding an INDEX instead of
a subscript, or moving spaces to some fields instead of INITIALIZEing
them. In my experience the big payoffs have been in such changes as
providing adequate VSAM buffers, sorting the input transactions on major
updates, etc. 

Bill Lynch

Standard disclaimer - my experience is limited to IBM mainframes. YMMV
```

```

> shaving an hour or two off their batch run by coding an INDEX instead of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Agreed, and none of which effected maintainability of the COBOL code.

followed by merges, etc.  Costs changes have changed our priorities.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<748lf7$kn2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net>...
>"Dennis J. Minette" wrote:
>What you say is true, Dennis, but I've never seen an example of anyone's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>updates, etc.


I tend to agree with Bill on this.  A change in algorithm can have major
effects, but with only a few exceptions a simple change in technique
seldom has a major impact on run times.  By that, I am referring to
changes like removing a "GOTO EXCEPTION" and making the
routine two top down perform routines.  It might, in a very few cases
where the loop is being executed millions of times, but such cases
are rare.  If an inner loop is so critical that such a change makes
hours of differences, then maybe it should be a hand coded assembler
routine.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#2rjcZCI#GA.332@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net>...
>"Dennis J. Minette" wrote:
>>
>(snip)
>> You don't care about run-time efficiency??  Have you never been
associated
>> with a large-scale batch processing system trying to reduce over-long run
>> times?  Some businesses have to be acutely concerned with completing
their
>> batch processing applications during the night sometime before their
doors
>> open for business on the next day.  Run-time efficiency is paramount in
that
>> scenario.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Standard disclaimer - my experience is limited to IBM mainframes. YMMV


<DEFENSIVE MODE>
I wouldn't count those _trivial_ things you mentioned as great time savers
either.
Did I actually sound like those sorts of things were the sort of "time
saver" I might have in mind? I simply can't understand (or easily tolerate)
an attitude of "not caring about run-time efficiency."  I was attempting to
make the point that run-time efficiency considerations have to be at least
kept in mind.  In my posting I had in mind (as I usually do <g>) large scale
systems that must process and act upon (_not_  just read and write) many,
many thousands of records per run.  For instance, calculating margins in a
brokerage back-office application used by LARGE brokerage firms - that
tended to exercise all of the run-time efficiency tricks I could muster.

Maybe I just haven't worked with enough small, low-volume, single-focus
applications to completely lose any concern for efficiency. <g>
</DEFENSIVE MODE>

<OFFENSIVE MODE>
Things I would count as beneficial to run-time efficiency are more subtle
things, such as arranging items in large lookup tables in the descending
order that the items are most likely to be matched on, based on probability
distribution analysis versus timings based on an ordinary binary search.  Or
specifically ordering conditional statements (WHENs in EVALUATE, "cascaded"
ELSE IFs at the zero level, UNTIL conditions, etc.), in descending order of
highest probability of truth, in so far as that is discernible, rather in
any alphabetic,  numeric or other arbitrary (visually appealing?) order.

I tend to concentrate on optimizing the logic flow and on designing
efficient
data structures. One pre-production efficiency to be realized in the data
structure arena is minimization of alternate-key maintenance overhead in
indexed files. I've fought (and won!) many battles against adding extraneous
alt-keys that had
been initially proposed "because _someone_ may need it _sometime_."  Many
programmers never consider (or even know of?) the processing burden added by
alternate keys.

Anyone care to join me on the side of run-time efficiency?  Or is it to be a
relative consensus that CPU cycles are so numerous, and memory so cheap,
that it doesn't matter at all anymore?  Maybe I am getting to be (more of?)
a "dinosaur".
</OFFENSIVE MODE>

<WHO CARES MODE>
Isn't "dinosaur" what the OO and C++ whizzes call us old COBOL farts that
have any concern with such things as run-time efficiency? <g>
</WHO CARES MODE>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74b89r$a65$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net> <#2rjcZCI#GA.332@upnetnews03>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote in message <#2rjcZCI#GA.332@upnetnews03>...

While I certainly do worry about runtime efficiency, I think
that by the time you get to the coding stage, it is about 90%
decided.  The file structures and types, and the overall
methodology chosen affect it far more than the code.

Now, before everybody jumps all over my head saying
that one can certainly slow it down by writing it poorly,
I am going to qualify that.  I *am* making the assumption
that it is written by a pro that knows what they are doing.
Simple differences in style will seldom make a huge
difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
The old rule, and probably best rule is that 80 percent of the
transactions will be on just 20 percent of the file. Do what you can
to learn which 20 percent, and give that group a lift via the method
you know, or can learn.

Warren
warrens@iname.com
```

```
FWIW, I'm with you on the above. I must not have expressed myself very
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_zAa2.4807$Jl.2025155@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net> <#2rjcZCI#GA.332@upnetnews03>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote:
>
>Anyone care to join me on the side of run-time efficiency?  Or is it to be a
>relative consensus that CPU cycles are so numerous, and memory so cheap,
>that it doesn't matter at all anymore?  Maybe I am getting to be (more of?)
>a "dinosaur".

Absolutely!  I completely agree with everything you have been saying,
but you have been saying it so well, I've just been enjoying it. :-)

Like you, I am appalled at the suggestion that "efficiency doesn't matter".
Just use Winslug 9.x for a while and you will see what little regard for
efficiency costs.  Two of my office computers are top of the line PCs, a
Pentium II/400 Gateway and a Pentium II/450 Dell, both with 128MB RAM and
10+GB disk drives, hot video, 100 baseTX network, etc.  Just firing up
Notepad can take two or three seconds!  Back when I first bought a new
Compaq 386/20 in 1988, you could type in a program name and press enter,
and you couldn't get your finger off the key before the program was up.
Progress?  Ha!  Hundreds of times more CPU horsepower, yet tens of times
slower!  The cost of "efficiency doesn't matter, we can just buy faster
hardware."  A false claim!  Unless I change platforms altogether, I can't
*get* faster hardware.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36691d00.1388075@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net>`

```
On 4 Dec 1998 11:54:18 GMT, William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> wrote:


>What you say is true, Dennis, but I've never seen an example of anyone's
>shaving an hour or two off their batch run by coding an INDEX instead of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>updates, etc. 


Bill,

PMJI, I did see an example where INITIALIZE cause an immense increase in runtime.  As far
as I remember, there were to programs, A and B, where A would call B a few hundred
thousand times using a static call.

In B there was a two dimensional array of binary numbers.  Both programs were written in
VS-COBOL (MVS).  About the first instruction in the PROCEDURE DIVISION was 

	MOVE LOW-VALUES TO WS-NUM-ARRAY

When the program was converted to COBOL II (about 12 years ago or so), the programmer
doing the conversion said:  Let's use instead:

	INITIALIZE WS-NUM-ARRAY

Elapsed time for the batch program increased from several minutes to about 14 hours!!!  He
didn't quite understand, why that was happening, and checked nearly everywhere.  When they
came to the resident MVS System programmer (yours truly) I was baffled as well.  But what
do SYSPROG types do when baffled - they look at the assembly listing - and there it became
obvious rather soon:  The MOVE statement generated an MVCL assembler instruction, while
the INITIALIZE generated a loop that set all individual elements of the array.  And now
you have a loop inside a loop, which really didn't make things run faster......
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366A5F04.5929F21C@att.net>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net> <36691d00.1388075@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:

(snip)
> 
> PMJI, I did see an example where INITIALIZE cause an immense increase in runtime.  As far
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> with kind regards

Here's an example of where it made a huge difference. I would say that
this is a special circumstance, because the figurative constant
"LOW-VALUES" which is the source for the group move, has the same bit
pattern as the numerical value of binary ZERO, for all the elementary
numeric fields. Surely wouldn't have worked if the counters were packed
(although you'd probably redesign it if it were; or maybe code an
initialized view of the array that you could MOVE to it, this would
accommodate any data type).

Then again, *I* still haven't seen one <g>

Bill Lynch

PS: PMJI?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uiUHzNXI#GA.79@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<#F9HCuwH#GA.268@upnetnews05> <3667CDB7.8C1D27F9@att.net> <36691d00.1388075@news3.ibm.net>`

```

Volker Bandke wrote in message <36691d00.1388075@news3.ibm.net>...
>On 4 Dec 1998 11:54:18 GMT, William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>PMJI, I did see an example where INITIALIZE cause an immense increase in
runtime.  As far
>as I remember, there were to programs, A and B, where A would call B a few
hundred
>thousand times using a static call.
>
>In B there was a two dimensional array of binary numbers.  Both programs
were written in
>VS-COBOL (MVS).  About the first instruction in the PROCEDURE DIVISION was
>
> MOVE LOW-VALUES TO WS-NUM-ARRAY
>
>When the program was converted to COBOL II (about 12 years ago or so), the
programmer
>doing the conversion said:  Let's use instead:
>
> INITIALIZE WS-NUM-ARRAY
>
>Elapsed time for the batch program increased from several minutes to about
14 hours!!!  He
>didn't quite understand, why that was happening, and checked nearly
everywhere.  When they
>came to the resident MVS System programmer (yours truly) I was baffled as
well.  But what
>do SYSPROG types do when baffled - they look at the assembly listing - and
there it became
>obvious rather soon:  The MOVE statement generated an MVCL assembler
instruction, while
>the INITIALIZE generated a loop that set all individual elements of the
array.  And now
>you have a loop inside a loop, which really didn't make things run
faster......
>with kind regards
>
>Volker Bandke
>(BSP GmbH)

An excellent example!  Thanks for the help.

Dennis J. Minette
CCCO
```

### Paged List box

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<747ijo$jel$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
In AcuCOBOL 4.0 GT under WinNT , is it possible, when programming paged list
boxes, to give them headings for each column.

Example.

heading 1  heading2     heading 3 ... ...
__________________________________________________________
column 1 | Column 2 |     column 3   | ... ...

I thought it was, but I just can't seem to recreate it...

Rob Annandale
```

#### ↳ Re: Paged List box

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366823d0.342336894@news.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<747ijo$jel$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
On Thu, 3 Dec 1998 18:48:12 -0000, "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
wrote:

>In AcuCOBOL 4.0 GT under WinNT , is it possible, when programming paged list
>boxes, to give them headings for each column.

Not with a paged list-box; the demonstration program uses labels above the
listbox to achieve this effect.

I would recommend that you take a look at the grid control if you need this sort
of functionality. It provides considerably more flexibility than the simpler
paged list-box.

# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
```

- **References:** `<3666E914.3D89@sunto.ing.unisi.it> <Nuw92.3899$td3.16475@news12.ispnews.com> <366710c9.31236094@news.enter.net>`

```
On Thu, 03 Dec 1998 22:31:04 GMT, rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe) wrote:

>I think that the person was looking for a VS COBOL compatible compiler

Does VS COBOL = WANG or IBM? IIRC, they both have something by that name.

>for Linux.  I may be wrong, but I don't think that Acu is VS COBOL
>compatible....any Acu-ites out there wanna set me straight on this
>one?

Not directly - we have compatibility options built into the compiler for VAX
COBOL, RM/COBOL, and ICOBOL in addition to the Acucobol extensions and COBOL-74
and 85. I'm not sure how much of an overlap there might be.

That said, if we're talking about WANG, we do have a number of customers who've
migrated. There are some conversion houses (Unicon, Task) that do this quite
frequently - drop a note to syseng@acucorp.com if you need the full contact
info.

# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com> <adamsc@ibm.net>
```

<a id="t-dae77ebf14"></a>
## Thread: GetUserName in NetExpress — 5 msgs

### GetUserName in NetExpress

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36669F61.58E48F10@IMN.nl>`

```
Dear Readers.

We, my collegue (Martin) and I (The Frog) keep getting problems when we
try to "read" the userid in our Win'95 application that we are building
using NetExpress v2.0 from Micro Focus. It is probably a
misunderstanding of the Windows (32 bit) API, but our implementation of
the GetUserName call fails. GetLastError yields error 1245 which is
documented in the Win32 SDK as:

" The operation being requested was not performed because the user has
not logged on to the network. The specified service does not exist.".

We reduced the problem source to one little small program. Please help
us making it function!
Martin & Huib


        $SET CASE
        $SET NOFOLDCALLNAME
        Identification division.
        Program-id. GetUserInfo.
        Environment division.
        Configuration section.
        Special-names.
            Call-convention 74 is Win32API.
        Data division.
        Working-storage section.
        77  ReturnCode                  pic X(02)  comp-5.
        77  LastErrorCode               pic X(04)  comp-5.
        77  UserId                      pic X(21).
        77  NullPos                     pic X(02)  comp-5.
        Procedure division.
        Main.
       *--- Make Windows 32 bit API available
            Call "COB32API"
       *--- Ask Windows for the user-id
            Move x"00" to UserId(1:1)
            Call Win32API "GetUserNameA"
                          using  by reference UserId
                                 by value     20 size 4
                       returning              ReturnCode
       *--- Check & show result
            Display "Rc = ", ReturnCode
            If (ReturnCode = zero)
            Then
                Call Win32API "GetLastError" returning LastErrorCode
                Display "Errcode = ", LastErrorCode
            Else
                Initialize NullPos
                Inspect UserId tallying NullPos
                    for characters before initial low-value
                Display "UserId = '" UserId(1:NullPos) "'"
            End-if
       *--- Quit processing
            Goback
            .
        End program GetUserInfo.
```

#### ↳ Re: GetUserName in NetExpress

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hrx92.133$hV5.274750@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<36669F61.58E48F10@IMN.nl>`

```
I don't know where you got the prototype for the call to GetUserName but my
SDK reference says the call is supposed to look like this:

 01  szUserName          pic x(80) value low-value.
 01  iUserNameLen        pic 9(9) comp-5 value 0.
 01  bResult             pic 9(9) comp-5 value 0.
   88  RESULT-TRUE                       value 1.
   88  RESULT-FALSE                      value 0.
 01  iError              pic 9(9) comp-5 value 0.
 01  iErrorDisp          pic 9(9)        value 0.

     Move LENGTH OF szUserName to iUserNameLen
     Call APIentry "GetUserNameA" using
         by reference szUserName
         by reference iUserNameLen
         returning    bResult
     End-Call
     If RESULT-TRUE
         Display szUserName
     Else
         Call APIentry "GetLastError"
             returning       iError
         End-Call
         Move iError to iErrorDisp
         Display iError
     End-If
     .



COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote in message <36669F61.58E48F10@IMN.nl>...
>Dear Readers.
>
<<SNIP>>
>--
>Dut: Vandaag is de eerste dag van de rest van je leven! Denk eens na!
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GetUserName in NetExpress

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667A202.17ADC0C4@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<36669F61.58E48F10@IMN.nl> <Hrx92.133$hV5.274750@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
Karl Wagner wrote:

> I don't know where you got the prototype for the call to GetUserName but my
> SDK reference says the call is supposed to look like this:

8< [snipped, see org. msg.]

Success!!! Thanks Karl

In essence, two errors where made by me:
1) I passed the length by value i.s.o. by reference.
2) The returncode data item was 2 i.s.o. 4 bytes long.

The LENGTH OF special register (IBM extension lso supported in NetExpress) was
changed to FUNCTION LENGTH OF to get as standards conforming as possible. So
now the following is the resulting *working* source:

$SET CASE
$SET NOFOLDCALLNAME
Identification division.
Program-id. GetUserInfo.
Environment division.
Configuration section.
Special-names.
    Call-convention 74 is Win32API
    .
Data division.
Working-storage section.
77  UserId                      pic X(21).
77  UserIdLen                   pic X(04)  comp-5.
77  ReturnCode                  pic X(04)  comp-5.
77  LastErrorCode               pic X(04)  comp-5.
77  NullPos                     pic X(02)  comp-5.
Procedure division.
Main.
--- Make Windows 32 bit API available
    Call "COB32API"
--- Ask Windows for the user-id
    Move x"00" to UserId(1:1)
    Move function length (UserId) to UserIdLen
    Call Win32API "GetUserNameA"
                  using  by reference UserId
                         by reference UserIdLen
               returning              ReturnCode
--- Check & show result
    Display "Rc = ", ReturnCode
    If (ReturnCode = zero)
    Then
        Call Win32API "GetLastError" returning LastErrorCode
        Display "Errcode = ", LastErrorCode
    Else
        Display "UserId = '" UserId(1:UserIdLen - 1) "'"
    End-if
--- Quit processing
    Goback
    .
End program GetUserInfo.

End Frog Errors.
```

#### ↳ Re: GetUserName in NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981203162640.03741.00001514@ng119.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36669F61.58E48F10@IMN.nl>`

```
Hi,

I have a working example of this API if you would like me to send it to you
please e-mail me.

Thanks,
Bob
```

- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667A280.31A96CD@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<36669F61.58E48F10@IMN.nl> <19981203162640.03741.00001514@ng119.aol.com>`

#### ↳ Re: Stupid COBOL tricks

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3666da35.244317139@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<fXn62.3961$I86.23075919@news.rdc1.tx.home.com>`

```
On Tue, 24 Nov 1998 01:26:35 GMT, "Ralph Coleman" <rocolema@home.com>
wrote:

>If you follow the rule that the only period coded in a paragraph is the
>mandated period at the end, a 'NEXT SENTENCE' statement exits the paragraph.

Dangerous. At some point, another programmer who didn't follow that
rule comes along and sticks  extra code in with a period and your code
no longer exits the paragraph.

I'd suggest evaluate and perform would be a better route.
```

<a id="t-6652599d88"></a>
## Thread: Hypercobol erratum link — 1 msgs

### RE: Hypercobol erratum link

- **From:** Thierry bricman <tbricman@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36671BBE.539D5182@bigfoot.com>`

```
Hi,

Have you ever had to work on old COBOL programs that were both not documented
nor explicit ? And with no debugger available because hardware and system are
too old ?
Well here is a small shareware I wrote that can save you a lot of time in
understanding how the hell do those wild and unstructured programs work !
You need two things first:
1 - A way to import COBOL source codes as ascii text files on a PC running
Windows (ftp or whatever).
2 - Winword (I tested Word 97 but Word 95 should work fine. I need feedback on
this matter)

What Hypcobsh.exe does:

It reads your COBOL ascii source code and makes a .RTF file of it.
After loading the RTF file with Winword, you will see that colored hypertext
links have been added in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
You will be able to use the Winword web tool bar to navigate into your source
code as you would do in a HTML text.
Try it. It's easy and self explicit.

you can download it here : http://www.multimania.com/nirrey/shareware/HYPCOBOL.ZIP
```

```
Would you also deny acceptance of email originated in MS Outlook 98 or MS
Outlook Express or Windows Messaging?  That would make as much sense to me
as including all addresses in the MSN.com domain in your spam filter.  I've
```

```
On Sat, 5 Dec 1998 03:34:09 -0500, "Dennis J. Minette"
<dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote:


>
>Would you also deny acceptance of email originated in MS Outlook 98 or MS

No - not that fussy.

> That would make as much sense to me
>as including all addresses in the MSN.com domain in your spam filter. 

It's a political thing but it does sort out the clueless. I actually
do accept msn.com and aol.com mail but it goes into my non-important
mail folder and I look through it at my leisure.

> I've
>been using MSN.com for internet access, along with Compuserve.com, for
>several years and it has been my experience that I get very, very little
>SPAM originated via the MSN.com domain.  But you are definitely on the right
>track about hotmail.com, which was purchased by M$ a few months back.

Both of which are why I don't accept hotmail at all. M$ have been
buying up quite a few companies like hotmail. LinkExchange being
another important one of late. Rumour has it that hotmail use Linux
for their servers although M$ wanted them to use NT servers - the NT
servers weren't reliable enough. ;-)

>
>BTW, I just read that hotmail now has a total of over 30 million users, and
>I definitely agree that way too many of them are prolific spammers.

...and pirates and hackers. For instance, a recent case was a hotmail
account being used inside a trojan horse. The trojan would email your
account details to a hotmail account. 700 accounts and passwords were
stolen this way before the author of the Amiga TCP/IP stack that was
being accused of piracy found out about it.
```

<a id="t-3a03f26d53"></a>
## Thread: how to filter a string ? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### how to filter a string ?

- **From:** merlyn@iaehv.nl (Jarl Wijman)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>`

```

Hi,

i have the following code of line :

INSPECT VAR-RIGHT-JUST REPLACING ALL " " BY "0".

VAR-RIGHT contains something like 
"                    64583932185735243964"

(20 spaces, and a number, 9 or 20 positions long)

What i would like it to do though, is replace all spaces by nothing.
Remove the leading spaces.

Only thing i thought of was making two loops, one to determine
the position of the last space, and one to determine the position
of the last character in the variable.

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

TIA,

	Jarl Wijman
	J.Wijman@face.nl
```

#### ↳ Re: how to filter a string ?

- **From:** B.Ewert@t-online.de (Ewert)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<749f7t$vbm$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Hi,
how about that ?

       identification division.
       program-id. demo.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 var-right pic x(40) value
              "                    64583932185735      ".
       01 left-pos  pic s9(4) binary value 1.
       01 right-pos pic s9(4) binary value 1.
      *
       procedure division.
           Inspect var-right Tallying left-pos for leading Space
           Inspect var-right(left-pos:) tallying right-pos for
                   Characters before space
           Display left-pos
           display right-pos
           display var-right(left-pos:right-pos - 1) "/"
           .
           stop run.

If I understood your problem right here is a LOOP-less solution.

Bernd



Jarl Wijman schrieb in Nachricht <3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>...
>
>Hi,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: how to filter a string ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HuP92.734$Lx2.1693@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Assuming the text contains no embedded spaces, you could do:

    MOVE SPACES TO TEMP-VAR-RIGHT-JUST
    UNSTRING VAR-RIGHT-JUST DELIMITED BY SPACE
        INTO TEMP-VAR-RIGHT-JUST
    MOVE TEMP-VAR-RIGHT-JUST TO VAR-RIGHT-JUST

Or, skip the last move and use it directly.

The UNSTRING will skip the leading delimiters (SPACEs) copying
the remaining characters.

A similar question appeared in the newsgroup several months ago.
In that case, a database was being used and the DB function LTRIM
was determined to be the best approach for that particular problem.
If you are working with a DB, you might also consider LTRIM.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

Jarl Wijman wrote in message <3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>...
>
>Hi,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: how to filter a string ?

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667CEFA.D740B14E@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Jarl Wijman wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not clear (to me, anyway) exactly what you're trying to accomplish.
Would you be better off using the TALLYING option instead of REPLACING?
This would give you a count of the spaces, but leave them intact.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: how to filter a string ?

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3667d5ed.308757009@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
On Fri, 04 Dec 1998 08:49:58 GMT, merlyn@iaehv.nl (Jarl Wijman) wrote:

>
>Hi,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>of the last character in the variable.

Couple of ways (at least)

INSPECT .... TALLYING ..... BEFORE INITIAL CHARACTER
MOVE var-right-just(x:) to temp-item

or 

move 0 to x
perform until var-right-just not = space
    add 1 to x
end-perform
MOVE var-right-just(x:) to temp-item

then move temp-item back.
```

#### ↳ Re: how to filter a string ?

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0009178b@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<3667a0db.2174653@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Am 04.12.98 schrieb merlyn # iaehv.nl zum Thema how to filter a string ?:

Dear Jarl,

JW> INSPECT VAR-RIGHT-JUST REPLACING ALL " " BY "0".
JW>
JW> VAR-RIGHT contains something like
JW> "                    64583932185735243964"
JW>
JW> (20 spaces, and a number, 9 or 20 positions long)
JW>
JW> What i would like it to do though, is replace all spaces by nothing.
JW> Remove the leading spaces.

Determine the position of the first non-blank character an do a MOVE like

MOVE VAR-RIGHT (POSITION:FUNCTION LENGTH (VAR-RIGHT) - POSITION + 1)
     TO VAR-RIGHT.

The statement

UNSTRING VAR-RIGHT
         DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
         INTO DUMMY ANOTHER-DUMMY

MOVE ANOTHER-DUMMY TO VAR-RIGHT

should do more or less the same.

If the number in VAR-RIGHT were not longer than 18 digits, you could  
redefine it with PIC Z

01 VAR-RIGHT                    PIC X(40).
01 FILLER REDEFINES VAR-RIGHT.
   05 FILLER                    PIC X(22).
   05 VAR-RIGHT-Z               PIC Z(18).

and MOVE VAR-RIGHT-Z into a numeric field.

Best regards

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```

<a id="t-a3785d8c7b"></a>
## Thread: Considering a new language....... — 10 msgs

### Considering a new language.......

- **From:** "Jaye Snidow" <Jaye.Snidow@123EDS.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Greetings.


I have posted questions here in the past where I have gotten an abundance of
well thought out responses and ideas so I thought I would pose this to the
"old-timers" in the group. I am considering taking up Assembler to add to my
arsenal. I have been called a sadist( or worse) by some of the more
experienced programmers at my office. After researching this a little I see
it to be in high demand with very few people programming in it. Is it worth
my time to do this? I am trying to increase my range and value in the
mainframe market. So far I have not found any classes being taught in my
area and would like some suggestions for books that I can use. Thanks in
advance.


Jaye
```

#### ↳ Re: Considering a new language.......

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36692fd9.13537166@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Jaye,

I can't say much about the "Market" value of learning assembler, but
it WILL increase your inteligence as a programmer.  Having an
understanding of what happens at the low level can be very benificial
when debugging a high level language, or trying to optimize one.   It
will also broaden your knowledge of operating systems and other stuff.

Being a sadist, I also think assembler is fun, and a challenge.  I've
only taken a couple of courses, and done some on my owm, but it has
helped me many times.

Paul

On Fri, 4 Dec 1998 11:23:25 -0500, "Jaye Snidow"
<Jaye.Snidow@123EDS.com> wrote:

>Greetings.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Considering a new language.......

- **From:** "Jaye Snidow" <Jaye.Snidow@123EDS.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<749kl6$7us$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <36692fd9.13537166@news.enteract.com>`

```
Thanks for all the various comments on this subject. Based on the responses
I am convinced that this is a good idea. When I used the term "market value"
I wasn't necessarily inferring that just knowing another language would
increase my salary or anything. I feel that the more a programmer knows
about the inner workings of the architecture/systems the more value he/she
can provide to his or her client/employer when it comes to providing
solutions to business needs and problem resolution. This, in conjunction
with dilligence and dedication, will increase compensation in the long run,
not just having another item to add to a resume. Thanks again for your
responses.

Jaye
PAL3000 wrote in message <36692fd9.13537166@news.enteract.com>...
>Jaye,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Considering a new language.......

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<749ev4$44r$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Jaye Snidow wrote in message <7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>...
>Greetings.
>
>I have posted questions here in the past where I have gotten an abundance
of
>well thought out responses and ideas so I thought I would pose this to the
>"old-timers" in the group. I am considering taking up Assembler to add to
my
>arsenal. I have been called a sadist( or worse) by some of the more
>experienced programmers at my office. After researching this a little I see
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>advance.


I would suspect that assembler would not increase your value
in the workplace much anymore.  Having said that, however, I
would then urge you to learn assembler anyway!

I do not think that anybody ever really understands how a
computer works until they have coded in assembler.  It does
not matter that much which assembler ... keeping in mind that
each processor uses a different assembler.  The value
is in learning the underlying structure on a computer, and
an appreciation for the general architecture of the basic
Turing/VonNeuman machine.
```

```
Ralph Jones wrote:
> 
> Ditto to the following.  In the 33 years I've been at this I've noticed the
> really good programers can 'program the metal'.

Plus, it's an enormous asset in debugging, especially in a case where
Abend-Aid (or the like) isn't available. I've always preferred
understanding what was going on.

Bill Lynch (also 33 years, Hi, Ralph)

PS: If you mean IBM S/360 Assembler, and its successors, I think it will
increase your market value. Very few Assembler people left due to aging
& downsizing and, IMHO, demand will remain high for at least the next
couple of years.
```

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<4601323D01335256.058C830EBE0B601A.6E11FF5BB5C28CB5@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <749ev4$44r$1@news.igs.net> <3668A3B3.C54C77C4@ibm.net> <366A60F4.1E1DA2EF@att.net>`

```
enlightened us:

>Ralph Jones wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>couple of years.

To add on to Bill's points, with which I totally agree, learning IBM
mainframe assembler can also lead to a career change should you wish
to leave application programming and go into system's programming.  

When I went to college, assembler was a required course for my
discipline.  Now, I don't believe it is even taught as an elective in
many colleges (much like Cobol, recently).

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Microsoft Windows:  Proof that P.T. Barnum was correct.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Considering a new language.......

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be2092$7ab13360$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<7492ar$qvt$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <749ev4$44r$1@news.igs.net>`

```
>I do not think that anybody ever really understands how a
>computer works until they have coded in assembler.  It does
>not matter that much which assembler ... keeping in mind that
>each processor uses a different assembler. 

Definitely.  When I started, it was with Fortran, Cobol and 360BAL.
I added CDC-6000 series assembly when I worked with one of those,
then PDP-8 assembly.  I've done serious amounts of 68000 and x86
assembly since then.  

Learning one machine's assembly language will teach you lots.  Learning
a second one, especially if for a very different kind of machine, will
teach
you more.  It will help you to code better in any HLL you work with also.

Depending on where you are working 360/370 assembler, PowerPC or
x86 could be good choices.  Although x86 is arcane, not clean like 68000 or
360.

Also you get to learn the various machine architecture paradigms
at a much more detailed level than you normally see in any HLL:
(examples only, not exhaustive)
stack oriented
register oriented
memory to memory oriented
one operand / two operand /  three operand
and computational paradigms (again examples)
binary vs. decimal
sign magnitude vs ones complement vs twos complement &c
integer vs fixed point vs floating point

Enjoy!
```

- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<hpK92.4210$td3.18442@news12.ispnews.com>`

```
On Fri, 4 Dec 1998 00:24:20 -0500, "Ken" <attitude@erie.net>
enlightened us:

>I need to find out how/how to pass files.  Every source I locate indicates
>that there is a way to do it, yet it is beyond the scope of the source
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Pass files?  From where to where?  From one program to another?  In
the mainframe world, look in the Cobol manual for verb Call
'program-name' using 'parameters'.


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Microsoft Windows:  Proof that P.T. Barnum was correct.

 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RFI: Please?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981205114215.19083.00000309@ng-fq1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<68FFB626222823C6.2E22B33A3C78FB45.D0185D232302EDB9@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
You can, syntactically, include a file name as one of the parameters of the
CALL statement. For example:

call 'subra' using work-area, infile


where 'infile' is the name in your select and on your fd. 

But there is no way to 'catch' the file. There is no 'linkage section' for
files, in other words.

Alternatively, look at the EXTERNAL attribute for files. You can share files
without passing them.

Cheers,

Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-c7d6d85b97"></a>
## Thread: occurs depending on weirdness — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### occurs depending on weirdness

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3668AA53.270@sprynet.com>`

```
First of all, let me state that I have already solved this problem  This
said, let you show you what my problem was.  Here is a copybook known
as CVCMUCHR:

01  CHR-USER-RECORD.
    05  CHR-FIXED-DATA.
        10  CHR-MISC-DATA            PIC X(1298).
        10  CHR-DEPENDING            PIC 9(3) COMP-3.
    05  CHR-VARIABLE-DATA            OCCURS 10
                                     DEPENDING ON CHR-DEPENDING.
        10  CHR-ACCOUNT              PIC 9(18).
        10  CHR-ACCOUNT-TYPE         PIC X(3).
        10  CHR-MORE-DATA            PIC X(17).

(CHR-MISC-DATA and CHR-MORE-DATA aren't really the data names, but I
didn't want to include all of the various fields.)

Now here's the thing.  These records are kept in a variable length file
call CHF (cardholder file).  The records vary in size from 1300 bytes
(for when there are no accounts attatched) to 1680 bytes (when there are
10 accounts attached).  Up until now, all of the programs that access this
file are written in DOS/VS COBOL.

I wrote a VS COBOL II program to read this file from a tape, forward some
dates by a certain number of days, and write the resulting record out to
a VSAM file.

The problem was that in some cases I would get an error status code of
'44'.  According the the COBOL II manual this means that I was trying to
write a record that was either smaller than the smallest defined record
for my FD, or larger than the largest.

What happened is in COBOL II, if you use "OCCURS ## TIMES DEPENDING ON .."
rather than "OCCURS #1 TO #2 TIMES DEPENDING ON.." it defaults the lower
end to *1*, when what I needed it to be was ZERO.  So what I had to
do when copying the copybook in was say "COPY CVCMUCHR REPLACING
==10 TIMES== BY ==0 TO 10 TIMES==."

So now it works.  Wierd thing is, I looked in the DOS/VS manual and it
appears to me to not even *allow* you to say "OCCURS ## TIMES DEPENDING
ON .."  But apparently it *does* allow that, and it treats it like
"OCCURS 0 TO ## TIMES DEPENDING ON ..", while COBOL II treats it like
"OCCURS 1 TO ## TIMES DEPENDING ON .."  How irritating!  In fact, the
DOS/VS COBOL manual we have not only says you have to use the "TO"
format, but that the low boundry must be a POSITIVE number!  (In other
words, in cannot be zero (or a negative number)).  Again, the manual
seems to be incorrect.

In any case, I will probably change the copybook to say "0 TO 10 TIMES".
That is, assuming DOS/VS will allow this!  Yikes.

Any comments?  I wasted a few hours today figuring this out, and a few
more hours complaining about it!  :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: occurs depending on weirdness

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74aagj$1ur$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3668AA53.270@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank,

    You can specify an unsigned integer (defined in WORKING-STORAGE) and use
a COBOL2 FD option, that basically reads 'RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM
xxxx to xxxx DEPENDING ON unsigned integer'. If you use a signed integer,
you'll get a COMPILE Error. For whatever reason, Big-Iron COBOL generates an
ALR (Add Logical Register) instruction (instead of a regular Add Register)
in the OPEN and CLOSE Macro expansion (check the PMAP) when calculating or
setting a record length. ALR implies that both registers contain unsigned
values (High-Order bit turned-off). Therefore, a signed integer throws a
real monkey wrench into the mix (It COULD be negative. High-Order bit
turned-on). The silence of this subtlety has been deafening in the COBOL
community. Go figure.

    Anyhow so much for my ranting. After you've read a record, the unsigned
integer is set to the actual length of the variable length record. Before
you write a record, set the unsigned integer to the desired record length.
I'm not sure if this will solve your dilemma, but it may lay some ground
rules for future designs and considerations.

    BTW, did you ever come across that Houston phone number?

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: occurs depending on weirdness

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366A3735.1E02@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3668AA53.270@sprynet.com> <74aagj$1ur$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
WOB wrote:
> 
>     You can specify an unsigned integer (defined in WORKING-STORAGE) and use
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> rules for future designs and considerations.

Hmm, well, I already solved the problem, as I said in the original
message.  I changed the "...OCCURS 10 TIMES DEPENDING ON..." to
"...OCCURS 0 TO 10 TIMES DEPENDING ON..."  I didn't know about the
RECORD VARYIES...DEPENDING ON, though.  Sounds interesting.  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: MFocusNetExpress, AcuCobol-GT, or PowerCobol V4

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00

```
On Fri, 4 Dec 1998 03:45:12, tjtony@telestream.com (TONY JOHNSON) 
wrote:

> Hello all,
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

For conversion efforts I would recommend using a product called COBOL 
SP2 from Flexus:  Http://www.flexus.com.

Using your current compiler, you can create 16 bit Windows 
applications.

If you obtain a 32 bit compiler, such as NetExpress you can use the 32
bit version of the Flexus tool and create  Windows 95/98/NT/2000 
applications.

E-mail me privately and I'll send you some writeups I have on the 
differences between the different GUI tools used by the different 
compiler vendors.  With SP2 you will not be "slaved" to a single 
compiler.  They support virtually every PC COBOL compiler.

If you want to see a working tutorial, you can also buy my new book - 
the last chapter is devoted to taking one of the character based 
examples used in the book and making a GUI out of it using SP2 and the
Fujitsu Compiler.  

Let me stress again - the SP2 product is not compiler specific.

For my book - visit my website listed below.

-------------------------
Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
tdrapeau@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<749d0c$mkm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Hello there,

>numeric variable? I am using MicroFocus COBOL 4.0 on an HP-UX 11 system.

I think only the machine, though I may be mistaken.  On the

correct answer now is address space.
```

```
Amazon.com currently has no reviews.  It seems to be structured, in an
overall sort of way, like COBOL Unleashed, or at least seems to cover a lot
of the same material.  None of the topics in any of the chapters seemed to
discuss anything more advanced than in COBOL Unleashed.

<http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/0471314811/qid=912812049/sr=1-6/002-
2268427-9544459/>
```

## Thread: calling sub-progs — 2 msgs

### calling sub-progs

- **From:** "Ken" <attitude@erie.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MS92.4512$td3.19362@news12.ispnews.com>`

```
More info is needed.

I'm sorry, to sub-programs, passing the contents of entire files.
I have found "pass"ing or calling using the phrase "...BY DESCRIPTOR..."
i.e.      CALL "sub-program" BY DESCRIPTOR ..........(everything leaves me
there)
maybe...                                                                  FD
name or (what I want to find out...)
or
file-name.

Passing by any means is OK (by reference or directly), it is to pass to a
printing sub-program so it will not be altered.

```

#### ↳ Re: calling sub-progs

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36688F67.28ED@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<9MS92.4512$td3.19362@news12.ispnews.com>`

```
Ken wrote:
> 
> More info is needed.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

If you literally want to pass the *contents* of a file, you have to
load it into memory, such as in WORKING-STORAGE, and then pass the
place where you stored it.

However, I don't think that's what you want to do.  I think you want
to open a file in one module, and read it (or write to it) in another.

One approach is to declare the file as EXTERNAL.  Then you can access
it from any module which declares it similarly.

I prefer to confine the file to a single access module, and provide 
multiple entry points (or a single entry point with multiple action 
codes) to perform various operations.  This approach provides better
encapsulation -- any changes to the file structure, or any special
processing which must be applied to it, are confined to a single
source file.

Both approaches are limited in that you can't switch files from one
call to the next.  You can't, for example:

	CALL "PRINTHDR" USING FILE-A

...in one paragraph and 

	CALL "PRINTHDR" USING FILE-B

in another, where A and B are any arbitrary files.  For this kind
of flexibility you would have to write your access module in some
other language such as C or Assembler, unless your flavor of COBOL
provides some kind of vendor extension (which is what BY DESCRIPTOR
looks like).

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

<a id="t-736a68e76b"></a>
## Thread: Paranoid? Savvy? — 1 msgs

### Paranoid? Savvy?

- **From:** jimausterman@email.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<748794$m79$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Did anyone else but me,
see the programmer flee?

To the heat of the desert sand,
with a 44 magnum in his hand?

My comrad, they say, has gone too far,
packing tiny tid-bits into little jars.

We'll live for a year, as he tells of the crash,
if you were savvy, you'd make your dash.

I'm sorry my comrad, for you and your wife,
my paranoid mind already lives that life.

So, I think I'll just stay here, and not take to flight,
just writting lines of cobol by dim candlelights.




-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

## Thread: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun? — 50 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** coboldoomster@hotmail.com (COBOL Doomster)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com>`

```
I am a COBOL programmer doing some major Y2K work...

I think that around Y2K the world will fall back into savagery...
(thanks to you, the boneheaded "old-timer" COBOL dumbasses who made so
many mistakes, which I am being wonderfully paid to remove now)

So, I am now actively preparing for Y2K.

I am considering buying a preban Yugoslavian AK-47 in .308. It is
"National Match".

Do you think that it is a good idea? I never owned guns before. Money
is no object as my COBOL work pays a lot.
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com>`

```
no, .308 is not a common caliber for combat.  it was around ww1 and ww2
but not now.  if you insist on a com-block weapon, go for an sks.  as
for nato configs, stick with .223/5.56mm
and for a sidearm, the obvious choice is a m1911a1 .45acp, tho i
personally prefer double-action so shell out some bucks for a good one.
if you go for a 7.62x39 com-block, be sure to get the sks sporter which
accepts ak-model mags.  this way, you can equip the 75 or 90 round drums
which you can load and store without spring degredation.
ok, now that everyone thinks i am a nut, my second bit of advice to this
person is to try to help fix y2k problems before they bring you to this
level.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** "Geoffrey L. Hardin" <geoffh@abcs.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com>`

```
Uh, "skidmike":

skidmike wrote:

> no, .308 is not a common caliber for combat.

Yes, the .308 Winchester (i.e., 7.62x51mm NATO) IS a common caliber for
combat.  M14's are currently in use by the military as sidearms to some
groups, all M60's use it, all M240's use it, many coaxial machine guns on
armored vehicles use it, etc.


> it was around ww1 and ww2 but not now.

No, it wasn't.  The 7.62x51mm NATO round was adopted in the 1950's when the
military took the M14 as their standard sidearm.  This was after WW's I and
II.  The 30-06 was the standard military caliber at those times.

>  if you insist on a com-block weapon, go for an sks.  as
> for nato configs, stick with .223/5.56mm
> and for a sidearm, the obvious choice is a m1911a1 .45acp, tho i
> personally prefer double-action so shell out some bucks for a good one.

While I would argue that the "obvious" choice for a beginning shooter is the
.45 ACP (handgun choices are, in my opinion, something that should be made
on the individual's personal situation, what fits their hand best, what
recoil they can handle, etc.), I would be interested in which .45 you
recommend.

> if you go for a 7.62x39 com-block, be sure to get the sks sporter which
> accepts ak-model mags.  this way, you can equip the 75 or 90 round drums
> which you can load and store without spring degredation.

While you make several recommendations, would you care to share with us your
reasoning?

> ok, now that everyone thinks i am a nut, my second bit of advice to this
> person is to try to help fix y2k problems before they bring you to this
> level.

  It's a tad too late for that, now.

Please send a copy of any response to my email address as well as posting to
the newsgroup.

Geoffrey L. Hardin,
geoffh@abcs.com
```

```
Latest (Uh, _Infantry_ or _Special Warfare_, can't recall which I saw it
in) is that all M60s will be phased out, in the active Army at least, and
replaced with the M240B.  Infantry, SOCOM and Engineers operating with
Infantry will be getting the initial issues.

I've handled a few USMC-issued M240s, not sure if their version is the
same as the Army's M240B.  Look to be pretty much a standard MAG-58, but
with plastic furniture (guess that dates the MAG-58s I've used).  I think
there's work being done on reducing the weight further.
```

```
going back to the original question in  this thread:  i remember seeing
a yugo .308/7.62x51
the retail was about 1500.
sorry, but i was thinking 30.06 for some reason originally.  still, i
have to insist that .308 is less acceptable in terms of "survivalism"
than .223 or 7.62x39
in terms of investment, i'd definitely say buy that yugo! (damn! did i
actually use that phrase?)  but in terms of a handy weapon, no that yugo
is not imho fieldable.  the only one i've seen around here was about
1500.  about the same as a colt ar-15.  i rest my case.
furthermore, as one who owns and shoots regularly a full-auto-converted
sks (7.62x39 caliber) i can honestly tell you that in the kalishnikov
(spelling?) receiver, a .308 would be too violent to control in
full-auto fire.  trust me, i've fired off a 30-round mag at a road sign
from a car in full-auto mode, it ain't like the movies.  burst-fire?
getting off a 3-round burst is a motherf*****.  semi-auto?  maybe.  but
the envelope is just plain too light for the power of that round.  to be
perfectly honest, if you want an investment, buy it.  i would love to
get a few rounds off on one.  probably the gunpowder-equivalent of an
orgasm to me.
```

```
the survivalist, being able to do anything a rifle can be called on to do, 
short of elephants. 

 can honestly tell you that in the kalishnikov

 trust me, i've fired off a 30-round mag at a road sign
```

```
The 223 just isn't a good hunting round. Now, In my other post I said 
that the 7.62x39 is cheap enough that it may actually be more popular.
Hell, with 50 million AKs in the world, it most certainly is. 
Buy a million rounds! :) At any rate, A .223 should not be one's onely gun.
IF you plan to hunt at all. The 223 will create titanic wounds in a human,
seeing as how we have out vitals close to the surface, are
relatively soft animals, and are thin, maybe 16 inches wide? But with a larger animal, 
even if it weighs the same, it doesn't have the same effect because it's built
in a differnt way. The 223 doesn't have the mass to drop large game.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<3674F824.5E43E8F1@flash.net>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3J3d2.3492$Ql4.1186646@news2.atl>`

```
Here we go again!  My brother and I shot and killed  seven deer with one shot each from a
Mini-14 or a Ranch Rifle.  They dropped on the spot each time and we never had to track
them.  The shortest range was about 40 yards, and the longest was 257 paces, which hit the
deer in the spine.  A deer has rather small rib bones and the .223 went through them quite
easily every time.  In the chest cavity there was massive trauma because the bullet usually
blew up.  We were using hollow point target bullets most of the time, which are not
recommended for hunting because of their frangibility.  Hunting bullets would have held
together even better, possibly permitting shots that we refused to take.  We only took
standing, broadside shots.  I would imagine that FMJ would easily penetrate deer on
broadside shots.  Humans and deer have comparable size torsos, and if a caliber is
effective on humans, it will probably work on deer about the same.  Is the .223 the best
whitetail cartridge?  No, probably not, but it will kill deer if you place the bullet in
the vitals.

GreyGhost wrote:

> In article <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, gene_s@my-dejanews.com Didst belch out...
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>  GreyGhost
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Mershon_Farms@ozip.net (Ace)
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<3675bc29.3510314@news.ozip.net>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3J3d2.3492$Ql4.1186646@news2.atl> <3674F824.5E43E8F1@flash.net>`

```
I am sure that a 223 is sufficient to kill deer easily even at
extended ranges.  However the range is best kept to less than 200yds
to insure a quick kill (so you don't have to track it).  

Deer poachers in my area prefer 22LR at close range.  With a good shot
the deer drops quickly and there is little noise compared to a high
power rifle or shotgun.  I do not recommend the 22LR for deer however.

My choices for deer hunting.  If I expect close range (less than 100
yards - brush hunting) - mini-14 (223).  If I am still hunting -
Sweedish M-38 Mauser (6.5X55).  If I am dog hunting with friends (my
usual hunt) - M1A (308).

BTW Lehman, what weights do the Barns X 22 bullets available in?

Thx
      Ace
PS My opinion is worth just what you paid for it.
On Mon, 14 Dec 1998 11:36:40 GMT, Lehman3 <lehman3@flash.net> wrote:

>Here we go again!  My brother and I shot and killed  seven deer with one shot each from a
>Mini-14 or a Ranch Rifle.  They dropped on the spot each time and we never had to track
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Lehman3 <lehman3@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<36771795.3565BE9D@flash.net>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3J3d2.3492$Ql4.1186646@news2.atl> <3674F824.5E43E8F1@flash.net> <3675bc29.3510314@news.ozip.net>`

```
The .223 is not my preferred choice for a deer cartridge; I was just pointing out that it will
do its part if you the shooter does his.  I really like a .243 with 87 gr. bullets, a .308 with
125 gr. bullets or a 7mm Mag with 165 Nosler Partition bullets.  Those loads should cover the
deer spectrum from smaller Texas whitetails to larger Northern deer without too much problem.
If you'll notice, I prefer light, for their caliber, bullets in .243 and .308.  I prefer the
bullet to shed most of its energy inside the deer instead of against the trees or whatever is on
the other side of it.  I have not used the Barnes X bullets, but the Barnes company has a
website at www.barnesbullets.com/   I can't comment on their use on deer, without having tried
them, but they look like they should work similar to the Nosler Partition bullets.

Ace wrote:

> I am sure that a 223 is sufficient to kill deer easily even at
> extended ranges.  However the range is best kept to less than 200yds
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Lehman3 <lehman3@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<367719F6.DC11594E@flash.net>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3J3d2.3492$Ql4.1186646@news2.atl> <3674F824.5E43E8F1@flash.net> <Apld2.77$uU3.194436@news4.atl>`

```
I've shot deer with a .375 H&H Magnum, that did not kill it, because of poor shot placement.
I admit it, I made a poor shot.  I didn't lose the deer because the shot nearly took off the
front leg.  It was facing me, and the shot hit the shoulder, destroyed it and just creased
the skin along the side and re-entered again at the rear hip where it destroyed it too. Like
I said the deer was facing me, and I guess had its body in a "C" sort of turn.  Anyway, if
you F*** Up any caliber will just wound deer.  I finished off that deer with a .22 to the
head.  I don't say the .223 is the best deer cartridge, I think either a .243 or a .308 class
cartridge is just about ideal, but if you have a .223 and you see a deer within reasonable
range, then you should be able to have venison that night.

GreyGhost wrote:

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** CogitoErgoZoom@GeoCities.com (Cogito Ergo Zoom (Lauren))
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<3675b0b1.25893943@news.accessone.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3J3d2.3492$Ql4.1186646@news2.atl> <753t1k$ils$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Mon, 14 Dec 1998 20:39:16 GMT, gene_s@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>
>  What if laws change and importing ammo stops.  All those AK rounds are over
>seas.  

Hardly, there are 100s of millions of rounds here in the US, if not billions. I
know of *one* wherehouse of a medium sized importer that had 75 million rounds
in it. The 7.62x39 cartridge is fairly popular in the US because of the AK and
SKS. As little as ten years ago it was a different story, but ammo is easy to
make or import and when there is a demand there is usually a supply. I wouldn't
say there is necessarily as much 7.62x39 as there is 5.56x45, but there is
certainly enough to go around.

There are other very popular cartridges that people recommend that are actually
fewer in number. The .30-30 for instance. But practically everybody has a rifle
chambered for .30-30 you say? Sure they do - I have two, but like most other
people who own these rifles I do NOT have a case or two of ammo for them. Most
people are not going to ever shoot that much ammo through their .30-30 so they
do not keep that much ammo. 

But a LOT of people have an AK or an SKS, and a lot of those people have at
least one case (1K to 1.44K rounds) of ammo, and boy does that add up real
quick. Every time I go to a gun show I see at least a dozen people leaving
(both when I enter and when I leave) with at least one case, and often multiple
cases of ammo, many of them are 7.62x39 cartridges.

>Also if peace "keeping forces" ever land here in America odd's are
>they well be armed with .223 weapons.  Just look at how NATO and UN countrys
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I cannot agree more with this paragraph (although I had to think a minute about
what "abudons" was - I think you meant abundance?) - I recommended in my posts
that people go with 5.56x45, 7.62x39 and 7.62x51. You seem to be lacking one of
those very popular chamberings. If you can afford it I would suggest you get
something in 7.62x39 - if you have an AR15 it is fairly easy to get an upper
that chambers 7.62x39, otherwise AKs are fairly cheap to come buy (about as
cheap as an AR upper - so it would not hurt to have one more rifle either).

FWIW,
LCB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** CogitoErgoZoom@GeoCities.com (Cogito Ergo Zoom (Lauren))
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00

```
>

My reserve/stash guns are:

MAK-90
Ishapore Enfield
FR8 Mauser
M94 .30-30

Each of these are fairly inexpensive enough to be left behind in necessary. I
don't like Ljungman for the same reason I don't like the Armalite - that gas
system dumps gas gunk right into the wrong place (on the
bolt/cartridge/chamber). The only rifle that I have ever seen that can get away
with this is the HK.

FWIW,
LCB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** CogitoErgoZoom@GeoCities.com (Cogito Ergo Zoom (Lauren))
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<3675739c.10256498@news.accessone.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3J3d2.3492$Ql4.1186646@news2.atl>`

```
On Mon, 14 Dec 1998 08:09:35 GMT, darklove@bellsouth.net (GreyGhost) wrote:

>
>The 223 just isn't a good hunting round. Now, In my other post I said 
>that the 7.62x39 is cheap enough that it may actually be more popular.
>Hell, with 50 million AKs in the world, it most certainly is. 

Re: 7.62x39 popularity, worldwide yes, in the US not quite, but certainly
enough not to dismiss it for being upopular in any sense of the word. I have
seen a few small stores in the boonies that carry supplies for the hunter,
including ammo, that do not have 7.62x39, but do have 5.56x45. 

I don't think that is really the way a Survivalist would be getting ammo - the
small stores and probably any store for that matter would be out ammo in short
order. Where ammo would come from is other sources such as other gun owners, a
black market if the scenario creates one, barter, and possibly from captured
military supplies or bartering with soldiers (or ex-soldiers depending on the
scenario).

I certainly would (and do) have at least one self-defense rifle chambered for
5.56x45, and it wouldn't hurt at all if it was a popular rifle like the
AR-15/M16. But I do agree, if ammo were not a consideration my first choice
would not be something chambered for 5.56x45, unless I was hunting small game.
It is capable of taking game up to a deer, but is not what I would choose for
that task if I had other options.

> The 223 will create titanic wounds in a human,

I disagree with the word "titanic" - having seen some of these wounds I would
not describe them that way. They look like any other rifle wound. Where I have
a problem with the 5.56x45 is in its limited range and its limited penetration
on less than optimum shots on game. Up close, with good placement the 5.56x45
can work on game, but be ready with a follow up, and I recommend something like
a heavy Barnes X-Bullet which will hold together and penetrate fairly well for
its mass. But most people will not go to the trouble of
getting/creating/testing good ammo - they will probably go out and buy the
cheapest surplus ammo they can find.

FWIW,
LCB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Arne Carlsten <agc@primenet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<752joj$bt6$1@nnrp03.primenet.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366B1D2D.F74B5226@mindspring.com> <366B67AC.B19975DA@abcs.com> <FXJa2.953$Zk.2459053@news1.atl> <IZ3b2.365$tI2.465@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <367166B8.4091C802@abcs.com> <74usf5$k7v$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <36732BDE.B808FEE6@mindspring.com> <5%Jc2.3264$Ql4.687891@news2.atl> <751vou$v5f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
gene_s@my-dejanews.com wrote:
:   I disagre with you about the .223 for survival. Yes it's a littel week
: balisticly but being the standard round of both Canada and the U.S. it well be
: around for a long time after the fall.  the .308 is a good choise also for the
: same reason but I belive it well be slighty harder to find.  Personaly I would
: not choose the 7.62x39 becaus it is not produced in enoufgh quanties in the
: Americas.


The 5.56mm and 7.62mm NATO rounds should both be around in military use if
there's any ammunition at all being produced or distributed in North
America.

The 5.56mm NATO is now the standard round of the US and Canada for rifles,
but the 7.62mm NATO remains the standard machinegun round for both
countries.  As such it will be out there, being distributed down to at
least the platoon level in military use.

Not sure what the status of the 5.56mm round is in Mexico.  Mexico has
used the 7.62mm NATO for years, but there are still a lot of .30-06
weapons in service as well I think.  Also some 7.62mmx39 stuff too?

At any rate, it's unlikely that there'll be any to spare down that way in
a TEOTW situation.

Outside of military use, the .308 Winchester/7.62mm NATO is much more
common a civilian "sporting" cartridge than the .223 Remington/5.56mm
NATO.  And is much easier to find usable reloading components.  These are
a couple points in its favor that are worth keeping in mind.
```

```
On 14 Dec 1998 08:54:43 GMT, Arne Carlsten <agc@primenet.com> wrote:


>Not sure what the status of the 5.56mm round is in Mexico.  Mexico has
>used the 7.62mm NATO for years, but there are still a lot of .30-06
>weapons in service as well I think.  Also some 7.62mmx39 stuff too?
>

Mini-14 is a popular alternative for the military and police down there - so it
should not be too hard to come by. In Canada they use the M16 and the FN-FAL.

FWIW,
LCB
```
