>
>no, .308 is not a common caliber for combat.  it was around ww1 and ww2
>but not now.  if you insist on a com-block weapon, go for an sks.  as
>for nato configs, stick with .223/5.56mm

Yeah, it is. Our Machine guns still use it, the Hot spots of the world 
are outfitted with yesterday's weapons, (FALs and the like,) and the 
clear majority of weapons used in Combat are AKs. Ok, .311 and not .308. 
But deal to the guy on the reciveing end.  In Fact, you could say that 
at any given time, the world stage sees far more combet with the various 
7.62 rounds then all others others combined.

As To NATO configs, the major selling point of the 5.556 is the big wound
with FMJs, and the lightness of the round. 
Put softpoint in your .30 and their equal on wounding. 
The .30 is heavyer,But you get more out of it.

#### ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** "Geoffrey L. Hardin" <geoffh@abcs.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<366A8A33.DD8857C9@abcs.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com>`

```
Couple of suggestions :

First of all, if money is no object, I assume that you will be able to
take some time off (say one week) and learn how to shoot your rifle
effectively.  After you get it, enroll in one of the better shooting
schools (Gunsite, Thunder Ranch) in their beginner rifle class and you'll
be able to handle just about anything.

Now, on to your selection.  Your Yugoslavian AK in .308 isn't a bad
choice.  Personally, I would recommend an Israeli Galil (Pre-Ban) AR in
.308.  The design is very similar, in fact, it 's almost identical.  The
Galil has a much higher quality control and will be just as accurate.  I
don't know how much your Yugo AK goes for, but the Galil can be found for
between $1800 and $2100 either New in box or Like New in box.

Make sure you get as many magazines as possible.  I'd recommend the
ability to hold a minimum of 200 rounds in magazines (e.g., if you can
find 20-rd mags, get 10: if you decide to use 35 rd mags, get 6).

Take your gun out and shoot with it.  Find a competent shooter (one who
can prove their ability, not just talk about it) and have them show you
how to load, shoot, clear and maintain the firearm.

I would also recommend keeping at least 1000 rounds of ammo on hand.  This
is your "working" ammo not your practice ammo.

Hope this helps, please do send a copy of any response to my email address
as well as posting to the newsgroup.

Geoffrey L. Hardin,
geoffh@abcs.com

COBOL Doomster wrote:

> I am a COBOL programmer doing some major Y2K work...
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> is no object as my COBOL work pays a lot.
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** Steve Hancock <steveh@shell12.ba.best.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<366a2ad0$0$225@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com>`

```
C.Doomster,

The .308 sounds OK.  However, I would replace the match
barrel with a short barrel for ease of transport and weight.
You may have a problem with parts unless you can locate
a warm spare in the same variety.

You should get a handgun.

For general handgun PRACTICE, get a 6 cylinder that fires
.22 LR (rifle ammo).  Then get something more serious,
like a 5/6 cylinder short barrel .38/.357 for on the road,
and a 6/7 cylinder short/med barrel for home defense
and hunting backup handgun.  Look at Smith and Wesson.

Semiautos are flatter (more concealable).  You could get
a .40 or .45.  The .45s tend to be larger but the ammo
is more readily available.  Or you could get both.
Look at Beretta.

For suburban defense, consider a shotgun.  Pump is
very reliable; semiauto also is good for hunting and times
when speed is important.  Why not get both?  Look at
Mossberg 590 and Remington 11-87.


A .22 rifle is good for hunting.  Two kinds, one piece
and knock down.  Knock down is good for ease of transport
and concealment in a backpack.  You could get both.  Look
at Ruger 10/22 and Marlin Papoose.

Air rifles are good for hunting in a manner that does not
attract attention.

For long range (eg 400-1000 yds) accuracy, get
a bolt action .308 rifle.  Check out Remington 700.

For the hopefully rare time when you need maximum nastiness,
there is the .223 semiauto rifle.  Consider Bushmaster AR-15
clone, 18" barrel.  Get 20 round or larger pre-ban mfg dated mags.

As a computing professional and politically savvy citizen,
you no doubt realize that
the Insta-Check FBI database means someone will probably be asking
for your toys back someday, and you'll want to comply fully
(returning the ammo first, of course).

If money is no object, get two or (remember your Arthur Clarke)
three of all kinds listed above.  Get two handguns for each
member of your family.

Get lots of ammo.  Sign up for and do a shooting course.
Get a simple loader.  (Dillon)  Learn how to use it.  Get lots of 
powder.

Get lots of pre-1965 "junk" silver, and more ammo for bartering.
Get a second home out of the 'burbs, stock it up with yet more
toys and provisions, and figure out a way to get you
and your loved ones there without using any main roads.

Didn't they cover all of this in COBOL 101? :-)

-s

PS. Don't blame the COBOL old timers, because they probably
were only doing exactly what they were told to do...

In misc.survivalism COBOL Doomster <coboldoomster@hotmail.com> wrote:
: I am a COBOL programmer doing some major Y2K work...

: I think that around Y2K the world will fall back into savagery...
: (thanks to you, the boneheaded "old-timer" COBOL dumbasses who made so
: many mistakes, which I am being wonderfully paid to remove now)

: So, I am now actively preparing for Y2K.

: I am considering buying a preban Yugoslavian AK-47 in .308. It is
: "National Match".

: Do you think that it is a good idea? I never owned guns before. Money
: is no object as my COBOL work pays a lot.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** Ralph Jones <leclay@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<366B0E47.F0970254@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com> <366a2ad0$0$225@nntp1.ba.best.com>`

```
Why bother with the small stuff, besides aimed fire is always superior to
full auto in any case, except for close in work.  Then you need a good
shotgun for effective fire suppression.

You might to want consider a .460 Weatherby,
http://www.weatherby.com/rifles/cal_460.shtml
or a  600 nitro express (.60 Cal).
http://www.ozemail.com.au/~mickay/nitro.htm

The .460 shoots a 500 grain bullet at 2,600 fps, with 7,507 ft/lbs.

The 600 will allow you to take out any elephant mounted infantry & light
tanks. Bullet Weight; 900 grains Muzzle Velocity; 1950 fps, Energy; 7591
ft/lbs. It can be found in a double barrel which is more aesthetically
pleasing.  It is said that a head shot on an elephant that missed the brain
would still knock him down and out for a considerable length of time. John
Taylor says up to 30 minutes. Rifles for the .600 usually weighed 16 pounds
or over. There were actually two loadings for the cartridge, one at 1950 fps
and another at 1850. The lighter load was used in Jeffery made doubles. It
is necessary to use the load for which the rifle is regulated or the two
barrels won't shoot to the same point of impact.

These loads, delivering almost 4 tons of energy should do for almost
anything except main battle tanks.

When the time comes don't fort up.  Go to ground and only come out in
complete darkness.  Practice not being seen while scavenging.  :-)

#### ↳ Re: Y2K: Preban Yugo AK-47 in .308 == perfect survival gun?

- **From:** CogitoErgoZoom@GeoCities.com (Cogito Ergo Zoom (Lauren))
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol,alt.survival,misc.survivalism
- **Message-ID:** `<366b1c40.5755505@news.accessone.com>`
- **References:** `<912923249756090@hotmail.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Dec 1998 23:47:29 -0600, coboldoomster@hotmail.com (COBOL Doomster)
wrote:

>I am a COBOL programmer doing some major Y2K work...
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would not go with either the Yugo, or the much better and more expensive
versions (Valmet or Galil) - even though I have 2 Valmets for sale. First -
parts, second - mags, third - ergonomics. Kalashnikovs for Survivalists are
suitable for two classes of people; those that cannot afford anything above
$3-400, and if well-heeled, those that really know what they are getting into.

This may seem strange coming from me - the guy who owns five of them (I collect
them), and is always arguing for them, but I have also always stated that no
one rifle is best for everyone. For some people an AR15 may be best (like for
my daughter who is really not into into firearms and will probably not practice
that much). For others who are willing to train or who cannot afford the $700
minimum of a an AR15, then a Kalashnikov *may* be better than other choices.

I love Kalashnikovs - but they are definitely not for everybody and take some
getting used to, whereas other rifles are a bit more sophisticated with regards
to ergonomics and precision (some people use the term accuracy - but it is not
the same thing), and are easier to get parts and mags for.

For 7.62x51 (.308) Battle class infantry rifles, I recommend first the FN-FAL
(if money is no object then an original FN can be acquired, or if one cannot be
found, the DSA SA-58 is the non-imported equivalent). I also recommend that you
first buy "The FAL Rifle" by R. Blake Stevens - excellend book, and it will
give you some very good background on the FN-FAL.

Next I would recommend the M1A and again I would recommend "Us Rifle M14 : From
John Garand to the M21" by R. Blake Stevens. I also recommend "Small Arms of
the World" by Dr. Edward Ezell. All of these books are put out by "Collectors
Grade" anad while on the expensive side, they are objectively authoritative and
very informative. If a person is going to spend close to $1K on a rifle, I
would think it would make sense to spend $100-$150 on a very good book that
tells you everything you want to know about it- vs. these $20 books that are
about half wrong on a number of issues.

I recommend that maybe you post some *generalized* facts about your plans and
situation - then maybe we can more precisely match up your needs with the
proper chambering and make/model of rifle, and handguns. Some people are well
served by a 9mm handgun and a .223 rifle, other people need something
different.

About the only things I recommend to all people when choosing firearms is:

1) Stick with a chambering that is common and easy to get ammo for. E.g.,
7.62x51 (.308), 7.62x39 (the common chambering for an AK), 5.56x45 (.223) - the
common chambering for the AR15, 9x19mm and .45 ACP (the two common chamberings
for military semi-autos, .22 LR, .357 Magnum, .44 Magnum and so on.

2) Stick with common makes and models - if you are in the US, AKs, AR15s,
Min-14, FN-FAL, M1A, and so-on. Just how many people in the US have Yugos in
308? Can you swap out parts with a Valmet or Galil? A lot of people have
Norinco/Maadi/etc. AKs in 7.62x39 and to a somewhat lesser degree chambered for
.223.

3) Stick with something that is simple, reliable and robust. Here you get into
trade-offs; the AK is supremely reliable compared to the AR15 which has some
problems in this area. But the AK has poor ergonomics, and this is definitely a
consideration for many people. It doesn't do any good to have a rifle that
always goes bang, if you cannot hit anything with it, fumble with the controls,
or you fumble reloading it (all of which some people do with the AK). At the
same time the AR15 can break or malfunction if you do not keep it relatively
clean, or fall down with it.

4) Finally, and most importantly, do not just buy the firearm and stick it away
in a closet thinking you are now prepared. Be willing to spend some time with
the firearm. If you have never owned firearms before then definitely take some
safety training and some basic marksmanship training. Then be willing to take
some more advanced training. This is where your money is going to be spent -
these courses can cost several times the expense of the firearm, but are well
worth it. Definitely be willing to regularly practice what you have learned. I
assume you are buying a firearm for self-defense. There are a number of good
books out there on the issue, two of which I would recommend you start with;
"In the Gravest Extreme" by Massad Ayoob, and "On Killing" by Lt. Col. Dave
Grossman. The latter book is not a book about how to defend yourself, but if
you have never been shot at, and/or have never had to shoot at someone, or even
if you have, I highly recommend it. It made a lot of things I have experienced
a lot clearer to me.

FWIW,
LCB
```

## Thread: Need help with SCREEN SECTION — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need help with SCREEN SECTION

- **From:** nospam_CRAPPIE@mail.dma.be (remove the nospam to reply)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36692af9.5374686@news.ping.be>`

```
Hi,

I'm a first year student learning Cobol, and I have a little problem I
hope some of you would be able to help me with.
In the project I'm working on now, I have to use DISPLAY to display
some lines on the screen. However, when the line I would like to
display is too long, it gets clipped automatically at runtime into the
next line, cause and overwrite ( not-so-neat effect). Since I am
obliged to use DISPLAY, I was browsing through the helpfile a bit to
see if there was an answer to my problems. The only thing I found was
a document on SCREEN SECTION, but it only proposed to use forms in
stead of DISPLAY as it would be much easier to use., but since I don't
know the first thing about forms and am obliged to use DISPLAY, this
didn't help me at all. Does someone have the magical solution perhaps
?
Oh yes, I am using CA-VISUAL REALIA version 1.0.7 level 9411.

Another thing, since the documentation (helpfile) in COBOL is rather
poor, I was wondering if any of you knew of another source where you
can get alot more info when you're stuck, besides this newsgroup. E.g.
websites, external helpfile,...

Kind regards

Sparkie
E-mail nospam_CRAPPIE@mail.dma.be (remove the nospam_to reply)
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with SCREEN SECTION

- **From:** Bert van de Bovenkamp <bert.van.de.bovenkamp@tip.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36722DA9.5BD0A3DC@tip.nl>`
- **References:** `<36692af9.5374686@news.ping.be>`

```
remove the nospam to reply wrote:

> ....
> In the project I'm working on now, I have to use DISPLAY to display
> some lines on the screen. However, when the line I would like to
> display is too long, it gets clipped automatically at runtime into the
> next line, cause and overwrite ( not-so-neat effect).

You can use the SIZE option to limit the length of the display.

Examples:

DISPLAY FIELD-A AT 0160 SIZE 20.

or

DISPLAY FIELD-A LINE 01 POSITION 60 SIZE 20.

This wil limit the display to 20 characters.
It works in RM/COBOL and MF-COBOL.

Greetings and Groetjes,

Bert van de Bovenkamp
The Netherlands
```

know what to do with this.
If I start Pogramming Staff then I can typeover a Cobol program, but what

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol85 help

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981205172300.28844.00001120@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<912887314.14159.0.spot.d4ee49ad@news.demon.nl>`

```

In article <912887314.14159.0.spot.d4ee49ad@news.demon.nl>, "Fendo"
<fendo@pater.demon.nl> writes:

>If I start Pogramming Staff then I can typeover a Cobol program, but what
>next?
>I can save it but I can't open it to run it.
>I

Once you've saved a COBOL Source file, you need to select 'Tools' from the
menu bar and click on 'Compile' to syntax check the source file, click on
'Link' 
to generate an EXE/DLL from the OBJ created by a successfull compile, click
on 'WinEXEC' to run the compiled/linked EXE program.

Hope this helps
```

Did you get a Fujitsu COBOL Reference Guide or a Fujitsu COBOL User's Guide
when you downloaded? There is a section in there about linking and running
your programs , including a sample program. I don't know how new you are to
COBOL, but you can't just write a program and then run it. Your original
program
has to be linked to other files. It's not as hard as it seems at first, though.
Just keep
trying! If you don't have the reference manuals, try going through the help
screen,
etc. at the top of the screen.
```

## Thread: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74c46k$p3b$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <qKQ82.16090$Gy.6464295@news4.mia> <7411lh$koj$1@callisto.clark.net> <sI592.16684$Gy.6999540@news4.mia> <743mqh$bpj$1@callisto.clark.net> <07DD703D74B51C4E.DBF29CF2086E0891.C254998AAE8F04EF@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3668AD8F.F89D1BD7@att.net> <848DDFF3125DF9A0.9DF24140DCFCDE40.451610A83CF1665B@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote in message


>I code as much Assembler as I do Cobol, but I do not use the
>structured macros and I'm not even sure I know what they are.


I don't know how much assembler you have written, but I
am assuming that you understand macros.  If not, say
so and I will explain.

"Structured macros" refer to a technique of coding assembler
using libraried macro's that mimic the typical topdown structure
of high level languages.  Using them, you can write top down
gotoless assembler.  It is not that difficult to write such a set
of macros for any decent macro assembler.

Although I tend to use macros extensively when writing in
assembler (a colleague once accused me of inventing my
own language after reading my assembler code) I have
only dabbled in a full structured set.  I do have a CASE
macro, though, that basically uses a two tables.  One is
of literals that go on the screen as a menu, and the other
is a jump table.  As long as the two are the same size
it is pretty well idiot proof.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<74eior$c8l$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <qKQ82.16090$Gy.6464295@news4.mia> <7411lh$koj$1@callisto.clark.net> <sI592.16684$Gy.6999540@news4.mia> <743mqh$bpj$1@callisto.clark.net> <07DD703D74B51C4E.DBF29CF2086E0891.C254998AAE8F04EF@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3668AD8F.F89D1BD7@att.net> <848DDFF3125DF9A0.9DF24140DCFCDE40.451610A83CF1665B@library-proxy.airnews.net> <74c46k$p3b$1@news.igs.net> <BC530A1D8C14AC6B.D6049E2E60699112.21588804E1509F19@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote in message ...

>Yes, I know what macroes are, however I seldom, if ever, write them
>because the majority of the Assembler programming I do is small single
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>exotic as you have described.


I seldom use them anymore either, and for the same reason. Back
in the days when entire systems were written in assembler, though
the standard I used for even library calls was that if you wrote a
standardized assembler subroutine, you immediately wrote a macro
to enforce the calling sequence. That would integrate it into the
language, in effect.

A macro facility is rather nice for adding features to a language,
and a great help if one is trying to standardize stuff.
```

## Thread: -- need help with index-record — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### -- need help with index-record

- **From:** freezer@bts.webbits.net (G.Harper)
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36690841.19993005@news.gionline.net>`

```
Hi,
I'm in a DOS based Cobol compiler, an early RM Cobol version.

In an interactive maintenance program I have found a problem where the
text is short on an explanation.  I want to be able to add a record,
change an entry in a record, or delete a record in my index-file.  I
have one index-file that has maybe 30 entries( records).  If a user
doesn't need to do anything with the first 28 entries, there must be
code that gets to that record( if I want to get through all of the
records in the file to get to the 28th record it appears that I need
to cycle through all previous 27 record to get to the 28th).  I have
used RECORD KEY IS NDX-FAC-NO, which is the first entry of each record
BUT I'm not sure how to use this.  It appears as though there is a
common idea that I should be able to code a direct path to any record
in the index-file, <<  The text is not clear on this idea >>.  The
text failed to give an example of how to use the RECORD KEY entry.  

Please give me advice.
This is the second time I have posted this problem.  I have worked all
semester on this program, trying different ideas, but nothing has
worked.  I would like to know what I need to know to get this coded
out and running.  It's not my fault that this text didn't explain.
Please send code examples, but if you do please explain how you intend
the code to work.
I have tried everything the text has offered but it doesn't work as
indicated.  I can't find any mention of making the RECORD KEY work as
a random look-up type of key.

Thank you,
GH
```

#### ↳ Re: -- need help with index-record

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74cma4$6pe$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36690841.19993005@news.gionline.net>`

```
If the file is indexed, then you simply move a value to the
key and read it.  For example:

    MOVE "TEES" TO THE-FILE-KEY.
    READ NAME-FILE INVALID KEY
            DISPLAY "There is no record with the key equal to TEES".

Keep in mind that alphabetic keys require an exact match,
including spaces.  The normal method is to convert key
fields to all upper or all lower case before writing the record.

G.Harper wrote in message <36690841.19993005@news.gionline.net>...
>Hi,
>I'm in a DOS based Cobol compiler, an early RM Cobol version.
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>GH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: -- need help with index-record

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0Aa2.4791$Jl.2007553@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36690841.19993005@news.gionline.net> <74cma4$6pe$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>If the file is indexed, then you simply move a value to the
>key and read it.  For example:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>fields to all upper or all lower case before writing the record.

You can specify ORGANIZATION IS DYNAMIC for the indexed file and:

   77  WS-NAME-PRESENT-FLAG   PIC  9(01) VALUE 0.

   MOVE "TEES" TO THE-FILE-KEY.
   MOVE 1 TO WS-NAME-PRESENT-FLAG.
   START NAME-FILE
        KEY NOT < THE-FILE-KEY
        INVALID KEY
            MOVE 0 TO WS-NAME-PRESENT-FLAG.
   IF (WS-NAME-PRESENT-FLAG = 1)
       READ NAME-FILE NEXT.

This obtains the first record in the file which has a key equal to
or greater than "TEES".  The state of WS-NAME-PRESENT-FLAG will
show if a record was found (=1) not not (=0).  You would rarely
use a direct read looking for a name, because it is too difficult
to spell names consistently correct.  Read the first one found
above, then display the next 10 or 20 names for selection, and
permit a 'view next page' option to display the next 10 or 20
names.  If COBOL 85 were not pathologically inhibited in indexed
access, you could also support a 'view previous page' option and
display the previous 10 or 20 names using READ NAME-FILE PREVIOUS.
Let's hope this clause is in the next standard.
```

<a id="t-f856477efd"></a>
## Thread: Alternative to ELIF — 2 msgs

### Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36695DD8.4D90@swbell.net>`

```
I'd like to propose another way to tame the wild END-IF: bracket a
complex set of conditionals with PERFORM and END-PERFORM.  E.g.:

    PERFORM
      IF A ...
      ELSE IF B ...
      ELSE IF C ...
    END-PERFORM

This is not my idea -- someone emailed it to me.  He didn't want to 
post it himself, or let his name be used, because he didn't want to 
be pestered with requests for homework assistance.

Admittedly this syntax looks bizarre.  As a perversion of the intended 
use of PERFORM/END-PERFORM, it is likely to confuse a maintenance
programmer who is not familiar with it.  That said, let us consider
its advantages.

The example is a simple IF/ELSE chain which you might just as well
code with EVALUATE TRUE, or (if it were available) some form of ELIF.
However, the PERFORM/END-PERFORM could enclose any arbitrarily
complex set of conditionals, including stuff you couldn't code with
EVALUATE or the proposed ELIF.

The END-PERFORM terminates the scope of this complex conditional, but
it is less of a blunt instrument than a period would be.  A period
terminates all scopes, bringing you abruptly to the outermost level.
By contrast, the END-PERFORM only brings you back to the level of the
corresponding PERFORM.  Thus this construct gives a degree of 
flexibility not otherwise available except through the (possibly 
lavish) use of END-IF.

I haven't used this construct myself, and I can't decide if it's ugly
or elegant.  Let's have a long interminable argument about it.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

#### ↳ Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981206025423.27202.00000703@ng18.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36695DD8.4D90@swbell.net>`

```
From: "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 1998 10:22:48 -0600
posted 

<< 
I'd like to propose another way to tame the wild END-IF: bracket a
complex set of conditionals with PERFORM and END-PERFORM.  E.g.:

    PERFORM
      IF A ...
      ELSE IF B ...
      ELSE IF C ...
    END-PERFORM

This is not my idea -- 
>>
snippettes
<<
I haven't used this construct myself, and I can't decide if it's ugly
or elegant.  Let's have a long interminable argument about it.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
>>

Very Interesting. Michael C. Kasten moves to the front of the class! Until we
find the mystery person who sourced this. Gold stars for all.

Best Wishes, 

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981205120214.19083.00000316@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
I finally finished the revisions to my two intro Assembler courses. Got all the
new instructions integrated into the flow instead of bunched together at the
end (the courses cover all hardware instructions except those that are:
privileged, semi-privileged, floating point, and vector). Also includes labeled
USINGs and other HLASM techniques. Details on our web page
(www.trainersfriend.com). 

We have the two courses scheduled in Denver in Jan / Feb (details on website).

Call it "COBOL Appreciation Week". It's always hard to fill an Assembler class
so I'm just asking if you would forward this opportunity on to anyone who might
benefit.

Thanks for your help.


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

>>US-MN   $60-$70K     IT Business Analyst - COBOL/CICS
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>60-70K for ONE YEAR! 

Things have changed, haven't they? Back in the Stone
Age ('77), I started my first job with a freshly minted BS
degree at $12,000. I was thrilled with it because I had been
working part-time at a truck stop for $2.50 an hour.

It all goes back to supply and demand. When I graduated,
Comp Sci was a hot degree. Now people aren't interested
in it. I read the the number of Comp Sci degrees awarded
has gone down 40% in the last (I think) 20 years. Which
is all nice for people starting out now, but kind of aggravating for people who
have been at it for awhile.
Oh well, still beats ditch digging.
```

## Thread: Sue The Programmers? — 21 msgs

### Sue The Programmers?

- **From:** kreskin@rocketmail.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74e03m$b3d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In the Halifax Chronicle-Herald on 1998/12/05:

Columnist Peter Duffy, under a page-wide page 2 headline:

Sue Those Whose Lack Of Foresight Bred Y2K Bug

The cost of trying to avert the millennium bug meltdown is truly shocking.
Billions of tax dollars (and private one, too) being spent to avert a
catastrophe when computer systems click over from 1999 to the year 2000.
Here's an idea: you know how cigarette makers are being sued to help pay the
cost of health care? Well, I say we sue the pants off the techno-nerds who
developed these about-to-fail programs in the first place. Why should the
rest of us have to carry the can for their shortsightedness.


*sigh* it was bound to happen.
If anyone here cares to give Mr. Duffy a history lesson and let him know the
real roots of the problem and who is truly at fault in not dealing with the
problem in a timely manner, he can be reached at duffy@herald.ns.ca

Kreskin

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

With out reading the entire article to fully understand the context of 
the statement you quoted from Mr. Duffy I find it hard to comment on what 
he said. Even having said that I still have some general thoughts on the 
subject, and here they are.

I think Mr. Duffy is just saying what a lot of others are all ready 
thinking and planning. The volume of litigation that will rain down 
on Microsoft alone will be staggering. Recently, I heard an estimate from
Lyods of London that the total dollar amount associated with the Y2K 
litigation will be around one trillion dollars. It seems that a group of
their actuaries came up with this number somehow. I think the number is a 
bit low. It might actually be a good starting point rather than a final 
total. 
```

anything out of other than our rusted Escorts???

>The volume of litigation that will rain down
> on Microsoft alone will be staggering.

Fine, if you want to sue the software development companies, go right ahead
... but Mr. Duffy is talking about suing the *programmers*. Talk about
getting blood from a stone! :-)

btw, I doubt anyone will have much luck getting anything out of Mr. Gates for
Y2k failures, other than maybe a free upgrade, since for the most part, MS
products have been "Y2k-capable" for a while now ... and I don't think there
are too many MS COTS products being used by Fortune 5000 companies as the
mission-critical software backbone of their enterprises.

>Recently, I heard an estimate from
> Lyods of London that the total dollar amount associated with the Y2K
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> total.

new to Y2k, Mr. Farkas??? That amount (I thought it was higher) is not the
amount of litigation that will be directed against software companies alone
... it includes litigation against *any* company that fails to provide
service as expected due to Y2k.

>
> --
> Jeff Farkas

says...

I have never heard of Mr. Duffy until today and I was not sure if he had 
written more on the subject in his Saturday column. 

> 
> >Even having said that I still have some general thoughts on the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> anything out of other than our rusted Escorts???

Nope, I have no plans on suing anyone since I would have to include 
myself. 

> 
> >The volume of litigation that will rain down
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> getting blood from a stone! :-)

Given the number of lawyers running around I would not bet on anything. 
While I think it is highly unlikely that the average programmer employed 
by a firm will be sued it is possible that a small number of contract 
employees might be sued for their work done for small firms.

> 
> btw, I doubt anyone will have much luck getting anything out of Mr. Gates for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mission-critical software backbone of their enterprises.

Capable and compliant are two different things and lawyers love to split 
hairs over these differences. BTW, I have seen a few systems in my day 
that use various MS products to create critical reports for various 
customers and they are not upgraded as frequently as they should be. 
And I am talking about Fortune 100 companies!!

A final thought, I wonder how well the MS argument that purchasing an 
upgrade is the "fix" for the Y2K problem? I bet there is at least one 
lawyer that will be able to rip that defense to pieces in no time. And 
then there is the old class action law suit!! 
 
> 
> >Recently, I heard an estimate from
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> service as expected due to Y2k.

Nope, I far from "new" to this problem. I have been working on this for 
quite some time now and I figure that I will be continuing to do so for 
quite some time to come.

When I saw the program on CSPAN I just caught bits and pieces of it and 
the number that was being thrown around was one trillion. And you are 
correct, the discussion was not just covering the direct costs to the 
software firms but it covered the costs of the year 2000 and how it would 
effect everyone.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sue The Programmers?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366C133B.229D5EE9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<74e03m$b3d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <MPG.10d46d9041cc94ed9896d0@news.earthlink.net> <74ep1g$uja$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <MPG.10d4c7aefc5c753b9896d2@news.earthlink.net>`

```
In a lawsuit, it doesn't matter who is responsible, who should have been
responsible, whether there is a real problem, or even whether or not the
problem was justified (as in  asbestos in W.W.II ships).  What really
matters is who has the money.
```

## Thread: Alternative to ELIF — 5 msgs

### Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981206030002.27202.00000706@ng18.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74bpqo$mnt@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
From: "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 1998 11:15:33 -0600

Posted a response to the PERFORM/IFFFFFS/END-PERFORM poposal

<<   This is NOT standard-conforming source code.  The only standard compliant
statement that can appear between a PERFORM and END-PERFORM is an imperative
statement.  As long as one (much less all) of the IF's are not terminated,
then it is illega.
...
>>

(Important elaborations snipped, See original posts.).

I wonder if some folks might try the construct and see which compilers rebel
and which co-operate. Maybe this is a secret weapon hidden in the compiler that
will be king of the hill.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3670b490.176291838@netnews>`
- **References:** `<74bpqo$mnt@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <19981206030002.27202.00000706@ng18.aol.com> <74n156$kkk$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
'Twas Wed, 9 Dec 1998 18:29:35 -0500, when "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
>    13     perform
>    14         if a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>**    Preceding statement is not imperative

>* Total Messages:     1
>* Unrecoverable :     0                    Severe  :     1
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Enough said!

Not enough.  In this compiler is "Severe" a fatal error?  To pass
validation compilers flag non-ANSI syntax, but does the compiler go ahead
and produce the code you want?   Most compilers allow this as an extension
to the tight-assed J4 interpretation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74r29c$8dv$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<74bpqo$mnt@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <19981206030002.27202.00000706@ng18.aol.com> <74n156$kkk$1@server.cntfl.com> <3670b490.176291838@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <3670b490.176291838@netnews>...
>'Twas Wed, 9 Dec 1998 18:29:35 -0500, when "Rick Smith"
><ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
>>    13     perform
>>    14         if a
[...]
>>    24         end-if
>>    25     end-perform
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The first line of the source file set the conditions.

     1$set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"s"

Allow ANS85 syntax, FLAG non-ANS85 usage, FLAG violations AS
severe.

With FLAGAS"S" no code is produced.

In the absence of FLAGAS (or with other options for FLAGAS),
code is produced and appears to work correctly.

As I understood it, the idea was presented as a means to provide an
alternative to a long string of END-IF scope-delimiters. I also understood,
perhaps inferred, that the alternative would work with all translators.

In using directives to enforce the strict ("tight-assed J4") interpretation,
I confirmed that it may not work with all translators.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

### OT - Programmer sought

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366A9E48.B3481B47@att.net>`

```
This is not a search for someone with IBM mainframe skills; however, a
good friend of mine who works for an investment banking firm in
Manhattan is looking for a programmer with UNIX/NT skills, preferably
with quant, to work for the fixed-income desk. I didn't ask about the
salary; but I do know the pay scale is high and you'll work for it. I'm
posting here thinking that if none of the regulars who posts here is
interested, you might know someone who is and personal references are
the best ones.

Please contact:

Bill Lynch
wblynch@att.net
```

## Thread: Natural/COBOL/DB2/Adabas Opportunities — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Natural/COBOL/DB2/Adabas Opportunities

- **From:** info@intellex.ab.ca
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.adabas,comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.databases.ibm-db2
- **Message-ID:** `<74evik$3ta$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Intellex has immediate contract or employment position available for senior
individuals with experience supporting large mainframe applications using
COBOL, Natural, DB2, and/or Natural.  Individual must be able to work
independently with minimal instructions.  On-call support required on a
rotational basis. Intermediate analyst positions also available.

Intellex is an equal opportunity employer located at Edmonton, Alberta.  The
office of Intellex is smoke free.  We offer medical/dental benefits and group
insurance to both our assoicates and employees.  For more information about
Intellex and other job opportunities, please see our website at
www.intellex.ab.ca.

Please e-mail your resume, day and evening phone numbers, 3 recent related
references, availablility date, and salary expectations to
info@intellex.ab.ca. Please quote opportunity #deja9812.  Due to the high
volume of responses, we regret telephone calls cannot be accepted.  Thank you
for your interest in Intellex.

Intellex Systems Inc.
website: www.intellex.ab.ca
 e-mail: info@intellex.ab.ca

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-cb537f4cc6"></a>
## Thread: Help Variable Length Output Records — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help Variable Length Output Records

- **From:** David Warring <David_Warring@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366A0230.633A3F5A@hotmail.com>`

```
Just a quickie...

I have a recurring Client requirement to create a file of  character
delimited output records. These are to be constructed from existing
working storage fields. Trimming of the fields is also required, to
produce fields. The records may be of (greatly) varying length. E.g.

aaa1|bbbb1|cccc .... |zzz1
aaaaa2|bbbbbbbbbb2|cccc .... |zz2

Language choices are Cobol or Cobol! The customer is using a VAX/VMS
Cobol compiler, although a Cobol-85 compliant solution is preferred.

Is this possible? What's the best approach. Any help appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Variable Length Output Records

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ebvn$88q$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366A0230.633A3F5A@hotmail.com>`

```
Check out the STRING verb.  Remember to move
space to the receiving field first, as it will not clear
to the end of field.  A quick example:

01 a    picture x(10) value "data".
01 b    picture x(10) value "dat".
01 c    picture x(15) value space.

string a delimited by " "
          "." delimited by length
          b delimited by space
     into
           c.


David Warring wrote in message <366A0230.633A3F5A@hotmail.com>...
>Just a quickie...
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help Variable Length Output Records

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-0812981446120001@138.202.137.168>`
- **References:** `<366A0230.633A3F5A@hotmail.com> <74ebvn$88q$1@news.igs.net>`

```
> David Warring wrote in message <366A0230.633A3F5A@hotmail.com>...
> >Just a quickie...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
here's how I handle it.  I wrote a program that loads class lists for the
USF web site (check it out -
http://www.usfca.edu/schedules/scheduleindex.shtml)

INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.

    SELECT HTML-FILE
       ASSIGN           DUMMY
    .
"dummy" is a placeholder, as I create many many files using the same
select.  The ASSIGN clause is required.

DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.

FD  HTML-FILE
    RECORD VARYING 1 TO 340 DEPENDING REC-LENGTH
    VALUE OF ID WS-FILESPEC.
01  HTML-REC                   PIC X(340).

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.              
01  PROGRAM-CONTROL-DATA.

01  HTML-STUFF.
    03  REC-LENGTH             PIC 9(09)  COMP.
    03  HTML-OPEN              PIC X(06)  VALUE '<HTML>'.
    03  HTML-CLOSE             PIC X(07)  VALUE '</HTML>'.
    03  HEAD-OPEN              PIC X(06)  VALUE '<HEAD>'.
    03  HEAD-CLOSE             PIC X(07)  VALUE '</HEAD>'.
    03  TITLE-OPEN             PIC X(07)  VALUE '<TITLE>'.
    03  TITLE-CLOSE            PIC X(08)  VALUE '</TITLE>'.
    03  H1-OPEN                PIC X(04)  VALUE '<H1>'.
    03  H1-CLOSE               PIC X(05)  VALUE '</H1>'.
    03  H2-OPEN                PIC X(04)  VALUE '<H2>'.
    03  H2-CLOSE               PIC X(05)  VALUE '</H2>'.
    03  H3-OPEN                PIC X(04)  VALUE '<H3>'.
    03  H3-CLOSE               PIC X(05)  VALUE '</H3>'.
    03  BODY-OPEN              PIC X(06)  VALUE '<BODY>'.
    03  BODY-CLOSE             PIC X(07)  VALUE '</BODY>'.
    03  PRE-OPEN               PIC X(05)  VALUE '<PRE>'.
    03  PRE-CLOSE              PIC X(06)  VALUE '</PRE>'.
    03  ULIST-OPEN             PIC X(04)  VALUE '<UL>'.
    03  ULIST-CLOSE            PIC X(05)  VALUE '</UL>'.
    03  TABLE-OPEN             PIC X(20)  VALUE '<TABLE WIDTH="100%">'.

here's one example:

    OPEN OUTPUT HTML-FILE

    MOVE 6                     TO REC-LENGTH
    WRITE HTML-REC           FROM HTML-OPEN

*   MOVE 6                     TO REC-LENGTH
    WRITE HTML-REC           FROM HEAD-OPEN

    MOVE TITLE-LENGTH          TO REC-LENGTH
    WRITE HTML-REC           FROM TITLE

    MOVE 7                     TO REC-LENGTH
    WRITE HTML-REC           FROM HEAD-CLOSE

    MOVE 6                     TO REC-LENGTH
    WRITE HTML-REC           FROM BODY-OPEN

in building details, I don't personally like STRING, tho' it works fine in
most cases.  To get the correct rec length, you would need to use STRING
... WITH POINTER, and then subtract 1 from the final value, since your
working-storage pointer item has the position (address) of the next byte.

Here is my problem with STRING:
say you have this:
    03  WS-CITY  PIC X(13)  VALUE 'San Francisco'.

If you STRING this delimited size, no problem, you just end up with alot
of white space (in most cases, i.e. Fresno), which in an HTML document is
a very bad thing.  If you delimit it by ' ' (one space), then you get
'San' in your output.  If you delimit it by '  ' (2 spaces) you get
nothing.  You would need to move the field to a 15 byte field to get it to
work in all cases.  Phooey.  I prefer the following:

    MOVE H2-OPEN               TO H2 
(this in lieu of moving spaces to H2, we be padded honey)
    MOVE SK-DEPT-TRANS         TO H2 (5:40)
                                  HOLD-DEPT-TRANS

    MOVE 44                    TO H2-PTR
    PERFORM UNTIL H2 (H2-PTR:1) > SPACE
               OR H2-PTR < 1
       SUBTRACT 1 FROM H2-PTR
    END-PERFORM
    ADD 1 TO H2-PTR
    MOVE H2-CLOSE              TO H2 (H2-PTR:5)

    ADD H2-PTR 4 GIVING REC-LENGTH

    WRITE HTML-REC           FROM H2

In an ideal world I would remove all of the magic numbers, but you get the idea
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Variable Length Output Records

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366a3066.361928064@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<366A0230.633A3F5A@hotmail.com>`

```
On Sun, 06 Dec 1998 15:04:00 +1100, David Warring
<David_Warring@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Just a quickie...
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is this possible? What's the best approach. Any help appreciated.

Yes.  Construct your data using STRING and then output it to a "Line
Sequential" file.  Yes - for those wondering VMS COBOL does support
this construct.
```

       01 DETAILS.

            '�����������������������������������������������������Ķ'.
        02 LINE 8 COLUMN 8 VALUE

        02 LINE 9 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '� CHILDREN NO:                ALLOWANCE:              �'.
        02 LINE 10 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '� INCOME GROSS:               TAX INCOME:             �'.
        02 LINE 11 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '�                             TAX PAID                �'.
        02 LINE 12 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '�                             TOTAL AMOUNT  INCOME    �'.
        02 LINE 13 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '�                             FOR YEAR:               �'.
        02 LINE 14 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '�                                                     �'.
        02 LINE 15 COLUMN 8 VALUE
            '�����������������������������������������������������Ķ'.

'�                                                     �'.

            '�����������������������������������������������������ͼ'.



       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       BEGIN.
           DISPLAY(1, 1) ERASE.
           ACCEPT DATE-IN FROM DATE.
           MOVE CORR DATE-IN TO HEADING-REC.
           PERFORM TAXI UNTIL CHOICE = 'N'
           STOP RUN.



       TAXI.
           DISPLAY(2, 45) HEADING-REC.
           ACCEPT(4, 13) NAME.
           ACCEPT(5, 28) ADDRESS.
           ACCEPT(6, 19) POSTCODE.
           ACCEPT(8, 25) MARRIED.
           ACCEPT(9, 22) CHILD.
           ACCEPT(10, 22) INCOME-GROSS.

           IF MARRIED = Y
              MOVE 2300 TO PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE
           ELSE.
              MOVE 1200 TO PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE.

           MULTIPLY 100 BY CHILD-ALLOWANCE
           ADD CHILD-ALL0WANCE TO PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE

           COMPUTE TAXABLE-INCOME = INCOME-GROSS - PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE

           WORKOUT-1.

           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 1000 MOVE ZERO TO TAX
           PERFORM WORKOUT-END

           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 2000
           COMPUTE TAX = 0.3 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 1000)
           PERFORM WORKOUT-END

           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 4000
           COMPUTE TAX = 0.3 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 2000)
           PERFORM WORKOUT-END

           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 24000
           COMPUTE TAX = 0.35 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 4000) + 800
           PERFORM WORKOUT-END

           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 64000
           COMPUTE TAX = 0.4 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 24000) + 7800
           PERFORM WORKOUT-END

           IF TAXABLE-INCOME < 64000
           COMPUTE TAX = .45 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 64000) + 23800
           PERFORM WORKOUT-END

           WORKOUT-END.
           COMPUTE AFTER-TAX = INCOME-GROSS - TAX
           DISPLAY (25, 1) AFTER-TAX.


#### ↳ Re: program help

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F8FEB6E78A2BEE32.DC2EAA81FC0D6E6F.F6AAD1CDC0D7B347@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<74e83i$l2c$1@barcode.tesco.net>`

```
Some possibilities pointed below.



On Sun, 6 Dec 1998 15:27:02 -0800, "Andrew Chidgey"
<Doss.House@tesco.net> enlightened us:

>Can anyone tell me where i am going wrong.Ihave an error on line 95-102
>somewhere
…[102 more quoted lines elided]…
>           ELSE.
Remove .     ^
>              MOVE 1200 TO PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE.
PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE is defined as PIC 99 -- you'll get truncation here
>
>           MULTIPLY 100 BY CHILD-ALLOWANCE
>           ADD CHILD-ALL0WANCE TO PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE
See above -- truncation
>
>           COMPUTE TAXABLE-INCOME = INCOME-GROSS - PERSONAL-ALLOWANCE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 1000 MOVE ZERO TO TAX
Should be:  IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT > 1000 MOVE ZERO TO TAX  (you need a
space between NOT and >)  BTW, didn't see a definition for
TAXABLE-INCOME.
>           PERFORM WORKOUT-END
>
>           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 2000
Need space between NOT and >
>           COMPUTE TAX = 0.3 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 1000)
Need space between 0.3 and * and ( 
In other words it should read:
             COMPUTE TAX = 0.3 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 1000)
>           PERFORM WORKOUT-END
>
>           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 4000
Need space between NOT and >
>           COMPUTE TAX = 0.3 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 2000)
See above COMPUTE correction.  Note:  If the space is there,
disregard.  In my newsreader it doesn't appear to be there.
>           PERFORM WORKOUT-END
>
>           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 24000
Need space between NOT and >
>           COMPUTE TAX = 0.35 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 4000) + 800
>           PERFORM WORKOUT-END
>
>           IF TAXABLE-INCOME NOT> 64000
Need space between NOT and >
>           COMPUTE TAX = 0.4 * (TAXABLE-INCOME - 24000) + 7800
>           PERFORM WORKOUT-END
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You should review the size of your output fields.  You have them all
described as PIC 99.  Given the computations you are doing, you are
going to most likely encounter truncation on most if not all of your
output fields thereby giving you erroneous results.

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Microsoft Windows:  Proof that P.T. Barnum was correct.

 Steve
```

- **References:** `<74e83i$l2c$1@barcode.tesco.net>`

```
Andrew Chidgey wrote:
> 
> Can anyone tell me where i am going wrong.Ihave an error on line 95-102

```
Hi,
I have the need to know where the user clicks when I received a LOST-FOCUS
event in DS 3.0.

this with the pf-get-next-event function in panels v2, but I dont want to
lose the event.
Anyone have suggestions?
Thanks
Micael

#### ↳ Re: How Can I look for a next event in Dialog System?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366aabbd.393508127@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74drg6$3of$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
On Sun, 6 Dec 1998 12:57:26 +0100, "Micael Chiampo" <semnet@tin.it>
wrote:

>Hi,
>I have the need to know where the user clicks when I received a LOST-FOCUS
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Micael

My method of handling this is a bit tedious but I never found a better
solution (other than giving up Dialog).

Make sure that the LOST-FOCUS event is coded on EACH object that you
want to test.  Don't code it in the Window (unless you want to test
the window losing focus) or at the Global level - code it on each
object.

Then set a switch.  Move the object handle to a data area or move a
literal (the literal is eaisier to understand and work with - I never
could get integrity with object handles).

I'd be interested in hearing any other solutions.
```

## Thread: using existing information — 3 msgs

### using existing information

- **From:** "Wayne Whitmore" <whitmore@gil.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74gi88$t6a20@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au>`

```
I am trying to find how to convert current cobol based files into files
delphi can use
```

#### ↳ Re: using existing information

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74glcn$ka8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<74gi88$t6a20@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au>`

```

Wayne Whitmore wrote in message <74gi88$t6a20@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au>...
>I am trying to find how to convert current cobol based files into files
>delphi can use


It depends on what type of Cobol file you are working with.
If it is an indexed file, then you would simply write a small
Cobol program to write it out as a flat ASCII file.

If it is already a flat ASCII file, there is no reason that
you cannot read it with Delphi as it sits. The same is
true of all the other file types.  You need to know what
is in it, of course, and how the data is laid out. Cobol
uses standard coding for data (ASCII on a PC) unless
the programmer overrode that deliberately for C-type
word or binary numbers.

Cobol can also access various data-bases. If that is
the case, then you would have to use that data-bases
access methods, the same as Cobol does. SQL would
probably be the easiest method under those circumstances.
```

#### ↳ Re: using existing information

- **From:** "Gerald Moull" <gerald@liant.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<913774275.9318.0.nnrp-05.9e981226@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<74gi88$t6a20@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au>`

```

Take a look at Relativity at www.liant.com.

You can either convert the data or share it dynamically with the host COBOL
application.

Gerald Moull

Wayne Whitmore wrote in message <74gi88$t6a20@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au>...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: converting files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74jn0r$p7n@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<74gis7$o7j8@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au> <366D33A0.FDBEA4C6@home.com> <74jja1$fbe$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <74jja1$fbe$1@news.igs.net>...
>
>Howard Brazee wrote in message <366D33A0.FDBEA4C6@home.com>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Whether or not you can EASILY read and use an ISAM file across compiler
languages really depends on 2 things:

1) In some environments, the "ISAM" file system is a built-in part of the
operating system (for example on IBM's MVS - with VSAM - and several other
mainframe systems).  In these environments, every language has easy access
to them.

2) In some systems, a 3rd party file system (e.g. C-isam) is used through a
documented interface.  The same interface is used by COBOL, C, and any other
language that is able to use the required interface.  Again, this makes
inter-language use easy and safe.

Really, the only place where using ISAM is NOT (necessarily) safe is where
the ISAM system is "unique" to the COBOL run-time system - and even then,
many vendors make their interfaces "public" and other languages can use them
with little or no difficulty.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: converting files

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366d5d9c.59056588@news.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<74gis7$o7j8@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au> <366D33A0.FDBEA4C6@home.com> <74jja1$fbe$1@news.igs.net>`

>>A file is a file.  Just make sure both applications (COBOL and the other
>>one) know the format of the file.

>While that is true, I would be a bit leery of trying to read

>should be quite simple, as you say.

Anyone contemplating this should definitely contact their vendor and see if they
can license the ISAM routines in a linkable format. It'll probably involve NDA
but it avoids enough grief to easily justify the hassle. 

I know that we've licensed our Vision routines in the past; obviously I cannot
speak for other vendors.

# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
```

The original question was about Windows based programs.  The answer must
therefore beto use a Windows standard.  This must be ODBC.  Any Windows tool
worth its money can use ODBC data.

What is required therefore is an ODBC driver for the COBOL data.  I'd
recommend you do a WEB search on ODBC and COBOL.

As for 'A file is a file' - !?!?!?!

Gerald Moull
gerald@liant.co.uk

they

NDA
>but it avoids enough grief to easily justify the hassle.

cannot

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: converting files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3677e453.689274664@news.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<74gis7$o7j8@iccu9.ipswich.gil.com.au> <366D33A0.FDBEA4C6@home.com> <74jja1$fbe$1@news.igs.net> <366d5d9c.59056588@news.acucorp.com> <913774860.9495.0.nnrp-05.9e981226@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Wed, 16 Dec 1998 02:21:53 -0800, "Gerald Moull" <gerald@liant.co.uk> wrote:

>The original question was about Windows based programs.  The answer must
>therefore beto use a Windows standard.  This must be ODBC.  Any Windows tool
>worth its money can use ODBC data.

Here's the contents of the original post from
http://x15.dejanews.com/getdoc.xp?AN=419613779:
>Can files in a cobol written program be converted to be used by other
>appllications?  Possibly windows based programs?

I see two interpretations:
	1) They have datafiles from a COBOL app that they are trying to replace.
They want to move the existing data into the replacement system.
	2) They have an existing COBOL program and something new (A Windows-based
replacement?). They want to sync data between both applications.

If #1 is true, ODBC is probably the wrong answer - it almost certainly entails
entirely too much work for a one-time conversion, not to mention that licensing
an ODBC driver for a one-time task seems rather costly.

If #2 is true, ODBC is probably the right answer. If you have a need for many
programs to access this data ODBC is definitely the right choice. On the other
hand, if there is a relatively small and static set of programs that must access
this data, ODBC might involve too much additional programmer time and it will
introduce a new subsystem to the maintenance workload. This hinges on whether
their non-COBOL app is written in such a way that ODBC support would be easy to
add. Writing a general solution generally takes more time and planning than a
problem-specific solution and that extra time may not be available.


>As for 'A file is a file' - !?!?!?!

I believe this was directed to the wrong post. I was merely recommending that
someone contact their COBOL vendor before attempting to write their own ISAM
routines. This line was quoted three levels deep - any flames should go to Mr.
Brazee.


# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
```

>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>actually get approved in 2000?

I sent a note to the chair about this paragraph.  WG4 (the ISO group) asked
what the current schedule was and J4 (the ANSI group - who actually writes
most of the proposals) couldn't really give a good answer.  The current
committment is that by April of '99 J4 will develop a NEW schedule to give
WG4.

IMHO - unless something quite drastic occurs, the EARLIEST we can see a new
approved Standard is 2002. However, the next OFFICIAL update will be
available next April. (Given the committee's past record on estimate
accurately - probably on April fools day!)

>
>William M. Klein wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

- **From:** "Stephen D. Burns" <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Some of the items that are shown on the standards page seem to be
changes made for the benefit of compiler writers.  The removal of
dynamic tables and disallowance of object data by reference look like
that.  Is this the case?

The reason I ask is that years ago I was part of an EIA committee for NC
standards (numerical control, used for manufacturing tools).  I was the
only user on the committee, the rest of the members were vendors of NC
tools.  I was constantly having to remind them that they should not
write standards just for the ease of implementation.  Users don't care
what it costs an implementer to provide a compiler, we want good useful
capabilities.

(By the way, the EIA committe was the one that had produced the infamous
RS232 standard.  Don't give me a hard time for that, though.  they had
completed that dog long before I joined them.)

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> The following note was just sent by the chair of J4,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> doncobol@mediaone.net"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74kc3d$576@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com>`

```

Stephen D. Burns wrote in message <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com>...
>Some of the items that are shown on the standards page seem to be
>changes made for the benefit of compiler writers.  The removal of
>dynamic tables and disallowance of object data by reference look like
>that.  Is this the case?

Well, yes and no,
  In my opinion, there have been very few changes made because specific
vendors have tried to get "their way" for compatibility with their existing
implementations.  On the other hand, this Standard is so full of "processor
dependent" (aka optional) features and implementor-defined stuff, that I
think there HAS been significantly MORE attention paid to ease of
implementation over true portability.  I got "shot down" every time I say
this (to the committee) but it certainly is my opinion.

P.S. The actual "direction" (unlike the details) of this Standard are being
given by WG4 (the ISO group) while the specification details are developed
by J4 (the ANSI group).  Interestingly enough, the WG4 members are
predominately USERS and not implementors - except for the US delegation.

>
>The reason I ask is that years ago I was part of an EIA committee for NC
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>(206) 662-5937                Act ridiculously......
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366DB451.5275@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com>`

```
Stephen D. Burns wrote:
> 
> Some of the items that are shown on the standards page seem to be
> changes made for the benefit of compiler writers.  The removal of
> dynamic tables and disallowance of object data by reference look like
> that.  Is this the case?

Definitely not.  Dynamic tables were removed because they are not 
really necessary with the capability of allocating and freeing 
storage.  Also, they would be extremely inefficient in many cases and 
if pointers were used to point at parts of them they would lead to 
bugs that would be almost impossible to find.

The object data one has nothing to do with implementation but has 
everything to do with preventing another really big class of bugs.  
Also, it is of dubious value.

> The reason I ask is that years ago I was part of an EIA committee for NC
> standards (numerical control, used for manufacturing tools).  I was the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> capabilities.

We have several users and many of the impelementors are on the user's 
side as well.  I can't recall any times we did something just because 
it eased implementation.  There may have been some.  Some we did or 
did not do because they were impossible to implement.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00

- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com> <366DB451.5275@compaq.com>`

```

>
>We have several users and many of the impelementors are on the user's
>side as well.  I can't recall any times we did something just because
>it eased implementation.  There may have been some.  Some we did or

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366EBCFF.4706@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com> <366DB451.5275@compaq.com> <W8wb2.10697$Jl.3822257@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

No it was not.  We had to have some limit for portability and 18 was 
chosen because the binary representation will fit into 64 bits.  I 
suppose you could call this making it easier on the implemetor, but no 
user said they ever needed anything more than 18 digits.  That is, 
when the '74 standard went out for review there was not a peep from 
anyone.  We recently expanded the number of digits to 31 for obscure 
reasons.  I think mainly because SQL allows it.  No user had a valid 
case for such a large amount of precision.  Floating-point gets you 
large magnitudes.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

```
Floating point also gets you a degree of imprecision that's absent from
integer representations, and in most financial environments that's ample
reason to use it very carefully indeed, if at all, in those environments!

(I once had to deal with an accounts receivable application written in
FORTRAN using floating-point variables.  The owners of the company could
never figure out why their books weren't quite balanced ...  Converted it
first to use integer, subsequently rewrote into COBOL).

    -Chuck Stevens

various places like the David Taylor Model Basin, and other labs, and
government sites as well as some very large corporations. However, I do
not believe at that time we were quite prepared for the use of the
comma, and the larger numbers needed in other world sites. It was not

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74odru$2m0@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com> <366DB451.5275@compaq.com> <W8wb2.10697$Jl.3822257@news3.mia> <366EBCFF.4706@compaq.com>`

```
(Posted to the NG - CC'ed to Don and Judson)

To me, one example of where the current draft has been designed to "make
implementation easier" is something that Judson has commented on frequently.
The current direction is to make READ PREVIOUS "processor dependent" (which
really now means "optional").  Not only do most (but not all) existing
indexed file systems already support this functionality, but it is seems no
more "demanding" to REQUIRE all implementations to support this than the
original requirement (many Standards ago) to require all implementations to
support an indexed file system.  Yes, it might require some additional
"implementation work" - but I just can't see any good reason (for the
language or users' sakes) not to REQUIRE this feature.  For that matter,
almost all the new "processor dependent" features and certainly the fact
that this whole category is now explicitly defined to allow implementors to
decide whether or not to include a feature based on "market demand - or
anything else" is very much an "implementor-oriented" decision rather than
something to improve the language (or make life easier or better for
programmers) - IMHO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael McKernan <mck@pobox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udbtl6q14u.fsf@tivoli.mv.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com> <366DB451.5275@compaq.com> <W8wb2.10697$Jl.3822257@news3.mia> <366EBCFF.4706@compaq.com>`

```
Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com> writes:

> Judson McClendon wrote:
> > 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well. your memory may be better than mine and your archives certainly
are, but as a compiler implementor for COBOL 85, 68 and 61 extended,
I seem to remember eighteen digits being quite prevalent, if not
standardized, long before the '74 standard.  I had always assumed
that the reason was that six six-bit characters fit comfortably into
a 36-bit word, which was common to many large scale machines before
the introduction of the 360.  Was the committee not influenced by
this widespread convention as well as for the reason you cite?

Mike McKernan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: !!! Everything you wanted to know about the current J4 work and status !!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3675643E.26C4@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<74hv1o$ksb@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <366D66DE.803BD99F@boeing.com> <366DB451.5275@compaq.com> <W8wb2.10697$Jl.3822257@news3.mia> <366EBCFF.4706@compaq.com> <udbtl6q14u.fsf@tivoli.mv.com>`

```
Michael McKernan wrote:
> 
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mike McKernan

I was a compiler implementor for all of those as well as the '74 
standard.  However, I was not on any standards committees until 1971, 
so the 18-digit thing was decided before then.  None of the standards 
and/or DOD specs before the '74 standard had a limit.  CODASYL did 
going back to 1970 (the oldest CODASYL spec I have).

I doubt if 6 six-bit characters fitting into anything mattered much 
(the machines I worked on used 48-bit words and 60-bit words).  I 
still feel that it was because of the binary representation.  However, 
I may be wrong.  Probably nobody remembers much from back then.
```

```
Many thanks to R.K.Rayhawk, Kevin Digweed, and David L. Craig.
Here is where I stand now: I have a script, "Edit.script",
 as follows...

PCPROMPT=/home/gapharb
export PCPROMPT
DD_INTSTMTS=$PCPROMPT/mforgw/intstmts
export DD_INTSTMTS
DD_TFPRMRPT=$PCPROMPT/mforgw/tfprmrpt.txt
export DD_TFPRMRPT
DD_TFSTMRPT=$PCPROMPT/mforgw/tfstmrpt.txt

DD_TFPGMLOG=$PCPROMPT/mforgw/tfpgmlog.txt
export DD_TFPGMLOG

export DD_TFSYSTEM
cobrun TFPEDIT "FORM==SPGDUPLY"
~
This sets my input and output files and runs my program (TFPEDIT.CBL),
passing FORM=SPGDUPLY into linkage.  (I called Micro Focus to find
out about the double equal signs ("FORM=SPGDUPLY" gave me FORM(SPGDUPLY)).
I also found out that running a script seems to create it's own
"temporary" shell - I tried using the script just to set the files
and then, after running the script, I typed the cobrun TFPEDIT "FORM==
SPGDUPLY" at the command line.  My environment variables weren't still
set.  Putting an env command within the script and then typing it after
the script ended seemed to prove it.
What I want to do now is to somehow set the parm "FORM=SPGDUPLY" as a
variable which I can pass into the script, so I can type Edit.script
"FORM==SPGDUPLY" or Edit.script "FORM=SPGT0001" or whatever, rather
than having to edit the script whenever I want to change the parm.
Does anybody know how ? Also, how do you do comments in a script?
By the way, I did buy a book (and here's my version #) - AIX Version
4.3 System User's Guide: Operating System and Devices.  I also borrowed
UNIX Applications Programming Mastering the Shell [Ray Swartz] and
UNIX Shell Programming [Lowell Jay Arthur & Ted Burns].

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
