###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74jqi0$hpj$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <731vhu$2a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36554E02.8DEA7046@mfltd.co.uk> <74hbjn$40b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <74hbjn$40b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
 <bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
> I also found out that running a script seems to create
> it's own "temporary" shell - I tried using the script
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> typing it after the script ended seemed to prove it.

Shell scripts execute in a child shell instance, with an
environment inherited from the parent shell.  The child
cannot change the parent's environment by design.  However,
there is a shell command, . (a period, pronounced "dot") in
Bourne, Korn, and POSIX shells, and source in C-shell, that
treats the file of commands defined in its argument as
though they were input to the shell processing the source
command.  The /etc/profile, $HOME/.login, and other
shell/session initialization files are all processed by
this mechanism.  Shell source files do not need to be
executable, and probably shouldn't be.

> What I want to do now is to somehow set the parm
> "FORM=SPGDUPLY" as a variable which I can pass into the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> comments in a script?

The foillowing should give you some direction:

#!/bin/sh
#
# This is a comment.  It starts with a # and goes to
                    # the end of the line.  So this is also
                    # a comment, and this, too, but any
                    # stuff to the left of the #s is not.

# The 1st line is a special comment that tells the shell
# invoking this shell script to use /bin/sh as the child
# shell to process this script.

# The following shows how to get a parameter into a script.
# For details, try "man sh" or your book on the shell.
# Hint: "[" is usually a hard link to the "test" executable
#       or a shell built-in alias of the shell built-in
#       "test" command.

if [ $# -ne 1 -o "$1" = '?' ] # If number of args not one arg or arg one is '?'
then echo "Syntax: $0 script_argument_1"
     exit 1
fi  # End of if -- fi is if spelled backwards

echo script_argument_1 is \"$1\"

> By the way, I did buy a book (and here's my version #) -
> AIX Version 4.3 System User's Guide: Operating System and
> Devices.  I also borrowed UNIX Applications Programming
> Mastering the Shell [Ray Swartz] and UNIX Shell
> Programming [Lowell Jay Arthur & Ted Burns].

Knowledge is power, especially in UNIX.  Get it off
the pages and into your head, and you'll do well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208202935.07840.00003801@ng20.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74hbjn$40b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Congrats! You are progressing quite well.

I agree that the Korn dot (.) command or C source command are probably the
right way for you to go as of now. 

But keep in mind, since you are doing a system and not just a script or two,
that the effect of this easy learning approach is to leave all those variables
set. And the effect could accumulate, and there are some 'down stream' programs
that you may want to _not_ see all of your current COBOL program files.

So when invoking shells, as you say, the vars are only set within that context
and when you exit, presto they are gone. Which is disconcerting in the
begining, and you will find your way around that with dot/source. But you are
missing the advantage of the automatic cleanup by the pop out of the shell with
exit.  That is okay, you will be able to get started this way. But until you
find script coding strategies that expoit the inherent design of UNIX you will
now need a slight amount of clean up scripts to get rid of those variables so
they will not cause problems down stream and will not cause problems on the
next days run.

The filenames need clearing at some point, and the variable you tell us you
want to be rather more dynamic, "FORM==SPGDUPLY" needs clearing, so tomorrow
does not inadvertantly default to the previous days setting.  As you get used
to UNIX, this clearing will simply be an automatic feature of exiting the
shell.

It is good that you have the export commands after setting the variables.
Technically that is not going to be necessary if you execute via dot/source.
You may wish to note that now so that you are not confused later.

What I mean is that if you use the dot/source approach in the begining (which
is a good idea), your export commands are irrelevant. You might accidentally
leave a few off which will still work for now. And then when you do improve a
few scripts to actually instantiate a complete shell, the only vars you will
see are the ones you had willy-nilly exported, and the others (lacking an
export) will at the improvement point 'drop out'. So even though you learn to
use dot/source, do not unlearn the export. Later you will need to be thorough
in your review of that issue.

((for completeness let me mention setenv in C shell as an analog to export in
Korn shell; even though you have made it clear you will be unlikely to use C
shell, this rounds out the picture and you can return to it when ready.))

Do you have InfoExplorer on that system?  Have you  gotten help to see to it
that all the supporting manuals are on line (mounted/installed)?  You can do a
search in InforExplorer for 'script', ... maybe start the coffee pot before you
do.

There  are man pages for sh ksh & csh: although much of that is for highly
technical invocations, those pages can be instructive. 

Your system has many script files already. The  icon used by aixterm identifies
which files are scripts (unfortunately if you just click them, they exeute, so
don't do that). You can use a text editor to view scripts as a tutorial
technique.  Many of IBMs scripts are heavily documented, although not for
script educational purposes, it is more for the functions they serve. The
comments help explain enough that you can pick up syntax tricks well just by
studying existing shells scripts.

You can view scripts with cat|pg or just pg. Get into a directory and just cat
a few files that you know or suspect are script files, that is harmless. Some
will be other kinds of files. When you find interesting ones just go back with
pg to view it slowly.

(In passing let me note for you that UNIX systems routinely bounce back and
forth between Bourne Shell and Korn Shell. As pointed out, that initial comment
line in some scripts is really a control mechanism that will switch the
identity of the shells to be used (in contrast to the shell identified for you
when you logged in). You appear to be sticking to Bourne Shell, and anything
you can do to keep things simple for now is a good idea; but don't shy away
from Korn Shell as you do the self teaching efforts.  You will soon be using
Korn Shell heavily.)

Keep up the good work, and let us know how COBOL treats you in the new
environment.  Please let us know how you re-implement your front end.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366E565B.18AF1185@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981118151833.22086.00000986@ng17.aol.com> <731vhu$2a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36554E02.8DEA7046@mfltd.co.uk> <74hbjn$40b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com wrote:

[snip details of progress so far]

> I also found out that running a script seems to create it's own
> "temporary" shell - I tried using the script just to set the files
> and then, after running the script, I typed the cobrun TFPEDIT "FORM==
> SPGDUPLY" at the command line.  My environment variables weren't still
> set.

Yes, when "running a script" you are really invoking a "non-interactive
command shell" - that is, a command shell which is taking it's commands
from a file. It's no different to typing "sh" or "ksh" on the command
line and getting a new level of *interactive* shell.

Bourne and Korn shells have the "." command to say to the current
shell "execute the named file in the current process (ie, as if each
line had been typed at the prompt)".
So if you have the script "setvars" in the current directory, the
shell command ". ./setvars" will execute "setvars" in the *current*
shell, then return to interactive mode. Note that this command also
works for non-interactive mode, where it will resume execution of
the script after the "." (rather than going interactive). This
effectively lets you include common "library" code into a script.

> What I want to do now is to somehow set the parm "FORM=SPGDUPLY" as a
> variable which I can pass into the script, so I can type Edit.script
> "FORM==SPGDUPLY" or Edit.script "FORM=SPGT0001" or whatever, rather
> than having to edit the script whenever I want to change the parm.

Again, in both Bourne and Korn shell: The command line parameters can
be accessed with the "numeric variables" $0 thru $9. $0 is the name
of the script itself, $1 is the first real parameter. $# tells you how
many parameters have been passed. So you can say something like:

cobrun TFPEDIT $1

then run the script with

scriptname FORM==SPGDUPLY

or, try:

cobrun TFPEDIT "FORM==$1"

and run it with:

scriptname SPGDUPLY

> Also, how do you do comments in a script?

Preceed them with a hash (#). Note that these are in-line comments
(which extend to the end of the line), so you can:

# This is a comment block
# this is the 2nd line of the comment block
cobrun blah  # this is an in-line comment for "cobrun blah"

Cheers, Kev.
```

---

```
for viewing "FBA" or "FBM" files - paying attention to the "carriage control
characters".

If you can find someone who can help you with this, you should be able to
continue using WRITE AFTER ADVANCING *and* seeing the output on your
terminal as you expect.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I write 2 blank lines before printing some output?

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208214624.05904.00001849@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74g9vv$ea8@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```

In article <74g9vv$ea8@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:

>If you can find someone who can help you with this, you should be able to
>continue using WRITE AFTER ADVANCING *and* seeing the output on your
>terminal as you expect.

This doesn't work in VSE either, when viewing through ICCF. The ICCF manual
even says it doesn't work. If one wants to see a blank line when displaying a
listing on the terminal, one needs to write a blank line, not use WRITE ...
AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES, or the equivalent in other languages, either with ASA
carriage control or the machine carriage control character.

Mark A. Young
```

```
When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates,
associated with the position(s) offered; doing otherwise leads many to
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to
determine rates.

DD
```

<a id="t-8ec9d7ef3c"></a>
## Thread: assignments — 2 msgs

### assignments

- **From:** ag <geau@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366C6319.6D375465@earthlink.net>`

```
Hi All,

I need to brush up on my Cobol and I'm looking for some good "homework"
assignments.  Basically, I need 5 or 6 problems dealing in batch and
interactive updates.

Anyone know of a good source?  Thanks.

Andrew
```

#### ↳ Re: assignments

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366D7A54.E059CBE2@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<366C6319.6D375465@earthlink.net>`

```
One idea:  Accept (after prompt) an unsigned 5digit integer representing
the number of days since the beginning of the 20th century (19010101 = day
zero) and display the resulting date in CCYYMMDD format.  Opposite problem,
accept any date between 1901-2099 and return (or display) the first
number.  Good practice in MOD arithmetic and Julian date conversion.  Have
fun!

ag wrote:

> I need to brush up on my Cobol and I'm looking for some good "homework"
> assignments.  Basically, I need 5 or 6 problems dealing in batch and
> interactive updates.
```

<a id="t-9dc8871e91"></a>
## Thread: ShellExecute Help — 4 msgs

### ShellExecute Help

- **From:** dougb1@cwix.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366c3190.256349050@news.icix.net>`

```
I'm trying to use the ShellExecute API call in my MF 4.0 application.
I want it to open a file selected from a pull down menu.  The file is
a Word 6.0 document (a form the customer can print out).  I'm
developing it on a NT 4.0 machine and when I run it on my machine, the
document opens in MS Word fine.  I moved the app to a Win95 machine
that doesn't have MS Office on it and it doesn't work.  I checked the
file associations in Explorer and Wordpad is the app that should open
the file.  If I double click on the file name, it opens in Wordpad as
I'd expect.  I can see the WordPad icon pop up for a few seconds, then
disappears.  I also tried it on a Win98 machine without MS Office
installed and it behaves the same way.

Here's the call as I have it coded in my program:

 Call WIN32API "ShellExecuteA"
         using by value SH-Handle
               by reference SH-Operation
               by reference SH-FileName
               by reference SH-Parameters
               by reference SH-Directory
               by value SH-ShowCmnd
         returning wreturncode.
     If wreturncode < 32
         display "ShellExecute wreturncode = "wreturncode
     end-if.

SH-Handle is the handle of the window currently in focus retrieved by
the GetActiveWindow API call.  SH-Operation is 'open' (null
terminated), SH-FileName is the file name without the path (null
terminated), SH-Parameters is null, SH-Directory is the path to the
file(null terminated) and SH-ShowCmnd is zero.  The return value is >
32 which the SDK says indicates a good status (sometimes it's very
large like +7095 and other times it's +33).  The only thing I'm not
sure of is the mention of DDE in the SDK for this call.  I've not
worked with DDE before so it's new to me.

Any suggestions?

Doug Blaze
```

#### ↳ Re: ShellExecute Help

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RFYa2.2868$3D5.3946@news.rdc1.md.home.com>`
- **References:** `<366c3190.256349050@news.icix.net>`

```
Look up article Q124133 in the SDK Knowledge Base. It might apply. It
suggests using SW_SHOWNORMAL for nShowCmd.


dougb1@cwix.com wrote in message <366c3190.256349050@news.icix.net>...
>I'm trying to use the ShellExecute API call in my MF 4.0 application.

<<SNIP>>
>
>Any suggestions?
>
>Doug Blaze
>
```

#### ↳ Re: ShellExecute Help

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366ea2da.350887479@news.icix.net>`
- **References:** `<366c3190.256349050@news.icix.net>`

```
OK, I got it to work.  The secret appears to be putting spaces in
SH-Directory and specifying the filename with the complete path.  I
ended up using SW_ShowDefault (value 10) for SH_ShowCmnd and it works
fine - once I got it work I didn't fool with it!

Example:  SH-Directory = <spaces>
          SH-FileName = c:\progra~1\myapp\test.doc (null terminated)

Doug



dougb1@cwix.com wrote:

>I'm trying to use the ShellExecute API call in my MF 4.0 application.
>I want it to open a file selected from a pull down menu.  The file is
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>Doug Blaze
```

#### ↳ Re: ShellExecute Help

- **From:** Frank Pohlmann <Frank.Pohlmann@baeurer.de>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366D39DE.386CB96C@baeurer.de>`
- **References:** `<366c3190.256349050@news.icix.net>`

```
I had the same problem in the same environment.
One solution is, to change the settings for Word documents in file
associations. If you swith off "DDE", it works (but I don't know why).
Another way (I use this solution) is to write a small C++ programm, that
you call instead of ShellExecute. This programm get some parameters, e.g.
the document name and just makes the same ShellExecute. This works.

Frank
```

### ODBC Preprocessor

- **From:** "Galen Gautreaux" <galen@eatel.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IzVa2.74669$gI1.15515@news2.giganews.com>`

```
I am writing a Microfocus COBOL program that will execute on a Windows95 PC.

This program will select some rows from an Oracle database, and populate a
Foxpro database.

We are currently using Access (and ODBC) to perform this function.

I want to write a COBOL program to replace Access, and add more intelligence
to this process.

Where do I get the SQL pre-processor for ODBC?
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC Preprocessor

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-12-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366d5648.1345552@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<IzVa2.74669$gI1.15515@news2.giganews.com>`

```
On Mon, 7 Dec 1998 13:00:12 -0600, "Galen Gautreaux" <galen@eatel.com>
wrote:

>I am writing a Microfocus COBOL program that will execute on a Windows95 PC.

>This program will select some rows from an Oracle database, and populate a
>Foxpro database.

>We are currently using Access (and ODBC) to perform this function.

>I want to write a COBOL program to replace Access, and add more intelligence
>to this process.

>Where do I get the SQL pre-processor for ODBC?

Hmm. first, its *bad* to mix programming languages. I suppose you are
porting Access Basic(and embedded SQL) to COBOL and SQL.

So what I would do first is abstract two calls

CALL EXECSQL USING SQL-STRING
CALL PREPARESQL USING SQL-STRING

then compare compilers, and choose whichever you want.


gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

```
Acucorp has a product called Acu4GL for ODBC that allows you to access Databases
through ODBC using standard COBOL syntax.

With release 4.2 (Available March/99) they will also have a product called
AcuSQL, which includes a SQL preprocessor and associated runtime libraries.

See www.acucorp.com for details.
```

```
Igor wrote:

> Ralph Jones <leclay@ibm.net> wrote:
> * Why bother with the small stuff, besides aimed fire is always superior to
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> measured in tons.

It's measured in Foot/pounds. I.E. Bullet Weight; 900 grains Muzzle Velocity;
1950 fps, Muzzle Energy; 7591 ft/lbs Close to "4 tons" you could tweek the load
up to 8000 ft/lbs.  That is what you get when you drop 8000 lbs one foot. :-)

rjj
```

```
The "after 2" construct will not work unless the file is
LINE SEQUENTIAL, which is different from SEQUENTIAL.
Might that be your problem?  You could also just try
doing two writes of blank lines ...
```

<a id="t-77a27b205b"></a>
## Thread: Another proposal — 99 msgs

### Another proposal

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be>`

```
As someone made a few days ago the ELSE-IF proposal - which I find
very interesting - I would also propose what I find an amelioration of
the language.
In Cobol, we use levels for the data in file, working-storage and
linkage sections. The 01 level might be considered as the record
structure name, compared to other languages.
Example:
01 date1.
	02 year  pic 9999.
	02 month pic 99.
	02 day pic.
01 date2.
	02 year pic 9999.
	02 month pic 99.
	02 day pic.
If you want to move month of date1 to month of date2, you have to
write:
	move month of date1 to month of date2.
I, personnaly, would prefer:
	move date1.month to date2.month.

I find it more concise (less verbose than 'move ... of...') and easyer
to read and manipulate with an editor. And for a succession of levels,
it would be easy to write:
01 date1.
	02 year.	
		03 part1 pic 99.
		03 part2 pic 99.
	02 month pic 99.
	02 day pic.
01 date2.
	02 year.	
		03 part1 pic 99.
		03 part2 pic 99.
	02 month pic 99.
	02 day pic.
You can write:
	move date1.year.part1 to date2.year.part1
	
Easy, isn't it?

Some reactions?

Alain Reymond
```

#### ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be>`

```
And the bad news is ...

This is exact proposal was considered and REJECTED by the Standards
committee early in the development of the next Standard.  They thought so
"poorly" of it that they didn't even put it in the list of things to
re-visit for the Standard after this draft.

Alain Reymond wrote in message <366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be>...
>As someone made a few days ago the ELSE-IF proposal - which I find
>very interesting - I would also propose what I find an amelioration of
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Please fill me in why they felt soo poorly of the idea.  I think it
makes name qualification more intuitive and I can see how it would lead
to more readable code by way of more consistent and descriptive names. 
I won't bother getting into the details of my viewpoint, they may be old
hat by now and throughly discredited by "the" commitee.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com>`

```
Scott Ramey wrote:
> 
> Please fill me in why they felt soo poorly of the idea.  I think it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Contrary to what Bill said, I don't recall that it was REJECTED (in 
big letters like Bill seems to feel necessary) out of hand.  We 
discussed it and it was put aside for consideration for the next 
standard.  We did not want to add it to this one because it was 
proposed too late in the game and the actual proposal was very cursory 
and did not consider all of the ramifications of such a change.  It 
was mentioned that some compilers do not require a space after a 
period, and for those this idea would be a disaster.  Obviosly, it is 
not at all COBOL-like.  The next idea will probably be to remove the 
MOVE statement and use = instead and change the = for relational 
conditions to ==.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com>`

```


Don Nelson wrote:
[snip]

>  We did not want to add it to this one because it was
> proposed too late in the game and the actual proposal was very cursory
> and did not consider all of the ramifications of such a change.  It
> was mentioned that some compilers do not require a space after a
> period, and for those this idea would be a disaster. 

Sounds like a manageable problem.  If the period is preceded by a valid
group name and followed by either a valid group or field name then the
period could be inferred as being used to join a qualifier(s) to a field
name.  And, if the syntax were goofed up somehow, the result would be
very traditional COBOL, 50 or so compiler error messages, one of which
actually means something.

As far as some compilers not requiring spaces following periods,
considering the period is usually a terminator, not having at least one
or two spaces doesn't lend itself to readable code.  So enforcing the
rule that a terminating period must be followed by white space wouldn't
be a bad standard to enforce.  Generally speaking, for the code to be
easily read and debugged the period should be followed by a new line. 
Having two statements on one line isn't a good practice.  

> Obviosly, it is not at all COBOL-like.  The next idea will probably be to remove the
> MOVE statement and use = instead and change the = for relational
> conditions to ==.

This sounds at least equally unCOBOL-like.  

The only reasonable argument I see here for not accepting the proposal
is that it came late in the game.  I don't accept the "disaster"
argument or "not at all COBOL-like" contention.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com>`

```
> As far as some compilers not requiring spaces following periods,
> considering the period is usually a terminator, not having at least one
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Having two statements on one line isn't a good practice.

I haven't yet seen a compiler which required a space after all periods. 
You can have a period in column 72 followed by sequence numbers.   I
once wrote a COBOL program which determined record sizes, putting size
in columns 73-80 and displacements in columns 1-6.  One thing I had to
consider was that a period and a decimal point use the same symbol.
   05   NAME-1    PIC 99.
                  VALUE 99.
   05   NAME-2    PIC Z9.9.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366FE06B.C3D777AC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <ezFkEB8I#GA.119@upnetnews03>`

```
> >Howard, I once had similar problems with:
> >      PIC ZZZ,ZZZ..
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>        05  filler                 PIC X VALUE ".".

Actually, I think my compiler handled the code correctly.  My program
had to also handle it correctly.  I am not surprised that other
compilers got confused.  It is confusing having the same symbol meaning
two different things.

Looking at your solution, I note that very few people use "B" to pad out
their display values.  Most people have a second line with FILLER VALUE
SPACE.  Thinking about it, I think that it is probably a good idea. 
Everybody is familiar with the multiple line method, so let's KISS for
the junior programmer who will be maintaining.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uLOor1IJ#GA.331@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <ezFkEB8I#GA.119@upnetnews03> <366FE06B.C3D777AC@home.com>`

```
Howard, do you intentionally strip all references (name, msg #, etc.) from
all of the "quotes" (here I am using the term loosely) from postings that
you respond to, or is that a feature of the news reader that you are using?
It is difficult for me to keep track of who said what in the "quotes" you
include in your responses.  I have to expand my newsreader "view" to include
all messages, instead of just new messages, to determine which c.l.c.
"players" are implicated in the context of your inputs.

Your edited "quote" (included below) starts with Leif's statement on
problems he'd experienced, and is followed by a comment I posted on one of
the possible solutions to those problems.  It is only by counting the ">"
indentation characters that it becomes clear that the "quote" is from (at
least) two separate individuals.  Lack of attribution of remarks can lead to
mis-attribution of remarks (in the minds of ng participants,) and
potentially cause bandwidth to be wasted on subsequent postings clarifying
who actually said what to whom.

When you quote, please don't delete the attribution detail.

Late-comers to a thread can then more easily follow the gist of the
discussion and know who the posters are without having to necessarily read
(or reread) earlier postings in the thread.

But (more importantly :-) I myself will then be able to more easily spot any
responses you have directed at my own postings.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <366FE06B.C3D777AC@home.com>...
>> >Howard, I once had similar problems with:
>> >      PIC ZZZ,ZZZ..
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>the junior programmer who will be maintaining.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<74p0j6$qda$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <366F355E.567AEF72@bdssoftware.com> <366FE1AD.2C60713E@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <366FE1AD.2C60713E@home.com>...
>
>Hmmm.  I've never played with this.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I bet it should be legal, but that compilers would mess it up.

I did! In the second move statement, the numeric literal is invalid.
Basically, the ANS85 rule is that a single decimal point may appear
anywhere within a numeric literal, except in the rightmost position.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367130E8.46D7509D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 'Twas Wed, 9 Dec 1998 09:40:14 -0600, when "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>        PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.,  OCCURS 2.

Now that's interesting.  A valid use for an (invisible) comma .  What's
interesting is that a comma is also an editing character.  I suppose you
could also have:
         PIC ZZZ,ZZZ,,  OCCURS 2.  if you want to end with a comma
or       PIC ZZZ.ZZZ,,  OCCURS 2.  in countries where commas are used as
decimals.
    ugly, but workable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Concerning ending PICTURE clauses with EITHER a comma or a period.

My '85 Standard is "on route" from CA to IL, but I am POSITIVE that there
was a substantive change concerning this, i.e. a change from '74 to '85.
Could someone who has an '85 Standard, please look up

A) the substantive change (not affecting) description of a change on final
periods and commas in PICTURE clauses

B) Confirm exactly what the '85 Standard really does and does not allow in
this area.

P.S. I will almost guarantee that adding a punctuation comma (rather than a
PIC comma) NEVER makes a difference in either the validity or meaning of a
PICTURE clause.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36719d3d.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
About your P.S. below, I agree that it *should* not make any difference.
With all the compilers I have (Realia, MF, Fujitsu, GCOS8, ...) it *does*,
with ZZZ,ZZZ.  OCCURS being invalid and ZZZ,ZZZ.,  OCCURS being
valid (and doing what I wanted). I guess the standards committee or
at least all the implementors were confused about the (overly 'precise')
specification of periods followed by space or implied space or understood
space (e.g. in col 73) or between cards. I'm not quite clear myself on this,
and don't really understand why ZZZ,ZZZ.,  OCCURS is okay while
ZZZ,ZZZ.  OCCURS is not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eAcDlRgJ#GA.71@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <36719d3d.0@news1.ibm.net> <74shvm$t03$1@news.igs.net> <3671eead.0@news1.ibm.net> <74t3tr$b3d$1@news.igs.net> <36726c07.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36726c07.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Donald Tees wrote in message <74t3tr$b3d$1@news.igs.net>...
>>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3671eead.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>


It seems to the ALTER statement given here, albeit now classified as
obsolete,  if Leif has transcribed it accurately, seems to fail the COBOL
Standard on 2 counts:

1. I believe the Standard form for an ALTER construct is (was?):
ALTER procname-1 TO [PROCEED TO] (_not_ GOTO) procname-2.
However, since the PROCEED TO portion appears to be optional, I could assume
(except for 2. below) that the statement would be valid if there is a
procedure named GOTO existing in the program :-)

2. I don't believe the qualification of procname-2 with a DEPENDING ON
clause was ever allowed in the Standard.  That is, the DEPENDING ON clause
should only be applicable to a stand-alone GO TO construct, as in

GO [TO] procname-1 [procname-2 .... DEPENDING ON integer-data-name].

Based on this analysis, it is hard to believe that the source program which
contains the given ALTER code snippet could ever have compiled, unless it
was compiled by an archaic, pre-Standard compiler, or a non-compliant
post-Standard compiler.

Leif - did you observe any executable code that matched with this bizarre
source code?  If it was really necessary, the given code snippet might be
(at least initially) remediated into compilable condition by the following
substitute code (although I abhor the use of the ALTER verb, I did use it on
some Q&D code years ago :-)

IF day-of-the-week = 1
     ALTER paragraph-styles TO PROCEED TO goto
ELSE
     IF day-of-the-week = 2
         ALTER paragraph-styles TO PROCEED TO author-name.

Or, if the GOTO in the given code should have been transcribed as PROCEED
TO, in which case there is obviously only one value for day-of-the-week that
would have any significance, the only other interpretation would seem to be:

IF day-of-the-week = 1
         ALTER paragraph-styles TO PROCEED TO author-name.

My final observation on this matter, despite having expended this time on
it, is that I should probably have just agreed with Donald's well-considered
advice -  to head for the hills. <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3674b71c.13865596@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <36719d3d.0@news1.ibm.net> <74shvm$t03$1@news.igs.net> <3671eead.0@news1.ibm.net> <74t3tr$b3d$1@news.igs.net> <36726c07.0@news1.ibm.net> <eAcDlRgJ#GA.71@upnetnews03> <36733f44.38426298@netnews> <etSXOnvJ#GA.226@upnetnews03>`

```
>>>>>The-main-line.
>>>>>       alter paragraph-styles to goto
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>What do I do?

assuming day-of-week is y2k-compliant in your compiler, don't worry
about doing anything.

otherwise, assume the code means that depending on which day it is,
go to the authors subroutine.

thus, there are 7 subroutines, and one is run per day.

also, there may be 7 authors, and only 1 alters the code per day, and
the code is re-structured for the author of the day.


gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Page XVII-44, paragraph 2.1(40) reads as follows:

"(40)    PICTURE CLAUSE (1 NUC).  The insertion character '.' (period) or
',' (comma) may be used as the last character of a PICTURE character-string,
provided it is immediately followed by the separator period terminating the
data description entry,"

Looks to me like "05  ABC OCCURS 20 PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.." is allowed but "05  ABC
PIC ZZZ.ZZZ. OCCURS 20." is not.

Does that help?

    -Chuck Stevens


William M. Klein wrote in message <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>...
>Concerning ending PICTURE clauses with EITHER a comma or a period.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36719ff0.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote in message <74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
>Page XVII-44, paragraph 2.1(40) reads as follows:
>"(40)    PICTURE CLAUSE (1 NUC).  The insertion character '.' (period) or
>',' (comma) may be used as the last character of a PICTURE
character-string,
>provided it is immediately followed by the separator period terminating the
>data description entry,"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Does that help?

no it does not, because ZZZ,ZZZ.,  OCCURS 2 is valid (at least 5 compilers
agree
on that) and produces an edited result ending in a period, thus the comma is
not
being treated as an editing character, yet the period is not 'immediately
followed
by the separator period terminating the data description entry'.

>
>William M. Klein wrote in message
<74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>...
>>Concerning ending PICTURE clauses with EITHER a comma or a period.
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>P.S. I will almost guarantee that adding a punctuation comma (rather than
a
>>PIC comma) NEVER makes a difference in either the validity or meaning of a
>>PICTURE clause.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>on that) and produces an edited result ending in a period, thus the comma
is
>not
>being treated as an editing character, yet the period is not 'immediately
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>


Leif,
  Have you run any or all of this compiles with their ANSI flagging on?  I
would be curious if any of them flag this as an extension.  My guess is that
it IS an extension - whether the compilers know it or not.  (There just
really REALLY isn't ever supposed to be a time that the separator comma can
be used and a separator space cannot.)
```

```
this is an interesting comment...

> (There just
>really REALLY isn't ever supposed to be a time that the separator comma can
>be used and a separator space cannot.)

That's why this whole topic is interesting...
It shows how overloaded the period is and how hard it is to get it right
despite (or because of) the committee's attempt to be precise.

I just tried MicroFocus (some of the others, e.g. GCOS8, don't have
ANSI flags), first with FLAG"ANS74" then with FLAG"ANS85". It makes
no difference at all.
Here is my test program.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    PICZ.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  98/12/11
000600     -REVISED:  98/12/11.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  MY-DATA1.
001900     02  X                       PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.     OCCURS 2.       <===
error
002000
002100 01  MY-DATA2.
002200     02  Y                       PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.,    OCCURS 2.       <===
ok
002300
002400 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
002500 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
002600     MOVE 123456 TO X (1)
002700     DISPLAY    "[" X (1) "]"
002800     MOVE 123456 TO Y (1)
002900     DISPLAY    "[" Y (1) "]"
003000     STOP RUN
003100     .
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7573co$q9s@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <36719ff0.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s9a6$s2h@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36768FC2.FDBCEE1F@home.com> <3676ec4c.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3676ec4c.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>Howard Brazee wrote in message <36768FC2.FDBCEE1F@home.com>...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My research (so far) indicates:

1) Having a difference between a separator comma and a separator space is
DEFINITELY non-Standard.

2) No compiler that allows the comma separator to end a PICTURE clause -
which comes before another clause "flags"it as an extension.

3) No vendor (that I have talked to) that currently allows this has any
current intention of either flagging it or making it an error.

4) No vendor currently documents this as an extension (or intends to
document it as an extension)

5) It is currently undocumented and non-Standard - Therefore, "caveat
user" - or "User beware".  Anything that currently works but isn't
documented and isn't Standard has a REALLY good chance of disappearing some
time in the future - without warning and possibly just as a side effect of
some other fix.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3677181d.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <36719ff0.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s9a6$s2h@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36768FC2.FDBCEE1F@home.com> <3676ec4c.0@news1.ibm.net> <7573co$q9s@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7573co$q9s@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>...
>>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3676ec4c.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>My research (so far) indicates:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>some other fix.


The problem I had was the semantics. What I wanted was that ZZZ,ZZZ. OCCURS
2
should give me two occurrences of an edited item ending with a period so
that
MOVE 1234 TO it would produce '1,234.'  My compilers all objected to that.
Now it seems that placing a comma after the period: ZZZ,ZZZ., OCCURS 2 gives
me the desired effect without any of the compilers having a problem with it.
The way I interpret this is that the period in ZZZ,ZZZ.  OCCURS 2 is
followed
by a space and is therefore the end of the description, while in the second
case, the period is followed by a comma and is therefore not terminating
the data-item description; since the comma is followed by a space it
is not an editing character and we get the desired effect.
If my interpretation is correct then the behavior that we have observed is
indeed standard conforming and there is no need to worry.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<757aed$4rn@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <36719ff0.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s9a6$s2h@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36768FC2.FDBCEE1F@home.com> <3676ec4c.0@news1.ibm.net> <7573co$q9s@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <3677181d.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3677181d.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>William M. Klein wrote in message <7573co$q9s@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>documented and isn't Standard has a REALLY good chance of disappearing
some
>>time in the future - without warning and possibly just as a side effect of
>>some other fix.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Now it seems that placing a comma after the period: ZZZ,ZZZ., OCCURS 2
gives
>me the desired effect without any of the compilers having a problem with
it.
>The way I interpret this is that the period in ZZZ,ZZZ.  OCCURS 2 is
>followed
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The problem with your logic (not with how compilers handle it - but with how
the Standard defines things) is:

The Standard doesn't know about  "spaces", "periods", and "commas" - rather
it knows about "separator spaces", "separator periods" "editing symbol
periods" "separator commas" and "editing symbol comma".  In theory
(according to the Standard), there should NEVER be a difference between a
"separator comma" and a "separator space" (while there are BIG differences
with separator periods).  Therefore,

  if and only if
     a separator comma is valid in a specific syntax
 then
    a separator space should also be valid in that same syntax
 end-logic-requirement

In your example, you have a case where the compiler is allowing a separator
comma following a PICTURE clause - which ends with an editing symbol period.
Therefore, (in Standard theory) - either it should also allow a separator
space - or it should prohibit the separator comma.

The rule in the Standard says that a PICTURE clause (actually the "picture
string") can end with either an editing symbol period or comma - but ONLY
when that is the last clause in the data definition.  As your sample is NOT
the last clause in this definition, it is non-conforming (and should - again
in theory - either be flagged as an extension or rejected as a syntax
error).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36775b26.11989303@netnews>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <74s6ju$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <36719ff0.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s9a6$s2h@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36768FC2.FDBCEE1F@home.com> <3676ec4c.0@news1.ibm.net> <7573co$q9s@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 15 Dec 1998 19:43:52 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>My research (so far) indicates:
>
>1) Having a difference between a separator comma and a separator space is
>DEFINITELY non-Standard.

I agree this is the intent of the standard, and the verbiage under the
PICTURE clause about ending a PICTURE with '..' or ',.' appears to be
specifically designed to enforce that concept.

>2) No compiler that allows the comma separator to end a PICTURE clause -
>which comes before another clause "flags"it as an extension.

I just verified that A Series COBOL85 allows it and doesn't flag it.  
See below.
>
>3) No vendor (that I have talked to) that currently allows this has any
>current intention of either flagging it or making it an error.

I don't have any data to contradict that.

>4) No vendor currently documents this as an extension (or intends to
>document it as an extension)

I believe that I have seen it documented it the dark and distant past.  I
have read dozens of Cobol books over the last 25 years, and at least one
of them was a compiler manual which documented this behavior, and
somewhere along the way I read something very ANSIish (probably comentary
on the 1981 draft) which discussed this behavior.

>5) It is currently undocumented and non-Standard - Therefore, "caveat
>user" - or "User beware".  Anything that currently works but isn't
>documented and isn't Standard has a REALLY good chance of disappearing some
>time in the future - without warning and possibly just as a side effect of
>some other fix.

In general you're correct, but in this case you're wrong.  This is very
conventional behavior, even if the standard says otherwise.  

It is rare that the difference between the scanner and the parser are
significant to the language, but here they are.  In the scanner, a period
is only a seperator period if it is followed by a space or end of line.
Similarly a comma is only a seperator comma when followed by space or end
of line.  A seperator comma is the same as whitespace, but to the scanner
PIC ZZZ,,, is the token ZZZ,, followed by one seperator comma.  The other
commas are not separator commas, because they aren't followed by space.
The parser then gets the token ZZZ,, which interprets the commas as
editing characters.  The scanner has to allow commas and periods as part
of the picture token, so they're allowed at the end.

I suppose some quadratarsch could get J4 to rule that PIC ZZZ,,, must be
the same as PIC ZZZ,, which must be the same as PIC ZZZ, which must be the
same as PIC ZZZ and then add those to the validation suite and see if all
the commas are correctly ignored, but what would that serve?  The current
and proposed standards endorse only period for this purpose, but are quiet
on comma (or semicolon).  In the long run I think the best course would be
for J4 to declare such PICTUREs illegal, and for all the compiler vendors
to flag this as an extension.  In the short run J4 should ignore it as
long as possible.

When I suggested "PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.,", (I had to put that in quotes to avoid
,,) I had no idea I was treading on a controversial area.  Of course it
works, I would be upset if a compiler ever did otherwise.  Have we
established which compilers reject this sneaky usage of comma?

Here's my test of Unisys A Series COBOL85 release 44.1A.27.  No matter
what FEDLEVEL I set it never flagged these PICs as extensions.

NEXT+ ...1....*....2....*....3....*....4....*....5....*....6....*....7..

010000$$ TITLE+="ANSI 85 FLAGGING TEST"

010050$$ SET LIST LISTDOLLAR FEDLEVEL=1

010100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

014600 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

015600 DATA DIVISION.

018100 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

019000 01  EDIT-THESE.

019100     02  ZPERIOD         PIC ZZZ.,   OCCURS 4.

019150     02  FILLER          PIC XX      VALUE "==".

019200     02  ZCOMMA          PIC ZZZ,,   OCCURS 4.

050600 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

051200 Z-TEST.

051400     MOVE  10  TO ZPERIOD (1) ZCOMMA (1)

051500     MOVE ZERO TO ZPERIOD (2) ZCOMMA (2)

051600     MOVE 0.8  TO ZPERIOD (3) ZCOMMA (3)

051700     MOVE -10  TO ZPERIOD (4) ZCOMMA (4)

051800     DISPLAY EDIT-THESE.

051990     STOP RUN.

R

#UPDATING

#COMPILING 2781

#ET=1.6 PT=1.3 IO=0.3

#RUNNING 2782

#2782 DISPLAY: 10.         10.== 10,         10,.

#ET=0.1 PT=0.1 IO=0.0  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
wrote:


>Howard, I doesn't matter much that is is valid or that the
>standard should allow for it, as long as all your compilers

when using commas, follow the old adage: keep it simple stupid
if i was a programmer, and saw

02 x pic zz,zzzz

i would add a period

and if I needed something like

34,56.78,90

I would write a display routine and skip the edit format stuff
```

```
DEC COBOL 2.4-863 - compiled as expected, gave expected results.
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1 - same, but I had to change the [ and
] to ( and ).

Interesting.

BTW:  I tried this:

Display Quote Y(1) Quote

The trailing quote did not appear under the OS/390 execution. -- but 
it did under VMS (DEC).

Even '"' failed to show the trailing quote.

That scares me.  I think I will avoid this construct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3673349b.35701524@netnews>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 15:43:06 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Concerning ending PICTURE clauses with EITHER a comma or a period.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>PICTURE clause.

Looking in the current draft I find:
p 324 13.17.38.4 Editing rules (5) says "If the insertion character is the
last symbol in the PICTURE character-string, the PICTURE clause shall be
the last clause of that data description entry and shall be immediately
followed by a separator period.  This results in two consecutive periods
appearing in the data description entry, or in the combination ',.' if the
DECIMAL-POINT IS COMMA clause is used."  Similar wording appears about ','
(comma).

P 329 13.17.38.7 Locale picture precedence rules says "3) If the '.' is
the last symbol in the character string, the PICTURE clause shall be the
last clause in the data description entry and shall be followed
immediately by a seperator period."

By this rule these are AOK:
    05  FIELD1           PIC ZZZ,ZZZ..
    05  FIELD2  OCCURS 2 PIC ZZZ,ZZZ..

This rule appears to rule out my comma example, but I've seen it allowed
in a discussion of this very point.  I think I have seen trailing comma in
the language manual for some compiler, but I've never had cause to use it.
I think I also read this is debates over the 1981 Cobol draft, but I don't
know how or if J4 ever ruled.  That's a problem when you've read the
debates about the standard, you've read stuff that read like the standard
but wasn't in the standard.

'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 17:17:36 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>  Have you run any or all of this compiles with their ANSI flagging on?  I
>would be curious if any of them flag this as an extension.  My guess is that
>it IS an extension - whether the compilers know it or not.  (There just
>really REALLY isn't ever supposed to be a time that the separator comma can
>be used and a separator space cannot.)

You're right, and that's probably the reason for the rules I cite above,
but only if someone goes out of his way to implement those rules.  In the
normal scanning of the language, a period is only a separator if it's
followed by a space or the end of the source line.  By adding a comma (or
semicolon) the period isn't a separator, so it's part of the PICTURE.

And now that I think about it, I also saw a compiler manual which
suggested PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.V OCCURS 2.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZZZ,ZZZ.. (was Another Proposal)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368b337e.3663827@netnews>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366D688C.821E7BFF@bdssoftware.com> <366D6D04.3A77@compaq.com> <366D814A.72CFFE55@bdssoftware.com> <366E9055.C42EFD9F@home.com> <366e99e0.0@news1.ibm.net> <3670bdcf.178658392@netnews> <367130E8.46D7509D@home.com> <367145bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s3ou$n0r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 15:43:06 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>My '85 Standard is "on route" from CA to IL, but I am POSITIVE that there
>was a substantive change concerning this, i.e. a change from '74 to '85.

I think you're right.  I think the 1974 and previous condition was that
this wasn't defined.  Different compilers did different things.  I quote
Burroughs B2500 COBOL (1969) (with thanks to Judson):

"Special insertion editing character period (.) ....  If the insertion
character is the last symbol in the character string, it must be
immediately followed by one of the punctuation characters, semicolon, or
period, followed by a space."

I figure that comma is one of the punctuation characters, but I can't
imagine what any others were.  As I explained before, this is simply the
normal result if the scanner insists that a separator must be followed by
a space (including implied space at end of line).  A scanner which treats
a comma not followed by a space as a separator would get different
results.

I believe X3.23-1985 addressed this previously undefined area, by: 

1)  Declaring that only a period (not comma or semi-colon) could do this
trick, to provide the functionality, albeit by forcing the PIC clause
after all other clauses, and
2)  Leaving trailing comma implicitly a syntax error, thus allowing
compilers which always let comma do this to continue unchanged (allowing
vendors to minimize incompatibility with previous releases).

>P.S. I will almost guarantee that adding a punctuation comma (rather than a
>PIC comma) NEVER makes a difference in either the validity or meaning of a
>PICTURE clause.

That's the intent, but the standard doesn't exactly say that.

Consider the following:

    05  EDIT-ME    PIC ZZZ,,, BLANK ZERO.

So how would you rule:

   A)  Syntax error.
   B)  The picture string contains three Zs.
   C)  The picture string contains three Zs and one comma (implausible).
   D)  The picture string contains three Zs and two commas. 

(Note:  This is part one of a two part quiz.)
```

```
'Twas 31 Dec 1998 09:06:21 GMT, when Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 15:43:06 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>imagine what any others were.  

I just reparsed this, and I realize that there may be only two punctuation
characters:  Semicolon and period.  Odd that comma would be omitted, since
it's the same as semicolon, but this was a pre-ANSI compiler.
```

```
   <some snippage>
>I haven't yet seen a compiler which required a space after all periods.
>You can have a period in column 72 followed by sequence numbers.
  <more snippage>

The Standard requires that source be treated as if an "assumed space" exists
between each source line.  (Obviously with some special rules concerning
continued literals.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10d7bbd537f0037e9896d8@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366d5a86.834099@news3.ibm.net>`

```
In article <366d5a86.834099@news3.ibm.net>, vbandke@ibm.net says...
> On Tue, 8 Dec 1998 11:11:30 -0600, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


I don't think there is an interest in C-fying Cobol and more then there 
would be an interest in Cobol-fying C. What would be nice is 
incorporating useful features from other languages into Cobol whether 
they come from C, FORTRAN or any other language. 

Just think of it as form of language enrichment. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366e3026.207539@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <74jmn8$p1t@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <366d5a86.834099@news3.ibm.net>`

```
If I want to write in C, i use C. That actually what I doing. But like
most of us, I have been working with many different languages: C
Pascal, Fortran, Cobol, etc..., etc...
Each of them has its strengh. So why not use some of the interesting
capabilities found in other languages and allow them into Cobol?
Especially when the proposition, IMHO, does not betray the language.
In that particular case, you can still write
	move part1 of date1.year to part1 of date2.year
The idea is just to give more possibilities to the programmer,
certainly not to replace the OF in the language.

In fact, if you look at Cobol, the big change has been introduced with
the Ansi 85 standard which completely changed the way of writing Cobol
with the introduction of structured programming. So, why not go on?
Even our common languages evolve, so does Cobol...

And there are many other prossibilities. I regret the lack of real
user defined functions in Cobol. I think they could be relatively
easely implemented without betraying the spirit of Cobol. But that is
another story...

Mit freundlichen Gr�ssen!

Alain  Reymond

>>And the bad news is ...
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** krw0315@aol.comXP (KRW0315)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com>`

```

In article <366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com>, Scott Ramey
<scottr@bdssoftware.com> writes:

>> 01 date1.
>>         02 year.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Some reactions?

Seems easy enough for those of us who have had exposure to C or Pascal.  But
COBOL is intended (or was) to read like "normal English"; your construct would
obfusticate that intention.  Now, were it an *optional* capability those who
prefer it could use and others could ignore it.  There could be maintenance
problems, however.


kw

remove XP (Chi Rho) to reply directly
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com>`

```
>>         move date1.year.part1 to date2.year.part1
>>
>> Easy, isn't it?

I've done that in other, more terse languages.  But most COBOL
environments which I work in avoid using the same name multiple times. 
Of those remaining, it is solely to allow for MOVE CORRESPONDING.  Since
we can rename values in COPY members, and since we tend to use long
field names which have their qualification as part of the field name,
this wouldn't help much.
    01  ACCOUNT-TEMP-RECORD.
        05   AT-NAME      PIC X(20.
        05   AT-AMOUNT    PIC 9(07)V99.

With this proposal, we could get rid of the "AT-", and have simpler
field names, instead of 
        MOVE AT-NAME            TO PRINT-NAME.
we would have
        MOVE ACCOUNT-TEMP.NAME  TO PRINT.NAME.

Nothing gained here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com>`

```
I use the referencing extensively.  In fact, one of the
standards that I have always followed is that any system
wide variables (like CUSTOMER-NUMBER) *always*
be refered to by the exact same name, and a reference.
So you end up with statements  like:

      MOVE  CUSTOMER-NUMBER OF CUSTOMER-RECORD
           TO CUSTOMER-NUMBER OF ACCOUNTS-RECEIVABLE-RECORD.

Most programmers do not like to *write* code that way,
as it involves a great deal of typing.  However, I have never
yet had anybody say they did not like to read it.  In fact,
the exact opposite is true.


Howard Brazee wrote in message <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com>...
>>>         move date1.year.part1 to date2.year.part1
>>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Nothing gained here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> I use the referencing extensively.  In fact, one of the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think your standard is great.  However, before free form coding it's
entirely possible you might not even get the name and qualifier on one
line of code.  I feel since God  gave me 30 characters for COBOL names
I'm going to use them if it makes the name more descriptive. So the move
statement might end up being:
    MOVE
    CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM OF CUSOTMER-AR-MASTER-RECORD
    TO
    CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM OF ACCTS-RECEIVABLE-DETAIL-RECORD

Now that is a tad verbose and could really take up a bunch of horizontal
bandwith in a card tray.  

Since this discussion started it occurred to me that with the advent of
good GUI text editors, and free form coding, a little cut and pasting
would solve the problem.  I'm toying around with the idea, in the
meantime my field names usually have a four character prefix such as:  
CARM-CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM.

There are no two ways about it though that:
    CUSTOMER-AR-MASTER-RECORD.ACCOUNT-OPENED-DATE.YEAR 
is a lot more readable than
    YEAR OF ACCOUNT-OPENED-DATE OF CUSTOMER-AR-MASTER-RECORD.

So, while the idea of qualification as originally intended by COBOL was
great, it's implementation is less than great.  Using the period would
be a real boon whose benefits while maybe appearing trivial are
pervasive enough to actually be significant.  I think someone on the
committee should have been tuned in enough to say "damn, wish I had
thought of that simple little idea!"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ True Cobol Programmer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36732a7f.33116699@netnews>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net> <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com> <3670b9da.177645685@netnews> <MPG.10daec2ab19c0a1f9896db@news.earthlink.net>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 08:46:08 -0500, when JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net
(Jeff Farkas) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In article <3670b9da.177645685@netnews>, Barticus@att.spam.net says...
>> 'Twas Wed, 09 Dec 1998 18:21:55 -0800, when Scott Ramey
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>could you please educate me and define this term. 

Gladly.  First, you've made a parsing error, you have "true" bound to
"COBOL" as though I'm refering to a programmer of "true COBOL".  I mean a
True Cobol Programmer, and I mean for "True" to bind to "Programmer",
meaning a programmer who is true to Cobol and true to his
Cobol-Programmerness.

Second, being true to Cobol means saying things in English words.  For the
most common things there are symbols such as < and >, but the words are
still available.  To express the concept of one thing containing another
we use the English prepositions:  OF and IN are currently used, but I
recall a Cobol on a CDC 3170 which took FROM.  

I can't think of an English propostion which expresses the concept the
other way.  Therefore even if there is someday a single symbol to
represent OF, the order of qualification will always be minor to major.

Third a True Cobol Programmer is one who writes sentences in Cobolese for
comedic effect, eg, 
PERFORM BEAT-DEAD-HORSE UNTIL FROZEN-OVER IN HELL

This is a specialized form of jargon humor.  It is used by programmers of
any language, but Cobol is the best because it uses so many English words.

And last, the simplest definition of a True Cobol Programmer is one who
agrees with the great vision of All That is Cobol which God bestowed upon
the great programmer Barticus Aurelius Maximus, the one the users call
Thank You Randall, or someone who agrees with that vision except for being
a fuddy-duddy. 

(you asked)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: True Cobol Programmer

```

…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Thank you Randall for your fine answer to my inquiry. I fully agree with 
you that one of the aspects of Cobol that I like the most is that it's  
based upon English and not a symbolic language which makes it both easier 
to read and understand. 
 
On the other hand, I would not mind seeing features added to Cobol that 
would make it a little easier while not loosing the core values that made 
Cobol last as long as it has.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: True Cobol Programmer

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3674A381.5F462D45@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net> <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com> <3670b9da.177645685@netnews> <MPG.10daec2ab19c0a1f9896db@news.earthlink.net> <36732a7f.33116699@netnews> <MPG.10ddf7a5bbd77e529896dc@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Jeff and Randall were discussing the need to add to COBOL.
They expressed a common desire that the language should remain
readable English. This heartens me a great deal. I see English today
I didn't learn in grade, high, or later.  I've even adopted some of it.
As I've expressed to some of you, I am very happy for the evolution
of Evaluate. However, I would have pushed for COMPARE.<G> I've seen
some samples in efforts by readers of this group that our outstanding,
and they reflect a lot of experience.  In the introduction of structured
programming in our shop, Dilbert would not underwrite the
idea of small teams, and weekly reviews. His background was tab, and
punched cards. You wired a board, and you tried it out. When it was
right, you "installed it".  The computer revolution which continues has
made a lot of people uncomfortable until it does something for them.

If I were to be granted a wish now for the future of systems, regardless
of type, it would be that a common language be created.
My vote for immediate placement in the public domain would be
all file management types, and all system features. CODASYL published 
an operating system spec which did not receive much play. Much of what
people go to the new languages for today seems to be due to the lack
of an English vocabulary and definitions that are common.

Locking and unlocking records has a meaning in clerical systems, so do
wait loops. I also remember when color, font, speech, and other graphic
tools were considered the domain of the far out high priced
applications.

Views from a pseudo founder...

Warren Simmons

Jeff Farkas wrote:

-----------more snip --------------

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net> <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com> <74qusv$1hn$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Scott Ramey wrote in message <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com>...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The dot is only "better" if you are used to that.

Me neither.  The biggest difference I see is that the former has to be
on one line.

Either way, in most shops I have worked in they go a different route. 
They use unique field names prefixed by the record name, e.g.
          CR-ACCOUNT-OPENED-YEAR.

I don't see anything wrong with this shop standard.  It gives up MOVE
CORRESPONDING, but also gives up accidental confusion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74rg9f$atc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net> <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com> <74qusv$1hn$1@news.igs.net> <367131BB.95F70A3D@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <367131BB.95F70A3D@home.com>...

>I don't see anything wrong with this shop standard.  It gives up MOVE
>CORRESPONDING, but also gives up accidental confusion.

Neither do I.  The reason for the "one data field, one name"
rule though, is also valid.  Before that rule, you never quite
knew what a field was until you displayed it somewhere.

The real issue, as I see it, is that when the same field is
used in several places, it should be instantly recognized
as such.
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <367131BB.95F70A3D@home.com>...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> as such.

Donald,

I hate this rule:   When I use a variable I would like to think that a
field Customer-No is exactly the same throughout the system.

If I get input-customer-no is this because there is something coming
from outside or because it is a simple redefinition.  If your shop
standards say use the same names then the answer is clear.

I also think that we should consider Database applications in all this.
database tools I have worked with will automatically generate a link
between field names  where they are exactly alike.  This does make life
easy.

Ken
```

- **Message-ID:** `<36714F1C.48023B9E@bdssoftware.com>`
