Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Donald Tees wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> on one line.

This doesn't matter much when "one" line can be 200+ characters.

> 
> Either way, in most shops I have worked in they go a different route.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> CORRESPONDING, but also gives up accidental confusion.

I agree with you Howard, the unique prefix is a workable solution.  It
was usually adapated early on because name qualification was too
verbose.  However, Donald's observation (made somewhere early on in this
thread), has considerable merit.  Given free form coding, big screens,
etc. I think the qualification Donald is doing is more superior to
"unique field names prefixed by the record name."  With a good GUI
editor the verbosity of name qualification can be deftly dispensed with
with a quick cut her and paste there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981211142706.28576.00001208@ng154.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36714F1C.48023B9E@bdssoftware.com>`

```
It is interesting that this discussion prompts some to comment on the value of
getting more on the screen.

Actually I think that neither the OF clause nor the dot syntax addresses the
real issue. I feel that the problem is not that we are taking up screen space
with a qualifier, but taking up space again and again with the same qualifiers.

It would be better to implement some kind of lexically scoped context setter,
as in ...

MANAGE 
   FROM
       from-data-level-2 of 
       from-data-level-1
   TO
      to-data-level-2 of 
      to-data-level-1
 END-MANAGE.

All ambiguous MOVEs would be disambiguated with the currently active data
movement MANAGEment directives.

We could ease the lexical scope complications by implementing this construct as
paragraph domain by default. To get it to apply to a section or an entire
program, you might need something like ...

MANAGE  [IN] SECTION
   FROM
       from-data-level-1
   TO
      to-data-level-1
END-MANAGE.

or ...

MANAGE  [IN] PROGRAM
   FROM
       from-data-level-1
   TO
      to-data-level-1
 END-MANAGE.


We could dodge the fact that some practitioners will not understand lexical
scope extent by requring MANAGEment at PROGRAM level to be the first thing in
the PROCEDURE DIVISION, MANAGEment at SECTION LEVEL to be the first thing in a
SECTION.

Paragraph level data movement MANAGEment statements would be trickier. For some
shops it would be best that industry tools permit a compilation parameter to
force paragraph level data movement MANAGEment statements at the begining of a
paragraph only.

That is the lexical scoping of paragraph level MANAGEment directives could be
very sophisticated, but some shops could suppress the fancy stuff (you want
simplicity in MOVE ... OF ... syntax, we have simplicity, but don't do anything
slick!)

The problem with wasted vertical dimmension, is not the isolated occurence, but
repeated occurence of waste; creating a program 'big picture' that is hard to
see, because almost none of it is on screen at any given time.

The proposed dot notation, does conserve horizontal realestate, a bit.  And
what has not been discussed, it also permits top-down expression of the data
structures, where as classical syntax with OF (or IN) phrases is inverted.

Compared to OF clauses, the unique name with qaulifiers built-in (usually) on
the front, does save space.  But in programs where you are not dealing with
movement back and forth between data structures, but are instead just operating
on a data occurence, there is a penalty in horizontal space and typing out the
front-qualifier over and over again.  So this excellent alternative is by no
means free.

In many shops the generation of the unique data name is a by-product of the use
of data dictionary tools, and shop standards for the DBAs work eliminates any
discussion of this. The names with a uniquely qualifying identifier on front
are there, please enjoy them. 

And although these do help you avoid the OF phrases, the generation of the
names by automata frequently introduces a revisitation of the problem. This is
because in large shops there are many projects and many components in each
project.  Inevetably you get names that look the same all over again.  As in,

   PROJDB01-CURRENT-BAL
   PROJBD01-CURRENT-BAL

or within a project

   PRJDB101-CURRENT-BAL
   PRJDB201-CURRENT-BAL

The unique qualifiers frequently look very similar because the relation between
support staff like DBA and projects will only tolerate relatively small
qualifiers.  The name space gets congested. The desire of DBAs to  keep similar
things together, leads to look-alike (at least look-similar) long before a name
space is exhausted.

The story goes on, though.  Initially in some shops there is a stong purist
policy, that tries to keep the same data element named exactly the same in all
projects, such as 

   PROJDB01-CURRENT-BAL
   PROJBD01-CURRENT-BAL

This usually derives from a very naive concept of data 'control'.  Eventually,
DBA policy may relent and allow names like

   PROJDB01-CURRENT-BAL-INPUT
   PROJBD01-CURRENT-BAL-GL
   PROJRP01-CURRENT-BAL-REPORT

Not always, but sometimes this is in response to complaints about coding errors
on look-similar data names.  The net result is dataname that will look unique,
even if you drop the front qualifier. A similar result flows from the
inexerable change in staff in DBA, where CURREN-BAL may later be generated as
CURR-BAL or CUR-BL.

In the end, the change of policy, inconsitent enforcement, and personnel change
leads to many data layouts that have unique names anyway and the front end
qualifier just becomes all burden no reward.


But even in the less complex environments it is a fact that small qualifiers on
the front of data names leads to similar names and therfore errors. But the
technique is still a very good solution.

OTOH, it would be nice to have a dot syntax, and anything else that will allow
top-down data structure explication.  And I think lexically scoped syntax
directives, such as something similar to a MANAGE statement could safe a lot of
human-machine interface resources in porjects where datanames are identical.

Best Wsihses,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36733e00.38102955@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36714F1C.48023B9E@bdssoftware.com> <19981211142706.28576.00001208@ng154.aol.com>`

```
'Twas 11 Dec 1998 19:27:06 GMT, when rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>MANAGE 
>   FROM
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-MANAGE.

In Pascal WITH does something similar, though it only provides one frame
of reference not two.  By splitting the FROM and TO, you have created a
monster.  What about ADD 1 TO A?  Does this fetch A from the FROM record,
add to it, then store it in the TO record?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981214103644.27910.00000295@ng135.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36733e00.38102955@netnews>`

```
Maybe MANAGE/END-MANAGE would not apply to arithmetic.  It would basically be
intended to ease work and reading on major data movement paragraphs.  Yet there
is some need for it in arithmetic.

I would think that in the example given, ADD 1 TO A, that A is only the 'TO'
field. Unary and Binary arithmetic might easily be disambiguated by convention.
Just lay it out you will see there is a common sense way to do it.  

It is Trinary arithmetic operators that could cause consternation.  A field in
a GIVING clause would be a 'TO' field, yet for the two source fields would be
harder in such cases to have an acceptable convention.  But we could try the
notion that both source fields are 'FROM' fields. And if that is not okay, go
back to brute force; namely OF clauses.

Concerning the other objection, having to hunt down all applicable data
movement MANAGEment directives.  Naw, it won't be that hard,  We could have
compiler options that present an output that tells us what the effective OF
clauses would be.  That is we could automate COBOL programming.  Flip the
switch, and the procedure division displays as though you had entered the OF
clauses.  Just like the compiler presenting nesting level information. The
display is temporary, for program review.

Flip the switch back off and the support facility turns off.  For control of
sysout size we could limit the scope (lexically) with management show
directive.  In other words, don't expand the whole procedure  division, just
show me several paragraphs.

Furthermore, It would not ever be a good idea to get a lot of such directives
going at once.  That would be a symptom of a very bad program design.  (in
which case it is perhaps better to explicate the OF clauses, given the strained
situation).

The core proposal is for paragraph level MANAGEment directives.  You could
potentially disallow SECTION and PROGRAM level directives by parameters and/or
shop standards. For the exact purpose of avoiding difficulty in finding current
operative directives. 

If the lexical scope of a paragraph level MANAGE directive ends at the end of
the paragraph by definition, how could it be hard to find.

The proposal is not intended to untangle the worst spaghetti programs; it is
intended to ease coding, and improve human machine interface efficiency, of
moderately sized programs with reasonable paragraph extent.

The idea is to get more on screen, and to remove clutter if possible, to
improve perception of detail AND the overall picture.  Paragraphs with lots of
OF clauses can be rather difficult to narrow in on. 

Also I would submit that in a program where you might have several major FROM
and/or TO segments you are interested in; that manual coding is less reliable
than would be an automated mechanism like a MANAGEment directive. This would be
especially true where the OF clause group item names are similar. In which case
the programmer almost certainly resorted to mass production editor techniques
to manifest the code. The machine is faster and more reliable than humans, when
dealing with mass production.

That is, I believe in automation, not just as a means to improve speed, but as
a means to improve reliability.

Maintenance in a data movement MANAGEment environment would sometimes involve a
check of a single directive, rather than studying perhaps dozens of MOVEs with
OF clauses. Again, especially useful if the OF group items are similar.

If a program needs to change the source or sink of a paragraph, then sometimes
only a single statement would change.  Automation is a good idea. COBOL can
remain English like and still become more automated. 


We may wish to consider slightly more abstract terms like SINK/SOURCE, rather
than TO/FROM to establish a syntax neutral arrangement for the arithmetic
statements to be supported by MANAGEment directives.

For example,

 MANAGE 
    SOURCE
        from-data-level-2 of 
        from-data-level-1
    SINK
       to-data-level-2 of 
       to-data-level-1
  END-MANAGE.

Anyway, thanks for the comments on this.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367149A5.3CD0AD0C@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net> <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com> <74qusv$1hn$1@news.igs.net>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Scott Ramey wrote in message <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com>...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The dot is only "better" if you are used to that.

Ipso facto, the "of" is only better if you are used to that.  What's
new?

Never the less, having been jarred out of the rut my 80 column heritage
put me in, and strongly attracted having only one unique name for fields
that have the same properties (such as customer accounts), I'm going to
take a whack at qualifiers.  As I mentioned (somewhere in the snipped
out quote) free form coding and better text editors have a lot to do
with it.  Not worrying about col 73 and being able to view 150+ columns
on a 21" screen makes it workable for me.  I say "for me" because I like
the code to be easily read and pleasing to the eye.  The additional
space required for the "of" qualifier takes up a lot more room than the
single dot which can lead to aesthetic problems.  Since I tend to use
long names (frequently right up to 30), having only 60 columns to work
with doesn't work if one of my objectives is to have readable code. 
Part of being readable is seeing enough code on a screen (forget
printouts, in the last 10 or 15 years I rarely do listings) to get at
least a smattering of the "big picture".  When qualifying data names
leads to move that potentially take up four or more rows then when
viewing a screen of procedure division code I may see only a very small
picture.  In this respect using the dot as a conjunction makes a lot
more sense.  Granted, the names get a bit run together with the dot, but
if that's the only sacrifice I think it's worth it and I can easily
adapt (it isn't a problem for C programmers).

Think about it, a dot can be very concise, as in ending this sentence.
Or signal something unending, as some of these threads are . . .  
Or you can definitely state something by driving a stake through your
dot!
Dot's all I have to say.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74rrq8$gsa$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366D1EE0.3C45867A@bdssoftware.com> <19981208093615.06816.00001774@ngol06.aol.com> <366E93B3.A91B81A@home.com> <74mkk5$991$1@news.igs.net> <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com> <74qusv$1hn$1@news.igs.net> <367149A5.3CD0AD0C@bdssoftware.com>`

```
Scott Ramey wrote in message <367149A5.3CD0AD0C@bdssoftware.com>...

>single dot which can lead to aesthetic problems.  Since I tend to use
>long names (frequently right up to 30), having only 60 columns to work
>with doesn't work if one of my objectives is to have readable code.

One of the things that I have found with using qualification
a lot is that data names end up being *much* shorter. The
reason is quite simple ... you do not have nearly as many.

Instead of something like CUSTOMER-RECORD-PRODUCT-CODE,
you end up with PRODUCT-MASTER of CUSTOMER-RECORD.

The increase in line length is not nearly as great as you
might think, though it does take a while for it to "work
it's way through the system".

>> Scott Ramey wrote in message <366F3043.D45D7DAF@bdssoftware.com>...
>>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>Dot's all I have to say.
```

> Also, Cobol is not like other
> languages. It is supposed to be descriptive, not unreadable.

Agreed.  I would save my effort for enhancements in functionality, not
in saving keystrokes.
```

#### ↳ Re: Another proposal - small change...

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10d8b1b49febee049896da@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be>`

```
In article <366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be>, areymond@csi.com says...

<Snip>

> I find it more concise (less verbose than 'move ... of...') and easyer
> to read and manipulate with an editor. And for a succession of levels,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 	move date1.year.part1 to date2.year.part1


Given that it is not possible to implement this in the upcoming version 
of Cobol and it may be difficult to make use of a period to fully qualify 
a data item as you suggest may I make a recommendation.

Let's use the colon. 
move date1:year:part1 to date2:year:part1.

The advantage of the colon is that it is not used frequently in the 
current versions of Cobol and therefore it should not be to difficult to 
implement it in future versions, IMO. 

Also, it is still somewhat consistent with the direction that Cobol is 
headed. Almost anything could be used to perform the same function but it 
would start to make Cobol looking cryptic. For example an exclamation 
could be used but it would look weird.

move date1!year!part1 to date2!year!part1.


Any thoughts???
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another proposal - small change...

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366F3263.62CD48B@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be> <MPG.10d8b1b49febee049896da@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Good idea.  Using the colon avoids the argument that some compilers
can't handle the aditional property we are trying to hang on the
period.  Visually the period is a little cleaner but the colon also has
the quality of making the conjunction stand out a little better without
being obtrusive or lookng strange.

Jeff Farkas wrote:
>

> Let's use the colon.
> move date1:year:part1 to date2:year:part1.

Colon is the substring operator.

Semicolon is unused and used as a list separator in English.

Ken

#### ↳ Re: Another proposal

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<286D84B8A5EADD97.4E0EF67689E251EC.7941C5FA9C45970E@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<366cfe8e.822647@news.skynet.be>`

```
Alain Reymond wrote:
> 
(snip)

> If you want to move month of date1 to month of date2, you have to
> write:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
(snip)
> 
> Some reactions?
> 
> Alain Reymond

My reaction is "interesting" but it looks "un-COBOL-like".  Also, from
the perspective of my shop it wouldn't have much affect.  

Is the use of MOVE DATA-ITEM IN RECORD-A TO DATA-ITEM IN RECORD-B
widespread among c.l.c. readers?  Our style is to prefix data names in
the FD with the filename.  e.g., data items in the Customer Account File
(CSTACTFI) will appear as CSTACT-NAME, CSTACT-ADDRESS, CSTACT-CITY,
etc.   We feel this style has a certain value and lends itself to less
ambiguous procedure code.  On the other hand, it makes the MOVE...IN
syntax fairly useless.  I can recall only coding it two or three times
in my career.
```

Sure is, and it's called PL/1 from IBM.
date2.year.part1 = date1.year.part1

Bob

## Thread: LE 1.8 wierd COBOL abend dump-longish ( x-posted to IBM-MAIN and COBOL ) — 6 msgs

### LE 1.8 wierd COBOL abend dump-longish ( x-posted to IBM-MAIN and COBOL )

- **From:** "Ian Reid" <reid.ian@pmintl.ch>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be22cc$b2a4cc50$1c00810a@pmecheew0267>`

```
Good Afternoon Listers

Anyone had this kind of abend before?
We're running OS/390 R5 and LE 1.8, and the program's batch :) .

In addition to the included U2120, we have had one (only one) occ. of a
U2000. 
I can't find anything on DIAL-IBM, the OS/390 Cd's or the net.

Someone throw me a life-bouy !

Dump info :

=================================================================

Condition Information for Active Routines                                  
    
  Condition Information for NLMC1210 (DSA address 0004F370)                
    
    CIB Address: 0002D478                                                  
    
    Current Condition:                                                     
    
      CEE0198S The termination of a thread was signaled due to an unhandled
cond
    Original Condition:                                                    
    
      CEE3250C The system or user abend U2120 R=NULL     was issued.       
    
    Location:                                                              
    
      Program Unit: NLMC1210 Entry: NLMC1210 Statement:  Offset: +000088BA

====================================================================

Also the following identification was found in the dump :


Program NLMC1210 was compiled 03/20/91 11:36:33 AM                         
 
COBOL Version = 1  Release = 3  Modification = 1      User Level = '    '

======================================================================

IEA995I SYMPTOM DUMP OUTPUT                                           
  USER COMPLETION CODE=4039 REASON CODE=00000000

   ACTIVE LOAD MODULE           ADDRESS=0AF14678  OFFSET=000CED7A     
   NAME=CEEPLPKA                                                      
   DATA AT PSW  0AFE33EC - 00181610  0A0D58D0  D00498EC               
   GPR  0-3  84000000  84000FC7  0002D478  0AFE33F2                   
   GPR  4-7  0AF128A0  00000000  0002D3D0  0002E017                   
   GPR  8-11 0AF3B175  0AF3A176  0002D3D0  8AFE3328                   
   GPR 12-15 00020910  0002F018  8AF3A81E  00000000                   
 END OF SYMPTOM DUMP                                                  

Regards
:Ian 
```

#### ↳ Demystifying LE and LE Abends

- **From:** Colin Campbell <ccampbe2@csc.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.asm370
- **Message-ID:** `<366EEFC9.6249@csc.com>`
- **References:** `<01be22cc$b2a4cc50$1c00810a@pmecheew0267>`

```
Ian Reid wrote:
> 
> Good Afternoon Listers
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

I've learned a bit about understanding LE's actions.  You mention having
OS/390 CD's; the OS/390 Language Environment bookshelf includes the
"Debug & Msgs" manual, which is where you start.  You find the U=40xx
abend explanation in Chapter 16.  (What you learn isn't all that
exciting, just that it is the end result of issuing the CEE0198S
message.)  Chapter 2 tells you that LE uses U4000 thru U4095 abend
codes, while reserving U0000 - U3999 for user programs.  It also
provides the high level information about message id's - CEE means LE,
IGZ means COBOL, etc.

If you have access to it, there is also a Licensed manual for LE, which
goes into depth explaining the ways in which LE works, and how it
changes things for experienced S/390 programmers.  Little things like
taking away one of your Gen. Purpose Regs., extending the Save Area and
calling it a DSA, etc.  In this manual, I was able to read about
CEEPLPKA (where you were at the time of abend).  It is a giant load
module, containing dozens of the CEExxxxx library routines, and residing
in the ELPA (Extended Link Pack Area above the 16M line).  Your
displacement is larger than our whole module, so I would guess that
either your later version of LE is larger, or your Sys Progs have been
tuning your system.

If you can make a module map of your CEEPLPKA, you can find out what
CSECT contains the address X'CDE7A' -- it is probably LE's "abend
routine", CEE3DMP, or something like that.  This sort of thing tends to
take some of the mystery out of the situation.

Another aspect of using LE is the run time options which you can
specify.  One of these is ABPERC(code), where 'code' is either a system
abend code coded as 'Sxxx' or a user abend code, coded as 'Udddd' that
you want LE to keep its hands off of.  If you ran again with
PARM='/ABPERC(U2120)', you could see the U2120 abend as you would have
under MVS, with no 'help' from LE.  Read about them in the LE
Programming Guide.  This manual will also show you how to control LE's
handling of exceptions globally.  For instance, our shop uses an abend
subroutine which issues U0999 abends accompanied by a message, and an
optional dump.  LE grabs the condition and produces a dump we don't
want!  We're going to have to create a table of codes we want LE to
ignore, or learn to live with big increases in the number of output
lines when jobs abend.  The CEEUOPT and CEEVOPT load modules let you
control single programs, or everything LE executes, or (I hope) some
level in between.

However, after all is said and done, I agree with one of your other
respondents, that this is just a program issuing a User Abend, for a
reason that is probably shown by a message somewhere in your output, or
is documented in the program's documentation.  One of your efforts
should be to determine whether this is a program that was written at
your shop or one that is part of a third party product.  No one knows
all of the world's mainframe products, and the program name doesn't look
at all familiar to me.

If you have the SoftAudit One tool (or one like it), you may be able to
tell if this program is identifiable to a product by looking at some of
the reports available from the SoftAudit product.

It looks as if LE is going to be with us on the mainframe for the long
term, so we all will have to get a good understanding of how to live
with it.
Good luck,
Colin Campbell
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Demystifying LE and LE Abends

- **From:** "Ian Reid" <reid.ian@pmintl.ch>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be24dc$c90585e0$1c00810a@pmecheew0267>`
- **References:** `<01be22cc$b2a4cc50$1c00810a@pmecheew0267> <366EEFC9.6249@csc.com>`

```

Colin Campbell <ccampbe2@csc.com> wrote in article
<366EEFC9.6249@csc.com>...
[Large snip]
> It looks as if LE is going to be with us on the mainframe for the long
> term, so we all will have to get a good understanding of how to live
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Colin, 
Thanks very much, what a clear and concise explanation.

As most repondents suggested, it was an application driven User Abend.
Security precluded looking at the source - hands tied and all that.

Many thanks to all who replied - I expect we've all learn't something here.

Regards

:Ian 
```

#### ↳ Re: LE 1.8 wierd COBOL abend dump-longish ( x-posted to IBM-MAIN and COBOL )

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208204236.07840.00003807@ng20.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01be22cc$b2a4cc50$1c00810a@pmecheew0267>`

```

I do not have the resources here at home to look this up. Can offer some
guesses that may help you.

The   ACTIVE LOAD MODULE       is      NAME=CEEPLPKA

I think LPKA has to do with Link Pack Area. So you are dealing with an
environmental/ linkage problem, perhpas.  A system module (LE or COBOL) is
expected to be present, by conventions communicated by the invoker (the
compiled program) and it is not there.

You posted message text to the effect 
"COBOL Version = 1  Release = 3  Modification = 1      User Level = '    '
".  Does this mean, do you think, that you are bringing in a pre COBOL II
module?  Have you planned for that, is the vendor telling you the OS/390 LE
environment that you have installed will be able to vector the antiquated
service module calls (ILB modules)? Have you relinked old code, that you did
not recompile (as might be necessary)?

We have had so many rapid changes in compilers that I may be guessing too far
back here.  But what compiler are you using that gets tagged "Version =  1"?

Anyway, I hope this guess is close enough to be useful; it looks like an older
module in a newer environment that is either not complete or not compatible.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: LE 1.8 wierd COBOL abend dump-longish ( x-posted to IBM-MAIN and COBOL )

- **From:** slug@fast.co.za (Chris Anderson)
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00

```
"Ian Reid" <reid.ian@pmintl.ch> wrote:


>Anyone had this kind of abend before?
>We're running OS/390 R5 and LE 1.8, and the program's batch :) .

May I suggest that you change your compiler options. 
There are certain combinations that produce this weird
stuff. Try it with TEST (Debug) options only.
Use the static mode options (e.g. for CICS), they seem
to be the most stable. Also check the order
of your LINKLIB concatenation.

<a id="t-a4731154ee"></a>
## Thread: Decoding COMP fields — 14 msgs

### Decoding COMP fields

- **From:** "Bob Grommes" <bgrommes@creditexchange.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74jq4p$ioq$1@nnrp03.primenet.com>`

```
I am looking for some documentation or help in hand-decoding numbers stored
in COMP fields.  My background is not in COBOL, but I work for a credit
bureau that receives data from miscellaneous providers, much of it output
from COBOL applications.  I have to get this data into a SQL Server database
which is ultimately published on a private web site for our members.

I use Data Junction for initial review and decoding of new data formats.
This is a general-purpose data conversion product that understands COMP and
various COMP-n numeric formats.  Generally I get some kind of documentation
from the data provider.  Some providers are more willing than others to
answer questions or provide support; the provider I'm currently working with
is in the "not helpful" category and has basically thrown a 9-track tape and
a semi-up-to-date documentation sheet "over the wall" at me.  My job is to
get this data into our system without antagonizing the provider with
anything so presumtuous as a sensible question.

Here's the deal:

The documentation for the file layout shows three different definitions for
numeric fields.  They are:

PIC S9(7)V9(2) COMP.
PIC 9(9)       COMP.
PIC S9(4)      COMP.

My understanding of the above notations is that the actual stored number
consists of:

9 digits with 2 implied decimal places
One digit integer  (although why one would bother to compress a single digit
is beyond me)
Four digit integer

I'm given to understand that a COMP field can be anywhere from 1 to 8 bytes
wide on disk.  But is it possible to look at, say, an S9(7)V9(2) format and
via some formula, be able to state with confidence that this would always be
a 4-byte value on disk?  Or might a programmer elect to make a field wider
than necessary, perhaps for future expansion?

I have a series of perhaps 20 numeric fields with a mixture of the above
three formats and no matter how I try to decipher these fields I end up not
accounting for all the bytes in the record.  There are a couple of clearly
identifiable character fields following these numerics and I'm trying to
figure out if there's some undocumented padding being used, or if I've got
the widths of some or all of the numerics wrong.

It'd be helpful in my analysis if I could look at the hex bytes and
recognize what's actually going on.  So I also would like to be pointed
toward an online or book source for the algorithm used to encode and decode
various COMP field types.  Or, perhaps the algorithms are simple enough for
someone to post here.

Can anyone enlighten me on either of these topics?

--Bob Grommes
  Phoenix AZ
```

#### ↳ Re: Decoding COMP fields

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366d7b67.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74jq4p$ioq$1@nnrp03.primenet.com>`

```
The answer would depend on several things:
which compiler, which compiler setting for interpreting COMP,
so you'll have to state these (you may not know the compiler
setting...).

COMP is typically binary or packed decimal, both of which
can be represented in a variety of ways, so we need to know
more about your compiler/settings.

Bob Grommes wrote in message <74jq4p$ioq$1@nnrp03.primenet.com>...
>I am looking for some documentation or help in hand-decoding numbers stored
>in COMP fields.  My background is not in COBOL, but I work for a credit
>bureau that receives data from miscellaneous providers, much of it output
>from COBOL applications.  I have to get this data into a SQL Server
database
>which is ultimately published on a private web site for our members.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>answer questions or provide support; the provider I'm currently working
with
>is in the "not helpful" category and has basically thrown a 9-track tape
and
>a semi-up-to-date documentation sheet "over the wall" at me.  My job is to
>get this data into our system without antagonizing the provider with
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>One digit integer  (although why one would bother to compress a single
digit
>is beyond me)
>Four digit integer
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>via some formula, be able to state with confidence that this would always
be
>a 4-byte value on disk?  Or might a programmer elect to make a field wider
>than necessary, perhaps for future expansion?
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Decoding COMP fields

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-0812981411500001@138.202.137.168>`
- **References:** `<74jq4p$ioq$1@nnrp03.primenet.com> <366d7b67.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
> >PIC S9(7)V9(2) COMP.

signed long integer (binary, 4 bytes), 2 decimal places
may or may not be synchronized

> >PIC 9(9)       COMP.

unsigned long integer

> >PIC S9(4)      COMP.

signed short integer (binary, 2 bytes)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decoding COMP fields

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Depends on the platform and the compiler settings.

>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decoding COMP fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-0812981545200001@138.202.137.168>`
- **References:** `<74jq4p$ioq$1@nnrp03.primenet.com> <366d7b67.0@news1.ibm.net> <casten-0812981411500001@138.202.137.168> <366da64d.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
> No, no, no.
> Depends on the platform and the compiler settings.
well OK, I should have noted that I live in VMS land.
so, that being the case:
PIC 9(n) COMP, n <= 4, word integer
PIC 9(n) COMP, 5 <= n <= 9, longword integer
PIC 9(n) COMP, 10 <= n <= 18, quadword integer

I see nothing in any of the compiler directives that change any of the
above.  You can specify floating types for your COMP-1 and COMP-2 fields
(which I use only when required by a system call) using the /FLOAT
directive.

So I am curious how other platforms implement this data type, academically
really...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decoding COMP fields

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74kg3s$aki$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<74jq4p$ioq$1@nnrp03.primenet.com> <366d7b67.0@news1.ibm.net> <casten-0812981411500001@138.202.137.168> <366da64d.0@news1.ibm.net> <casten-0812981545200001@138.202.137.168>`

```
Unisys A Series COBOL74 and COBOL84 implement COMP[UTATIONAL] as packed
decimal, that is, four bits containing decimal digits 0 through 9 per "9" in
the PICTURE clause.

Unisys A Series COBOL (68), on the other hand, implemented PIC 9(n) COMP (as
well as COMP-1) as native binary integer.  Where 1 <= n <= 11, a
single-integer resulted; for 12 <= n <= 23, a double-integer.  A canonic
single-precision integer occupies a 48-bit word; the sign appears in bit 46
(zero-relative), and the mantissa occupies from bit 38 to the low-order end
of the word, which is elsewhere zero. A canonic double-precision integer
occupies two 48-bit words; the low-order word is  as described above, and
the high-order word contains 13 in the exponent (downward from bit 44 for 6
bits).

Well, you asked.      ;->

    -Chuck Stevens (Unisys A Series Languages)

John Casten wrote in message ...
>> No, no, no.
>> Depends on the platform and the compiler settings.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Like everyone who is not in love, he imagined that one chooses the person
one loves after endless deliberation and on the strength of diverse
qualities and advantages.
>- Proust
```

<charles.stevens@unisys.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Unisys A Series COBOL74 and COBOL84 implement COMP[UTATIONAL] as packed
>decimal, that is, four bits containing decimal digits 0 through 9 per "9" in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>well as COMP-1) as native binary integer.

Chuck omits that COBOL74 and COBOL85 have a Compiler Control option
BINARYCOMP, which causes COMP to be binary format, just like COBOL(68).

```
John Casten wrote in message ...
>> No, no, no.
>> Depends on the platform and the compiler settings.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>really...


E.g. for the Realia compiler on a PC, you have two choices for COMP,
little endian (COMP-5) or big endian (COMP-4). There is a compiler
directive (COMP=COMP-5 or COMP=COMP-4) that lets you select
which one.
There is a similar one for the MicroFocus compiler:
 (e.g. OVERRIDE"COMP-5" = "COMP").
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decoding COMP fields

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981208233225.17038.00002225@ng135.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74kphb$2uq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
 casten@usfca.edu (John Casten) wrote: in part
(heavy snipping)
<<... I should have noted that I live in VMS land.
>>

The posts so far should come close to enumerating the possible sizes, which we
all are hoping helps account for all the bytes in your record. If it does not
we would need example code and hex dumps to help you decipher more.

Incidentally, does this involve variable length records?

But I have an alterior motive for posting.  I hope you can clarify something
for me.  Once you know how wide a data item is then you will need to know the
endianness of the item.  And that is where the old compiler/machine defaults
come in and the modern compiler comp-4 and comp-5 options can relate.

But on VMS machines, hardware wise, was there not actually an intrisic integer
item that was neither big-endian nor little-endian.  I would call it
transverse-endian.  

This related to 4-byte integers. There are two theoretical possibilities. I
think the actual machine pattern was to transpose the pair of 16-bit halfs,
low-order first, but within the 16-bit half the bits were high-order first.
This can be done the other way around (and still be neither big-endian not
little-endian).  It only relates to 32 bit data items.  It results from a
16-bit architecture being extended to a 32-bit architecture.

This was hardware dependent, not OS dependent.  I do not know if it could still
be evident in the COBOL generated file you are dealing with.  

Hope you can comment on this.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

Hi Bob.

>I am looking for some documentation or help in hand-decoding

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>on a private web site for our members.

	You might want to try PDT-Windows (the Pro~Formance Data
Tool for Windows):   www.pro-central.com	

	PDT is a terrific tool to help you view/edit/figure out data
files like those you describe.  It "supports" over 30 different
types of data fields, and lets you manage files up to 18
quintillion bytes in size (2 gigabytes in the DOS version).
It supports ASCII and EBCDIC files, and lets you view/edit
**any** file on your pc (and edit all 256 ASCII characters).

	Note that PDT-Windows is not a data-conversion tool
(although one is in the works to do the types of things you
describe).  PDT-Windows excells in helping you figure out
what you have, and in viewing/editing files no other program
can handle.

>Some providers are more willing than others to answer 
>questions or provide support; the provider I'm currently working with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>anything so presumtuous as a sensible question.


	Exactly!  PDT is especially handy in situations like this.
(See the "Review of PDT" on the web site.  The author describes
using PDT to solve many problems, including the type you
mention.)

>I have a series of perhaps 20 numeric fields with a mixture of 
>the above three formats and no matter how I try to decipher 
>these fields I end up not accounting for all the bytes in the record.  

	Again, PDT supports over 30 "types" of data fields --
including little/big endian integers and long integers.  And
once you "define" a field type, PDT then displays the "values"
in each field, letting you see/verify/edit them.

>It'd be helpful in my analysis if I could look at the hex 
>bytes and recognize what's actually going on.  

	PDT lets you view/edit files in either binary or
hex modes.  The normal binary mode would probably
be most helpful in your case -- just highlight a cell
and wave the mouse over it.  PDT will display the 
hex and decimal values of each byte; and at the same
time, display the "value" of the bytes in that field.

	The Windows and DOS versions of PDT are
described (and available) at:  

	www.pro-central.com


	Hope this helps .............. <rob>


=======================================

on all our products, such as PDT-Windows,
our 18-quintillion byte file editor!

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Rocks

- **From:** Clifton Ivy <clif@purdue.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36704C28.585A1B9A@purdue.edu>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ILdltJrB7yj8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <dbryantF3oE1J.Myr@netcom.com>`

```
Maybe you already have this is mind, but you can speed up your table
search if you ensure the entries are sorted on ZIP code.

The simplest way is to stop scanning the table when TBL-ZIP (SUBSCRIPT)
> EMP-ZIP.

At this point you are done; you either have found a match, in which case
you have done the "MOVE TBL-NAME (SUBSCRIPT) TO CITY-NAME" statement, or
you aren't going to find a match.

A bigger speed-up would be to do a binary search.

At my IBM big-iron shop, I would use the "SEARCH" verb to do this.  Does
WANG COBOL have that capability?

When the table is defined, you have to define the key to the table (the
field on which the table entries are sorted).

This changes the table definition to:

01  CITY-TABLE.
    05  TABLE-ROWS    OCCURS 250 TIMES
        ASCENDING KEY IS TBL-ZIP
        INDEXED BY TRI.
      10  TBL-ZIP      PIC X(05).
      10  TBL-NAME     PIC X(25).

The "indexed by" is necessary to use the SEARCH verb.

This is ***MUCH*** better than the "PERFORM... nnn times" approach,
because sooner or later the number of entries in the table will change
-- and you will have to find all of the times you coded that "nnn"
throughout the program, and which of those need to change. You don't
have to code the "nnn" with the SEARCH verb, COBOL takes care of it for
you.

The SEARCH statement would be like:

SEARCH ALL TABLE-ROWS
    AT END
        MOVE 'NOT FOUND' TO CITY-NAME
    WHEN EMP-ZIP = TBL-ZIP (TRI)
        MOVE TBL-NAME (TRI) TO CITY-NAME.

The "ALL" above tells COBOL to do a binary search of the table.  You
know: 

try the middle entry of the table; 
  if the value you are looking for is found there, do the "WHEN"; 
  if the value you are looking for is less than the table value, go
half-way down the table and try again;
  if the value you are looking for is greater than the table value, go
half-way up the table and try again;
Keep at this, splitting the remainder of the table in half until you
find a value, or you have run out of "halves" to split.

At least, that is a rude-and-crude explanation of the search.

You should be able to search the table with an average of 9 (8?) "looks"
into the table, instead of the 250 you have been doing.

Have fun with it!


David K. Bryant wrote:

> Speaking of...
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>   have to think too much."  Seriously.
snip...
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
They're just my opinions...
Clifton Ivy, Mgmt Info, Purdue University
clif@purdue.edu
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Rocks

- **From:** mrbiueskyz@aol.comatose (MrBIueSkyz)
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981211015012.18814.00000710@ng29.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36704C28.585A1B9A@purdue.edu>`

```
Search all is definitely the way to go when processing
large tables and/or large input files. Remember that the
table entries must be in ascending/descending key
sequence with no duplicates; otherwise a given value
might not be found.

An example of the power of binary searches: Last year
I was a member of the team converting claims records
of another insurance company that our company had
bought. People were complaining about a particular pgm
that was running for four hours. Two tables with several
thousand entries were being searched with perform
varying statements. This processing was being performed
on an input file with hundreds of thousands of rcds. I
added a couple of upstream syncsorts to sort the table
entries into ascending sequence, and then loaded them
into internal tables with occurs depending on & ascending
key clauses, and replaced the perform varying statements
with search alls. Results: clock time & cpu usage were
reduced by 90%. In fairness to the original programmer,
she's a SAS person, doesn't do much COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Rocks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74qu8h$1cb@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36704C28.585A1B9A@purdue.edu> <19981211015012.18814.00000710@ng29.aol.com>`

```

MrBIueSkyz wrote in message <19981211015012.18814.00000710@ng29.aol.com>...
>Search all is definitely the way to go when processing
>large tables and/or large input files. Remember that the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <snip>

Some implementors have already done it and the draft of the next Standard
has it - but SEARCH ALL is really easy with "TABLE SORTS".  This feature
lets you sort the elements of a table using the same old SORT verb that you
already know and love.  This means you don't have to do your own external or
internal sort, but can (in a single statement) sort your table and then use
SEARCH ALL safely (it even defaults - but can be overridden - to sort on the
key fields defined for your SEARCHs.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Rocks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367135D8.85A37E53@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36704C28.585A1B9A@purdue.edu> <19981211015012.18814.00000710@ng29.aol.com>`

```
MrBIueSkyz wrote:
> 
> Search all is definitely the way to go when processing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> might not be found.

And if that's not possible, SEARCH is usually better than doing
PERFORMS.  But if the table is real small sometimes it is faster to look
at each element individually.

I agree with you that sorting tables ahead of time can offer tremendous
efficiencies.  Also, sorting files can allow merge logic which can also
be very efficient.  It seems that lots of programmers, some even fairly
senior, don't think about merge logic enough.
```

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

Skyz,

A little 'trick' I found was with sorted Ascending Tables having duplicates.
I had a CICS Table (Static Assembler Program) with 3000 sorted entries.
Beginning with entry 2701, the next three hundred entries were High-Values.
My DSECT was an occurs 1 to 3000 depending on high-bound, with the first
high-value entry (nbr 2701) being my high-bound. Using this method, my SEARCH
ALL always worked just fine.

Cheers,

WOB :-)

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

Tom Davidson <tedavids@execpc.com> wrote:
>I have a need to decode the header of several Microfocus Cobol files.  The
>files were provided by one of our vendors, but the source code is not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

You did not state the file attributes, so a precise
explanation is not possible.  We would need to know
the ORGANIZATION, RECORDING MODE, and platform.

The best thing to do is get ahold of the Micro Focus
COBOL System Reference manual for the platform and
look at the appendix entitled "File Formats".  This
should answer all your questions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus file format

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366ebf57.1407611@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<74k8p4$qcc@newsops.execpc.com>`

```
On 8 Dec 1998 22:21:24 GMT, "Tom Davidson" <tedavids@execpc.com>
wrote:

>I have a need to decode the header of several Microfocus Cobol files.  The
>files were provided by one of our vendors, but the source code is not
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tom Davidson

The first part of the file is probably generated because the file is
stated as an "IDX FORMAT 4" or similar in the select statement. If the
files are from Win'95 (DOS) they also are a bit weird and you need to
add the following after the FD 

FD FILE-NAME
     RECORDING MODE IS V.

The V stands for variable and you should be able to read them as a
sequential file thereafter. Alternatively cut out the first part of
the file using an editor that doesn't expand or contract any embedded
control characters (ie a specially written C program !)
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus file format

- **From:** "Tom Davidson" <tedavids@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74mu95$fk5@newsops.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<74k8p4$qcc@newsops.execpc.com>`

```
Thanks to all who responded.  I have received a chapter of the manual and
have successfully validated the header info.

All your help has been much appreciated.

Tom
```

## Thread: NetExpress GUI screens on win-95 — 2 msgs

### NetExpress GUI screens on win-95

- **From:** vgibson@westga.edu (Vince Gibson)
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366d546e.11310964@news.westga.edu>`

```
I am using NetExpress 2.0 on a NT v4 machine. I am developing  GUI
programs using the dialog system. The problem is when I run the
programs on win-95 machines, the screens appearance is all messed up.
The entry fields are shown very small which chops off the information
inside. When you resize the window to make the fields taller, the test
labels repeat across the screen making it a bloody mess.

What can I do to make the appearance of the GUI windows on the win-95
appear the same as it does in the dialog system development.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress GUI screens on win-95

- **From:** hippy@ndirect.co.uk (Guy C. Fountain)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366ef10c.15272220@news.netdirect.net.uk>`
- **References:** `<366d546e.11310964@news.westga.edu>`

```
On Tue, 08 Dec 1998 16:40:35 GMT, vgibson@westga.edu (Vince Gibson)
wrote:

>I am using NetExpress 2.0 on a NT v4 machine. I am developing  GUI
>programs using the dialog system. The problem is when I run the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>appear the same as it does in the dialog system development.

Vince,

Dialog has dynamic window sizing.
Example below of global dialog, object dialog and data block that uses
this.


***Global Dialog***

 SCREENSET-INITIALIZED
*
* This CALLOUT loads the NetExpress API Class Library
* NOTE this must always occur before any function which
* causes the creation of a window.
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 1 CONFIG-FLAG $NULL
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 2 CONFIG-VALUE $NULL
     MOVE 15 CONFIG-FLAG
     MOVE 1 CONFIG-VALUE
     CALLOUT "dsrtcfg" 3 $PARMLIST
*
* This CALLOUT enables Default push button tracking
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 1 CONFIG-FLAG $NULL
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 2 CONFIG-VALUE $NULL
     MOVE 8 CONFIG-FLAG
     MOVE 1 CONFIG-VALUE
     CALLOUT "dsrtcfg" 3 $PARMLIST
*
* This CALLOUT enables Dynamic Window Sizing
* and multiple resolution support
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 1 CONFIG-FLAG $NULL
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 2 CONFIG-VALUE $NULL
     MOVE 9 CONFIG-FLAG
     MOVE 2 CONFIG-VALUE
     CALLOUT "dsrtcfg" 3 $PARMLIST
*
* This CALLOUT sets correct background color on Dialog Box text
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 1 CONFIG-FLAG $NULL
     CALLOUT-PARAMETER 2 CONFIG-VALUE $NULL
     MOVE 10 CONFIG-FLAG
     MOVE 1 CONFIG-VALUE
     CALLOUT "dsrtcfg" 3 $PARMLIST
*
* This procedure does a callout to handle your statusbar resize events
   RESIZE-PROCEDURE
     MOVE "RESIZE" CALL-FUNCTION(1)
     CALLOUT "SBAR" 0 FUNCTION-DATA
*
* This procedure does a callout to handle your statusbar refresh
   REFRESH-STATUS-BAR
     SET OBJECT-REFERENCE(1) CURRENT-WINDOW-SBAR
     MOVE "REFRESH-OBJECT" CALL-FUNCTION(1)
     CALLOUT "SBAR" 0 FUNCTION-DATA


***Primary window dialog***

 WINDOW-CREATED
*
* Status bar processing
     SET CURRENT-WINDOW-SBAR MAIN-WIND-SBAR-OBJREF
     TIMEOUT 25 REFRESH-STATUS-BAR
*
 GAINED-FOCUS
     SET CURRENT-WINDOW-SBAR MAIN-WIND-SBAR-OBJREF
 WINDOW-SIZED
     SET OBJECT-REFERENCE(1) MAIN-WIND-SBAR-OBJREF
     BRANCH-TO-PROCEDURE RESIZE-PROCEDURE
 WINDOW-RESTORED
     SET OBJECT-REFERENCE(1) MAIN-WIND-SBAR-OBJREF
     BRANCH-TO-PROCEDURE RESIZE-PROCEDURE
 WINDOW-MAXIMIZED
     SET OBJECT-REFERENCE(1) MAIN-WIND-SBAR-OBJREF
     BRANCH-TO-PROCEDURE RESIZE-PROCEDURE
   @EXIT
     BRANCH-TO-PROCEDURE EXIT-MAIN-WIND


***Data Block***

EXIT-FLAG                        9      1.0           
CONFIG-FLAG                      C5     4.0           
CONFIG-VALUE                     C5     4.0           
FUNCTION-DATA                          1              
  WINDOW-HANDLE                  C5     4.0           
  OBJECT-REFERENCE               OBJ-REF              
  CALL-FUNCTION                  X     30.0           
  NUMERIC-VALUE                  C5     4.0           
  NUMERIC-VALUE2                 C5     4.0           
  SIZE-WIDTH                     C5     4.0           
  SIZE-HEIGHT                    C5     4.0           
  POSITION-X                     C5     4.0           
  POSITION-Y                     C5     4.0           
  IO-TEXT-BUFFER                 X    256.0           
  IO-TEXT-BUFFER2                X    256.0           
CURRENT-WINDOW-SBAR              OBJ-REF              
MAIN-WIND-SBAR-OBJREF            OBJ-REF              
```

## Thread: NetExpress 2.0 — 3 msgs

### NetExpress 2.0

- **From:** "Bhavesh Patel" <bpatel@unipac.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74jp81$7rg$1@news1.rmi.net>`

```
I'm trying to call MS Excel functions from within NetExpress COBOL.  Does
anyone have some sample code that I can use to get myself started?  Code
interacting with any of the MS Office products would be helpful.

I'm at the point where I can launch Excel, but that's about it...

Your help would be greatly appreciated.  Maybe in the next version of
NetExpress they will leave the product alone, and fix the documentation.

Thanks,
Bhavesh
bpatel@unipac.com
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 2.0

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74lhgb$s2c@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<74jp81$7rg$1@news1.rmi.net>`

```
"Bhavesh Patel" <bpatel@unipac.com> wrote:
>I'm trying to call MS Excel functions from within NetExpress COBOL.  Does
>anyone have some sample code that I can use to get myself started?  Code
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>NetExpress they will leave the product alone, and fix the documentation.

Hi Bhavesh,
NetExpress comes with a sample application (called olerep) which gives an 
example of calling MS Excel from NetExpress COBOL using Excel's OLE 
Automation interface.

If you need help on what methods Excel contains, then look in VBX_XL.HLP 
in Office 95 (in the Excel directory on the CD) or VBAXL8.HLP in Office 97 
(in the Office directory, I think).
All the documentation is in VB, which requires some trivial translation.

Micro Focus does not document Microsoft products, but there is full, 
on-line help on using OLE automation included with the product in the 
Distributed Computing book (start with OLE in the index, and follow 
links).

Hope that helps,
Steve.
Micro Focus.
```

<a id="t-730b2c1c54"></a>
## Thread: Help with COBOL Module — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with COBOL Module

- **From:** akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent-0912981834180001@g27.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
I am attempting to work with a COBOL II program which is not structured
very well, and I need some recommendations.  Unfortunately, I cannot take
the time to rewrite a substantial portion of the code.  So, here is how
the problem area is structured:  

   PERFORM 100-SORT-ROUTINE.     

100-SORT-ROUTINE SECTION. 

   SORT SORT-FILE 
       USING TEMP-FILE  
       OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS 200-SORT-RECS.

200-SORT-RECS SECTION.  

   PERFORM 210-RELEASE-SORT-REC. 
   IF  END-OF-SORT-FILE
       NEXT SENTENCE
   ELSE
       PERFORM 300-BATCH-HEADER. 
200-END.  EXIT.   

210-RELEASE-SORT-REC SECTION.       
   RELEASE SORT-REC
      AT END MOVE 'X' TO WS-SORT-END-OF-FILE.
210-END.  EXIT.

300-MATCH-HEADER SECTION.

   (ALOT OF IF LOGIC CHECKING FOR SOME SPECIFIC CONDITIONS.  NOTHING OUT OF THE
    ORDINARY)

   MOVE SORT-REC TO WS-HOLD-REC.
   PERFORM 200-SORT-RECS.
300-END.  EXIT.

Yuk!  Yes, the PERFORM 200-SORT-RECS at the end of the 300-MATCH-HEADER
routine is of course "piss poor" structure.  My dilemma is that I have
been abending with "Unsucessful Sort 322 S", and then the usual S322
abend.  Through displays, I discovered that after the last Sort Record is
processed, right after the PERFORM 300-BATCH-HEADER statement in the
200-SORT-RECS routine, I displayed the last Sort Record processed (a
trailer record), along with the variable
WS-SORT-END-OF-FILE, and it had a value of 'X".  These Displays were
subsequently repeated, repeated, repeated, etc.  That was the place which
resulted in the S322.

Does anyone know a simple solution to this awful code?  I would really
appreciate it.

Thanks,
Arthur M. Kent
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with COBOL Module

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981210004320.20543.00003987@ng23.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<akent-0912981834180001@g27.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
To begin with your OUTPUT PROCEDURE needs to be doing a RETURN not a RELEASE. 
(RELEASE is for the input into the SORT, RETURN is fot the output back from the
SORT).

Also the reinvocation of the output procedure is not merely poor it is a
formula for disaster.  There is some chance that you will never achieve
    END-OF-SORT-FILE
because it will only happen on RETURNs, and you don't appear to be doing any. 


Replacing RELEASE with RETURN will help, but you should use a PERFORM UNTIL of
the returning paragraph in the basic OUTPUT procedure.  Do not recurse. In this
case the recursion is

200-SORT-RECS SECTION  
PERFORMs 300-BATCH-HEADER 

300-MATCH-HEADER SECTION
PERFORMs 200-SORT-RECS

On a CPU with stack architecture you could blow stack space this way when
sorting large files. The mainframe 322 is a variation of a time-out (It is a
tribute to the compiler runtime and sort routine that it can get that far. It
actual illustrates the advantage of non-stack architecture).

The recursion does not cause the time-out. The lack of an END condition does.
But you are at substatial risk doing recursion in a SORT context on a
mainframe. You could consume gigantic resources.

Here you timeout because you are succeeding too much in senselessly RELEASing
records.
You actually probably get an error on those release statements, since the
releases phase is over as of commencement of the OUTPUT PROCEDURE.  

But interestingly enough the AT END on the erroroneous RELEASE statement is
blind to errors.  An object lesson.  Errors are NOT AT END!  Within NOT AT END
you can interrogate file statuses, should you choose to, on regular files.  I
am not sure such syntax is available to you in COBOL II on SORT RETURNs.

But you will be much better off if you do RETURNs.

Other than the inappropriate recursion, your code does not look that badly
structured.  You have the rudiments of file status checking, which you might
want to expand. (With a check of SORT-RETURN at the top when done.)

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with COBOL Module

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74olle$mo1$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0912981834180001@g27.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
In article <akent-0912981834180001@g27.dialup.seanet.com>,
Arthur M. Kent <akent@seanet.com> wrote:
>I am attempting to work with a COBOL II program which is not structured
>very well, and I need some recommendations.  Unfortunately, I cannot take
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>   PERFORM 210-RELEASE-SORT-REC. 

You are 'working with' a program which cannot run in its present form and
must be rewritten; contact your boss and tell him this...

... or please do your own homework.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with COBOL Module

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<759gs5$s0v@journal.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0912981834180001@g27.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
>My dilemma is that I have
>been abending with "Unsucessful Sort 322 S", and then the usual S322
>abend.

An MVS abend S322 means that that job timed out. Increase the time
parameter on your job card... in addition to fixing the lousy code.
```

<a id="t-415bba8713"></a>

<a id="t-8bbe0a5202"></a>
## Thread: COBOL HELP NEEDED! — 5 msgs

### COBOL HELP NEEDED!

- **From:** RICHARD ELDRIDGE <eldridge@coventry.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.981209184115.3609A-100000@leofric>`

```

I wonder if anyone can help me with a small problem.  I am writing a 
program that will allow the entry of stock orders.  Certain fields 
entered by the user need to be validated for obvious reasons, and I have 
a problem with one of the fields.

The code in WS is as follows:

01  WS-ORDPRICE				PIC ZZ9.99.
    88  VALID-WS-ORDPRICE		VALUES ???????.

In my PROCEDURE DIVISION, I have it accepting WS-ORDPRICE until 
VALID-WS-ORDPRICE but don't know how to get it to validate the values 
from 1 thru 99999.

What do I need to put in place of the ??????? in the code above so that 
there will be a decimal point on the screen when the field is accepted.  
I don't seem to be able to get this to work!

Thanks in advance... :)


                             \\\|///
                           \\  - -  //
                            (  @ @  )
--------------------------oOOo-(_)-oOOo----------------------------------------

      	**** Richard Eldridge ****    E-Mail : eldridge@coventry.ac.uk

	 You can get a beer from the f-ridge but not from RIDGE!!!!!!

---------------------------oooO----Oooo----------------------------------------
                           (   )   (   )
                            \ (     ) /
                             \_)   (_/
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL HELP NEEDED!

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74mlqe$9u2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.981209184115.3609A-100000@leofric>`

```
RICHARD ELDRIDGE wrote in message ...

The ZZ9.99 field is really designed for screen usage and
printer usage only.  The usual way of doing this is to use
that for the user, but convert to 999v99 before validation.

If you use the screen section, then the conversion can be
completely automatic; the screen section picture is ZZ9.99,
but the from/to used for the input data would be 999V99.

You are going to have to have it in a numeric format before
you can use it for anything beyond playing it back to a human
anyway, so you might as well validate in that form ...

>
>I wonder if anyone can help me with a small problem.  I am writing a
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>--------------------------oOOo-(_)-oOOo------------------------------------
----
>
>      **** Richard Eldridge ****    E-Mail : eldridge@coventry.ac.uk
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------oooO----Oooo------------------------------------
----
>                           (   )   (   )
>                            \ (     ) /
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL HELP NEEDED!

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10d8a2c45c42da9a9896d9@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.981209184115.3609A-100000@leofric>`

```
In article <Pine.OSF.3.91.981209184115.3609A-100000@leofric>, 
eldridge@coventry.ac.uk says...
> 
> I wonder if anyone can help me with a small problem.  I am writing a 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't seem to be able to get this to work!


Well, what I would do is first change the PIC from ZZ9.99 to 9(3)V99. 
Since the field in question is on the input side I would not use an 
edited field in this situation. 

The code in the procedure division would simply test WS-ORDPRICE as 
follows:

IF (WS-ORDPRICE IS NUMERIC)
THEN
    IF (WS-ORDPRICE >=1 OR WS-ORDPRICE <= 999.99)
    THEN
        valid data.

The logic seems a bit redundant but the first test verifies if numeric 
data is present in the field and then the second test verifies the value
range. It may not be necessary to check for the upper limit since it is 
the highest value that is possible but it can't hurt to have it in place.

Just my 2 cents.
 
> 
> Thanks in advance... :)
> 
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL HELP NEEDED!

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74mr7q$gvr$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.981209184115.3609A-100000@leofric>`

```

RICHARD ELDRIDGE wrote in message ...
>
>I wonder if anyone can help me with a small problem.  I am writing a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

First off, I agree with the others, change WS-ORDPRICE to 9(3)v99.

Your condition then is:
    88 VALID-WS-ORDPRICE VALUES 0.01 THRU 999.99.

But to answer your question with zz9.99:

      $set ans85 flag"ans85"
       identification division.
       program-id. rangeval.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws-ordprice pic zz9.99.
         88 valid-ws-ordprice value "  0.01" thru "999.99".
       procedure division.
       begin.
           perform with test after until ws-ordprice = "  0.00"
               accept ws-ordprice
               if valid-ws-ordprice
                   display "Order Price is Valid"
               else
                   display "Order Price is INVALID"
               end-if
           end-perform
           stop run
           .
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
Others have answered this, but two points:

1.  CICS eats up this kind of stuff.  Aren't you using it?

2.  Ever hear of Berkshire Hathaway Class A?   It was trading today
for about $61,000 per share.  Even the Class B is in the thousands, as
I recall.  Better make your price field a little bigger, or you'll
have some happy customers getting BRK.A at quite a bargain...or have
the door shown you.

## Thread: Y2K Testing — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K Testing

- **From:** "Ed Macri" <edmond.macri@bain.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366f0906.0@209.215.151.131>`

```
Has anyone found good mainframe Y2K testing software? How does this fit with
what you used for remediation?

Any help would be appreciated. Thanks!

Ed
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K Testing

- **From:** kthompson@netexas.net (Kevin)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DA50E066892C6C20.829DC1506EC0E265.3B09BD3204E76ED2@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<366f0906.0@209.215.151.131>`

```
On Wed, 9 Dec 1998 18:39:34 -0500, "Ed Macri" <edmond.macri@bain.com>
wrote:

>Has anyone found good mainframe Y2K testing software? How does this fit with
>what you used for remediation?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
We've been using Platinum's Data Aging tool which isn't too bad as
long as people don't try to age key fields.  Time Machine Journey
(TMJ) is being used to run programs in YR2k on a mainframe box which
can't be IPL'd forward.  The latter leaves much to be desired...still
prefer using the YR2k box but randomly IPL'ing the box is much more
difficult than setting up TMJ for a job/inquiry for several quick date
tests.

Full system testing is done on the YR2k box using the aged data.  

cheers,
Kevin
```

## Thread: What's Happening at PSE&G? (4th post) — 3 msgs

### What's Happening at PSE&G? (4th post)

- **From:** unlisted.to.avoid.spam@anon.lcs.mit.edu ( )
- **Date:** 1998-12-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<19981209223000.XVQ16802@81.new-york-05-10rs.ny.dial-access.att.net>`

```
I worked on the customer system at PSE&G over a dozen years ago.

That system is very large and had a lot of Y2K exposure.  

         (To clarify: I stress the past tense here: "HAD," "HAD,"
         "HAD." I have no knowledge if such Y2K considerations
         still exist.  If I did, then I would not have had any need
         to post this query to begin with!  I think that most normal,
         reasonable people would be able to figure this out without
         this clarification.)

What approach, if any, has been taken: windowing or field expansion,
or has the system been totally rewritten as was indicated for many,
many non-Y2K reasons?

         (The recommendation that the system be rewritten in
         whole or in part came up from time to time having been
         brought up BY MANAGEMENT themselves, so it really
         isn't something that hasn't already been let out of the
         bag.  Be that as it may, if over the course of the last
         umpteen years or so, if management there has been doing
         what is right, that is, what they are being paid to do,
         then they have nothing to worry about, and certainly not
         about my post here.  If management there hasn't been doing
         what is right, then they have infinitely more important
         things to worry about than about being "whacked" by a
         tiny obscure post like this!)
```

<a id="t-9dcaf3d273"></a>
## Thread: Sorts and such... — 21 msgs

### Sorts and such...

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Hi guys-
  I'm doing a RAD model of some software that will be ran under CICS,
perhaps with a database backend, and today I ran into a bit of a 
puzzler. Figured I would ask older and wiser heads for a pointer. :) 

  Since I suspect this will have to be hosted in a RDBMs, I've
been treating the COBOL files like db tables, with appropriate 
indcies and so on.  well imagine a pair ot tables like
this:

        fd  fund-master-table.
        01  fund-master-table-record.
            10 fund-number               pic 9(4).
            10 fund-name                 pic x(25).
            10 fund-report-number        pic 9(6).
              .... <more fields> ....

        fd  fund-history-table.
        01  fund-history-table-record.
            10 fh-id-number              pic 9(12).
            10 fd-fund-number            pic 9(4).
            10 fd-fund-date              pic 9(8).
            10 fd-fund-value             pic s9(11)v99.
              .... <more fields> ....

with selects similar to:

            select fund-master-table
              assign to fmt-file
              organization is indexed
              access mode is dynamic
              record      key is fund-number
              alternate   key is fund-name
              alternate   key is fund-report-number with duplicates.

            select fund-history-table
              assign to fht-file
              organization is indexed  
              access mode is dynamic
              record      key is fd-id-number
              alternate   key is fd-fund-number with duplicates
              alternate   key is fd-fund-date with duplicates.

Now the task here is to print those two little buggers out on x number
of separate reports (report number in the master file) by fund, and only
for a particular (user selected) date.

The only way I can think to do it is to sort the history file first,
by fund, then process each record individually in an output procedure.
COnversely, I could use an input and output procedure to a sort, 
selecting by date and outputing to a particualr report queu or 
something. Both of these solutions feel clumsy, and I of course,
cannot get the SQL solution out of my head. 

If someone else knows of an easier way to accomplish this probably
fairly common task, I'd be appreciative of a pointer or two. :) 
I have writing 'clunky' code!

-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36706e5b.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
I would do a start on the history table, with the date given and
a not less condition. If I find a record, I'd check if the date
matches, then do a next record until the date changes.
For each record found, go pick up fund data from the master
based on fund-number.

I don't understand the role of fund-report-number so maybe
I'm off the mark.

paulr wrote in message <74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Hi guys-
>  I'm doing a RAD model of some software that will be ran under CICS,
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74q32h$gqj@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com> <36706e5b.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
The fund-report-number is a key to the lookup table that says
which report *this* particular fund belongs on. There are a half dozen
or so reports, and certain funds belong on certain reports. 

That is along the same lines of what I first starting thinking
about, but then realized the output has to be sorted by fund (
and associated group subtotals and so forth and so on, all the 
common jazz...) 

Using START I can sort on *either* fd-fund-number ot fd-fund-date,
and of course pick up the relevant data from the FUND-MASTER-TABLE,
but not both at the same time.

In SQL, I would solve that with a query similar to:

SELECT  fd-fund-number, fund-report-number, fd-fund-date,
        ... <all the fields I need > ...
        where fund-number = fd-fund-number   and
              fd-date = '12/10/1998'
        order by fd-fund-number

The WHERE clause causes a UNION to occur between the two tables,
as well as selecting just records that macth the date I need. 

In COBOL, the only way I figured out how to do that was to select
by date in the INPUT procedure to a SORT, and have the SORT order    
the output by FUND-NUMBER. Essentially waht you suggested, just
once pick and choose in the INPUT, and another put *this* in *THAT*
queue in the output. 

But it feels so clumsy, and I blusingly admit, I have not really 
ever used the COBOL SORT verb in a production environment,
though I have encountered it in maintenance. There will be about a
year's worth of data in a many-to-one relationship there. I am
a little concerned about how long it may take to run when the
files are populated.

-Paul

         

Leif Svalgaard (leif@ibm.net) wrote:
: I would do a start on the history table, with the date given and
: a not less condition. If I find a record, I'd check if the date
: matches, then do a next record until the date changes.
: For each record found, go pick up fund data from the master
: based on fund-number.

: I don't understand the role of fund-report-number so maybe
: I'm off the mark.

: paulr wrote in message <74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com>...
: >Hi guys-
: >  I'm doing a RAD model of some software that will be ran under CICS,
: >perhaps with a database backend, and today I ran into a bit of a

: >been treating the COBOL files like db tables, with appropriate
: >indcies and so on.  well imagine a pair ot tables like
: >this:
: >
: >        fd  fund-master-table.
: >        01  fund-master-table-record.
: >            10 fund-number               pic 9(4).
: >            10 fund-name                 pic x(25).
: >            10 fund-report-number        pic 9(6).
: >              .... <more fields> ....
: >
: >        fd  fund-history-table.
: >        01  fund-history-table-record.
: >            10 fh-id-number              pic 9(12).
: >            10 fd-fund-number            pic 9(4).
: >            10 fd-fund-date              pic 9(8).
: >            10 fd-fund-value             pic s9(11)v99.
: >              .... <more fields> ....
: >
: >with selects similar to:
: >
: >            select fund-master-table
: >              assign to fmt-file
: >              organization is indexed
: >              access mode is dynamic
: >              record      key is fund-number
: >              alternate   key is fund-name
: >              alternate   key is fund-report-number with duplicates.
: >
: >            select fund-history-table
: >              assign to fht-file
: >              organization is indexed
: >              access mode is dynamic
: >              record      key is fd-id-number
: >              alternate   key is fd-fund-number with duplicates
: >              alternate   key is fd-fund-date with duplicates.
: >
: >Now the task here is to print those two little buggers out on x number
: >of separate reports (report number in the master file) by fund, and only
: >for a particular (user selected) date.
: >
: >The only way I can think to do it is to sort the history file first,
: >by fund, then process each record individually in an output procedure.
: >COnversely, I could use an input and output procedure to a sort,

I like Leif's idea of STARTing on a given date, but then you should assemble
all the data needed to print the report detail in one sort record (this
should include report #) and pass this to a sort (this will utilize an input
procedure and output procedure) and process the report in the output
procedure.  If you need summaraization the sort can do this for you but you
must pass a sort control card in a $ORTPARM card in JCL.

Leif Svalgaard wrote:

> I would do a start on the history table, with the date given and

…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

On Thu, 10 Dec 1998 21:39:00 -0500, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>Have you thought of introducing a split key that has date,
>fund number in it?  Often in history files, I will have 9-10
>different key combinations, reflecting the various combinations
>of reports required.

He mentioned CICS - which means VSAM - if he had DB2 he'd just do the
SQL thing.  VSAM does not allow "split keys" --- (It's one of those
"Platform Specific" items in the new standard ... don't you know!) <G>
```
