###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-12-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74u9hi$b4i@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Of course CICS doesn't - since the CICS application owns all the files,
straight in from the JCl. :)  But it is FAR more convienent to talk 
about the shape and organization of VSAM files in COBOL syntax, than
it is to talk about them in terms of key starts and extents. <GRIN>

The system will unfortunately, not be able to run with DB2, as the 
corporate standard is AdaBASE, and I want to avoid another level of
support if possible. VSAM should serve just fine in this instance,
which means that essentially all the normal things you do in COBOL
are available with CICS equivalents, and the file structures are
identical. 

I'm also going to have to figure out some way to updates CICS files
from a batch process, oh joy...


-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36741080.1844512@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com> <74q06u$d80$1@news.igs.net> <3670aef3.86681934@news1.ibm.net> <3670d454.73652706@news3.ibm.net> <74u9hi$b4i@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On 12 Dec 1998 17:35:46 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:


>I'm also going to have to figure out some way to updates CICS files
>from a batch process, oh joy...


If you need to do updates to the VSAM files from inside CICS and from batch, I would
strongly advise AGAINST Shareoption 4....You could either use Shareoption5 (which is a CA
product (yukk)) or, much better, you could use EXCI (External CICS Interface)  to send the
update requests from batch to the owning CICS region and have them done there, by CICS, on
behalf of the batch program.

HTH


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981213192454.07170.00000772@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36741080.1844512@news3.ibm.net>`

```

In article <36741080.1844512@news3.ibm.net>, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
writes:

>If you need to do updates to the VSAM files from inside CICS and from batch,
>I would
>strongly advise AGAINST Shareoption 4

Yes, there is an I/O penalty for using SHR(4), but we have never experienced
corrupt data due to it.

Our heavily-used in-house stuff is currently in Datacom/DB and we have a lot of
applications using VSAM/Transparency to get to it. It provides the concurrent
batch and online updates but without the heavy I/O penalty. If there is a
transaction abend, when CICS does a backout the updates will automatically be
rolled back. Likewise, if a batch job does some updates and abends, those
updates will be rolled back.

One problem would be if both VSAM and VSAM/T files are updated in batch and an
abend occurs--only the VSAM/T (Datacom/DB) will be rolled back.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3674693f.331018828@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com> <74q06u$d80$1@news.igs.net> <3670aef3.86681934@news1.ibm.net> <3670d454.73652706@news3.ibm.net> <74u9hi$b4i@lotho.delphi.com> <36741080.1844512@news3.ibm.net>`

```
On Sun, 13 Dec 1998 19:11:05 GMT, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
wrote:

>On 12 Dec 1998 17:35:46 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>behalf of the batch program.

BTW  - I agree.  Share option 4 does not maintan total data integrity
- but it's a lot better than 3!

Anyway, I never had any problems using Shareoption 4 with Batch and
online updates - so long as only a single batch region/partition
updated the file when CICS had it open.  When 2 batch regions, AND
CICS could update the file we almost ALWAYS had corruptions of the
VSAM file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3674c2f2.45057178@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com> <74q06u$d80$1@news.igs.net> <3670aef3.86681934@news1.ibm.net> <3670d454.73652706@news3.ibm.net> <74u9hi$b4i@lotho.delphi.com> <36741080.1844512@news3.ibm.net> <3674693f.331018828@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Mon, 14 Dec 1998 01:27:56 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:


>
>BTW  - I agree.  Share option 4 does not maintan total data integrity
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>VSAM file.  

Thane,

as far as I know Shareoption 4 does not provide ANY update integrity across address
spaces.  It does provide READ integrity be rereading the VSAM buffers every time a record
is requested (that way defeating LSR and other caching like mechanism that might be
emploied to increase performance).  I believe that Shareoption 4 only means that VSAM
allows two MVS jobs to open the file simultaneously for update, and the users (i.e. the
jobs opening the file) need to provide record locking themselves, using MVS mechanisms (I
do not know anything about VSE, things there might be different)

BTW, how do you handle inserts to a VSAM file with shareoption 4?  You could have CI
splits, changes of the HURBA in one address space, and the data in the new CIs would not
be available to the other address space - all in all I find Shareoption4 to be less then
optimum  <g>


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981215213038.07764.00001048@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3674c2f2.45057178@news3.ibm.net>`

```

In article <3674c2f2.45057178@news3.ibm.net>, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
writes:

>(I do not know anything about VSE, things there might be different)

In VSE, SHR(3) allows multiple jobs to open the file for update, but has no
mechanism for preventing corruption. (That is up to the application.) SHR(3) is
very dangerous.

SHR(4) allows multiple appliations to open the file for update, and further
provide CI locking and, within a CICS, will also do record-level locking
(record-level only within that CICS but still lock the CI for everything else
outside of that CICS).

Furthermore, on a random read (with or without update intent) SHR(4) will
always reread the sequence set (lowest-level index) CI and reread the data CI
(even when LSR is used). (The reading of the sequence set CI and data CI on
every random read is what gives the high I/O penalty for SHR(4).) Sequential
processing will not reread the CI and can do read-ahead.

We have never experienced any file corruption when using SHR(4), but then we
almost never do inserts from batch while CICS has the file open.

Mark A. Young
```

>SHR(4) allows multiple appliations to open the file for update, and further

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74rc41$8hj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com> <74q06u$d80$1@news.igs.net> <74ra0o$e6r@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
The split key feature is present is apparently non-standard,
so not all compilers have it.  Both MF and Fujitsu do, though.
A typical select is as below.  (The answer is yes). The code
shown is MF code.  DTRK-INTO-THE-YARD is *not* a field
in the record of the file ... the name appears *only* in the
select clause, and can be used in the normal way in read
statements.  The various fields are concatenated into a
pseudo field which is placed into the index.  Note that with
Fujitsu, you omit the key name, and list the actual fields
when you do the read.

The method will not save you scanning the file, but it will save
you having to do a sort.  You should also note that if you do
a bunch of split key stuff, the index can actually get quite a bit
bigger than the actual data file. I have found it quite a useful
technique, especially for history files as they do not tend to
have a lot of updating. It can also be handy when an existing
file gets a new requirement, as you do not have to change
the file layout, though you would have to re-index and recompile
everything.

  SELECT DTRK-FILE ASSIGN TO DISK
   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
   ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
   FILE STATUS IS DTRK-STATUS
   RECORD KEY IS DTRK-KEY
   ALTERNATE KEY IS DTRK-INTO-THE-YARD =
                                DTRK-SHOW, DTRK-TARE-DATE
                                DTRK-IN, DTRK-KEY
   ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS DTRK-DISPATCH-KEY
    WITH DUPLICATES.

paulr wrote in message <74ra0o$e6r@lotho.delphi.com>...
>?? I honestly don't know how to do that in COBOL. You can assign
>to or more fields to a single key, as you can in a database?
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

#### ↳ Re: Sorts and such...

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3671d4ee.73806788@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<74po46$58a@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On 11 Dec 1998 00:13:58 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

>Hi guys-
>  I'm doing a RAD model of some software that will be ran under CICS,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>              .... <more fields> ....


Paul,

if your program is to run under CICS, then you cannot use FD, SELECT, OPEN, etc in your
program.  You have to use either the native CICS functions (EXEC CICS READ FILE),  or you
should revert to DB2  (EXEC SQL SELECT)


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem ansi cobol 85

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7OQb2.1447$CE2.539766@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9812101155470.82828-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <74obas$1uc_001@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812101253490.60392-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be>`

```
joris.poelmans <9410355@student.luc.ac.be> wrote:
>I solved the problem already, in a really stupid way, I just retyped the
>code completely (without changing  anything) and the stuff worked, ...
                  ==========================
>really mysterious and it seems I'm not the only one who has this problem,
>a lot of my fellow students had to do the same thing, maybe it's the ansi
>cobol, maybe tbox, I really don't know,

As you will learn with experience, the above underlined phrase is an
unwarranted assumption.  If you become a good programmer it will be in
part due to learning one particular thing: There is a great difference
between human perception and reality.  Just because you do not perceive
a difference (or a problem or an error) does *not* mean that there isn't
one there.  Working with computers will teach you a great deal about
human fallibility.  Once you fully appreciate how fallible you are, and
how unreliable your perception really is, you can begin to develop habits
which protect you from error, in a way analogous to how we build error
correction into data transmission protocols.  All the best programmers
do this, though they may not consciously think of it in this way. :-)
```

>joris.poelmans <9410355@student.luc.ac.be> wrote:
>>I solved the problem already, in a really stupid way, I just retyped the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>--


Judson, I believe your excellent observations here could be paraphrased in
the old homily "learn from your mistakes."  At the very least, one should
determine what mistakes have been made, to avoid repetition of those same
mistakes in the future.  Effectively, that's what "experience" is - it
incorporates the knowledge of what _not_ to do, which, in the final
analysis, may be equal in value to the knowledge of what must or should be
done.
```

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)

- **Message-ID:** `<74psaj$1b4_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9812101155470.82828-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <74obas$1uc_001@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812101253490.60392-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <7OQb2.1447$CE2.539766@news4.mia>`

```
In article <7OQb2.1447$CE2.539766@news4.mia>,
   "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
+joris.poelmans <9410355@student.luc.ac.be> wrote:
+>I solved the problem already, in a really stupid way, I just retyped the
+>code completely (without changing  anything) and the stuff worked, ...
+                  ==========================
+>really mysterious and it seems I'm not the only one who has this problem,
+>a lot of my fellow students had to do the same thing, maybe it's the ansi
+>cobol, maybe tbox, I really don't know,
+
+As you will learn with experience, the above underlined phrase is an
+unwarranted assumption.  If you become a good programmer it will be in
+part due to learning one particular thing: There is a great difference
+between human perception and reality.  Just because you do not perceive
+a difference (or a problem or an error) does *not* mean that there isn't
+one there.  Working with computers will teach you a great deal about
+human fallibility.  Once you fully appreciate how fallible you are, and
+how unreliable your perception really is, you can begin to develop habits
+which protect you from error, in a way analogous to how we build error
+correction into data transmission protocols.  All the best programmers
+do this, though they may not consciously think of it in this way. :-)

Amen! Amen!

One of the biggest pitfalls of debugging one's own code is that when you
write a program, eat, sleep, and breathe that program, you become so
familiar with it that when you attempt to debug it, you will stare and
stare at a flawed line of code and your brain will read what you know it 
is *supposed* to say, and *not* what is actually there before your eyes.

I'll bet that if joris.poelmans <9410355@student.luc.ac.be> happens to
compare his original, broken program with the current, working version
using some utility program (rather than scanning it visually) he will
discover at least one slight but crucial difference (e.g. switching
an alpha O and a numeric 0).
```

On Fri, 11 Dec 1998 01:25:39, dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller) 
wrote:


> One of the biggest pitfalls of debugging one's own code is that when you
> write a program, eat, sleep, and breathe that program, you become so
> familiar with it that when you attempt to debug it, you will stare and
> stare at a flawed line of code and your brain will read what you know it 
> is *supposed* to say, and *not* what is actually there before your eyes.

This is why I am not shy about grabbing a collegue and asking them to 
look over my sholder as I walk then thru the broken code.  MOST times 
they don't have to DO or say anything.  It becomes obvious when you 
start explaining to someone else what it does and why.

The problem seems to be coming back and as you guys seem to know what
the problem is, please take a look at it, the program compiles ok but
gives a runtime error when going to a certain screensection,
.... the code goes wrong like this, choose reports and ..... then
"illegal character in numeric field".

All help is appreciated


      $set ans85 mf noosvs sqldb(sample)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       Program-ID. startprog.

       DATA DIVISION.
       Working-storage section.
       01 hmitem pic 9.
       01 smitem pic 9.
       01 cmitem pic 9.
       01 rmitem pic 9.
       exec sql begin declare section end-exec.
       01 supid pic x(4).
       01 custid pic x(4).
       01 supname pic x(25).
       01 custname pic x(25).
       01 supstreet pic x(25).
       01 custstreet pic x(25).
       01 supstrno pic x(2).
       01 custstrno pic x(4).
       01 supcity pic x(25).
       01 custcity pic x(25).
       01 ccurrcredit pic 9(5).
       01 cstatus pic x(2).
       01 cmaxcredit pic 9(5).
       01 einde-buffer pic x.
       exec sql end declare section end-exec.
       exec sql include sqlca end-exec.
       01 einde pic x.
       01 stop1 pic x.
       01 stop2 pic x.

       SCREEN SECTION.
       01 hoofdscreen.
          03 blank screen.
          03 line 1 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 2 col 15 "*      Inventory system      *".
          03 line 3 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 5 col 6 "1. Supplier order entry screen".
          03 line 6 col 6 "2. Customer order entry screen".
          03 line 7 col 6 "3. Add or change supplier".
          03 line 8 col 6 "4. Add or change customer".
          03 line 9 col 6 "5. Add new product".
          03 line 10 col 6 "6. Reports ".
          03 line 11 col 6 "7. Exit program".
          03 line 12 col 2 value "INPUT?".
          03 line 12 col 9 pic 9 using hmitem  required.

       01 supplscreen.
          03 blank screen.
          03 line 1 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 2 col 15 "*    Supplier order screen   *".
          03 line 3 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 5 col 6 "1. Add new supplier order".
          03 line 6 col 6 "2. Change existing order".
          03 line 7 col 6 "3. Delete pending order".
          03 line 8 col 6 "4. Print existing order".
          03 line 9 col 6 "5. Back to main screen".
          03 line 11 col 2 value "INPUT?".
          03 line 11 col 9 pic 9 using smitem required.

        01 custscreen.
          03 blank screen.
          03 line 1 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 2 col 15 "*    Customer order screen   *".
          03 line 3 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 5 col 6 "1. Add new customer order".
          03 line 6 col 6 "2. Change existing order".
          03 line 7 col 6 "3. Delete pending order".
          03 line 8 col 6 "4. Print  existing order".
          03 line 9 col 6 "5. Back to main screen".
          03 line 11 col 2 value "INPUT?".
          03 line 11 col 9 pic 9 using cmitem required.

       01 repscreen.
          03 blank screen.
          03 line 1 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 2 col 15 "*       Report screen        *".
          03 line 3 col 15 "******************************".
          03 line 5 col 6 "1. Generate reports".
          03 line 6 col 6 "2. Back to main screen".
          03 line 7 col 6 "3. Print customer records".
          03 line 8 col 6 "4. Print supplier records".
          03 line 11 col 2 value "INPUT?".
          03 line 11 col 9 pic 9 using rmitem required.

       01 newcustscreen.
          03 blank screen.
          03 line 1 col 15 "*******************************".
          03 line 2 col 15 "*  New customer screen        *".
          03 line 3 col 15 "*******************************".
          03 line 5 col 4 value "Custid (4 positions):".
          03 col 29 pic x(4) using custid required.
          03 line 6 col 4 value "Name:".
          03 col 10 pic x(25) using custname required.
          03 line 7 col 4 value "Street:".
          03 col 12 pic x(25) using custstreet required.
          03 line 8 col 4 value "Streetno:".
          03 col 14 pic x(4) using custstrno required.
          03 line 9 col 4 value "City:".
          03 col 10 pic x(25) using custcity required.

       01 newsupscreen.
          03 blank screen.
          03 line 1 col 15 "*******************************".
          03 line 2 col 15 "*  Change supplier screen     *".
          03 line 3 col 15 "*******************************".
          03 line 5 col 4 value "Supid (4 positions):".
          03 col 29 pic x(4) using supid required.
          03 line 6 col 4 value "Name:".
          03 col 10 pic x(25) using supname required.
          03 line 7 col 4 value "Street:".
          03 col 12 pic x(25) using supstreet required.
          03 line 8 col 4 value "Streetno:".
          03 col 14 pic x(4) using supstrno required.
          03 line 9 col 4 value "City".
          03 col 10 pic x(25) using supcity required.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       mainsection.
        move 'n' to einde
        perform hoofdmenu until einde="y"
        display "Program terminated"
        stop run.

       hoofdmenu.
        move 0 to hmitem
        display hoofdscreen
        accept hoofdscreen
        if hmitem = 1 then perform supscr end-if
        if hmitem = 2 then perform custscr end-if
        if hmitem = 3 then
           move 'n' to stop1
           perform adsup until stop1='y'
           end-if
        if hmitem = 4 then
           move 'n' to stop2
           perform adcust until stop2='y'
           end-if
        if hmitem = 5 then perform adprod end-if
        if hmitem = 6 then perform repscr end-if
        if hmitem = 7 then move "y" to einde end-if.


       supscr.
       move 0 to smitem
       display supplscreen
       accept supplscreen
       if smitem = 1 then perform addnewsupord end-if
       if smitem = 2 then perform chsupord end-if
       if smitem = 3 then perform delsupord end-if
       if smitem = 4 then perform printsupord  end-if
       if smitem = 5 then perform hoofdmenu end-if.

       adsup.
       display newsupscreen
       accept newsupscreen
       exec sql insert into sup values (:supid, :supname, :supstreet,
                  :supstrno, :supcity, 0, 0, null)
       end-exec
       display "Add, change or delete another supplier"
       accept stop1.

       adcust.
       display newcustscreen
       accept newcustscreen
       exec sql insert into cust values (:custid, :custname,
       :custstreet, :custstrno, :custcity, 0, 0, null)
       end-exec
       display "Add, change or delete another customer"
       accept stop2.

       adprod.
       display "test".

       custscr.
       move 0 to cmitem
       Display custscreen
       accept custscreen
       if smitem = 1 then perform addnewcustord end-if
       if smitem = 2 then perform chcustord end-if
       if smitem = 3 then perform delcustord end-if
       if smitem = 4 then perform printcustord end-if
       if cmitem = 5 then perform hoofdmenu end-if.

       repscr.
       Display repscreen
       accept repscreen.
       if rmitem = 1 then perform rep end-if
       if rmitem = 2 then perform hoofdmenu end-if.
       if rmitem = 3 then perform pcust end-if
       if rmitem = 4 then perform psup end-if.

       pcust.
       exec sql declare bufptr cursor for
         select custid, name, street, streetno, city, maxcredit,
         currcredit, status from cust
       end-exec
       exec sql open bufptr end-exec
       perform leesbuf until einde-buffer='J'.

       psup.
       display "test".

       leesbuf.
       exec sql fetch bufptr into :custid, :custname, :custstreet,
       :custstrno, :custcity , :cmaxcredit, :ccurrcredit, :cstatus
       end-exec
       if SQLCODE = 100 move 'J' to einde-buffer
          else display custid, ' ', custname, ' ', custstreet, ' ',
          custstrno, ' ', custcity, ' ', cmaxcredit, ' ', ccurrcredit,
          ' ', cstatus
       end-if.

       addnewsupord.
       display "test".

       addnewcustord.
       display "test".

       chsupord.
       display "test".

       chcustord.
       display "test".

       delcustord.
       display "test".

       delsupord.
       display "test".

       printcustord.
       display "test".

       printsupord.
       display "test".

       rep.
       display "test".

     *******************************************************
     *             Joris.Poelmans@advalvas.be              *    
     *     J-Tech Invest : http://lisa.luc.ac.be/jtech     *
     *                The truth is a virus                 *     
     *******************************************************

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem ansi cobol 85

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75312b$4cp@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9812101155470.82828-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <74obas$1uc_001@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812101253490.60392-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <7OQb2.1447$CE2.539766@news4.mia> <74psaj$1b4_001@news.netdirect.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-nUwuEUCeCCWK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <74s38p$2mc_003@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812141254350.50886-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be>`

```
I haven't studied your program in detail (much less tried to compile and run
it myself) BUT I think you have several NUMERIC fields on your screen
defined with the USING phrase.  This means that the "sending item" must be
numeric at the time of your very first DISPLAY.  I think you initialize (to
zero) one of these fields, but my guess is that you haven't initialized them
all - and so when it tries to do a DISPLAY using the unitialized fields,
they have invalid data in them.

There are several ways to fix this, certainly the easiest (but not
necessarily the most user-friendly) would be to place a "Value Zero" on all
your USING field input fields.

joris.poelmans <9410355@student.luc.ac.be> wrote in message ...
>The problem seems to be coming back and as you guys seem to know what
>the problem is, please take a look at it, the program compiles ok but
…[262 more quoted lines elided]…
>> +> One of the biggest pitfalls of debugging one's own code is that when
you
>> +> write a program, eat, sleep, and breathe that program, you become so
>> +> familiar with it that when you attempt to debug it, you will stare and
>> +> stare at a flawed line of code and your brain will read what you know
it
>> +> is *supposed* to say, and *not* what is actually there before your
eyes.
>> +
>> +This is why I am not shy about grabbing a collegue and asking them to
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9812101155470.82828-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <74obas$1uc_001@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812101253490.60392-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <7OQb2.1447$CE2.539766@news4.mia> <74psaj$1b4_001@news.netdirect.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-nUwuEUCeCCWK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <74s38p$2mc_003@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812141254350.50886-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be>`

```
Note to regular c.l.c. lurkers -  this program could be a considered a good
example of the need for an ELSE-IF construct.

Joris - see my suggested changes interspersed within your COBOL code below.
BTW, just because the value you will accept and test is numeric does not
necessarily require a PIC 9 for the sending, receiving and display data
elements.  I prefer to use a PIC X for these sorts of data elements, but
YMMV.

Please allow me another gratuitous comment.  Those series of IF ... END-if
conditionals are bad style - any following conditional expressions are
unnecessarily tested even when a prior condition has already been found to
be TRUE.  This is a case where the proposed ELSE-IF discussed here in c.l.c.
could be put to good use.

You have used both single quote and double quote marks in your code to
surround literal values.  I couldn't guess which is your compilers preferred
style so I used single quotes for the revised values in all conditional
tests.

joris.poelmans <9410355@student.luc.ac.be> wrote in message ...
>The problem seems to be coming back and as you guys seem to know what
>the problem is, please take a look at it, the program compiles ok but
>gives a runtime error when going to a certain screen section,

And we are supposed to guess _which_ screen section it was?  I've noted
below an error in your code (involving a run-on equal sign in a conditional
test) that IMHO would preclude a clean compile.  So I'll consider your
assertion that "the program compiles ok" is a Clintonistic statement :-)

>.... the code goes wrong like this, choose reports and ..... then
>"illegal character in numeric field".
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>       01 hmitem pic 9.

>       01 smitem pic 9.

>       01 cmitem pic 9.

>       01 rmitem pic 9.
>       01 hmitem pic 9.

         01 smitem pic X.
         01 cmitem pic X.
         01 rmitem pic X.
         01 hmitem pic X.

        * Use the ALPHANUMERIC values in your screen display/accept items.

>       exec sql begin declare section end-exec.
>       01 supid pic x(4).
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>          03 line 12 col 2 value "INPUT?".


>         *03 line 12 col 9 pic 9 using hmitem  required.


             03 line 12 col 9 pic X using hmitem required.

>
>       01 supplscreen.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>         *03 line 11 col 9 pic 9 using smitem required.


             03 line 12 col 9 pic X using smitem required.

>
>        01 custscreen.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>         *03 line 11 col 9 pic 9 using cmitem required.

             03 line 12 col 9 pic X using cmitem required.

>
>       01 repscreen.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>         *03 line 11 col 9 pic 9 using rmitem required.


             03 line 12 col 9 pic X using rmitem required.

>
>       01 newcustscreen.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>        move 'n' to einde

>        perform hoofdmenu until einde="y"
>        display "Program terminated"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>        *move 0 to hmitem

           move space to hmitem

>        display hoofdscreen
>        accept hoofdscreen


>        if hmitem = '1' then perform supscr end-if
>        if hmitem = '2' then perform custscr end-if
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>        if hmitem = '6' then perform repscr end-if

>        if hmitem = '7' then move "y" to einde end-if.

        *  note that the above style is very inefficient - how about trying:
           evaluate hmitem
                  when '1' perform supscr
                  when '2' perform custscr
                  when '3'
                           move 'n' to stop1
                           perform adsup until stop1='y'
                  when '4'
                           move 'n' to stop2
                           perform adcust until stop2='y'
                 when '5' perform adprod
                 when '6' perform repscr
                 when '7' move "y" to einde
                 when other
                          display "Enter a number (1 through 7)"
         end-evaluate.
        *  you could (should?) also use this style in your
        *  other accept/display routines

>
>
>       supscr.
>      *move 0 to smitem

         move space to smitem

>       display supplscreen
>       accept supplscreen

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>This is why I am not shy about grabbing a collegue and asking them to
>look over my sholder as I walk then thru the broken code.  MOST times
>they don't have to DO or say anything.  It becomes obvious when you
>start explaining to someone else what it does and why.

I completely agree.  I am pretty careful about explaining to them just
how my code works, keeping pretty much to explaining the end result and
my general approach.  I have found that when you really need them to
help you find a bug, you can actually give them your own bias which is
keeping you from seeing the problem.  For example, you point to a bit
of code and say "this does blah, blah, blah" and they unconsciously take
your word for it, not realizing that is where your bug is. :-(
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem ansi cobol 85

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VtRc2.5465$xx1.996390@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9812101155470.82828-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <74obas$1uc_001@news.netdirect.net> <Pine.A41.4.05.9812101253490.60392-100000@rsftew.luc.ac.be> <7OQb2.1447$CE2.539766@news4.mia> <74psaj$1b4_001@news.netdirect.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-nUwuEUCeCCWK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <VJsc2.272$E43.347160@news4.mia> <36734AF3.E1C34A2B@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>then the flow of the program becomes very obvious.

Absolutely.  I have done this hundreds of times, which is why I agreed
with Thane's comments.  It's interesting to see the expression on a
newbie programmer face the first time an experienced old war-horse grabs
them to help spot a bug in their code.  "Who, ME?!?" :-)

>There is of course the polution of bias for QA.  I still have not worked
>this out yet though.

I have seen it happen many times, which is why I am cautious when I
explain my code to someone who is trying to help me.  It is a careful
tradeoff, because the more detail in which you explain your code, the
more likely you are to see your own error.  At the same time, the more
detail you go into, the more likely you are to bias the other person's
thinking.  The idea is to explain your logic and intent without biasing
the other person's assumptions.  Hopefully, you will see your error in
the process.  If not, then the other person may be able to spot it.
After some 30 years of doing this, I have developed a pretty good feel
for it, but it is too subtle for me to articulate more clearly than
that you want to convey what you the code is supposed to do rather than
exactly what you *think* the code is doing.  Can anybody help me out
here, and express this better?
```

In article <VtRc2.5465$xx1.996390@news3.mia>, judmc123@bellsouth.net 
says...

<snip 1>

> 
> I have seen it happen many times, which is why I am cautious when I
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> here, and express this better?

<snip 2>

I doubt that I can come close to what you are capable of Judson but I 
will have a crack at it and see what I can accomplish.  

In many cases I have found myself so caught up in the details of a 
problem/task so much that I no longer understand the nature of the 
problem. At that point the best thing I find to do is to get out of the 
detail level and go back to a higher level where the problem/task is more 
abstract. Discussing this with someone else at this level will allow them 
to come up with a detailed solution that they would use. It also lets me 
start over and pull back to see the big picture. 

I think the old saying "not seeing the forest for the trees" or something 
like that explains the whole concept as simply as anything. 

Well that's my 2 cents.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem ansi cobol 85

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981212155046.07736.00000535@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-nUwuEUCeCCWK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-nUwuEUCeCCWK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>,
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) writes:

>This is why I am not shy about grabbing a collegue and asking them to 
>look over my sholder as I walk then thru the broken code.  MOST times 
>they don't have to DO or say anything.  It becomes obvious when you 
>start explaining to someone else what it does and why.

That method is how one of our operators is a great help to the programmers--she
says she can get a second job easily as a sounding board.

When I was helping our student programmers, another method I found personally
helpful was, "If I wanted to reproduce this effect, how would I do it?" About
half the time it would lead me to the problem in just a couple of minutes.

Fresh eyes are also very helpful--many people (including programmers) see what
they want to see, not what is in front of them.

In the past I had drawn lines to show conditional levels, but now the compiler
shows the depth into conditional statements, which also helps.

Mark A. Young
```

seem to be less than other Unix flavors. I believe pricing follows this
relationship: DOS < Win95 < NT / Linux < UNIX.

speaking officially. Contact info@acucorp.com for a price quote.

- **From:** peter.derichs@rocketmail.com (Peter)
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10d9ad02e33ac616989683@news.wxs.nl>`

I'm still testing Powercobol V4 for conversion of large programs and 
databases. One problem I came across was when working with values that 
are returned from OCX's containing numeric or alphabetic values, 
PowerCOBOL gave an error trying to use the 'value'-item. It receives only 
numeric data and gives an error when using this.
It does work in VB5 however. 

Does anybody have an idea how to retrieve those fields?
Is there a special conversion field?

Thanks in advance,
Peter Derichs.
```

<a id="t-7ebd003d37"></a>
## Thread: This Is Your Brain On PL/I (In 1977)...This Is Your Brain On COBOL (In 2000)...Any Questions? — 7 msgs

### This Is Your Brain On PL/I (In 1977)...This Is Your Brain On COBOL (In 2000)...Any Questions?

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981210014652.01024.00000480@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
COBOL.Standards-Commitee.Proposal 
vs. 
Proposal Of Standards-Commitee Of COBOL?

30 posts and counting in the ongoing debate for a feature that's been available
in PL/I for several decades, but one that the COBOL Standards Commitee feels
can't be attained, at least in this Millennium...

Hey, Standards Dudes (And Dudettes):

1. In France I might go to la maison de Jim (pardon my poor recollection of
high school French), but in America, I go to Jim's house, NOT to the house of
Jim (OTOH, the House of Blues is another story).

2. The lack of this feature has given rise to tens of millions of lines of
COBOL in unnecessary copybooks and record descriptions embedded in COBOL
programs that are essentially duplicates of "real" copybooks, with the addition
of a prefix (e.g., OLD-, NEW-) to employ prefix notation, needlessly
complicating coding and Y2K remediation efforts. Some shops have even taken to
writing compiler exits to allow a prefixing convention for COPY statements.

3. It might be instructive to pick up another programming language's manual
from time to time to see what you might learn - PL/I, SQL, REXX, VB, VBA etc.
all permit this type of prefixing notation.

4. As far as legacy code goes, backwards compatibility could be provided. Also,
it wouldn't take a compiler design genius to write code to replace ' OF '
notation with '.' notation (or vice versa with a preprocessor approach).

My two cents...
```

#### ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On PL/I (In 1977)...This Is Your Brain On COBOL (In 2000)...Any Questions?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74of97$3c0@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981210014652.01024.00000480@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```

Theo DP wrote in message <19981210014652.01024.00000480@ng-ca1.aol.com>...
>COBOL.Standards-Commitee.Proposal
>vs.
>Proposal Of Standards-Commitee Of COBOL?
>
>30 posts and counting in the ongoing debate for a feature that's been
available
>in PL/I for several decades, but one that the COBOL Standards Commitee
feels
>can't be attained, at least in this Millennium...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>high school French), but in America, I go to Jim's house, NOT to the house
of
>Jim (OTOH, the House of Blues is another story).
>
>2. The lack of this feature has given rise to tens of millions of lines of
>COBOL in unnecessary copybooks and record descriptions embedded in COBOL
>programs that are essentially duplicates of "real" copybooks, with the
addition
>of a prefix (e.g., OLD-, NEW-) to employ prefix notation, needlessly
>complicating coding and Y2K remediation efforts. Some shops have even taken
to
>writing compiler exits to allow a prefixing convention for COPY statements.
>
>3. It might be instructive to pick up another programming language's manual
>from time to time to see what you might learn - PL/I, SQL, REXX, VB, VBA
etc.
>all permit this type of prefixing notation.
>
>4. As far as legacy code goes, backwards compatibility could be provided.
Also,
>it wouldn't take a compiler design genius to write code to replace ' OF '
>notation with '.' notation (or vice versa with a preprocessor approach).
>
>My two cents...

1) To the best of my knowledge no one has yet come up with a final proposal
to MAKE to the Standards committee. (So I don't know how or why you think
this has already be rejected)

2) Yes, the Standards committee has said that they do not think they
can/will add ANY new features into the next draft at this time. However, it
has been "open for input" for over 7 years now - and they have over 150
major or semi-major corrections that still need to be made to what is
already done.  Do you really want to wait for a Standard until they not only
fix these but add and fix MORE new features?

3) There is a "volunteer" list available from the chair of J4 for work to be
done on the existing draft.  If you think you want to get the Standard out
more quickly or get more features in sooner *AND* you are willing to follow
(and understand) the Standard's drafting rules, I am positive that any help
you might want to offer would be accepted quite happily!
```

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-10T00:00:00

```
I visited the link below, and found that those of you who are 
really interested in making changes can get a better idea of the
problem you face by reading the items there. There agenda, and
open item list, etc. and proposals can give you a running play by
play. I'm amazed that it continues in this manner after so long.
It must work, even if we don't think it works fast enough.

The community of participants can grow substantially, but the necessary
effort to contribute is far from just writing a proposal. I believe
you all could be like the congressional and senate clerks for this
effort by running down material, supplying alternatives, and detailing
objections and compromises. I know that most of the people who are 
working on these committees are not full time on this job. I hope they
would welcome your participation.

Warren Simmons

Warren G. Simmons wrote in message <36706789.359DD105@inficad.com>...
>I visited the link below, and found that those of you who are
>really interested in making changes can get a better idea of the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Warren Simmons

To clarify something, it is POSSIBLE to contribute (both comments and
proposals) withOUT being a member of the committee.  Normally, this is done
by those of us who have either been on the committee in the past or at least
worked with it in some detail.  HOWEVER, there are also those who submit
papers without ever seeing the committee or going to a meeting.  (Often a
committee person will "take over" the paper once it is discussed and needs
updating - so it is very helpful if you can provide it electronically - but
I assume most readers of this NG can do that.)

IMHO, there probably are NOT many members of this NG who could contribute
significantly to the draft at the stage it is at now (because of the
"bureaucracy" involved in proposal writing), but I do think that offers
might well be accepted.

>
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >William M. Klein wrote in message
<74of97$3c0@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>...

needing

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36716906.4393@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<19981210014652.01024.00000480@ng-ca1.aol.com> <74of97$3c0@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <74om53$k92$1@server.cntfl.com> <74pd3l$4j0@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <36706789.359DD105@inficad.com>`

```
Warren G. Simmons wrote:
> 
> I visited the link below, and found that those of you who are
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Actually, if a whole bunch of people flooded us with suggestions and 
the like you can kiss the standard goodby.  We are required to respond 
to every outside comment.  This means the committee time would be used 
responding to these comments and nothing else would get done.  
Obviously, something pointing out a clear error is extremely useful.  
Or, if there was a sort of "clearinghouse" to filter out the 
non-relevant stuff.
```

```
I am currently looking for a team of IBM Mainframe COBOL developers at
various levels.
The position is for a major French Bank that is soon to be opening a South
London office. Work will initially be based in Paris for about 6 months,
after which time the project will be permanently London based.
It is not essential that you speak French, but it will be a distinct
advantage, especially for junior positions.
This is a superb opportunity for anyone looking to progress their career
rapidly within a challenging and demanding environment. If you are
interested in this or any other vacancies e-mail, or phone me now.

Stuart Clarke 
IT Prospects.
stuart.clarke@itprospects.co.uk
Telephone- 0181 336 6841

I look forward to hearing from you.
```

windows is using SP2 from www.flexus.com

Best

FF

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36732abb.33176457@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36695DD8.4D90@swbell.net> <74bpqo$mnt@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3670b40e.176161996@netnews> <74qu1j$17g@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 04:59:15 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
>Randall Bart wrote in message <3670b40e.176161996@netnews>...
>>'Twas Sat, 5 Dec 1998 11:15:33 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>in-line perform? 

An in-line PERFORM *must* be terinated by END-PERFORM.  But even assuming
this compiler allows optional END-PERFORM, as in your END-ADD example, the
terminator pairs to the closest possible verb.

>(P.S. as asked by another person, which compilers DO accept
>this?)

Unisys A Series Cobol85 accepts it when FEDLEVEL is set to 5.  I'll try
setting the FEDLEVEL down to see if it gets flagged.  I know it worked on
some PC Cobol.  I usually put all my terminators in nested statements, but
I've never had a compiler berate me when I didn't.  Most of the programs I
work on have non-ANSI database sysntax, so I prefer to have most such
flagging options turned off.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36771fcf.92055862@news1.ibm.net>`

```

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>Randall Bart wrote in message <3670b40e.176161996@netnews>...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bot MicroFocus (as far back as 3.1) and CA-Realia (as far back as 4.2)
both accept the inline perform with no internal statements.

Fujitsu and VMS COBOL do not.  I have not tried it using COBOL for
this and that.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00

- **References:** `<36695DD8.4D90@swbell.net> <74bpqo$mnt@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3670b40e.176161996@netnews> <74qu1j$17g@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

>>>>    PERFORM
>>>>      IF A ...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>this?)

There I go again - commenting without reading - the post was talking
about the above and *I* was talking about

PERFORM VARYING CNTR FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL CNTR > 10
END-PERFORM

As opposed to the standard comforming versoin which needs a CONTINUE
in it.

<a id="t-8192d0626b"></a>
## Thread: Dummy paragraphs — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Dummy paragraphs

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367133C0.82CFB635@home.com>`

```
The EXIT paragraph is also used for dummy paragraphs which are performed
to meet UNTIL conditions. 

     PERFORM 1000-DUMMY 
         VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
            UNTIL MY-INDEX > MY-INDEX-MAX
               OR MY-VALUE (MY-INDEX) = SPACES.
1000-DUMMY.
     EXIT.

I have mainly used older compilers, but I was thinking that an in-line
PERFORM would be cleaner here, except that I can't use the No-op command
EXIT.  What do you think?
```

#### ↳ Re: Dummy paragraphs

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36713db1.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<367133C0.82CFB635@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <367133C0.82CFB635@home.com>...
>The EXIT paragraph is also used for dummy paragraphs which are performed
>to meet UNTIL conditions.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>EXIT.  What do you think?

This is a non-objectionable use of EXIT. You can make it better by
using a more descriptive name that makes it clear what is going on:
PERFORM LOOK-FOR-FIRST-SPACE
   VARYING MY-INDEX ...
               OR MY-VALUE (MY-INDEX) = SPACES

This nice and free comment is why actually the above construction
is better than an inline perform. This would be even more the case
if the condition were more complicated.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dummy paragraphs

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36734147.38940232@netnews>`
- **References:** `<367133C0.82CFB635@home.com> <36713db1.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Dec 1998 09:43:43 -0600, when "Leif Svalgaard"
<leif@ibm.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>This is a non-objectionable use of EXIT. You can make it better by
>using a more descriptive name that makes it clear what is going on:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>if the condition were more complicated.

No, the dummy paragraph should have a name like DUMMY-PARAGRAPH.  If you
name the dummy paragraph then I have to go look at LOOK-FOR-FIRST-SPACE to
find out it does nothing, and the paragraph can't be resued for other
purposes without confusion.  Do you really want to come upon code like
this:
PERFORM LOOK-FOR-FIRST-SPACE
   VARYING MY-INDEX FROM END-POS BY -1
               UNTIL MY-VALUE (MY-INDEX) = "."

In COBOL-85 the correct form is like this:

PERFORM VARYING MY-INDEX FROM END-POS BY -1
               UNTIL MY-VALUE (MY-INDEX) = "."
    CONTINUE
END-PEFORM

It's too bad that the spec requires the CONTINUE -- the meaning is clear
without it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dummy paragraphs

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<753ed1$56e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<367133C0.82CFB635@home.com> <36713db1.0@news1.ibm.net> <36734147.38940232@netnews>`

```

>
> In COBOL-85 the correct form is like this:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I just tried this under MF Cobol Workbench, with the ANS85 dialect switch and
it worked fine!

PERFORM VARYING MY-INDEX FROM END-POS BY -1
               UNTIL MY-VALUE (MY-INDEX) = "."
END-PEFORM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dummy paragraphs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<755onu$o3t$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<367133C0.82CFB635@home.com> <36713db1.0@news1.ibm.net> <36734147.38940232@netnews> <753ed1$56e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote in message
<753ed1$56e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>
[...]
>I just tried this under MF Cobol Workbench, with the ANS85 dialect switch
and
>it worked fine!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Use some additional directives.

FLAG"ANS85" will create a message file referencing the
non-conforming syntax. The file is named prog-name.msg.

If you also use FLAGAS"S", the non-conforming syntax will
be treated as severe.

Try it with and without the CONTINUE statement.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<74s40t$n5h@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

>to meet UNTIL conditions.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

>         VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1

#### ↳ Re: Dummy paragraphs

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367304e2.239776728@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Fri, 11 Dec 1998 08:01:20 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>The EXIT paragraph is also used for dummy paragraphs which are performed
>to meet UNTIL conditions. 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>EXIT.  What do you think?

Perform varying My-Index From 1 by 1 
             Until My-Index > My-Index-Max
             Or My-Value (My-Index) = Spaces
                  Continue
End-Perform

- **Message-ID:** `<74s6ac$md6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<74rt8l$6j6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Which Cobol?

Chen.Arbel@us.aks.com wrote in message <74rt8l$6j6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Hello All,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Link To DLL

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367524BA.C62E67BE@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<74rt8l$6j6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Chen.Arbel@us.aks.com wrote:

> Hello All,
>
> I have a question
>
> How how to link from Cobol program to a DLL???

>From COBOL, you do not link but CALL.

> What is the command?
> Or short example how to do that?

State platform (compiler version etc.)

> Thanks very much.

To give you something, I suppose you've Micro Focus.
Two possibilities: 1) Single-entry-point DLL and 2) multiple entry-point
DLL.

1)
DLL:
Identification division.
Program-id. Single.
Environment division. ...
Data division. ...
Procedure division [using ...].
    ...
    Exit program.
end program single.

Calling the DLL:

...
    CALL "SINGLE" [using ...]

2)
DLL:
Identification division.
Program-id. Multiple.
Environment division. ...
Data division. ...
Working-storage section.
... global data, available from within all DLL-entries
Procedure division [using ...].
Main-para.
    Display "this code is reachable by call as in single"
    Exit program.

    ENTRY "Multi1" [using ...]
    ...
    Exit program.

    ENTRY "Multi2" [using ...]
    ...
    Exit program.

    ENTRY "Multi3 etcetera" [using ...]
    ...
    Exit program.
end program Multiple.

Calling the DLL:

Procedure division.
    ...
    Set pp to entry "Multiple" *> just once needed,
            this makes the DLL addressable, I think that a "LoadLibrary"
win-API call is done behind the scenes.
    ...
    CALL "MULTIn" [using ...] *> as many as you like

> Chen
>
> -----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

The Frog
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Link To DLL

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36762BEB.54A6D7DE@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<74rt8l$6j6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <367524BA.C62E67BE@IMN.nl>`

```
COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote:
> 
> Chen.Arbel@us.aks.com wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >From COBOL, you do not link but CALL.

I think the question is relating to the command
required to *create* a DLL from a COBOL source file,
rather than a CICS LINK statement or similar.

> > What is the command?
> > Or short example how to do that?

Again, assuming a reasonably up-to-date Micro Focus system,
the simplest form is:

cbllink -D source.cbl

There are additional options for being more specific
about which RTS the DLL should use etc.

Cheers, Kev.
```

>Randall Bart wrote:
>> Oddly, many compilers generate different code for IF NUM-FIELD ZERO and

- **Message-ID:** `<74s4gk$nh8@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3662de01.0@news1.ibm.net> <19981130142756.20613.00000236@ng21.aol.com> <eYnV4ndH#GA.123@upnetnews05> <36652831.0@news1.ibm.net> <#USW42eH#GA.214@upnetnews05> <36657d96.168710913@news.acucorp.com> <ewBuzUvH#GA.161@upnetnews05> <74781b$o5k$1@news.igs.net> <3667d1bb.180873407@netnews> <74a5db$bvq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3668B05F.F1AAE0E3@att.net> <74b6ar$5jb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <74br9c$nli@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3670aa96.173737112@netnews> <36713857.5C61DE73@home.com> <36713ee3.0@news1.ibm.net>`

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36713ee3.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Howard Brazee wrote in message <36713857.5C61DE73@home.com>...
>>Randall Bart wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Leif, old boy,
  I think you missed the beginning of this thread

   If Num-Field Zero

is most definitely VALID COBOL and has been for many, many years.  This is
the "sign test" and not an equality test.  It works just the same as

   If Num-Field Positive
        and
   If Num-Field Negative

These don't require any 88-level and they do not require (in fact prohibit)
the use of "=" or "EQUAL".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZERO or ZEROS or ZEROES

```
Bill is, of course, correct here. I just jumped the gun and ASSumed
(bad, bad) that it was a typo and therefore wrong. I don't remember
though having ever used this construction in its narrow meaning.

William M. Klein wrote in message <74s4gk$nh8@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36713ee3.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>There could be a difference if you could collect all the tests on
NUM-FIELD
>>as a series of 88-conditions. Since these would all collected in one place
>>maintenance might be a bit easier.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ZERO or ZEROS or ZEROES

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3662de01.0@news1.ibm.net> <19981130142756.20613.00000236@ng21.aol.com> <eYnV4ndH#GA.123@upnetnews05> <36652831.0@news1.ibm.net> <#USW42eH#GA.214@upnetnews05> <36657d96.168710913@news.acucorp.com> <ewBuzUvH#GA.161@upnetnews05> <74781b$o5k$1@news.igs.net> <3667d1bb.180873407@netnews> <74a5db$bvq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3668B05F.F1AAE0E3@att.net> <74b6ar$5jb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <74br9c$nli@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3670aa96.173737112@netnews> <36713857.5C61DE73@home.com> <36713ee3.0@news1.ibm.net> <74s4gk$nh8@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <36719b95.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I suspect you are not alone in never having used (or even being aware of the
existence of) the construct IF dataname ZERO.  After over 30 years of COBOL
on a multitude of platforms, I just became aware of it on c.l.c. a short
while ago.

Are there any "heavy" users of this COBOL feature out there that would care
to comment on it's applicability?

Randall Bart wrote in message <3670aa96.173737112@netnews>...
>'Twas Sat, 5 Dec 1998 11:40:25 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>However, IBM does (for some unknown reason) allow ZEROS and ZEROES (at
least
>>with the older OS/VS COBOL and DOS/VS COBOL compilers) for the sign test -
>>as an extension.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I understood from a previous posting of Bill Klein that IF dataname ZERO
acted as sort of a complement of IF dataname POSITIVE and IF dataname
NEGATIVE.  A TRUE value for the test would indicate that dataname was
neither POSITIVE nor NEGATIVE.  I stand ready to be corrected if I have the
wrong slant on this belief.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ZERO or ZEROS or ZEROES

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74s4ok$nmk@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3662de01.0@news1.ibm.net> <19981130142756.20613.00000236@ng21.aol.com> <eYnV4ndH#GA.123@upnetnews05> <36652831.0@news1.ibm.net> <#USW42eH#GA.214@upnetnews05> <36657d96.168710913@news.acucorp.com> <ewBuzUvH#GA.161@upnetnews05> <74781b$o5k$1@news.igs.net> <3667d1bb.180873407@netnews> <74a5db$bvq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3668B05F.F1AAE0E3@att.net> <74b6ar$5jb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <74br9c$nli@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3670aa96.173737112@netnews> <#VEH7$SJ#GA.87@upnetnews03>`

```

Dennis J. Minette wrote in message <#VEH7$SJ#GA.87@upnetnews03>...
>
>Randall Bart wrote in message <3670aa96.173737112@netnews>...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>with the older OS/VS COBOL and DOS/VS COBOL compilers) for the sign
test -
>>>as an extension.
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't vouch for this, but I have a vague memory that on IBM mainframe's
you can (thru assembler or "odd" COBOL) end up with either a negative or
positive zero (i.e. sign-nibble of C or D).  The sign-test If data-name ZERO
gets a "true" (as I recall) for either a negative or positive zero (or an
unsigned with X"F") while the equality test If data-name EQUAL ZERO only
passes when the zero is positive.  (As I say, this is a pretty vague memory,
so I may have it backwards - or even completely wrong, but it is my memory.)
```

I have seen code in mid to late 60's era programs (IBM mainframe):
SUBTRACT ZERO FROM data-name
and the comment was to force a sign - I never really understood the

<a id="t-05f7a716cd"></a>
## Thread: Alternative to ELIF — 1 msgs

### Re: Alternative to ELIF

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3671CF18.7173@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<36695DD8.4D90@swbell.net> <74bpqo$mnt@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <36695DD8.4D90@swbell.net>...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >    END-PERFORM

[snip -- saying it's not conforming code.]

> Furthermore, it is probably NOT what you would want any way.  Where you
> (actually "he") have
>     ELSE IF C
> that "C" could be an inline PERFORM and the END-PERFORM would have to match
> up with it.

I'll take your word for it that the code is not ANSI-conforming.
However I don't understand your point about "C" being an inline
PERFORM.  Suppose I make the example a little less schematic:

 PERFORM
    IF AAA = 3
	PERFORM PARA-A3
    ELSE IF BBB = 7
	PERFORM PARA-B3
    ELSE IF CCC = 5
        PERFORM UNTIL COUNTER > 10
           PERFORM PARA-C5
           ADD 1 TO COUNTER
        END-PERFORM
 END-PERFORM

If that's the sort of thing you had in mind, then I don't see the
problem.  The first END-PERFORM would terminate the inner inline
PERFORM, and the last END-PERFORM would terminate the outer one.
With only one END-PERFORM there would be a syntax error because the
outer PERFORM would be unterminated.

Were you thinking of something else?  Off the top of my head I can't
think of a case which would lead to ambiguity.  Since others have
indicated that some compilers accept this syntax, it must not be too 
difficult to implement.

I'm beginning to like this proposed construct.  Legalizing it would
disrupt the language less than adding OR-IF (or one of its other 
spellings).  Unlike OR-IF, it would enable us to eliminate excessive 
END-IFs in cases where EVALUATE wouldn't help.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```
