<a id="t-d81f4ce272"></a>
## Thread: How will Y2K affect ME????? — 42 this month / 59 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** ismokealot@mailexcite.com (ACHED)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>`

```
I've heard stories about the potential power outages and loss of water
due to y2k, and am wondering how likely these things are to occur.
Also I have heard a number of people plan to take their money out of
banks and the stock market. I realize if this occurs that there will
not be enough money on hand in the banks or the market. How likely is
a situation of mass hysteria like this? What are some other
ramifications?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3_ad2.54$Ji.106437@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>`

```
ACHED wrote:
>I've heard stories about the potential power outages and loss of water
>due to y2k, and am wondering how likely these things are to occur.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>ramifications?

The general consensus among most programmers who are working on Y2k
(including myself) appears to be that there will be some problems,
but probably nothing catastrophic.  I believe that government is the
most likely place to have catastrophic problems, if any.  There is a
bureaucratic mindset that simply cannot understand that meetings and
planning do *nothing* toward fixing the problem.  Some agencies have
had meeting after meeting, committee after committee, study group
after study group, wringing their hands and accomplishing nothing but
wasting the time we have left.  It will be the troops in the trenches
fixing the code which will make it work, if it works.  Unfortunately,
many bureaucrats have no clue about this.  I know one local bureaucrat
who stated that "Y2K is a hoax."  Can you believe that?!?  If these
people don't get it straight Real Soon, they will be paying the piper
come early 2000.

Bankers hate spending money, but they hate going out of business or
facing a court trial for negligence even more.  I'm expecting they
will have their house in order.  I simply don't believe the power
system is as vulnerable to shutdown from Y2K problems as some think.
According to a number of posts by people who should know, the power
grid has virtually no concept of date or time.  Some people worry
about getting fuel to the power plants, but I would think the people
who do the transport are not fools, and that most of the schedules are
regular stuff.  Just keep doing what you did last week and the week
before that, etc.  If Europe could continue to function under the
unimaginable damage and disruption of WWII, when the infrastructures
themselves were destroyed, this should not even be a bump in the road
by comparison.  And consider that millions of very intelligent, clever
and creative people will be thinking of every conceivable way to 'get
by' any disruption.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981214193906.24236.00000368@ng125.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3_ad2.54$Ji.106437@news2.mia>`

```
FWIW,

I'm right in the middle of a supply chain that stretches from the
manufacturer to the retailer.  At this stage I'm confident all three
legs of this stool will be up and supporting commerce as usual on
January 1, 2000.

I've had lengthy discussions with our vendors (the manufacturers)
and our customers and am satisfied that there has been honest and
open disclosure of problem areas and that these problem areas 
will be remediated and tested no later than March 1, 1999.  I personally
am 100% finished with code conversion and am well into testing where
I'm employed.

HOWEVER, our corner of the marketplace is commercial landscaping
equipment.  Not bloody likely to cause mass hysteria in the event the
local landscaper can't get bearings for his aerator.

>And consider that millions of very intelligent, clever
>and creative people will be thinking of every conceivable way to 'get
>by' any disruption.

Also those of us that are just borderline average.

JC Jones.
```

#### ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** ismokealot@mailexcite.com (ACHED)
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3675da21.8960377@news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>`

```

I was thinking not along the lines of the allies who survived the
devastation of WWII but along the lines of the post-WW1 Germans, who
saw their currency devalued to the point where they were hauling
wheelbarrows of cash to the market, eventually reaching a state where
Hitler became a viable option. Besides, I feel the WWII analogy is a
bit flawed because they in no way depended on government et al the way
we depend now on computers, and their lives were much more modest and
self-sufficient.

Although I am just finishing my first semester of COBOL and defer to
your experience, I can not help believing that there will be some very
serious problems. 

Let me work from the radical to the realistic. If the government, as
you say, will experience catastrophic problems, wouldn't this be the
ideal time for Iraq (or Lybia, India, whoever) to either launch a
conventional or nuclear attack against us? Would our Patriot Missiles
work? Or other defence systems? I can't help thinking of the
Nostradamus documentary hosted by that fat bastard Orson Welles...
"huuuh, in the fourth quatrain, Nostradamus predicted an attack from
the east..." , and they showed an Arab, turbin and all, nuking New
York City.

Anyway, I digress. As far as banks and lending institutions go, yes I
was assured that my bank was Y2K ready, but are the other institutions
that they deal with? Especially with this so-called Global Economy.
How much do we have invested in foreign markets? Quite a bit. I think
it's very probable for people to panic, and to see a complete
financial meltdown by the third quarter. I was hoping for convincing
evidence otherwise.

Thirdly, the only practical problem I can speak of with any authority
is my job. I work for the nations largest cable operator,which employs
32,000 people. None of our test or headend equipment is y2k ready,
which in reality is not a big deal since this season really sucks, but
we are still using bubble-sheets for payroll. When 2000 rolls around
it is unlikely that the 100 or so in payroll will be able to manually
calcualte and write 32,000 paychecks. If you think that through as
much as I have many, many problems arise.

I don't think that my worries about y2k are on the same level as
Chicken Little, (and if you want I will tell you what I am personally
doing to prepare for the worst and you can see for yourself how
paranoid I am) but it seems to me that you are downplaying this a bit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G1td2.584$ME4.382508@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3675da21.8960377@news.prodigy.net>`

```
ACHED wrote:
>
>I was thinking not along the lines of the allies who survived the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>self-sufficient.

They depended on roads and bridges, buildings for shelter, supply lines
for food, and telephones and radio for communications, and power lines,
though to a lesser degree than we.  You think our banks having computer
glitches would compare with their banks, houses or even whole cities,
being bombed into rubble? ;-)

>Although I am just finishing my first semester of COBOL and defer to
>your experience, I can not help believing that there will be some very
>serious problems.

Could be.  Only God knows for sure right now, but we're going to find
out in 13 months. :-)

>Let me work from the radical to the realistic. If the government, as
>you say, will experience catastrophic problems, wouldn't this be the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>York City.

That scenario presumes their weapons would be effective and ours would
not.  My guess is that our SSN alone would make them seriously regret
it, at least for the few minutes before annihilation.  Having been in
the military, you can be sure that when it comes to anything which
affects 'mission', they take it *very* seriously.

>Anyway, I digress. As far as banks and lending institutions go, yes I
>was assured that my bank was Y2K ready, but are the other institutions
>that they deal with? Especially with this so-called Global Economy.

What makes you think your bank will 'go down' if they can't communicate
with a few other banks?  Don't you think there have been instances when
bank computers fail for a day or two (I've seen it happen.  Bad for the
bank, not a big deal for other banks.), or when banks themselves fail?
It doesn't bring down the whole system.  Human institutions are so
error prone by nature, that there must be a lot of tolerance built into
them just to function.  There probably has never been a time when the
entire U.S. power grid has been totally operational at once.  Companies
survive when half or more of the staff is sick.  The fact is that we
deal with failures in our systems every day.  That's why we have backup
systems and plans.  By and large, they work.

>How much do we have invested in foreign markets? Quite a bit. I think
>it's very probable for people to panic, and to see a complete
>financial meltdown by the third quarter. I was hoping for convincing
>evidence otherwise.

It's pretty hard to prove a negative.  But I have seen few programmers
actually involved in Y2K who are spreading gloom and doom.  I have spent
the last several months doing Y2K remediation for a couple of local
government clients.  The fact is that fixing programs for Y2K is a fairly
simple task.  There are just a lot of programs.  The key is to start in
time, and concentrate on doing it rather than talking about it.  But it
is often the case that organizations could survive on a fraction of the
software that they have.  Probably most reports which are printed go
unread most of the time anyway.

>Thirdly, the only practical problem I can speak of with any authority
>is my job. I work for the nations largest cable operator,which employs
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>much as I have many, many problems arise.

After you have been in computers for a while, and have experienced a
few times when the ox gets in the ditch, you will have a better
appreciation for how things can be kludged when necessary.  Consider
this.  Your company payroll wacks out.  So you print the previous
payroll again, then manually print checks for new employees and take
out checks for terminated employees.  Under normal conditions, this
would be untinkable, but to save the company you can bet they would
try it.  Cleaning it up would be a mess, but it could allow you to
scrape by until a fix was implemented.  Better to lose some money
than all of it. :-)  I suspect similar kludges could be employed for
food stamps, welfare, etc.  You can bet that during WWII there were
millions of such kludges employed on a daily basis.  Many computer
systems will work fine starting 2/1/2000, because it is only the
century transition which they can't handle.  Some companies plan to
go out of business on 12/31/1999 and open business as a new company
on 1/1/2000.  No history, no century crossover, etc.  Kludgy, but it
will work in many cases.  Under times of severe need, the government
can move quickly to pass laws relaxing restrictions on things like
this.  This process has already started.  For example, the feds have
removed the restriction of hiring as contractor people who have only
recently retired from government, if they are Y2K programmers.  It
is my opinion, shared by many here, that the main reason a lot of
people are scared is that they clearly see the 'boogers', but aren't
seeing the possible mitigating factors.

>I don't think that my worries about y2k are on the same level as
>Chicken Little, (and if you want I will tell you what I am personally
>doing to prepare for the worst and you can see for yourself how
>paranoid I am) but it seems to me that you are downplaying this a bit.

I don't totally discount the possibility that it could be very bad; I
just don't think it likely.  Historically, when people are faced with
severe catastrophe (e.g. hurricane Andrew), people coped.  If people
can cope when their whole city has been flattened (no job, no bank,
no food, only the clothes on their backs), how can we think it will
be worse when we still have our homes, our cars, our roads, etc.?
Just because the computer doesn't print out a schedule, don't you
think the people driving food trucks will still have sense enough to
come work and do their daily or weekly routes?  If the computer were
to turn off a switch in a power plant, don't you think there are
engineers available smart enough to wire around it?  Do you think
they are going to sit there and stare at it for weeks and months and
do nothing? :-)  If people can survive when almost the entire support
structure is devastated, how can we not survive when all the physical
structures are untouched, everybody is still healthy and ready to make
it work?  Are not history and experience more reliable guides than
sheer speculation?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3676C52D.95FB93BB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3675da21.8960377@news.prodigy.net> <G1td2.584$ME4.382508@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> ACHED wrote:

> >Although I am just finishing my first semester of COBOL and defer to
> >your experience, I can not help believing that there will be some very
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> out in 13 months. :-)

Actually, we have been finding out.  A large portion of the Y2K problems
occur at times other than Jan 1, 2000.  Some people were inconvenienced
when they tried to charge things with expiration dates of 01/00 for
instance.   Banks and other financial institutions are constantly
working with future dates.

What happened when these future dates were dependent upon faulty
programs?  Not much, some people were inconvenienced, others lost some
money.  A couple of articles were written up in the papers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** "DENNIS A BROUSE" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75j1f4$1elo$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3675da21.8960377@news.prodigy.net>`

```
At our bank, we just finished the last test on our check processing
software, so you better not count on any extra float, come 1/1/2000 :~).
All in all, our bank will be ready.  But it's all academic if people really
believe that we're not ready and decide to pull all their money out.  In
that case, just look in the mirror for the real Y2K problem.  There will be
some bumps for sure, but as Thane pointed out, we can kludge things to get
by if need be.  Heck, I still drive a standard shift car that I can push to
start if my battery decides to quit on 1/1/2000.
We need a little more confidence in our own abilities here.

Denny
```

#### ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** joker@yahoo.com (Zoogs)
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>`

```
This is an excerpt form
http://www.amcity.com/baltimore/stories/1998/12/21/focus3.html

Frautschi, a former Johns Hopkins University instructor who serves on
the President's Council on Year 2000 Conversions, warns that less than
1 percent of the 50 billion integrated chips used in computer systems
around the world are Y2K-compliant -- a scenario that could lead to
disruptions ranging from massive power outages and food shortages to
the inadvertent launching of nuclear weapons. 

A recent report on the millennium bug issued by the federal Office of
Management and Budget said that only about 40 percent of the
government's computers were ready for 2000, including critical systems
in the departments of Defense, Energy, Health and Human Services, and
Transportation. 

Seated in front of his Y2K-compliant laptop computer, Mark Frautschi
spoke with the Baltimore Business Journal about one of the biggest
technological challenges in recent memory. 

BBJ: What should people do to prepare for Y2K? 

Frautschi: I suggest that you study the problem. Spend a week surfing
the Web and develop your own sense of what a credible worst-case
scenario is for your life, and then develop your sense of what one
notch worse is. Then prepare for one notch worse. 

BBJ: That sounds like a civil defense drill. What are some of the
critical systems that could fail as a result of the millennium bug? 

Frautschi: There's a good chance there will be disruptions in electric
power. My intuitive answer is that there will be a series of
disruptions for maybe the first 48 hours, and then there may be some
places that get the lights back on relatively quickly and there will
be some places like Quebec, where it took seven weeks [parts of Quebec
suffered a major power loss earlier this year due to ice storms]. What
you had in Quebec was that within 72 hours people were stealing
generators. In Y2K, it's possible that we might lose other
infrastructures, maybe not at exactly the same time or the at the same
level, but you might be more than one generator away from normalcy. 

BBJ: There have been reports that major food shortages could be
another result of Y2K. Do you agree? 

Frautschi: Not only are those reports credible, they're illustrative
of how much we've invested ourselves in technology. I think you can
now operate a super tanker with a crew of about 10. It used to be more
than double that. Today when you build a super tanker, you don't build
bunk beds, you build in a lot of embedded systems. 

I think there are a lot of metaphors around the Titanic that are
useful for thinking about Y2K, including lack of preparation and a
certain amount of denial that the possibility of the problem could
exist. If just 1 percent of the rivets on the Titanic failed at
midnight, what would the consequences be? 

Would it be another thing to put on the ship's maintenance list? Would
the ship sink, or would it be somewhere in between? Putting that small
a stress on our infrastructure when we've stressed our infrastructure
to the limit is an issue. 

BBJ: Is Y2K a network administrative manager's nightmare? 

Frautschi: How do you coordinate 10,000 PCs in your organization where
they are all different and were all bought at different times? Even if
you get them all Y2K-compliant, somebody's going to break their hard
drive and they're going to go to back it up, and then they're not
going to be compliant anymore.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net>`

```
Zoogs wrote:
>This is an excerpt form
>http://www.amcity.com/baltimore/stories/1998/12/21/focus3.html
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the inadvertent launching of nuclear weapons.
<snip>

We don't have to read any farther than that to see we have an extremist
here.  The statement "less than 1 percent of the 50 billion integrated
chips used in computer systems around the world are Y2K-compliant" is
very misleading.  Only a very small percentage of those chips *need* to
be Y2K compliant, because the date has absolutely nothing to do with the
devices they control.  The CPU(s) in your printer, for example, could
care less about date.  To say that they "aren't Y2K compliant" verges
on a lie, it presents so distorted an impression.  Ask yourself "How
many devices do I use that I must enter the date when I buy it, or when
I change the batteries?"  Then ask yourself "If those devices failed,
how much problem would it be for me?"  Yes, most VCRs do know the date,
and many of them won't time record properly after 1/1/2000.  But does
it explode when the power fails and it comes up blinking 12:00?  Or if
you neglect to set the date properly is your life ruined?  If your VCR
doesn't accept dates after 1/1/2000, then simply select an earlier year
which has the same weekday configuration (starts on the same day of the
week and is/is not a leap year) tell your VCR it's that year.  You and
the VCR will be just as happy.

A number of technical people who have worked in the power generation
industry for decades have posted that there is nowhere in the power
generation system which is even aware of the date.  The systems are
responsive moment to moment; date is of no consequence.  When you
think about it, this is completely logical.  Administrative computer
systems are date aware, just as in any management function of any
company.  But those systems don't produce the power, they produce
reports for management.  The power system won't fail if the CEO does
not get his P/L sheet on time!

How many of you have ever experienced your automobile refusing to run
because you failed to enter the correct date?  How many of you have
ever *heard* of somebody ever entering a date into an automobile,
other than the dashboard clock?

Yes, there is no doubt that when 1/1/2000 rolls around, every farmer
in the world will become paralyzed, their tractors won't run, the
produce will cease to grow, and all the meat animals will immediately
cease eating and die.  The truck drivers will forget to come to work
to deliver the groceries to your shelves.  All the people in the food
production and distribution industry will completely forget to come
to work and follow the schedule they followed the week before, and
the week before that, etc.  Yes, we are all going to starve to death
from Y2K.  Sheesh!  God save us from simpletons!

Do you *really* think that nuclear weapons are designed such that a
date error will launch them?  If the people who handle nuclear weapons
were that stupid, we would have been blown to bits long before now.
Not to say that they are all Y2K compliant, just that it is not that
easy to set off a nuclear weapon.  Several years ago there was an
accident in a U.S. ICBM silo which caused the rocket engine to explode.
It blew the top off the silo and the warhead was thrown hundreds of
yards away.  However, the warhead did not go off.  Not a nice thing,
but remember that these weapons aren't stored in residential areas,
they are miles from anything else.  In fact, there have been many
occasions when planes carrying nuclear weapons have crashed, and none
of them have ever gone off.  It is difficult to create a nuclear
explosion, even when you want one, and those devices have many fail-
safe features.  Consider that there have been something like 60,000+
nuclear devices around for decades, handled by thousands of people,
and not one of them has ever gone off by accident.  Don't you think
that, in all that time, there have been all kinds of failures of the
electronics in those things, far worse than a date failure?  It is
not an accidental launch or detonation of weapons which is the problem
with Y2K, but the fact that some of the guidance systems depend on
precise time measurements.  So, if we just happen to get into a
wholesale nuclear exchange right after 1/1/2000, the missiles may not
all strike their target.  Considering that the targets are often high
population areas, probably *less* people would be killed, and *less*
damage would be done.  Not that there would be that many of us left
around to keep track of it.  So let's keep our perspective on the Y2K
issue.  We may see a nuclear Armageddon, but it won't be from Y2K.  A
nuclear terrorist attack is almost infinitely more likely, almost a
probability.  If you want to worry about something, worry about that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>here.  The statement "less than 1 percent of the 50 billion integrated
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

[snip Judson's rational and well-thought-out comments]

...but Judson...you forgot about the worst Y2K catastrophe of all!
Everybody knows that at the center of the Sun is a large nuclear
fusion reactor built millions of years ago by aliens (it was
engineered one of DD's ancestors!)

Now despite the fact that he was a brilliant alien and used a 7 digit
year field, the reactor core temperature is controlled by a small
microprocessor which used a windowing technique that will fail at the
turn of the century.  Trust me...I know these things.

Ok...now I've already purchased a massive amount of stock in the Acme
Sun Lamp Company, so everyone should immediately go to their local Wal
Mart and buy several dozen of them before it's too late!  Don't delay!

Oh...and by the way, don't purchase any other brand.  The other sun
lamp brands are not Y2K compliant!  Acme Sun Lamps are!  Pick up a
couple Acme Sun Lamp brand extension cords while you're at it too!
The extension cords only allow electrons with 4 digit year fields to
pass through.

;-)

Happy holidays to all and thanks for putting up with my bit of
insanity.  The good news is that my insanity is caused by solar
flares, so I'll be feeling much better after December 31, 1999.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75pi77$2c8_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <75p6ib$itt$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <75p6ib$itt$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>,
   "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
+
+Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
+>Zoogs wrote:
+>>This is an excerpt form
+>>http://www.amcity.com/baltimore/stories/1998/12/21/focus3.html
+>We don't have to read any farther than that to see we have an extremist
+>here.  The statement "less than 1 percent of the 50 billion integrated
+>chips used in computer systems around the world are Y2K-compliant" is
+>very misleading.  Only a very small percentage of those chips *need* to
+>be Y2K compliant, because the date has absolutely nothing to do with the
+>devices they control.
+
+#Ah, but there are surprises! The Baton Rouge Fire Department found
+that their fire trucks could pump water after 1/1/00, but the ladders would
+not work! It seems the computer detected that required maintenance had
+not been performed in 99 years.

Proof, please. This sounds like an urban legend to me.

+
+The CPU(s) in your printer, for example, could
+>care less about date.  To say that they "aren't Y2K compliant" verges
+>on a lie, it presents so distorted an impression.  Ask yourself "How
+>many devices do I use that I must enter the date when I buy it, or when
+>I change the batteries?"
+<snip>
+>A number of technical people who have worked in the power generation
+>industry for decades have posted that there is nowhere in the power
+>generation system which is even aware of the date.  The systems are
+>responsive moment to moment; date is of no consequence.  When you
+>think about it, this is completely logical.
+
+## But the chips do measure time - "moment-to-moment" - and are, in many
+cases, off-the-shelf chips. It's just that the application does not access
+the
+"Year" function of the chip. Now, if these chips fail to measure the year
+properly,
+who knows what value they will give for the moment.

You're assuming that there *is* a year function. No proof.

+
+>How many of you have ever experienced your automobile refusing to run
+>because you failed to enter the correct date?  How many of you have
+>ever *heard* of somebody ever entering a date into an automobile,
+>other than the dashboard clock?
+
+##The mechanic enters the date during maintenance.

Proof, please (again). I have been doing my own car repairs for many
years, and I have never entered a date into anything -- and my cars
still run just fine. If the engine computer was going to shut down 
because of some mythical internal maintenance schedule that hadn't
been followed, it would have happened to me long before now.

In any event, your comments here betray your lack of knowledge of
automobile maintenance schedules -- nearly all of which are based
on *miles*driven* and have nothing to do with dates.

+>
+>Yes, there is no doubt that when 1/1/2000 rolls around, every farmer
+>in the world will become paralyzed, their tractors won't run,
+
+## Much heavy equipment will not work at all according to the
+manufacturers (Catipillar, Ingram, Mack, et al). Fixes are in the works.
+
Here we go again. Proof, please. Otherwise, you're just making unsupported
assertions.

+>
+>Do you *really* think that nuclear weapons are designed such that a
+>date error will launch them?

Those were power reactors, not weapons systems. Duuuuuuh.
```

```
Doug Miller (dxmixxer@netdirect.net) wrote:
: +## But the chips do measure time - "moment-to-moment" - and are, in many
: +cases, off-the-shelf chips. It's just that the application does not access
: +the
: +"Year" function of the chip. Now, if these chips fail to measure the year
: +properly,
: +who knows what value they will give for the moment.

: You're assuming that there *is* a year function. No proof.

Usually, a "moment-to-moment" routine will start a counter and will stop
either when the counter decremented to zero or incremented to a certain
value. No year here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia>`

```
On Tue, 22 Dec 1998 13:04:47 GMT, "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>
>A number of technical people who have worked in the power generation
>industry for decades have posted that there is nowhere in the power
>generation system which is even aware of the date.

I am not quite sure if I can fully agree with this:  There are lots of systems for
maintenance control , especially in Power Plants - and what could happen if they would
report that 99 year, 364 days, and 23 hours have peen passed since the last maintenance -
and then shut down the plant for safety reasons?

>
>How many of you have ever experienced your automobile refusing to run
>because you failed to enter the correct date?  How many of you have
>ever *heard* of somebody ever entering a date into an automobile,
>other than the dashboard clock?


Ever owned a BMW with electronic support modules?  Date and time have to be set (by a
mechanic, e.g.)
>
>Yes, there is no doubt that when 1/1/2000 rolls around, every farmer
>in the world will become paralyzed, their tractors won't run,....

I don't think this slightly sarcastic tone really helps the argument.  The possible food
shortage would not happen because the food stops growing, but because the logistics might
fail , like Computers that calculate the load and the routes of trucks, like trucks etc
failing to work because of imbedded systems, etc
>
>Do you *really* think that nuclear weapons are designed such that a
>date error will launch them?

Of course nobody designed the system that way.  But, being a programmer, have you never
experienced a situation where a bug introduced some strange side effects, causing you to
say: "THAT should NEVER have happened"

>We may see a nuclear Armageddon, but it won't be from Y2K.  A
>nuclear terrorist attack is almost infinitely more likely, almost a
>probability.  If you want to worry about something, worry about that.
Sure, that's what we were told after the Tschernobyl event - Don't worry, nothing will
happen...

I do NOT believe that civilization ends with the end of 1999, but I don't believe that the
transition to 2000 will be absolutely painless, either.


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>plant for safety reasons?

Possible, but I sort of doubt it.  Remember, shutting the power plant down
is a serious safety risk in itself, not done unless there is a real need
to do so.

>>How many of you have ever experienced your automobile refusing to run
>>because you failed to enter the correct date?  How many of you have
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to be set (by a mechanic, e.g.)

I don't doubt you, but I seriously doubt if the actual engine/drive
train function is dependent on it.  It is almost certainly for keeping
track of maintenance.  Just imagine the irate owners of vehicles shut
down because mechanics entered an erroneous dates.  Not smart, eh? :-)

>>Yes, there is no doubt that when 1/1/2000 rolls around, every farmer
>>in the world will become paralyzed, their tractors won't run,....
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to work because of imbedded systems, etc

You cleverly snipped my comments which addressed that. ;-)  There is
a difference between sarcasm and hyperbole.  Now, don't you think the
people who work in the food distribution system would have at least a
clue about what kind of schedules they have been keeping for the last
10 years?  Do you think the companies shut down because their computer
goes down for 24 hours, or the printer which prints the truck schedules
malfunctions?  If our systems were that failure sensitive, we would be
running out of food and fuel all the time.

>>Do you *really* think that nuclear weapons are designed such that a
>>date error will launch them?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>happened"

I never seen bug cause a computer to explode.  Sure, bugs can cause
unanticipated results.  But there things which *can* be caused by bugs,
and there are things which *cannot* be caused by bugs.  Could a bug
in a program of yours cause the monitor to fly out the window?  We must
use common sense here.  As far as I know, every nuclear device in the
U.S. arsenal has a human in the circuit somewhere.  That means that a
manually activated switch (in most cases *two* manual switches) must be
engaged for the device to be fired.  A computer glitch cannot throw a
manually activated switch, any more than a glitch could cause your
monitor to fly out the window.  Just because you cannot necessarily
predict exactly what a glitch might cause, that does not mean that you
cannot define *any* limits to what a glitch might cause.

>>We may see a nuclear Armageddon, but it won't be from Y2K.  A
>>nuclear terrorist attack is almost infinitely more likely, almost a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>worry, nothing will happen...

That's pretty lame.  It is precisely because a reactor accident was
known to be a real possibility that containment systems and other fail-
safe measures were instituted.  Anybody who ever said that a nuclear
reactor accident was impossible was an idiot.  Anybody who knows zip
about how a reactor works can see a hundred ways it could fail.  But
any conceivable chain of events leading from a Y2K problem to a nuclear
launch is so wildly improbable that it borders on fantasy.

>I do NOT believe that civilization ends with the end of 1999, but I
>don't believe that the transition to 2000 will be absolutely painless,
>either.

Neither do I.  I expect a host of aggravations and inconveniences, and
a few major problems.  But I really doubt anyone will starve over it,
and I am not the least bit worried about a nuclear launch over it.  If
there is one, it will be intentional because somebody decided to take
advantage of any real or perceived chaos from Y2K.  Spreading panic is
only going to make that more likely, not less.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia>`

```
On Sat, 26 Dec 1998 21:13:18 GMT, "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>

>I never seen bug cause a computer to explode.  Sure, bugs can cause
>unanticipated results.  But there things which *can* be caused by bugs,
>and there are things which *cannot* be caused by bugs.  Could a bug
>in a program of yours cause the monitor to fly out the window? 

In fact, I did see software hurt hardware - When I had my first microcomputer, I wrote my
own opersating system for driving the floppy disks - In order to access a certain track, I
had to feed a hex number into an IO port, wait for a cuple of milliseconds, and then try
to retrieve the data.  The device supported 40 tracks, and thus my routine saveguarded
against too large tracks numbers by comparing against X'40'.  Later I tested with a loop
with ever increasing track numbers, and was really astonished what a stepping motor can do
to the drive's mechanic - software CAN damaged hardware

The other incident I have only heard about - must have been in the same time frame.
Someone allegedly had coded a video routine in such a way to as to cause the CRT cathodes
do filp status too often (sorry, I am not a hardware person, but I believe it would e
similar to driving a 60 Hz Monitor with 120 Hz)  This was supposed to damaged the monitor
as well
> We must >use common sense here.

Unfortunately, common sense is not too comon <g>
>  As far as I know, every nuclear device in the
>U.S. arsenal has a human in the circuit somewhere.  

snip...

Actually, I do agree with you on the point that nuclear weapons are probably much safer
than other devices, nuclear or not.  But, BECAUSE of the high level of safety mechanism in
nuclear power plants problem may arise.  In My area the electrical power comes to 80% out
of nuclear power reactors, and if they were to be shut down, even if this happens on
purpose, this would lead to major power disruptions
>

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>In fact, I did see software hurt hardware

Naughty! Naughty!  I did not say "hurt hardware", but "cause a computer
to explode", quite a difference!  Yes, of course, software can cause harm
to computer hardware, especially low level hardware drivers.  Hard drives
can be damaged by improper manipulation of head positioning, and computer
monitors can be damaged by improper settings of the vertical/horizontal
signals.  But I purposely said "explode", to illustrate that some things
are simply impossible, barring supernatural intervention.  This is my
central point here.  Just because you can't anticipate the exact result
of a bug does *not* mean that you cannot define limiting parameters of
any kind.  It is quite reasonable and proper to use common sense filters
to segregate consequences into such categories as 'impossible', 'highly
improbable', 'possible' and 'probable'.

I see two serious flaws in the 'thinking' of many doomsayers.  One is
their apparent inability to use the 'common sense filters' I just
described.  There is probably nothing we can do to help these people,
except try to encourage them, because they seem to lack the analytical
equipment to evaluate these things.  They are the same people who are
always falling prey to con artists, 'get rich' gimmicks and the like.
Unfortunately, they don't have 'analytically challenged' tattooed on
their foreheads, so the rest of us have to wait until they have said
enough to reveal themselves before we disregard any logical evaluation
they try to give us. :-)

But the most significant logic error made by the doomsayers is that
they only see the potential problems, and do not consider the very
significant mitigating effects of literally many millions of creative,
intelligent individuals working within the system to implement any kind
of stop gap kludge you could imagine to get by until the problems are
resolved.  Anybody who has worked in the practical end of things for a
while has probably had opportunity to observe this.  For example, when
things are normal, companies almost never willingly do something which
would cause problems with audit trails, etc.  But you let the ox get in
the ditch and management is looking company failure in the face, and the
bean counters will get locked in the closet so the technical weenies can
work their kludges.  Do you think some bureaucrat is going to watch
people starve instead of 'tweaking' the system?  The ones which try will
get fired (or shot) before you can turn around in an emergency the likes
of which is described by many doomsayers.  The one thing we learn from
history is that we don't learn from history.  Just look at the virtually
unbelievable devastation throughout Europe caused by WWII.  If those
societies could continue to function when the very infrastructures were
literally blown to rubble, what kind of thinking says that civilization
is going to fail when everything is intact but a few computer controlled
switches?  This borders on the absurd.  If you exclude those who were
intentionally persecuted, and those caught in battle, probably very few
people actually starved to death during WWII in Europe.  In other words,
a disaster of similar magnitude, but in which there were no hostilities,
would probably starve no one, and might cause relatively few deaths. (In
evaluating 'relatively few', remember, we sometimes have hundreds of
deaths during one heat wave in the U.S., and hundreds are killed here
over a holiday weekend by drunk drivers.)  And anyone who thinks that
Y2K might cause a disaster of the magnitude of WWII simply has no
comprehension of the scale of devastation caused by WWII.  If the black
plague and WWII did not cause civilization to collapse, where do we get
off saying that Y2K will do it?

In my opinion, we have far worse to fear from unreasoned fear and panic
than the actual consequences of Y2K.  For example, Y2K computer problems
*might* give the banks problems, but if everybody panics and tries to
withdraw all their money, it *will* crash the banking system!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
[...]
>
>In my opinion, we have far worse to fear from unreasoned fear and panic
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can tell you that not everyone is going to panic. I have enough
confidence in the minimal effect of Y2K that I intend to conduct
business as usual.

Banking Collapse versus Economic Collapse:

The problem with massive withdrawals isn't, so much, the
crash of the banking system as with the economic collapse
that would occur due to banking reserve requirements. I think
the current reserve requirement is around 4 percent; it may be
less. This means that banks may loan up to 96 percent of their
deposits.

Since these loans are redeposited into the same or different
banks, the bank receiving the deposit may loan up to 96 percent
of that deposit and so on. Looking at it another way, this means
that for each dollar removed from the banking system, the banks
must reduce their outstanding loans by 24 dollars. A massive
withdrawal of cash from the banking system will cause a
corresponding massive decrease in economic activity since
banks would not normally be able to write new loans until their
reserves come into compliance with the reserve requirement.
Alternatively, loans would become much more expensive.

It is this type of contraction in loan volume that led to a massive
decrease in money supply in the US and subsequently to the
world-wide economic collapse in the 1930's; even though
there was no reserve requirement at that time.

Having said that, both the US Federal Reserve and the US
Treasury Department have taken steps to prepare for the
worst. The Treasury has printed billions of dollars of cash to
replace any massive withdrawals from banks and the Federal
Reserve is prepared to reduce the reserve requirement and
interest rates to whatever level is required to prevent
economic collapse.

A direct result of these actions is that the banking system
will survive. It would require a prolonged economic downturn,
IMO lasting perhaps 5 to 10 years, before the banking system
would have serious problems. I just don't see that happening.

My guesstimates:

Well over 90 percent of all Y2K related problems will have
been resolved before Jan 1, 2000. Of the unresolved problems
(mostly unforseen), I would expect 90+ percent of problems to
be resolved within one month, 99+ percent within 6 months.
The most prolonged effects of Y2K (isolated problems like
individual companies collapsing) will be resolved within 1
to 2 years.

On Nostradamas:

Early in this thread, there was a comment concerning the
prophesies of Nostradamas. Of all the prophesies attributed
to Nostradamas, none, to my knowledge, were of major
economic collapse.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com>`

```
This is a correction and clarification (I hope).

Rick Smith wrote in message <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com>...
>
[...]
>
>Banking Collapse versus Economic Collapse:
>
[...]
>
>It is this type of contraction in loan volume that led to a massive
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It was the absence of reserve (and capital) requirements that
led to the closure of many banks in the US and to the subsequent
collapse of the banking system, contraction in loan volume, etc.

At that time, banks were lending a higher percentage of their
deposits than is currently permitted. This increased the negative
effects of "bank runs." For every dollar of deposits lost to the
banking system, the total of loans needed to be reduced by
100 or more dollars. Furthermore, the Federal Reserve, at that
time, had no procedures for taking control of a failing bank and
there was no deposit insurance. The absence of these
additional safeguards cause panic and hoarding of cash.

There are now, IMO, adequate safeguards to protect against
the conditions that had previously caused bank failures. There
are also safeguards against other conditions that might
potentially cause bank failures. Therefore, IMO, the possibility of
a banking collapse is, effectively, zero.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> At that time, banks were lending a higher percentage of their
> deposits than is currently permitted. This increased the negative
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> additional safeguards cause panic and hoarding of cash.

I'm wondering if I am seeing a bit of the "fighting the last war"
syndrome here.  We all are familiar with the Generals who study WWI to
come up with a defense which won't work in WWII.  Well, the changes in
our financial world since the depression are greater than the
differences in our military world!!  Or soon will be.

Money is becoming beyond liquid to gaseous!  It will soon be able to
find every pore and react instantly without human intervention.  Virtual
marketplaces will have agents bickering and making sales without humans
watching.  It is quite possible that such agents will see a criterion
and pull out most of the money in a whole country within minutes!

These scenarios have little to do with Y2K - but it is quite possible
that the DP industry's biggest REAL impacts will be seen in the next 20
years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** walt121@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76fmas$ghl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com>`

```
In article <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com>,
  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
> This is a correction and clarification (I hope).
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> collapse of the banking system, contraction in loan volume, etc.

It was only partly related to banking.	An anti-trade law, Smoot-Hawley, led
to retaliatory embargoes by many countries around the world.  A decade of
stock market speculation led to absurd valuations, which were built on very
limited margin requirements (similar to reserve requirements), which meant
that many people had to withdraw funds from banks to pay their stock losses.

While there was no mandatory, nationwide reserve system, there were state-run
reserve systems and private-run reserve systems.  The only thing that reserve
systems have proven is that in the long run, they all fail.

>
> At that time, banks were lending a higher percentage of their
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> additional safeguards cause panic and hoarding of cash.

And that's why we very nearly had a banking collapse in the late 1980s?
Remember the S&L crisis?  Banks were nearly as bad off.  They loaned billions
to third world oil economies and governments.  When these borrowers defaulted
or slowed payments, nearly all the big banks teetered on collapse.  Remember
Continental Illinois?  The Fed or the FDIC saved it, but if First Chicago had
gone much farther down, the rescue fund would have been empty.	And Citicorp,
Bank America, Chase, Crocker, First Interstate, Wells Fargo, and other large
banks were also very close to failing.

>
> There are now, IMO, adequate safeguards to protect against
> the conditions that had previously caused bank failures. There
> are also safeguards against other conditions that might
> potentially cause bank failures. Therefore, IMO, the possibility of
                                                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
> a banking collapse is, effectively, zero.
  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

All it takes is for serious problems to arise in several banks at once, and
the Federal Reserve, FDIC, and NCUA will all fold.

--Walt.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76fr4u$2u0_002@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76fmas$ghl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <76fmas$ghl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, walt121@my-dejanews.com wrote:
+In article <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com>,
+  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
+> This is a correction and clarification (I hope).
+>
+> Rick Smith wrote in message <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com>...
+> >
+> [...]
+> >
+> >Banking Collapse versus Economic Collapse:
+> >
+> [...]
+> >
+> >It is this type of contraction in loan volume that led to a massive
+> >decrease in money supply in the US and subsequently to the
+> >world-wide economic collapse in the 1930's; even though
+> >there was no reserve requirement at that time.
+> >
+> It was the absence of reserve (and capital) requirements that
+> led to the closure of many banks in the US and to the subsequent
+> collapse of the banking system, contraction in loan volume, etc.
+
+It was only partly related to banking.	An anti-trade law, Smoot-Hawley, led
+to retaliatory embargoes by many countries around the world.  A decade of
+stock market speculation led to absurd valuations, which were built on very
+limited margin requirements (similar to reserve requirements), which meant
+that many people had to withdraw funds from banks to pay their stock losses.
+
Not quite right. Rather than withdraw funds from banks, people sold other
stocks to cover their margin requirements, depressing the prices of those
stocks, causing margin calls to the holders of those stocks, causing the
holders to sell other stocks to cover *their* margins, depressing the
prices of *those* stocks, causing *more* margin calls ...

+While there was no mandatory, nationwide reserve system, there were state-run
+reserve systems and private-run reserve systems.  The only thing that reserve
+systems have proven is that in the long run, they all fail.
+
The 1929 crash and ensuing depression prove only that a poorly-designed 
reserve system failed. There is not sufficient evidence to make such broad 
statements as "they all fail."

+>
+> At that time, banks were lending a higher percentage of their
+> deposits than is currently permitted. This increased the negative
+> effects of "bank runs." For every dollar of deposits lost to the
+> banking system, the total of loans needed to be reduced by
+> 100 or more dollars. Furthermore, the Federal Reserve, at that
+> time, had no procedures for taking control of a failing bank and
+> there was no deposit insurance. The absence of these
+> additional safeguards cause panic and hoarding of cash.
+
+And that's why we very nearly had a banking collapse in the late 1980s?
+Remember the S&L crisis?  Banks were nearly as bad off.  They loaned billions
+to third world oil economies and governments.  When these borrowers defaulted
+or slowed payments, nearly all the big banks teetered on collapse.  Remember
+Continental Illinois?  The Fed or the FDIC saved it, but if First Chicago had
+gone much farther down, the rescue fund would have been empty.	And Citicorp,
+Bank America, Chase, Crocker, First Interstate, Wells Fargo, and other large
+banks were also very close to failing.
+
+>
+> There are now, IMO, adequate safeguards to protect against
+> the conditions that had previously caused bank failures. There
+> are also safeguards against other conditions that might
+> potentially cause bank failures. Therefore, IMO, the possibility of
+                                                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+> a banking collapse is, effectively, zero.
+  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+All it takes is for serious problems to arise in several banks at once, and
+the Federal Reserve, FDIC, and NCUA will all fold.
+
No, they won't fold. Because of the severity of the consequences, the U.S. 
Government will not allow that to happen. We'd see a severe contraction in the 
U.S. economy as the Government swallowed up massive amounts of money to prop 
up the banks -- not a pretty picture, but better than the alternative.

But this is getting rather far off-topic, I think.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76g542$c1$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76fmas$ghl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

walt121@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<76fmas$ghl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>In article <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com>,
>  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >
[...]
>
>It was only partly related to banking. An anti-trade law, Smoot-Hawley, led
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>that many people had to withdraw funds from banks to pay their stock
losses.

But, the money that was withdrawn from one bank was subsequently
deposited into another bank (or another account at the same bank);
therefore, no change in total bank deposits!

Although Smoot-Hawley had some effect, toward exporting the
economic problems of the US, another was the elimination of the
gold standard and the subsequent refusal of the US to export gold
owed to banks in other countries.

The other notable economic problem of the time was the drought in
the midwest. Banks suffered some large loan losses on foreclosed
farms.

[...]
>>
>> At that time, banks were lending a higher percentage of their
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Remember the S&L crisis?

Real estate speculation and excessively low premiums to FSLIC.
Subsequently bailed out by the Fed.

>  Banks were nearly as bad off.  They loaned billions
>to third world oil economies and governments.  When these borrowers
defaulted
>or slowed payments, nearly all the big banks teetered on collapse.
Remember
>Continental Illinois?  The Fed or the FDIC saved it, but if First Chicago
had
>gone much farther down, the rescue fund would have been empty. And
Citicorp,
>Bank America, Chase, Crocker, First Interstate, Wells Fargo, and other
large
>banks were also very close to failing.
>
[...]
>
>All it takes is for serious problems to arise in several banks at once, and
>the Federal Reserve, FDIC, and NCUA will all fold.
>
But, as you pointed out serious problems arose in several banks
at once and the Fed, etc. did not fold!

"What ever doesn't kill you will make you stronger." Author unknown.

IMO, the banking system is stronger today than it has ever been.

Going back to Judson's earlier reference to (the proverbial) everybody
removing their money at one time; if one were to apply the 80/20 rule,
what might that demonstrate?

One generalization is that 80 percent of deposits are owned by
20 percent of depositors. Who might those 20 percent be? I would
suggest they are mostly businesses and retirees (income from CDs)
who have no place else to put their money.

Of the 80 percent who might be able to remove their money, one
might, again, apply the 80/20 rule, as 80 percent are either not
excessively concerned or are unconcerned and 20 percent are
excessively concerned. (This may not be a correct application of
the rule; but it is done.) This means that 20 percent of 20 percent
of deposits are subject to being withdrawn in December 1999.
Four percent of deposits is a lot of money but it does not represent
a serious threat. This is particularly true since the money would be
re-deposited in January 2000, when the problems don't materialize.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
[snip]
> >Banking Collapse versus Economic Collapse:
[snip]
I just realized that my previous response could be misconstrued to sound like
I'm one of the hysterical people that fear total breakdown under Y2K.

Actually, I think that the problem will be largely transitory, with effects
mostly showing up in the records of banks, government agencies, and large
corporations.  I sincerely doubt that planes and trains will crash because of
it, or that cars won't run, or that electricity will shut down.

And no, I am not an expert on Y2K.

--Walt.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368a251a.17118104@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia>`

```
On Mon, 28 Dec 1998 14:54:38 GMT, "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:


>
>Naughty! Naughty!  I did not say "hurt hardware", but "cause a computer
>to explode", quite a difference! 

Well, I have interpreted as a "figure of speech", and have NOT taken it literally.  It
would really be in interesting problem to write software that make a computer explode (its
easier to make the end user explode...)

>I see two serious flaws in the 'thinking' of many doomsayers.  One is
>their apparent inability to use the 'common sense filters' I just
>described.  There is probably nothing we can do to help these people,
agreed
>But the most significant logic error made by the doomsayers is that
>they only see the potential problems, and do not consider the very
>significant mitigating  (rest snipped)

agreed, even if not in its entirety.  The end of WWII kept people in Europe (and
especially in Germany) in a "Must cope and can cope" state of mind.  This type of thinking
is (unfortunately) found only sparingly, and self healing mechanisms will be much more
difficult to find

>In my opinion, we have far worse to fear from unreasoned fear and panic
>than the actual consequences of Y2K.  For example, Y2K computer problems
>*might* give the banks problems, but if everybody panics and tries to
>withdraw all their money, it *will* crash the banking system!

Agreed - If there is a problem, certain doomsayers will make it worse. But - acting before
the problem occurs is always better than reacting to a problem, and that is where the
doomsayers have a reason of existing: They may point to problems previously unnoticed.

Please keep in mind that I say all of this from the perspective of my (German)
environment.   While the US started to work on Y2K analysis and remediation, the problem
was totally ignored in Germany (and large parts of Europe) until recently. A spokesman of
the german parliament just recently said:  "Well, we managed the transition from 4-digit
zip codes to 5-digit zip codes without too much of a problem, then this Y2K thing must be
a snap in comparison"  This gives me the creeps.  BTW, this Zip code conversion took the
better part of a year....

Also,  anonther problem is the transition to Euro - the common monetary unit in a few
European countries,  This has a much higher political weight and is viewed as much more
important (and toted to be much more complicated) than the Y2K problem(s). 

That's why I personnaly am not overly optimistic about what's going to happen about a year
from now.


But anyhow - it is nice e-talking with you

Have a very good and succe$$full New Year
  

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%Tni2.15930$Vg2.4131442@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <368a251a.17118104@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
>
>Please keep in mind that I say all of this from the perspective of my
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>BTW, this Zip code conversion took the better part of a year....

I can believe that attitude.  I know a local government bureaucrat who
stated only two or three months ago that "this Y2K thing is a hoax!"
Is it possible that Europe has fewer old programs than the US, making
the total problem a bit less there?

>Also, anonther problem is the transition to Euro - the common monetary
>unit in a few European countries,  This has a much higher political
>weight and is viewed as much more important (and toted to be much more
>complicated) than the Y2K problem(s).

That is one bit of insanity that I find absolutely amazing!  Having
worked in and around government for most of my career, I still find it
incredible how detached from reality the political mind usually is.
To them, reality *is* politics.  If it is politically expedient, then
it simply must be.  Politicians generally have absolutely no concept
about any practical considerations.  Even my MIS upper management
friends who must deal with politics every day have come to think that
way to some extent.  Y2K *cannot* be put off, and I suspect they will
come to sorely regret implementing the Euro simultaneously with Y2K.
One thing is for sure; if we don't fix the critical code at least,
there will be some serious problems.  I just do not believe it will
be, even at worst, remotely comparable to a disaster like WWII.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3298363.70075.20483@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
In message <<368a251a.17118104@news3.ibm.net>> vbandke@ibm.net  writes:

> doomsayers have a reason of existing: They may point to problems previously unnoticed.

Unlikely.  But the real problem with 'doomsayers' is that they
'point to' dozens of 'problems' which do not exist or are
irrelevant.  If there is a genuine issue in the masses of
uninformed rubbish it will be ignored along with the bulk
of the message.

Of course if that one issue does come to pass they will say
'I told you so' even when they are dead wrong about the
majority of their ramblings.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75obco$bi0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net>`

```
In article <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net>,
  joker@yahoo.com (Zoogs) wrote:
> This is an excerpt form
> http://www.amcity.com/baltimore/stories/1998/12/21/focus3.html
>
> Frautschi, a former Johns Hopkins University instructor

Instructor of what subject?  In academe, isn't an "instructor" just a
half-step above a graduate assistant?

> who serves on
> the President's Council on Year 2000 Conversions, warns that less than
> 1 percent of the 50 billion integrated chips used in computer systems
> around the world are Y2K-compliant

How many of those chips NEED to Y2k?  How many of them actually contain logic
that accesses a RTC and performs date comparisons?

> -- a scenario that could lead to
> disruptions ranging from massive power outages and food shortages to
> the inadvertent launching of nuclear weapons.

Food shortages?  Do the crops in the fields care about poor computer
programming?  Will the trucks that deliver the food fail to run?  Sure, some
highly automated inventory control systems at warehouses and supermarkets may
fail to place an order for next weeks brocolli, but inventory control people
in all industries work around this kind of problem all the time.  They don't
just sit on their hands because the computer failed to place an order, or
ordered too much too soon.

I'm quite certain that nuclear weapons systems do not contain any logic that
automates a launch, bypassing a double-key switch circuit as well as a bunch
of other safety features, just because a comparison of two dates went awry.

> Seated in front of his Y2K-compliant laptop computer, Mark Frautschi
> spoke with the Baltimore Business Journal about one of the biggest
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> notch worse is. Then prepare for one notch worse.

When in fear, when in doubt, run in circles -- scream and shout.

>
> BBJ: That sounds like a civil defense drill. What are some of the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> suffered a major power loss earlier this year due to ice storms].

So, that lengthy recovery was due to the physical destruction (power lines
brought down by ice) of infrastructure that had to be rebuilt.  Hardly
applicable to a simple logic problem tripping a relay.

> What
> you had in Quebec was that within 72 hours people were stealing
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> of how much we've invested ourselves in technology.

How are those reports credible? The author doesn't explain how a computer
program mis-calculating the difference between two dates will cause "major
food shortages."

> I think you can
> now operate a super tanker with a crew of about 10. It used to be more
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here, the author is finally close to having a valid point, but still
overlooks real world stuff.  We may have 10,000 PCs in our organization, but
even at that, how many of them are doing anything that matters if they are
Y2k or not? On my desktop, the terminal emulation programs I use don't care. 
My word processor doesn't care.  Some functions of my spreadsheet pgm care,
but I don't use those functions anyway.

Yes, Y2k is a problem.	Yes, we should have started working on it a lot
sooner. BUT --- it is not the end of civilization.  Not every computer or
computer program is going to fail on 01-JAN-2000.  Machinery is not going to
self- destruct.  An lot of computers are being used for things that don't
know or care what day it is.


Opinions are mine and do not necessarily reflect the postion of my employer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75p349$8jne$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net>`

```
As a COBOL programmer who also happens to live on a farm, it was important
to me to check the embedded chips in my neighbor's cows.  I'm happy to say
that I found no chips in those particular cows.  However, the milker may or
may not have electricity according to Mr. Frautschi, so I offered my
services to "hand" milk and also offered my daughter's horse to transport
the milk to our immediate neighbors. The rest of the world will have to find
their own milk.  I guess jumper cables don't exist at a higher level.

All joking aside, unfortunately, as I posted before, if enough people
believe the absolute worst case scenario, then indeed panic will rule.  As
most of the programmers on this newsgroup know, Windows 3.1, 3.11 and 95 are
not "technically" compliant.  If you create a file using notepad with your
PC date set in the year 2000, then view it using the 3.1 or 3.11 file
manager, it will display as ":0" instead of "00".  If you run "winfile.exe"
under Win95, same thing.  What people like Mr. Frautschi FAIL to tell you is
that the date still is kept internally as "2000", not ":0".  Aye, captain,
she's breakin' up now :~).
The panic mongers also FAIL to tell you that you can download patches from
http://support.microsoft.com/support/kb/articles/Q172/7/07.asp or
http://support.microsoft.com/support/kb/articles/q85/5/57.asp
to fix this minor inconvenience, if you so desire. Microsoft even added a
new version of the Win95 "command.com" to allow the old DOS non-believers a
"DIR /4" option to see the dates all nice and proper.

I, for one, have been working rather diligently to get the software under my
responsibility compliant, actually to the point of changing the data bases
involved to 8 digit dates.  No windowing for me. No siree.
Will some of the reports show "00" instead of "2000"? Maybe, but we're on
target to have EVERYTHING done by March 31, 1999, including these reports.
Actually, we're running ahead of that schedule. Some of these programs
contain 35,000-40,000 lines of code, but I'm only changing about .5 to 1% of
those MAX.  If you change the right pictures, the procedure code just
magically seems to work, although you still need to give it the once-over,
just to be sure.  I.E., change the REPORT-YEAR to PIC ZZZZ, redefine the
INPUT-CCYY from my redesigned data base as INPUT-YEAR PIC 9(4), and the
procedure code
" MOVE INPUT-YEAR TO REPORT-YEAR" works as it should have all along. GO
FIGURE!

Anyhow, I think I'll be quite annoyed if everyone believes the "former
instructor Mr. Frautschi " and not those of us actually doing the work.
This means the masses will put a run on the bank I work for and put it out
of business.  Guess I better start practicin' milkin', 'cause I'll be
unemployed.  Hmmm....let me ponder that a little deeper.......

Denny
```

#### ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75p7d8$jsr$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>`

```

ACHED wrote in message <36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>...
>What are some other ramifications?


1. Social Security will not go broke in 2018-2020. Here's why: There will
be a massive power failure on 1/1/2000. All the lights will go out. This
will
cause a huge increase in births nine months later. All these Y2K babies
will then enter the work force beginning in 2018 to provide a gargantuan
infusion of cash into the Social Security System, thereby enabling retirees
to continue getting their checks. It was set up this way by us old farts who
wrote the programs back in the '60s.

2. There is reason to believe the US will change to a "Flat Tax." Not
because
the idea is politically a good one, but simply because the IRS computers
won't work at all. The only easy way the gov't will have to collect money is
to say: "How much did you make? Multiply that by "x" and send it in."

3. Of the various solutions proposed, the one I like the best is the one
promulgated by Pope Gregory in 1358 (I think 1358) when he lopped
eleven days off the calendar. We could just as easily trim, oh, say, a
year or two.

4. One need not stock up on food, generators, medical supplies, gold,
clothing, fuel, spare parts, etc. If you think about it, a very large supply
of ammunition takes up considerably less space that all that stuff. See,
it's easy to be prepared.
```

#### ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

- **From:** joker@yahoo.com (Zoogs)
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3681c4c4.200413501@news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net>`

```
Sorry to do this to you again guys, but here is another excerpt. this
one from www.garynorth.com . I believe this tidbit is all it will take
to spark your interest enough to visit the site. 

We all know the logistical problems of y2k, but I say watch for this
to be THE news story of 1999. And watch every doomsayer be given full
credibility because it will make for some great ratings. The y2k
crisis will begin long before the turn of the century thanks to people
like this Gary North. Code all you want, we're going to have a major
meltdown I assure you, completely constructed and perpetuated by the
media. 

Here is the excerpt, this site is full of this kind of crap:

In the Category "Programmers' Views," he warns programmers that it may
soon be time to quit their big city jobs and head for safer places.
See the posting: Yourdon: Should Programmers Quit and Leave Town in
1999? If they do, there will be no solution for y2k. Will they quit?
I'm betting my life on it. The exodus of programmers will begin no
later than 1999.
```

## Thread: Printing in Landscape (win 98) — 4 msgs

- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gB9d2.106131$gI1.22855@news2.giganews.com>`

```
I am using a MicroFocus compiler on a Win 98 system, when printing a report
I would like it to come out in landscape, not portrait like it does by
default.
Could someone please let me know if this is possible.

Thanks
Jim

PS. I checked the FAQ and nothing came up.
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing in Landscape (win 98)

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-xWlV1B6Ygtrl@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<gB9d2.106131$gI1.22855@news2.giganews.com>`

```
On Mon, 14 Dec 1998 14:49:00, "Jim" <jimyt@REMOVEMEyahoo.com> wrote:

> I am using a MicroFocus compiler on a Win 98 system, when printing a report
> I would like it to come out in landscape, not portrait like it does by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PS. I checked the FAQ and nothing came up.

Yes it is possible.  What version of MF are you using?  See if in your
DOCS directory you have a file named PRINTER.DOC, or PRINT.DOC.  It 
will explain the windows based printing.  I'll see if I can post some 
examples on my website also.  (Won't be till later tonight)

-------------------------
Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
to the generic text printer using MF Windows calls.  Not Landscape -

- does have the examples.
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing in Landscape (win 98)

- **From:** <@usaor.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dCbd2.348$n4.185788@news.sgi.net>`
- **References:** `<gB9d2.106131$gI1.22855@news2.giganews.com>`

```
move x"1b266c304f" to print-rec. write print-rec.
             (Portrait)
move x"1b266c314f" to print-rec. write print-rec.
             (Lanscape)


Joe Hunter


Jim wrote in message ...
>I am using a MicroFocus compiler on a Win 98 system, when printing a report
>I would like it to come out in landscape, not portrait like it does by
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
