## Thread: Homework help again. — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Homework help again.

- **From:** "David Watson" <davidwatson@home.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xIkd2.273$EV6.54@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
First I would like to say thanks for all the help I have received from this
group. I plan in the next few weeks to open a web site that will help others
learn cobol..

Anyways I need Help One last time.. I know that some of my detail lines are
not included but That I can fix.   I am getting a File error that I need to
figure out..

The file error is ..
RCL0002: File status 41 on A:prog3.dat
         Error detected at offset 023F in segment 00 of program PROG4

Error 41 = An OPEN statement  is attempted for a file in the OPEN mode.

Here is my program. I am also going to re-read the chapter on sorting to see
if I can figure out what I need to do. My program is due Wed night and This
is stopping me..  Sorry for such a long file.. oh the data files are all
there... Sorry about the caps.. That is they way it is programmed.. I use
the cobol compiler realcob is it cobol-85
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
------------------

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.    PROGRAM4.
       AUTHOR.        DAVID WATSON.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT PAYROLL-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\PROG3.DAT'.
           SELECT TITLE-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\title.tab'.
           SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\prog4.rpt'.
           SELECT SORT-WORK-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\sortwk.dat'.
           SELECT SORTED-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\sortout.dat'.


       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  PAYROLL-FILE
            RECORD CONTAINS 38 CHARACTERS
            DATA RECORD IS EMPLOYEE-FILE.
       01  EMPLOYEE-FILE    PIC X(38).

       FD  PRINT-FILE
            RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS
            DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
       01  PRINT-LINE       PIC X(132).

       FD  TITLE-FILE.
       01  TITLE-IN         PIC X(16).

       SD  SORT-WORK-FILE
            RECORD CONTAINS 38 CHARACTERS
            DATA RECORD IS SORT-RECORD.
       01  SORT-RECORD.
            05 SORT-DEPT          PIC X(3).
            05 SORT-NAME          PIC X(17).
            05 FILLER             PIC X(9).
            05 SORT-TITLE         PIC 9(2).
            05 FILLER             PIC 9(7).

       FD  SORTED-FILE
            RECORD CONTAINS 38 CHARACTERS
            DATA RECORD IS SORTED-OUTPUT.
       01  SORTED-OUTPUT         PIC X(38).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DATEX            PIC 9(6).


       01  TITLE-TABLE.
           05 TITLES OCCURS 10 TIMES
             INDEXED BY TITLE-INDEX.
              10 TITLE-CODES  PIC 9(2).
              10 TITLE-NAME   PIC X(14).

       01  PROGRAM-SWITCH-WS.
            05 END-OF-TITLE-FILE  PIC X(3)  VALUE "NO".
            05 END-OF-FILE-SWITCH PIC X(3)  VALUE "NO".
            05 NUMBER-OF-TITLES   PIC 999   VALUE ZEROS.
            05 TOT-TAX            PIC 9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
            05 F1                 PIC 99    VALUE ZEROS.
            05 TOT-NET            PIC 9(5)v99.
            05 TOT-NET1           PIC 9(5)V99.
            05 PAGE-COUNT         PIC 9(2)  VALUE ZEROS.

       01  WS-PAYROLL-FILE.
            05 DEPT          PIC X(3).
            05 NAME          PIC X(17).
            05 SOC-SEC       PIC 9(9).
            05 TITLE-CODE    PIC 9(2).
            05 SALARY        PIC 9(5)V99.

       01  DEPT-VALUE.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "ACCACCOUNTING  00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "ACTARCHITECTURE00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "BIOBIOLOGY     00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "BNKBANKING     00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "BUSBUSINESS    00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "CISCOMPUTERS   00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "ENGENGLISH     00000000".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "MATMATH        00000000".

       01  DEPT-TABLE REDEFINES DEPT-VALUE.
            05 DEPTC OCCURS 8 TIMES
              INDEXED BY DEPT-INDEX.
             10 DEPT-CODE  PIC X(3).
             10 DEPT-DESC  PIC X(12).
             10 DEPT-AMT   PIC 9(6)V99.

       01  SALARY-TABLE.
            05 TAX-VALUE.
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "09000000110".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "06000000085".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "02000000065".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "01000000050".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00300000035".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00100000015".
             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00000000010".

           05 TAX-TABLE REDEFINES TAX-VALUE
               OCCURS 7 TIMES
               INDEXED BY TAX-INDEX.
                10 TAX-AMT    PIC 9(6)V99.
                10 TAX-CODE   PIC V999.

       01  HEADING1.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(26) VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "DATE".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 DATE-OUT      PIC 99B99B99.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(21) VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE "PAYROLL".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "LIST".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(25) VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "PAGE".
            05 TOT-PAGE-NUM  PIC Z9.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(29) VALUE SPACES.

       01  HEADING2.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "DEPT".
            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "DESCRIPTION".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "NAME".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(14) VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "SOCSEC".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE "TITLE".
            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "CODE".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE "TITLE".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(10)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "SALARY".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE "TAX".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE "NET".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE SPACES.


       01  DETAIL-LINE.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-DEPT-NAME   PIC X(16).
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-NAME        PIC X(16).
            05 FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-SS          PIC 999B99B9999.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-TIT-COD     PIC 9(2).
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-TITLE       PIC X(14).
            05 FILLER        PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-SAL         PIC $99,999.99.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-TAX         PIC $Z9,999.99.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 D-NET         PIC $99,999.99.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.


       01  TOTAL-HEAD.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(86) VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "TOTALS".
            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(2)  VALUE "BY".
            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE "DEPT:".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "DEPT".
            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "NAME".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "AMOUNT".
            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE  SPACES.

       01  TOTAL-DETAIL.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(98) VALUE SPACES.
            05 T-DEPT        PIC X(12).
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
            05 T-TOTALS      PIC $Z99,999.99.
            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.



       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       100-PROCESS-PAYROLL-LIST.
           PERFORM 150-INITIALIZE-TITLE-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT PAYROLL-FILE
           OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
           OPEN INPUT SORTED-FILE
                OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.
           PERFORM WRITE-HEADINGS.
           PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE-SWITCH = "YES"
               READ PAYROLL-FILE INTO WS-PAYROLL-FILE
                  AT END
                    MOVE "YES" TO END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
                  NOT AT END
                    PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BY-DEPT
                    PERFORM 203-PROCESS-BY-TITLE
                    PERFORM 207-PROCESS-BY-NAME
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           PERFORM 400-TOTALS.
           PERFORM PRINT-TABLE
               VARYING DEPT-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL DEPT-INDEX > 8.
           CLOSE PAYROLL-FILE
           CLOSE TITLE-FILE
                 PRINT-FILE.
           STOP RUN.

       150-INITIALIZE-TITLE-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
           PERFORM VARYING F1 FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL END-OF-TITLE-FILE = "YES"
                    OR F1 > 10
               READ TITLE-FILE INTO TITLES (F1)
                  AT END
                     MOVE "YES" TO END-OF-TITLE-FILE
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TITLE-FILE.

       160-SORT-BY-DEPT.
           SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
                ASCENDING KEY SORT-DEPT
                              SORT-NAME
                              SORT-TITLE
                WITH DUPLICATES IN ORDER
                USING PAYROLL-FILE
                GIVING SORTED-FILE.

       170-SORT-BY-TITLE.
           SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
                ASCENDING KEY SORT-TITLE
                              SORT-DEPT
                              SORT-NAME
                WITH DUPLICATES IN ORDER
                USING PAYROLL-FILE
                GIVING SORTED-FILE.


       180-SORT-BY-NAME.
           SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
           ASCENDING KEY SORT-NAME
                         SORT-TITLE
                         SORT-DEPT
           WITH DUPLICATES IN ORDER
           USING PAYROLL-FILE
           GIVING SORTED-FILE.

       200-PROCESS-BY-DEPT.
           PERFORM 160-SORT-BY-DEPT.
           PERFORM 220-EXPAND-DEPT.
           MOVE NAME TO D-NAME.
           MOVE SOC-SEC TO D-SS.
           INSPECT D-SS REPLACING ALL " " BY "-".
           PERFORM 210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
           PERFORM 230-TAXES.
           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE.

       203-PROCESS-BY-TITLE.
           PERFORM 170-SORT-BY-TITLE.
           PERFORM 220-EXPAND-DEPT.
           MOVE NAME TO D-NAME.
           MOVE SOC-SEC TO D-SS.
           INSPECT D-SS REPLACING ALL " " BY "-".
           PERFORM 210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
           PERFORM 230-TAXES.
           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE.

       207-PROCESS-BY-NAME.
           PERFORM 180-SORT-BY-NAME.
           PERFORM 220-EXPAND-DEPT.
           MOVE NAME TO D-NAME.
           MOVE SOC-SEC TO D-SS.
           INSPECT D-SS REPLACING ALL " " BY "-".
           PERFORM 210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
           PERFORM 230-TAXES.
           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE.

       210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
           SET TITLE-INDEX TO 1.
           SEARCH TITLES
               AT END
                   MOVE "IMPROPER TITLE" TO D-TITLE
               WHEN TITLE-CODE = TITLE-CODES (TITLE-INDEX)
               MOVE TITLE-CODE TO D-TIT-COD
               MOVE TITLE-NAME (TITLE-INDEX) TO D-TITLE
           END-SEARCH.


       220-EXPAND-DEPT.
           SET DEPT-INDEX TO 1.
           SEARCH DEPTC
               AT END
                    MOVE "TITLE NOT FOUND" TO D-DEPT-NAME
               WHEN DEPT = DEPT-CODE (DEPT-INDEX)
                    MOVE DEPT-DESC (DEPT-INDEX) TO D-DEPT-NAME
           END-SEARCH.

       230-TAXES.
           SET TAX-INDEX TO 1
               SEARCH TAX-TABLE
                  AT END
                     MOVE ZERO TO D-TAX
                  WHEN SALARY >= TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX)
                     MOVE SALARY TO D-SAL
                     COMPUTE TOT-TAX = SALARY * TAX-CODE
                      (TAX-INDEX)
                     SUBTRACT TOT-TAX FROM SALARY GIVING TOT-NET
                     MOVE TOT-NET TO TOT-NET1
                     MOVE TOT-NET1 TO D-NET
                     MOVE TOT-TAX TO D-TAX
                     ADD TOT-NET TO DEPT-AMT (DEPT-INDEX)
               END-SEARCH.

       PRINT-TABLE.
            MOVE DEPT-DESC (DEPT-INDEX) TO T-DEPT.
            MOVE DEPT-AMT (DEPT-INDEX) TO T-TOTALS.
            WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM TOTAL-DETAIL.


       WRITE-HEADINGS.
           ADD 1 TO PAGE-COUNT.
           MOVE PAGE-COUNT TO TOT-PAGE-NUM.
           ACCEPT DATEX FROM DATE.
           MOVE DATEX TO DATE-OUT.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING1
              AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING2
              AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE.


       400-TOTALS.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM TOTAL-HEAD
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM TOTAL-DETAIL
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help again.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<754mo8$mb7@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<xIkd2.273$EV6.54@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
This is an easy one.
  Don't open a file specified in either the USING or GIVING phrases of the
SORT statement; COBOL will open (and close) them for you.  (If you use an
INPUT or OUTPUT procedure, then you DO need to OPEN and CLOSE them
yourself.)

David Watson wrote in message ...
>First I would like to say thanks for all the help I have received from this
>group. I plan in the next few weeks to open a web site that will help
others
>learn cobol..
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Here is my program. I am also going to re-read the chapter on sorting to
see
>if I can figure out what I need to do. My program is due Wed night and This
>is stopping me..  Sorry for such a long file.. oh the data files are all
>there... Sorry about the caps.. That is they way it is programmed.. I use
>the cobol compiler realcob is it cobol-85
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
>------------------
>
…[354 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ RE: Homework help

- **From:** Duane Lee - ATCX <DLee@mail.maricopa.gov>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44C2247EAFF4D011B0BD00805F85B09C0ED5092C@newsfeed.maricopa.gov>`
- **References:** `<xIkd2.273$EV6.54@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
A cursory glance shows both PAYROLL-FILE and SORTED-FILE opened as input
then you drop into a perform where you try to do a SORT USING...
GIVING... with these same two files.  The SORT USING... GIVING... does
an implicit OPEN and CLOSE of the files and sine they are already open
you got trouble.

Duane

	-----Original Message-----
	From:	David Watson [SMTP:davidwatson@home.net]
	Posted At:	Monday, December 14, 1998 8:30 PM
	Posted To:	cobol
	Conversation:	Homework help again.
	Subject:	Homework help again.

	First I would like to say thanks for all the help I have
received from this
	group. I plan in the next few weeks to open a web site that will
help others
	learn cobol..

	Anyways I need Help One last time.. I know that some of my
detail lines are
	not included but That I can fix.   I am getting a File error
that I need to
	figure out..

	The file error is ..
	RCL0002: File status 41 on A:prog3.dat
	         Error detected at offset 023F in segment 00 of program
PROG4

	Error 41 = An OPEN statement  is attempted for a file in the
OPEN mode.

	Here is my program. I am also going to re-read the chapter on
sorting to see
	if I can figure out what I need to do. My program is due Wed
night and This
	is stopping me..  Sorry for such a long file.. oh the data files
are all
	there... Sorry about the caps.. That is they way it is
programmed.. I use
	the cobol compiler realcob is it cobol-85
	
------------------------------------------------------------------------
----
	------------------

	       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
	       PROGRAM-ID.    PROGRAM4.
	       AUTHOR.        DAVID WATSON.

	       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
	       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
	       FILE-CONTROL.
	           SELECT PAYROLL-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\PROG3.DAT'.
	           SELECT TITLE-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\title.tab'.
	           SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\prog4.rpt'.
	           SELECT SORT-WORK-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\sortwk.dat'.
	           SELECT SORTED-FILE ASSIGN TO 'a:\sortout.dat'.


	       DATA DIVISION.
	       FILE SECTION.
	       FD  PAYROLL-FILE
	            RECORD CONTAINS 38 CHARACTERS
	            DATA RECORD IS EMPLOYEE-FILE.
	       01  EMPLOYEE-FILE    PIC X(38).

	       FD  PRINT-FILE
	            RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS
	            DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
	       01  PRINT-LINE       PIC X(132).

	       FD  TITLE-FILE.
	       01  TITLE-IN         PIC X(16).

	       SD  SORT-WORK-FILE
	            RECORD CONTAINS 38 CHARACTERS
	            DATA RECORD IS SORT-RECORD.
	       01  SORT-RECORD.
	            05 SORT-DEPT          PIC X(3).
	            05 SORT-NAME          PIC X(17).
	            05 FILLER             PIC X(9).
	            05 SORT-TITLE         PIC 9(2).
	            05 FILLER             PIC 9(7).

	       FD  SORTED-FILE
	            RECORD CONTAINS 38 CHARACTERS
	            DATA RECORD IS SORTED-OUTPUT.
	       01  SORTED-OUTPUT         PIC X(38).

	       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
	       01  DATEX            PIC 9(6).


	       01  TITLE-TABLE.
	           05 TITLES OCCURS 10 TIMES
	             INDEXED BY TITLE-INDEX.
	              10 TITLE-CODES  PIC 9(2).
	              10 TITLE-NAME   PIC X(14).

	       01  PROGRAM-SWITCH-WS.
	            05 END-OF-TITLE-FILE  PIC X(3)  VALUE "NO".
	            05 END-OF-FILE-SWITCH PIC X(3)  VALUE "NO".
	            05 NUMBER-OF-TITLES   PIC 999   VALUE ZEROS.
	            05 TOT-TAX            PIC 9(5)V99  VALUE ZEROS.
	            05 F1                 PIC 99    VALUE ZEROS.
	            05 TOT-NET            PIC 9(5)v99.
	            05 TOT-NET1           PIC 9(5)V99.
	            05 PAGE-COUNT         PIC 9(2)  VALUE ZEROS.

	       01  WS-PAYROLL-FILE.
	            05 DEPT          PIC X(3).
	            05 NAME          PIC X(17).
	            05 SOC-SEC       PIC 9(9).
	            05 TITLE-CODE    PIC 9(2).
	            05 SALARY        PIC 9(5)V99.

	       01  DEPT-VALUE.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "ACCACCOUNTING
00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE
"ACTARCHITECTURE00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "BIOBIOLOGY
00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "BNKBANKING
00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "BUSBUSINESS
00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "CISCOMPUTERS
00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "ENGENGLISH
00000000".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE "MATMATH
00000000".

	       01  DEPT-TABLE REDEFINES DEPT-VALUE.
	            05 DEPTC OCCURS 8 TIMES
	              INDEXED BY DEPT-INDEX.
	             10 DEPT-CODE  PIC X(3).
	             10 DEPT-DESC  PIC X(12).
	             10 DEPT-AMT   PIC 9(6)V99.

	       01  SALARY-TABLE.
	            05 TAX-VALUE.
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "09000000110".
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "06000000085".
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "02000000065".
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "01000000050".
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00300000035".
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00100000015".
	             10 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "00000000010".

	           05 TAX-TABLE REDEFINES TAX-VALUE
	               OCCURS 7 TIMES
	               INDEXED BY TAX-INDEX.
	                10 TAX-AMT    PIC 9(6)V99.
	                10 TAX-CODE   PIC V999.

	       01  HEADING1.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(26) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "DATE".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 DATE-OUT      PIC 99B99B99.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(21) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE "PAYROLL".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "LIST".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(25) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "PAGE".
	            05 TOT-PAGE-NUM  PIC Z9.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(29) VALUE SPACES.

	       01  HEADING2.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "DEPT".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(11) VALUE "DESCRIPTION".

	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "NAME".

	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "SOCSEC".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE "TITLE".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "CODE".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE "TITLE".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(10)  VALUE SPACES.

	            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE "TAX".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.

	            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE SPACES.


	       01  DETAIL-LINE.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-DEPT-NAME   PIC X(16).
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-NAME        PIC X(16).
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-SS          PIC 999B99B9999.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-TIT-COD     PIC 9(2).
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-TITLE       PIC X(14).
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-SAL         PIC $99,999.99.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-TAX         PIC $Z9,999.99.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 D-NET         PIC $99,999.99.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.


	       01  TOTAL-HEAD.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(86) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "TOTALS".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(2)  VALUE "BY".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(5)  VALUE "DEPT:".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "DEPT".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)  VALUE "NAME".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(7)  VALUE SPACES.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE "AMOUNT".
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(6)  VALUE  SPACES.

	       01  TOTAL-DETAIL.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(98) VALUE SPACES.
	            05 T-DEPT        PIC X(12).
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
	            05 T-TOTALS      PIC $Z99,999.99.
	            05 FILLER        PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.



	       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

	       100-PROCESS-PAYROLL-LIST.
	           PERFORM 150-INITIALIZE-TITLE-FILE.
	           OPEN INPUT PAYROLL-FILE
	           OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
	           OPEN INPUT SORTED-FILE
	                OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.
	           PERFORM WRITE-HEADINGS.
	           PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE-SWITCH = "YES"
	               READ PAYROLL-FILE INTO WS-PAYROLL-FILE
	                  AT END
	                    MOVE "YES" TO END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
	                  NOT AT END
	                    PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BY-DEPT
	                    PERFORM 203-PROCESS-BY-TITLE
	                    PERFORM 207-PROCESS-BY-NAME
	               END-READ
	           END-PERFORM.
	           PERFORM 400-TOTALS.
	           PERFORM PRINT-TABLE
	               VARYING DEPT-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
	               UNTIL DEPT-INDEX > 8.
	           CLOSE PAYROLL-FILE
	           CLOSE TITLE-FILE
	                 PRINT-FILE.
	           STOP RUN.

	       150-INITIALIZE-TITLE-FILE.
	           OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
	           PERFORM VARYING F1 FROM 1 BY 1
	               UNTIL END-OF-TITLE-FILE = "YES"
	                    OR F1 > 10
	               READ TITLE-FILE INTO TITLES (F1)
	                  AT END
	                     MOVE "YES" TO END-OF-TITLE-FILE
	               END-READ
	           END-PERFORM.
	           CLOSE TITLE-FILE.

	       160-SORT-BY-DEPT.
	           SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
	                ASCENDING KEY SORT-DEPT
	                              SORT-NAME
	                              SORT-TITLE
	                WITH DUPLICATES IN ORDER
	                USING PAYROLL-FILE
	                GIVING SORTED-FILE.

	       170-SORT-BY-TITLE.
	           SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
	                ASCENDING KEY SORT-TITLE
	                              SORT-DEPT
	                              SORT-NAME
	                WITH DUPLICATES IN ORDER
	                USING PAYROLL-FILE
	                GIVING SORTED-FILE.


	       180-SORT-BY-NAME.
	           SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
	           ASCENDING KEY SORT-NAME
	                         SORT-TITLE
	                         SORT-DEPT
	           WITH DUPLICATES IN ORDER
	           USING PAYROLL-FILE
	           GIVING SORTED-FILE.

	       200-PROCESS-BY-DEPT.
	           PERFORM 160-SORT-BY-DEPT.
	           PERFORM 220-EXPAND-DEPT.
	           MOVE NAME TO D-NAME.
	           MOVE SOC-SEC TO D-SS.
	           INSPECT D-SS REPLACING ALL " " BY "-".
	           PERFORM 210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
	           PERFORM 230-TAXES.
	           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
	           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE.

	       203-PROCESS-BY-TITLE.
	           PERFORM 170-SORT-BY-TITLE.
	           PERFORM 220-EXPAND-DEPT.
	           MOVE NAME TO D-NAME.
	           MOVE SOC-SEC TO D-SS.
	           INSPECT D-SS REPLACING ALL " " BY "-".
	           PERFORM 210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
	           PERFORM 230-TAXES.
	           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
	           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE.

	       207-PROCESS-BY-NAME.
	           PERFORM 180-SORT-BY-NAME.
	           PERFORM 220-EXPAND-DEPT.
	           MOVE NAME TO D-NAME.
	           MOVE SOC-SEC TO D-SS.
	           INSPECT D-SS REPLACING ALL " " BY "-".
	           PERFORM 210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
	           PERFORM 230-TAXES.
	           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
	           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE.

	       210-EXPAND-TITLE-CODE.
	           SET TITLE-INDEX TO 1.
	           SEARCH TITLES
	               AT END
	                   MOVE "IMPROPER TITLE" TO D-TITLE
	               WHEN TITLE-CODE = TITLE-CODES (TITLE-INDEX)
	               MOVE TITLE-CODE TO D-TIT-COD
	               MOVE TITLE-NAME (TITLE-INDEX) TO D-TITLE
	           END-SEARCH.


	       220-EXPAND-DEPT.
	           SET DEPT-INDEX TO 1.
	           SEARCH DEPTC
	               AT END
	                    MOVE "TITLE NOT FOUND" TO D-DEPT-NAME
	               WHEN DEPT = DEPT-CODE (DEPT-INDEX)
	                    MOVE DEPT-DESC (DEPT-INDEX) TO D-DEPT-NAME
	           END-SEARCH.

	       230-TAXES.
	           SET TAX-INDEX TO 1
	               SEARCH TAX-TABLE
	                  AT END
	                     MOVE ZERO TO D-TAX
	                  WHEN SALARY >= TAX-AMT (TAX-INDEX)
	                     MOVE SALARY TO D-SAL
	                     COMPUTE TOT-TAX = SALARY * TAX-CODE
	                      (TAX-INDEX)
	                     SUBTRACT TOT-TAX FROM SALARY GIVING TOT-NET
	                     MOVE TOT-NET TO TOT-NET1
	                     MOVE TOT-NET1 TO D-NET
	                     MOVE TOT-TAX TO D-TAX
	                     ADD TOT-NET TO DEPT-AMT (DEPT-INDEX)
	               END-SEARCH.

	       PRINT-TABLE.
	            MOVE DEPT-DESC (DEPT-INDEX) TO T-DEPT.
	            MOVE DEPT-AMT (DEPT-INDEX) TO T-TOTALS.
	            WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM TOTAL-DETAIL.


	       WRITE-HEADINGS.
	           ADD 1 TO PAGE-COUNT.
	           MOVE PAGE-COUNT TO TOT-PAGE-NUM.
	           ACCEPT DATEX FROM DATE.
	           MOVE DATEX TO DATE-OUT.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING1
	              AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING2
	              AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
	           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE.


	       400-TOTALS.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM TOTAL-HEAD
	               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
	           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM TOTAL-DETAIL
	               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help again.

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u#knCw#J#GA.222@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<xIkd2.273$EV6.54@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
In addition to what Bill Klein said on the subject of file opens and closes,
if you have opened a file for some reason in processing before a
USING/GIVING sort, where that file will be input to the sort, you must close
the file _before_ the sort is executed.

I also note that _all_ your files appear to be assigned to a floppy disk
drive (a:). Files on a floppy disk cannot be opened simultaneously.

I don't know the details of your homework assignment, but it appears to me
that the logic in your program won't accomplish whatever it is that you are
intending to do without some significant rework.  Good luck on getting it
done by Wednesday.

David Watson wrote in message ...
>First I would like to say thanks for all the help I have received from this
>group. I plan in the next few weeks to open a web site that will help
others
>learn cobol..
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Here is my program. I am also going to re-read the chapter on sorting to
see
>if I can figure out what I need to do. My program is due Wed night and This
>is stopping me..  Sorry for such a long file.. oh the data files are all
>there... Sorry about the caps.. That is they way it is programmed.. I use
>the cobol compiler realcob is it cobol-85
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
>------------------
>
…[350 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-a6f6c625b2"></a>
## Thread: Line Sequential problems — 3 msgs

### Line Sequential problems

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36752104.1235165@news.enteract.com>`

```
Hello, 

I was wondering if someone could point me in the right directrion on
this.

We have a program in Microfocus 2 Cobol that would read a Line
sequential file with TAB delimited input, and would then write some
data into another Line sequential file, imbedding modem charactes into
the output.  Eveything worked fine until......

The problem is that we now compile the program under Microfocus 3
cobol, and it messes up the Line sequential input and output.  On
input, it converts each TAB character to spaces.  On output, it
prefixes every non-alphaneumiric character with a binary zero.
This really messes things up.

I suspect that it has something to do with MF 3 handling international
character sets that use 2 bytes instead of one.  I tried to find how
to turn this off, but haven't had any luck.

Thanks for your time,

Paul
--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: Line Sequential problems

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KP9d2.750$qt.404049@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<36752104.1235165@news.enteract.com>`

```
Try setting the environment variable COBSW=-N.

Look up Line Sequential Organization in the 'File Formats' appendix of the
COBOL System Reference for more information.


PAL3000 wrote in message <36752104.1235165@news.enteract.com>...
>Hello,
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Line Sequential problems

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3675465a.10794295@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<36752104.1235165@news.enteract.com> <KP9d2.750$qt.404049@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
Karl,

Thanks for the info.  The "-N' COBSW is exactly what I was looking
for!  

I wonder why MF changed the way sequential files worked for version 3?
My guess it was for a new standard to deal with non alphanumeric
characters in Line Sequential files.  It sure caused me a lot of
headaches.

I like what they say about Line Sequential in the "File Formats"
section:
"We recommend that you ensure that line sequential records contain
only printable characters in the range hex 20 to hex 7A......."

It was our fault, in that we should have been using Binary, or Record
Sequential format in the first place.

Thanks again!

Paul

On Mon, 14 Dec 1998 15:06:18 GMT, "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
wrote:

>Try setting the environment variable COBSW=-N.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

## Thread: 2 semesters of Cobol: job prospects? — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### 2 semesters of Cobol: job prospects?

- **From:** niemeyer@iastate.edu ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<755pb2$2bq$1@news.iastate.edu>`

```


Hello,

If I were to take one year's worth of Cobol classes at a community
college (amounting to two semester's worth of class) would I be able to
land a nice-paying job with a company somewhere?  I mean, just on the
strength of those two classes?  Let's just assume that I make an A both
semesters.  

I'm only wondering about this because I'm reading more and more doomsday
scenarios about Y2K and how there are not enough programmers, etc.  If
the situation is really that bad with billions of lines of code that
need to be debugged I'm just wondering how marketable 2 semesters worth
of Cobol would be.

Any thoughts on this would be appreciated.

Thanks,

Chris N.
```

```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of Cobol would be.

Just make sure that your not going into the field because of the abundant 
number of jobs due to the Y2K problem. I would suggest that you take the 
classes and see if you enjoy the work before you decide to make become a 
"professional" programmer. 

Most of the members of this or any other programing new group enjoy 
working with computers and programming and they do this all of the time. 
I would dare say that "we" would be programming even if we were not paid 
to do this. That might be a stretch but most of us enjoy programming and 
if you enjoy programming then it would be a good career choice.

#### ↳ Re: 2 semesters of Cobol: job prospects?

- **From:** carloss940@aol.com (CarlosS940)
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981217024359.08009.00000020@ng120.aol.com>`

```
I believe that it is much better for you to learn C++ and the demand for C++
programmer will  stay over many years.  In addition, if you are interested in
web programming, then, you should also learn Perl, HTML and Unix.  By having
all these skills, you should be able to get a programming job pretty easier. 
You should be able to complete all those courses in about a year.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 2 semesters of Cobol: job prospects?

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36794bbb.781283115@news.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<755pb2$2bq$1@news.iastate.edu> <19981217024359.08009.00000020@ng120.aol.com>`

```
On 17 Dec 1998 07:43:59 GMT, carloss940@aol.com (CarlosS940) wrote:

>I believe that it is much better for you to learn C++ and the demand for C++
>programmer will  stay over many years.  In addition, if you are interested in
>web programming, then, you should also learn Perl, HTML and Unix.  By having
>all these skills, you should be able to get a programming job pretty easier. 
>You should be able to complete all those courses in about a year.

I disagree, but only about the values of one language over another. Once you
learn how to *think* like a programmer languages are easy to switch.
Understanding OOP is far more important than learning C++.

Also, as a side note I've noticed that a lot of people seem to be considering
moving from C++ to Java. Java now performs close enough to C++ for the
differences to be negligible and the language is a lot easier on the programmer.
OTOH, there are COBOL programmers who've been working in the same language for
many years.

# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
```

#### ↳ Re: 2 semesters of Cobol: job prospects?

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367a4dcb.11184981@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<755pb2$2bq$1@news.iastate.edu>`

```
On 15 Dec 1998 13:48:18 GMT, niemeyer@iastate.edu () wrote:

>If I were to take one year's worth of Cobol classes at a community
>college (amounting to two semester's worth of class) would I be able to
>land a nice-paying job with a company somewhere?  I mean, just on the
>strength of those two classes?  Let's just assume that I make an A both
>semesters.  

>I'm only wondering about this because I'm reading more and more doomsday
>scenarios about Y2K and how there are not enough programmers, etc.  If
>the situation is really that bad with billions of lines of code that
>need to be debugged I'm just wondering how marketable 2 semesters worth
>of Cobol would be.

Here's the deal. if you have no programming experience, go to burger
king. if you know about variables, keys, methods for finding possible
problems (such as moves with y2k vars), byte expansion, and OSes, and
just need exp in COBOL syntax, go for it. If you have to ask, then the
answer is you are a recruiter.

If you don't know OS'es, but know compiler spec OS functions (or have
code to study), that is good enough.

If you can read tech documents, you might want to be a hardware
consultant instead.

If you have a screwdriver, you can find can examine machines and specs
on lost machines

If you know C, and assembly, you get to take rewrite old lost code
from the bottom up.

If you know hex editors you might be able to re-diagram data files. (I
wouldn't though, as one file might affect another)

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

I have asked Fujitsu the same question twice.

The first time was some months ago when we first received version 4.0 and I
was told that the ODBC driver for use with Crystal Reports should be ready
by about Dec '98 and got the impression it was being developed by Fujitsu
themselves.

When I enquired again in late November I was told -

"Crystal Reports access to Fujitsu COBOL files is still be developed. The
support is directly to Crystal Reports. We are not building an ODBC
interface.

The current plans call for the tools to be release in February/March."

Regards, Robert Kovacic.

<a id="t-d44ed6dc8a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL- Tables help. — 3 msgs

### COBOL- Tables help.

- **From:** "Steve Reid" <asreid@lineone.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Prd2.2530$Uh5.62@news-reader.bt.net>`

```
I'm a student trying to get my head around writing tables. At present I'm
writing a programme to produce a table of student grades. First in Working
Storage to hold a count of the number of students for each of the four
grades(Distinction, Credit, Pass and Fail) within each exam paper (3). Max
of 99 students.  Then I have to produce a complete Procedure Division to
read each record in turn using Paper and Mark fields from incoming data to
add 1 to the relevant counter in the table.

Any help on this subject would be appreciated.

Steve.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL- Tables help.

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7564lm$3go$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<0Prd2.2530$Uh5.62@news-reader.bt.net>`

```
In article <0Prd2.2530$Uh5.62@news-reader.bt.net>,
Steve Reid <asreid@lineone.net> wrote:

[snippage of table-handling assignment]

>Any help on this subject would be appreciated.

The help offered will depend on the work already done... please either
post the code to show how far you've already gotten or do your own
homework..

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL- Tables help.

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75b5p7$nkr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<0Prd2.2530$Uh5.62@news-reader.bt.net>`

```
I won't code your solution, but think about this:

Visualize a spreadsheet, with each row representing an exam and each column a
grade.	Now, when you code your table in working storage, each row is a
single occurance of the group field to which you apply your OCCURS clause. 
Within that group item that OCCURS n times, each column of your spreadsheet
is a field - one field for each possible grade.

In procedure, when you read a record, if the PAPER (exam?) field is numeric,
use that field as the subscript value to point to a row of the table and add
1 to the appropriate field/column.  How you determine the appropriate field
to increment will depend on the nature of the data in your MARK field on the
input file.
```

<a id="t-160353c636"></a>
## Thread: CobolTransformer 3.2 Announce — 2 msgs

### CobolTransformer 3.2 Announce

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36773BFD.C04723C2@siber.com>`

```
Siber Systems is pleased to announce availability of 
ver. 3.2 of CobolTransformer Library, Tools and Converters.

CobolTransformer is a complete toolkit that makes
developing Cobol program analyzers and converters
an easy and satisfying job. 

If you need to automate analysis and/or conversion 
of your legacy Cobol code and you want the automated 
solution to be delivered fast and have high quality, 
then it is time to get a CobolTransformer license.

To get more information, please direct your browser
to http://www.siber.com/sct/

Starting with ver. 3.2 CobolTransformer is not just
one product but a family of products that consists of:

- CobolTransformer Library. This is the base library
on which other tools are built. Licensable to 3rd parties.

- Cobol Tools: General purpose tools that use CobolTransformer
Library: CobolBeautifier, CobolGrep, CobolReporter.

- Cobol Converters: Cobol dialect converters and Y2K tools
that address specific Cobol conversion paths.


Major New features in CobolTransformer 3.2
------------------------------------------

* CobolGrep tool and Query Language (QL) used in it.

We invented CobolGrep and Query Language (QL) so that 
Cobol developers can perform program searches such as
"Show me all the lines of code that contain data item 
declarations that have VALUE clause and belong 
to LINKAGE SECTION or FILE SECTION".

The above search looks as follows in Query Language:
 E.OP==DECL_DD_ENTRY AND 
(E.ISDESCOF(SECTION_WORKING_STORAGE) OR 
 E.ISDESCOF(SECTION_LINKAGE)) AND
 E.ARG(DD_VALUE) != NULL

It means: "Find a node in Cobol Program Tree such that
this node is data item declaration and
this node is a descendant of Working-Storage Section node
or of Linkage Section node and this node has Value Clause node".

QL is a powerful SQL-like language.
Using QL you can write both simple and complex queries
that search your Cobol sources for Cobol constructs that 
you need to find.


* Data Item Length And Offset computation.

Now for every data item we compute:
- its length,
- its offset from 01 level start,
- its offset from the surrounding section start.

This data is necessary for figuring out
data item aliasing information.

* CobolReporter tool.

Reports lengths and offsets of every data item.
Also generates a wealth of other useful information
about your Cobol program.


* Performed usability analysis of command line tools
and reworked the set of available command line options.
Allow multiple files in converters.

* Use Kenonic Controls CrypKey License Manager software 
to enforce CobolTransformer licensing agreements.

* CobolTransformer Library manual is now distributed
for free to prospective licensees.

* Developed formal manual for mf2fsc and ibm2fsc converters.

* Converted all manuals to Adobe PDF format.

* Implemented Micro Focus $IF/$ELSE/$ENDIF/$SET preprocessor 
directives to further enhance MF Cobol parser.

Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
```

#### ↳ Re: CobolTransformer 3.2 Announce

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<3677DB8B.648BD825@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<36773BFD.C04723C2@siber.com>`

```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Please point your browsers to 
http://www.siber.org/sct/ meanwhile.

It is an exact mirror of www.siber.com.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

## Thread: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS? — 4 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1998-11](1998-11.md#t-99681098de)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7557se$nmh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org> <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <72oms2$s8d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> Combining the two is VERY "doable" - but does require some care (and
> certainly you need to make certain that you were using ALL "res"
> applications with VS COBOL II - but that was supposed to be done any way).

<snip the rest>

I asked for a PMR at IBM Switzerland about the difference between the content
of the CICS manuals and the COBOL manuals. Under PMR number 08129, I got the
following answer from the CICS laboratory:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
It appears that both manuals are correct but could possible include more text to
make the point about run units.

I think the key word in the text is 'run unit' and the distinction between
CALLs and EXEC CICS LINK. The CICS Application Programming Guide for CICS TS
1.2 also indicates that a CICS transaction consisting of many run units can
use the CICS LINK or XCTL commands to call other programs compiled by
different compilers (including non-COBOL compilers). This is documented right
after the section of the manual that you referenced ('... mixture of levels
in a RUN UNIT...'). A run unit being a set of programs that communicate with
each other by COBOL static or dynamic CALL statements.

In the COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming Guide after the bullet you quoted
there is another bullet containing the following: "COBOL for OS/390 & VM,
COBOL for MVS & VM, and VS COBOL II programs cannot call or be called by
OS/VS COBOL programs. EXEC CICS LINK must be used instead."

This sort of agrees with the CICS documentation that restricts run units to
programs compiled with the same compiler. However, the COBOL Programming Guide
does narrow it down to just OS/VS COBOL programs. This could use a little
clarification.

Please submit a "Reader's Comments' form for both manuals to get a better
description in both manuals and to ensure both books are in agreement.

But to answer your question, if you are using COBOL CALL statements, the
program being called should be either Assembler language routines or COBOL
programs compiled by the same compiler. If you use a CICS LINK or XCTL
command for program navigation, the compiler level does not matter.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
---

Conclusion:

The comments of Mr Klein and Mr Rayhawk to this matter where correctly and the
documentation really needs some clarification. I will do that as soon as I
can. And Sterling Software Inc. should stop now to demand their customers
to recompile all their generated and self-written COBOL sources.

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3676C735.980C3EBC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org> <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <72oms2$s8d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7557se$nmh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <755kop$fb5@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I will follow up with my COBOL sources to get CICS to STOP saying (as they
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the same compilers (which they aren't)

We have COBOL programs with various ANSI versions.  Part of the Y2K
process was to first convert all common called programs to COBOL for
MVS.  We never cared whether the old code was ANSI 68 or ANSI 72 when
calling old programs.  Never had any problems arise because we were
using different compilers.  We had to add a line to the all JCL when we
made these changes so that old jobs would find the called program's
required libraries, but that was it.
```

<a id="t-35174dc0f7"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu editor — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu editor

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75639t$b9c@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Over the last few months there have been several threads about what you can
and cannot do with the Fujitsu V4 editor.  I think the following note that I
received from their support may help a bit.

"The editor of COBOL V4 has two modes for the search command. One is the V3
mode, the other is the V4 mode. The V4 mode can search for dashes, asterisks
and spaces without tabs.

If you had installed COBOL V3 into your PC before you installed V4, the
search command is the V3 mode. Of course, you can exchange the V3 mode to
the V4 by the following command.
 [Options]menu of Editor
 -> [Set Enviroment for Editor (Basic Status)]command
 -> [Use Find/Replace Commands Of PowerGEM Plus V3 Version] option

When you install COBOL V4 into PC at the first time, the search command is
the V4 mode.

The editor and the builder of COBOL are components of the PowerGEM Plus."
```

## Thread: Records of variable length... — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Records of variable length...

- **From:** Quan Ngo <le_spaceboy@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36786EDC.7946629A@usa.net>`

```
Hi, I don't know how to read a file with records of variable length.
I'm supposed to read a sequential file and create an indexed file.
Here is a sample of the data file:

8012435STEVENS TERRY            M4200041200078120205612080761220078
8014325WAGNER PETER             M4200041202076120706712200551225089
8021534RICHARDSON EMILE         M30703712000681209081
8023879RANCOURT FRED            M20200212070781215089
8028765WAGNER THERESA           F2020011207078

This is how I declared my file section...!

 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD STUDENT-FILE-IN     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 STUDENT-REC-IN.
    05 ID-IN                        PIC X(7).
    05 NAME-IN                      PIC X(25).
    05 GENDER-IN                    PIC X.
    05 PROGRAM-CODE-IN              PIC X(5).
    05 NUM-COURSE-IN                PIC X.
    05 COURSE-IN.
       10 SECTION-NO-IN             PIC X(4).
       10 FINAL-MARK-IN             PIC 9(3).
 FD STUDENT-FILE-OUT    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 STUDENT-REC-OUT.
    05 INDX-ID-OUT                  PIC X(4).
    05 INDX-NAME-OUT                PIC X(6).
    05 INDX-GENDER-IN               PIC X.
    05 INDX-PROGRAM-CODE-IN         PIC X(5).
    05 INDX-NUM-COURSE-IN           PIC X.
    05 INDX-COURSE-IN.
       10 INDX-SECTION-NO-IN        PIC X(4).
       10 INDX-FINAL-MARK-IN        PIC 9(3).
 ...

How do I read in the courses once I know how many courses there
are...???
All I have so far is this...

 100-MAIN.
     OPEN INPUT STUDENT-FILE-IN
          OUTPUT STUDENT-FILE-OUT
     PERFORM UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS
         READ STUDENT-FILE-IN
             AT END
                 MOVE 'N' TO MORE-RECORDS
             NOT AT END
                 PERFORM 200-CREATE-RTN
         END-READ
     END-PERFORM
     PERFORM 300-END-OF-JOB-RTN
     STOP RUN.



One other problem...
I'm using the Fujitsu Cobol Compiler.  Whenever I read from a file
for example:

111111111111
222222222222
333333333333
444444444444

it doesn't seem to know when is the end of record so that I have to
modify
my file like this:

111111111111222222222222333333333333444444444444

so that it reads in each record like it's supposed to...
anyone have an explanation to this???

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Records of variable length...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<759oah$nuj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36786EDC.7946629A@usa.net>`

```
If you make your file line sequential in the select clause
then you will get one record per line.  Make sure your record
size is big enough for the longest line.  Shorter records
will be blank filled on read.

Quan Ngo wrote in message <36786EDC.7946629A@usa.net>...
>Hi, I don't know how to read a file with records of variable length.
>I'm supposed to read a sequential file and create an indexed file.
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Records of variable length...

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981216221245.06813.00003172@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36786EDC.7946629A@usa.net>`

```

In article <36786EDC.7946629A@usa.net>, Quan Ngo <le_spaceboy@usa.net> writes:

>One other problem...
>I'm using the Fujitsu Cobol Compiler.  Whenever I read from a file
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What you have described here indicates you want LINE SEQUENTIAL organization. 
Unless specifically stated in the SELECT statement, the files
are assumed to be RECORD SEQUENTIAL.
As for the variable length issue, Your record definition does not indicate any
field that occurs 'n' times depending on 'xxxx-field'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Records of variable length...

- **From:** jeromelump@aol.com (JeromeLump)
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981227220850.24253.00002525@ng-fc2.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36786EDC.7946629A@usa.net>`

```
To read variable length records, use the "Record is varying in size from x to z
depending" clause in your FD entry.
```

## Thread: need IMS briefing — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### need IMS briefing

- **From:** ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```

I've worked with DB2, and IDMS.
Can someone give me a quick overview of accessing IMS from COBOL?
```

#### ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
>I've worked with DB2, and IDMS.
>Can someone give me a quick overview of accessing IMS from COBOL?


Most shops are slightly different....but here  is some of what I know. I am
a bit rusty, havent had to do this stuff in over a year.

IMS is a database arraged in what I see as a tree structure. Where each
segment can have child segments under it. Where there are typically many
different types of segements and hopefully there is a utility to describe
there relationships. To retrieve a particular segment you would need the
keys to the parent segments of that particular segment, or you would have to
cycle through all segments of that type. Typically you would retrieve
segments by specifying the function (GU- get unique, GN get next, GNP get
next within parent) and the SSA which specifies the qualifications of the
segment you wish to retrieve. You specify the SSA as follows:
|Segment name | | Field name | Relational Operator | Field Vlalue | |
|8 bytes      |(| 8 bytes    | 2 bytes             | variable     |)|

A call would look something like :

CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING GU PCB WS-SEGMENT-GROUP SEG-A-SSA

Where:
GU is typically a WS- field which holds a 4 byte function to be used, in
this case 'GU  '
PCB is a kind of status area where error messages will be stored
WS-SEGMENT-GROUP is the working storage area where the requested segment
will be placed
SEG-A-SSA is the specification of wht to bring back

The PCB is sort of a pointer storing where you are in the database. Thus
using different SSA's with the same PCB traverses through the database. If a
database had the following segments and relationships:
PERSON segments containing personal id info such as SSN name birth date and
such
ADDRESS segments which would contain the many addresses a person lived at

To cycle through all of the addresses a particular person lived at you would
do something like:

01  ADDRESS-USSA.
    05 SEGMENT-NAME                   PIC X(09) VALUE 'ADDRESS   '.

01  PERSON-QSSA.
    05 SEGMENT-NAME                   PIC X(08) VALUE 'PERSON  '.
    05 FILLER                         PIC X(01) VALUE '('.
    05 PERSON-KEY                     PIC X(08) VALUE 'SSN     '.
    05 PERSON-RO                      PIC X(02) VALUE 'EQ'.
    05 PERSON-KEY-VALUE               PIC X(09) VALUE '000000000'.
    05 FILLER                         PIC X(01) VALUE ')'.
.
.
.
MOVE '000000001' TO PERSON-KEY-VALUE
CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING GU PCB1 PERSON-DATA PERSON-QSSA
PERFORM 8000-CHECK-PCB1-STATUS
CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING GNP PCB1 ADDRESS-DATA ADDRESS-USSA
PERFORM 8000-CHECK-PCB1-STATUS
PERFORM UNTIL NO-MORE-ADDRESS-SEGMENTS
    CALL CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING GNP PCB1 ADDRESS-DATA ADDRESS-USSA
    PERFORM 8000-CHECK-PCB1-STATUS
    PERFORM 3000-PROCESS-ADDRESS
END-PERFORM
.
.
.
GOBACK.



This may seem like a long explanation, but there is still much more to it,
but this gives you an idea of what is involved in IMS. Obviously you can do
inserts, deletes and replaces. As well as check points, for restarting the
program in the middle of processing after an abend or some other error. You
can lock segments by doing a GHU get hold unique, or GHN get hold next, or
GHNP get hold next within parent. Error checking is everything. Checking the
status code not only tells you about missing segments and other errors as
well as when the level of the PCB changes. Such as if you were to do the
above example with a GN instead of GNP which would allow you to jump to a
particular person then process the addresses of everyone following. The
status code would change when you jump from ADDRESS to PERSON and back down
to the next ADDRESS segment. Inside of the PCB is also what is called a
CONCATENATED KEY which has the keys of all parent segments and the current
segment in one long string. You can also retrieve multiple segments with
each call such as retrieving a PERSON segment as well as the ADDRESS segment
with one call......

but those can be explained better by someone with more knowledge than I.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10e256205ac5b84d9896e2@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com>`

```
In article <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com>, a@a.com says...
> >I've worked with DB2, and IDMS.
> >Can someone give me a quick overview of accessing IMS from COBOL?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> a bit rusty, havent had to do this stuff in over a year.

And you did a fine job. I would like just add a point or two, if you
don't mind. And if you don't mind I will snip a bit here and there.

> 
The IMS data structure is Hierarchical and looks a lot like and 
organization chart. Also, the language that is used to access IMS is 
called DL/I.



> IMS is a database arraged in what I see as a tree structure. Where each
> segment can have child segments under it. Where there are typically many
> different types of segements and hopefully there is a utility to describe
> there relationships. 

At this point lets define a few terms:

   DBD - Data Base Definition. This contains the structure of the entire 
Data Base. It has the various keys for each segment defined and all of 
the other important information. Usually the DBA, Data Base 
Administrator, will perform this function. This is also referred by some 
as the Physical Data Base.

   PSB - Program Specification Block - PCB - Program Control Block: These 
are used to define the logical portion of the Data Base that may be 
accessed by each program. So, if you have a large Data Base with a lot of 
segments not every program may need to have access to each segment. The 
PSB/PCB will allow access to only the segments that each program needs.

In many cases there is only one PSB/PCB for a DBD. But, this is just a 
thumbnail of the three key terms in IMS you will need a good book to have 
them fully explained. 

> To retrieve a particular segment you would need the
> keys to the parent segments of that particular segment, or you would have to
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

<snip code>

An SSA is a Segment Search Argument. All of the other information seems 
to be quite correct, IMO.

> 
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
At this point I sure you are confused but it is a "rough" description of 
IMS and its interface with COBOL. Granted there is a whole lot here but 
most if not all of it is correct. 

The next step to take is to get a good book that will cover the topics in 
more detail. Preferable one that covers both IMS and using DL/I.

> 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75ar6q$4v0@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com> <MPG.10e256205ac5b84d9896e2@news.earthlink.net>`

```

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
>In article <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com>, a@a.com says...
>> >I've worked with DB2, and IDMS.
>> >Can someone give me a quick overview of accessing IMS from COBOL?
>>
>>

  <much snippage>
There have been a couple of good responses - and I think they probably meet
what you are looking for, BUT I did want to add something that is rather
important to know.  There are actually two "flavors" of IMS  - what has been
talked about so far is what used to be called "IMS/DB" - or the database
flavor.  (And from the fact that you talk about DB2 and IDMS, this is
probably what you are interested in).  There is another flavor - which used
to be called IMS/DC (or data communication).  It is quite comparable to CICS
(and if you have done ANY online transaction programming with DB2 or IDMS -
if you don't know IMS/DC already, then you probably know CICS.  If you just
know "batch" then this is irrelevant anyway).

If you do know CICS, then you are probably already familiar with EXEC CICS
statements in a COBOL program.  Although
    Call "CBLTDLI" using ...
is probably the most common way of accessing IMS databases, there is another
way available in CICS environments and that is using
   EXEC DLI
statements,  The two are similar (but not identical) in function.  Depending
on what you want to learn for, you might want to look at both.

As with other posts, this is quite superficial, but might help in pointing
you in the right direction.
```

>flavor.  (And from the fact that you talk about DB2 and IDMS, this is
>probably what you are interested in).  There is another flavor - which used
>to be called IMS/DC (or data communication).  It is quite comparable to
CICS


Correct me if I am worng, but I seem to remmeber yet another flavor which no
one seemed to know, Conversational IMS. When I worked at Pa. Blue Shield
there was only one app which used it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75fv8r$g5i@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com> <MPG.10e256205ac5b84d9896e2@news.earthlink.net> <75ar6q$4v0@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3sDe2.317$hE2.2788697@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message <3sDe2.317$hE2.2788697@storm.twcol.com>...
>>what you are looking for, BUT I did want to add something that is rather
>>important to know.  There are actually two "flavors" of IMS  - what has
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>probably what you are interested in).  There is another flavor - which
used
>>to be called IMS/DC (or data communication).  It is quite comparable to
>CICS
>
>
>Correct me if I am worng, but I seem to remmeber yet another flavor which
no
>one seemed to know, Conversational IMS. When I worked at Pa. Blue Shield
>there was only one app which used it.
>
>

I don't think so.  There is Conversational and pseudo-conversational CICS
(the former is (almost?) never used in real world production) - but the
nature of IMS is different so IMS/DC programs don't fall into those
categories.  (Now there are BMPs vs MPP - maybe that is what you are
thinking of, but those are both part of IMS/DC - now known as the
"transaction server" if my memory serves me right).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36804CBC.6378@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com> <MPG.10e256205ac5b84d9896e2@news.earthlink.net> <75ar6q$4v0@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3sDe2.317$hE2.2788697@storm.twcol.com> <75fv8r$g5i@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> >Correct me if I am worng, but I seem to remmeber yet another flavor which
> no
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> "transaction server" if my memory serves me right).

The is a Conversational feature within IMS/DC, but it is rarely used, as
the Scratch Pad Area (SPA) and the processing involved consumes
mega-resources.  Most programmers will instead, for example in multiple
screen data entry apps, try to hide data entered in the first screen on
the second screen.  Another technique is to use a work-in-progress data
base to store the data between screens.  Both approaches supposedly more
efficient than conversational transactions.

In a conversational transaction, the first 'Get-Unique' using the IO-PCB
will retrieve the input message (screen), and the second GU will
retrieve the SPA.

Did it once twenty something years ago, can't remember too many more
details.

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Dennis Fyke" <DEFyke@aol.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be2eae$5bcb7440$2c198318@default.mw.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com> <MPG.10e256205ac5b84d9896e2@news.earthlink.net> <75ar6q$4v0@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3sDe2.317$hE2.2788697@storm.twcol.com> <75fv8r$g5i@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <36804CBC.6378@netbox.com>`

```
Many moons ago I attended a conference at IBM.  The IBM personnel
themselves said not to bother with SPAs.  

I can't see any need for them at all.

Long ago I just developed the habit of creating a work area database with
the LTERM as its key.  That way, I could always keep  what  user A was
doing separate from user B.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need IMS briefing

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36815C67.2365AB23@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<758j2o$1mm@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <5pZd2.189$hE2.1453137@storm.twcol.com> <MPG.10e256205ac5b84d9896e2@news.earthlink.net> <75ar6q$4v0@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3sDe2.317$hE2.2788697@storm.twcol.com> <75fv8r$g5i@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <36804CBC.6378@netbox.com> <01be2eae$5bcb7440$2c198318@default.mw.mediaone.net>`

```
Dennis Fyke wrote:

> Many moons ago I attended a conference at IBM.  The IBM personnel
> themselves said not to bother with SPAs.

I once worked for IBM (town Uithoorn, the Netherlands), on some customer
engineering systems. Those used IMS DC on-line programs, and in them a SPA by
design.

> I can't see any need for them at all.

The SPA is a lasting work area which is separate per terminal and
automatically re-addressed when doing a GetUnique.

> Long ago I just developed the habit of creating a work area database with
> the LTERM as its key.

That'f functionally the same as what the SPA is.

>  That way, I could always keep  what  user A was
> doing separate from user B.

That's functionally the same reason as for using the SPA.

And then something about terminology:

pseudo-conversational programming means breaking up a (logivally designed)
user dialog in separate programs, each starting with reading in the data from
a screen (CICS: receive / IMS: Get), doing some internal processing and then
writing the next (or same) screen (CICS: send / IMS : Insert). This ensures
that no program is active (not consuming main memory space nor CPU cycles)
during the time that the user is "working with the terminal" for seconds long
(which could very well mean "drinking coffee", "scratching his back", "being
in a meeting").

In IMS, what is called "conversational programming" and using the SPA as
mentioned above is effectively the same as what the CICS manuals call
"pseudo-conversational" and using a TWA. (So far for the logic and
consistency of IBM terms in different manuals :-((  ).

There is a performance goal fos using the SPA: The file in which IMS keeps
those areas is much better accessed by IMS if the variation in length of the
SPA's is minimal, preferably no more than 4k f.e. I know a lot of sites that
standardize the SPA length to 4096 or some other 2power of kBytes.

The Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: try this Cobol compiler

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367c23ac.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<#l61OWfK#GA.293@upnetnews03> <19981218100102.01423.00000733@ng01.aol.com> <e4vy#zuK#GA.59@upnetnews05> <367b0a64.0@news1.ibm.net> <eOJkOC5K#GA.131@upnetnews05>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <367b0a64.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>>Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>>>IMHO, among the PC users in c.l.c.,  there are probably more users of
>>>Windows compilers than MSDOS compilers, but I could be wrong.  Let's have
>>>a show of hands from all those lurkers in this ng that are still doing
their
>>>thing with MSDOS compilers and not planning to move to Win9x/NT.
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>In your definition of portable, is portable = non-GUI?  It seems to me
that,
>in general, GUI implies Win9x/NT and that essentially excludes portability.
>At least that seems to be so in the case of the DOJ's latest assault on M$


No, I include GUI as portable. All my stuff have a virtual screen which
includes
the standard GUI element (pull-downs, push buttons, pop-ups, etc). For each
platform, there is a peer component that handles the GUI from this virtual
screen. The peers may generate ANSI control sequences, HTML for a
Web-based interface, or a Windows interface, all depending on the platform.
This is somewhat similar to the Java AWT approach. Netscape's browser
used to run on many different platforms too (PC, Mac, Unix) using the
same approach.
The high-level control of the program is event-driven on all platforms,
and the actual API calls are part of code GENERATED for the platform
and thus invisible to the programmer.
So, I stand by the portability even in a GUI world.

About the DOJ and M$:  Mister Gates's power comes from everybody
being a lamb and giving in to his crap without realizing that they don't
have
to. But, somehow they all want too. Something about writing 'true' Windows
applications as if that were in someway desirable.
```
