<a id="t-f12d05318b"></a>
## Thread: Y2K Remediation — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K Remediation

- **From:** "Steve Green" <jmdp@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<759lfv$e59$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Dear COBOL professional:

Sorry for the intrusion, but I would like to share a Y2K opportunity with
you.  By now you realize that most companies will NOT meet the deadline.
For whatever reason they headed down the wrong path, chose the wrong vendor,
or didn't believe all the warnings.  Well, get serious folks, this problem
not only isn't going away, but fixing it this late is only going to be more
complex, and much more expensive.

I have contacted you with a simple proposal that can put some very serious
money into your life.  I represent a firm that is in the y2k business. Sure,
they are 11,000,000 others, but, do they have 12 Cray XMP-XXX super
computers, and did they spend10 years gearing up for this process?

Simply put, I am looking to make contact with the MOST senior IT, IS, or
Data Processing person in ANY company that has legacy systems that are NOT
y2k compliant.

If you have EVER worked for, are working for, put bids in for, or are in the
middle of any y2k conversion process I would like very much to speak and
work with you.

I will PAY you a very HANDSOME commission on any work that we do that is the
direct result of an introduction from you .

What makes us different, and gets us in the door, is that we will perform
complete and absolute remediation on any of their COBOL applications, or
segments thereof, at absolutely NO COST TO THEM.  Yes, that's right, for
FREE!!!!!!

If they like the work, the cost is $1.00 per line of code.

Now, get out your Rolodexes, your email address book, and anyone's name you
can remember that is in a position to get a simple phone call from the
President of the company.  All we want to do is talk with them.  There is NO
SELLING on your part whatsoever!  The most you would have to do is make a
phone call, and tell them to expect a call from our company.

What could be simpler than that!!!!!

Since the money is so large, we all need to protect ourselves here.  So, if
you want to find out more, please contact us and we will provide a very
SIMPLE Finder's Fee Agreement and Non-Disclosure/Non-Compete form that will
protect us all.  When that's been completed and signed, we will supply you
with the complete program, and the names of some of our existing clients.
We have already performed this service on literally billions of lines of
codes, for some of the largest Corporations in the world.

This is a NO Brainer my friends, and a few simple calls could garner you
more money that you have ever seen in your life!

Regards,
Steve Green

Communication Access Technology
```

#### ↳ Thane Hubbell - CPU Hog - was Re: Y2K Remediation

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3678759c.179569213@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<759lfv$e59$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Wed, 16 Dec 1998 20:11:13 -0500, "Steve Green" <jmdp@earthlink.net>
wrote:


>I have contacted you with a simple proposal that can put some very serious
>money into your life.  I represent a firm that is in the y2k business. Sure,
>they are 11,000,000 others, but, do they have 12 Cray XMP-XXX super
>computers, and did they spend10 years gearing up for this process?

Do they run OS/390?  I've a conversion process (from one installation
to ours) and I have our machine running at 100% CPU for the last 72
hours. I sure could use some of that spare CPU.  

PS. On the subject - or off - these are large VSAM file loads.  They
are never updated, only loaded and read.  I'm tight on space.  So I
tried some tricks.  There are two related files - an ESDS data file -
containing the detail information - large - and a KSDS data file
containing the key information for a variable range of records in the
ESDS data file - referenced by RBA.  Large system.  I'm not going to
re-engineer it, I just need to move it.  Anyway, I decided to play
with the VSAM file definitions to make it more efficient.  CI SIZE was
4096 - the records in the KSDS are 48 bytes - 40 bytes are key
information.  The records in the ESDS are variable length from 87 to
350.  Cool.  So I decided I'd used SPANNED.  Then I changed the CI
Size to 32K.  Figured I would waste the least amount of space that
way.  Then I loaded a test file.  It went from 250 Cyl on the ESDS to
255 - from 82 Cyl on the KSDS to 84.  Fine idea that was.  So  I
decided to just leave out the CISIZE definition and let VSAM do what
it will.  The physical records on the ESDS ended up around 16K - a
little less - but the utilization went down to 220 cyls on the ESDS
and 67 on the KSDS.  Interesting.  I left it spanned.  Now all I have
to do is test to see if the programs (ASM) can read the data still.
If so, I think I'll just let the system do what it wants from now on.
OS/390 2.5 and VSAM seem to be smarter than I am these days...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Thane Hubbell - CPU Hog - was Re: Y2K Remediation

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3679af0e.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<759lfv$e59$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3678759c.179569213@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane,

I'm not quite understanding your post.  While I can vaguely see why it
doesn't have anything to do with the post it appears to be responding to,
I've got a couple of things I'd like to say.

One is: a 32K block size doesn't necessarily get you the best space savings.
On our aging 3390 mod 2s, the optimum blocksize in vsam is 18432 bytes, for
a total track usage of 98.2%.  4K only gets 87.2%, and 32K only gets 90.9%.
But these numbers, or so I have been led to believe, is pretty much device
dependent.
Modern storage arrays use virtual storage techniques that allow the array to
"appear" as any device or number of devices to the mainframe that the system
programmer wants, but actually stores the information where ever it feels
like it, so I don't know how this would impact things.
For the best results, check your device manual or with your friendly
neighborhood systems programmer.


Thane Hubbell wrote in message <3678759c.179569213@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Wed, 16 Dec 1998 20:11:13 -0500, "Steve Green" <jmdp@earthlink.net>
>wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>money into your life.  I represent a firm that is in the y2k business.
Sure,
>>they are 11,000,000 others, but, do they have 12 Cray XMP-XXX super
>>computers, and did they spend10 years gearing up for this process?
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Thanks from the Student ...

- **From:** njdave65@aol.com (NJDave65)
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981219170503.01425.00001347@ng01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36780B2D.925441EF@Brighton.ac.uk>`

```
Mark, 
Vsam status codes are a little different than abend codes in that depending on
their type an abend will not occur. Depending on what system you are 
running in, you could open a VSAM file twice and you won't get an abend, but a
status code of '92' which you would know if you put in a display of the status
code. IBM has an extensive listing of several different error codes in their
books on line section.
```

### linkage

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981216011153.10291.00000526@ng03.aol.com>`

```
I have a question, is there any possibility to pass data between programs in
MicroFocus Cobol without using linkage section?
```

#### ↳ Re: linkage

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7598ae$iqb$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19981216011153.10291.00000526@ng03.aol.com>`

```

DRadule390 wrote in message <19981216011153.10291.00000526@ng03.aol.com>...
>I have a question, is there any possibility to pass data between programs
in
>MicroFocus Cobol without using linkage section?

If both programs are running in the same runtime, an EXTERNAL working
storage item works well.

Rob Annandale
```

#### ↳ Re: linkage

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<757k3t$npi@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981216011153.10291.00000526@ng03.aol.com>`

```

DRadule390 wrote in message <19981216011153.10291.00000526@ng03.aol.com>...
>I have a question, is there any possibility to pass data between programs
in
>MicroFocus Cobol without using linkage section?

Is there a reason WHY you want to do this?  If so, it might help us to
answer.

You can use the EXTERNAL attribute on any Working-Storage 01-level item.
Define it this way in both the calling and called program - and its data
will be available in both programs.  Similarly, you could write your data
out to a file in the calling program, then close the file and open it again
in the called subprogram.  However, (as stated earlier) depending on what
you really WANT, these may or may not solve your problems.
```

<a id="t-f2dd298856"></a>
## Thread: Trailing Zs in PIC — 3 msgs

### Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<36775cb4.12386466@netnews>`

```
I recent have had a problem with Unisys A Series Cobol85.  The problem
turned up in a check printing program, and related to the picture
character *, frequently called the check protect character, and Z.  

The PIC in question was "***,***.ZZ".   There is consensus that this PIC
is invalid according to every ANSI standard and according to the current
ISO draft.  The question is how Unisys interprets it.  A long standing
Unisys rule is that any character which is invalid in a PIC according to
ANSI, may be used as a fixed insertion character.  Thus the (then)
Burroughs Cobol-68 compilers accepted /, while it wasn't added to IBM
compilers until ANSI-74, and a host of miscellaneous insertion characters
such as =, :, [, >, 4, ?, and W can also be used.

But what about ***,***.ZZ?  Until a recent change from Unisys, the COBOL74
and COBOL85 compilers, interpretted those Zs the same as 9s, with the
peculiar exception in COBOL74 that a value of zero caused the ammount
field to be completely blank (on a CHECK).

Now,  Unisys says that it is incorrect to interpret those Zs as digit
positions, they must therefore be insertion characters.  Therefore a check
printing program (which was recompiled on Y2K implementation weekend)
started printing amounts like *****23.ZZ.

This is why I say that the Supreme Court of Unisys Cobol has ruled
incorrectly:  

This behavior is new.  Cobol74 and Cobol85 always took Zs as digit
positions up until and including the 44.1A base release.  This behavior
was introduced in patches late in 1998.  It has not yet reached most
users.  To the best of my knowledge, no Unisys A Series or Burroughs Cobol
compiler ever exhibited this behavior until 1998.

This behavior is non-ANSI.  Any action other than a syntax error is a
Unisys extension.  Changing the behavior to comply with ANSI is an
acceptable change.  Changing bahavior of non-ANSI syntax to comply with
Unisys standards is potentially acceptable.  But to interpret them as
non-digit insertion characters is A Bad Thing.  To take non-ANSI syntax
which has had certain Unisys semantics since the beginning of time, and
suddenly change it to do something else (and not even something useful) is
just making trouble for the users.


I therefore request that all Unisys A Series users express their opinion.
Which should it be:

A)  The Zs should be insertion characters (the new Unisys interpretation)
B)  The Zs should be digit positions (the old Unisys interpretation)
C)  A syntax error (ANSI)
```

#### ↳ Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<759con$41e$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<36775cb4.12386466@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart has asked for user input on the behavior of Unisys A Series
COBOL85 in the context of a data item declared "PICTURE ***,***.ZZ", but he
didn't post Unisys' reasons for rejecting his assertions that were provided
to him in private correspondence.  This is an effort to put forth the
reasons that the architects of the Unisys COBOL85 compiler (as well as the
COBOL74 and COBOL(68) compilers)  disagree with Mr. Bart's assertions and
position.

Unisys agrees that the PICTURE string in question exceeds what is permitted
by ANSI-85 (or for that matter ANSI-74)  rules.   That isn't the point here.

Unisys' current COBOL85 reference materials states, in "Simple Insertion
Character":   "Also, any characters that are not defined PICTURE characters
are used as insertion characters.  These include right parentheses, left
parentheses, and any digits that are not in the strict syntax to indicate
consecutive occurrences of a preceding symbol.  Precedence rules for these
characters and rules for determining the categorty are the same as those for
the simple insertion character 0".

This notation is encoded in such a way as to identify it as an extension to
X3.23-1985 COBOL standards.  This feature is similarly noted in the Unisys A
Series COBOL74 and COBOL(68) reference manuals, and identified as extensions
to the standard.  .

The underlying philosophy behind the implementation of this extension is
this, stated a little more informally:

"If you're parsing a PICTURE string, and encounter a character that doesn't
fit the narrowest rules that apply where you are in that PICTURE string,
treat that character that doesn't make sense in that context as an insertion
character."

It doesn't matter whether the character can or cannot be used elsewhere in
the PICTURE string and be identified as a PICTURE character in that context,
and it doesn't matter what the meaning of that character, if it is
legitimate in another context, is in that context.  If it doesn't fit in
context, it's *supposed* to be treated as an insertion character, as an
extension to ANSI rules, instead of receiving a syntax error.

This rule dates from the introduction of COBOL (68) on the Burroughs Large
System, and was carried intact into COBOL74 because that compiler was based
on the same symbol file as COBOL(68), and is maintained there for
compatibility between those two languages.  And it is maintained as an
extension in Unisys A Series COBOL85 for compatibility with both of that
compiler's predecessors.

Whether the documentation completely, correctly and clearly reflects this
implementation design feature is a separate issue (I personally suspect it
could perhaps be clarified some).

This rule was *originally* intended (circa 1970!) to provide a solution in
(then) Burroughs Large Systems COBOL to such problems as phone numbers and
social security numbers; according to this rule, the appropriate encoding
for such purposes would be "PIC 999-99-9999" and "PIC (999)999-9999"
respectively.  But neither the implementation nor the intent was limited to
dashes, parens, and digits other than "9" or "0" in PICTURE strings.

Other mechanisms do exist to provide this capability on other systems.  I
have been told, for example, that "I" has been used as a PICTURE character
to introduce a following insertion character outside of the strict syntax,
which implies that PIC 999I-99I-9999 and PIC I(999I)999I-9999 produce the
desired results in those environments.  We think, for example, that "PIC
***,***.IZIZ" would provide the functionality on such systems that is
correctly provided by "PIC ***,***.ZZ" on Unisys A Series.

Personally, I think the use of "I" as an insertion flag would have been a
vastly superior approach to the provision of arbitrary insertion characters
in a PICTURE string, and think the Unisys A Series community would have been
a whole lot better off had that approach been implemented in the late 1960's
in Unisys A Series COBOL(68).   But it wasn't, and it's now some thirty
years, three generations of compiler implementation, and who knows how many
programs and corporate coding convention decisions, later.  This extension,
and the rule that underlies it, is here to stay.

Mr. Bart is indeed correct; the behavior of "PIC ***,***.ZZ" did indeed
change, in HMP 3.0 / SSR 44.2.  Prior to that release, COBOL85 failed to
treat "Z" characters as insertion characters in contexts other than
floating-Z, and also failed to treat trailing asterisks as insertion
characters in contexts other than floating-asterisks.   This behavior was
corrected, *in response to a customer UCF*, in HMP 3.0 / SSR 44.2 and
subsequent releases, and in all prior supported Interim Correction streams.

We stand by the correctness of that modification, in the light of the
standards prohibition and Unisys' documented extension to that prohibition.
Given the documented extension to the ANSI standards in this area, and the
three choices  Mr. Bart has proposed for the community, namely,

>A)  The Zs should be insertion characters (the new Unisys interpretation)
>B)  The Zs should be digit positions (the old Unisys interpretation)
>C)  A syntax error (ANSI)

Unisys continues to take the position that (A) is the correct approach, and
furthermore that (A) is the *original interpretation*, not a new one.  The
failure was in the original implementation of the design, and that failure
has been corrected.

There may well be cases in which the implementation cannot meet the
expectation of treating the character as an insertion character, and in
those cases the correct "fallback" position is a syntax error, not on the
basis of ANSI violation, because we already permit this encoding, but on the
basis of conflicting requirements or even simply on the basis that the
combination wasn't considered.  Any case that does not fall into one of
these categories represents an error in the compiler that should be
corrected.

Had Unisys made the decision to use "I" as an insertion flag as an extension
to the applicable standards on Unisys A Series systems nearly thirty years
ago, "PIC ***,***.ZZ" would indeed be receiving a syntax error today.  But
that path was not taken at that time, and one of the implications of the
path that *was* taken back then is that, in "PIC ***,***.ZZ", the "Z"
characters are correctly treated as insertion characters.

        -Chuck Stevens    [charles.stevens@unisys.spam.com] remove the spam,
of course

Randall Bart wrote in message <36775cb4.12386466@netnews>...
>I recent have had a problem with Unisys A Series Cobol85.  The problem
>turned up in a check printing program, and related to the picture
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>l    |/ MS^7=6/28/107   http://members.aol.com/PanicYr00/Sequence.html
```

#### ↳ Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** "Colin Zealley" <colin.zealley@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<7585hp$kon$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<36775cb4.12386466@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <36775cb4.12386466@netnews>...
[snip]

>I therefore request that all Unisys A Series users express their opinion.
>Which should it be:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>C)  A syntax error (ANSI)


I'd go for (C), personally, but then I'm a 2200 guy, and our compilers have
always (AFAIK) interpreted PICTURE clauses strictly according to the
standards.

Anyway, this is my personal opinion, not to be considered to be anything to
do with any official Unisys position.

Colin

<a id="t-771b159d75"></a>
## Thread: Starting a resource on a web-server through HTTP.. — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Starting a resource on a web-server through HTTP..

- **From:** "tommy" <tommynilsen@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7593pj$uli$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Hi

I need to start a resource/html-page on a web-server through the
http-protocol, from a cobol-program.

The resource started should return a string to my cobol-program...

Has anyone done this before ?

Are there any win32-api's I can use ???

Any help would be greatly appreciated !!

I use NetExpress 2.0 from MicroFocus on Win'98.

Tommy Nilsen
tommynilsen@yahoo.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Starting a resource on a web-server through HTTP..

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <spg@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3678E884.61C71129@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7593pj$uli$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Hi Tommy,

If you want to get/post a request to a web server from COBOL you 
can use any of the following APIs:
 1) w3c has a set of 'generic' APIs that allow you get/post
    information to your server.
      http://www.w3.org/Library/

   (ideal if you need a cross platform solution)

 2) windows has a set of wininet APIs that allow you 'get' a
    document. This can be used to submit a simple CGI request.
    The apis are: HttpOpenRequest, HttpSendRequest... etc.. 
    these are documented in the SDK

Hope this points you in the right direction.

> tommynilsen@yahoo.com

## Thread: Burroughs B-heads — 1 msgs

### Burroughs B-heads

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)

<a id="t-890b2cd08a"></a>
## Thread: Trailing Zs in PIC — 1 msgs

### Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 1998-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<01010064.9qgksy@titipu.paleo.org>`

```
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) writes:
> Now,  Unisys says that it is incorrect to interpret those Zs as digit
> positions, they must therefore be insertion characters.  Therefore a check
> printing program (which was recompiled on Y2K implementation weekend)
> started printing amounts like *****23.ZZ.

This is clearly a priority A FTR, and I hope you've already
submitted it.

Also, this kind of sudden change violates Burroughs/Unisys'
long-standing committment on the A-Series to give two
releases of warnings for deimplementations. This is clearly
a deimplementation (of the interpretation of Z after
decimal point as 9). You should also make this clear in
your FTR.

If Unisys does not correct this promptly, please bring up
the issue with the UNITE A-Series Working Group officers
for referral to the UNITE Resolutions process. (I'm no
longer part of that, so you'll need to go through the
channel, which is now the working group officers.)

> This behavior is non-ANSI.  Any action other than a syntax error is a
> Unisys extension.  Changing the behavior to comply with ANSI is an
> acceptable change.  Changing bahavior of non-ANSI syntax to comply with
> Unisys standards is potentially acceptable.

Any change without adequate warning is unacceptable.

I think both Unisys interpretations are confusing. I prefer
that Unisys change it (WITH WARNING) to a syntax error.
There's an adequate substitute, it's non-standard, and it's
easy to convert -- even automatically.

Edward Reid
```

---

#### ↳ Re: Indexed files...

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74qe2.236$bJ4.448695@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<3679DD4D.255A14E6@usa.net>`

```
The internal format of indexed files is almost always proprietary to
the specific compiler.  Unless you happen to know the format, it is
really pointless to examine the file in the manner as you have below.
Would you examine a Microsoft Access file in the same way and expect
to understand what you see? :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed files...

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3681456f.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<3679DD4D.255A14E6@usa.net>`

```
I'll prefix this whole thing with: I work on an IBM mainframe running
MVS/XA, COBOL II, and some version or other of VSAM.

If you are referring to a file indexed by a key (instead of a relative
record number), and you need to create such a file with data you have at
hand, you need to do a few things.

1-Decide what information in each record makes it different from every other
record.  This will be the "key".  That "key" will be identified in your
COBOL as a elementary or sub-group item under some 01 level group item
(unless, somehow, your key is the entire record).  On the SELECT statement,
you have to name that same data-item (no PIC on the SELECT, just the name).

2-You usually run the IDCAMS utility to create a "definition record"
(cluster, in vsam talk).  Then you run your COBOL program, and the program
on the operating system get together with various vsam routines (and the
data in the cluster) to start putting new records into the indexed file
whenever WRITE statements occur.

3-Once you have your key field defined in the record layout, and the key
field named on the SELECT statement (this is assuming you are taking data
from a flat file and putting into the indexed file), you need to make sure
the data in the flat file is ready to be loaded.  What do I mean by "ready"?
Well, the data in the key field (at least on mainframes), MUST be in
ascending order.  You ask why?  Especially when indexed files are capable of
inserting records anywhere depending on their key value?  Well, usually when
you load records to an indexed file, you don't access that file RANDOMly.
Why?  Because if all you are doing is putting one record in after another,
the overhead of RANDOM processing can be very slow.  If you specify
SEQUENTIAL, and have all those flat file records in order by ascending key
field value, then your data load will go much faster.

4-What will your data "look like"?  Using either an editor capable of
reading your implementation of indexed files (or, as one person has already
recommended, reversing the process I just mentioned (that is, unload your
indexed files to a sequential file)), it should look just the same as when
you you were able to look at it in the original sequential file.  On my
system, when I use FileAid to look at indexed files, it separates the key
fields from other fields with an empty column, and changes the color of the
characters in those fields to highlight them.


  Quan Ngo wrote in message <3679DD4D.255A14E6@usa.net>...
  Hi,
  What does an indexed file supposed to look like...?

  if you have let's say this kind of data to index:

      1200420401
      1202420411
      1207420919
      1208420919
      1209420919
      1215420912
      1220420912
      1225420911

  with the following, the record key being SECTION-NUM:

   DATA DIVISION.
   FILE SECTION.
   FD SECTIONS-FILE-IN     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
                           RECORD CONTAINS 10 CHARACTERS.
   01 SECTIONS-REC-IN.
      05 SECTION-NUM-IN               PIC X(4).
      05 COURSE-NUM-IN                PIC X(6).


  I get in my indexed file a bunch of odd characters, is it normal?
  I can see the data I indexed though.

  �����������������������������������������������������������������
  1202420411�
  1207420919�
  1208420919�
  1209420919�
  ...

  Thanx.
```

#### ↳ Re: File create-date from COBOL?

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75olfv$efo@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<75bt1a$cpf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
FILEINFO CSECT                                                          00001
*                                                                       00008
*          TO CALL THIS ROUTINE FROM COBOL:                             00009
*                                                                       00010
*      01  PARMAREA.                                                    00011
*          05 DDNAME                   PIC X(8).                        00012
*          05 I-O-OPTION               PIC X(1).                        00013
*          05 VOLSER-LIST              PIC X(34).                       00014
*          05 DATASET-NAME             PIC X(44).                       00015
*          05 DATASET-CREATE-DATE      PIC X(7).                        00016
*                                                                       00017
*      CALL 'FILEINFO' USING PARMAREA.                                  00018
*                                                                       00019
*      DDNAME - THE 'DDNAME' OF THE FILE FROM WHICH YOU ARE             00020
*               RETREIVING INFORMATION.                                 00021
*                                                                       00022
*      I-O-OPTION - 'I', 'O', OR 'W'                                    00023
*                   'I' - THE FILE WILL BE OPENED AS AN INPUT FILE      00024
*                   'O' - THE FILE WILL BE OPENED AS AN OUTPUT FILE     00025
*                   'W' - THE FILE WILL NOT BE OPENED AND THIS          00026
*                         SUBROUTINE WILL ASSUME THAT THE FILE HAS      00027
*                         BEEN OPENED BY THE CALLING PROGRAM.           00028
*                    NOTE: FOR OPTIONS 'I' AND 'O' THE FILE MUST        00029
*                          BE CLOSED WHEN THIS SUBROUTINE IS CALLED.    00030
*                          THE FILE WILL BE CLOSED UPON RETURN TO       00031
*                          THE CALLING PROGRAM.                         00032
*                          FOR OPTION 'W' THE FILE MUST BE OPEN WHEN    00033
*                          THIS SUBROUTINE IS CALLED.                   00034
*                          THE FILE WILL BE OPEN UPON RETURN TO         00035
*                          THE CALLING PROGRAM.                         00036
*                                                                       00037
*     VOLSER-LIST - THE LIST OF VOLSERS (UP TO 5) RETURNED IN THIS      00038
*                   AREA. FORMAT:                                       00039
*                   999999,999999,999999,999999,999999                  00040
*                                                                       00041
*     DATASET-NAME - THE FULLY QUALIFIED DATASET NAME RETURNED IN       00042
*                    THIS AREA.                                         00043
*                                                                       00044
*     DATASET-CREATE-DATE - CREATION DATE OF THE DATASET IN             00045
*                    JULIAN FORMAT (YYYYDDD)                            00046
*                                                                       00047
*                                                                       00048
*                                                                       00049
*                                                                       00050
*        REGISTER EQUATE MACRO
         REGEQUS                                                        00051
*                                                                       00052
         USING *,R3                                                     00053
         STM   R14,R12,12(R13)                                          00054
         LR    R3,R15                                                   00055
         ST    R13,SAVEAREA+4                                           00056
         LA    R12,SAVEAREA                                             00057
         ST    R12,8(0,R13)                                             00058
         LR    R13,R12                                                  00059
         L     R4,0(R1) PARM LIST                                       00060
         USING PARMAREA,R4                                              00061
         LA    R1,FILE                                                  00062
         USING IHADCB,R1                                                00063
         MVC   DCBDDNAM,DDNAME         MOVE DDNAME INTO DCB             00064
         DROP  R1                                                       00065
         CLI   IOOPT,C'O'              OUTPUT FILE                      00066
         BE    OPENOUT                                                  00067
         CLI   IOOPT,C'W'              FILE OPENED BY CALLER SO         00068
         BE    RDJFCB                  OPEN NOT NEEDED                  00069
         OPEN  (FILE,(INPUT))          DEFAULT IS INPUT FILE            00070
         B     CLOSEF                                                   00071
OPENOUT  OPEN  (FILE,(OUTPUT))                                          00072
CLOSEF   CLOSE (FILE)                                                   00073
RDJFCB   RDJFCB FILE                                                    00074
         LA    R7,JFCBAREA                                              00075
         USING IEFJFCBN,R7                                              00076
         MVC   DSN,JFCBDSNM            RETURN DSN TO CALLER             00077
         LA    R5,JFCBVOLS             RETURN VOLSERS TO CALLER         00078
         LA    R8,VOLSERS                                               00079
         LA    R6,5                                                     00080
MOVVOL   MVC   0(6,R8),0(R5)           MOVE VOLSER TO CALLER AREA       00081
         CH    R6,=H'1'                IF LAST VOLSER                   00082
         BE    CREATDAT                QUIT                             00083
         CLC   6(6,R5),=CL6' '         SEE IF NEXT VOLSER IS BLANK      00084
         BE    CREATDAT                QUIT IF IT IS                    00085
         MVI   6(R8),C','              MOVE ',' AFTER VOLSER            00086
         LA    R8,7(R8)                BUMP TO NEXT IN CALLER AREA      00087
         LA    R5,6(R5)                BUMP TO NEXT IN JFCBAREA         00088
         BCT   R6,MOVVOL               MOVE UP TO 5 VOLSERS             00089
*                                                                       00090
CREATDAT SR    R5,R5                                                    00091
         IC    R5,JFCBCRDT                                              00092
         AH    R5,=H'1900'                                              00093
         CVD   R5,DOUBLE                                                00094
         UNPK  CRATYYYY,DOUBLE+5(3)                                     00095
         OI    CRATYYYY+3,X'F0'                                         00096
*                                                                       00097
         MVC   HALF,JFCBCRDT+1                                          00098
         LH    R5,HALF                                                  00099
         CVD   R5,DOUBLE                                                00100
         UNPK  CRATDDD,DOUBLE+6(2)                                      00101
         OI    CRATDDD+2,X'F0'                                          00102
*                                                                       00103
GOBACK   L     R13,SAVEAREA+4                                           00104
         LM    R14,R12,12(R13)                                          00105
         LA    R15,0                                                    00106
         BR    R14                                                      00107
*                                                                       00108
SAVEAREA DC    18F'0'                                                   00109
*                                                                       00110
DOUBLE   DS    D                                                        00111
HALF     DS    H                                                        00112
*                                                                       00113
         LTORG                                                          00114
*                                                                       00115
FILE     DCB   DDNAME=DDNAME,MACRF=(GM,PM),DSORG=PS,EXLST=JFCB          00116
*                                                                       00117
JFCB     DS    0F                                                       00118
         DC    X'87'                                                    00119
         DC    AL3(JFCBAREA)                                            00120
*                                                                       00121
         DS    0F                                                       00122
JFCBAREA DS    CL176                                                    00123
*                                                                       00124
PARMAREA DSECT                                                          00125
DDNAME   DS    CL8                                                      00126
IOOPT    DS    C                                                        00127
VOLSERS  DS    CL34                                                     00128
DSN      DS    CL44                                                     00129
DSNCREAT DS    0CL7                                                     00130
CRATYYYY DS     CL4                                                     00131
CRATDDD  DS     CL3                                                     00132
*                                                                       00133
         DCBD  DSORG=PS,DEVD=DA                                         00134
*                                                                       00135
IEFJFCBN DSECT                                                          00136
         IEFJFCBN LIST=YES                                              00137
*                                                                       00138
         END                                                            00139
```

<a id="t-34a6fbc703"></a>
## Thread: help — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help

- **From:** Quan Ngo <le_spaceboy@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net>`

```
Hi, me again...
thanx to those who helped me previously...

I might have not been very clear last time...
again here is the sample data:

8012435STEVENS TERRY            M4200041200078120205612080761220078
8014325WAGNER PETER             M4200041202076120706712200551225089
8021534RICHARDSON EMILE         M30703712000681209081
8023879RANCOURT FRED            M20200212070781215089
8028765WAGNER THERESA           F2020011207078
*                                      ^-----^course info


Here is my file definition:

 FD STUDENT-FILE-IN     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 STUDENT-REC-IN.
    05 ID-IN                        PIC X(7).
    05 NAME-IN                      PIC X(25).
    05 GENDER-IN                    PIC X.
    05 PROGRAM-CODE-IN              PIC X(5).
    05 NUM-COURSE-IN                PIC X.
    05 COURSE-INFO-IN.
       10 SECTION1-NUM-IN            PIC X(4).
       10 FINAL1-MARK-IN             PIC 9(3).

NUM-COURSE-IN determines how many courses a student has, the courses
are listed right after.
If NUM-COURSE-IN = 4, 4 courses are listed right after in the record;
If NUM-COURSE-IN = 2, 2 courses are listed right after in the record;

How do I read this kind of data...?

Is there something like

    IF NUM-COURSE-IN = 2
*       READ COURSE-INFO1
*       READ COURSE-INFO2
    ELSE
    ...
    END-IF
    ...

I have no idea how to do this.
BTW I'm supposed to create an indexed file with this.

Thanx.
```

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75cefc$e9d$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net>`

```
Define the rest of the record as an array, like so:

>    05 COURSE-INFO-IN  occurs 9 times.
>       10 SECTION1-NUM-IN            PIC X(4).
>       10 FINAL1-MARK-IN             PIC 9(3).

I dimensioned it as 9 times, but the actual number should be
the maximum number of courses  a student can take.
You then simply reference the course data using a subscript.

Quan Ngo wrote in message <36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net>...
>Hi, me again...
>thanx to those who helped me previously...
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75d93l$cjg@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net> <75cefc$e9d$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <75cefc$e9d$1@news.igs.net>...
>Define the rest of the record as an array, like so:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>You then simply reference the course data using a subscript.

This variation will ONLY work if each record contains 9 course "slots" -
even if the person is only taking one course.  Instead code,

         05 NUM-COURSE-IN    Pic 9
         05 COURSE-INFO-IN  occurs 1 to 9 times
                                                  depending on
Num-course-In.
             10 SECTION1-NUM-IN            PIC X(4).
             10 FINAL1-MARK-IN             PIC 9(3).

Although your compiler should "figure out" that this has made this a
"variable length" record, you might also want to add a RECORD VARYING clause
to your FD (check the syntax in your book).  Once this has been done, each
READ will get in the full record (including however many courses you need).

Note: You will need to make certain that your input file actually includes
the number of courses as a field.  If it doesn't and you can't change the
data description, then you will need to use another - much more complicated
approach.  This approach would require you to use the
   Record Varying in Size depending on ws-field-name

Then, once you read each record, you would calculate how many clases were
included from the value that the READ put into the ws-field-name field.


>
>Quan Ngo wrote in message <36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net>...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>>       10 FINAL1-MARK-IN             PIC 9(3).

>>NUM-COURSE-IN determines how many courses a student has, the courses
>>are listed right after.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75djb3$620$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net> <75cefc$e9d$1@news.igs.net> <75d93l$cjg@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Quite to the contrary, Bill, it will work with any Cobol
I have ever seen. The records are line sequential,
and the array does not need to be variable length.
The unused array space will be blank filled.

When the data is processed, it is a simple matter
to do a perform varying the subscript from 1 by 1
until greater than the number of courses. You
process the data using the actual size of the data,
but you read a line sequential data file with the
buffer size equal to the largest record.

Using an array length in an FD record that varies
depending on a number contained in the record
being read, however, I have my doubts.  That is
a construct I would never use, and just makes the
code more difficult to fathom, at least in my opinion.

Even if it does work, the processing should not
vary an iota, (though it must vary depending on)<S>.

William M. Klein wrote in message <75d93l$cjg@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>Donald Tees wrote in message <75cefc$e9d$1@news.igs.net>...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>"variable length" record, you might also want to add a RECORD VARYING
clause
>to your FD (check the syntax in your book).  Once this has been done, each
>READ will get in the full record (including however many courses you need).
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367A6816.337A0012@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net> <75cefc$e9d$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Define the rest of the record as an array, like so:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> You then simply reference the course data using a subscript.

He should first determine that this maximum occurance fits on his input
record.  If not, he needs to re-think how to get his data.

And he needs to be reminded how vital it is to check for overflow.  He's
obviously a beginner, so we need to stress to him where bugs will be
which may not come out in testing.  It is so automatic with experienced
COBOL programmers that we sometimes forget to mention it!
```

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367A66F0.A487EABC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net>`

```
I prefer not having field names, beyond what is absolutely required in
my FD 01 description.  I either READ INTO or do a READ then a MOVE.  

The simplest way to do your work is to figure out what the maximum value
can be for NUM-COURSE-IN.  (The size of your input record may determine
this).

First check to see that your value for NUM-COURSE-IN is in the accepted
range - this check is ABSOLUTELY necessary in COBOL because COBOL does
not check to make sure you're processing within your table.

Say the max = 12.
Then also make
05 COURSE-INFO-IN        OCCURS 12 TIMES. 

Create a subscript variable      COURSE-IDX   PIC 9(03)

Now I can't really tell you what to do next, it depends on what you want
to do with your data!  But here's an example:

    PERFORM 0100-PROCESS-COURSES
       VARYING COURSE-IDX FROM 1 BY 1
         UNTIL COURSE-IDX > NUM-COURSE-IN.

0100-PROCESS-COURSES.
    DISPLAY "SECTION = ' SECTION1-NUM-IN (COURSE-IDX)
            "  GRADE = ' FINAL1-MARK-IN  (COURSE-IDX).


Quan Ngo wrote:
> 
> Hi, me again...
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanx.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75duh0$bnc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36799CD0.D1795B24@usa.net> <367A66F0.A487EABC@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <367A66F0.A487EABC@home.com>...

>The simplest way to do your work is to figure out what the maximum value
>can be for NUM-COURSE-IN.  (The size of your input record may determine
>this).

It is defined as a single digit; that is why I gave him the
example with 9 as the dimension of the array.  As you say,
keep it simple.  The only thing I did *not* mention, is that
it is defined in the record as an X field, and it really should
be picture 9.  If the size is gauranteed to be some number
less than 9, then he can make the array smaller. Personally,
I would just dimension it at 9, then validate.
```

<a id="t-4af184cd5a"></a>
## Thread: need basic y2k source code. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### need basic y2k source code.

- **From:** "netone" <netone@pacbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eoke2.480$Mk.2047@typhoon-sf.snfc21.pbi.net>`

```
If anybody could supply me a generic set of how a COBOL Y2K problem look
like in a source code. Doesn't have to be complete, only what the problem
looks like in code. Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: need basic y2k source code.

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hpqe2.239$bJ4.454855@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<eoke2.480$Mk.2047@typhoon-sf.snfc21.pbi.net>`

```
netone wrote:
>If anybody could supply me a generic set of how a COBOL Y2K problem look
>like in a source code. Doesn't have to be complete, only what the problem
>looks like in code. Thanks.

Your question is too simplistic.  The Y2K 'problem' could be expressed
in code a truly infinite number of ways.  I could post 500 pages here,
and not scratch the surface.  This sounds like a homework assignment,
but I'll give you some examples.

Example 1:

  Data Division:

           03  MAST-TRAN-DATE.
               05  MAST-TRAN-YY      PIC  9(02).
               05  MAST-TRAN-MM      PIC  9(02).
               05  MAST-TRAN-DD      PIC  9(02).

           03  WORK-TRAN-DATE.
               05  WORK-TRAN-YY      PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-TRAN-MM      PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-TRAN-DD      PIC  9(02).

  Procedure Division:

           IF (WORK-TRAN-DATE < MAST-TRAN-DATE)
               ...

As you can see, the date only allows for a 2 digit year.  As soon
as the year reaches 2000, the If statement in the Procedure Division
will no longer be valid, because 00 < 99.  To correct this problem,
both dates must be expanded like this:

           03  MAST-TRAN-DATE.
               05  MAST-TRAN-CC      PIC  9(02).
               05  MAST-TRAN-YY      PIC  9(02).
               05  MAST-TRAN-MM      PIC  9(02).
               05  MAST-TRAN-DD      PIC  9(02).

           03  WORK-TRAN-DATE.
               05  WORK-TRAN-CC      PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-TRAN-YY      PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-TRAN-MM      PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-TRAN-DD      PIC  9(02).

Or you could expand them like this:

           03  MAST-TRAN-DATE.
               05  MAST-TRAN-YYYY    PIC  9(04).
               05  MAST-TRAN-MM      PIC  9(02).
               05  MAST-TRAN-DD      PIC  9(02).

           03  WORK-TRAN-DATE.
               05  WORK-TRAN-YYYY    PIC  9(04).
               05  WORK-TRAN-MM      PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-TRAN-DD      PIC  9(02).

Of course, every reference to these two date fields must be examined
and changed if necessary to accommodate the new format.  A typical
program may have from none, one or two date references to literally
hundreds, perhaps thousands, of date references.  Each of them must
be carefully examined, analyzed, and corrected.  The above example
was very simple.  A more complex situation is when the dates are used
in calculations, say to compute the number of days since a payment
has been made on a loan.  Calculating the number of days between two
dates is very common, and the algorithms are different between dates
with two and four digit years.  And if the year only has two digits,
both dates are assumed to be in the same century.  Another common use
of dates is to calculate the day of the week on which the date falls.
The pattern of weekdays is different between the 20th century and
the 21st century.  Once the year reaches 2000, all those routines
will fail, giving the wrong day of the week.
```

#### ↳ Re: need basic y2k source code.

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367CA565.5BB3@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<eoke2.480$Mk.2047@typhoon-sf.snfc21.pbi.net>`

```
netone wrote:
> 
> If anybody could supply me a generic set of how a COBOL Y2K problem look
> like in a source code. Doesn't have to be complete, only what the problem
> looks like in code. Thanks.

DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  DATES.
    05  DATE-1             PIC 999999.
    05  FILLER             REDEFINES DATE-1.
        10  DATE-1-YY      PIC 99.
        10  DATE-1-MM      PIC 99.
        10  DATE-1-DD      PIC 99.
    05  DATE-2             PIC 999999.
    05  FILLER             REDEFINES DATE-2.
        10  DATE-2-YY      PIC 99.
        10  DATE-2-MM      PIC 99.
        10  DATE-2-DD      PIC 99.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     IF DATE-1 IS GREATER THAN DATE-2
         DISPLAY 'DATE 1 IS MORE THAN DATE 2'
     ELSE
     IF DATE-1 IS EQUAL TO DATE-2
         DISPLAY 'DATE 1 IS THE SAME AS DATE 2'
     ELSE
         DISPLAY 'DATE 2 IS MORE THAN DATE 1'.

I'll let you figure out the results if one of the dates is December 31, 1999
and the other is January 1, 2000.
```

<a id="t-60a31ab69c"></a>
## Thread: Trailing Zs in PIC — 2 msgs

### Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 1998-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<01010064.9t49r9@titipu.paleo.org>`

```
Chuck Stevens argues at length that, because the COBOL68
and COBOL74 compilers treated the ZZ in ***.ZZ as fixed
insertion characters, that it was proper to change the
COBOL85 behavior to match as an FTR correction, without
warning.

I see several flaws in Chuck's arguments:

  1) In fact COBOL74 did not act as Chuck implies.

  2) In fact the COBOL74 compiler still (as of 44.2) does
     not act as Chuck claims.

  3) The 44.2 COBOL85 compiler does not act as Chuck claims.

  4) The change altered existing compiler behavior suddenly
     without warning to users, with the result of altering
     existing user program output to be incorrect but
     without a syntax error or program fault.

It would be much easier to agree with some of Chuck's
points if his premises were correct. In particular, there
are good reasons, based on long asserted user desires, to
keep features from the old compiler in the new one, even
when those features are less than ideal. Users don't want
to do a conversion just to stay with the A-Series systems.

Below, I'll detail the differing behaviors. I'll address
point 4) first, as it's the critical one in my view.

The COBOL85 compiler is no longer a new product. It's been
out for at least five years, more I think. Many new
programs have been written using only COBOL85, and many
programs converted from COBOL68 and COBOL74 have been
extensively modified using COBOL85. It's no longer accurate
to think of COBOL85 as being used mainly to compile
converted programs -- it's a primary compiler in its own
right.

Therefore it's no longer acceptable to make sudden changes
to COBOL85 just to conform to COBOL74. Such changes are
major anathemas to users, because they require sudden and
unplanned expenditures of manpower to alter user programs.
This is the reason that Unisys, for the A-Series, has for
many years guaranteed two releases of warnings for
deimplementations, including major changes of behavior.
This change came with no warning, not even as part of the
release which implements the change. That is, the compiler
does not flag the syntax in question with a "warning:
meaning of insert characters is changing on 44.2, see note
blahblah".

Worse, it altered existing program behavior with no
indication. The altered compiler produces no warning on the
item whose behavior has changed. The program which no
longer works correctly gets no syntax error, no fault,
nothing to indicate a change except incorrect output. While
we would complain about a sudden syntax error or fault,
neither is as bad as suddenly and silently receiving
incorrect output.

Based on Chuck's attitude, it's obvious that large parts of
software engineering still do not understand how damaging
such changes can be in a production environment..

OK, now for the technical issues. Chuck said these changes
were made "for 44.2". Randall Bart says "this behavior was
introduced in patches late in 1998". In fact, the 43.2 and
44.2 COBOL74 compilers act exactly the same in this
respect, but the 44.2 COBOL85 compiler exhibits the new
behavior. I don't know whether COBOL74 patches have been
released post-44.2 to change its behavior.

Consider this program fragment:
     02 EDITED-VAL  PIC ***,***.ZZ.
     02 DATA-VAL    PIC 9(6)V99.

     MOVE DATA-VAL TO EDITED-VAL.

Let's compare the behaviors of old (43.2) COBOL74 and
COBOL85 with the new (44.2) behaviors.

  Old COBOL74 treats the ZZ as though it were 99, except that
  the entire field is blank if the source is zero. Chuck
  implies, but does not directly state, that COBOL74
  previously treated the ZZ as fixed insertion characters.
  What he actually says is that this behavior "was carried
  intact into COBOL74" from COBOL(68), implying that it's
  always been that way. I did not try to investigate farther
  back than 43.2, but it clearly has not always been that
  way.

  New COBOL74 acts exactly like old COBOL74: it still does not
  treat the ZZ as fixed insertion characters.

  Old COBOL85 I did not test, since everyone seems to agree
  that it treated the ZZ as though it were 99.

  New COBOL85 treats the ZZ as insert characters when the
  source is non-zero, but inserts ** when the source is zero!
  This flatly contradicts Chuck's assertion that the ZZ is
  now treated as simple insertion characters.

Thus it appears that the fixed insertion behavior has not
existed in any supported software for several years. Since
existing behavior is the premise of Chuck's arguments, I
can't accept any of his conclusions.

Here's the program I used to test:

  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
  ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
  INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
  FILE-CONTROL.
      SELECT RM ASSIGN REMOTE.
  DATA DIVISION.
  FILE SECTION.
  FD RM.
  01 RM-REC.
     02 RM-SOURCE  PIC 9(6).99.
     02 RM-SPACE   PIC XX.
     02 RM-EDITED  PIC ***,***.ZZ.
  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  01 WS-SOURCE     PIC 9(6)V99.
  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  BEGIN.
      OPEN OUTPUT RM.
      MOVE SPACES TO RM-SPACE.
      MOVE 12345.67 TO WS-SOURCE.
      MOVE WS-SOURCE TO RM-SOURCE RM-EDITED.
      WRITE RM-REC.
      MOVE 0 TO WS-SOURCE.
      MOVE WS-SOURCE TO RM-SOURCE RM-EDITED.
      WRITE RM-REC.
      MOVE 0.01 TO WS-SOURCE.
      MOVE WS-SOURCE TO RM-SOURCE RM-EDITED.
      WRITE RM-REC.
      CLOSE RM.
      STOP RUN.

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<3679f986.18511317@netnews>`
- **References:** `<01010064.9t49r9@titipu.paleo.org>`

```
Long post, combining three quotes.

'Twas Wed, 16 Dec 1998 14:36:04 -0800, when "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Randall Bart has asked for user input on the behavior of Unisys A Series
>COBOL85 in the context of a data item declared "PICTURE ***,***.ZZ", but he
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>position.

I did not mean to misrepresent Unisys position, though I will give it my
own spin.  Let me state that Unisys replied promtly in this matter.  At
first I thought it was simply an errant side effect of some bug fix.  When
they said that they had changed the PIC handling deliberately, I appealed,
stating many of the arguments that I (and now Ed) have stated here.

>Unisys agrees that the PICTURE string in question exceeds what is permitted
>by ANSI-85 (or for that matter ANSI-74)  rules.   That isn't the point here.

Agreed.

>Unisys' current COBOL85 reference materials states, in "Simple Insertion
>Character":   "Also, any characters that are not defined PICTURE characters
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the simple insertion character 0".

Z is a defined PICTURE character.  It is neither parenthesis nor digit.  

>"If you're parsing a PICTURE string, and encounter a character that doesn't
>fit the narrowest rules that apply where you are in that PICTURE string,
>treat that character that doesn't make sense in that context as an insertion
>character."

This is the new interpretation.  The rule says "not defined PICTURE
characters".  The rule does not say "in context".  The de facto Unisys
rule has always been that Zs are always digits.

It is true that there is special handling for -.  I believe this handling
was added in the early 1980s.  In Medium Systems COBOLV(68) we used = or _
in phone numbers because - wasn't allowed.  Then the insertion rule was
expanded to allow - as an insertion character if it wasn't a leading or
trailing sign, and in adding -, + and other things were added.  But I
don't believe Z, 9, $, *, X, or A were ever insertion characters.

>Other mechanisms do exist to provide this capability on other systems.  I
>have been told, for example, that "I" has been used as a PICTURE character
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>correctly provided by "PIC ***,***.ZZ" on Unisys A Series.

This was used in Medium Systems COBOL(74).  Their reasoning was similar to
your own, they wanted to provide the functionality which the older
compilers had without such wide open syntax.  It covers the character set
completely, except for space which is already represented by B.

>Mr. Bart is indeed correct; the behavior of "PIC ***,***.ZZ" did indeed
>change, in HMP 3.0 / SSR 44.2.  

Changed without deimplementation warning.

>>A)  The Zs should be insertion characters (the new Unisys interpretation)
>>B)  The Zs should be digit positions (the old Unisys interpretation)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>has been corrected.

Help me date this original interpretation:  When did Unisys first release
a compiler which took Z as an insertion character?


'Twas Wed, 16 Dec 98 10:57:32 -0400, when edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) writes:
>> Now,  Unisys says that it is incorrect to interpret those Zs as digit
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>submitted it.

I can't submit FTRs anymore, those were standardized out of existance many
years ago.  I did file a UCF.  When they defended this change, I appealed
and they stuck to their guns (that was my allusion to the Supreme Court).
Now that they have rejected my appeal it is necessary to go the public
route.  I am not a memeber of Unite, so I started here.

>Also, this kind of sudden change violates Burroughs/Unisys'
>long-standing committment on the A-Series to give two
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>your FTR.

You are correct, this is a violation of the Unisys deimplementation
warning policy.  But they are claiming this is not a deimplementation of
an old feature, but the correction of a bug.  They are saying that the
rule always called for these Zs to be insertion characters, and they were
mearly fixing a bug in the software.  Certainly they don't need to give
two releases of warning before they fix a bug?

But in this case the new interpretation of the rule is simply wrong.  Has
there ever been a Burroughs/Unisys Cobol compiler which took Z as anything
but a digit?  Has there ever been a Burroughs/Unisys Cobol compiler which
took Z as an insertion character?  TTBOMK, there has not; the de facto
rule has always been that Z is always a digit. 

It is a violation of Unisys deimplementation warning policy to change this
de facto rule without warning.

'Twas Thu, 17 Dec 98 10:45:15 -0400, when edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Chuck Stevens argues at length that, because the COBOL68
>and COBOL74 compilers treated the ZZ in ***.ZZ as fixed
>insertion characters, that it was proper to change the
>COBOL85 behavior to match as an FTR correction, without
>warning.

Let me introduce the characters.  I worked for the A Series languages
department form 1985 to 1988.  Chuck was the MVP on COBOL74 before I got
there, and he is Mr COBOL74 to this day.  

Chuck is not arguing that this change was to be compatible with any
previous compiler or release.  The only compatibility argument is that
COBOL85 and COBOL74 are both being changed to be compatible with each
other.  COBOL85 decided on this new interpretation of the insertion
character rule.  Then Chuck changed COBOL74 to be compatible with the
change in COBOL85.  This was the compatibility issue Chuck dealt with.

>  1) In fact COBOL74 did not act as Chuck implies.
>  2) In fact the COBOL74 compiler still (as of 44.2) does
>     not act as Chuck claims.
>  3) The 44.2 COBOL85 compiler does not act as Chuck claims.

We ran into this change on COBOL85 release 44.1A.25.  It was not in the
44.1 base release.  I believe that it was only after I filed a UCF against
COBOL85 that COBOL74 was changed to match.  I don't know if the COBOL74
patch has even been shipped yet.  If it hasn't been shipped, I hope that
we can stop it from shipping.

>  4) The change altered existing compiler behavior suddenly
>     without warning to users, with the result of altering
>     existing user program output to be incorrect but
>     without a syntax error or program fault.

This is going to hit lots of users exactly like it hit us.  The check
writing program had been compiled against the test database without
problem.  Then we needed to install a bug fix release of COBOL85.  At
implementation weekend we compiled the check writer against the new
production database.  When we ran the check writer, we had Zs on our
checks.  It was quickly caught; we were out about a hundred spoiled
checks, and a couple hours of programmer time.

But in a bigger shop, tens of thousands of checks might be spoiled.  In a
more automated shop, those checks might get mailed.  And not just checks,
but bills, 1099s and other statements might be mangled.  That is why it is
important that Unisys fix this before it is released to more users.

>It would be much easier to agree with some of Chuck's
>points if his premises were correct. In particular, there
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>to do a conversion just to stay with the A-Series systems.

In this case the old feature is that Z is always a digit position, never
an insertion character.

>  New COBOL85 treats the ZZ as insert characters when the
>  source is non-zero, but inserts ** when the source is zero!
>  This flatly contradicts Chuck's assertion that the ZZ is
>  now treated as simple insertion characters.

Moving zero to PIC ***.99 results in ***.** not ***.00.  This is correct
ANSI behavior.

<a id="t-3c85299c9a"></a>
## Thread: Age old riddle.. — 8 msgs

### Age old riddle..

- **From:** Mark Celli <nickelbag@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```
Tell me please...
     I am one month away from graduating the Chubb institute here in NJ
and have this question to ask:
How does one get hired without any experience but can't get any
experience without being hired?? This is a career change for me and I
don't have any of the baggage that comes with college(read: I couldn't
afford to go). I'm so 
f***ing frustrated that after 8 months I can't get one of these places
to even give me an interview because I don't have any PRACTICAL
experience. Then in the same breath they complain how they don't want to
pay for experienced programmers?? I don't get it and they sure aren't
helping in the school with the answer to this one.
Not looking for sympathy by the way just some kind of answer other than
"Someone will Eventually take a chance on me".
  Maybe some of you could give a little re-assurance or helpful hints
around this?
                                                 Thanks all,
                                                Mark Celli
```

#### ↳ Re: Age old riddle..

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9%pe2.234$bJ4.447409@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```
Mark Celli wrote:
>  I am one month away from graduating the Chubb institute here in NJ
>and have this question to ask:
>How does one get hired without any experience but can't get any
>experience without being hired??

Mark, you may want to consider offering your services on a contractual
basis, rather than as an employee.  Companies are less reluctant to try
you out on contract, because you can be out the door in a flash if you
don't work out.  And for your first contract, consider going for less
money than you might like.  Also consider taking something which is a
longer commute than you might want.  Is moving an option?  Once you get
some experience, you can look around for something better.
```

#### ↳ Re: Age old riddle..

- **From:** no.spam@no.spam
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367d5ab1.4748758@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```
On Fri, 18 Dec 1998 01:36:47 -0500, Mark Celli
<nickelbag@earthlink.net> wrote:

>Tell me please...
>     I am one month away from graduating the Chubb institute here in NJ
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You've applied at MetLife in Bridgewater or NYC, United HealthCare in
Somerset, and Prudential at Roseland, and *none* of them will talk to
you?  (Other people can probably chime in with additional large COBOL
shops in NJ and NYC that always seem to have droves of
wet-behind-the-ears programmers coming in regardless of recent
layoffs.)  Seems odd although things should loosen up in January when
1999 budgets become effective.  Maybe you need to have someone review
your resume to see if it needs work to become more eye-catching.
```

#### ↳ Re: Age old riddle..

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75evpj$6n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```
In article <3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>,
  nickelbag@earthlink.net wrote:
> Tell me please...
>      I am one month away from graduating the Chubb institute here in NJ
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Mark,

What Judson M. says is true. Try a contract first and a long commute. I'm from
the Island originally and did the LIRR (from Suffolk County) for my first shoe
in the door with GM, 59th and 5th. Got 2 years under my belt and left.

I'm sure you could probably get in on SOME Y2K contract stuff SOMWHERE in the
NY/NJ Metro area for your 1st position or maybe something else. Just keep
hitting the pavement and you'll succeed. Watch the pimps (recruiters). They
can be SLIMEY....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Age old riddle..

- **From:** Rick Overmaat <rover@dds.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367AF7F9.A54435EF@dds.nl>`
- **References:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```
Mark Celli wrote:
> 
> Tell me please...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                 Mark Celli

I'll give you the answer in strategic wording.

First: shoot MORE "bullets" (job applications).

Second: shoot DIFFERENT "munition". Like visiting places where you can
meet personell managers, publishing nice superficial essays on
programming
or whatever your possibilities are. Have nice telephone chats with the
chaps who reject your applications. Ask them for advice. And if you like
to, ask them how they got their first job without practical experience.
And after that, send them a card to thank them. Etcetera.

Third: persevere at the WEAKEST point of the "enemy's frontline".
Once someone might want to hire you, you might be tempted to think:
o boy, this works so easily, it can't be a good company!
(Or even: they must be mad to take a looser like me.)
In fact the company that wants you is your STRONGEST point.

Fourth: WIDEN the attack. Career change? There's no such thing, never
say
that any more! You only increased your possibilities to find a job! And
loving both professions is not adultery ...

Fifth: create FUN. Take a break once a wile, to give yourself the
opportunity to get some distance and regain self esteem, no matter
what the circumstances suggest.

With kind regards,
Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: Age old riddle..

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367dd434.531518505@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```
On Fri, 18 Dec 1998 01:36:47 -0500, Mark Celli
<nickelbag@earthlink.net> wrote:

>Tell me please...
>     I am one month away from graduating the Chubb institute here in NJ
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>around this?

Hi Mark,

Get one of the "Free" compilers - I suggest the Fujitsu v3 starter kit
- and write some programs.  Take them with you to your interviews.
Find a small business (you have a friend with one?) and write some
code to help him/her.  Have them give you a recommendation.  Just a
few ideas.  

Alternately - since you are not getting paid now anyway, ask a company
to take you on as an unpaid intern.  Get some experience that way, get
a good recommendation.  Hope these ideas help!
```

#### ↳ Re: Age old riddle..

- **From:** jraben19@mail.idt.net (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36802265.7996365@news.idt.net>`
- **References:** `<3679F7FF.43970BBF@earthlink.net>`

```

snip

>and have this question to ask:
>How does one get hired without any experience but can't get any
>experience without being hired?? This is a career change for me and I
snip

Use your strengths.  If you have any business experience that shows
that you are honest.. trustworthy    a real boy scout  willing to work
TELL them.  It counts.  

Also, try going to places that can use your prior experience and build
on that.


JR


 
and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

   Approach many consulting companies.  You'll find one that has a sweetheart

<a id="t-415f48ca74"></a>
## Thread: Need ODBC or (better yet) OLE DB data driver/provider for CA-Realia ISAM data — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Need ODBC or (better yet) OLE DB data driver/provider for CA-Realia ISAM data

- **From:** "Tim Cornwell" <Cornwell@ClarityConnect.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75egu9$c$1@remarQ.com>`

```
Does anyone have any experience with either an ODBC or OLE Db data source
for Computer Associates Realia ISAM files?

I am in the process of checking out Parkway-Software's SQL Connect, but
would like some real-world input.

I am (of course) trying to use one of those wacky new programming langs. to
access some tried & true COBOL ISAM data files.

Also, can anyone shed some light on the actual layout of the Realia ISAMs?
Is this proprietary, or does it follow a (relative) standard?

TIA,
Tim Cornwell
```

#### ↳ Re: Need ODBC or (better yet) OLE DB data driver/provider for CA-Realia ISAM data

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75pme5$gkn$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<75egu9$c$1@remarQ.com>`

```

Tim Cornwell wrote in message <75egu9$c$1@remarQ.com>...
>I am (of course) trying to use one of those wacky new programming langs. to
>access some tried & true COBOL ISAM data files.

## You can't.
>
>Also, can anyone shed some light on the actual layout of the Realia ISAMs?
>Is this proprietary, or does it follow a (relative) standard?

## It is proprietary. And secret. Although very effecient.

Best trick is to write a Realia program to read the ISAM file sequentially
and dump it to a fixed-length file which you should be able to read with
another system.
>
```

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need ODBC or (better yet) OLE DB data driver/provider for CA-Realia ISAM data

- **From:** "Tim Cornwell" <Cornwell@ClarityConnect.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75ti2d$s5i$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<75egu9$c$1@remarQ.com> <75pme5$gkn$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```

Jerry Peacock wrote in message <75pme5$gkn$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...
>
>Tim Cornwell wrote in message <75egu9$c$1@remarQ.com>...
>>I am (of course) trying to use one of those wacky new programming langs.
to
>>access some tried & true COBOL ISAM data files.
>
>## You can't.

Parkway software can do it - or so they claim.  EasySoft also claims they
can read/write these files.  I am looking at their solutions now.

>>
>>Also, can anyone shed some light on the actual layout of the Realia ISAMs?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Everyone says it's so f*ing 'effecient'.  What do you mean?

>Best trick is to write a Realia program to read the ISAM file sequentially
>and dump it to a fixed-length file which you should be able to read with
>another system.
>>
Not much of a trick, I'm afraid.  Gigabytes of data files don't lend
themselves to constant duplication very well.

What I'm looking to do is read & write these files directly from other
(non-COBOL) apps.

Any other ideas?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need ODBC or (better yet) OLE DB data driver/provider for CA-Realia ISAM data

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75um1n$bif$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<75egu9$c$1@remarQ.com> <75pme5$gkn$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <75ti2d$s5i$1@remarQ.com>`

```

Tim Cornwell wrote in message <75ti2d$s5i$1@remarQ.com>...
>>>Also, can anyone shed some light on the actual layout of the Realia
ISAMs?
>>>Is this proprietary, or does it follow a (relative) standard?
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In this context efficiency means:
1. MUCH smaller file sizes than competing compilers.
2. MUCH faster access than competing compilers.
3. MUCH faster updating than competing compilers.

>>Best trick is to write a Realia program to read the ISAM file sequentially
>>and dump it to a fixed-length file which you should be able to read with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>themselves to constant duplication very well.

Oops! Sorry. I thought it was a one-time deal.
>
>What I'm looking to do is read & write these files directly from other
>(non-COBOL) apps.
>
>Any other ideas?


Hmmm! Could you write a Realia interface routine, callable from the
non-Cobol application, to perform the Realia ISAM I-O? Otherwise I'm
stumped. Obviously the original designers never contemplated
accessing the database with another tool, otherwise they would not
have chosen a proprietary format.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need ODBC or (better yet) OLE DB data driver/provider for CA-Realia ISAM data
