<a id="t-e1bc21074d"></a>
## Thread: How to put REBUILD.EXE output to STDOUT? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to put REBUILD.EXE output to STDOUT?

- **From:** "J���rgen Ibelgaufts" <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367A1134.995A19CE@gfc-net.de>`

```
Hello,

i have written a batch program (rexx under os/2) to rebuild all isam files in a
directory. the program catches the text output or rebuild.exe into a log file so
that it is possible to read the log and find what rebuild has done with each file.

does anyone know why the hell microfocus changed rebuild.exe in version 4.0 so
that it does not print to stdout anymore so that you cannot catch its output
anymore?

does anybody have a workaround for this?

Thanks in advance

Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

#### ↳ Re: How to put REBUILD.EXE output to STDOUT?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75dc6s$kbn@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<367A1134.995A19CE@gfc-net.de>`

```
My usually reliable sources at Micro Focus say,

"I think the user might just need to setup COBSW.

ie., COBSW=+s5

I hope this works."

J�rgen Ibelgaufts wrote in message <367A1134.995A19CE@gfc-net.de>...
>Hello,
>
>i have written a batch program (rexx under os/2) to rebuild all isam files
in a
>directory. the program catches the text output or rebuild.exe into a log
file so
>that it is possible to read the log and find what rebuild has done with
each file.
>
>does anyone know why the hell microfocus changed rebuild.exe in version 4.0
so
>that it does not print to stdout anymore so that you cannot catch its
output
>anymore?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

## Thread: So What's Happening at PSE&G? — 17 msgs

### So What's Happening at PSE&G?

- **From:** unlisted.to.avoid.spam@anon.lcs.mit.edu ( )
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<19981219003442.RJJ2495@235.new-york-11-12rs.ny.dial-access.att.net>`

```
I worked on the customer system at PSE&G over a dozen years ago.

That system is very large and had a lot of Y2K exposure.  

What approach, if any, has been taken: windowing or field expansion,
or has the system been totally rewritten as was indicated for many,
many non-Y2K reasons?

What else is happening on the customer system project?
```

#### ↳ Re: So What's Happening at PSE&G?

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10e4e20ba47c0a679896e4@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19981219003442.RJJ2495@235.new-york-11-12rs.ny.dial-access.att.net>`

```
In article <19981219003442.RJJ2495@235.new-york-11-12rs.ny.dial-
access.att.net>, unlisted.to.avoid.spam@anon.lcs.mit.edu says...
> I worked on the customer system at PSE&G over a dozen years ago.

Nice. 

> 
> That system is very large and had a lot of Y2K exposure.
 
That's good to know.  
 
> 
> What approach, if any, has been taken: windowing or field expansion,
> or has the system been totally rewritten as was indicated for many,
> many non-Y2K reasons?

Nope, this is how it went.
 
It was late one night,  and everyone was hard at work trying to figure 
out how to solve this vexing year 2000 problem. Suddenly, the room was 
awash in a strange glowing light that seemed to fill every nook and 
cranny of the office. Well, before you know it they all were swept up to 
a space ship where each and every one of them were administered a very 
painful anal probe. 

Well, after ET had finished butt fucking them without the aid of K-Y 
jelly he figured that, hey maybe I should by flowers or something. Better 
yet, he would help them fix their Y2K problem. Unfortunately, good old ET 
would not tell them how it was done. Well, he would but it would have a 
cost no one was willing to pay.

> 
> What else is happening on the customer system project?
He/she was the cost to find out how ET fixed the Y2K problem! I don't 
want to think about what is happening to them right now, it just makes me 
shudder.

> 

Hey pal, if you ran into a wall face first nine times in a row and it 
hurt like hell do you have to try a tenth time to see if it still hurts 
or not??  
```

<a id="t-e110105ceb"></a>
## Thread: Trailing Zs in PIC — 3 msgs

### Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 1998-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<01010064.9vi2r3@titipu.paleo.org>`

```
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) writes:
> In Medium Systems COBOLV(68) we used = or _
> in phone numbers because - wasn't allowed.  Then the insertion rule was
> expanded to allow - as an insertion character

That goes much farther back on A-Series, probably to the
early 1970s. I can't remember it ever *not* being available
there, though my memory of things 25 years ago is rather
less than perfect.

> I can't submit FTRs anymore, those were standardized out of existance many
> years ago.  I did file a UCF.

Every UCF is subcategorized as either an FTR or an NFS,
though it's true that in common lingo UCF usually means
FTR. I was involved in the CUBE/UNITE NFS handling for
several years, and I think this confusion is one reason the
number of NFSs from the field has declined dramatically. To
submit a New Feature Suggestion you must file a UCF, and
because people think a UCF is an FTR, they don't know where
to file an NFS. Now I'll get my face out fo the alphabet
soup.

> Certainly they don't need to give
> two releases of warning before they fix a bug?

If it's been in existence so long that users are using it,
counting on the existing behavior, then yes they do need to
give two releases of warnings. It doesn't matter that the
documentation says it works a different way. We judge and
debug our program output by the actual behavior of our
programs, not by the documented behavior.

In contrast, they can change the documentation any time
without warning. As long as it's only a content change, no
one will complain. (Note the specification of content
change. When they made documentation unavailable on
character-mode terminals without warning, there were some
very loud complaints.)

> >  New COBOL85 treats the ZZ as insert characters when the
> >  source is non-zero, but inserts ** when the source is zero!
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANSI behavior.

Thanks for the explanation.

However, my point is that in 44.2 COBOL85, moving 0 to a
PIC ***.ZZ field results in output of ***.**. This is NOT
fixed insertion behavior for the ZZ. Thus in a ***.ZZ
picture, the ZZ is now treated as a fixed insertion
character (as Chuck claims) when the source is non-zero,
but treated as a digit when the source is zero (contrary to
Chuck's claim that it is now a fixed insertion character).

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<367b1361.3540959@netnews>`
- **References:** `<01010064.9vi2r3@titipu.paleo.org>`

```
'Twas Fri, 18 Dec 98 08:52:43 -0400, when edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>> I can't submit FTRs anymore, those were standardized out of existance many
>> years ago.  I did file a UCF.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>FTR.

Back when UCFs were called FCFs (and I was trying to fix them) there was a
boxes on the FCF for FTR or NFS or some other things.  But on the online
UCF form I have never seen the letters FTR.

>> >  New COBOL85 treats the ZZ as insert characters when the
>> >  source is non-zero, but inserts ** when the source is zero!
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>fixed insertion behavior for the ZZ.

I don't have a copy of ANSI X3.23-1985, but I do have a copy of the
current ISO draft.  

P 326, 13.17.38.4 (8):  "If the value is zero and the suppression symbol
is '*', the entire data item, including insertion editing symbols except
the actual decimal point, will be '*'.  In this case the actual decimal
point will appear in the item."

That *should* be the same as Cobol-85.  IIRC, the rule has been all stars
but the decimal point since ANSI X3.23-1974.

BTW, the compiler served up a humorus warning today.  I was scanning thru
a program with over 100 truncation warnings, and I found one that I won't
blame on the programmer.  One of a long string of datanames was flagged
for a trunction warning.  I scanned up to the begining of the statement;
it was MOVE ZERO!

It seems the field was PIC V9(4), which isn't enough digits to hold ZERO.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** slurn@rmovengr.sgi.com (Scott Lurndal)
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<75mngl$59nvc@fido.engr.sgi.com>`
- **References:** `<01010064.9vi2r3@titipu.paleo.org> <367b1361.3540959@netnews>`

```
In article <367b1361.3540959@netnews>, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) writes:

|> >
|> >However, my point is that in 44.2 COBOL85, moving 0 to a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
|> point will appear in the item."

I have ANSI X3.23-1985 here, and while this paragraph is indeed part of
1985, it is only part of the relevent text which includes a prior paragraph
on the user of 'Z' as a zero suppression character, and is followed by 

(9):  The symbols '+', '-', '*', 'Z', and the currency symbol, when
      used as floating replacement characters, are mutually exclusive 
      within a given character-string. 

The only 'correct' action for a conforming COBOL85 compiler then
would be to reject PIC ***.ZZ as syntactically incorrect.

(Additional text states:

Each 'Z' in a character-string may only be used to represent the
leftmost leading numeric character positions which will be replaced by
a space character when the content of that character position is a
leading zero.  
)

The 12 Days of an Enlightened Christmas
--> author unknown


On the 12th day of the Eurocentrically imposed midwinter festival, my
Significant Other in a consenting, adult, monogamous relationship gave to
me:

TWELVE males reclaiming their inner warrior through ritual drumming,

ELEVEN pipers piping (plus the 18-member pit orchestra made up of
members in good standing of the Musicians Equity Union, as called for in
their union contract, even though they will not be asked to play a note),

TEN melanin deprived testosterone-poisoned scions of the patriarchal
ruling class system leaping,

NINE persons engaged in rhythmic self-expression,

EIGHT economically disadvantaged female persons stealing milk-products
from enslaved Bovine-Americans,

SEVEN endangered swans swimming on federally protected wetlands,

SIX enslaved Fowl-Americans producing stolen non-human animal products,

FIVE golden symbols of culturally sanctioned enforced domestic
incarceration,

(NOTE: after members of the Animal Liberation Front threatened to throw red
paint at my computer, the calling birds, French hens and partridge have been
reintroduced to their native habitat. To avoid further Animal-American
enslavement, the remaining gift package has been revised.)

FOUR hours of recorded whale songs,

THREE deconstructionist poets,

TWO Sierra Club calendars printed on recycled processed tree carcasses
and

ONE Spotted Owl activist chained to an old-growth pear tree.

Merry Christmas, Happy Chanukah. Good Kwanzaa. Blessed Yule.
Happy Holidays! (unless otherwise prohibited by law) *

*Unless, of course, you are suffering from Seasonally Affected Disorder
(SAD). If this be the case, please substitute this gratuitous call for
celebration with suggestion that you have a thoroughly adequate day.
```

<a id="t-c807e85898"></a>
## Thread: EXTERNAL data and maintainability — 16 msgs

### EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In response to a note that I sent a while ago, one person said to NEVER
recommend the use of the EXTERNAL attribute (for Working-Storage items -
this note/issue has nothing to do with EXTERNAL files which have quite
different uses).  The reason that they gave was that such items could cause
serious maintenance problems.

As someone who is not currently developing and maintaining ANSI'85 programs,
I was wondering what others' opinions were on this.  Have you used EXTERNAL
working-storage data and if so, did this cause maintenance problems?  If so,
what guidelines (if any) would you have on when the problems out-weigh the
advantages?

Just curious about this and thought it might be an interesting thread.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** B.Ewert@t-online.de (Bernd Ewert)
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75h47j$2t1$1@news05.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I use EXTERNAL in my programs very often and I didnï¿½t find any maintenance
problems. It is just the opposite. I use EXTERNAL with to restrictions:
  -  EXTERNAL groups are only allowed in COPY-members. This shall help to
avoid multiple
     definitions of external groups.
  -  No part of local or global data shall have the same dataname like a
part of the external group.
     This shall help to avoid the question "which datafield is it ??"
All my programs are automaticly checked if they match the rules.
Mostly I use EXTERNAL for tables and their related information (maxsize)
that must be accessed in different programs.
In critical programs EXTERNAL may be a security problem. Every program in
the run unit can access the EXTERNAL data area if it knows the name and the
size of the group.




William M. Klein schrieb in Nachricht <75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>...
>In response to a note that I sent a while ago, one person said to NEVER
>recommend the use of the EXTERNAL attribute (for Working-Storage items -
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>As someone who is not currently developing and maintaining ANSI'85
programs,
>I was wondering what others' opinions were on this.  Have you used EXTERNAL
>working-storage data and if so, did this cause maintenance problems?  If
so,
>what guidelines (if any) would you have on when the problems out-weigh the
>advantages?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>[...]  Have you used EXTERNAL
>working-storage data and if so, did this cause maintenance problems?  If
so,
>what guidelines (if any) would you have on when the problems out-weigh the
>advantages?

Yes, I use EXTERNAL data. No, I have not had any maintenance
problems as a result of using EXTERNAL data.

I am in the process of updating a system to which I will be adding some
EXTERNAL working-storage data. The EXTERNAL data will consist of
values that do not change during the use of the application, i.e.,
configuration data and security information. These values are set at the
start-up of the application and remain the same through its termination.

I am also removing some EXTERNAL data and placing it into
programs as constants. In eight years, the data has never changed.
It was thought that it might vary among customers.

I have primarily used EXTERNAL data to hold program names. This
has allowed me to customize the system with far less concern for the
distribution of the application. Every customer receives the same
distribution. Customizations are sent separately. At start-up, the
application searches for a custom library. If a library is found, it calls
a program within the library to change the program names in the
EXTERNAL data. During execution, the core programs will call the
custom programs, rather than the standard programs. Again, these
values do not change during the execution of the application.

Since I have not experienced any problems, I am not certain when
the problems would out weigh the advantages. However, it seems
that using EXTERNAL data as configuration constants is probably
the least controversial use.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
G'day,
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> William M. Klein wrote in message <75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

You are right passing configuration data is probably one of the least
contraversial.  What is the problem with creating a low level routine
that retrieves the configuration and then returns the information
(through pointers or whatever) to all subroutines.

I notice that the people commenting say 'I did it' and "I don't have a
problem maintaining it".  I am more concerned about the new blood coming
in, will they know that the data in the high level program is
miraculously transformed after a call to a file read routine.   If the
data is required then an explicit passing of the information is the
order of the day.  I regularly read arguments that 'cobol was meant to
be readable',  this is one of the cases where extra typing certainly
pays off.  By the way new blood might have 20 solid years of
programming.

I have coded C for about 20 odd years and every serious book on C
programming states to eliminate the implicit passing of external
variables.  C the unreadable speaks!

Thanks
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75oeqb$mn9$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com> <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au>...
>G'day,
>Rick Smith wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
[,,,]
>>
>> Since I have not experienced any problems, I am not certain when
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>(through pointers or whatever) to all subroutines.

It is not my style to introduce unecessary complications into the
contruction of systems. :-)

>I notice that the people commenting say 'I did it' and "I don't have a
>problem maintaining it".  I am more concerned about the new blood coming
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>programming.

All EXTERNAL data items are "explicitly" declared in the
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION of the program using that data.

Why is that not sufficient for "new blood"?

There is a distinct difference between data that is the subject (or
object) of a process and data that is used to augment that
process. I stated that I use EXTERNAL for configuration constants
and security information.

How does, for example, the color of screen text or the type of
printer affect the processing (or transformation) of data?

>I have coded C for about 20 odd years and every serious book on C
>programming states to eliminate the implicit passing of external
>variables.  C the unreadable speaks!

The first serious book on C programming, "The C Programming
Language, Kernighan and Ritchie, 1978," does not appear to make
any such recommendation! In fact, in the C language, all data is
external by default. This has probably resulted in some unfortunate
experiences, which may have lead "some" authors make an
"extreme" recommendation. :-)

It is possible to distinguish between a variable and a parameter-less
function in C by the presence of parentheses after the function name.

After J4 eliminates the parentheses after parameter-less functions,
how will one distinguish between a variable name and function name,
in COBOL-200X?

In other words, could I be using, in COBOL-85, EXTERNAL
variables to emulate parameter-less functions?
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com> <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au> <75oeqb$mn9$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> All EXTERNAL data items are "explicitly" declared in the
> WORKING-STORAGE SECTION of the program using that data.
> 
> Why is that not sufficient for "new blood"?

In a a large system with many module calls where is the external
variable used.  If you have 100 calls in the program to different
routines which ones actually use or change the values, what about if
called modules have modules that use the external when intermediate call
does not.

What about when you buy a set of precompiled cobol support modules and
they use external variables and you use the same name by chance.

As you stated before your case is reasonably straight forward.  One
process would create the data and many other would simply reference it.
The optimal idea here is to enforce that 'read only' attribute
automatically by the compiler.  Is this the case?

This is possibly one of the most difficult arguments for Cobol
programmers to accept because data in Cobol is global to the program /
call that you work on and most programs written are monolithic not
modular.  If you want to write modular code then consider all the
benefits of modular logic including explicit documentation of what
routine use what data.

> 
> There is a distinct difference between data that is the subject (or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If the data you are passing is not read only, then the where it is
updated become important.  I was giving an alternative solution to the
problem of accessing a single set of configuration parameters rather
than using external.  This is probably not the right application of
external to use as a base for this discussion, something complex and
many updates of data such as a trading system.

A compiler cannot optimize for external variables where a call is in the
middle of a loop for example.  If the call is explicit and the variable
is not external then the compiler can work out some performance
improvements.

> How does, for example, the color of screen text or the type of
> printer affect the processing (or transformation) of data?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> "extreme" recommendation. :-)

I think that you would call K&R the reference text not how to write good
C code.

Writing Solid Code page 155, Steve Maguire  'Never pass global buffers
unless you absolutely have to'.  Extreme, maybe especially if you read
the section that says that after you modify/create code you should step
through it with a debugger, worth reading his arguments about it though.

I have spoken to many programmers who code C for a living and they all
(rather quite a few) state that over using global causes unmaintainable
code. 

> It is possible to distinguish between a variable and a parameter-less
> function in C by the presence of parentheses after the function name.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> in COBOL-200X?

This is interesting and worth following up.  If you look at the design
of Eiffel this is exactly the way it works, you honestly do not 'know'
whether a value used is a function or a value of the object.  This is
actually beneficial when you can substitute a calculated variable by a
routine to calculate the value without any code modification except
writing the function.  (Although likely the function would use external
data :->)

> In other words, could I be using, in COBOL-85, EXTERNAL
> variables to emulate parameter-less functions?
> -------------------------------
> Rick Smith
> e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>does not.

There is no problem with this. It is neither defined nor referenced
in the intermediate modules.

>What about when you buy a set of precompiled cobol support modules and
>they use external variables and you use the same name by chance.

1. If the vendor fails to inform me that EXTERNAL variables are used,
    the vendor is at fault.
2. If the vendor fails to supply a list of EXTERNAL variables, the
    vendor is at fault.
3. If the vendor supplies an incomplete list of EXTERNAL variable,
    the vendor is at fault.
4. If I fail to coerce the vendor into supplying a list, then it is my
    fault.
5. If I fail to read a list that was supplied, then it is my fault.

In any case, I get to fix the problem.

>As you stated before your case is reasonably straight forward.  One
>process would create the data and many other would simply reference it.
>The optimal idea here is to enforce that 'read only' attribute
>automatically by the compiler.  Is this the case?

Obviously not, but I am willing to accept the non-optimal conditions
because it simplifys the system.

[...]
>
>I have spoken to many programmers who code C for a living and they all
>(rather quite a few) state that over using global causes unmaintainable
>code.

The key phase is "over use". This is not the same as never!

Incidently, I, like so many others, also code in C for a living! I write
embedded control programs for communicating, appliance-like
devices that run 24/7 for years without so much as a burp.

An observation:

There are certain phases, which, when used in some newsgroups,
may be construed as insulting. I would guess that most readers
of this group code "for a living." Yet, we may still disagree.

The insult occurs through the relationship:

[By definition] Those who code "for a living" are professionals.
    Professionals say "Do not use global variables."
    You use global variables.
[By implication] Therefore, you are not a professional.

Since I am a professional, I could contrue your comment as an
insult. I will accept that it was unintentional since, neither of us
writes to newsgroups "for a living."

If, in fact, you do write to newsgroups "for a living," I apologise.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3685E51D.597D15A8@zip.com.au>`

```
Rick

Sorry if I offended, I don't consider myself to 'code C for a living'
just toying with it for 20 years does not equal 10 hour days on cobol
and therefore I reference more authoritative though unnamed sources.
This is 'new stuff' for the cobol language and therefore I referenced
sources which had experienced 'global' variables across modules, i.e. C
coders. This is a difficult concept to get across to people especially
where they have only experience with  monolithic Cobol which only has
global variables within a module.

The problem with beginners is that every occurrence where it is just
plain easier to code a certain way must be the exception to the rule. 
It takes experience to understand when to break the rules.  If you make
it a rule and then accept arguments for exceptions then the person
breaking the rule has to feel strongly about the particular instance to
make the case not just because it is easier.  (Must remember to write
this one into my procedures :->)

It might be interesting to follow up on the concept of name spaces in
C++ as this is where the idea of naming conflicts come from.  Once we
get into multiple application modules and the conflicts of names across
the suites the problem will rear it's ugly head.  It could occur in
internal systems as much as vendor bought products.  Has anyone got a
perfectly documented internal system?

Thanks
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Also a laughable Y2K problem (was: Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<768drh$7e8$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com> <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au> <75oeqb$mn9$1@server.cntfl.com> <3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au> <75r59d$mrn$1@server.cntfl.com> <3685E51D.597D15A8@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <3685E51D.597D15A8@zip.com.au>...
>Rick
>
[...]
>This is 'new stuff' for the cobol language and therefore I referenced
>sources which had experienced 'global' variables across modules, i.e. C
>coders.

Why not find some FORTRAN coders who have experience with
blank COMMON? ;-)

I found one such coder and I regret having had the misfortune
to maintain her program.

Tenatively scheduled for conversion to COBOL, summer of 99:
a FORTRAN program written in C. This particular program has
mutable data as 'extern' (i.e., blank common) and immutable
configuration data passed as multiple arrays (typical of
FORTRAN); rather than as an array of structures (typical of C).

Among the changes, convert the multiple arrays of immutable
configuration data to a single record with arrays of records to
hold the various configuration items and allocate the whole thing
as EXTERNAL. This eliminates four parameters from each of
more than 100 (function) ENTRYs; thereby simplifying the system.
The mutable data will be moved from 'extern' into subprograms.
The C source level 'static' data will be made GLOBAL, the C
'extern' functions will be made ENTRY, and the C 'static' functions
will become COMMON single-level nested programs.

I have determined that, by using this technique, the conversion
will be nearly mechanical. This is important because most local
variables have names like i1, i2, and i3 for integers; z1, z2, and
z3 for double; and istr1, istr2, and istr3 for character strings. A
few actually have more reasonable names! Also, as is typical
of FORTRAN programs there is a sprinkling of 'go to label'
in many of the C functions. Of course all of the function names
are 6 characters.

Approximately 1000 lines of this FORTRAN-C source is
unavailable due to a contract/licensing dispute and the C library
database, which cannot be upgraded for the same reason, has
a few bugs. Without recreating the missing code, the system
can not be made Y2K-ready.

I would be unconcerned with this and would simply discontinue
the system since a Y2K-ready replacement is available and
has been offered to all the customers. Some, of the more than
3000 users, have refused to upgrade to the replacement for the
most interesting of reasons: The replacement system requires
Microsoft Windows and there is no way they are going to put
Windows on their machines. (The C program uses MS-DOS).
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76bkk0$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com> <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au> <75oeqb$mn9$1@server.cntfl.com> <3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au>...
  <much snippage>
>>
>> After J4 eliminates the parentheses after parameter-less functions,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Let's clear up what is in the draft (and some of what is still being worked
on for the draft)

A) The word "function" will still be supported before BOTH intrinsic
functions and user-defined functions (hence no confusion with data names)

B) The word "function" will be optional before intrinsic functions IF AND
ONLY IF
  - there is a declaration in the REPOSITORY paragraph requesting it
  - the same COBOL word is NOT used as a user-defined word any where in the
compilation group

C) The word "function" will be optional before a user-defined option IF ANY
ONLY IF
  - the same COBOL word is NOT used as a user-defined word any where in the
compilation group
    and
 - either there is a function proto-type or a repository paragraph entry for
it (I am saying this based on my memory - there may actually be slightly
different rules - but they boil down to "declaring" which user-defined
functions you plan on using in your program)

D)  The original statement about "J4 eliminates the parentheses after
parameter-less functions" is quite confusing.  In fact J4 is *adding* the
OPTION of including parenthesis for parameter-less functions.  Today, it
would be illegal to say
    Move Function Current-Date () to ABC
In the next Standard this will be allowed (along with the upwardly
compatible syntax without any parenthesis)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368ad9f1.108464242@netnews>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com> <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au> <75oeqb$mn9$1@server.cntfl.com> <3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au> <76bkk0$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 29 Dec 1998 15:17:19 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>D)  The original statement about "J4 eliminates the parentheses after
>parameter-less functions" is quite confusing.  In fact J4 is *adding* the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>compatible syntax without any parenthesis)

The optional parentheses were added to resolve this syntactic ambiguity:

ADD FUNCTION RANDOM (FIELDA * FIELDB) TO FIELDC

Because of the parentheses, (FIELDA * FIELDB) is taken as the parameter to
RANDOM.  If this is not what is intended, RANDOM can be specified with a
null parameter list in parentheses:

ADD FUNCTION RANDOM () (FIELDA * FIELDB) TO FIELDC
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76f3u4$6id@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<75g543$ja6@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <75ha2p$3mk$1@server.cntfl.com> <367E4C0D.DB88DFE@zip.com.au> <75oeqb$mn9$1@server.cntfl.com> <3680D6C7.555156B6@zip.com.au> <76bkk0$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <368ad9f1.108464242@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <368ad9f1.108464242@netnews>...
>'Twas Tue, 29 Dec 1998 15:17:19 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>l    |/ MS^7=6/28/107   http://members.aol.com/PanicYr00/Sequence.html

The original example had a problem in that EITHER way it used either an
arithmetic expression (e.g. FieldA * FieldB) or a FUNCTION as a parameter to
the ADD statement - which is a "No-No" in all Standard variations of COBOL.
A better example of the problem is

Compute Out-Field = Function Max (Function Random (FieldA * FieldB))

The question is whether there are one or two parameters passed to the MAX
function.  The (existing) interpretation is that the parenthesis around
(FieldA * FieldB) *automatically* make it a parameter to the Random
function - and hence this is a single parameter passed to Max (which I think
can take an arithmetic expression as input - or otherwise my example is just
as bogus as the original one).

And of course it never really WAS ambiguous - it was just not what some
people wanted.  If you wanted the semantics of the new "empty parameter
list" feature, you can (already) in '85 Standard say

Compute Out-Field = Function Max ((Function Random) (FieldA * FieldB))

and force Function Max to get 2 parameters.  But in the next Standard, you
will also be able to say (as a synonym for this 2nd example and not the
first one)

Compute Out-Field = Function Max (Function Random() (FieldA * FieldB))

Which saves you EXACTLY *zero* keystrokes - but which seems more natural to
some  (I think primarily those who have worked with other languages)
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>

```
We use EXTERNAL extensively in Casegen generated applications and I
appreciate your concern about keeping the programs in sync. Once (before I
put all EXTERNAL variables in a COPY book) I defined the same variable as
EXTERNAL in two programs in the same run unit but with different attributes.
One of the definitions may have been PIC X(25) and the other PIC S9(9) COMP.

The Fujitsu compiler runtime picked up this error and even named the
variable, so it was easy to resolve!

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd
www.casegen.co.uk

#### ↳ Re: EXTERNAL data and maintainability

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> In response to a note that I sent a while ago, one person said to NEVER
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I think the problem is in not recognizing variables as External by
sight.  I have found the same problem in VB - differentiating between
global, form-level,  and procedure-level variables, and the best
solution I have encountered is in using a prefix to indicate the scope
of the variable. Perhaps in Cobol, using 'Ext-' or 'WS-Ext' as a prefix,
would eliminate this confusion.

Bob 
```

#### ↳ Re: Still having problem...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

```
How was the indexed file created?  This program is opening it as INPUT - so
something must have created it already.  If you tried to create it in any
way OTHER than from another COBOL program, then you probably will have
problems opening it (because the internal structure won't be correct).  Try
writing a "simple" program to read a sequential file in - and write an
indexed file out.  Then try running your program that reads it in and see if
that works.

Thane Hubbell wrote:
>

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> that in your program.  Can you tell us how you indexed the data?

This is how I did it from another program:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. SECTIONS.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT SECTIONS-INPUT-FILE ASSIGN TO 'SECTIONS.DAT'
        ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     SELECT SECTIONS-INDX-FILE ASSIGN TO 'INDXSEC.DAT'
        ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
        ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
        RECORD KEY IS INDX-SECTION-NUM
        ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS INDX-COURSE-NUM WITH DUPLICATES.
*
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD SECTIONS-INPUT-FILE     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 SECTIONS-INPUT-REC.
    05 INPUT-SECTION-NUM            PIC X(4).
    05 INPUT-COURSE-NUM             PIC X(6).
 FD SECTIONS-INDX-FILE      LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 SECTIONS-INDX-REC.
    05 INDX-SECTION-NUM             PIC X(4).
    05 INDX-COURSE-NUM              PIC X(6).
    05 INDX-NUM-STUDENTS            PIC 99.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 WS-WORK-AREA.
    05 MORE-RECORDS                 PIC X VALUE 'Y'.
       88 NO-MORE-RECORDS                 VALUE 'N'.
*
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
****************************************************************
* This is the main procedure.                                  *
* It opens the files and reads the input file.                 *
****************************************************************
 100-MAIN.
     OPEN INPUT SECTIONS-INPUT-FILE
          OUTPUT SECTIONS-INDX-FILE
     PERFORM UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS
         READ SECTIONS-INPUT-FILE
             AT END
                 MOVE 'N' TO MORE-RECORDS
             NOT AT END
                 PERFORM 200-CREATE-RTN
         END-READ
     END-PERFORM
     PERFORM 300-END-OF-JOB-RTN
     STOP RUN.
****************************************************************
* This procedure writes to the indexed file and                *
* checks for invalid data.                                     *
****************************************************************
 200-CREATE-RTN.
     MOVE INPUT-SECTION-NUM TO INDX-SECTION-NUM
     MOVE INPUT-COURSE-NUM TO INDX-COURSE-NUM
     MOVE 00 TO INDX-NUM-STUDENTS
     WRITE SECTIONS-INDX-REC
         INVALID KEY DISPLAY 'INVALID RECORD'
     END-WRITE.
****************************************************************
* Closes files.                                                *
****************************************************************
 300-END-OF-JOB-RTN.
     CLOSE SECTIONS-INPUT-FILE
           SECTIONS-INDX-FILE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Still having problem...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75lcun$nu@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<367C2257.757228E5@usa.net> <367ddb75.533376187@news1.ibm.net> <367DE3E4.4AF8004B@usa.net>`

```
I think you defined an alternate key when you defined (created as OUTPUT)
your file, but not when you  read (OPEN INPUT) the file in the other
program.  Some systems allow this while others do not.  Try making certain
that both your FD and your SELECT/ASSIGN statements are identical in the
program that creates the file and the one that reads it.

Quan Ngo wrote in message <367DE3E4.4AF8004B@usa.net>...
>
>
   <snip>
>     SELECT SECTIONS-INDX-FILE ASSIGN TO 'INDXSEC.DAT'
>        ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>*
   <xnip>
> FD SECTIONS-INDX-FILE      LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
> 01 SECTIONS-INDX-REC.
>    05 INDX-SECTION-NUM             PIC X(4).
>    05 INDX-COURSE-NUM              PIC X(6).
>    05 INDX-NUM-STUDENTS            PIC 99.
 <more snip>
```

You may not set the access mode 'ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL' when you read it as an
indexed file.

Try to change it with 'access is random' or 'access is dynamic'.

#### ↳ Re: Still having problem...

- **From:** jraben19@mail.idt.net (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00

Quan Ngo <le_spaceboy@usa.net> wrote:

>Hi, I'm starting to really hate indexed files...
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The file specs for this reading program must be exactly like the
program that created the index file.  The index file structure is not
a line-seq file and has keys and links.

JR


and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: Still having problem...

- **From:** veronem@aol.com (Veronem)
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981224094103.00854.00001210@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<367C2257.757228E5@usa.net>`

```
Since the key of '1111' does not exist in your file and you have not used an
invalid key statement on your read, you have errored out.  I suggest you use a
START to start where the key is great than '1111'.  or simple eliminate setting
the key and using  NEXT RECORD with your read, that is if you want to read thru
the entire file sequentially.  

Hope this helps,
Mary Veronesi

>
>I created this test program just to see how to access an indexed file
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>see some very weird characters... is that normal???
```

<a id="t-e50796118f"></a>
## Thread: Trailing Zs in PIC — 1 msgs

### Re: Trailing Zs in PIC

- **From:** edward@paleo.org (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 1998-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<01010064.a283ok@titipu.paleo.org>`

```
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) writes:
> P 326, 13.17.38.4 (8):  "If the value is zero and the suppression symbol
> is '*', the entire data item, including insertion editing symbols except
> the actual decimal point, will be '*'.  In this case the actual decimal
> point will appear in the item."

Oops. Sorry to add to the confusion on that point.

To summarize what remains, which is most of the case:

  1) This is a major change to compiler behavior, which
     requires advance warnings to users.

  2) The COBOL74 has not recently (perhaps never)
     treated a trailing ZZ as fixed insert characters.

Edward Reid
```

## Thread: fujitsu cobol v3 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### fujitsu cobol v3

- **From:** "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie>
- **Date:** 1998-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75jcvd$pdh10@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
Can anyone tell me why fujitsu cobol v3 won't accept  "working-storage
section"?  And if you know, is there an alternative?
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol v3

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981220130701.06806.00004188@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<75jcvd$pdh10@spock.tinet.ie>`

```

In article <75jcvd$pdh10@spock.tinet.ie>, "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie>
writes:

>Can anyone tell me why fujitsu cobol v3 won't accept  "working-storage
>section"?  And if you know, is there an alternative?

All COBOL compilers _should_ accept WORKING-STORAGE SECTION. immediately after
the DATA DIVISION. header. Both should start in Area A. (most compilers will
accept column 8 through 11 as Area A.)

Mark A. Young
```

- **Date:** 1998-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367d4bd8.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<75jcvd$pdh10@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
Mark Reid wrote in message <75jcvd$pdh10@spock.tinet.ie>...
>Can anyone tell me why fujitsu cobol v3 won't accept  "working-storage
>section"?  And if you know, is there an alternative?


Depending on compiler settings, you may have to use UPPER CASE:
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, but then everything else in lower
case won't be legal either. Post your program.
```

## Thread: Calling from COBOL program to C program and vice versa. — 5 msgs

### Calling from COBOL program to C program and vice versa.

- **From:** saarg@hotmail.com (Saar Ginzburski)
- **Date:** 1998-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367e62ef.6939458@soint1>`

```
Hi,

We are trying to link a Cobol program, which call a C program, which
call another Cobol program.

The link command we are using is for creating an RTS file in order to
debug the parent Cobol program.

We keep getting the error message:
ld: Duplicate symbol "SYMBOL_NAME" in files C_LIBRARY.a(Cfile.o) and
/var/tmp/cob24453/%cob0.o

SYMBOL_NAME is the name of the function from within her we are calling
the Cobol program.
C_LIBRARY.a is a library containing all my C objects.
Cfile.o is the name of the object where the SYMBOL_NAME exist.

Does anyone have any idea ?

Thanks in advance,
Saar Ginzburski,
Amdocs (Israel) Ltd.
saarg@amdocs.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling from COBOL program to C program and vice versa.

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75mk0j$7k3$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<367e62ef.6939458@soint1>`

```
In article <367e62ef.6939458@soint1>,
Saar Ginzburski <saarg@hotmail.com> wrote:
> We are trying to link a Cobol program, which call a C
> program, which call another Cobol program.  The link
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the object where the SYMBOL_NAME exist.

You didn't tell us what COBOL compiler you are using or
what platform.  Also show us the commands you are using
to build the objects, archives, and executables.  If you
are using Micro Focus under UNIX or Windows NT, you need
to link using the Micro Focus compile/link commands to
build your executables.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling from COBOL program to C program and vice versa.

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3680E489.74BB04DC@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<367e62ef.6939458@soint1>`

```
Saar Ginzburski wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does anyone have any idea ?

This appears to be Micro Focus COBOL - if it's not then what I will
say obviously does not apply!

The "%cob0.o" file in the temporary directory is a file that "cob"
creates which contains stub routines to handle various options relating
to mapping symbol names, dynamic loading of symbols (eg as .gnt) etc.

I think you must have an option on the "cob" command line, something
like "-d SYMBOL_NAME" or "-m SYMBOL_NAME=OTHER_SYMBOL_NAME". It's
difficult to tell without the exact command line you are using, but
that's my guess.

What you could also do is grab the %cob0.o file and dissasemble it
(assuming it's created directly - if not, there will be a %cob0.s file
you can grab instead). That may provide more clues (you have to be
quick, though - the file gets removed when "cob" has finished).

Cheers,
Kev.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling from COBOL program to C program and vice versa.

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75rjej$4to$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<367e62ef.6939458@soint1> <3680E489.74BB04DC@dial.pipex.com>`

```
In article <3680E489.74BB04DC@dial.pipex.com>,
Kevin Digweed  <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com> wrote:
>
>What you could also do is grab the %cob0.o file and dissasemble it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You can use the undocumented -DDD parameter to tell
cob not to remove its temporary directory and files,
in addition to displaying all the commands it issues.
```

## Thread: RM COBOL Question — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM COBOL Question

- **From:** Jessica Corbin <jessica_corbin@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367E583C.54106D6D@hotmail.com>`

```
I wonder if you can help me. I need to find out if RM COBOL can use
shared memory areas in the same way that MF COBOL can ?
If not, I need to find out how easy/difficult it is to pass data from RM
COBOL to a C program which can then handle the shared memory area.

The reason for all this is that I need a way to access data which
currently being written and updated by RM COBOL. I can't use a converted
copy of the data because it changes all the time. I can't convert
everything to MF because we don't have access to the sources of the
application. We want to create some web enquiry systems using NetExpress
but need to access the RM data.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM COBOL Question

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-9SqM6Jp1GV5d@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<367E583C.54106D6D@hotmail.com>`

```
On Mon, 21 Dec 1998 14:16:28, Jessica Corbin 
<jessica_corbin@hotmail.com> wrote:

> I wonder if you can help me. I need to find out if RM COBOL can use
> shared memory areas in the same way that MF COBOL can ?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You apparently do have an RM COBOL compiler.  But I don't think you 
need it.  Liant makes an ODBC tool that will allow you to access the 
RM data via ODBC - which you CAN do from your MicroFocus program.  So,
I suggest contacting Liant, asking them about their ODBC tool and then
using it to access the RM native data from your MicroFocus NetExpress 
product.

-------------------------
Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

Albert Ratzlaff

Possibly it's a bit late ... but here we go:

<a id="t-6c0bf82208"></a>
## Thread: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

- **From:** lamas@pathcom.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367f0c8c.3021698@news.pathcom.com>`

```
Do anyone has any insight to the following problem:

Three variables are defined in the working storage like the following:

01 RECORD-1.
    05 A 	pic s9(7) comp-3.
    05 B	pic 9(17) comp-3.
    05 C	pic s9(5) comp-3.

The content of A = X'1234567C', 

The content of B was put into the record incorrectly by another
program as a signed packed decimal field.

Therefore, The content of B = X'12345678901234567C'  instead of
X'12345678901234567F'

The content of C = X'12345C'

The program checks the field in the following statements:

IF A IS NUMERIC
   PERFORM A-ACTION
END-IF.

IF B IS NUMERIC
   PERFORM B-ACTION
END-IF.

IF C IS NUMERIC
   PERFORM C-ACTION
END-IF.

IF A IS NUMERIC AND
    B IS NUMERIC AND
    C IS NUMERIC 
    PERFORM A-B-C-ACTION
END-IF.

My result was totally unexpected, A-ACTION, B-ACTION, C-ACTION has
been executed, but guess what, A-B-C-ACTION did not execute. This is
wierd, the numeric check seems to work independently but in a compound
logical expression, it treated B as not numeric.

Does anyone has this similar experience or any explanation.

Please post your experience in this group or e-mail me at
lamas@pathcom.com.

Thanks....
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75rrhe$bid$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<367f0c8c.3021698@news.pathcom.com>`

```
Check your compiler options for NUMPROC(PFD) or NUMPROC(NOPFD). The PFD
flavor basically tells the compiler to use (for example) CLC (Compare
Logical Character) instructions instead of CP (Compare Pack) instructions
against Packed-Decimal data greater than 1-Byte. I've also seen CLI (Compare
Logical Immediate) instructions used instead of CP when the Packed-Decimal
data is only 1-Byte and PFD is active.

The generated instruction for your numeric check should be a TRT (Translate
and Test). You'll have to look at the PMAP (specify LIST,NOOFFSET for PMAP
generation along with your other compiler options). Also, if the numeric
checks are in fact TRT instructions, you should find that the Translate
Table address (operand 2 of the instruction) is the same for Variables 'A'
and 'C'. Variable 'B' should point to a different table address.

If someone has specified PFD, tell them that this is a risky option and that
they need to always use NOPFD just to be on the safe side. I use CLC's in
ASSEMBLER only if I KNOW without a doubt that I have LIKE sign-nibbles. Data
from other sources should always be handled by program's using NOPFD. That's
just my opinion. The savings is NOT worth the headache. Granted, CLC's use
about 30% of the clock-time needed by CP's, but you'll find this to be
almost negligible in the overall scheme of things.

HTH....

Happy Holidays,

WOB,
Atlanta

lamas@pathcom.com wrote in message <367f0c8c.3021698@news.pathcom.com>...
>Do anyone has any insight to the following problem:

…[47 more quoted lines elided]…

```

> Curious Frog

Greetings,

Well, I should have verified my previous information before posting. I just
got through proving my first post is incorrect.

It is NECESSARY to use NUMPROC(NOPFD). When I set-up a 3-Byte Signed Packed-
Decimal field, gave it a value of +12345 and then moved X'5F' to the last-byte
(using reference modification and a redefines), the NUMERIC test WORKED.
Naturally, without the alteration of the last-byte it also PASSED the NUMERIC
test.

I then set-up a 3-Byte Unsigned Packed-Decimal field, gave it a value of
12345, moved X'5C' to the last-byte (same method as above) and the NUMERIC
test FAILED. Then I just moved the Unsigned field to ITSELF. The PMAP showed
an MVC of Operand 1 to Operand 2 (itself), as well as a W Compiler Warning
Message, followed by an OI X'0F' on the last byte. Afterwards, the NUMERIC
test PASSED. I also had a successful NUMERIC test by adding ZERO to the
Unsigned field. Again, the compiler issued an OI X'0F', due to the AP
instruction, which had forced the sign-nibble to 'C'.

I verified that the Translate-Table was the SAME one used in ALL tests and had
not been altered by the compiler for whatsoever.

Well, I'm stumped....

WOB

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981224003924.17693.00000393@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

In article <75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, WOB@my-dejanews.com writes:

>
>I then set-up a 3-Byte Unsigned Packed-Decimal field, gave it a value of
>12345, moved X'5C' to the last-byte (same method as above) and the NUMERIC
>

Excuse me, but how would you have gone about defining an Unsigned
Packed-Decimal field??  It is my understanding that ALL Packed-Decimal fields
in this (MVS) environment MUST be signed regardless of how the field is coded.
That is PIC S9(05) COMP-3 and PIC 9(05) COMP-3 both produce X'12345C'.
I would most definitely like to know how to reference an UnSigned Packed field
stored as X'012345'.  I have several situations where client data is produced
in this format.  Any enlightenment would be greatly appreciated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3681FB68.679CFB20@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981224003924.17693.00000393@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, WOB@my-dejanews.com writes:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> in this format.  Any enlightenment would be greatly appreciated.

In IBM mainframe COBOL, a PIC 9(5) COMP-3 field would have an "F" sign. 
The value 12,345 would have an internal representation of X'12345F'.

There's no way in IBM mainframe COBOL to define an elementary three-byte
numeric field containing X'012345'.  That's BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)
and it's not a supported numeric type.  IBM packed decimal always has a
low-order sign nybble, X'C' for positive, X'D' for negative, and X'F'
for unsigned.

You can define a field as PIC X(3) VALUE X'012345'.  But you won't be
able to do arithmetic with it in the IBM world.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75thu9$kj9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981224003924.17693.00000393@ngol05.aol.com> <3681FB68.679CFB20@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3681FB68.679CFB20@worldnet.att.net>,
  arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>
> In IBM mainframe COBOL, a PIC 9(5) COMP-3 field would have an "F" sign.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hey Arnold,

03 WS-PACKED-X.
   05  WS-PACKED-V9 PIC S9(16)V9 PACKED-DECIMAL.
03 WS-BCD PIC X(08) VALUE X'1234567812345677'.

MOVE WS-BCD TO WS-PACKED-X (1:8).
MOVE X'0C' TO WS-PACKED-X (9:1).
ADD +1 TO WS-PACKED-V9.
MOVE WS-PACKED-X TO WS-BCD.

WS-BCD now contains X'1234567812345678'. Check your PMAP and see.

I don't know if other platforms can use this technique. I guess it's a matter
of trying it out.

Happy Holidays,

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<36831EA5.52B9F0FF@worldnet.att.net>`

```
WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> In article <3681FB68.679CFB20@worldnet.att.net>,
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> WOB

Sure, something like that can be made to work.  The point is, there's no
way in IBM mainframe COBOL to define a NUMERIC BCD field and perform
arithmetic directly on that field.  Perhaps Burroughs or UNISYS COBOL
support a numeric BCD type, but not IBM.

Merry Christmas!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76bkk1$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981224003924.17693.00000393@ngol05.aol.com> <3681FB68.679CFB20@worldnet.att.net> <3682E863.463F@sprynet.com>`

```
Look for COMP-6 in several compilers.  (I know that MF COBOL supports it -
but I think that it is an emulation of someone else's system.)

COMP-6 supports a "truly" unsigned packed-decimal item (so you can fit 6
digits in 3 bytes).

FYI,
  The Standard says that even USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL is implementor defined
(to some extent) and this is exactly why.  Given
   05  Whatever Pic 9(06) Packed-Decimal
the Standard neither requires nor prohibits the use of a "sign-nibble"

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Check in MVS COBOL II

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75tgsk$jop$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981224003924.17693.00000393@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981224003924.17693.00000393@ngol05.aol.com>,
  sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:
>
> In article <75sb5a$mcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, WOB@my-dejanews.com writes:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Try this to force a low-order 'C' sign-nibble -

03  WS-PKD-UNSIGNED-X.
    05  WS-PKD-UNSIGNED PIC  9(05) PACKED-DECIMAL.

MOVE 12345 TO WS-PKD-UNSIGNED.
MOVE X'5C' TO WS-PKD-UNSIGNED-X (3:1).

Cheers,

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ENTRY and Next COBOL Standard

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687E891.26C1@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<75onei$sct$1@server.cntfl.com> <914417997.10711.0.nnrp-02.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk> <75t11q$hmt$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <760f9k$4k4$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
ENTRY is not being taken out of any standard.  It never has been in 
any standard or proposed standard.  It is the antithesis of good 
structured programming practice and there are many ways of 
accomplishing what people tend to use it for.  It results in poor 
documentation value, lots of bugs (I have had to help out people using 
the same thing in other languages), and so on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ENTRY and Next COBOL Standard

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76ap39$9rb$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<75onei$sct$1@server.cntfl.com> <914417997.10711.0.nnrp-02.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk> <75t11q$hmt$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <760f9k$4k4$1@server.cntfl.com> <3687E891.26C1@compaq.com>`

```

Don Nelson wrote in message <3687E891.26C1@compaq.com>...
>ENTRY is not being taken out of any standard.  It never has been in
>any standard or proposed standard.  It is the antithesis of good
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the same thing in other languages), and so on.

Though I appreciate your comments and understand how
ENTRY may be considered to be unstructured, the way in
which I am using ENTRY is structured and provides a clear
and precise definition (documentation) of the interface for
each ENTRY. Furthermore, it is much cleaner and more
understandable than the two other means that are available
to me (using standard COBOL or other extensions) and
that could be used to accomplish the same task.

I think this is a case where it is not so much how it appears
on a structure chart or other diagram but on how it is used
in practice.

My experience in working with ENTRY, during the past several
days has convinced me that it is an important and valuable
addition to the language. As a practical matter, whether it
makes it into the standard, or not, is irrelevent. No
implementation will make it on to my computer, if it does not
contain ENTRY.

Incidently, the particular program, I developed, that provided
the convincing evidence of the utility of ENTRY, contains
27 distinct data definitions in the LINKAGE SECTION and
38 ENTRY statements. It validates and converts many of
the common formats for dates and times. The first paragraph
in the PROCEDURE DIVISION does some initialization and
contains an EXIT PROGRAM as its last statement. This is
followed by 38 paragraphs, each containing an ENTRY as the
first statement in the paragraph. The last statement in each of
these paragraphs is EXIT PROGRAM. There are no GO TOs.
Following this is all the common procedures.

After careful consideration, I have determined that only one
other means would make the solution to this problem easier
or clearer. FUNCTION-ENTRY function-entry-name USING
data-name-1 ... RETURNING data-name-2. No implementation
offers this and I am not suggesting that FUNCTION-ENTRY
and ENTRY be (re)considered for the proposed standard.
Maybe next time!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

## Thread: Trim — 10 msgs

### Trim

- **From:** "Camp David" <dvdwaaij@advantage.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net>`

```
Can anybody tell me how to CUT OFF spaces (XXXXX (right)).
Sometimes VARIABLE_OUT contains words of 3,5,7 etc. lenght.
e.g.

VARIABLE_OUT        PIC X(12); VALUE

                                      "WORDXXXXXXXX".

I only need:  <WORD>
```

#### ↳ Re: Trim

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36801189.C617C2A0@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net>`

```
Take a look at the STRING and UNSTRING statements and the phrase
DELIMITED BY SPACE within that statement.  E.g.

STRING "The word of the day is " DELIMITED BY SIZE VARIABLE-OUT
DELIMITED BY SPACE "." DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO OUT-BUFF.

If OUT-BUFF is large enough, it will contain "The word of the day is
WORD."

hth

> Can anybody tell me how to CUT OFF spaces (XXXXX (right)).
> Sometimes VARIABLE_OUT contains words of 3,5,7 etc. lenght.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I only need:  <WORD>
```

#### ↳ Re: Trim

- **From:** "Tim Nicholson" <timn3508@aol.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36813086.0@news>`
- **References:** `<75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net>`

```
Another method I have used with AcuCOBOL is this:

77  SPACE-COUNT   PIC 99 VALUE 0.
77  VARIABLE-LENGTH PIC 99 VALUE 0.

INSPECT VARIABLE-OUT
     TALLYING SPACE-COUNT FOR TRAILING SPACES.
COMPUTE VARIABLE-LENGTH = 12 - SPACE-COUNT.
DISPLAY VARIABLE-OUT (1:VARIABLE-LENGTH).

This method will depend on your compiler's ability to use TRAILING in the
INSPECT statement, so consult your manual.  I haven't been able to find this
convention in the MicroFocus manual.

This also requires the 85 standard COBOL.

Camp David wrote in message <75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net>...
>Can anybody tell me how to CUT OFF spaces (XXXXX (right)).
>Sometimes VARIABLE_OUT contains words of 3,5,7 etc. lenght.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Trim

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36816506.100359819@news.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net> <36813086.0@news>`

```
On Wed, 23 Dec 1998 13:03:50 -0500, "Tim Nicholson" <timn3508@aol.com> wrote:

>INSPECT VARIABLE-OUT
>     TALLYING SPACE-COUNT FOR TRAILING SPACES.

Another option is using UNSTRING with a suitably large number of SPACES as the
delimiter and a WITH POINTER clause. I believe this relies on standard behaviour
but it does require a temporary variable to unstring into and will fail if your
data can have large strings of spaces in it.

# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Trim

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368166FE.5F604A2C@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net> <36813086.0@news>`

```
Tim Nicholson wrote:

> Another method I have used with AcuCOBOL is this:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> convention in the MicroFocus manual.

With no TRAILING available, replace the INSPECT + COMPUTE in above code with:

PERFORM VARYING VARIABLE-LENGTH
        FROM 12 BY -1
        UNTIL VARIABLE-LENGTH = ZERO
           OR VARIABLE-OUT (VARIABLE-LENGTH : 1) NOT = SPACE)
  CONTINUE
END-PERFORM

> This also requires the 85 standard COBOL.

Idem for the PERFORM

The Frog
```

#### ↳ Re: Trim

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368690CB.6543FB80@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<75ovnu$bdb@news3.euro.net>`

```
An alphanumeric move in COBOL is done from left to right.  If a longer
field is moved to a shorter field, the result is truncated to the length
of the receiving field.  If the receiving field is longer than the
sending field, the receiving field is padded with spaces.

Remember, this works for alphanumeric moves!  Both operands must be PIC
X.

Camp David wrote:

> Can anybody tell me how to CUT OFF spaces (XXXXX (right)).
> Sometimes VARIABLE_OUT contains words of 3,5,7 etc. lenght.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I only need:  <WORD>
```

## Thread: Brief Commercial: Assembler Students Wanted — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Brief Commercial: Assembler Students Wanted

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981222102106.01060.00000061@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
We are offering our two part class "OS/390 Assembler Workshop" in Denver. Part
1 will be January 25-29, and Part 2 will be February 8-12. This is an excellent
(and rare) opportunity for a) programmers new to Assembler and b) programmers
who once knew Assembler but need a refresher.

As the author and instructor, I am very excited about this class (I know, no
accounting for taste) because I enjoy Assembler and don't get too many
opportunities to teach it. But this class is going to happen. Right now we have
a small enrollment (5), so I am trying to get a few more students (the more
merrier when it comes to Assembler).

In addition to the outstanding student handout (thank you, thank you), students
will receive the latest edition of several IBM pubs in hardcopy format (read:
books, manuals) including the POO (or PoPs if you prefer), the reference
summary ("green card" which is currently a blue booklet), and several others.

If you are interested, check out the details on our web site (contains course
descriptions, objectives, outlines and public course schedule). Call or email
us to enroll. Time is getting close and I have to order those pubs.

A brief synopsis:

This two-week course covers all instructions except those that fall into these
categories: control instructions, floating point, vector, cryptographic. In
other words, the basics. It also covers the Assembler (assuming HLASM, but
pointing various differences as appropriate), debugging (including dump
reading), and basic system interfaces (DCB, OPEN, CLOSE, GET, PUT, SAVE,
RETURN, CALL, LOAD, LINK, DELETE, XCTL, GETMAIN, FREEMAIN, ABEND, WTO, TIME,
and more) and some insight into various coding techniques. Details on our
website at www.trainersfriend.com

I hope no one is offended by this brief commercial message, and I won't promote
this offering again on this newsgroup, because I do appreciate all the insights
and sharing and other benefits I receive from the group. Also this posting will
appear on three other related newsgroups once.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-a62bb40dad"></a>
## Thread: DELAY COMMAND or whatever — 10 this month / 13 total

### DELAY COMMAND or whatever

- **From:** insane_o@hotmail.com (Gil)
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368052f1.9329575@news.inter.net.il>`

```
Hey,
 Is there a command to make a DELAY during the run? like pause or whatever?
```

#### ↳ Re: DELAY COMMAND or whatever

- **From:** mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-fnCSn9dutg0m@dsm4merlin.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<368052f1.9329575@news.inter.net.il>`

```
On Wed, 23 Dec 1998 02:18:53, insane_o@hotmail.com (Gil) wrote:

> Hey,
>  Is there a command to make a DELAY during the run? like pause or whatever?
>  
>  
You mean a COBOL command?  Probably several ways to do it with 
date/time calls, but if you want a JCL invoked waiter for pauses 
between job steps:

         TITLE 'WAIT FOR PARAMETER TIME'
WAITERX  BEGIN 12,SAVE               JCL CALL VERSION
         SPACE 2
         L     R1,0(R1)                GET PARM AREA
         ST    R1,SAVPARM               SAVE FOR FUN
         MVC   INTV2,2(R1)             BUT THIS IS FOR REAL
         STIMER WAIT,DINTVL=INTV6
         B     GOBYE
GOBYE    L     R13,SAVE+4
         RETURN (14,12),RC=0
          DC    C'INTV1 STARTS HERE---'
         DS    2F
INTV6    DC    C'000'        THIS IS CONSTANT
INTV2    DC    CL4'0'          FROM THE PARM
         DC    X'C0'         DO NOT CHANGE
SAVPARM  DC  A(0)           PARM ADDRESS
         DC    C'END OF WAITERX'
         END   WAITERX
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DELAY COMMAND or whatever

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76bcc5$ft1@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<368052f1.9329575@news.inter.net.il> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-fnCSn9dutg0m@dsm4merlin.iix.com>`

```
In case you don't LIKE coding in Assembler (and you are talking about IBM
mainframes - which I think you are), see bellow

mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com wrote in message
<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-fnCSn9dutg0m@dsm4merlin.iix.com>...
>On Wed, 23 Dec 1998 02:18:53, insane_o@hotmail.com (Gil) wrote:
>
>> Hey,
>>  Is there a command to make a DELAY during the run? like pause or
whatever?
>>
>>


In all versions of MVS COBOL from OS/VS COBOL on, you can simply call

  "ILBOWAT"

passing it (below the 16M line, i.e. with DATA(24) if you are using a
current COBOL compiler) the time for which you want to "pause". (I can't
remember, with some of the more recent run-time libraries - you might need
to call the entry point ILBOWAT0.)

This is a little known (but documented in the old OS/VS COBOL logic manual)
feature that has been maintained with both the VS COBOL II and LE run-time
libraries.

P.S.  Mark Young -- is there an ILBDWAT (or similar) VSE module?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DELAY COMMAND or whatever

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76betq$60t$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<368052f1.9329575@news.inter.net.il> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-fnCSn9dutg0m@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <76bcc5$ft1@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <76bcc5$ft1@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>...

>P.S.  Mark Young -- is there an ILBDWAT (or similar) VSE module?


Don't know about VSE, but the PC has a "sleep/dispatch" that
can be called by subroutine.  The only Cobol I know that does
not is DOS, but there it is easy to just loop and look at the clock.
Since it is single user anyway, that is sufficient for most purposes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DELAY COMMAND or whatever

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298364.19792.10092@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3689F318.A83999B3@conexion.com.py>`

```
In message <<3689F318.A83999B3@conexion.com.py>> Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py> writes:
> 
> Donald Tees wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> you have a multi-user DOS.

I think you mean 'multi-tasking', multi-user is something different
though NT TSE (Terminal Server Edition) does finally achieve
multi-user, it does it rather poorly because underneath it
is still inherently a single user design.

Given that I have used a multi-user DOS (but not from MS)
for a dozen years or more that also supported multi-tasking
at each terminal then the best that MS can claim is that
they have at last caught up.

MS-DOS and Windows do actually have a despatch OS function
using the multiplex interrupt INT 0x2F with AX=0x1680.
This should be called in each loop waiting for time:  ie

        ACCEPT Time-Now FROM TIME
        ADD 100 Time-Now GIVING Time-Out
        PERFORM UNTIL Time-Now > Time-Out
            ACCEPT Time-Now  FROM TIME
            CALL x"84"
                USING Interrupt-2F
                      etc
         END-PERFORM
```

William Lynch wrote in message <36899A6D.121FBDB8@att.net>...
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> remember, with some of the more recent run-time libraries - you might
need
>> to call the entry point ILBOWAT0.)
>
>What does the time field passed to ILBOWAT look like?
>
>Bill Lynch

My memory is that it is a half-word binary (i.e. Pic S9(04)) - what I can't
remember is exactly what it means, i.e. does "1" mean a second or
one-hundredths of a second.  I think it is the latter.

(Easy way to test it - but I don't have a compiler and run-time handy) is to
display time-of-day (or equivalent), call with a value of 100, then display
the time again.  That should give a fairly accurate answer.
```
