```
In article <368052f1.9329575@news.inter.net.il>,
  insane_o@hotmail.com wrote:
> Hey,
>  Is there a command to make a DELAY during the run? like pause or whatever?
>
>

Below is another variation of the STIMER macro in a sub-program named
SNOOZER. It's called by COBOL and passes a 7-Byte Display-Numeric parameter,
defined as PIC 9(05)V99. The label MAXINTVL is USER defined. It's set to
300.00 seconds (5 minutes) in the sub-program.

Syntax specifying a WAIT of 3.57 Seconds (a favorite number of mine) ===>

03    WS-SNOOZER     PIC X(08)       VALUE 'SNOOZER'.
03    WS-INTERVAL    PIC 9(05)V99    VALUE 3.57.

CALL WS-SNOOZER USING WS-INTERVAL.

HTH....

Happy Holidays,

WOB,
Atlanta

SNOOZER  CSECT	00000100  USING *,R12  R12 IS OUR BASE-REGISTER  00000200 
PRINT GEN  ACTIVATE MACRO-EXPANSION  00000300 SNOOZER  AMODE 31  SHOULD
ALWAYS BE '31'	00000400 SNOOZER  RMODE ANY  SHOULD ALWAYS BE 'ANY'  00000500
 YREGS	MVS REGISTER-MACRO  00000600  SAVE  (14,12)  SAVE ALL REGISTERS 
00000700  LR  R12,R15  ESTABLISH ADDRESSABILITY  00000800  B  GETPARM  BRANCH
AROUND EYECATCHER  00000900  DC  CL19' PROGRAM NAME ===>'  00001000 PROGNAME
DC  CL8'SNOOZER'  00001100  DC	CL21', LAST ASSEMBLY ===>'  00001200  DC 
CL8'&SYSDATE'  00001300  DC  CL2','  00001400  DC  CL5'&SYSTIME'  00001500 
DC  CL22', ASSEMBLER USED ===>'  00001600  DC  CL21'&SYSASM'  00001700
MAXINTVL DC  PL4'0030000'  MAX-INTERVAL FOR 'STIMER'  00001800 TWNTY4HR DC 
PL4'8640000'  24-HOUR MAX-INTERVAL  00001900  EJECT  00002000 GETPARM  DS  0H
 00002100  L  R9,0(,R1)  PARAMETER ADDRESSABILITY  00002200  PACK 
DWORD,0(7,R9)  PACK AS P'00000000SSSSSTH'  00002300  OI  DWORD+7,X'0F' 
ENSURE 'F' SIGN-NIBBLE	00002400  LA  R15,4  SET TO F'4' (RETURN-CODE) 
00002500  MVC  MSGAREA,=CL69'SNOOZER ===> SLEEP-INTERVAL IS <INVALID>,
X00002600  RETURN-CODE 004'  00002700  NC  MSGAREA+14(4),=7X'BF'  CONVERT TO
LOWER-CASE  00002800  NC  MSGAREA+20(7),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO 
00002900  NC  MSGAREA+28(2),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO  00003000	NC 
MSGAREA+43(5),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO	00003100  NC 
MSGAREA+50(3),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO	00003200  LA  R6,RTN2CLLR 
POINT R6 AT RETURN-LABEL  00003300  CP	DWORD,TWNTY4HR	PARAMETER EXCEEDS
24-HOURS?  00003400  BH  PUT2CNSL  YES, THANKS FOR PLAYING  00003500  SLL 
R15,1  RESET TO F'8'  00003600	MVI  MSGAREA+56,C'8'  INSERT INTO MSGAREA 
00003700  CP  DWORD,MAXINTVL  PARAMETER EXCEEDS MAX INTERVAL?  00003800  BH 
PUT2CNSL  YES, THANKS FOR PLAYING  00003900  SLL  R15,1  RESET TO F'16' 
00004000  MVC  MSGAREA+55(2),=C'16'  INSERT INTO MSGAREA  00004100  CVB 
R7,DWORD  CONVERT TO BINARY  00004200  LTR  R7,R7  PARAMETER ZERO?  00004300 
BZ  PUT2CNSL  YES, THANKS FOR PLAYING  00004400  ST  R7,DWORD+4  POPULATE
'STIMER' INTERVAL  00004500  MVC  MSGAREA+13(53),=CL53'BEGIN 00000.00 SECOND
SLEEP-INTERVAX00004600	L, PLEASE WAIT....'  00004700  MVC 
MSGAREA+19(5),0(R9)  INSERT WHOLE-SECONDS  00004800  MVC  MSGAREA+25(2),5(R9)
 INSERT TENTH/HUNDRETHS  00004900  NC  MSGAREA+14(4),=7X'BF'  CONVERT TO
LOWER-CASE  00005000  NC  MSGAREA+29(5),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO 
00005100  NC  MSGAREA+36(4),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO  00005200	NC 
MSGAREA+42(7),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO	00005300  NC 
MSGAREA+52(5),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO	00005400  NC 
MSGAREA+59(3),=7X'BF'  DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO	00005500  BAL  R6,PUT2CNSL 
ISSUE 'WTO'  00005600  STIMER WAIT,BINTVL=DWORD+4  ISSUE 'STIMER'  00005700 
MVC  MSGAREA+13(5),=CL5'END' INDICATE END OF SLEEP-INTERVAL  00005800  NC 
MSGAREA+14(2),=7X'BF'  CONVERT TO LOWER-CASE  00005900	MVC 
MSGAREA+51(15),=CL15'RETURN-CODE 000'  00006000  NC  MSGAREA+52(5),=7X'BF' 
CONVERT TO LOWER-CASE  00006100  NC  MSGAREA+59(3),=7X'BF' 
DITTO-DITTO-DITTO-DITTO  00006200  BAL	R6,PUT2CNSL  ISSUE 'WTO'  00006300 
XR  R15,R15  RETURN-CODE IS NORMAL  00006400  EJECT  00006500 RTN2CLLR DS  0H
 00006600  RETURN (14,12),RC=(15)  RESTORE REGISTERS AND RETURN  00006700
PUT2CNSL DS  0H  00006800  BAL	R1,WRT2OPER  ISSUE SVC 35  00006900  DC 
Y(L'MSGAREA+4)	MESSAGE-LGTH PLUS 4  00007000  DC  X'8000'  MCS-FLAGS 
00007100 MSGAREA  DS  CL69  WTO MESSAGE-AREA  00007200	DC  H'0' 
DESCRIPTOR-CODES  00007300  DC	X'0024'  ROUTING-CODES	00007400 WRT2OPER DS 
0H  00007500  SVC  35  ISSUE 'WTO'  00007600  BR  R6  RETURN TO THE 'NSI' 
00007700 DWORD	DS  D  ALIGNED DOUBLEWORD WORKAREA  00007800  END  00007900

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-6f2f80c244"></a>
## Thread: COMTI data "conversion" problems! — 6 this month / 9 total

### COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **From:** "Jean-Philippe Valois" <jean-philippe.valois@bellsygma.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.microsofttransactionserver.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<75tlpj$sgl2@dm1cns.on.bell.ca>`

```
Hi all!

This is a strange one.

Here is what our layers look like.

top : VB
lower : COMTI
   deep : CICS
     deeper : COBOL
        way down there : DB2

The problem : A recordset data element given a value of 4.2 in VB is stored
in DB2 as 4.1.

The additionnal info :
COMTI data type correspondance is :
VB : Single
COBOL : PIC S9(2)V9(1) COMP-3
The DB2 data type is DECIMAL (3,1)


Go figure.   Also, all multiples of 4 are similarly converted (8.2 to 8.1,
12.2 to 12.1...)

Surprisingly, if we force a value of 4.21 into the recordset element, it
gets correctly written as 4.2.


Could this be some kind of alignment problem?

Thanks for any answers.

Jean-Philippe Valois
Software Engineer
Montreal, Quebec.


P.S.  Please forward any responses to jean-philippe.valois@bellsygma.com
```

#### ↳ Re: COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981224143453.01167.00001204@ng-cc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<75tlpj$sgl2@dm1cns.on.bell.ca>`

```
"Jean-Philippe Valois" <jean-philippe.valois@bellsygma.com>
Date: Thu, 24 Dec 1998 10:16:01 -0500


posted  
<<
This is a strange one.

Here is what our layers look like.

top : VB
lower : COMTI
   deep : CICS
     deeper : COBOL
        way down there : DB2

The problem : A recordset data element given a value of 4.2 in VB is stored
in DB2 as 4.1.

The additionnal info :
COMTI data type correspondance is :
VB : Single
COBOL : PIC S9(2)V9(1) COMP-3
The DB2 data type is DECIMAL (3,1)


Go figure.   Also, all multiples of 4 are similarly converted (8.2 to 8.1,
12.2 to 12.1...)

Surprisingly, if we force a value of 4.21 into the recordset element, it
gets correctly written as 4.2.


Could this be some kind of alignment problem?



>>


This looks like a truncation problem. You seem to be indicating that the
original value is stored as a single precision floating point numeric data item
in VB. The value 4.2 actually can not be recorded exactly in binary. The .2 is
1/5 which in binary is a continuous fraction.

The value stored can be very nearly correct and as long as you stay within one
language discipline storage and retrieval will present the value approximately
correctly, if favorable rules of rounding are in force.

It seems that you may have the effect of a truncation when you are moving to
the comp-3 (BCD) field.  I am fairly confident that CICS is not doing this, but
confess ignorance about your COMTI. Most likely candidate is VB, as the
truncator. 

You may be able to influence conversions with compile (or runtime) parameters,
but the scope would be broad, possibly fixing this field and damaging others.

You may need to wrap the specific field with rounding functions.

It is not obvious that this is a bug, maybe you should just look at it as an
inconvnience that has lots of ramifications.

Not being familiar with the objectives of the specific application, one can not
criticize your data definitions.  But it is worth mentioning, that if you are
sensitive to the difference between 4.1 and 4.2, you MAY need to preserve
atleast another digit of precision.  A COBOL definition of 9(x).9 instructs the
computer to ignore hundredths. Yet you are telling us you care about the effect
in data format conversion of hundredths.

There are two basic solutions at the field definition level. Code the other
digit in the interfaces, as in 9(x).99, or multiply the number by ten before
shipping it about, and then demodulate it at the last moment before
presentation by dividing by ten, (perhaps in COBOL with exact control per field
with ROUNDING phrases).

You can combine this with compile (runtime) parameter controls to get the
overall effect that you want. But from what you have presented this does not
look like a bug or a strange thing. As you become accustom to it you will
recognize that somewhere in this mix you have actually instructed the
compilation tools to truncate. 

The value 1/5 can not be represented exactly in binary. Same effect in 4.2, 8.2
and 12.2, ad infinitum.

There are many such problems. I can see with the mix of software that you are
no novice; sometimes newbies are amazed at this simple difficulty, and
sometimes we each have to be reminded.  Generally, you should carry around a
bit more precision (more digits) then you think you need, to compensate for
rounding/truncation problems. When problems arise still, you should isolate
them and apply the parms or clauses that fix the specific behavior.

Can you state exactly where the single precision data item gets converted to
BCD, and by what software?  Can you inform us of the parameters you are using
to control rounding?


Best Wishes, 


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.microsofttransactionserver.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<368A3C5E.464E381E@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<75tlpj$sgl2@dm1cns.on.bell.ca>`

```
This is most probably caused in (not *by* but *in*) VBasic. It is the Single
precision floating point variable that mis-behaves. Floating point is not as
precise as fixed point due to the linited amount of storage for the rabge of
possible values.

At various times other people have explained this stuff in great detail in this
n.g, in a way I could understand but not repeat.

Somebody of those floating cracks out there to give this explanation once again
for Jean-Philippe Valois (and me)

The Frog

Jean-Philippe Valois wrote:

> Hi all!
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> P.S.  Please forward any responses to jean-philippe.valois@bellsygma.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.microsofttransactionserver.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<368A488F.55A9FA95@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<75tlpj$sgl2@dm1cns.on.bell.ca> <368A3C5E.464E381E@IMN.nl>`

```
The only floating point values which can be represented with 100% accuracy are
equal to an integer (positive or negative), the mantissa, multiplied by 2 to an
integer(+/-) power (exponent).  It may not be represented that way internally, but
could be derived if necessary.  For example 48 could be expressed as 11 (binary) *
2**4 but may be 0.110000 (binary) * 2**6 in the computer.

Since only a finite number of digits are available for the mantissa and exponent,
only a finite number of floating point numbers can be represented on a computer.
For numbers such as 1/3 or pi, something close is all that can be done.

COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote:

> This is most probably caused in (not *by* but *in*) VBasic. It is the Single
> precision floating point variable that mis-behaves. Floating point is not as
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> for Jean-Philippe Valois (and me)

Warren Porter
---
Rainy Days OR Mondays Always Get Me Down.
```

- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.microsofttransactionserver.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<368BF91D.FA0780D8@IMN.nl>`

```
Warren Porter wrote!

> The only floating point values which can be represented with 100% accuracy are
> equal to an integer (positive or negative), the mantissa, multiplied by 2 to an
```

```
  "Cedric" <cgroscolas@bplorraine.fr> wrote:
> Hello,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I was fighting this recently too (on a table of about 15 million rows....).
The error had to do with pointers between pages, and didn't affect programs
which use matching index access, just those using seq. prefetch (or some
variety of tablespace scan).  Our systems folks had to 'fix' the page, but I'm
not sure exactly what they had to do.  My guess is that a reorg was performed,
but I don't know...  In any case, it's not an application problem, so if you
can, hand it off to your data administration or systems folks!

Good Luck,
Mark Arnold

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
On Thu, 24 Dec 1998 13:35:15 +0100, "Cedric" <cgroscolas@bplorraine.fr> wrote:

~Hello,
~
~While i execute my job, there is bug: I've got a 'S04E' Error.
~My job Is Abended.
~
~Could anyone explain this error?
~
~This is a Cobol V2 program, using OS390, TSO
~
~Thanks

The 04E abend code is DB2's way of telling you it could not continue.   There
will be a reason code associated with this error that provides more specific
information about the cause.     The reason codes are documented in the DB2
Messages and Codes manual.


djl
djl47-spambgone@sirius.com   <--- Address Antispammed.  Lose the numerics, the spambgone and the dash.
```

<a id="t-b8484182a4"></a>
## Thread: WRITE statement specification — 6 msgs

### WRITE statement specification

- **From:** BuyukYarak@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
What does the current standard actually say about the WRITE statement
committing the data to the device?

In the implementation I am working with, it is clear that the WRITE statement
does not commit the data (to disk) at the time it is executed, which is a pain
when trying to follow debugging logs.  Is the implementation following the
standard?
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITE statement specification

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36844F98.E328D3EA@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote:
> What does the current standard actually say about the WRITE statement
> committing the data to the device?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard?
...

I confess I have not read the standard recently, but from a practicality
standpoint I think it highly unlikely such a requirement would ever be
accepted into any standard for the language.  To require commitment to
disk for an individual WRITE statement would disallow the use of
buffering and blocking techniques by the underlying operating system,
and at least for mainframe systems could only be implemented at the cost
of severe performance degradation.  I believe successful completion of a
CLOSE is the only point at which commitment of all output records to the
actual device is guaranteed.  It would be nice if error recovery at
abnormal termination could always resolve things and write out any
pending records, but even in environments which might attempt to do
this, the overwriting of file control information by a berserk program
could make it impossible.
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITE statement specification

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368a2aa8.103109813@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Thu, 24 Dec 1998 14:08:28 GMT BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote:

:>What does the current standard actually say about the WRITE statement
:>committing the data to the device?

I doubt it says anything.

:>In the implementation I am working with, it is clear that the WRITE statement
:>does not commit the data (to disk) at the time it is executed, which is a pain
:>when trying to follow debugging logs.  Is the implementation following the
:>standard?

In the case of MVS, one can single block and then the write will be committed
(though it may only be committed to cache, but there probably is some DCB
attribute to bypass that). I am sure that there is some system service that
can be called to force all buffers to be flushed on other platforms.

Or you can simply close and re-open the file.

But why would one want to do that? The application will crawl!
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITE statement specification

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<762e8l$ee0$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote in message <75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>What does the current standard actually say about the WRITE statement
>committing the data to the device?

The execution of the WRITE statement releases a logical record
to the operating system.

It is not possible for the COBOL standard to specify how the operating
system will treat logical records.

>In the implementation I am working with, it is clear that the WRITE
statement
>does not commit the data (to disk) at the time it is executed, which is a
pain
>when trying to follow debugging logs.  Is the implementation following the
>standard?

It is most likely the implementation is following the standard. Even
if there were some minor technical violation, it is not likely that
complaining about it would solve "your" immediate problem.

There may be an option for the COBOL system you are using,
which option will allow unbuffered writes; if unbuffered writes are
allowed by the operating system.

For example, with Micro Focus, the directive is WRITETHROUGH.

As always make sure you understand the effect of using the option.
In this case, forcing unbuffered writes may negatively affect the
performance of the application.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WRITE statement specification

- **From:** BuyukYarak@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<762rtm$iq1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <762e8l$ee0$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
> BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote in message <75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance of the application.

The COBOL environment appears not to have the extensions (or options) you
mentioned as for the Micro Focus system, but rather scrupulously adheres to
the standard and just that.  (However, to be fair, there is one extensible
part of the system I can exploit, but it's major work if the only thing to be
done is setting up a debugging log file...having said that, I will probably
do it.)

Out of curiosity, is it contemplated by the writers of the next standard to
make a great many of these extensions in some systems part of the standard? 
From what I can see, the current environment (based on the current standard)
I am using assumes that the program will run, do its job, and quit, and then
and only then will all file output be available (short of me putting
close-and-then-re-open statements as part of loop in a continually running
program, which must also affect performance a bit)?

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITE statement specification

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7631kr$mc7$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<75thsr$kc2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <762e8l$ee0$1@server.cntfl.com> <762rtm$iq1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote in message <762rtm$iq1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>>
[...]
>> There may be an option for the COBOL system you are using,
>> which option will allow unbuffered writes; if unbuffered writes are
>> allowed by the operating system.
>>
[...]
>
>The COBOL environment appears not to have the extensions (or options) you
>mentioned as for the Micro Focus system, but rather scrupulously adheres to
>the standard and just that.  (However, to be fair, there is one extensible
>part of the system I can exploit, but it's major work if the only thing to
be
>done is setting up a debugging log file...having said that, I will probably
>do it.)

Although you are free to not disclose the COBOL system you are
using, it is possible that someone in this NG might know what option
to use; if they knew which system you are using.

>Out of curiosity, is it contemplated by the writers of the next standard to
>make a great many of these extensions in some systems part of the standard?
>From what I can see, the current environment (based on the current
standard)
>I am using assumes that the program will run, do its job, and quit, and
then
>and only then will all file output be available (short of me putting
>close-and-then-re-open statements as part of loop in a continually running
>program, which must also affect performance a bit)?

I can't address what is contemplated by the standard committees;
only on what they have published, that I know about. I am also
free to be very wrong in my comments! ;-)

Within the area of file access, the biggest changes will be in the
addition of file and record locking. In the case of OPEN OUTPUT,
the operation will continue to be as you describe since the file
locking will be exclusive. There are some areas of file and record
locking that are under review; hence it is not possible to know,
at this time, what the final standard will be.

Having said that, it would appear that one file connector could
be opened:

OPEN EXTEND SHARING WITH ALL OTHER file-name-1,

while another file connector could be opened:

OPEN INPUT SHARING WILL ALL OTHER file-name-2.

In this case, it may be possible for the second file connector to
read the records written by the first file connector; however, this
may also be platform dependent. That is, it may not be available;
or it may be possible to read, only those records that were available
prior to adding records, with file-name-1; or it may be be that
all records in the file will be available to file-name-2.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <75tigu$6le$1@news.netvision.net.il>,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

More to the point, this can be highly installation dependent.  You need
to know what COBOL compiler your installation uses, what standard JCL
PROC's they have customized and made available for general use, any
special library names you need to use, etc.  You need to get this
information from someone knowledgable with your specific installation or
from documentation created by your specific installation.  The
appropriate COBOL Programming Guide will show how to do this with the
"standard" IBM JCL PROC's, but this still may not be directly compatible
with your specific installation.  (for example, we use ISPF dialogs to
generate all compile JCL and for the most part don't bother to customize
the IBM PROC's to make them functional).
```

```

> More to the point, this can be highly installation dependent.  You need
> to know what COBOL compiler your installation uses, what standard JCL
> PROC's they have customized and made available for general use, any
> special library names you need to use, etc

Yep, right on - fer inst'nce - for me this will work fine:


//D JOB 7000,DWIGHT
//  EXEC  COBLNK,MODULE=STRAWN

but I'd be willing to bet that nobody else here except Thane could use
it without doing a bit of background work!
```

## Thread: VB VSAM files in CICS 4.1 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### VB VSAM files in CICS 4.1

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981223211548.16247.00001020@ng34.aol.com>`

```
after migratin to cics 4.1 + cobol 390, we got problem with EXEC CICS WRITE.
it takes about 10-15 seconds to execute command with producing dump with AEZD
abend. we working with VB VSAM file 575 bytes and 96 bytes fixed part.
we did not have this problem when we used previos versions of cics and cobol
any ideas???
```

#### ↳ Re: VB VSAM files in CICS 4.1

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36821edb.3742190@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19981223211548.16247.00001020@ng34.aol.com>`

```
On 24 Dec 1998 02:15:48 GMT, sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN) wrote:

>after migratin to cics 4.1 + cobol 390, we got problem with EXEC CICS WRITE.
>it takes about 10-15 seconds to execute command with producing dump with AEZD
>abend. we working with VB VSAM file 575 bytes and 96 bytes fixed part.
>we did not have this problem when we used previos versions of cics and cobol
>any ideas???


Abendcode AEZD:  Attempt to run EXEKCEY(USER) program in a transaction with
TASKDATAKEY(CICS) specified.

Solution:  Change Transaction definition to TASKDATAKEY(USER)


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

---

<a id="t-8a56f84c4c"></a>
## Thread: Refromating the Seq File — 4 msgs

### Refromating the Seq File

- **From:** "sankar Ravi" <rsankar@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<760kkf$8fe$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
Hello Cobol Gurus,

I am in the process of converting a sequential file from  one format to
another format:

Example are mentioned below:

File:


DATE        ERRO_CODE        NO OF TIMES OCCURED

981101    2000                            40
981101    2001                            20
981102    2000                            50
981102    2001                            10
981102    2003                            10

'
'
'
'
'

I  Want to reformat  the above  file like:



                             date1        date2            date3
                            981101     981102        981103
error_code

2000                     40             50
2001                     20             10
2003                        0            10


How this can be achieved using cobol?.

Thanks

 Sri
```

#### ↳ Re: Refromating the Seq File

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uO#$OhDM#GA.231@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`
- **References:** `<760kkf$8fe$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
You can use OCCURS DEPENDING and RECORD VARYING integer1 TO integer2.

Using sequential recording mode, you'd have to have some sort of a master
file update scheme (MasterIN, transaction file, MasterOUT), so a variable
length indexed sequential (ISAM) file design would probable be in order for
your purpose.

You'll have to give some thought to limiting the record length to some
finite value, such as one year's worth of error counts per error record.

sankar Ravi wrote in message <760kkf$8fe$1@winter.news.rcn.net>...
>Hello Cobol Gurus,
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Refromating the Seq File

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687A063.39C794FE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<760kkf$8fe$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
This is very basic COBOL, taking in data in one format and outputting it
in another format - I am trying to think if I know of a COBOL program
which doesn't do this.

I guess your question has to do with the fact that you combine many
input files to create an output one.

The hardest part if deciding how many dates you have going across your
report.  The following code fails because the heading is wrong (You
didn't think I would do all your homework for you did you!?!?).   You
will have to decide how you are going to make your headings and handle
too many dates for a page.  You may need to do an internal sort or two
COBOL programs to determine which dates are to be handled or make some
type of business decision to do the same thing.

When I do this, I first make sure the input file is sorted by error-code
and date.

Then I loop:
    (
    READ INPUT-FILE AT END SET SW-EOF TO TRUE.
    PERFORM READ-LOOP UNTIL SW-EOF.
    IF DATE-COUNT (1) > ZERO
         PERFORM WRITE-OUTPUT
    END-IF
    ... 

READ-LOOP.
    IF ERROR-CODE = HOLD-ERROR-CODE
        CONTINUE
    ELSE
        PERFORM WRITE-OUTPUT
    END-IF    

    IF INPUT-DATE = OUTPUT-DATE (DATE-INDEX)
        CONTINUE
    ELSE 
       ADD 1        TO DATE-INDEX-MAX
       IF DATE-INDEX > DATE-INDEX-MAX
           PERFORM OVERFLOW-ROUTINE
       END-IF
       MOVE INPUT-DATE TO OUTPUT-DATE (DATE-INDEX)
    END-IF.
    ADD 1            TO DATE-COUNT (DATE-INDEX).
    READ INPUT-FILE AT END SET SW-EOF TO TRUE.

WRITE-OUTPUT.
    MOVE HOLD-ERROR  TO OUTPUT-ERROR
* May make the following a perform  
    MOVE DATE-COUNT (1)  TO OUTPUT-DATE-COUNT-1
    MOVE DATE-COUNT (2)  TO OUTPUT-DATE-COUNT-2
    MOVE DATE-COUNT (3)  TO OUTPUT-DATE-COUNT-3
    IF LINE-COUNT > MAX-LINES
        PERFORM WRITE-HEADINGS
    END-IF
    WRITE OUTPUT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1
    ADD 1   TO LINE-COUNT
    INITIALIZE COUNT-ARRAY.

sankar Ravi wrote:
> 
> Hello Cobol Gurus,
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Sri
```

## Thread: screens — 3 msgs

### screens

- **From:** "pieter" <ota006654@online.be>
- **Date:** 1998-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7607kg$380$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
Hello,

Does anyone know how to use screens in Cobol?
All information is welcome.
Thanks

Pieter
```

#### ↳ Re: screens

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<761aqb$2pc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7607kg$380$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```

pieter wrote in message <7607kg$380$1@trex.antw.online.be>...

>Does anyone know how to use screens in Cobol?


Yes, most in the NG do, though the methodologies
vary by compiler and platform.
```

#### ↳ Re: screens

- **From:** "Sven" <informatica@freemail.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<762rce$kkn$1@nickel.uunet.be>`
- **References:** `<7607kg$380$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
That is no problem.

You use the screen section, you declare it in the data division, directly
after the working-storage section.

You use entries like
01  S-RECIPE-LIST HIGHLIGHT.
           04  LINE YPOS COL XPOS.
           04  BACKGROUND-COLOR 1 FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
               08  PIC X(15)BB
                   USING LSTRECIPE(LISTRPOS).
               08  PIC Z9 USING LSTRUNIT(LISTRPOS).
               08  VALUE "/" HIGHLIGHT.
               08  PIC Z9 USING LSTRFLOAT(LISTRPOS).
               08  PIC BBX(5) USING LSTRSOLIDUNIT(LISTRPOS).
               08  PIC BZ9 USING LSTRALCPER(LISTRPOS).

Using uses a variable in the W-S or file section.

You use them as follows : display s-recipe-list.
You can use accept to : accept s-recipe-list.  To accept, instead of using
"using" you put "to", as in

     *//ACCEPTS INPUT TO EDIT,CREATE A NEW COCKTAIL
       01  S-COCKTAIL-ACCEPTNEW HIGHLIGHT.
           04  LINE  5 COL 46 PIC X(25)
               TO C-NAME.
           04  S-COCKTAIL-ACCEPTEDIT.
               08  LINE  6 COL 46 PIC X(25)
                   TO C-DESCR.
               08  LINE 7 COL 46 VALUE "         ".
               08  LINE  7 COL 46 PIC 9.999,99
                   TO C-PRICE.

Here you can type "accept s-cocktail-acceptnew
or "accept s-cocktail-acceptedit"

Hope this is any help.


>Hello,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Sven,

If you do use NetExpress without a valid licence key then you
will be breaking the law.  Software piracy is a serious matter
and I would advise you to be very careful before you do this.

Micro Focus does have a University Edition of NetExpress
available.  May I suggest you contact Barbara Tillett at Micro
Focus (bat@microfocus.com) for more details.

Finally, you should not love or hate programming languages.
They are simply tools to do a job.  The skill is selecting the right
tool for a particular task and then being able to wield that tool
in a mature and professional manner to solve a business/technical
problem.  Don't get caught up in all the hype.

Merry Christmas.

Paddy Coleman
Team Leader, Distributed Computing Support (WinTel)
Micro Focus International.
```

<a id="t-8dc5d9c098"></a>
## Thread: Merging variable data with forms — 7 msgs

### Merging variable data with forms

- **From:** "Vic Spainhower" <vicspain@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<763bqh$p0a$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
We are in the process of evaluating Micro Focus COBOL on the PC to handle
custom print jobs. We are a service bureau and we do large print jobs on an
IBM mainframe using Xerox laser printers.

The way it works is the forms are designed using various PC Programs (Corel
Draw) and then loaded to the Xerox printer. We then write a COBOL program to
load the form using Xerox DJDE and merge the customer's data with the form
to produce custom forms such as checks, invoices, statements etc.

Now the problem that I'm struggling with is how to accomplish this without
the mainframe since the forms on the Xerox printers are not accessible. We
have the printers now attached to Sun workstations and can be switched
between the network and the mainframe. When they are switched to the network
they look like postscript printers and supposedly we can use postscript
commands to load forms, select feed trays etc.

I've been trying to setup bitmaps on the PC and see if I can merge variable
data into the bitmap to accomplish the same thing but so far not much luck.
Currently for testing I'm using HP laser printers but when I do the call
"PC_PRINTER_WRITE_BMP"  the return code just says failed to print the bmp. I
did the Load bmp and it apparently was ok according to the return code. I'm
not sure what I'm going to run into after I get the Write bmp to work as far
as that goes.

Is anyone doing anything similar to what I'm trying to do and if so maybe
you have some sample code that you could share.

Thanks Very Much,
```

#### ↳ Re: Merging variable data with forms

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36853e0e.2649178@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<763bqh$p0a$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
On Sat, 26 Dec 1998 11:16:33 -0800, "Vic Spainhower"
<vicspain@sprynet.com> wrote:


>Now the problem that I'm struggling with is how to accomplish this without
>the mainframe since the forms on the Xerox printers are not accessible. We
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You want COBOL Form Print from Flexus:  http://www.flexus.com - you
can download a 60 day eval copy to see how well it works, but by using
the "multi page" form feature, you can load the bitmap then the print.
Or you can map the fields directly onto the bitmap.  Either way - the
result is the same as you desire - pre-designed forms filled with data
from a COBOL program and printed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Merging variable data with forms

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3685EC69.1DBEF059@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<763bqh$p0a$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Vic,

Xerox forms are not BMP they have their own format.  In order to print
the form on a desktop you must have a Xerox DJDE compatible printer or
an emulator.  My recommendation is to purchase a printer which is not
cheap.

I used Lapres for many years successfully.  There is a new product that
supersedes it release from XLprint in Australia (try the web somewhere).

The DJDE format is basically a series of lines with tags on them.  These
tags are referenced to a location by a printer definition which must be
emulated by whatever you are using.  The flow of the positioning is
'weird' it involves adding the previous font depth to the current
position so changing fonts to fit stuff into the forms causes slight
misalignments.  Even when using an emulator you should ALWAYS check the
result on the real printer before going for it (obvious really).

Xerox printers now support AFP printout.  The formats are more complex
but if you use AFP toolbox from IBM then it is not to bad.  This is
viewable using AFP Viewer and will print on far more printers.


Vic Spainhower wrote:
> 
> We are in the process of evaluating Micro Focus COBOL on the PC to handle
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> vic.spainhower@perfected.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Merging variable data with forms

- **From:** "Vic Spainhower" <vicspain@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<769h4a$isq$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<763bqh$p0a$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <3685EC69.1DBEF059@zip.com.au>`

```
Hi Ken,

Yes I realize that Xerox printer forms are loaded using DJDE statements
which is how we are using them when channel attached to the IBM mainframe.
However, what I�m trying to come up with is a way to use printer forms when
they are connected to a LAN using a Sun workstation which is what we have
just installed on 2 of our printers (4635). With this arrangement we can
switch the printer back and forth between the network and the mainframe.
When they are switch to the mainframe print jobs simply queue up in the
Windows NT print queue.

When attached to the Sun they are Postscript printers and we have Windows/95
and NT drivers for them. Now what I would like to do is be able  to design
and develop forms in a WYSIWYG environment and then bring our COBOL programs
off the mainframe and convert them to a PC COBOL which would have this
capability. SO, the only reason I was playing around the bit map commands
was an attempt to accomplish this but it is to crude and I will not pursue
it.

Now, is Lapres some kind of a printer form designer tool for COBOL and is it
something that I should look for?

 Thanks for the reply,

Vic

Ken Foskey wrote in message <3685EC69.1DBEF059@zip.com.au>...
>Vic,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>> Vic Spainhower
>> vic.spainhower@perfected.com
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merging variable data with forms

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3688B227.A8E4243F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<763bqh$p0a$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <3685EC69.1DBEF059@zip.com.au> <769h4a$isq$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Vic Spainhower wrote:
> Now, is Lapres some kind of a printer form designer tool for COBOL and is it
> something that I should look for?
> 

No.  It emulates a Xerox Printer with DJDE's and output's HP or
Postscript.   It could do what you want to do but you want to base this
on Cobol.  There are many other products as well and I don't sell any of
them :-).

Ken
```

```
What we do is send the "resources" (i.e. the Xerox form and any fonts required)
to the printer in as 129 byte blocked data with an "*" in col 129.  Then send
the form data as plain text line by line output with DJDE statements.
```

## Thread: Getting back to programing. Help! — 4 msgs

### Getting back to programing. Help!

- **From:** "Richard J. Wee" <richardwee@stellarnet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<762hm7$hl$1@remarQ.com>`

```
I am getting back into programming after leaving programming six years ago.

I would like to know if there is any major changes to COBOL. I'm looking at
a programming job and would like to know if I would need to retake COBOL
again or if I could just jump back into it.

Before you ask. Yes I have been writing code, but in BASIC for a company
testing pay tables for a casino game.

Richard
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting back to programing. Help!

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<762la1$h4h$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<762hm7$hl$1@remarQ.com>`

```

Richard J. Wee wrote in message <762hm7$hl$1@remarQ.com>...
>I am getting back into programming after leaving programming six years ago.
>
>I would like to know if there is any major changes to COBOL. I'm looking at
>a programming job and would like to know if I would need to retake COBOL
>again or if I could just jump back into it.
[...]

The COBOL language is currently undergoing some major changes.
Although ANSI 85/ISO 89 is still the current standard, a new
standard will be approved (hopefully!) in the year 2000 or so. Many
of the proposed changes have been implemented in current compilers.

As a starting point, you may wish to obtain a compiler and some
reference books.

Read the FAQ < http://www.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm >
for a list of compiler vendors, reference books, etc.

For the compiler, you may wish to download the Fujitsu V3.0
starter set from < www.adtools.com >. It may be downloaded for
free. This is not the most recent version, but is ANSI 85 and
should help you to re-learn the language.

Any text or reference books that you may still have and that were
based on COBOL 85 should be useable. Should you wish to
consider some more recent texts, the authors/contributors to
"COBOL Unleashed" and Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours"
are regular contributors to this newgroup.

Finally, once you have, again, experienced the "joy of COBOL",
you may decide whether taking COBOL classes again is more
appropriate for you.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting back to programing. Help!

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981226113213.22193.00001452@ng140.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<762hm7$hl$1@remarQ.com>`

```
Richard J. Wee writes ...

>I am getting back into programming after leaving programming six years ago.
>
>I would like to know if there is any major changes to COBOL. I'm looking at
>a programming job and would like to know if I would need to retake COBOL
>again or if I could just jump back into it.

It depends a little on what level COBOL you were using before. Although COBOL
85/89 is the standard I have found many shops still coding as if ANSI COBOL 74
(or even '68 for some shops) is all they know, even if they are using the COBOL
II compiler.

<commercial>
We have a two-day course coming up in the Seattle area, Feb 18-19 that might
meet your needs. We call it "Beyond COBOL II"; other, more marketing types,
call it "COBOL for the New Millenium".

In any case, the course covers all the changes to COBOL introduced by COBOL II
(this is a review for some, but really new information to most, I've found) and
then covers the new releases of IBM COBOL: COBOL/370; COBOL for MVS & VM; COBOL
for OS/390 & VM. Since these compilers also require Language Environment (LE),
there is a brief introduction to LE in the class, too. All this for only $500
tuition!

Check our website: www.trainersfriend.com for details on the course, schedule,
etc. If you're interested, send us an email, fax, or give us a call.

</commercial>

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
I also had a layoff of sorts, but what you know already will fly in a modern
shop a vast majority of the time IMHO.  Reference modification, EVALUATE,
END-PERFORM, and END-IF were the only new things in this shop I had to pick up
on.

Richard J. Wee wrote:

> again or if I could just jump back into it.--

Warren Porter
```

```
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x

1. Copying this FAQ.
This FAQ is copyright 1994-1998 by William M. Klein.
It  may  be  freely  redistributed as long as  it  is  completely
unmodified and that no attempt is made to restrict any  recipient
from  redistributing it on the same terms. It may not be sold  or
incorporated  into  commercial  documents  without  the   written
permission of the copyright holder.
Permission is granted for this document to be made available  for
file  transfer from sites offering unrestricted file transfer  on
the Internet and from the COBOL Forums on CompuServe and Bix.
This  document  is  provided as is, without  any  warranty.  Your
mileage may vary.

2. Where can I get this FAQ?
This  document should be archived at many sites on the  Internet,
including  rtfm.mit.edu -- the archive site for all FAQs.  It  is
also     available     via     e-mail     from     the     author
(wmklein@ix.netcom.com).
An HTML version of the latest FAQ is also available from
     http://www.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

2.1 Where do I turn if my question isn't answered in this FAQ?
If you have access to the web, then you should go to
http://www.dejanews.com/home_ps.shtml
Then  enter "comp.lang.cobol" as the group and whatever  you  are
looking  for as the subject. The chances are that you  will  find
more  than enough answers already (and many quite recent) to your
question. If you don't find an answer here, then look at 18. What
can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?

3. Where can I get a COBOL compiler?
There  are many vendors who sell COBOL compilers. Almost  all  of
the  mainframe  hardware/operating system vendors,  also  sell  a
COBOL  compiler for their systems. The following are some of  the
vendors providing COBOL compilers for systems where they are  not
the operating system vendor.

3.1 for DOS, Windows, or OS/2?
Acucorp,  CA,  Fujitsu, Liant, IBM, and Micro Focus  all  produce
compilers  for one or more of these environments. Microsoft  used
to re-badge the Micro Focus compiler, but not any more.

3.2 for Windows/NT or Windows/95?
Micro  Focus has a 32-bit development environment for Windows  95
and Windows/NT called NetExpress. A student version of NetExpress
is also available from Micro Focus. Fujitsu's compiler also works
under  both Windows/95 and Windows/NT. Liant has both 16 bit  and
32  bit  development systems for Windows, Win95 and NT.  Computer
Associates' CA-Realia II Workbench Release 3.0 provides a  32-bit
COBOL  compiler and runtime. IBM's VisualAge for  COBOL  is  also
available for Windows/NT.

3.3 for UNIX?
Acucorp,  Fujitsu, Liant, and Micro Focus have products available
across  a  large  number of UNIX platforms.  Some  OEMs  re-badge
and/or re-engineer these products for their own systems too.
Liant used to provide LPI COBOL for Sun SPARC with Solaris 2,  HP
9000  with  HP-UX and Intel-based machines with UNIX SVR4,  SVR3,
and SCO. They no longer make this product.
IBM sells its COBOLSet for AIX.

3.4 for Linux?
AcuCOBOL-85  and AcuCOBOL-GT are available for Linux.  Also,  the
iBCS2 code for Linux should mean that it is possible to get  some
of the i486 COBOL packages for operating systems such as SCO UNIX
to work.
There  were reports that earlier versions of Micro Focus products
worked   under   SCO  UNIX  emulations  under   Linux.   However,
representatives from Micro Focus doubt that this would  still  be
true with their current compiler technology.

3.5 for the Macintosh?
Acucorp  produces a COBOL development system for the Mac  running
A/UX.

3.6 for other environments?
Fujitsu COBOL is also available for IBM MVS and Fujitsu MSP.
Ryan McFarland COBOL is also available for OpenVMS.
AcuCOBOL-85  and  AcuCOBOL-GT are available on a  wide  range  of
environments, including OpenVMS.
Most  major vendors have their own COBOL implementation, or  have
someone else's ported to their platform(s). There are quite a few
available for CP/M and MP/M, and one is even rumored to have been
available for the PERQ workstation.

4. Is there a free COBOL compiler?
Just for the record, no COBOL tools are listed in the Catalog  of
compilers,  interpreters,  and other  language  tools  posted  to
comp.compilers and comp.lang.misc. This probably means that there
are no freely available COBOL compiler sources.
However, there are a number of free compilers that do not include
the  source. For example Fujitsu does offer a FREE COBOL  Starter
Set  for Windows. (See below for a list of various free compilers
and the environments in which they are available.)
Also  several books in the booklists come with a COBOL  compiler.
See section 10 for details.

4.1 for DOS, Windows or OS/2.
There  is  a freely available COBOL compiler for DOS. It  can  be
found  on  many archive sites, named COBOL650.ZIP. You also  need
DPATH30.ZIP. Have a read through Section 8 before you start.
Bob Wolfe has made the compiler available at the Flexus FTP site,
http://www.flexus.com.
It  is  widely  rumored that the sources for  this  compiler  are
available  from  a BBS. This no longer appears to  be  the  case.
Numerous  attempts  have  completely failed  to  track  down  the
sources.
There  is a COBOL701.ARJ archive that contains a version of COBOL

6.50  with a limited number of compiles. It was an attempt  at  a
full  integrated  development environment, including  an  editor.
Unfortunately, no documentation is included.
Also,  it  may  be  possible  to run the  freely  available  CP/M
compiler (see 4.5) under a freely available CP/M emulator.
In  addition,  Fujitsu  is currently offering  a  copy  of  their
Fujitsu COBOL V2 Enterprise Edition which includes PowerCOBOL  to
those who register at
     http://www.adtools.com/

4.2 for Windows/NT or Windows/95?
Fujitsu  is currently offering a copy of their Fujitsu  COBOL  V3
Starter Set to those who register at
     http://www.adtools.com/
The CP/M compiler/emulator combination should work here too.

4.3 for UNIX?
There are no well-documented examples of a freely available COBOL
compiler for UNIX. COBOL 6.50 might run under a UNIX emulation of
a  DOS  system,  however. (For example, VP/ix, SoftPC  or  dosemu
under Linux.)
The  CP/M compiler (see 4.5) should run under a CP/M emulator for
UNIX in a similar fashion.

4.4 for the Macintosh?
Not that I know of.

4.4a for Amiga?
According to HarriottC@cardiff.ac.uk (on March 2, 1998),
There  is  a  freely available COBOL compiler/interpreter  (Amiga
WB2.0+).  GUI/CLI, largely ANSI'85 compliant takes  the  form  of
Microsoft Cobol 2.xx ?)
This is available as postware via Aminet Dev/lang nrcobol_1?.lha

4.5 for other environments ?
There is a freely available CP/M COBOL compiler/interpreter  (NPS
Micro   COBOL).  This  is  available  via  anonymous   FTP   from
oak.oakland.edu  in  /pub/cpm/cobol.  However,   Stefano   Priola
(s70829@galileo.polito.it) comments :
     "I've  used the CPM COBOL ... I think that this compiler  is
     much too old to use or for a student to learn COBOL."

5. Commercial COBOL Products
In order to present an un-biased list of commercial COBOL product
offerings  I've  pulled  in  the  product  overviews  from   each
company's    marketing   information.   For   detailed    product
descriptions, you should probably contact the specific vendor.

5.1.5 COBOL Workbench� v4.0
Workbench  integrates the industry-standard COBOL  compiler  with
development   and   testing   tools   that   promote   programmer
productivity,  satisfaction and quality systems  development.  It
features   powerful   editing,   debugging   and   communications
facilities  as  well  as graphical data  tools  -  all  within  a
powerful  and  easy to use development environment.  Micro  Focus
COBOL   Workbench  supports  the  creation  and  maintenance   of
enterprise-wide  client-server  applications   in   addition   to
offloading  development and maintenance of mainframe  application
to PCs.

5.1 Acucorp
5.1.1 AcuCOBOL-GT
AcuCOBOL-GT  enables COBOL programmers to implement  and  deliver
full-featured  GUI  COBOL  applications  on  any  of   over   600
platforms,  with full object code portability using a single  set
of   COBOL   source   code.  Programmers  can  create   graphical
applications including floating windows, graphical controls (such
as  entry  fields,  frames,  radio  buttons,  push  buttons,  and
labels),  menu bars, bitmaps, and toolbars using COBOL extensions
that are consistent with traditional COBOL syntax.

5.1.2 AcuCOBOL-85
AcuCOBOL-85  is  a  powerful ANSI-85 COBOL compiler  that  offers
exceptionally compact, highly optimized machine-independent COBOL
code.   AcuCOBOL-85  is  portable  across  over   600   different
platforms,  including: AIX, AOS/VS, BSD/OS, DG/UX, Digital  UNIX,
HP-UX,  Linux,  MS-DOS, MPE/iX, Open VMS, OS/2, Solaris,  Ultrix,
UNIX, UnixWare, Windows, Windows NT, and Xenix.

5.2 Computer Associates/Realia
5.2.1 CA-Realia� II Workbench
CA-Realia� II Workbench provides an exciting mainframe-compatible
COBOL  development environment on the PC. CA-Realia II  Workbench
uses  the  power of the PC environment to improve the development
and   maintenance   of   COBOL  and   CICS   applications.   This
revolutionary  technology provides a new and  exciting  graphical
user  interface (GUI) workstation environment that operates under
MS-Windows, Windows 95, Windows NT, and OS/2.
CA-Realia  II Workbench includes a high-speed COBOL compiler,  an
interactive  debugger,  a  COBOL-Intelligent  analyzer,  a   CICS
emulator,  a  COBOL-sensitive editor, and a complete  life  cycle
manager  with  mainframe  connectivity. Its  latest  version  for
Windows  NT  and Windows 95 includes a 32-bit COBOL compiler  and
runtime.  Its  mainframe  options  allow  mainframe-compiled  and
executing programs to be debugged and analyzed under the friendly
Workbench GUI

5.3 Fujitsu Software
5.3.1 Fujitsu COBOL
Fujitsu  COBOL is the highest performing, mission critical  COBOL
available  for  mainframe, UNIX, and Windows  platforms.  Fujitsu
COBOL supports IBM VS COBOL II, Micro Focus, and the latest  ANSI
and  ISO  standards and allows developers to access,  reuse,  and
retarget   their   existing   legacy   COBOL   applications   for
Client/Server  and Internet platforms while fully exploiting  all
the functions of Microsoft Windows 95/NT and BackOffice.

5.3.2 PowerCOBOL�
PowerCOBOL   is  a  visual,  object  oriented,  enterprise   wide
client/server Rapid Application Development(RAD) tool  for  COBOL
programmers.   The  PowerCOBOL  development  environment   allows
programmers  to  use  their  existing COBOL  knowledge  to  build
scaleable and maintainable state-of-the-art Windows two-tier  and
three-tier  client/server  and Internet  applications  with  data
access to local and remote databases.

5.3.3 NetCOBOL�
Fujitsu NetCOBOL is a revolutionary COBOL compiler that generates
100%  Pure Java Bytecode-based applications/applets from existing
legacy  COBOL programs. NetCOBOL programs execute on any  machine
which  supports  the  Java�  Virtual Machine,  including  Network
Computers.   NetCOBOL  is  the  first  and  only  true   platform
independent COBOL compiler.

5.3.4 PowerBSORT�
PowerBSORT  is a high speed Business SORT, MERGE, and  COPY  tool
for  handling  large quantities of data. PowerBSORT significantly
shortens the time needed to sort, merge or copy data for business
processing.  PowerBSORT provides a record reconstruction  feature
that  creates an output record by relocating and combining fields
from  the input record with self-defined values. PowerBSORT same-
key  feature combines values from multiple records into a  single
record by adding the summation field values when records have the
same  key field value. The same-key feature is used with the sort
or merge functions.

5.3.5 PowerFORM�
PowerFORM  is  a  tool  for  designing  and  programming  printed
reports. These reports can use all fonts supported by the  target
printers  and  can include graphics such as boxes,  shading,  and
bitmaps.  COBOL  applications create  reports  by  writing  to  a
special  print  file, or they can gain greater control  by  using
PowerFORM RTS calls.

5.3.6 PowerGEM Plus
PowerGEM  Plus is a graphical tool for managing the versions  and
histories  of your development resources, including source  code,
screen  forms,  and  documents.  PowerGEM  Plus  makes  resources
available   to  multiple  users  simultaneously,  but   restricts
modification to a single user. Resources are thus protected,  and
modifications  are  traceable. PowerGEM  Plus  keeps  and  tracks
version  and  history statistics for each part of  a  development
project.

5.4 Liant/Ryan McFarland
5.4.1 RM/COBOL Compiler and Runtime System
RM/COBOL's  sophisticated runtime system permits the  maintenance
of  single  source and object code - and the easy  deployment  of
applications  on  a wide choice of open client/server  platforms.
Their  "claim  to fame" is that their compiler generates  objects
that are portable between platforms without recompiling. This  is
why  you need a runtime for the desired platform to interpret the
object

5.4.2 RM/Interface Builder
RM/COBOL developers can now use their choice of industry-standard
tools  such as Microsoft's Visual Basic and Borland's  Delphi  to
develop a true Windows client user interface for Windows or UNIX-
based COBOL application

5.4.3 RM/Enterprise CodeBench
RM/COBOL  developers  can  now  take  full  advantage  of   their
client/server development environments. This new version  of  our
powerful  graphical workbench enables the management of  existing
UNIX- or Windows-based RM/COBOL applications to be handled from a
remote Windows workstation.

5.4.4 RM/Panels
RM/Panels allows you to develop COBOL based applications  with  a
whole new event-driven look-and-feel with true GUI functionality,
while maintaining complete object portability.

5.4.5 Relational Data Bridge (formerly Relativity)
This  product allows you ODBC access to your COBOL data. You  can
update and insert records from ODBC.

5.5 Micro Focus
5.5.1 Micro Focus� Object COBOL v4.0 (32-bit) for OS/2
Micro  Focus  Object COBOL v4.0 is a powerful 32-bit  development
environment,  enabling development and deployment  of  COBOL  and
Object COBOL applications for OS/2.� Micro Focus has brought  the
power of COBOL to the PC with extensive syntax support, including
object  oriented  COBOL, IBM� OS/VS COBOL, DOS COBOL,  COBOL/370�
and  a wide range of other dialects. This release of Object COBOL
extends  the power of previous versions of Micro Focus  COBOL  by
adding   full   support  for  object  oriented  programming   and
exploiting the power of the 32-bit architecture.

5.5.2 Micro Focus� NetExpress V2.0 for Windows NT and Windows� 95
NetExpress  combines Micro Focus Object COBOL� with a specialized
set  of  tools  to  allow  you to develop applications  for  PCs,
distributed environments and the internet. It includes a powerful
integrated   development  environment,  the  Micro  Focus   COBOL
compiler,  a  powerful  debugger, a complete  set  of  tools  for
developing    either   web-based   applications   or    graphical
applications  for  Win32 platforms, full  support  for  accessing
relational  databases, support for creating  ActiveX  objects  in
COBOL and many other features.
A  student  version  of NetExpress V2.0 is  also  available  that
provides  most  of  the  functionality of  the  full  version  of
NetExpress, but with limits on the size of applications that  can
be  developed  and it cannot be used to develop applications  for
sale.

5.5.3 Object COBOL � D e v e l o p e r Suite v4.1 for UNIX
Micro  Focus  Object COBOL Developer Suite v4.1 for  UNIX  is  an
advanced  integrated programming environment for  developing  and
deploying  client  /server  Object  COBOL  and  procedural  COBOL
applications on a wide range of UNIX � operating environments. It
includes:  a flexible, cross-platform COBOL compiler;  a  set  of
powerful  programmer productivity tools; a set  of  Object  COBOL
class libraries; transparent support for large files; support for
DBCS  applications; user interface development tools; new on-line
documentation; the ability to execute COBOL CGI's developed using
NetExpress;  and  run-time  facilities  to  simplify  application
deployment.

5.5.4 Personal COBOL for Windows v3.1, with Object Orientation
Micro   Focus  Personal  COBOL  for  Windows  3.1,  with   Object
Orientation, is an educational version of Micro Focus  COBOL  for
anyone interested in learning procedural COBOL programming or the
new   object-oriented  COBOL  extensions   in   a   Windows   3.1
environment.
Personal  COBOL is ideal for educational institutions,  students,
training organizations and self-learners who need an inexpensive,
yet  full-featured and modern learning tool for  introductory  or
advanced COBOL.

5.6 PCYACC
PCYACC  7.5  is a complete language development environment  that
generates  C, C++, Java, Delphi, and VBS source code  from  input
Language Description Grammars for building Assemblers, Compilers,
Interpreters,  Browsers,  Page  Description  Languages,  Language
Translators,   Syntax  Directed  Editors,  Language   Validators,
Natural  Language  Processors, Expert System  Shells,  and  Query
Languages.  The PCYACC Tool-Kit includes PCLEX, Visual  Debugging
Tools, Object-Oriented Class Library's, and Pre-Written "Drop-In"
Language  engines for virtually every computer  language  in  the
world.
Note the original information stated that support was for the  77
and  90  Standards (which are good years for the Fortran Standard
but  not for COBOL). It is assumed that they actually support the
74 and 85 Standards of COBOL.
Contact them at:
http://www.abxsoft.com/

7. What about COBOL standards?
7.1 What standards exist?
The  current  COBOL standard is the ISO/ANSI '85  standard.  This
replaced the ANSI '74 standard.
There  are  two amendments to the COBOL '85 standard -- intrinsic
functions and corrections.
The  ANSI  (J4) and ISO (WG4) groups are working on  created  the
next Standard. See below for its current status and contents.

7.2 Can I get the standards via FTP?
The standards do not appear to be available via FTP.
     Note:  See  the next section for information on obtaining  a
     copy of the draft of the next COBOL Standard.
This  is probably because they are very large and are copyrighted
by  ANSI  and/or ISO, although the issue of copyright is in  some
doubt.

Whether  or  not  the next COBOL Standard will  or  will  not  be
copyrighted  is  a  matter of discussion  and  argument.  As  the
current  Standard was put into the "public domain" and  the  next
standard is a "derived" work, it is questionable whether  or  not
the  next  Standard  will have an ISO or  ANSI  -  or  any  other
copyright.

7.3 What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard
and what is in it?
NOTE: To see (or download) a copy of the latest draft of the next
COBOL Standard, see
http://www.microfocus.com/Standards/basedoc.htm
First  "9x"  has  become "0x". After the last public  review,  it
turns  out  that there will need to be at least one  more  public
review before it gets approved.
  1.   Major features/enhancements:
       A.   OO gets all the press
       B.   Common exception handling, C-ification (pointers, call
prototypes, typedefs, and everything else needed to use C-type
APIs), user-defined functions, file-sharing/record-locking, 31-
digit numbers, portable arithmetic, National character handling
(MOCS or DBCS but more so), are all some of the "biggies" that
some people are looking for.
      C.  In the category of "old technology" getting a new face lift
and being added to the Standard (as required) see Report Writer
enhancements, VALIDATE feature, and character screen I/O support
via ACCEPT/DISPLAY.
  2.   Little things (and there are too many for me to remember off
     the  top  of  my head) include everything from dynamic  file
     assignment (in the SELECT/ASSIGN statement), assigning multiple
     values via the VALUE statement in tables, sorting tables, hex
     literals, GOBACK verb, apostrophe as quote, bits and Boolean
     support,
          and LOTS more

8. COBOL 6.50
8.1 How do I compile my programs?
It  is  assumed you have installed cobol650.zip in the  directory
C:\COBOL650.  In  install.doc you will find some  information  on
running the compiler.
  1.   Add C:\COBOL650 to the PATH
2.   Run APPEND on C:\COBOL650 :
     APPEND C:\COBOL650
  3.    The  install.doc contained in cobol650.zip  refers  to  a
     program DPATH.COM to be run instead of APPEND. The DOS program
     APPEND seems to work too.
4.   Now you can compile your .cob files as explained in
install.doc.
When  trying  to compile sources in a directory other  than  that
where  the compiler is installed, the compiler terminates without
an  error.  This  restriction is not documented  in  install.doc,
which is probably a result of using APPEND instead of DPATH.
The  compiler accesses drive A:. You should have a disk  in  this
drive.
Peter Mikalajunas adds:
To avoid the need to use drive A:, you should do the following :
subst a: c:\cobol650
When you type A: you will drop into the C:\COBOL650 subdirectory.
The  compiler will behave normally at this point, not  constantly
searching drive A:.
When you are done with a session do the following :
C:
subst a: /D

8.2 How do I link my objects ?
There  is no linker with the COBOL 6.50 compiler. To link objects
you need to use the linker from MS-DOS v3.3 or earlier.
Ralf Laemmel adds :
You  can  use  newer  linkers, especially  from  newer  Microsoft
compiler products, too.
And Peter Mikalajunas has found that :
Tlink  compiled with obj files without complaint, but  the  exe's
were useless. What did work was Link version 5.31.009 which comes
with Visual Basic for DOS. It compiled all obj files I tried  and
the EXEs ran perfectly.
Clinton G. Downing also reports :
The  linker from IBM DOS v2.1 does now work, at least on the PS/2
70. The MS-DOS v3.3 linker works fine, however.
Steve ??? <steve@mado.demon.co.uk> has reported some success with
a linker from the SimTel archives. Look for sl101a.zip.

9. What about OO COBOL?
9.1 ANSI and ISO Work
The  draft  of  the Committee Draft (CD !>!) of  the  next  COBOL
Standard   which  underwent  public  review  in   1997   included
significant  OO COBOL support. Based on the comments received  J4
(ANSI) and WG4 (ISO) or currently revising it (and deleting  some
features.

9.2 Micro Focus
Micro  Focus has an OO COBOL product. It does not conform exactly
to  the  OO COBOL proposal currently being discussed, however  --
the  syntax  is  a  subset of the current  proposal  with  a  few
variations.   Multiple  inheritance,  conformance   and   garbage
collection   are   not   implemented.  Also,   vocabularies   are
implemented  though these are not currently part of the  proposed
standard.

9.3 IBM
IBM offers OO COBOL products for MVS, OS/2, Windows, and AIX. (It
does   not   currently  offer  it  for  OS/400  or   VM).   There
implementation is a subset of s snap-shot of the Standard  as  it
was  in  1995. The syntax is portable across their platforms  and
interfaces with SOM and DSOM.

9.4 Others
I believe that other vendors are at various stages of developing,
testing, and distributing OO COBOL products. For specific product
information,  you  should contact the vendor  (such  as  Fujitsu,
Hitachi, Realia, etc)

To  install the Fujitsu compiler that comes with this  book,  you
will need to enter a serial number that was accidentally left out
of  the  first  printing of the book. It will install  with  this
number:
 103-2001 1699-03317-70168
The first part of the number should already be in the window,  so
all you will have to enter is
99-03317-70168
Another reported problem with the Fujitsu compiler provided  with
this  book  is linking of programs. The following information  on
the  problem and solution was provided by Thane Hubbell on  March
20, 1998.
  "When Linking you will get a warning message that says:
  No Entry Point
  The program continues to link however. It will not run. Here
  are the steps to get it to Link properly, eliminate the
  error message and run.
  >From the WINCOB window - NOT the WORK FRAME window, just
  the plain WINCOB window, click
  OPTION
  Click
  ADD
  Scroll down to MAIN
  Click
  ADD
  Select the option for "Compile as Main Program"
  Click
  OK
  Now the program will compile and link as a main program and
  life will be just wonderful.
  PS: Thanks to Robert S. Robbins for these instructions, I do
  not know if he still monitors the group, but he deserves the
  credit."
```
