11. Is there a COBOL to C converter ?
Asking  this  question anywhere appears to generate much  general
flaming  and  general  language  wars  and  very  little   useful
information.

No  such beast is listed in the free compilers FAQ, but an ad has
appears  in  the US publication "Programmer's Shop  Catalog"  for
COBOL to C (and PL/I to C) translators. Contact :
Micro-Processor Services,
92 Stone Hurst Lane,
Dix Hills, NY 11746
  Tel: (516) 499 4461
  Fax: (516)- 499-4727
E-Mail: info@mpsinc.com, mpsinc@netusa.net
www: http://www.mpsinc.com
http://www.netusa.net/~mpsinc
A toolset for conversion from COBOL to several other languages is
available.  A  tool  first produces structured  diagrams  (Nassi-
Shneiderman)  from existing source files. Structural  errors  are
identified  and you can edit to correct them. Another tool  takes
those  same  diagrams and produces source code in one of  several
languages  (COBOL,  C,  Ada,  Basic, Clipper,  dBaseIV,  Fortran,
Modula 2, Natural, PL/1, etc.)
The  toolset  is called XperCase by Siemens, and is available  in
the US from:
Boston Technical Distribution Corp.
3 Center Plaza, Suite 440
Boston, MA 02108
  Tel: (617) 248-8989
  Fax: (617) 248-8986
Laurent Sabarthez contributed :
  "Some years ago I was Project Leader on a software project
  termed COBTOC (COBol TO C translation). The company is by
  now out of business, but the rights on this product were
  purchased by NSI (Network Solutions Inc., Herndon, VA, USA -
  Emitt McHenry was Chairman).
  COBTOC is actually a translator generator. It can produce a
  specialized translator for any reasonable COBOL dialect,
  given a dialect description very close to the usual syntax
  notation one can find into any COBOL Reference Manual.
  "semantics" peculiarities are also described in this way.
  Once a translator has been produced in this way, a source
  management module allows automated translation of the COBOL
  source modules. A run-time library is also automatically
  produced as a by-product of the translator.
  The COBTOC user gets a set of C files, each being the
  translation of a corresponding COBOL file. You can get K&R
  C, ANSI C, or common variants like Turbo C. The overall
  structure of the COBOL program is preserved upon
  translation. Identifiers are straightforward transformations
  of COBOL names. Paragraph structure and flow control are
  also preserved, like all name space properties attached to
  I/O and file management.
  The C files are compiled and linked with the run-time
  library, which supports data handling, edition, arithmetic,
  direct I/O, file I/O and transaction management (e.g. CICS).
  Executables are intended to run on any platform supporting
  POSIX C compiling and standard library linkage.
  COBTOC was left by my co-workers and me in an alpha release
  state, mid 1993.
  I don't know the end of the story, but NSI should provide
  more up-to-date information about it.
  There is also a project running to create a COBOL to C
  converter (possibly COBOL to C++ ?) available under the GNU
  license. "
  Another project that can be viewed as either a COBOL to C
  converter or as a compiler, is the "CobCy Project"
For    more    details,    see    the    CobCy    homepage     at
http://www.netsis.it/~asantini/cobcy/.

12. COBOL code generators
12.1 CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation
CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation) are complete
solutions for designing, generating and maintaining your mission-
critical  COBOL and PL/1 applications. High-level, non-procedural
design  and prototyping, combined with automatic code generation,
result  in  higher  productivity, consistent standards  and  good
programming  practices. With CA-Telon PWS, you can  offload  your
application  development projects from the mainframe and  provide
your   programmers   with  a  complete  application   development
environment on the workstation.
The CA-Telon Design Facility (TDF) provides a "fill-in-the-blank"
approach  for  designing online and batch programs. An  automated
prototyping facility assists in the analysis/design phases of the
life  cycle. CA-Telon generates structured portable  code  for  a
multitude  of target environments and DBMSs. Available for:  MVS,
DOS, OS/2

12.3 ETK
ETK (Easy ToolKit) was developed by SEMA Group in Belgium. It  is
now marketed by TOSC International, Inc :
TOSC International, Inc.
3900 Essex Lane, suite 250
Houston, TX 77027
  Tel: 713 961 1201
  Fax: 713 961 4989
Contact Ted Langlie. TOSC will also soon be on the Internet.
ETK   works   with  a  repository  where  the  programmer/analyst
describes  the  system to be generated in terms of data  domains,
elements,  tables,  relationships,  business  rules,   and   data
mappings.  From  this description ETK generates  complete  COBOL-
based applications that will run on many platforms.

13. COBOL Tools
This  section  documents  some  of  the  add-on  tools  that  are
available  for use with COBOL compilers. Further submissions  are
welcomed,  but  please try to keep them as  free  from  marketing
"hype" as possible.
Note: This section seems to be entirely devoted to tools that run
in  or  are  targeted  at  the PC and/or  Unix  world.  Mainframe
additions are welcome, but will probably be a while before I  can
add them.

13.1 creating GUI's
13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL.
VanGui  consists  of two major components: a design  tool  and  a
runtime  system.  The design tool is a Windows application  which
provides  COBOL developers with the capability to define windows,
populate  those windows with standard Windows and  VBX  controls,
adjust  the properties of those controls and attach COBOL  event-
handling logic to their events.
The  VanGui  Runtime  is  a  Windows .DLL  that  manages  Windows
messages, provides runtime support for the controls, and provides
a COBOL interface to the Windows API.
For more information on VanGui, see 13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL
http://www.liant.com/products/language/rm/ib.html.
For another GUI tool from Liant, see 5.3.4 RM/Panels

13.1.2 From Acucorp
AcuBench

AcuBench  is an integrated development environment for ACUCOBOL.-
GT. Available for the Windows 95 and
Windows    NT   operating   systems,   AcuBench   combines    the
internationally acclaimed ACUCOBOL-GT
(Graphical Technology) compiler and runtime system with a set  of
graphically-based,  GT-optimized  WYSIWYG  screen  Painter,   and
language sensitive source Code Editor.

ACUCOBOL�-GT

ACUCOBOL�-GT enables COBOL programmers to implement  and  deliver
full-featured GUI COBOL
applications on any of over 600 platforms, with full object  code
portability using a single set of COBOL
source   code.  Programmers  can  create  graphical  applications
including floating windows, graphical
controls  (such  as  entry fields, frames,  radio  buttons,  push
buttons, and labels), menu bars, bitmaps, and toolbars
using COBOL extensions that are consistent with traditional COBOL
syntax.

13.1.3 Flexus COBOL spII
COBOL spII allows the COBOL programmer to create GUI or character
mode  screens  using ANSI standard COBOL CALL  USING  statements.
COBOL  spII screen definitions and source programs are 100% COBOL
compiler independent, 100% operating system independent and  100%
text mode to GUI mode independent.
Automatic   screen   conversion  tools   for   many   proprietary
environments  are also available from Flexus. These automate  the
task  of converting screen definitions from proprietary character
mode screens to GUI screen definitions.

13.1.4 CA-Visual Realia
[From CA's Web site:]
CA-Visual  Realia  is a complete Windows development  environment
that  provides COBOL programmers with an easy-to-use set of tools
for creating mission-critical GUI client/server applications. CA-
Visual  Realia  allows you to create Windows  applications  using
COBOL  for  the  business  logic  and  GUI  tools  for  the  user
interface.  CA-Visual Realia combines tools designed for  Windows
user   interface   development  with   powerful   COBOL   program
development tools. Creating a program's user interface can be  as
easy  as  dragging  controls onto forms, and then  modifying  the
control's properties.

13.1.5 Fujitsu PowerCOBOL
PowerCOBOL   is  a  visual,  object  oriented,  enterprise   wide
client/server Rapid Application Development(RAD) tool  for  COBOL
programmers.   The  PowerCOBOL  development  environment   allows
programmers to use COBOL as the GUI scripting language  to  build
scaleable  and maintainable state-of-the-art COBOL-based  Windows
two-tier  and  three-tier client/server and Internet applications
with data access to local and remote databases.

13.2 Sinc Inc. products
13.2.1 FlexGen 4GL Rapid Application Development Environment
A  repository-based, data dictionary-driven  tool  set  and  code
generator. It supports and generates code for RM/COBOL,  Acucorp,
Micro Focus COBOL, Realia (DOS Only), mbp (DOS Only) and runs and
deploys under DOS, Windows, Windows 95, NT, many flavors of UNIX,
VAX/VMS and Open VMS.

13.2.2 Legacy Liberator COBOL Migration Toolset
For   rehosting  proprietary  COBOLs,  converting  them  to  open
systems,  Acucorp, RM/COBOL or MF COBOL. User interfaces  can  be
converted  to  FlexGen user interfaces including the  ability  to
upgrade them to GUI.

13.2.3 Easy Query
A point-and-shoot report writer and query tool.

13.2.4 ODBC Access Tools
Allows Windows ODBC-enabled applications to access COBOL data.

13.3 Siber Systems
13.3.1 CobolTransformer
CobolTransformer  (SCT) is a next generation Cobol  reengineering
and  parsing toolkit (SDK). By using CobolTransformer components,
developers  are  able  to develop Cobol-transforming  (or  Cobol-
analyzing) product or project faster and the resulting tools  are
of much higher quality.

CobolTransformer is a library with API in C++ that includes:
  �    High quality Cobol Parser that parses 12 most popular Cobol
     dialects and that has tremendous error recovery capability.
  �     C++ library that client uses to browse and transform  the
     tree-based Internal Representation of Cobol programs. Definition-
     Use links attached to the Program Tree effectively make it a
     general case Graph.
  �     PrettyPrinter that transforms our Internal Representation
     back into beautifully indented
     human-readable Cobol program.

13.3.2 Cbl-Beau
CobolBeautifier does the following things:
  a.   Beautifully indents your Cobol program.
b.   Renumbers paragraph-names, section-names, data-names
c.   attach an increasing or decreasing prefix or suffix to all
paragraph-names and/or section-names and/or data-names in your
Cobol program. (Based on CobolTransformer.)
A   free   version  is  available  from  their   Web   site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
The   Commercial   version  will  allow  to  specify   your   own
indentation/pretty-printing rules, Program  you  style  rules  in
CobolTransformer and make all programs to comply automatically.
To be available soon.

13.3.3 Mf2Fsc
Mf2Fsc is a Micro Focus COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL converter.
It   converts  Micro  Focus  Cobol-85  to  Fujitsu  Cobol-85  and
beautifies the program at the same time.
A   free   prototype  is  available  from  their  web  site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
A commercial version will be available soon.

!3.4 What about Year 2000 Tools?

13.4.1 CA-Fix/2000�
CA-Fix/2000,   a   mainframe-based  application-at-a-time   COBOL
remediation  tool,  performs rapid automated  date  analysis  and
correction of your COBOL source code to provide the fastest  path
to  Year  2000  compliance. CA-Fix/2000 eliminates  the  tedious,
error-prone  tasks of finding and correcting the date  fields  in
your programs. Because it can correct an entire application in  a
single step, CA-Fix/2000 helps ensure consistent corrections even
when  date  fields  are  passed from one program  to  another  or
records  are  redefined.  CA-Fix/2000 can  determine  which  date
fields  are  at  risk  for failing to handle the  century  change
correctly  and corrects only these at-risk fields,  limiting  the
impact on programs and reducing testing requirements.

13.5 Misc. tools

13.5.1 Flexus WinPrint
COBOL  WinPrint allows the COBOL programmer to make ANSI standard
COBOL CALL USING statements to completely control and communicate
with   the   Windows  Print  Manager.  Forms  may   be   designed
interactively  with the Forms Editor to include bitmaps,  special
fonts,  colors and many other modern features. These reports  may
be viewed on the screen prior to printing or sent directly to the
Print Manager.

14. Other sources of information.
14.1 CompuServe
CA has a Forum. To get to this, use
     GO CAIDEV
Liant has a Forum too, to support the RM/COBOL and Relativity(TM)
family of products. To access it, use
     GO LIANT
14.2 Bix
There is a COBOL forum on Bix. Don Nelson is the moderator.

14.3 Micro Focus Faxback
In  Palo  Alto,  Micro Focus runs a system for obtaining  certain
technical information by fax. You need a tone-dial 'phone to  use
it.
  The number is (415) 496 7170.

14.4 Micro Focus WWW server
Micro Focus has a WWW server covering many COBOL issues. The
URL is http://www.microfocus.com/

14.5 CA WWW server
CA also has a WWW server. It's URL is http://www.cai.com/
14.6 Liant and Ryan McFarland WWW server
Liant has a WWW server at http://lsc.liant.com/. This server also
hosts the RM WWW site at http://lsc.liant.com/rm/.

14.7 The Acucorp WWW server
This is at http://www.Acucorp.com/.

14.8 The COBOL Foundation
Dave  McFarland,  formerly  of  Ryan  McFarland,  has  begun   an
organization  aimed at promoting COBOL and providing  information
to  and about the COBOL community. Members (including RM, MF, and
IBM)  pay  yearly  dues  and  in  return  are  included  in   the
Foundation's promotion efforts, literature, directories, etc. and
have  their  company  and  product  information  posted  on   the
Foundation's web server. The URL for the server is
http://www.cobol.org/

14.9 The IBM COBOL products WWW server
IBM  has  enhanced  the  COBOL you currently  use  with  powerful
features  to  increase  development  productivity,  simplify  the
maintenance of your legacy code, and provide seamless portability
from your host to your workstation. Whether you're maintaining or
reengineering   legacy  code  or  creating  new   object-oriented
client/server  applications, IBM's COBOL family  offers  you  the
year  2000 ready application development environment designed  to
do the job right.

IBM  COBOL  provides  a  complete offering of  compatible,  cross
platform,  cross product compilers which support OS/2�,  OS/390�,
MVS�,  VM,  VSE/ESA�, AS/400�, AIX�, and Windows�. IBM gives  you
the tools you need to tackle your COBOL year 2000 challenge while
leveraging  your existing applications. IBM COBOL  also  provides
the  tools you need to amplify your program development, enabling
you  to  position your enterprise to take advantage of tomorrow's
technologies.
See http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/

14.10 The Flexus WWW server
Contains all sorts of information, including a copy of the  COBOL
650 compiler. It's at http://www.flexus.com/

14.11 COBOL User Groups
There are a large number of Micro Focus User Groups. Rather  than
reproduce  the list here and have it constantly out of  date,  it
can be found at
http://pita.microfocus.com/UserGroup_PC/
The  list  and the web pages are maintained by Debbie  Vincent  <
tommygigio@aol.com>.

15. Information required for the FAQ.
Corrections  and  additions  to  existing  material  are   always
welcome. I'd like to add a section of reviews of different  COBOL
books. If I am sent any reviews I will collate them and add these
to the FAQ. More information on mainframe COBOL products would be
useful.
A section covering COBOL programming could be worthwhile.
Note:  I still have a "folder full" of comments sent to the  last
owner of this FAQ that have yet to be applied. It is my intent to
get them verified and applied as soon as possible.

16. Contributors to the FAQ.
Many  people  have  contributed  to  this  FAQ  in  each  of  its
iterations. In the past an ongoing list of these people have been
included  within  the  FAQ.  At this time,  i  am  stopping  this
practice, although I ,in no way, want to deprecate all  the  work
and  input  that these people have provided. I have continued  to
identify people whom I am quoting (directly or indirectly) and do
still  hope  that everyone who has input for this  document  will
continue to provide it.

17. What about the Y2K (Millenium) Issue?
  �    Yes, the year 2000 IS a leap year
  �     Yes, the millenium really starts on January 1, 2001 - but
     the software "bug" refers to January 1, 2000 (and several other
     dates)

17.1 Where can I get information about the Y2K problem?
The  Year 2000 Information Center home page on the World Wide Web
is at
     http://www.year2000.com.
The Information Center has a countdown clock, articles on various
aspects  of the problem, vendor information, this FAQ, and  links
to related information.
There is an Internet mailing list operated by Peter de Jager  for
discussions  of year 2000 computer problems. The  list  has  over
1200  members,  and  gets an average of  about  25  messages  per
business day. This is a moderated mailing list managed by a  paid
administrator and fully supported by its members on a "shareware"
basis.  Each member is asked to pay a subscription fee of US  $50
yearly. (You may contribute more if you wish.)
New subscribers get a 30-day free trial.
To  subscribe, select the "Year 2000 Announcement List" link from
the    Year    2000    Information   Center    home    page    at
http://www.year2000.com. On the subscription form, select the Yes
adio  button  for the Discussion List. You can also subscribe  by
sending e-mail to 2k@tor.hookup.net with
     SUBSCRIBE
in  the  subject line of the message. Subscriptions are processed
manually,  so  please  be  patient. The list  can  optionally  be
received  in digest form instead of individual messages. Invoices
and  receipts are available if needed. Details will be sent  when
you subscribe.
The  Year  2000 Forum on CompuServe has discussions of  all  year
2000 issues, and a collection of files, including this FAQ. There
are over 1000 members. To reach the forum, GO YEAR2000.
The  Usenet  newsgroup comp.software.year-2000  is  dedicated  to
discussions of year 2000 computer issues.
The  current  version  of this Year 2000 FAQ  is  available  from
several web sites, an FTP site, and on CompuServe. It is in ASCII
text form at all these sites.
On the Year 2000 Information Center home page at
http://www.year2000.com,
select  the  "Year 2000 Archive" link. The FAQ is the  last  item
listed on the Archive page

18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.
This means that there is no controlling authority regulating  the
content  of the posts in those groups. Anyone is able  (but  most
assuredly  not welcome) to post anything, relevant  to  COBOL  or
not.   Off-topic  posts  (and  particularly  prolonged  off-topic
threads)are  discouraged, but it is impossible to  prevent  them.
Different  readers of these newsgroups have different  thresholds
for  tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you  post,  the
more  likely  it  is  that  some  readers  will  simply  apply  a
"killfile"  to all of your posts - often after sending  you  (and
possibly the newsgroup) rather pointed comments on your use/abuse
of the newsgroup.
You  should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that  will
help  you  get the most out of both your viewing and postings  at
these sites.
  �     Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially
     for technical issues related to COBOL.
  �    Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted
     your inquiry is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the
     more likely you are to receive a useful and prompt response. (If
     you  know that you are leaving the current subject, consider
     changing the subject line in your post.)
  �     When asking about a specific structure, error message, or
     situation, it is always best to specify both the compiler that
     you are using and the operating system where you are working.
     (What you may think is a general COBOL question may actually be
     very specific to your current environment.)

Note:  For  those  who  are interested,  the  "charter"  for  the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup can be found at:
ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/
comp.lang.cobol

18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes,  you  can get help with your homework via these  newsgroups.
HOWEVER, that does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT
find many participants who will be very happy if you ask them  to
do  your  homework FOR YOU. Some hints that you  should  consider
when drafting your post for assistance with homework include:
  �     Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking  for
     homework help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good
     at sniffing out those who try and pose homework questions as
     "business need" questions - and they are not very polite  in
     replying to such questions).
  �     Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating
     system  your  homework is targeted at.  Solutions  may  vary
     significantly based on this. (You might also want to include what
     text book you are using.)
  �     The  more information that you can give that demonstrates
     that you really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using
     your text book, class material/presentations, lab assistance,
     etc), the more likely it is that you will get useful and friendly
     responses. If you let us know what you have found on your own and
     why you are still uncertain or confused, you will usually get
     helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
     without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very
     pointed replies.

NOTE:  if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't  want  to
see  the  "Do  your own homework" notes that occur in  the  COBOL
newsgroups), you might want to try,
     http://www.tutoraid.org/

18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what
should I include?
These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings.
Some  participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of  the
bandwidth  was  spent on job postings, but,  for  those  who  are
looking for positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful.
There  are, however, some guidelines that you should follow  when
posting  positions  - unless you like getting  abusive  and  non-
responsive replies to your postings.
  �     Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or  your
     best information on what rates are available. Phrases like "based
     on experience" and "competitive" are bound to receive replies
     questioning your motives. The assumption is that ALL such posts
     are "trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches.
  �     Try  to give the best summary of job location and desired
     expertise in the subject line of your posting. This will assist
     you  in  attracting  readers that are  actually  potentially
     interested in your openings.
  �     The  newsgroups  are international  in  nature,  but  are
     dominated by Americans. If you are posting a position outside the
     US,  please make it clear whether foreigners are or are  not
     welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other paperwork is
     required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
     requirements, please make that clear from the start.
  �     In general, the more information that you provide in your
     postings, the better the match will be from those who reply. If,
     however, you are "attaching" a job description, please  make
     certain that you make it clear what type of document-reader is
     required to process it AND make certain that you have  virus
     scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE:  For  those looking for jobs, you might want to  check  out
Dice at
     http://www.dice.com

Appendix A. Changes in recent revisions
This  appendix  documents the major changes  that  were  made  to
create the various (recent) versions of this document.
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Huge  numbers of changes! Version numbering jumped from 1.xxx  to
2.0 to reflect this.
FAQ availability details.
Added section on Commercial COBOL Products.
Removed 'what happened to Realia?' section.
Removed references to Wang, as I can find no information  on  any
Wang COBOL Products.
Checked all URLs, and updated/added as appropriate.
Removed reference to IBM 'COBOL Products WWW site'.
Added  entries  in  'GUI Tools' section for  Micro  Focus  Dialog
System and Net Express, Flexus COBOL spII, and CA-Visual Realia.
Removed  ADS/Online from 'Code Generators' section as  it's  not,
apparently.  Also noted that Cullinet have been acquired  by  CA,
who now sell the Telon product.

Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
  �    Significant reformatting
  �    Updated OO COBOL Section
  �    Add URL for IBM COBOL Web site
  �    Added information on "COBOL for Dummies"
  �    Added initial Y2K section
  �    Added information on using the newsgroups
  �    Added information on PCYACC

Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
  �    Started adding Logos
  �    Removed the detailed list of FAQ contributors
  �    Fixed erroneous Standards information
  �    Corrected information about Liant (who acquired RM)
  �    Added information on Amiga freeware compiler
  �    Added information on Siber tools
  �    Corrected CA company and product information
  �     Added information on where to turn for information not in
     the FAQ
  �    Rephrased the information on what can/should be put in the
     newsgroups.
  �    Revised the information on the CobCy project/product
  �    Updated Micro Focus information
  �    Added URL for "Dice" for those looking for COBOL jobs.
  �    Added information on getting a "COBOL Tutor"

Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x
  �    Added and corrected information on Fujitsu
  �    Re-instated the information on obtaining a copy of the draft
     COBOL Standard.
```

<a id="t-5a6ad6b938"></a>
## Thread: IBM and Mainframe Jobs and Resumes Board — 1 this month / 2 total

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM and Mainframe Jobs and Resumes Board

- **From:** TandemTal@aol.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<763gf7$23m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
The IBM and Mainframes Jobs and Resumes Board is now not only Fixed but also
has some new features such as Jobs, Resumes, Consultant, Counsulting Searchs:

1. When you get to the board there is an option to be notified via EMAIL of
any  new posts. You may say well "SO WHAT !". Well here are some reasons why
you  might want to do this;

   (a) You are looking for Mainframe Talent ("Employees or Consultants") for
       yourself or a client ("Agent").

       When someone posts a resume or is looking for a Mainframe Postion you
       will receive an EMAIL notice at the same time that the message is
       posted on the board.

   (b) You have posted your resume or are looking for Mainframe work.

       As soon as an employer or agent posts a job offer you will receive
       an EMAIL at that same time.

  (c) If you have already posted a message on the board and someone replies 
on the board to your message you will receive an EMAIL at that same time.

2. Employers, Agents and sometime Mainframe Job seekers are normally charged
   some type of FEE. This creates overhead for both sides.

   If you are the Mainframe Job seeker, you may have been able to get a better
   salary or hourly rate if the employer or agent could have cut their cost to
   locate you.

   Since this board is FREE, that can help this problem.

3. The Mainframe Job Market is and has been growing rapidly, one estimate is
   that 2 out of every 5 Mainframe positions are unfilled.

   If you are looking for a new  job or contract would it not be nice to have
   several options on where or who you could do work for ?

4. Finally with Y2K as well as many NEW systems being used for 
Internet/Intranet purposes, and it is after all the beginning of a new year, 
meaning that many corporations are getting new Budget money to hire both 
employees as well as consultants, it can't hurt.

We do NOT make ANY money providing the board as a service to the IBM and
Mainframe community, in the future the we may use banner ads but at this time
we have not done so.

This is your Board, we will make changes as we get suggestions from you as how
we can make it better.

Intenet IBM and Mainframe Programming Job and Resume Board ("NEW")

http://www.trav-tech.com/ibm

 Again Thank you, and Happy holidays, wishing you all the best for the New
Year

Don Kennedy

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

## Thread: Signed Numbers arrrgghhhh! — 4 msgs

### Signed Numbers arrrgghhhh!

- **From:** The Chameleon <sarvec@letterbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36858249.4E84@letterbox.com>`

```
Don't worry, it's not my homework, it's self study before I start COBOL
*again* in January.  :-)
Arrgh! Simple problem has me frustrated. I need to print an * if the
annual salary is greater than 29,000 and the person is married.
A sample of the input data:
Bessy the Cow           Married                               1784
input record: the variable used to read in the hourly rate.
05  salary         pic s9(4)v99.

the picture clause to display it.
05  pr-annual    pic +++,+++.99.

The working storage variable to hold annual salary after a compute. 
01  annual             pic S9(6)   value zeros.

The compute statement:
compute annual = (salary * 2080).

The if statement:
if mstatus equals 'Married' and annual is greater than 
     29000 then move '*' to pr-asterick
         else move spaces to pr-asterick
     end-if.
And finally! The output from these code snippets:
Name                    Marital Status     Hourly    Annual
Salary              
Bessy the Cow               Married        17.84      
+37,107.00               
Harold the Knife            Married        16.64       +34,611.00        

The problem defined.
There needs to be a * next to all persons who make more than 29000 for
an annual salary.
I have snipped the if statement to read:
if mstatus equals 'Married' then move '*' to pr-asterick
         else move spaces to pr-asterick
     end-if.
and only the married people receive an * beside their annual salary.
The number displayed in the output are positive, at least I think they
are judging by the + sign leading them.
Any help would be appreciated.
Casey
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed Numbers arrrgghhhh!

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10ef69bb50cf948f9896ed@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<36858249.4E84@letterbox.com>`

```
In article <36858249.4E84@letterbox.com>, sarvec@letterbox.com says...
=> Don't worry, it's not my homework, it's self study before I start COBOL
=> *again* in January.  :-)
=> Arrgh! Simple problem has me frustrated. I need to print an * if the
=> annual salary is greater than 29,000 and the person is married.
=> A sample of the input data:
=> Bessy the Cow           Married                               1784
=> input record: the variable used to read in the hourly rate.
=> 05  salary         pic s9(4)v99.

I doubt this is the problem, but where is the decimal point on the 
input data?

<snip>

=> I have snipped the if statement to read:
=> if mstatus equals 'Married' then move '*' to pr-asterick
=>          else move spaces to pr-asterick
=>      end-if.
=> and only the married people receive an * beside their annual salary.
=> The number displayed in the output are positive, at least I think they
=> are judging by the + sign leading them.
=> Any help would be appreciated.
=> Casey

Your logic seems to be correct, but post some code so we can have a look 
it over. 
```

- **Message-ID:** `<36859553.24993538@news1.ibm.net>`

<sarvec@letterbox.com> wrote:


>the picture clause to display it.
>05  pr-annual    pic +++,+++.99.

#### ↳ Re: Signed Numbers arrrgghhhh!

- **From:** mikec54@aol.com (MikeC54)
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981228012036.12881.00002325@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36858249.4E84@letterbox.com>`

```
Do you want an '*' to appear if the person makes more than 29,000 and is
unmarried?  As you've coded it, the '*' will appear if the person makes more
than 29,000 and is married.  if the person is not married, then the '*' will
not appear.  I suggest you use an evaluate (if you are codeing in cobol 2) and
the problem will be simplified for you.  If you use the If - End-if form why
not break it down like this    
If married
   if more than 29000
      ..........
   else
     ..........
   end-if
else
  if more than 29000
     ........
  else
    ........
end-if

It might be easier for you to see

Remember if within an if is = to an and
```

---

<a id="t-24ad533ea7"></a>
## Thread: So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM! — 62 this month / 67 total

### So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@replay.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>`

```
There is a front page story about Y2K in today's _The New
York Times_ featuring, in large part, the Y2K goings-on at
Public Service Electric and Gas of New Jersey.

Here is a pertinent excerpt:

        One of those lines contained the name of a 25-
     digit serial number.  Suspicious, Mrs. Zavlyanova zeroed
     in on this segment and there it was: a millennium bug!
     Six of the digits in the serial number referred to a date:
     two for the month, two for the day, and only two for the
     year.

     [The next paragraph, snipped, goes on to detail the 
     logistical problems of expanding the serial number to 27
     digits to accommodate two more digits for the century, with
     no mention being made of the probability of a monumental
     file conversion.]

        Before she undertook this task, however, Mrs. Zavlyanova
     checked and found that another programmer who had encoun-
     tered the serial number in another part of the program
     had already expanded it.  But that programmer apparently
     made an error, so more work would be needed.

        "Y2K work isn't easy as a lot of people think," said
     Mr. Zavlyanova, who has on her desk a certificate praising
     her as a top performer.


Guffaw!  Guffaw!  Guffaw!

First of all, the discovery of a two digit year does *not*
equate to the discovery of the dreaded millennium bug.

If the date is part of a serial number, then it is probably
just a date stamp, and as such, if it doesn't go through a
date comparison nor date calculation, it is harmless.  (This
is why I laugh when people are concerned about chips in
toasters malfunctioning.  In all probability, the dates
of those chips, if they do in fact exist, never go through
a date comparison nor date calculation which will cause
problems.)  As a matter of fact, even certain date calculations
are harmless.  Say you have date 12/15/99 and you want to
add 390 days to it.  If the program handles the rollover to
the next century by properly and logically lopping off the high
order digit(s) of the year coming out of the date calculation
resulting in "01" for the year of the final answer, then
there is no problem here (otherwise, of course, remedial action
is warranted!)

So here we have a company that foolishly delayed high
priority work seeking to fix that which may not need to be
fixed at all!  And if they expand the 25-digit serial
number to a 27-digit serial number, then they must be
asking for an onerous file conversion because what serial
number exists in main storage like an intermediate
result of a calculation without hitting a disk file some-
where?

The above, of course, is based on some supposition, but
what sane programming project allows more than one
programmer to work on the same program at the same time.
It's bad enough when one programmer is assigned to one
thing while other programmers are handling drivers and
subroutines and such upon which the first programmer is
relying, but to have two programmers monkeying around with
the same program is total stupidity!


Now here's the best stupidity:

        To save time, PSE&G is not expanding years to four
     digits in most programs, but is instead using a technique
     called windowing.  Years are left as two digits, but the
     computer is instructed to interpret 00 to 49 as being 2000
     to 2049 and 50 to 99 as 1950 to 1999.  Of course, if the
     program is still in use in 2050, it will have to be fixed
     again.


"To save time???"  "TO SAVE TIME!?!?!"

Don't those bozos know that there are windowing techniques that
will last indefinitely and which don't involve more than a
tiny bit of extra effort??

And by the way, remedial action may have to be taken again
earlier than 2050 depending on the nature of future dates
that come into the system.

And no wonder no one from PSE&G reads this newsgroup.
Apparently all the programmers there these days speak
Russian!  Read the article in the newspaper!
```

the logical processes which lead up to your concluding that it was a Good

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<768uhi$dc0$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com> <768nsd$h5t$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <768pbs$4s0$1@clarknet.clark.net> <914880016.8091.0.nnrp-05.9e98616e@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
In article <914880016.8091.0.nnrp-05.9e98616e@news.demon.co.uk>,
Eoghan Maher <Eoghan@mesmer.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <768pbs$4s0$1@clarknet.clark.net>...
>
>My apologies for not snipping - an oversight in excitement.

Not to worry, overmuch... errrr... you *did wash your hands afterwards,
didn't you?

>
>I do object to a rant behind the cover of anonymity.

And so, I'm sure, would the *real* Mr Maher... if we ever might find him.

>All rants should be
>conducted in the spirit of open debate, allowing proper reply and due
>consideration.

I believe what we have here is a difference in terminology.  A rant, by
definition, brooks no reply... quite the reverse, it usually engenders
avoidance seen in Major Metropolises when one passes a disheveled fellow
of questionable habits of hygiene declaiming the Obvious Evils of (insert
your ideology here).  The less said, the better, and for heaven's sake
*don't* make eye-contact!

If the activity allows for reply then it falls, obviously, out of the
category of rant and lands minimally into the category of Holding Forth.
Holding Forth is similar to ranting but it is usually a bit more
coherent... but just a *bit*.  The only appropriate reply to a Holding
Forth is... another Holding Forth.

'Aborigines?  You think you know about Aborigines?  Let me tell you,
bucko, you don't know the least bit about 'em... now I'll tell you, when I
was down 'round Christchurch way, back when Men was Men, *we* had dealin'
with Aborigines...'

'Christchurch?  You call that a town?  Well, no *wonder* your thinkin's as
woolly-headed as it is, what with spendin' time in Christchurch!  Now for
clear-headedness ya need some time in Sydney, ya does...'

I trust the difference is clear, now?

>In that way, we can have specious maxims, and general
>accusations with a great deal more enjoyment and clarity.

Maxims?  Sorry, you've been out in the boonies too long; all the
up-to-date folks have been fitted out with Vickers, maybe even a few
Brownings!

>Talking to a computer is no fun unless there is a sense of someone behind
>it! ;-)

I'm sorry, Dave... I can't do that.

DD
```

>No. People will continue to COMIT them. Next question?

Does that mean all that can be done is to rollback and enjoy it?

>
>"Al Gol is divided into three parts. Co Bol is divided into 4." J.C., in his Golic
>Wars.

I once knew a fellow who had so much gall he could have been divided into
three parts, too.

DD
```

> >> >If the activity allows for reply then it falls, obviously, out of the
> >> >category of rant and lands minimally into the category of Holding Forth.
> >> >Holding Forth is similar to ranting but it is usually a bit more
> >> >coherent... but just a *bit*.  The only appropriate reply to a Holding
> >> >Forth is... another Holding Forth.

Of all the languages which I have come and gone which I have tried
(COBOL has come and stayed), I liked Forth the best.

##### ↳ ↳ Anonymity (was So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<76bkk2$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com> <914785357.263.0.nnrp-07.9e98616e@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I am curious.  How many regular posters in this newsgroup (or any newsgroup)
are tempted to change their newsgroup "signatures" to be anonymous.  I know
that I am tempted - given the amount of SPAM that I get (that can be
directly attributed to NG posts).

I know that there are ways of setting up my "from" to indicate one email ID
and to set up my signature line to include my email ID - but in an
"encrypted form" (such as spelling out "dot") - but I find that pretty
cumbersome.  Furthermore, I *LIKE* to be able to easily respond to some
people by private email.  (I have YET to figure out how to reach Mark A.
Young - for example).

As I say, I know there are solutions, but I just thought I would post my
"sympathies" with anonymous posts.

Eoghan Maher wrote in message
<914785357.263.0.nnrp-07.9e98616e@news.demon.co.uk>...
>Why don't youbog off Mr Anonymous, until you can be bothered to put your
>name in with your drivel.
>
>Anonymous wrote in message <199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anonymity (was So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<Ieoi2.15935$Vg2.4137764@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com> <914785357.263.0.nnrp-07.9e98616e@news.demon.co.uk> <76bkk2$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>I am curious.  How many regular posters in this newsgroup (or any newsgroup)
>are tempted to change their newsgroup "signatures" to be anonymous.  I know
>that I am tempted - given the amount of SPAM that I get (that can be
>directly attributed to NG posts).

Bill, I can tell you that my SPAM dropped to *zero* when I changed my
e-mail ID and instituted the obfuscation below.  I still have my old
e-mail address, and I still get SPAM there, even after a year of no
posts!  Yes, I frequently get e-mail where the sender mentions or
complains about the obfuscation.  I wish I could eliminate that, but
eliminating SPAM entirely is worth it.  Remember, you *must* change
your e-mail ID at the same time, or it will do you little good.
```

William M. Klein wrote:

> I am curious.  How many regular posters in this newsgroup (or any newsgroup)
> are tempted to change their newsgroup "signatures" to be anonymous.  I know
> that I am tempted - given the amount of SPAM that I get (that can be
> directly attributed to NG posts).

Munged or invalid From is pretty common these days as an antispam measure. It's
less common to see posters who do not even identify themselves in their sigs.


> I know that there are ways of setting up my "from" to indicate one email ID
> and to set up my signature line to include my email ID - but in an
> "encrypted form" (such as spelling out "dot") - but I find that pretty
> cumbersome.  Furthermore, I *LIKE* to be able to easily respond to some
> people by private email.

Yes, that is part of the damage that spammers have done. Just as burglars have
stopped people from leaving their doors unlooked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anonymity (was So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

- **From:** "NoOneYouKnow" <BogusAddress@SpammersSuckBigTime.Com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00

```
I made the change to an anon posting name a while back to eliminate
unsolicited email and it has worked pretty well. I don't get near as much as
I used to. I also changed my display name to NoOneYouKnow as more of a
nickname than anything, since no one could use that for spamming anyway. If
someone really wants to email me, and they don't already have my address, I
figure they'll ask. Its not like I take incredible pains to hide who I am -
like altering my message headers - its just enough to fool the spambots.

As for people who don't like anon posters - tough, its a free country (more
or less).

I don't usually put my email address in my sig either, since I got pretty
bored with sigs about 10 years ago and pretty much stopped using them. Now I
just sign my initials manually:

---JRE---

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anonymity

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<368A38F4.B096596F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com> <914785357.263.0.nnrp-07.9e98616e@news.demon.co.uk> <76bkk2$ifn@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I am curious.  How many regular posters in this newsgroup (or any newsgroup)
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "sympathies" with anonymous posts.

I set my return address once.  I could have added a signature line. 
When people reply to my messages they can easily edit out the NOSPAM. 
If they forget, they can resend the message when they get a "no
addressee found".

However I very rarely want messages sent to me privately.  The other day
I got a private e-mail and started to reply to it before I realized that
it was from a posting I made in a news group.  I figured my reply should
be public so I killed the message and went to the newsgroup to reply to
it (usually such messages are posted both to me and to the newsgroup -
what a pain).  Didn't find the message and thus didn't reply to it.

If I want a private reply, I'll ask for it.  But if I embarrass myself
and someone wants to take me aside quietly and tell me that it's
impolite to use HTML in my replies for instance, or if I am talking
about my IP address in an @home newsgroup and privacy is important -
people can figure out my real address.
```

- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>`

```
http://pages.prodigy.net/bershinger/y2k.htm


Anonymous wrote in message <199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>...
>There is a front page story about Y2K in today's _The New
>York Times_ featuring, in large part, the Y2K goings-on at
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>     year.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: My take on Y2K agree or disagree

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36874fee.91879748@netnews>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com> <7677al$92ag$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
'Twas Sun, 27 Dec 1998 22:08:40 -0800, when "Bill Bershinger"
<BERSHINGER@prodigy.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>http://pages.prodigy.net/bershinger/y2k.htm

The number of factual errors on that page is staggering.  Let me quote
enough to point out five.

>Well, first a few facts. Fact 1. Even PC's do not use the commonly know
>DDMMYY format for date storage. 

The common storrage format for dates on a PC is not MMDDYY, but the Real
Time Clock (a major source of Y2K issues) stores CC, YY, MM, DD, hh, mm
and ss, each in its own two digit byte.

>Really? What do PC's use? 

>PC's use 2 bytes, or 16 bits to store the number of days since January 1,
>1980. Now these two bytes, can have any value from 0 to 65,535 i.e. 2 to
>the 16th power.

The standard PC date storage format uses four bits for MM, five bits for
DD, and seven bits for YYYY-1980.

>Compare that with what you are told, namely that 6 bytes are not enough 
>for more than 100 years. 

That's not what you are being told, except by the loonies.  6 bytes can
hold a lot, but it's all in how it's encoded.  Some data encoding schemes
compress the data better than others.  Using 48 bits to represent a period
of only 100 years is a very low data content per bit, but that was the
encoding that was human readable, so it could be used without translation
of the data, and in the old days shuffling those bits around took a lot of
processor power.  Those data formats got entrenched, and it is always
traumatic to change data formats, so formats from the 1960s (and earlier)
are still in use.

>It is only application level software, written by programmers of varying 
>degrees of talent, that have given us this problem to the extent that it 
>exists. 

That's quite a big "only", but even there you are wrong.  There are
computer operating systems with serious Y2K problems.  There are models of
computers which IBM made only 15 years ago for which no Y2K compliant
operating system is available. And it's not just IBM, most most computers
from the 1970s (and there are many thousands still around) have no Y2K
compliant operating system.

>Ya see, in order to follow the PC example and use 2 bytes to represent 179 
>years, you also need a program to convert the 2 bytes into DDMMYY. 

Most programmers would get a chuckle out of this use of the word
"program", but it's not entirely incorrect.

>That takes work, is best written in Assembler for performance reasons, and
>Assembler is a foreign language to most programmers. 

Well of course it takes work, but going into Assembler just to shuffle
some bits around was rarely necessary.  Most code that I have written to
fiddle with dates is in Cobol.  The "program" to convert the 48 bit date
to the PC date format is:

   COMPUTE PC-DATE = (YYYY - 1980) * 512 + MM * 32 + DD

Going the other way is

   COMPUTE YYYY = 1980 + (PC-DATE / 512) 
   COMPUTE MM = FUNCTION MOD (PC-DATE 512) / 32
   COMPUTE DD = FUNCTION MOD (PC-DATE 32)

No foreign Assembly language required.

It is really odd about this page that after starting out saying Y2K is a
hoax, he then starts saying there is a lot of work to be done, and that a
lot of the money being spent is being squandered.
```

#### ↳ Computers and Year 2000: A Race for Security (and Against Time) (New York Times)

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36874069.87913723@netnews>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>`

```
While I don't agree with much of what Mr Anonymous Nobody had to say (I
think his main point is that he doesn't like PSE&G), the NY Times article
is very good.  You can read it at 

http://www.nytimes.com/library/tech/98/12/biztech/articles/27millennium.html

(You have to register to view the NY Times site, but it's free, and if you
let it save a cookie, you don't need to log in again.)

If you get the actual printed copy of the New York Times, the article is
in the Sunday Dec 27 issue.  The article starts on the fron page and
continues too pages 18 and 19.  Please read the whole article, especially
the top of the last column on page 19.  B^)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Computers and Year 2000: A Race for Security (and Against Time) (New York Times)

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687F0C8.7287EC2B@att.net>`
- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com> <36874069.87913723@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> While I don't agree with much of what Mr Anonymous Nobody had to say (I
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the top of the last column on page 19.  B^)

Thanks for the URL. I will finish the article shortly, but thought the
third paragraph might be of interest to the denizens of c.l.c (from the
NY Times, 12/27/98):

"Mrs. Zavlyanova and about 40 other immigrants from the former Soviet
Union are in the sub-basement of the Newark headquarters of Public
Service Electric and Gas Co., New Jersey's largest utility. They are
here not because they understand Russian but rather Cobol, a computer
language that runs many of the United States' mainframe computers but is
so old that relatively few American programmers know it."

COBOL is so old that relatively few American programmers know it! I
surely hope Thane, Leif, Judson and the rest who published "COBOL
Unleashed" are studying Russian! (Great book, by the way, I was given a
copy for Christmas). I cannot speak for PSE&G, but the rising proportion
of Russian-born programmers in NYC is due to two factors: 1. they are
well trained, and 2. they are cheap (compared to native born Americans).

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

- **From:** "Lane Core Jr." <elcore@sgi.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000

- **References:** `<199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>`

```
Anonymous wrote in message <199812271440.PAA13191@replay.com>...

>It's bad enough when one programmer is assigned to one
>thing while other programmers are handling drivers and
>subroutines and such upon which the first programmer is
>relying, but to have two programmers monkeying around with
>the same program is total stupidity!


It may very well be "total stupidity", but it is a common practice (in my
experience) for two or more programmers to be "monkeying around" with the
same program at the same time. In certain (unenviable) situations, it is
unfortunately necessary.

See also http://users.sgi.net/~elcore/quandary.htm


---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Mr. Lane Core Jr.  elcore@sgi.net  http://users.sgi.net/~elcore/elc_y2k.htm
I welcome e-mail replies. :) But I have no time for e-mail debates. :(
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

"More software projects have gone awry for lack of calendar time than for
all other causes combined". Frederick P. Brooks, _The Mythical Man-Month_
```

>There is a front page story about Y2K in today's _The New
>York Times_ featuring, in large part, the Y2K goings-on at
>Public Service Electric and Gas of New Jersey.


I have been reading this NG for over a year now, and for
some reason PSE and G seems to be the only company
that gets this kind of treatment here.  I was rather astounded,
frankly, that anybody would be so unprofessional as to talk
about a client's business in a public forum.  Regardless
of who they are, or how they treat programmers, that kind
of thing is a firing offence in any shop I know, including mine.

Do you guys all have you shorts in a knot over something
specific, or are you just blabbing out of school?  If it is
something specific, why not hire a good lawyer, and do
something about it?  Why air it here?  In here you are just
proving yourselves to be foolish and rather amateurish.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@replay.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<199812282039.VAA10721@replay.com>`
- **References:** `<768ceu$9vm$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> I have been reading this NG for over a year now, and for
> some reason PSE and G seems to be the only company
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> proving yourselves to be foolish and rather amateurish.


I see your points, and I understand and appreciate them fully.

You have to remember, though, that my original post was commentary on a
widely publicized newspaper article, albeit harsh commentary.

As harsh as my commentary was, however, it is a fact that as long as PSE&G
keeps up doing what they're doing to address the Y2K problem, as
sub-optimal as it is, they will be able to tide themselves over the
immediate crisis for a good long time.

You also have to bear in mind that PSE&G is no stranger to bad publicity. 
As a matter of fact, there was a time period a while back when there
seemed to be a front page story in the _Newark Star-Ledger_ on PSE&G
covering unflattering things about that company just about every other
day.  One of the worst things a number of years ago was when there was a
record breaking cold snap in New Jersey, and PSE&G was woefully unprepared
for this contingency.  I don't remember the exact specifics, but I think
that among other things, the company had inadequate oil supplies for its
oil-fired generating plants, and it had allowed coal stocks to become wet
and frozen solid.  To the best of my knowledge, no other utility company
in the region had experienced similar problems.  The inability of PSE&G to
meet peak demands for power almost caused the whole power grid of New
Jersey to go down even in areas not serviced by PSE&G.  If that happened,
it would have been down for four days at a time when temperatures were
hovering around zero.  I remember heeding the warnings on TV to eliminate
all unnecessary power usage, and I kept my thermostat set to 50 degrees to
prevent my boiler's water circulator from running excessively. (I was not
in the PSE&G service area.)  PSE&G responded to the emergency by cutting
the peak off the peak demand.  They did this by blacking out selected
areas for two hours at a time. I think that some people got stuck in
elevators without heat for those hours.  (There was no blackout where I
was.)  In any case, if the power went out, could you imagine 7,600,000
evacuating the state of New Jersey all at once?  Would there be enough
hotel rooms in the surrounding region to accommodate them?  Would they be
able to make it out of the state in bumper-to-bumper traffic with just the
gas in their tanks?  You know, gas pumps do not work in a blackout!

Yes, I know that companies want you to kowtow to clients and their way of
doing things.  However, if you blindly and blithely subscribe to this
philosophy when the client is really, really stupid which is, in fact, the
case quite frequently, then you will be contributing to the pollution of
your own environment, and, in time, you will sink so deeply into the muck
in a self-nailed coffin that you won't even know how you got there.

I don't really know much about the quality of the current data processing
staff of PSE&G, but in the past I have heard *unbelievable* things about
that staff.  Maybe this is why the company has so many Russians there. 
Anything, and I mean just about anything, is better than the conditions
that exist in Russia. 


Lane Core Jr. wrote:

> It may very well be "total stupidity", but it is a common practice (in my
> experience) for two or more programmers to be "monkeying around" with the
> same program at the same time. In certain (unenviable) situations, it is
> unfortunately necessary.


I'd like to say that having two programmers working on a program at the
same time is not the same thing as having two programmers looking into a
program at the same time and making recommendations about what should be
done.  In my book, two programmers working on a program means two
programmers updating that program, and when that happens, then you have
two versions of the program with updates that are totally uncoordinated
and out of sync with each other.

If you find yourself resorting to having two programmers working on a
program at the same time, then what you really need is a new programmer
who is qualified to do all the work by himself.
```

illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In my book, two programmers working on a program means two
>programmers updating that program, and when that happens, then you have
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

What if it's a big program, and there's a lot of work to be done; tell the
users it will take five years to complete the job?  When I worked on

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: So, Here's What's Happening at PSE&G: MORONISM!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Shmuel (Seymour J.) Metz" <nospam@nsf.gov.invalid>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<3689093C.DDB48378@nsf.gov.invalid>`
- **References:** `<768ceu$9vm$1@news.igs.net> <199812282039.VAA10721@replay.com> <368837ae.26376852@netnews>`

```


Randall Bart wrote:


> What if it's a big program, and there's a lot of work to be done; tell the
> users it will take five years to complete the job?

Writing a large application as one monolithic program will cause you to take
more time to complete the job, not less. If you design it as a number of
smaller modules with clean well-documented interfaces, then you can assign
each module to a single programmer.

> When I worked on
> compilers with Unisys, there were a dozen people working on the same
> program.

Your nickel.


> Furthermore, we were supporting that program in multiple release
> streams.  But with proper patch management tools, the job can be
> coordinated.

But still harder and more error prone than if the compiler had been properly
designed in a modular fashion.
```

Anonymous wrote in message <199812282039.VAA10721@replay.com>...

>Lane Core Jr. wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>and out of sync with each other.


Yes, that is exactly what happens. And it DOES happen. It happens every day.

(I'm not saying that it SHOULD happen; I'm saying that it DOES happen, all
the time.)

>If you find yourself resorting to having two programmers working on a
>program at the same time, then what you really need is a new programmer
>who is qualified to do all the work by himself.

Have you not had to work with systems that include a dozen 70,000-line COBOL
programs that do most of the key functions? I have, so I know that your
suggestion is advisable theoretically but impossible practically. Especially
when modifications are required in the same program for several completely
unrelated projects simultaneously.

I have already written about this at length.

See http://www.y2ktimebomb.com/Computech/Management/elc9844.htm

Mr. Lane Core Jr.  elcore@sgi.net  http://users.sgi.net/~elcore/elc_y2k.htm
I welcome e-mail replies. :) But I have no time for e-mail debates. :(
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

"More software projects have gone awry for lack of calendar time than for
all other causes combined". Frederick P. Brooks, _The Mythical Man-Month_

### A bit of assistance for a newbie..

- **From:** Green <new@at.cbl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl>`

```
Hello everyone!,

 I hope that I haven't interupted or am speaking out of turn.

May I first tell you where I am coming from...

 In June, I will be starting a computer programing class, that mainly
focuses on Cobol. I would like to get a headstart and decided to buy a
beginner's book on Cobol programing.

Ok..here is the trouble part. It came with a CD with alot of Cobol
stuff. A whole package of Fujitsu Cobol programs.

I have so many questions, I don't want to chew anyone's ear off on one
shot! I have experience with Basic/Pascal and C. I thought that Cobol
would be simple like C in editing/compiling/running. I seem to be able
to type a program in, and compile it, but all I get is a MSG listing
things that I have done wrong I presume. I can't see any result of the
program.

Well, I hope that my intrusion in this group doesn't cause any
disruptions, as I am very 'green' to the Cobol field, and am truely
lost. I am asking for any assistance.

Thank you for your time
```

#### ↳ Re: A bit of assistance for a newbie..

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981227114852.21271.00000992@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl>`

```

In article <3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl>, Green <new@at.cbl> writes:

>I have so many questions, I don't want to chew anyone's ear off on one
>shot! I have experience with Basic/Pascal and C. I thought that Cobol
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Speaking for the Fujitsu COBOL side ofthings is very similer in processes as in
compiling a C program.  The .MSG file is a list of Errors and Warnings and the
.LST file contains the complete source listing with the Errors / Warnings
included.  Until all the Errors are corrected you will not see an .OBJ file. 
You must successfully compile to an .OBJ before being able to procede to LINK
the .OBJ into an .EXE

HTH.
```

#### ↳ Re: A bit of assistance for a newbie..

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

On Sun, 27 Dec 1998 10:59:58 -0500, Green <new@at.cbl> wrote:


> In June, I will be starting a computer programing class, that mainly
>focuses on Cobol. I would like to get a headstart and decided to buy a
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

I'm assuming that you are talking about "COBOL for Dummies" - which
was not directly geared toward the included compilers.  If you are
interested in another book - there is a new one called "Sams Teach
Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours".  I'm pretty familiar with it, and it is
more of a step by step tutorial.  The CD that comes with it includes
the full Fujitsu 3.0 starter kit and step by step instructions on
editing, compiling and running the programs.  

In case there is still any confusion, for each example in the book the
CD includes a Lotus Screen Cam movie of the compile and execution of
the examples.

Feel free to post specific questions about specific errors here, and
we will be glad to help!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Thanks and a question...

- **From:** Green <new@at.cbl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl>`

```
Thank you all for replying! I see that this group was a good find for
me.

Yes, the book I got was Cobol for Dummies. I figured it would be a good
start!

I am having problems compiling. I am getting a ton of errors. So, for
now, I'll just ask these small questions....

 How are you all entering in programs, in regards to the CAPS and
lowercase wording? Does the compiler recognize command words, or do you
do a heck of a lot of shifting/caps lock???

 Next question....I tried opening a source file I typed in, and it asks
if I want to list the numbers, that seven number punchcard thing. If I
select no, it like cuts some of the left margin, words and all. Is it
important for the seven punch thing for compiling?

 Well...sorry, I am gonna beg for another question....There are so many
filetypes, cbl, cob, win, prj, sheets....*gasp* what am I getting myself
into????*smile*

 Ok, kidding aside, is 'project' similar to 'build all' in C? Example,
in C, I can just build all instead of compiling, and linking. Does

Thanks again for your assistance

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thanks and a question...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl>`

```

Green wrote in message <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl>...

> How are you all entering in programs, in regards to the CAPS and
>lowercase wording? Does the compiler recognize command words, or do you
>do a heck of a lot of shifting/caps lock???


The only place Case has an effect is in literals ... for
example: MOVE "hello" TO DATA-NAME.  Within the
code itself, Cobol considers upper and lower case identical.
I enter my code in uppercase, but that is more a matter
of me being a traditionalist than anything.

> Next question....I tried opening a source file I typed in, and it asks
>if I want to list the numbers, that seven number punchcard thing. If I
>select no, it like cuts some of the left margin, words and all. Is it
>important for the seven punch thing for compiling?


No, it does not.  However, Fujitsu (and this is compiler specific) is
particularly fussy about the column 8 and column 12 margins.  If
you use a tab in the first character of the line to get over to column 8,
rather than 8 spaces, Fujitsu Cobol can get rather excited about it.
It you see messages refering to "column A" and "column B", then
that is probably your problem.

> Well...sorry, I am gonna beg for another question....There are so many
>filetypes, cbl, cob, win, prj, sheets....*gasp* what am I getting myself
>into????*smile*


That is all the IDE stuff, rather than Cobol.  Nevertheless, yes,
it is a big part of learning to use the language.  Give thanks you
are using a micro, and do not have to learn JCL<S>.

>
> Ok, kidding aside, is 'project' similar to 'build all' in C? Example,
>in C, I can just build all instead of compiling, and linking. Does
>project in Cobol do the same thing?
>

Yes, the Fujitsu "build all" is a recompile and re-link of everything
in the project.  A project, though, can contain more than one
program.  Think of it as the make file for a C project with multiple
run modules.  In fact, it does exactly that behind the scenes. First
it deletes all the "exe", "obj", and "lib" files, then it creates a make
file (on your disk as "001", "002", etc.), then it runs MS make.

>Thanks again for your assistance


you're welcome.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76944s$mas$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl>`

```
I am not a mainframer, so I am likely to be corrected by
someone on this, but CICS is a separate screen handler
that works with Cobol ... I do not know if it can be used
with other languages or not.  DB2 is a database package,
and can be used with any language ... it can even be
used standalone, after a fashion. Access is a second data
base(ditto), while JCL has nothing to do with a specific
language ... it is the mainframe equivalent to DOS commands.
SQL is a language for communicating data-base requests
to a data-base manager, and is common to several languages
and database managers.

Green wrote in message <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl>...
>> > Well...sorry, I am gonna beg for another question....There are so
>> many
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3688525f.84293757@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <76944s$mas$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Mon, 28 Dec 1998 18:36:30 -0500, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>I am not a mainframer, so I am likely to be corrected by
>someone on this, but CICS is a separate screen handler
>that works with Cobol ... I do not know if it can be used
>with other languages or not.  

CICS is an entire system under which COBOL (and other languages can
run).  There is no direct interface to the user or data within the
programs that run under CICS - these are handled using CALLS to CICS
specific modules that handle the management of these functions.  In
order to make the calls understandable - the programmer codes "Command
Level CICS" statements in their programs - these are converted via the
translator program to "Call" statements and the translated program is
compiled and linked.  Any language that runs on an IBM mainframe can
make use of CICS command level statements - these include C, Pascal,
BAL, COBOL, PL/1 and perhaps (don't quote me) RPG.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <76944s$mas$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Mon, 28 Dec 1998 18:36:30 -0500, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> enlightened us:

>I am not a mainframer, so I am likely to be corrected by
>someone on this, but CICS is a separate screen handler
>that works with Cobol ... I do not know if it can be used
>with other languages or not. 

CICS is more than a screen handler.  You could think of it as an
operating system because of the way it handles requests, tasks etc.
You can write IBM mainframe Assembler programs to run under CICS as
well as Cobol.  I'm not sure of any other mainframe languages.

>and can be used with any language ... it can even be
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

wonder what else you can do while you're down there.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Simon Elliott <Simon_L.Elliott@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-jJ3OHOfCtIVt@dsm4merlin.iix.com>`

```


mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com wrote:

> On Mon, 28 Dec 1998 21:25:58, Green <new@at.cbl> wrote:
>
> > Ack! JCL goes into the bad things category???...

As a CICS SYSPROG of ten years experience I would appreciate the chance
to offer a few comments.

CICS is a mini operating system. My experience suggests that there are
subtle changes in the approach required to COBOL coding for CICS that
are not always initially appreciated by an experienced batch programmer.
That a CICS system is always attempting to spread machine resources as
wide as possible made dabbling in Visual Basic (for example) easier once
I grasped that the fundamental concepts beneath each system were very
similar. Batch programming (and I don't want to appear to be negatively
critical here - I've done both and neither is 'better' than the other)
doesn't require the programmer to be too concerned about other users
with whom they are sharing the machine.

JCL, the mechanism by which work is presented to the machine, is... (I
tried here to think of a suitable word here, and failed) very difficult
to learn. That said, an application programmer who can use JCL is one
who makes my life (and ultimately their own) easier because they're not
waiting for me to build them some compile JCL or a file copy (You can
never know too many languages - the more you know the easier it is to
learn another).

Incidentally. Is JCL a langauge? I reckon that strictly speaking it
isn't, but I'm not sure why. Can anyone confirm my prejudice?

Simon Elliott
TSIS NSG CICS/DB Support
Cap Gemini Southbank
London

Any opinions expressed are not necessarily those of my employer.
```

## Thread: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** Ramesh Baskaran <rbaskara@sg.oracle.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com>`

```
Hi

I have been just told by some COBOL programmers on IBM systems that
explicit decimal point is not possible. i.e. using 9(10).99 will not
produce as decimal point but will produce the same result as 9(10)v99.
It is actually the printer program that puts the
decimal point. I personally cannot buy this idea.

I would like IBM COBOL programmers opinion on this. I am actually asking
the COBOL programmers here to create me an ASCII file so that I can
import into my system. Is there anyway in IBM COBOL to create a standard
ASCII text file ?

Thanks in advance

Regards
Ramesh
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** "The Programmer" <thevaxer@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7657ih$qs6$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com>`

```
LOL! And you actually *believed* them?

Of course straight text files can be generated off IBM systems. They're
called sequential files. Easy enough to do from an AS/400 as well as a
mainframe. In fact, it's done constantly where I work -- that's how I
'import' stuff like invoices slated for special billing using Word and
FrontPage. Generate a sequential extract on the mainframe (done using MSA's
IE by one of the end users in fact!), automatically transferred to one of
the servers where my VB program pulls in the text file data and populates an
MS Access database.

So... maybe you should forget about them know-nothing programmers, and have
one of your power end users who knows either EZTrieve or MSA IE generate the
extract file for you.

Good luck!! :D
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36864c6e.71876196@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com>`

```
On Sun, 27 Dec 1998 13:08:18 +0800, Ramesh Baskaran
<rbaskara@sg.oracle.com> wrote:

>Hi
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>ASCII text file ?

It's not really an IBM COBOL issue.

The 9(10).99 is an edit pattern.  The field is an alphanumeric field
at this point - you can't use it as anything other than the
destinatoin field in a calculation.  So what they are saying is
technically correct - with one exception.  It's not the printer that
adds the decimal point!!!  You can write it to a data file with the
picture clause9(10).99 and you WILL get the decimal point in the data.

However -- It's not a number anymore.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7660ao$qqp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com> <36864c6e.71876196@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36864c6e.71876196@news1.ibm.net>...

>picture clause9(10).99 and you WILL get the decimal point in the data.
>
>However -- It's not a number anymore.


To clarify just a bit, it is not a number as far as Cobol is
concerned.  However, if the file you are creating is to
export data to a system that does use "." as part of numeric
fields, then it still is a number.
```
