```
Nut in Cobol-85 it is stil a number. You can move data from the PIC
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7687jk$8aj$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com> <7660ao$qqp$1@news.igs.net> <3686d1bc.59611255@netnews> <3687489a.90007904@netnews>`

```
In article <3687489a.90007904@netnews>,
Randall Bart <Barticus@att.spam.net> wrote:
>'Twas 28 Dec 1998 08:57:37 GMT, when Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
>clouded comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>"$$,$$$,$$$.99+" to be "$$,$$$,$$$,$$$.99+".

Not having much of problems, old stocking; I am capable of *many* of
understandings, typographical errors or maybe!

... but back to the matter at hand: a question!  *I* would say that your
conclusion (that 'in Cobol-85 it is stil (sic) a number') is incorrect.
Yes, the field in question can be 'de-edited' into an numeric field BUT...

... is it possible to perform arithmetic manipulations upon it while still
in its un-de-edited state?  If not, thence my disagreement, as I would say
that a number, by definition, is That Upon Which One Can Perform
Arithmetic Manipulations.

Have at it!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76bc1l$fli@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com> <7660ao$qqp$1@news.igs.net> <3686d1bc.59611255@netnews> <3687489a.90007904@netnews> <7687jk$8aj$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <7687jk$8aj$1@clarknet.clark.net>...
>In article <3687489a.90007904@netnews>,
>Randall Bart <Barticus@att.spam.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>9(10).99 field, or even a PIC $$,$$$,$$$.99+ field, and the number will
be
>>>"de-edited".
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

14 layers higher in this discussion, someone called 9(10).99
"alphanumeric" - and we all "know" <g> that this isn't true.  What it is, is
"numeric-edited" - which has different rules than either numeric or
alphanumeric (or alphanumeric-edited) fields.

It is perfectly true that "numeric-edited" fields can be used most places a
numeric field can be used AS A SENDING ITEM in ANSI'85 (but not earlier).
Furthermore, it can be used as an argument to the NumVal and NumVal-C
intrinsic functions in ANSI'89 (with the output of the function being usable
wherever an arithmetic expression is valid - and in the next Standard
wherever a numeric sending item can be used).  Therefore, (almost as the
name implies) it really is "half way between" a numeric item and an
alphanumeric-edited item.

Bottom-line:

A) of course the original post was just plain foolishness  (about IBM
mainframes and explicit decimal points)

B) (assuming ANSI'85)  any numeric-edited item can be used lots of places
that a numeric item (but not an alphanumeric or alphanumeric-edited item)
can be used - but certainly not all such places.
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36868BAE.568928BD@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com>`

```
If you have a PC connection to the mainframe TSO environment, you should
have the ability to download a dataset to your PC.
The download option will convert the dataset from EBCIDIC to ASCII and,
optionally, append a CR and LF to each record.

I frequently download and upload programs when I am developing on the
PC, but have to perform integration testing on the
mainframe.  The ability has been in the TSO connectivity packages that
my clients have had for both Win '95 and OS/2.

Regards,

Greg Ferro
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368772B2.E73B7F9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com> <36868BAE.568928BD@sprynet.com>`

```
Greg Ferro wrote:
> 
> If you have a PC connection to the mainframe TSO environment, you should
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Greg Ferro

Since you mention TSO connect I assume that you are using IND$FILE and a
TSO based transfer.  This is not a 'production' level transfer protocol.
Recommend that you look for an FTP on TSO (either client EXEC=FTP or
server started task of FTP??? (seen FTPSERVER and FTPD)) or better still
one of the specialist transfer tools eg XCOM.  When you transfer have
your own checks to ensure the download was sucessful.  I have used
precalculated byte counts and last record checks sucessfully.

Ken
```

```
Rubbish!   How would the printer know where it is required or not.  A
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687967C.6CC7375D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com>`

```
Ramesh Baskaran wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> ASCII text file ?

I agree with you - but I have a couple of questions: 
 What kind of system were your IBM programmers using?
 If you're IBM is an EBCDIC machine and you need ASCII, how are you
moving the data from the IBM to your ASCII environment?

When I code in ZZZ9.99 in an MVS environment, the decimal certainly is
there before it gets to the printer.  I can see it in my screens and in
my dumps.  I have to account for the space.

Now my environment is EBCDIC, so I have to check the appendix of my
COBOL manual to check all of the things I want to do for ASCII.  Even if
I use my terminal program to download my output, I should be aware of
COLLATING SEQUENCE.  If I am using an ASCII printer, I use CODE-SET IS
ASCII-SEQUENCE.   I believe there are a couple of options when I FTP my
files.
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal Point in IBM COBOL

- **From:** mikec54@aol.com (MikeC54)
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981228012414.12881.00002326@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3685C0C1.28D03D6C@sg.oracle.com>`

```
Your programmers are correct, however if you print output to a file and define
a field as 9(10).99 the decimal will actually appear in the file.  I don't
recommend this, however, it can be done.  It's easier if you leave it as
9(10)v99 and let the reading program be aware of the format and interpret it as
such.  If you have any questions, email me at mimec54@aol.com
```

## Thread: signed numbers — 2 msgs

### signed numbers

- **From:** The Chameleon <sarvec@letterbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687F488.4BB3@letterbox.com>`

```
process-records.
     display 'entered process records paragraph'.
     compute annual = (salary * 2080).
     move annual to pr-annual.
     display annual
     move in-name to pr-name.
     move salary to pr-hourly.
     display pr-hourly
     move mstatus to pr-stat.
     if annual is greater than 
     29000 and mstatus = 'Married' then move '*' to pr-asterick  [I know
it's spelled wrong]
         else move spaces to pr-asterick
     end-if.

********************* Solved **************
I was "saying"  
               IF  PR-ANNUAL IS GREATER THAN 29000 THEN MOVE '*' TO
PR-ASTERICK
            END-IF.

But shouldn't PR-ANNUAL hold the same value as ANNUAL?
What happens when you move a value? 

I have another program, the one that did me in and resulted in an
incomplete in COBOL
if anybody is interested in helping me on that one I would really
appreciate it.
:-)
Goodness I love these newsgroups and the wonderful people in them.
```

#### ↳ Re: signed numbers

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7695at$7v4$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<3687F488.4BB3@letterbox.com>`

```

The Chameleon wrote in message <3687F488.4BB3@letterbox.com>...
[...]
>********************* Solved **************
>I was "saying"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
PR-ANNUAL is a numeric edited field. Numeric edited items are
of class alphanumeric and comparisons follow the rules as for other
alphanumeric items. For Bessie the Cow, the comparison was
equivalent to:

    IF "+37,107.00" IS GREATER THAN "29000     " ...

Since "+" is less than "2" (it occurs earlier in the collating sequence),
the condition will evaluate to false and the MOVE "*" instruction will
not be executed.

HTH
-------------------------------
```

<a id="t-e412b94e6a"></a>
## Thread: Fundamentals of the UNSTRING statement — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Fundamentals of the UNSTRING statement

- **From:** BuyukYarak@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<768crf$p5r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


Suppose the following is true (lots of edited code fragments):

[Hierarchy has been messed with a lot here to make this presentable.]
   10 Storage-Record.
      15 Patient-Code           PIC 9(7).
      15 Test-Code              PIC 9(4).
      15 Test-Result            PIC 9(6)V99.
   10 Read-Buffer               PIC X(2048).
   10 Read-Ptr                  PIC 9(4).
   10 Read-Buffer-Size          PIC 9(4) VALUE 2048.
   10 Read-Len                  PIC 9(4).
   10 Write-Buffer.
      15 Test-Count             PIC 9(3).
      15 FILLER                 PIC X VALUE ";".
      15 Successful-Records     PIC X(2044).
   77 Parse-Size                PIC 999.


Now the following operations are done for purposes of this post:
1.   CALL "SYSREAD"
           USING CALL-Error-Explanation, Input-Pipe-FD,
                 Read-Buffer, Read-Len.
2.   UNSTRING
        Read-Buffer DELIMITED BY ";"
        INTO Test-Count COUNT IN Read-Ptr.
3.   ADD 1 TO Read-Ptr.
4.   UNSTRING
        Read-Buffer DELIMITED BY ";"
        INTO Storage-Record COUNT IN Parse-Size
        WITH POINTER Read-Ptr.
5.   ADD Parse-Size TO Read-Ptr.

According to my understanding, after operation #n:

1. a system-dependent operation which works as verified with a debugger;
   Read-Buffer contains (except for quotes):  "001;2204102003300002200;F9"
        after this operation, Read-Len contains 26
2. Test-Count should contain value '1' and Read-Ptr value '4'.
3. Read-Ptr contains value '5'.
4. Patient-Code contains 2204102, Test-Code contains 33, Test-Result is 22.00
   Parse-Size contains 20.
5. Read-Ptr has value of 25.

The values as indicated after Operations 2 and 3 are verified using the
debugger.
My only problem is that after operation #4, the group item Storage-Record
 (and therefore all its elements) contain blanks/spaces, and Parse-Size is 0.

What am I failing to understand about the WITH POINTER clause of the UNSTRING
statement?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fundamentals of the UNSTRING statement

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<769dcf$dms$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<768crf$p5r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote in message <768crf$p5r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>
[...]
>
>What am I failing to understand about the WITH POINTER clause of the
UNSTRING
>statement?
>
The pointer is incremented for each character examined. There is
no need to manipulate the pointer between UNSTRING statements.

Try the following:

       CALL "SYSREAD"
           USING CALL-Error-Explanation, Input-Pipe-FD,
                 Read-Buffer, Read-Len.
* Initialize pointer
       MOVE 1 to Read-Ptr.
       UNSTRING
           Read-Buffer DELIMITED BY ";"
           INTO Test-Count WITH POINTER Read-Ptr.
* Pointer now contains 5, 1 plus 4 characters examined
       UNSTRING
         Read-Buffer DELIMITED BY ";"
         INTO Storage-Record WITH POINTER Read-Ptr.
* Pointer now contains 25, 5 plus 20 characters examined

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

<a id="t-2eea166431"></a>
## Thread: Ok..asking for an opinion.. — 6 this month / 19 total

### Ok..asking for an opinion..

- **From:** Green <new@at.cbl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>`

```
Hello,

 Just wanted to start a thread regarding Cobol.

Is anyone out there just doing Cobol programing? Or are you doing a mix
of other languages?

Are there plentyful cobol jobs, or just contract work?

What about cobol's future? I heard that cobol is a dying languge, being
phased out along with mainframes, any truth in that? I know servers are
the new thing...

Just a comment..I am impressed with all that you can do with cobol. It
seems that the language is ridgid, but being able to link with VB, C,
etc...still kinda makes it valuable..
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..asking for an opinion..

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36879AB3.CBC8367D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>`

```
Green wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of other languages?

I use a lot of English in meetings, a fair amount of Jargon, but the
large majority of my batch programs is in COBOL.
 
> Are there plentyful cobol jobs, or just contract work?

I am a contractor who usually gets offered permanent jobs where I
contract.  Contractors are expensive and rarely are given the type of
responsibility which requires coming in at 2:00 AM to fix a problem.
 
> What about cobol's future? I heard that cobol is a dying languge, being
> phased out along with mainframes, any truth in that? I know servers are
> the new thing...

Nothing else has come along to replace it for enterprise type business
environments.  I seriously doubt if I will ever need to switch to
"modern" languages.  But even so, I just got through spending a lot of
money to get my masters - specializing in OO technology and will
practice OO languages at home.  It is possible that I will be wrong and
I won't take that chance.

But since COBOL is needed now, if you don't think it will be around for
your career - use COBOL to get your job, and work for a company which
gives you opportunities to program in other stuff as well!
 
> Just a comment..I am impressed with all that you can do with cobol. It
> seems that the language is ridgid, but being able to link with VB, C,
> etc...still kinda makes it valuable..

Curious.  Why do you think it wouldn't be valuable if it couldn't link
with VB or C?  What do you think COBOL is used for?  What would make it
less rigid?
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..asking for an opinion..

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76868u$6d0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>`

```

Green wrote in message <3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>...

>Is anyone out there just doing Cobol programing? Or are you doing a mix
>of other languages?


I do just Cobol now, but used to mix languages much more.  The
reason is that both the hardware and the software is now becoming
sophistocated enough to do that.  In the past, there were a lot of
things you could not do without resorting to C or Assembler. That
tends not to be nearly as true any more.  To give an example, I just
converted a slew of assembler serial I/O routines into Cobol.

>Are there plentyful cobol jobs, or just contract work?


Don't know, though I am told that 50% of all programming jobs
are Cobol.  I do know that it is the only language that I have
been able to use thoughout an entire thirty year career that
encompasses over sixty languages that I learnt, used, then
dropped as they became obsolete or the platform evolved
into something else.
>
>What about cobol's future? I heard that cobol is a dying languge, being
>phased out along with mainframes, any truth in that? I know servers are
>the new thing...


The language has always been far superior for business
work than anything else.  I have not done mainframe work
since the 70's (with some interfacing exceptions). The big
problem has always been that the PC was inadequate to
do some things in Cobol.  That has changed.


>Just a comment..I am impressed with all that you can do with cobol. It
>seems that the language is ridgid, but being able to link with VB, C,
>etc...still kinda makes it valuable..


The rigidity is it's strength. It is extremely highly typed, far
more so than any other language except, perhaps, PL1. That
allows the same code to run on everything from PC's to
mainframes. It also gives exceptionally solid code.

Note that many of the Y2K problems arise specifically because
there is Cobol code still running from the 60's.  I doubt that
is true for any other language.  I am still using program libraries
that I wrote twenty years ago, as are all the old-timers in this
NG.  That is not true of any other language.

Note as well that Cobol make few concessions to the computer.
It is designed to make code easy to read for *groups* of
programmers, not to make code easy to compile for groups
of computers like most languages.  A lot of the idiosynchracies
of Cobol can only be understood when you realize the language
was designed to be read easily, not to be compiled or even
written easily.  It is assumed that it will be written by a pro.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..asking for an opinion..

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7686ta$rlc$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>`

```
In article <3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>, Green  <new@at.cbl> wrote:
>Hello,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>of other languages?

Well... some of the above.  I make my living as a COBOL jockey but I make
a better living when I speak both COBOL and CICS.  It can be argued, of
course, that CICS is not 'another language' but merely an extension of a
given language... well and good!  But... I make an even *better* living
when I speak COBOL, CICS and DB2... and DB2 is driven, more-or-less, by
SQL, the Structured Query Language... so I don't know if I am then doing
COBOL or 'a mix of other languages'.

... oh ... did I mention Job Control Language, too?  I'm in an IBM shop
and one has to use that to run batch programs...

... but ain't nobody shown up yet as can pay me enough to start slinging
BAL again.

>
>Are there plentyful cobol jobs, or just contract work?

The answer to that is C) Some Of The Above... and since when is a contract
not a 'job', anyhow?

>
>What about cobol's future? I heard that cobol is a dying languge, being
>phased out along with mainframes, any truth in that? I know servers are
>the new thing...

If someone is *that* good at guessing the future then I think they would
be making a living in the stockmarket, not in predictions about computer
languages.  As someone here so eloquently put it: 'I was told that COBOL
isgoing to die and be replaced by something better... the next week I was
told that a man had finally walked on the moon.'

>
>Just a comment..I am impressed with all that you can do with cobol. It
>seems that the language is ridgid, but being able to link with VB, C,
>etc...still kinda makes it valuable..

As my first programming instructor, the respected and honored Mr Mullen,
told us:  'If you want to put bread on the table, learn COBOL... ifyou
want to put butter on the bread, learn *more* COBOL.'

... but what do *I* know?  After all, I only do this for a living.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..asking for an opinion..

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687B1F0.11622F42@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>`

```
Knowing other languages can be a BIG help, one should not be embarassed by
having too many arrows in their quiver.  Although this is a COBOL shop, C
was a natural choice for programs to create record layouts (i.e. beg cols,
end cols, and lengths) or to convert COBOL programs to uppercase while
leaving comments and literals alone.

Being able to link to modules written in other languages (and understanding
the linkage) may still be important in some shops but IMHO isn't as
critical now as it used to be.

Green wrote:

>  Just wanted to start a thread regarding Cobol.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> etc...still kinda makes it valuable..

Warren Porter
---
Rainy Days OR Mondays Always Get Me Down.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..asking for an opinion..

- **From:** "Dennis Fyke" <DEFyke@aol.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be32c8$7c513f60$2c198318@default.mw.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<3687813B.150C7C94@at.cbl>`

```
Well, here's my 2cents worth...

As far as whether COBOL will be here in the future or is it a dying
language....  I've been told that it's a dying language my entire 30 year
career.  There were always 'superior' languages coming out but they're all
gone and COBOL is still here!

Also, it sure doesn't make much business sense to spend hundreds of
millions of dollars in upgrading old COBOL code for the Y2K problem just to
dump these programs in the near future.

As has been pointed out, the only reason we have a Y2K problem is because
of the unexpected durability of these programs.  They have been around for
decades for one simple reason - they work.
```

---

## Thread: Ok..making this right..or..my little cbl file... — 4 msgs

### Ok..making this right..or..my little cbl file...

- **From:** Green <new@at.cbl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687F657.D236C55@at.cbl>`

```
Ok, this was a small program that I typed in that should have simple
output. I tried to compile it threw Fuiji's WINCOB. The MSG file gives a
bunch of errors, and I am uncertain of how to correct it.

Ok..here is the cbl source.....

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. ReRose.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 Flower.
 05 Rose PIC X(20).
66 Nasturtium RENAMES Rose.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
INIT.
 MOVE "sweet" TO Rose.
 DISPLAY Nasturtium.
 DISPLAY Rose.
 STOP RUN.

well...looks like I lost some indention from pasting, but I entered it
exactly(I thought), from the book.

Is anyone else able to use Fujitsu to compile and run this? Any tips on
how it should be entered in?
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..making this right..or..my little cbl file...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368813E1.5FB21BAF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3687F657.D236C55@at.cbl>`

```
> well...looks like I lost some indention from pasting, but I entered it
> exactly(I thought), from the book.
> 
> Is anyone else able to use Fujitsu to compile and run this? Any tips on
> how it should be entered in?

That's interesting.  I don't know anything about your editor or Fujitsu
Cobol, but if I were doing any language which is column sensitive, I
would set up my editor to convert tabs to spaces.  It might not make any
difference this time (I don't know), but it probably will bite in a
different environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..making this right..or..my little cbl file...

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981228172827.25338.00000895@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3687F657.D236C55@at.cbl>`

```

In article <3687F657.D236C55@at.cbl>, Green <new@at.cbl> writes:

>Ok, this was a small program that I typed in that should have simple
>output. I tried to compile it threw Fuiji's WINCOB. The MSG file gives a
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I had no problem with this.  I cut/paste the code from your post into an editor
workspace. (I use the Project Management facility and selected 'Editor' from
the 'Tools' drop down list.)
I did a few alignment changes to fit my rules for positoning and formatting of
code. No changes to any of the acutal verbiage.
Saved the file as rerose.cbl.
Selected 'Compile' from the 'Tools' pull-down menu.
*** Note I use this list of options which match reasonably close to the
mainframe requirements of my current work environment.
[Compile Options]
COPY=Yes
DLOAD=Yes
FLAG=I
FLAGSW=No STDH
GEN=No
LANGLVL=85
LIB=g:\source\cbl\cpy
LINECOUNT=60
MAIN=Yes
MODE=STD
NUMBER=No
OBJECT=Yes g:\source\cbl\obj
PRINT=Yes g:\source\cbl\lst
QUOTE=APOST
RSV=ALL
SDS=No
SOURCE=Yes
SRF=FIX,FIX
SSIN=SYSIN
SSOUT=SYSOUT
STD1=JIS2
TEST=Yes g:\source\cbl\svd
TRACE=No
TRUNC=Yes
ZWB=Yes


Everything compiled fine. LINK worked and EXECute ran fine as well 
reporting the two display lines reporting the content of ROSE as 'sweet'
and the renamed reference of NASTURTIUM as 'sweet'.

HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..making this right..or..my little cbl file...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7694di$mbc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3687F657.D236C55@at.cbl>`

```

Green wrote in message <3687F657.D236C55@at.cbl>...
>Ok, this was a small program that I typed in that should have simple
>output. I tried to compile it threw Fuiji's WINCOB. The MSG file gives a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Space this to column 8.
>IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
>PROGRAM-ID. ReRose.
>DATA DIVISION.
>WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
>01 Flower.
space this to column 12
> 05 Rose PIC X(20).
>66 Nasturtium RENAMES Rose.
column 8.
>PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>INIT.
column 12.
> MOVE "sweet" TO Rose.
> DISPLAY Nasturtium.
> DISPLAY Rose.
> STOP RUN.
>
Also, put the program id into quotes for Fujitsu. (IE: "ReRose".)
That is compiler specific.

>well...looks like I lost some indention from pasting, but I entered it
>exactly(I thought), from the book.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol book?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<14Nh2.2816$vu.2822788@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36875856.18512441@hotmail.com>`

```
Eric wrote:
>
>Could anyone tell me a good book or a web tutorial to learn Cobol?


"Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" by Thane Hubbell, a denizen of this
newsgroup.  Great book, even comes with a free Fujitsu COBOL compiler
on an included CD, and all the sample programs compile using the Fujitsu
compiler.  You can get it from www.amazon.com or other web or local
bookstores.  List price is about $25 US, and you can get it for less
than $20 US.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol book?

- **From:** j5rson@frontiernet.net (Jeff Iverson)
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dd4688.48677709@news.frontiernet.net>`
- **References:** `<36875856.18512441@hotmail.com>`

```
These books may help:

Cobol for Dummies
http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/0764502980/iversonsoftwarecA

Teach Yourself Cobol in 21 Days
```

```
I have the following problem running a program compiled under MF COBOL
2.4.

I have a program CTR.INT which calls an executable program ARRAY.EXE.
In the later program it searchs the item file and sends back an array
filled with those items which have included a certain string that was
sent to it by CTR.INT. Now this is the problem:

In the production version there is no problems. I did some
modifications of BOTH ARRAY.EXE AND CTR.INT for year2k. Now when I run
CTR after 41 calls to ARRAY I get 'load failure error 198.' When I
reran CTR under animator it took only 7 calls to get that message.
ARRAY is 'called' and then 'canceled.' This happens on all machines no
matter what the amount of memory the machine has.

My questions are: 1. What could be the possible problems? 2. What can I
do to try and see what is happening in the program that could cause
this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error Microfocus Cobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76auqq$r20$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<767l8u$4re$1@koza.nl.net> <768c8f$1il$1@saltmine.radix.net> <76amvc$rs2@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
A 198 is a load error.  It can be caused by a bad path,
or lack memory. From the sound of the behaviour under
debug, it sounds like memory, but you say amount of
memory does not affect things.  It may be that you are
running into a 64k boundary problem.  Do you dynamically
expand memory anywhere?

Moshe Shulman wrote in message <76amvc$rs2@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>...
>I have the following problem running a program compiled under MF COBOL
>2.4.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Empire State Supply
```

<a id="t-b247b2ffe1"></a>
## Thread: Signed Numbers — 2 msgs

### Signed Numbers

- **From:** The Chameleon <sarvec@letterbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687F6D6.6A5B@letterbox.com>`

```
I think I found the answer to my last post.
ANNUAL is in working storage as a 01 level, specifically it is:

 01  annual             pic S9(6)   value zeros.
and PR_ANNUAL is in: 
 01  ws-output-record.
     05  pr-name      pic x(22).
     05  filler       pic x(6).
     05  pr-stat      pic x(7).
     05  filler       pic x(8).
     05  pr-hourly    pic 9(2).99.
     05  filler       pic x(7).
     05  pr-annual    pic ---,---.99.
     05  filler       pic x.
     05  pr-asterick  pic x.
But why can't the value of pr-annual be checked as I have it here?
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed Numbers

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3687FE1D.31E7BAAE@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<3687F6D6.6A5B@letterbox.com>`

```
The Chameleon wrote:

> I think I found the answer to my last post.
> ANNUAL is in working storage as a 01 level, specifically it is:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> But why can't the value of pr-annual be checked as I have it here?

A very good habit to get into would be to never use a numeric edited
field except as the target of a MOVE statement, indeed some compilers
won't let you use it in any other way.  If you CAN use it, treat it as
alphanumeric e.g:  IF PR-ANNUAL > " 29,000.00" then whatever (although a
negative value of "-10,000.00" would test as true).

Warren Porter
---
Rainy Days OR Mondays Always Get Me Down.
```

```
LV
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3689c40b.88547411@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com> <36891EDC.715FE542@at.cbl>`

```
On Tue, 29 Dec 1998 13:26:36 -0500, Green <new@at.cbl> wrote:

>LVenick wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>the same boat you are in.....

This is the story of how I got my first Data Processing job.  (As it
was called back then).

I had been working full time (at Mc Donalds!) and going to a Jr
College.  My SAT's were good enough for me to go anywhere, but I could
not afford it.  And I was impatient.  I was taking my "general"
courses in college -- but working full time I was having trouble
staying awake.  

I had decided I wanted a job in computers - I could program the heck
out of my TI 57 and TI 59 calculators, I had a TI 99 4A I wrote
sort/merges on in basic, and I had a Timex Sinclair with 16K!  I was
hooked.  

I decided to take out a student loan and I applied to Computer
Learning Center in Anaheim CA.  I passed their entrance exam for
programmers - but they said I was too young to be a programmer (I was
20!) - so they shuffeled me into Operations.  

These tech schools provide the bare bones background you need to learn
DP (er IT).  If you are motivated you can learn more than they teach.
You will either love it or hate it.  The course started with an Intro
to DP - (Number systems how the CPU actually works etc).  The next
course was RPG II.  I learned how to program in RPG.  Then there was a
DOS clas and an OS (back then) class.  They used VM.  They ran the RPG
stuff, COBOL and BAL that they taught the programmers under DOS - so
OS was not up much.  But I learned the HASP commands and I ran the
operating system from the card reader to get my OS utility assignments
done while DOS was running.  You have to learn all you can.

So there I was - fresh out of operations school, ready to become an
operator - wanting to be a programmer.  I used their placement service
- and interviewed with several defense contractors - who while
interviewing tech school grads were looking for degreed individuals.  

Eventually they found me a job with a temp agency - I was contracted
out to Data Products and I worked in Quality Assurance.  I didn't do
much, but they paid me well.  They had a PDP 11 that they had some
BASIC programs on that they had trouble with - I fixed them.

I hated the drive though - 50 miles in LA traffic.  

At the same time I took the job, I saw a newspaper article talking
about a local (to me) business that was making it big.  The article
was an interview with the director of ops, and the paper showed a
picture of an IBM tape drive.  I got his name from the article and
called him.  He didn't have a job - but he thought they might have one
shortly.  I called him every day.  After a week he called me in to
interview - no promises.  I had no experience.  

I brought with me all of my RPG programs, my utilities and assignments
from school.  I asked them what OS they ran and it was a DOS variant
called MVT/VSE from Software Pursuits.  

He sat me down and had me explain the work I did - what meant what-
just to see what I understood and if I really knew my stuff.

A week later (I called every day) he told me I could start - but I
would not be paid much  $6.00 an hour.  Graveyard shift.  I took it.  

The first night I dumped a stack of paper boxes on the lead operator.
But I learned on the job.  I got very good at the job.  There was a
contract programmer there that worked nights sometimes - to get things
done.  I bought a COBOL book and he taught me COBOL - and the right
way to do things.  I wrote programs to make operations eaiser.  I
modified JCL to prevent problems - I automated processes - to give me
more time to learn to program.  

Eventually they hired more graduates from the same tech school - some
were great some were not - but I moved into being a
programmer/operator - for a short time - then a full time programmer.
They sent me to school at IBM for several different things including
CICS - I've never looked back.

So what's the answer to your job search.  Take ANYTHING you can get.
Prove yourself.  Take your work from school to the interview.  Be
persistant.  Someone will see your fire and take a chance on you.  If
you don't have that fire, or the desire to excel - I know of a Mc
Donalds that is still looking to replace their best swing manager.
```

#### ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Oeai2.3619$9H6.3517506@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com>`

```
LVenick wrote:
>I've been listening for months on end about this so called shortage of
>qualified personnel for the ever increasing number of IT jobs out there. So how
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>there that don't require 2 year's experience.

When you hire a newbie it takes a considerable amount of time to get
them up to speed.  Just knowing the technical programming details is
only a part of what a working programmer must know.  They must learn
the systems (applications) they will be working on.  And beyond that
they must learn about systems in general; what makes a good one or a
bad one, etc.  Believe me, they teach you virtually zip in that area,
and what they did teach you, you will probably need to unlearn.  Most
of the academic crowd has almost no real-world experience.  Ironically,
shops which are short for people simply do not have the extra time to
hire newbies.  It's almost a 'catch-22'.  But don't be discouraged,
there are lots of jobs out there, you just have to look around a
little for a beginning position.  I believe there are some companies
and government agencies out there who have yet to really get serious
about Y2K.  When they do, it will be panic time, and they will be
hiring anyone who can spell COBOL.  May not help them a lot if it's
too late, but it may help get you in the door. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981229153547.01018.00003034@ng-fq1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Oeai2.3619$9H6.3517506@news4.mia>`

```
Along the same line as Judson wrote, a number of companies will hire entry
folks and then put them through a "boot camp" to learn the real world details
and local / company styles, procedures, business, etc.

Companies that I know do this: Airborne Express (Seattle), Alaska Air
(Bellevue, WA), US West (Denver, Bellevue, Omaha). Over the years I have seen
others doing this.

We run EPT (Entry Programmer Training) series for both new hires and internal
career-switching employees; we have done these at US West, Neodata (in Colorado
and Ireland), Security Life, and other companies. I know IBM offers a "Boot
Camp" package that some of their clients use.

So you have to find companies who will invest the time and money and personal
support to grow their own programmers. But these companies are out there.

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76bfsf$6ld$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com> <Oeai2.3619$9H6.3517506@news4.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>When you hire a newbie it takes a considerable amount of time to get
>them up to speed.  Just knowing the technical programming details is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>hire newbies.  It's almost a 'catch-22'.  But don't be discouraged,


Just to emphasize this, my experience with new programmers is
that each one reduces my output by 25% while I explain things to
them, while at the same time, that produce very little for at least
a year.  In effect, as a one man shop, if I hire four new grads
I close down for a year while I teach them.

Traditionally, newbies are hired to do junior jobs, like training
users in 123, installing software on PC's, maintaining small
networks, that sort of thing.  While they make themselves useful
doing grunt work (that the average office worker *cannot* do),
they get trained by inserting them into the programming staff
on relatively minor jobs.  As they pick up experience, they are
given bigger and bigger jobs.  It takes about 2-3 years before
they can do a decent job on something like designing a new
system from scratch, and that is for the better ones.

The bottom line is that the last thing you need in a crisis is
a junior underfoot.  The best place for a junior is in a stable
shop with lots of new work, and lots of grunt work like training,
installations, machine set-up, etc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** ramdan@mailexcite.com (tony)
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yxwi2.4239$re.998250@newscene.newscene.com>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com> <Oeai2.3619$9H6.3517506@news4.mia>`

```

I found that where you live makes a big difference.  in 1997 i took a 6 month 
programming course in Chicago for individuals with degrees in other fields 
(M.A.psychology).  I looked for work in both Los Angeles(old home town) and 
Chicago.  I got no offers in LA.  in Chicago, in one afternoon at a job fair, 
I got 11 interview offers.  I interviewed with top 4 and got 4 offers.  
90% of the people in the course, none with IS backgrounds, got positions.  
Since then the market here has gotten even hotter. 



In article <Oeai2.3619$9H6.3517506@news4.mia>, "Judson McClendon" 
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>LVenick wrote:
>>I've been listening for months on end about this so called shortage of
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>too late, but it may help get you in the door. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3689695B.B72BBD58@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com>`

```
This is true.  Noone wants to have to train someone in their business.  You may
know how to code but I doubt you know any real business applications and can hit
the ground running.  Try getting a job at a body shop (consulting house) where they
can bury you in with more experienced personnel on a client site (and let's not
hear that this doesn't happen, I've seen it all too often).  Don't be picky about
your first job.

LVenick wrote:

> I've been listening for months on end about this so called shortage of
> qualified personnel for the ever increasing number of IT jobs out there. So how
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> LV
```

#### ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368A4270.5ACA4F07@home.com>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com>`

```
Occasionally you will find an environment which cannot adjust
programmer's pay to match the going rate.  Maybe that company is broke,
or maybe it is a government which lumps programmers with administrative
people.  These environments cannot keep most of their programmers, but
still have to do their Y2K fixes and maintenance.

Figure out which places are likely to be losing their programmers in
your area.  They will be most likely to accept whatever they can get. 
Who knows?  Maybe by the time you have experience, they will have a
turnaround and be able to give you big raises  (which won't look bad on
your job history!)
```

## Thread: COBOL85 PRO WorkBench — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL85 PRO WorkBench

- **From:** "Sean Lu" <sean@kistem.po.my>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be32e7$d6755340$6c2110ac@sean>`

```
Hi All,

Anyone hear of this WIndow-based programming tool called COBOL85 PRO
WorkBench version 5 ?  I want to get some help on this tool.

Thanks.

Sean Lu
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol COMP Format

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94ai2.3615$9H6.3511596@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<368926F9.447AFC10@t-online.de>`

```
Sabahattin Kunas wrote:
>Who can help me, with COBOL COMP format. I want to a COMP format in a
>text-File in the binary Format
>example:
> PIC 9(16) 9(3) how long is this as String

The format of COMP items is compiler dependent.  Frequently it is
binary.  In general, you can not/should not place COMP items in a
text file.  This is because COMP items can contain  binary values which
could be mistaken for control characters.  Your PIC above is in error.
I assume you intended either 9(16)V9(3) or 9(16).9(3), both of which
are invalid, because the current COBOL 85 limit is 18 numeric digits.
However, if you intended 9(16) and 9(3) as separate fields, then, of
course, the first is 16 bytes and the second is three bytes.  COBOL
terminology is 'display' not 'string'.  We say a field is 'display'
or 'comp'.
```

## Thread: With a little help...... — 2 msgs

### With a little help......

- **From:** "Patriot" <patriot@videon.wave.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fEbi2.3751$EG3.2736@typhoon.mbnet.mb.ca>`

```
Hello,

For anyone out there willing to help me.  I am a student getting into
intermediate COBOL, my next assignment requires me to search a table by
index.  Direct access, since we are on  holidays, I would like to get a jump
on this.  Can anyone tell me where I can find some good online information
about Indexing, direct access, searching and multi-level tables?
I would appreciate it.

Bill Hanson
patriot@videon.wave.ca
```

#### ↳ Re: With a little help......

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3689579D.1E38CDD0@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<fEbi2.3751$EG3.2736@typhoon.mbnet.mb.ca>`

```
I'm not sure where the 'direct access' comes in since that is a way of reading
files.  For reading tables in working-storage, the table needs to be defined
OCCURS xx TIMES INDEXED BY index-name.  In referencing a specific item in the
table you would use 'index-name' the same way you would a subscript.  In the
procedure division, initialize your index with a SET statement before a SEARCH.
That wouldn't be necessary in a SEARCH ALL statement (binary search), but you'll
have to tell the compiler how the table is sorted (KEY).

HTH

Patriot wrote:

> I am a student getting into
> intermediate COBOL, my next assignment requires me to search a table by
> index.  Direct access, since we are on  holidays, I would like to get a jump
> on this.  Can anyone tell me where I can find some good online information
> about Indexing, direct access, searching and multi-level tables?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Friendly Arguement Cobol Question

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76dv8u$hkd$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<368A251A.CE854439@mail.fwi.com> <76di94$f4u$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <76di94$f4u$1@news.igs.net>...
>Your friend is right.  In fact, you can write a game
>in any language, though you may not be able to do
>it easily.  The only deficit Cobol has as a games language
>is in graphics.  For a text orient game (scrabble for
>example), it may be better than most.

Neither C nor C++ have graphics as part of their languages.

Graphics are very device or platform dependent. Input
devices are even more platform dependent.

If I had any desire to write a game for, let's say, Windows, I
would probably get the Microsoft DirectX SDK and write to the
API. Basically, this means that, if a COBOL implementation
for Windows can call a C language API that is implemented
as a DLL, then COBOL can be used to write games.

It can, therefore it can!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

>
>Manakid wrote in message <368A251A.CE854439@mail.fwi.com>...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Friendly Arguement Cobol Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76gju2$ach$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<368A251A.CE854439@mail.fwi.com> <76di94$f4u$1@news.igs.net> <76dv8u$hkd$1@server.cntfl.com> <368BA8DF.E6195ABA@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <368BA8DF.E6195ABA@home.com>...
>> Neither C nor C++ have graphics as part of their languages.
>
>Yeah, but when we buy a C++ environment, it comes with graphics.

I am sure you had a particular system in mind; but I will ask if C++
comes with graphics in all UNIX implementations. Probably not.

As for Windows and any C++ environment which uses MFC, a quick
check under the hood will show that the GDIObjects in MFC maintain
some information about the objects but the actual graphics work is
done by calling Windows.

>  When
>we buy a COBOL environment, graphics are much more limited.

If the COBOL environment is for Windows, it has (proverbially) all the
graphics capabilities of C++. It is just that it may be somewhat more
difficult to use; or it might just be far less familiar to COBOL
programmers.

WRT older MS-DOS implementations, MS C did have some
presentation graphics capabilities that were extensions to the
standard library and I seem to recall Micro Focus having an
add-on package, CO-Graph. for presentation graphics. IMO,
these graphics capabilities were probably not suitable for games
programming.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Friendly Arguement Cobol Question

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298364.20681.10401@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<76di94$f4u$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<76di94$f4u$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> in any language, though you may not be able to do
> it easily.  The only deficit Cobol has as a games language
> is in graphics.  For a text orient game (scrabble for
> example), it may be better than most.

It is no more deficient in this respect than most languages.
There is no graphics in the standard for C, C++, Pascal,
or most others.

To do graphics they call libraries of routines (such as
the Windows API or DOS based libraries).  Cobol can do that
too.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

```
Sven wrote:
>Forget it, if you ask me, I have never seen a graphical window in COBOL!

I once wrote an ultrasound chart program in COBOL.  Had to write all the
low-level graphics primitives from dot plot up.  Sent the binary graphics
from the EBCDIC COBOL machine over a 7-bit ASCI connection to a laser
printer connected to a PC.  Was lots of fun, but it did work.  Wrote a
compiler and p-code interpreter in COBOL too.  That was also fun, but it
worked.  COBOL is not well suited for that sort of thing, but you can do
almost anything you want in COBOL.
```

```
You can - but you can also use a wrench to pound a nail into a wall. 
It's not very common though.  Text games should be easy, maybe a
character based version of snake, but once you get into graphics you
would be working around the language.

Of course using the wrong language is common.  Businesses which make
decisions to move away from COBOL when they shouldn't are just as stupid
as game makers who would use COBOL would be.
```

#### ↳ Re: Friendly Arguement Cobol Question

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368A7C24.FDD57A02@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<368A251A.CE854439@mail.fwi.com>`

```
COBOL itself has some means to do character screen handling via ACCEPT and
DISPLAY. This is enough to write elementary games. The ones with high
resolution stuff could also be made using object oriented features (to be
included in the next language standard) or vendor-specific extensions.
Today, sound and graphics are quit easy possible from within COBOL, however
the language is not primarily designed for such things, so it will always be
better to do game programming in other languages.

The COBOL Frog

Manakid wrote:

> My friend and I are having a tinsy, little fight.  He says that you are
> able to make games in Cobol, like every other language.  I say he's
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> What? That's your computer !?!?"
```

#### ↳ Re: Friendly Arguement Cobol Question

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298364.20273.10270@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<368A251A.CE854439@mail.fwi.com>`

```
In message <<368A251A.CE854439@mail.fwi.com>> Manakid <dede@mail.fwi.com> writes:
> able to make games in Cobol, like every other language.  I say he's
> wrong, cuz I've only know Cobol to belong to my stereotypical vision of
> being a OS/2 banking language (knowing nothing about the language).  All
> of you are experts, and some of you have been programming since the IBM
> System/360, so I trust you. Please Help us

Of course Cobol can be used to program Games.  At a simple
level the original Cave Quest was just text display and
accept well within bog-standard ANSI Cobol. (in fact the
original was written in Fortran).

I have a large completely graphics Cobol program for planning
container ships.  It directly accesses the Windows API, there
is no reason why a Cobol program could not use this to
create a Windows Game, or call a graphics library under
other OSes.

But I doubt that it would be anyones first choice as a game
writing language, or that anyone using Cobol would be
bothered to write a game.
```

```
Hi.

I'm trying to use the InternetConnect API in Microfocus Netexpress 2.0,
```

## Thread: No accented characters in COBOL? (Newbie Q) — 5 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### No accented characters in COBOL? (Newbie Q)

- **From:** textuur@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76d5vj$irf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I am new to COBOL and have downloaded Fujitsu COBOL 3.0.
One of the things I notice is that COBOL (at least this
version) doesn't seem to support accented characters.
I checked the manuals but couldn't find pertinent information,
just a 7 bit ASCII-table.

Can somebody give some info please, or a pointer? Thank you.

Karel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: No accented characters in COBOL? (Newbie Q)

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368A7B18.5B35A1F5@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<76d5vj$irf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
textuur@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> I am new to COBOL and have downloaded Fujitsu COBOL 3.0.
> One of the things I notice is that COBOL (at least this
> version) doesn't seem to support accented characters.

Not in the RESERVED words of the language, nor in the programmer-defined
names (variable, procedures). But in alphnumeric variables and literal
strings the whole bunch of characters from ASCII, EBCDIC or whatever
char.set you're working with is available.

Read more about this in your COBOL manual. The following is an extract from
Micro Focus docs:

[start]
The most basic and indivisible unit of the language is the character. The
set of characters used to form COBOL character strings and separators
includes the letters of the alphabet, digits and special characters, and is
defined below:

Character Meaning
0 to 9 Digits
A to Z Uppercase letters
a to z Lowercase letters
Space
+ Plus sign
ï¿½ Minus sign or hyphen
* Asterisk
/ Oblique stroke/slash
= Equal sign
$ Dollar sign
. Period or decimal point
, Comma or decimal point
; Semicolon
" Quotation mark
( Left parenthesis
) Right parenthesis
> Greater Than symbol
< Less Than symbol
: Colon
Lowercase letters can be used in character strings and text words; except
when used in nonnumeric literals and except for some picture symbols, each
lowercase letter is equivalent to the corresponding uppercase letter.
This COBOL implementation is restricted to the above character set, but the
content of nonnumeric literals, comment lines, comment entries and data can
include any of the characters available under the character encoding scheme
used for the COBOL source program.
[end]

The Frog

> I checked the manuals but couldn't find pertinent information,
> just a 7 bit ASCII-table.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: No accented characters in COBOL? (Newbie Q)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76gudq$cdf@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<76d5vj$irf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <368A7B18.5B35A1F5@IMN.nl>`

```
FYI,
  Some implementations (including MF) allow "extended characters" as part of
user-defined words - if the correct directives are set.  Furthermore, this
will be allowed as a part of the next Standard - but there are a bunch of
things needed to figure out what characters are allowed and what characters
are considered upper-/lower-case equivalents.

Check your current compiler's manual under (probably) "national character"
support (or possibly under "DBCS" or "MOCS") for further information on
this.

COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote in message <368A7B18.5B35A1F5@IMN.nl>...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Yes, they are there, but (Re: No accented characters in COBOL?)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76hbo1$r1g$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<76d5vj$irf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76gcki$1cg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

textuur@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<76gcki$1cg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
[...]
>
>Now if only an indexed file would sort these characters right.
>No, not in extended ASCII sequence. Help is appreciated.
>
If you do not mind scrambling the index key, you can cause the
records to be arranged in any order you choose.

Consider the following:

       01 native-order pic x(52) value
           "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ" &
           "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz".
       01 lexical-order pic x(52) value
           "ACEGIKMOQSUWYacegikmoqsuwy" &
           "BDFHJLNPRTVXZbdfhjlnprtvxz".
       01 ascending-digit pic x(10) value "0123456789".
       01 descending-digit pic x(10) value "9876543210".

If, before writing the index record, I do:

            inspect key-name
                converting native-order to lexical-order.

The values in the key will be ordered as: AaBb ... Zz.

After reading the record, I do:

            inspect key-name
                converting lexical-order to native-order.

The key will again be returned to its correct representation.

It is a matter of setting the correct values into the native and lexical
orders. Characters that are unchanged in their order need not be
represented. Note that the example would allow the ASCII characters
between 'Z' and 'a' to be sorted into their normal order. This would
cause those character to appear between the letters 'm" and "N".

Ordering an index for descending numeric values is another
interesting use of key scrambling; but is left, as an exercise,
to the reader.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
number you have to send into MicroFocus seems to be derived from the
processor ID.  We attempted to use the same key on two machines just to see
```

<a id="t-43488226b6"></a>
## Thread: Ok..another opinion request.. — 2 msgs

### Ok..another opinion request..

- **From:** Green <new@at.cbl>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368A37AF.5EA910F@at.cbl>`

```
First, I want to thank everyone for being polite, and answering my
questions. It is very important to me, as I don't know anyone in the
Cobol buisness, and only know game programmers, and your opinions and
statements are meaningful to me.

Ok..sentimentality aside, I am curious of your opinion regarding
possible work outlook for a 'green' programmer in the buisness
programing field.

I am not 'firmly' commited to the tech school, but do have a place
reserved. They ofcourse promise 'job assistance' and boast a high 98%
hire rate, at a decent starting salary.

I am in the Northeast area, near the big city of Philadelphia, so a
bounty-full of big buisnesses.

Is this 'boot-camp' stuff the best chance of getting a job? I understand
from the buisness aspect of the troubles of training a newbie. I am
willing to pay my dues, as long as I am learning something useful, and
not just 'busy work'

The schooling is expensive, and I would hate to waste time and money for
a bleak future.I guess what I am asking is......Should I take the jump?
```

#### ↳ Re: Ok..another opinion request..

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368AE90A.4EA7@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<368A37AF.5EA910F@at.cbl>`

```
Green wrote:
> 
> First, I want to thank everyone for being polite, and answering my
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> a bleak future.I guess what I am asking is......Should I take the jump?

Don't know if this answers any questions, but here's my "how I became a
programmer" story.

Between sixth and seventh grade a took a summer computer course.  I learned
to program BASIC on a TRS-80 Model III.  I then took another BASIC course
in seventh grade.  I took Pascal in eleventh grade (UCSD Pascal on an Apple II).
I also bought Borland Turbo Pascal to use at home.  Later I bought Borland
Turbo C and a few books and tought myself C.  I majored computer science
in college.  We used, I believe, Pascal first, and then C.  Also took one
semester of 8086 assembler.  I dropped out after 1 1/2 years of college
(CU-Boulder).  Sometime later I bought Borland C++ and a few books and
taught myself C++.

I worked at a retail electronics store (Fry's Electronics) for two years.
The cash register, purchase order, and inventory systems were all computerized
on PC compatables.  It was actually rather incredible because the company
only had one programmer!  I'm not sure if they bought the system, but he
made quite a few changes in the time I was there.  Anyway, I am a rather
timid person so, although I would have loved to program on their system, I
don't think I ever even brought it up.  Still, I didn't lose my passion
for programming, even though I was working 60 hours a week and didn't even
have a computer at the time (parents having owned the one I used prior to
this) so I didn't do any actual programming for the whole two years.

After that I got a job at a bank.  After a time I was able to get my own
computer.  I converted a partially written text editor that I had written
in C in my college years to C++.  I then did a very stupid thing.  I made some
major changes to it without saving a previous working version.  I then broke
it and was never able to get it working again.  So I simply gave up.  Pretty
sad, I know.  I didn't do *any* programming at all for at least another three
years.

During that time, I was working for this bank.  I did teller, new accounts,
bookkeeping and customer service for five years.  Then I saw an internal job
posting for a COBOL programmer at our corporate data center.  I applied and
got an interview, but I had never even *seen* COBOL at the time.  I didn't
get the job.

What I *did* get was a great incentive to learn COBOL!  I purchased
Micro Focus Personal COBOL for DOS and "Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days."
After I completed that book I purchased "COBOL By Command," which filled
in a lot of the blanks that "21 Days" missed.  I also bought "CICS for the
COBOL Programmer (Part 1)."  Less than six months after not getting the first 
job I applied again.  I didn't have one single day of actual job related 
programming experience.  What I *did* have was five years bank experience and 
a "know-it-all" kind of attitude.  I got the job!

That was less than 2 1/2 years ago.  I learned JCL on the job, and some
CICS.  I also took a one week CICS course paid for by the job.  (It was
*very* useful, btw).  I'm now the primary programmer on a purchased 
ATM/card processing system.  Late last year and early this year I wrote 
a 10000+ line CICS online program from scratch.  (Actually five different 
programs, but all interrelated).

In the time I've been in the programming department we have hired about
six experienced programmers and four programmer trainees (which is what
I started as).  Every single one of the trainees were already employeed
by the company (one was in accounting, one was in audit/loan review, one
was a computer operator, and one was already in the programming department
as a tester/admin asst/user services person).  Three of the four are
still with the company.  Three of the six experience programmers did not
last more than two months.  One just disappeared one day, we know not
where.  One job a higher paying job elsewhere.  One, who allegedly had
30 years experience, couldn't program his way out of a paper bag!  He
quit before he could get fired.  (The other three are still productive
members of society...err, the department.)

Anyway, of the programmers who were there when I started, I believe five
also were hired from within the company.  Two have since quit, but two
remain in mainframe applications and one has transferred to the client/server
(PC) programming department.

What's my point?  One main point is that it seems a very good idea to work
for a company who has a programming department, even if you don't start there.
Learn everything you can there.  Any company, I'm sure, is more likely to
hire a programmer trainee from within the company rather then from outside.

A new programmer trainee is actually starting next week, but as far as I can
tell all of the other programmers were either hired from within the company
or were already experienced programmers from elsewhere.

One other thing I'd like to say is make sure you *like* programming, and
seem to be good at it, before deciding to take a job as a programmer.  I
mean, this guy with 30 years experience obviously had to be able to
program to some degree, but I don't think he was a programmer at heart.
Sure, you can make a living simply being "OK," but I sure wouldn't want to
work with you!  :-)
```

---

```
Hello,

I am just in the process of evaluating PC COBOL and would like to know if
someone has done any sort of comparison between these 2. or used both of
them.  Micro Focus (Net Express) costs about 5 times as much as Fujitsu and
I would think there would be some fairly significant differences between the
2. I talked to Micro Focus and they couldn't really provide any reasons that
would justify the difference.

We need  support for the following:

    1. MS SQL Server
    2. Be able to call COBOL programs from Visual Basic and visa versa
    3. Handle large volumes of data (millions of records)
    4. Capability to create GUI applications
    5. Print support for HP PCL or postscript printers using some sort of
forms capability

Thanks

Vic Spainhower
VicSpain@Sprynet.com
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In an attempt to explain to a newbie "why Cobol", I demo'd
the following to him:

10 FOR i = 1 TO 100
20 IF ((1 / i) * (i * i)) <> i THEN PRINT ((1 / i) * (i * i)), i
30 NEXT i

(which fails on 4 values in the 100 under QBASIC)

I was curious how VB fares on the same test, but do not
have a copy.  Anybody willing to run the test, out of curiosity?
```

```
I am a relative newbie, and I don't really understand what your test is

results of the calculation (1/i)*(i*i) are the same as i.  Is that the
point?

-Art
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76gbuc$7ef$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <76g80h$kqe$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
In qbasic, they are not.  In fact, that program will
print four values.  Basic switches all to floating point
to do calculations, then changes it back unless you
specifically do string math.  You end up with small
differences that are almost impossible to predict.

If you are trying to balance a set of books, the
result is absolute anarchy.

That VB does not is interesting.  Could you try the same test
up to the value of 10000, instead of 100?

Art Perry wrote in message <76g80h$kqe$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>...
>Donald Tees wrote in message <76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net>...
>>10 FOR i = 1 TO 100
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>trying to show.  I ran it in visual basic and it didn't print anything.
The
>results of the calculation (1/i)*(i*i) are the same as i.  Is that the
>point?
>
>-Art
```

```
<donald@willmack.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In an attempt to explain to a newbie "why Cobol", I demo'd

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I'm not this familiar with QBasic (though two weeks ago I billed about 40
hours of QBasic work at my A Series Cobol guru rate).  It appears to be
impossible to precisely store an intermediate result.  I tried LONG,
SINGLE and DOUBLE in the DIM statement below, but I still got your four
failures.

  DIM j AS DOUBLE
  FOR i = 1 TO 100
  LET j = ((1 / i) * (i * i))
  IF j <> ((1 / i) * (i * i)) THEN PRINT j; "-"; ((1 / i) * (i * i)); "=";
j - ((1 / i) * (i * i))
  NEXT i
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76grrn$j59$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <368bddea.46528011@netnews>`

```
The real problem with basic is that it converts to floating
point for all decimals.  As soon as you get away from integers
for dollars and cents, you are in trouble.  Ten cents expressed
as "$0.10" is a repeating decimal, like it or not.

If you have to write financial stuff in basic, the only way it
will ever work reliably is to do all accounting in pennies, and
convert only for output.  As long as you have enough precision
that will work.  Even then, however, when it comes to stuff
like percentage calculations, you had better insure that you
convert back to integer form before you start adding and
subtracting. By the time you are done, the code is about four
times as verbose as Cobol.

This one always astonishes beginners:

10 for i = 1 to 1000000
20 x = x + 0.1
30 next  i
40 print x

Randall Bart wrote in message <368bddea.46528011@netnews>...
>'Twas Thu, 31 Dec 1998 09:18:39 -0500, when "Donald Tees"
><donald@willmack.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>l    |/ MS^7=6/28/107   http://members.aol.com/PanicYr00/Sequence.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76h6g4$ngc$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <368bddea.46528011@netnews> <76grrn$j59$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <76grrn$j59$1@news.igs.net>...
>The real problem with basic is that it converts to floating
>point for all decimals.  As soon as you get away from integers
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>times as verbose as Cobol.
[...]

Although your statements are correct, with respect to the older
BASICs, they are not particularly valid with Visual Basic. The
Visual Basic "currency" data type is intended for use with fixed
decimal values. It is equivalent to specifying S9(14)V9(4)
BINARY, in COBOL.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
Thats interesting to know. It would certainly make
life a lot easier.  Is there anyway of declaring any
other "picture" types, or is that the only one?

There were also a bunch of Basics a few years back
that had "character arithmetic".  It was done with ASCII
type strings much like Cobol data but null terminated.
```

```
>Environment (LE) ?

The easy answer is "VERY differently".
  I will work on getting you a more helpful answer - but this is a HUGE
question and is one of the major issues related to LE migrations.  (Have you
read the LE Migration Guide from IBM yet?  That would certainly be the first
place to start.)

>
>Any experiences and/or examples would be appreciated
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Abend handling using Language Environment

- **From:** Inga Campbell <ingac@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.programming,comp.protocols.ibm
- **Message-ID:** `<368C0BF2.45BB@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<76gnvd$ahp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
georgelnj@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> How are ABENDS handled differently while running under Language
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

Our shop decided to upgrade to LE for language support while upgrading
to OS/390.  This decision has led to experiences  requiring a lot of
"catch up".  The majority of programs, both on line and batch, are
running with LE and not creating any problems.  Others, such a COBOL
programs called as subroutines by Assembler callers, can cause BIG
headaches.  LE has also been poorly supported by some third party
product vendors, though several have by now upgraded to make their tools
"LE Conforming".

Be sure to look into LE run time option settings for your most important
language (I haven't determined whether the settings recommended for the
various supported languages are 100% compatible).  Also, read about
ABPERC and the method for specifying that certain abend codes should be
"percolated" (not handled by LE).  We use an abend routine which
produces a U=999 abend, with or without a dump.  LE intercepts it and
always dumps!  The additional output (messages on SYSOUT and CEEDUMP
output) need to be handled, too.

I have studied the LE Migration Guide, read the entire COBOL for MVS &
VM Migration Guide, and also had to get into parts of the guides for
FORTRAN and PL/1.

It is my plan to attend SHARE in San Francisco on February 21 - 26,
where about 28 sessions dealing with LE are scheduled.  I would
recommend this as a good place to get knowledge and contacts, who may be
able to help you avoid a lot of pain.

Depending on your shop's mix of new and old COBOL compilers (and those
for other languages), LE could be simple or really, really, tough.

Lots of work and, to me, FUN!
Colin Campbell
```

---

## Thread: Post Y2K — 1 this month / 2 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Post Y2K

- **From:** Philippe Le Marchand <mkt@raincode.com>
- **Date:** 1998-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368ACEE4.5E04665A@raincode.com>`

```
All the institutions experiencing problems with the millenium are now
facing another one:
their thousands of lines of code have been crushed by lots of
manipulations, and the result is an unreadeable and unmanageable pile of
source code.

This situation will result in a huge amount of work each time you need
to bring a tiny improvement to your system.

RainCode can help rebuilding the structure of your source code.

All the modifications applied to your source code by a specific Y2K tool
or by an empirical procedure can be indexed by RainCode.
It will give you a powerful help to reorganize your source code, and
return you a clearly structured one, easy to maintain.

Today, RainCode is able to parse Cobol and PL1.

Check out our website at http://www.raincode.com, to have an overview of
our product, as well as a trial version of it, and mail us if you're
worried about one of your problems: RainCode can help...
```
